n200011: tn The noun вАП„Ю÷Є„©÷Є„Б„ЬвАО (mashal) can mean an object lesson based on or using a comparison or analogy. It may be a short pithy statement (Ezek 16:44), object lesson drawn from experience (Ps 78:2-6), saying or by-word (Deut 28:37), or an oracle of future blessing (Ezek 21:1-5). Here it means an object lesson setting out courses of action. It helps one choose the course of action to follow or avoid.
n200012: sn The phrase вАЬThe Proverbs of SolomonвАЭ is a title for the entire book. The title does not mean that Solomon authored or collected all the proverbs in this book. Some sections are collections from different authors: the sayings of the wise (22:17-24:22), more sayings of the wise (24:23-34), the words of Agur (Prov 30:1-33) and Lemuel (Prov 31:1-9). The title does not imply that the book was in its final canonical form in the days of Solomon; the men of Hezekiah added a collection of Solomonic proverbs to the existing form of the book (25:1-29:27). The original collection of Solomonic proverbs appears to be the collection of short pithy sayings in 10:1-22:16, and the title might have originally introduced only these. There is question whether chapters 1-9 were part of the original form of the book in the days of Solomon because they do not fit under the title; they are not вАЬproverbsвАЭ per se (sentence sayings) but introductory admonitions (longer wisdom speeches). Chapters 1-9 could have been written by Solomon and perhaps added later by someone else. Or they could have been written by someone else and added later in the days of Hezekiah.
n200013: tn The infinitive construct with вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) here designates purpose. This is the first of five purpose clauses in the opening section (1:2a, 2b, 3a, 4a, 6a). These closely related clauses reveal the purpose of the collection of proverbs in general.
n200014: tn As a stative verb вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ) can mean вАЬto knowвАЭ or, as here, вАЬto come to know,вАЭ or вАЬto become wise inвАЭ (BDB 394). This term refers to experiential knowledge, not just cognitive knowledge; it includes the intellectual assimilation and practical use of what is acquired.
n200015: sn The noun вАЬwisdomвАЭ (вАП„Ч÷Є„Ы÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО, khokhmah) could be nuanced вАЬmoral skill.вАЭ It refers to вАЬskillвАЭ that produces something of value. It is used in reference to the skill of seamen (Ps 107:27), abilities of weavers (Exod 35:26), capabilities of administrators (1 Kgs 3:28), or skill of craftsmen (Exod 31:6). In the realm of moral living, it refers to skill in livingвАФone lives life with moral skill so that something of lasting value is produced from oneвАЩs life. Deut 4:6 refers to the statutes and laws given by God as IsraelвАЩs wisdom.
n200016: tn Heb вАЬinstruction.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„°÷Є„®вАО (musar) has a three-fold range of meaning: (1) physical or parental: вАЬdiscipline; chastisementвАЭ (2) verbal: вАЬwarning; exhortationвАЭ and (3) moral: вАЬtraining; instructionвАЭ (BDB 416; HALOT 557). Throughout the Book of Proverbs the term includes moral training and instruction which may go hand in hand with either of the first two areas of meaning. The вАЬparentalвАЭ discipline may also be chastisement from God. Four times (1:2, 7; 15:33; 23:23) the term is paired with вАП„Ч÷Є„Ы÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (khokhmah, вАЬwisdom, moral skillвАЭ).
n200017: tn The infinitive construct with вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) here designates a second purpose of the book: to compare and to make proper evaluation of the sayings of the wise. The term вАП„С÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (bin, вАЬto discernвАЭ) refers to the ability to make distinctions between things, to understand, or to consider (HALOT 122).
n200018: tn Heb вАЬwords of discernment.вАЭ The noun вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„†÷Є„ФвАО (binah, вАЬdiscernmentвАЭ) functions as an attributive genitive: вАЬdiscerning wordsвАЭ or вАЬwise sayingsвАЭ (so NLT). This noun is a cognate accusative of the infinitive of the same root вАП„Ь÷∞„Ф÷Є„С÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (lehavin, вАЬto discernвАЭ). The phrase вАЬto discern words of discernmentвАЭ refers to understanding words that give insight, or wise sayings, such as in Proverbs.
n200019: tn The infinitive construct with вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) here designates a further purpose of the book: This focuses on the purpose of the book from the perspective of the student/disciple. The verb вАП„Ь÷Є„І÷Ј„ЧвАО (laqakh, вАЬreceiveвАЭ) here means to acquire something. It is parallel to the verb вАЬtreasure upвАЭ in 2:1.
n2000110: tn Heb вАЬinstruction.вАЭ See note on the same term in 1:2.
n2000111: tn Heb вАЬprudence.вАЭ The Hiphil infinitive absolute вАП„Ф÷Ј„©÷∞„В„Ы÷µ÷Љ„ЬвАО (haskel, вАЬof prudenceвАЭ) functions as a genitive of content (вАЬabout prudenceвАЭ) or of result, (вАЬleading to prudenceвАЭ). The basic meaning of вАП„©÷Є„В„Ы÷Ј„ЬвАО is вАЬto be prudentвАЭ or вАЬbe successfulвАЭ (cf. BDB 968; HALOT 1328). In the Hiphil it means вАЬto act in a prudent way,вАЭ вАЬto have insight (leading to prudent behavior),вАЭ to вАЬhave success (resulting from prudence)вАЭ, or вАЬto dispense prudenceвАЭ (i.e., instruct in wisdom). Elsewhere, the term describes the prudent actions of Abigail in contrast to her foolish husband Nabal (1 Sam 25).
n2000112: tn Heb вАЬrighteousness and justice and equity.вАЭ The three nouns that follow вАЬinstruction in prudence (or skillful living)вАЭ are adverbial accusatives of manner, describing the ways in which prudent living should be manifested: вАЬwith righteousness, justice, and equity.вАЭ The term вАЬwithвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the syntax and inserted for clarity.
n2000113: sn The word вАЬrighteousnessвАЭ (вАП„¶÷ґ„У÷ґ„ІвАО, tsedeq) describes conduct that conforms to a standard. Elsewhere it is used in a concrete sense to refer to commercial weights and measures that conform to a standard (Deut 25:15). In the moral realm it refers to вАЬrighteousвАЭ conduct that conforms to GodвАЩs law.
n2000114:
tn Heb вАЬand justice.вАЭ The Hebrew conjunction вАЬandвАЭ is omitted in the translation for the sake of English style.
sn The noun вАП„Ю÷і„©÷∞„Б„§÷Є÷Љ„ШвАО (mishpat, вАЬjustice, judgmentвАЭ) refers especially to legal matters in court whether the legal case, precedent, or judicial decision (e.g. 1 Kgs 3:28). But it also refers broadly to the concept of justice. Part of learning prudence is to make decisions that are just and right.
n2000115: sn The Hebrew noun translated вАЬequityвАЭ comes from the root вАП„Щ÷Є„©÷Є„Б„®вАО (yashar) which has the basic idea of вАЬupright, straight, right.вАЭ It refers to activity that is morally upright and straight, that is, on the proper moral path. Elsewhere it is used in a concrete sense to describe cows walking straight down a path without turning right or left (1 Sam 6:12). Wisdom literature often uses the motif of the straight path to describe a morally вАЬstraightвАЭ life.
n2000116: tn Heb вАЬto give.вАЭ The infinitive construct with вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) here introduces the fourth purpose of the book, now from the perspective of the teacher. It is what the wise instructor, or sage, wants to impart to the naive youths.
n2000117: tn The noun вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО ( њormah) вАЬprudence, shrewdness, craftinessвАЭ (BDB 791 s.v.) or вАЬclevernessвАЭ (HALOT 886 s.v. 1) refers to a shrewd plan of action, viewed positively or negatively. It is used negatively of planned deception (Josh 9:4) and premeditated murder (Exod 21:14). The related adjective described the serpent as вАЬshrewd, crafty, cunningвАЭ (Gen 3:1); it describes cunning plans (Job 5:12) and deception (Job 15:5). The related verb describes a wicked concocted plan (Ps 83:4). The term is used positively of a morally prudent lifestyle (Prov 8:5, 12; 15:5; 19:25). There is no virtue for simpletons to be unaware in this world; they need to be wise as serpents. Proverbs provide a morally shrewd plan for life.
n2000118: tn Heb вАЬthe naiveвАЭ or вАЬsimpleton.вАЭ The substantival adjective вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„™÷і„ЩвАО (peti) means вАЬsimple; open-mindedвАЭ in the sense of being open and easily influenced by either wisdom or folly (BDB 834 s.v.; HALOT 989 s.v. I вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„™÷і„ЩвАО). The simpleton is easily enticed and misled (Prov 1:32; 7:7; 9:6; 22:3; 27:12); believes everything, including bad counsel (Prov 14:15); lacks moral prudence (Prov 8:5; 19:25); needs discernment (Prov 21:11); but is capable of learning (Prov 9:4, 16). The related verb means вАЬto be wide open; open-minded; enticed, deceivedвАЭ (BDB 834). The term describes one easily persuaded and gullible, open to any influence, good or bad (cf. NLT вАЬthe simplemindedвАЭ). This is the вАЬwide-eyed youthвАЭ who is headed for trouble unless he listens to the counsel of wisdom.
n2000119: tn Heb вАЬknowledge and purpose.вАЭ The noun вАП„У÷Ј÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО (da њat, вАЬknowledgeвАЭ) may be nuanced вАЬdiscernmentвАЭ here (HALOT 229 s.v. I вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО 4). The nouns вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™ „Х÷Љ„Ю÷∞„Ц÷і„Ю÷Є÷Љ„ФвАО (da њat umezimmah, вАЬdiscernment and purposeвАЭ) form a hendiadys (two nouns joined with vav to describe the same thing). The first noun functions adjectivally and the second functions as a noun: вАЬdiscerning plan.вАЭ This parallels вАЬa shrewd plan for the morally naiveвАЭ or вАЬa discerning plan for the young person.вАЭ
n2000120: tn The noun вАП„Ю÷∞„Ц÷і„Ю÷Є÷Љ„ФвАО (mezimmah) may mean (1) вАЬplanвАЭ or (2) вАЬdiscretionвАЭ (BDB 273 s.v.; HALOT 566 s.v.). It describes the ability to make plans or formulate the best course of action for gaining a goal (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 7). The related verb вАП„Ц÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЭвАО (zamam) means вАЬto plan; to deviseвАЭ (BDB 273 s.v.; HALOT 272 s.v. I вАП„Ц„Ю„ЭвАО; e.g., Gen 11:6). Here the nouns вАЬknowledge and planвАЭ (вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™ „Х÷Љ„Ю÷∞„Ц÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО, da њat umezimmah) form a hendiadys: knowledge of how to form and carry out a morally wise plan for life.
n2000121:
tn Heb вАЬyoung manвАЭ or вАЬyouth.вАЭ The term sometimes applies to an assistant, or servant, or someone in training for a higher position.
sn As this second clause does not begin with вАЬandвАЭ in Hebrew, it may be understood as an expansion what it means to impart shrewdness.
n2000122: tn The term вАЬalsoвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation since the audience has shifted from the young and inexperienced to those already having some wisdom. As such v. 5 functions as a parenthesis in the purpose statements of 1:1-7. The book is not just for beginners; even the wise can become wiser.
n2000123: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷і„©÷∞„Б„Ю÷Ј„ҐвАО (yishma њ) functions as a jussive (rather than a imperfect, вАЬhe will hearвАЭ) as supported in conjunction with the following jussive вАП„Х÷∞„Щ„Х÷є„°÷ґ„£вАО (veyosef, вАЬLet him addвАЭ or вАЬso that he may addвАЭ).
n2000124: tn Heb вАЬadd.вАЭ Or вАЬincreaseвАЭ in insight. The Hiphil verb вАП„Х÷∞„Щ„Х÷є„°÷ґ„£вАО (veyosef) is a jussive rather than an imperfect as the final short vowel (segol) and accent on the first syllable shows (BDB 415 s.v. вАП„Щ÷Є„°÷Ј„£вАО Hiph).
n2000125: tn The Niphal substantival participle вАП„†÷Є„С„Х÷є„ЯвАО (navon, вАЬdiscerningвАЭ), rather than the noun, is used to describe a person who is habitually characterized by discernment. 1:5 forms a striking contrast to 1:4вАФthere was the simpleton and the youth, here the wise and discerning. Both need this book.
n2000126: tn The noun вАП„™÷Ј÷Љ„Ч÷∞„С÷ї÷Љ„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (takhbulah, вАЬdirection; counselвАЭ) refers to moral guidance (BDB 287 s.v.). It is related to вАП„Ч÷є„С÷µ„ЬвАО (khovel, вАЬsailorвАЭ), вАП„Ч÷і„С÷µ÷Љ„ЬвАО (khibbel, вАЬmastвАЭ) and вАП„Ч÷ґ„С÷ґ„ЬвАО (khevel, вАЬrope; cordвАЭ), so BDB suggests it originally meant directing a ship by pulling ropes on the mast. It is used in a concrete sense of God directing the path of clouds (Job 37:12) and in a figurative sense of moral guidance (Prov 11:14; 20:18; 24:6). Here it refers to the ability to steer a right course through life (A. Cohen, Proverbs, 2).
n2000127: tn The infinitive construct with вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) means вАЬto discernвАЭ and introduces the fifth purpose of the book. It focuses on the benefits of proverbs from the perspective of the reader. By studying proverbs the reader will discern the hermeneutical key to understanding more and more proverbs.
n2000128: tn The phrase вАЬthe meaning ofвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2000129: tn The noun вАП„Ю÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ„¶÷Є„ФвАО (melitsah) means вАЬallusive expression; enigmaвАЭ in general, and вАЬproverb, parableвАЭ in particular (BDB 539; HALOT 590). The related noun вАП„Ю÷µ„Ь÷і„Щ„•вАО (melits) means вАЬinterpreterвАЭ (Gen 42:23). The related Arabic root means вАЬto turn aside,вАЭ so this Hebrew term might refer to a saying that has a вАЬhidden meaningвАЭ to its words; see H. N. Richardson, вАЬSome Notes on вАП„Ь÷і„Щ„•вАО and Its Derivatives,вАЭ VT 5 (1955): 163-79.
n2000130: tn This line functions in apposition to the preceding, further explaining the phrase вАЬa proverb and a parable.вАЭ
n2000131: tn The noun вАП„Ч÷і„Щ„У÷Є„ФвАО (khidah, вАЬriddleвАЭ) designates enigmatic sayings whose meaning is obscure or hidden, such as a riddle (Num 12:8; Judg 14:12, 19), allegory (Ezek 17:2), perplexing moral problem (Pss 49:5; 78:2), perplexing question (1 Kgs 10:1 = 2 Chr 9:1) or ambiguous saying (Dan 8:23); see BDB 295 s.v. and HALOT 309 s.v. If this is related to Arabic hada (вАЬto turn aside, avoidвАЭ), it refers to sayings whose meanings are obscure. The sayings of the wise often take the form of riddles that must be discerned.
n2000132: tn Heb вАЬfear of the Lord.вАЭ In this expression (вАП„Щ÷і„®÷∞„Р÷Ј„™ „Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО, yir Њat yehvah) вАЬthe LordвАЭ functions as an objective genitive. He is the object of fear and wonder. The term вАП „Щ÷і„®÷∞„Р÷Є„ФвАО(yir Њah) comes from the root вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷µ„РвАО (yare Њ), the common root for fear in the OT which has a basic three-fold range of meanings: (1) вАЬbe in dread or terrorвАЭ (Deut 1:29; Jonah 1:10), (2) вАЬto stand in aweвАЭ (1 Kgs 3:28), (3) вАЬto revere; to respectвАЭ (Lev 19:3). With the Lord as the object, it captures the tension of shrinking back in fear and drawing close in awe and adoration. Both categories of meaning appear in Exod 20:20 (where the Lord descended upon Sinai amidst geophysical convulsions). Moses encouraged the Israelites to not be afraid of God striking them dead for no reason (вАЬDo not fear!вАЭ) but informed the people that the Lord revealed himself in such a terrifying manner to scare them from sinning (вАЬGod has come only to test you and to put the fear of him in you so that you do not sinвАЭ). The fear of the Lord is expressed in reverential submission to his willвАФthe characteristic of true worship. The fear of the Lord is the foundation for wisdom (9:10) and the discipline leading to wisdom (15:33). It is expressed in hatred of evil (8:13) and avoidance of sin (16:6), and so results in prolonged life (10:27; 19:23).
n2000133: tn The noun вАП„®÷µ„Р„©÷і„Б„Щ„™вАО (re Њshit) has a two-fold range of meaning (BDB 912 s.v.): (1) вАЬbeginningвАЭ = first step in a course of action (e.g., Ps 111:10; Prov 17:14; Mic 1:13) or (2) вАЬchief thingвАЭ as the principal aspect of something (e.g., Prov 4:7). So fearing the Lord is either (1) the first step in acquiring moral knowledge or (2) the most important aspect of moral knowledge. The first option is preferred because 1:2-6 focuses on the acquisition of wisdom.
n2000134: tn Heb вАЬknowledge.вАЭ The noun вАП„У÷Є÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО (da њat, вАЬknowledgeвАЭ) refers to experiential knowledge, not just cognitive knowledge, including the intellectual assimilation and practical application (BDB 394). It is also used in v. 4 with the nuance вАЬdiscernmentвАЭ and the variation of this motto in Prov 9:10 substitutes вАП„Ч÷Є„Ы÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (khokhmah, вАЬwisdom, moral skillвАЭ) at this point.
n2000135: tn The conjunction вАЬbutвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the antithetical parallelism. It is supplied in the translation for clarity. Note, however that the Hebrew word order is вАЬwisdom and instructionвАФfools have despised.вАЭ Without a conjunction the clash or contrast is held poetically until the end.
n2000136: tn The term вАП„Р÷±„Х÷і„Щ„ЬвАО ( Њevil, вАЬfoolвАЭ) refers to a person characterized by moral folly (BDB 17 s.v.). Fools lack understanding (10:21), do not store up knowledge (10:14), fail to attain wisdom (24:7), and refuse correction (15:5; 27:22). They are arrogant (26:5), talk loosely (14:3) and are contentious (20:3). They might have mental intelligence but they are morally foolish. In sum, they are stubborn and вАЬthick-brainedвАЭ (J. H. Greenstone, Proverbs, 6).
n2000137: tn The verb вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Ц÷Є„ФвАО (bazah, вАЬdespiseвАЭ) means to treat things of value with contempt, as if they were worthless (BDB 102 s.v.). The classic example is Esau who despised his birthright and sold it for lentil stew (Gen 25:34). The perfect verb places the life that fools have lived in contrast with the beginning of moral knowledge. Here is the way of wisdom; fools have gone a different way. Now by implicationвАФwhat is your choice? The translation of the perfect verb depends on whether the verbвАЩs root is stative or dynamic. Stative verbs (verbs that describe a state) may be present time in the perfect and so can have gnomic force (cf. KJV, NASB, ESV, NIV). Dynamic verbs (verbs that describe actions) in the Hebrew perfect form are past or perfective. They may describe a past action which is prototypical of ongoing behavior. This type of root does not have a morphological test to distinguish if it is stative or dynamic. But the meaning вАЬto treat withвАЭ despite suggests that it is dynamic, making the perfective translation вАЬhave despisedвАЭ preferred.
n2000138: sn Heb вАЬwisdom and instruction fools have despised.вАЭ Placing вАЬwisdom and moral instructionвАЭ first makes this the focus. The reader is not asked to think primarily about the nature of fools but about the choice regarding wisdom. The pair of terms echoes v. 2a, perhaps forming an inclusio.
n2000139: tn The imperative вАП„©„Б÷∞„Ю÷Ј„ҐвАО (shema њ, вАЬListen!вАЭ) forms an urgent exhortation which expects immediate compliance with parental instruction.
n2000140: tn Heb вАЬmy son.вАЭ It is likely that collections of proverbs grew up in the royal courts and were designed for the training of the youthful prince. But once the collection was included in the canon, the term вАЬsonвАЭ would be expanded to mean a disciple, for all the people were to learn wisdom when young. It would not be limited to sons alone but would include daughtersвАФas the expression вАЬthe children of (вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„†÷µ„ЩвАО, bene) IsraelвАЭ (including males and females) clearly shows. Several passages in the Mishnah and Talmud record instructions to teach daughters the Mosaic law so that they will be righteous and avoid sin as well. The translation вАЬmy child,вАЭ although not entirely satisfactory, will be used here.
n2000141: tn Heb вАЬtrainingвАЭ or вАЬdiscipline.вАЭ See note on 1:2.
n2000142: tn Heb вАЬof.вАЭ The noun вАП„Р÷Є„С÷і„Щ„Ъ÷ЄвАО ( Њavikha, вАЬof your fatherвАЭ) may be classified as a genitive of source.
n2000143: tn Heb вАЬinstruction.вАЭ In Proverbs the noun вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО (torah) often means вАЬinstructionвАЭ or вАЬmoral directionвАЭ rather than вАЬlawвАЭ (BDB 435 s.v. 1.a). It is related to вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (yarah, вАЬto point [or, show] the wayвАЭ in the Hiphil (BDB 435). Instruction attempts to point a person in the right direction (e.g., Gen 46:28).
n2000144: tn Heb вАЬof.вАЭ The noun вАП„Р÷і„Ю÷Љ÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО ( Њimmekha, вАЬof your motherвАЭ) may be classified as a genitive of source.
n2000145: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2000146: tn Heb вАЬa garland of grace.вАЭ The word вАП„Ч÷µ„ЯвАО (khen, вАЬgraceвАЭ) refers to qualities that make a person pleasant and agreeable, e.g., a gracious and charming person (BDB 336 s.v.). The metaphor compares the teachings that produce these qualities to an attractive wreath.
n2000147: tn The noun вАП„Ь÷і„Х÷∞„Щ÷Ј„ФвАО (livyah, вАЬwreath; garlandвАЭ) refers to a headdress and appears only twice in the OT (Prov 1:9; 4:9; BDB 531 s.v.; HALOT 524 s.v.).
n2000149: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2000150: tn Cf. KJV, ASV вАЬchainsвАЭ; NIV вАЬa chainвАЭ; but this English term could suggest a prisonerвАЩs chain to the modern reader rather than adornment.
n2000152: tn The term вАП„Ч÷Ј„Ш÷Љ÷Є„РвАО (khatta Њ) is the common word for вАЬsinnerвАЭ in the OT. Because the related verb is used once of sling throwers who miss the mark (Judg 20:16), the idea of sin is often explained as вАЬmissing the moral markвАЭ (BDB 306-8 s.v.). But the term should not be restricted to simply falling short of the moral ideal. Its basic meaning is to do wrongly. For a slinger or an archer that would mean missing the mark, but in the arena of morality and relationships, behaving wrongly refers to committing an offense or sinning, doing what is wrong. Here it involves the conscious intent to harm, referring to a gang of robbers.
n2000153: tn The Piel stem of the verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„™÷Є„ФвАО (patah) means вАЬto persuade, enticeвАЭ (BDB 834 s.v. вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„™÷Є„ФвАО 1; see, e.g., Judg 14:15; 16:5; Prov 16:29; Hos 2:16). In this context, the imperfect form вАП„Щ÷∞„§÷Ј„™÷Љ„Х÷Љ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (yefattukha) considers the process of offering persuasion rather than the result of someone being persuaded and may be nuanced modally: вАЬ(If) they attempt to persuade you.вАЭ
n2000154: tc The MT reads the root вАП„Р÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО ( Њavah, вАЬto be willing; to consentвАЭ). Some medieval Hebrew mss read the root вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ, вАЬto goвАЭ): вАЬdo not go with them.вАЭ The majority of Hebrew mss and the versions support the MT reading, which is the less common word and so the more likely original reading.
n2000155: tn The cohortative verb (вАП„†÷ґ„Р÷ґ„®÷∞„С÷Є„ФвАО; ne Њerevah) could indicate exhortation (вАЬLet us lie in wait!вАЭ), resolve (вАЬWe will lie in wait!вАЭ), or purpose (вАЬso that we may lie in waitвАЭ). Regardless of classification the verse indicates both invitation and intention.
n2000156: tn The verb вАП„Р÷Є„®÷Ј„СвАО ( Њarav, вАЬto lie in waitвАЭ) is used for planning murder (Deut 19:11), kidnapping (Judg 21:20), or seduction (Prov 23:28).
n2000157: tn Heb вАЬfor blood.вАЭ The term вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (dam, вАЬbloodвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of effect for вАЬblood shed violentlyвАЭ through murder (HALOT 224 s.v. 4).
n2000159: tn The term вАЬinnocentвАЭ (вАП„†÷Є„І÷і„ЩвАО, naqi) intimates that the person to be attacked is harmless.
n2000160: tn Heb вАЬwithout causeвАЭ (so KJV, NASB); NCV вАЬjust for fun.вАЭ The term вАП„Ч÷і„†÷Є÷Љ„ЭвАО (khinnam, вАЬwithout causeвАЭ) emphasizes that the planned attack is completely unwarranted.
n2000161: tn Heb вАЬlife.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ч÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (khayyim, вАЬlifeвАЭ) functions as an adverbial accusative of manner: вАЬalive.вАЭ The noun is a plural of state, where the plural morpheme is used for the abstract concept (вАЬlifeвАЭ not вАЬlivesвАЭ).
n2000162: tn The noun вАП„©„Б÷∞„Р„Х÷є„ЬвАО (she Њol) can mean (1) вАЬdeath,вАЭ cf. NCV; (2) вАЬthe grave,вАЭ cf. KJV, NIV, NLT (3) вАЬSheolвАЭ as the realm of departed spirits, cf. NAB вАЬthe nether world,вАЭ and (4) вАЬextreme danger.вАЭ Here it is parallel to the noun вАП„С„Х÷є„®вАО (vor, вАЬthe PitвАЭ) so it is the grave or more likely вАЬSheolвАЭ (cf. ASV, NRSV). Elsewhere Sheol is personified as having an insatiable appetite and swallowing people alive as they descend to their death (e.g., Num 16:30, 33; Isa 5:14; Hab 2:5). The grave is often personified similarly in ancient Near Eastern literature, e.g., in Ugaritic mythological texts Mot (= вАЬdeathвАЭ) is referred to as вАЬthe great swallower.вАЭ
n2000163: tn Heb вАЬand whole.вАЭ The vav (вАП„ХвАО) is asseverative or appositional (вАЬevenвАЭ); it is omitted in the translation for the sake of style and smoothness. The substantival adjective вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (tamim, вАЬwhole; perfect; blamelessвАЭ) is an adverbial accusative describing the condition and state of the object. Used in parallel to вАП„Ч÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (khayyim, вАЬaliveвАЭ), it must mean вАЬfull of healthвАЭ (BDB 1071 s.v. вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО 2). These cutthroats want to murder people who are full of vigor.
n2000164: tn Heb вАЬfind.вАЭ The use of the verb вАП„Ю÷Є„¶÷Є„РвАО (matsa Њ, вАЬto findвАЭ) is deliberate understatement to rhetorically down-play the heinous act of thievery.
n2000166: tn The noun вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Є„ЬвАО (shalal, вАЬplunderвАЭ) functions as an adverbial accusative of material: вАЬwith plunder.вАЭ This term is normally used for the spoils of war (e.g., Deut 20:14; Josh 7:21; Judg 8:24, 25; 1 Sam 30:20) but here refers to вАЬstolen goodsвАЭ (so NCV, CEV; e.g., Isa 10:2; Prov 16:19; BDB 1022 s.v. 3). The enticement was to join a criminal gang and adopt a life of crime to enjoy ill-gotten gain (A. Cohen, Proverbs, 4). Cf. NAB, NRSV вАЬbootyвАЭ; TEV вАЬloot.вАЭ
n2000167: tn Heb вАЬThrow in your lot with us.вАЭ This is a figurative expression (hypocatastasis) urging the naive to join their life of crime and divide their loot equally. The noun вАП„Т÷Љ„Х÷є„®÷Є„ЬвАО (goral, вАЬlotвАЭ) can refer to (1) a lot thrown for decision-making processes, e.g., choosing the scapegoat (Lev 16:8), discovering a guilty party (Jonah 1:7) or allocating property (Josh 18:6); (2) an allotted portion (Josh 15:1) or (3) allotted fate or future destiny (Prov 1:14; Dan 12:13; see BDB 174 s.v.). Here the criminals urged the lad to share their life. The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЬвАО (tappil) is an imperfect of injunction: вАЬThrow inвА¶!вАЭ but might also be an imperfect of permission: вАЬyou may throw.вАЭ It functions metonymically as an invitation to join their life of crime: вАЬshare with usвАЭ (BDB 658 s.v. 3).
n2000168: tn Heb вАЬthere will be to all of us.вАЭ
n2000169: tn Heb вАЬone purseвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NRSV). The term вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ„°вАО (kis, вАЬpurse; bagвАЭ) is a synecdoche of container (= purse) for contents (= stolen goods). The adjective вАП„Р÷ґ„Ч÷Є„УвАО ( Њekhad, вАЬoneвАЭ) indicates that the thieves promised to share equally in what they had stolen.
n2000172: tn Heb вАЬyour foot.вАЭ The term вАЬfootвАЭ (вАП„®÷ґ„Т÷ґ„ЬвАО, regel) is a synecdoche of part (= your foot) for the whole person (= yourself).
n2000173: sn The word вАЬpathвАЭ (вАП„†÷∞„™÷і„Щ„С÷Є„ФвАО, netivah) like the word вАЬwayвАЭ (вАП„У÷Љ÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО, derekh) is used as an idiom (developed from a hypocatastasis), meaning вАЬconduct, course of life.вАЭ
n2000174: tn Heb вАЬtheir feet.вАЭ The term вАЬfeetвАЭ is a synecdoche of the part (= their feet) for the whole person (= they), stressing the eagerness of the robbers.
n2000175: tn Heb вАЬrun.вАЭ The verb вАП„®„Х÷Љ„•вАО (ruts, вАЬrunвАЭ) functions here as a metonymy of association, meaning вАЬto be eagerвАЭ to do something (BDB 930 s.v.).
n2000176: tn Heb вАЬto harm.вАЭ The noun вАП„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (ra њ) has a four-fold range of meanings: (1) вАЬpain, harmвАЭ (Prov 3:30), (2) вАЬcalamity, disasterвАЭ (13:21), (3) вАЬdistress, miseryвАЭ (14:32) and (4) вАЬmoral evilвАЭ (8:13; see BDB 948-49 s.v.). The parallelism with вАЬswift to shed bloodвАЭ suggests it means вАЬto inflict harm, injury.вАЭ
n2000177: tn The imperfect tense verbs may be classified as habitual or progressive imperfects describing their ongoing continual activity.
n2000178: tc The BHS editors suggest deleting this entire verse from MT because it does not appear in several versions (Codex B of the LXX, Coptic, Arabic) and is similar to Isa 59:7a. It is possible that it was a scribal gloss (intentional addition) copied into the margin from Isaiah. But this does not adequately explain the differences. It does fit the context well enough to be original.
n2000179: tn Heb вАЬfor the net to be spread out.вАЭ The Pual participle of вАП„Ц÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (zarah) means вАЬto be spreadвАЭ (HALOT 280 s.v. I вАП„Ц„®„ФвАО pu.1). The subject of this verbal use of the participle is the noun вАП„Ф÷Є„®÷Є„©„Б÷ґ„™вАО (harashet, вАЬthe netвАЭ). It is futile for the net to be spread out in plain view of birds.
n2000180:
tn Heb вАЬin the eyes of.вАЭ
sn This means either: (1) Spreading a net in view of birds is futile because birds will avoid the trap, but the wicked are so blind that they fail to see danger; or (2) it does not matter if a net is spread because birds are so hungry they will eat anyway and be trapped; the wicked act in a similar way.
n2000181: tn Heb вАЬall of the possessors of wings.вАЭ
n2000182: sn They think that they are going to shed innocent blood, but in their blindness they do not realize that it is their own blood they shed. Their greed will lead to their destruction. This is an example of ironic poetic justice. They do not intend to destroy themselves, but this is what they accomplish.
n2000183: tn Heb вАЬtheir own souls.вАЭ The term вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, вАЬsoulвАЭ) is used as a metonymy (= soul) of association (= life). The noun вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО often refers to physical вАЬlifeвАЭ (Exod 21:23; Num 17:3; Judg 5:18; Prov 12:10; BDB 659 s.v. 3.c).
n2000184: tn The exclamation вАП„Ы÷µ÷Љ„ЯвАО (ken, вАЬso; thus; suchвАЭ) marks a conclusion (BDB 485 s.v.). It draws a comparison between the destruction of the wicked in v. 18 and the concluding statement in v. 19.
n2000185: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷Є„®÷∞„Ч„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њorkhot, вАЬpaths; waysвАЭ as a figure for mode of life): вАЬso are the ways [or, paths] of all who gain profit unjustly.вАЭ The BHS editors suggest emending the text to вАП„Р÷Ј„Ч÷≤„®÷і„Щ„™вАО ( Њakharit, вАЬendвАЭ as figure for their fate) by simple metathesis between вАП„ЧвАО (khet) and вАП„®вАО (resh) and by orthographic confusion between вАП„ЩвАО (yod) and вАП„ХвАО (vav), both common scribal errors: вАЬso is the fate of all who gain profit unjustly.вАЭ The external evidence supports MT, which is also the more difficult reading. It adequately fits the context which uses вАЬwayвАЭ and вАЬpathвАЭ imagery throughout 1:10-19.
n2000186:
tn Heb вАЬthose who unjustly gain unjust gain.вАЭ The participle вАП„С÷є÷Љ„¶÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (botsea њ, вАЬthose who unjustly gainвАЭ) is followed by the cognate accusative of the same root вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„¶÷Ј„ҐвАО (batsa њ, вАЬunjust gainвАЭ) to underscore the idea that they gained their wealth through heinous criminal activity.
sn The verb followed by the cognate noun usually means seeking gain in an unjust way (1 Sam 8:3), or for selfish purposes (Gen 37:26), or gaining by violence. The word may have the sense of covetousness.
n2000187: tn The subject of the verb is the noun вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„¶÷Ј„ҐвАО (batsa њ, вАЬunjust gainвАЭ), which is also the referent of the third person masculine singular suffix on вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Є„Щ„ХвАО (be њalav, вАЬits ownersвАЭ). Greed takes away the life of those who live by greed (e.g., 15:27; 26:27). See G. R. Driver, вАЬProblems in the Hebrew Text of Proverbs,вАЭ Bib 32 (1951): 173-74.
n2000188: tn The term вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, вАЬsoulвАЭ) is used as a metonymy (= soul) of association (= life). The noun вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО often refers to physical вАЬlifeвАЭ (Exod 21:23; Num 17:3; Judg 5:18; Prov 12:10; BDB 659 s.v. 3.c).
n2000190: tn The noun вАП„Ч÷Є„Ы÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (khokhmah, вАЬwisdomвАЭ) is the abstract feminine plural form. It probably functions as a plural of intensity, stressing the all-embracing, elevated wisdom (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 272). As in 8:1-9:11, Wisdom is personified as a righteous woman in 1:20-33.
n2000191: sn The verb вАП„®÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (ranan, вАЬto cry out, give a ringing cryвАЭ) always expresses excitement, whether of joyful praise or sorrowful lament (BDB 943 s.v.). Here it is an excited summons.
n2000192: tn Heb вАЬshe gives her voice.вАЭ The expression means to shout loudly (BDB 679 s.v. вАП„†÷Є„™÷Ј„ЯвАО Qal 1.x, HALOT 735 s.v. вАП„†÷Є„™÷Ј„ЯвАО Qal 12).
n2000193: sn The word вАП„®÷∞„Ч÷є„С„Х÷є„™вАО (rekhovot, вАЬplazasвАЭ) refers to the wide plazas or broad open spaces near the gate where all the people assembled. The personification of wisdom as a woman crying out in this place would be a vivid picture of the public appeal to all who pass by.
n2000194: tc MT reads вАП„Ф÷є„Ю÷і„Щ÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО (homiyyot, вАЬnoisy streetsвАЭ; Qal participle feminine plural from вАП„Ф÷Є„Ю÷Є„ФвАО [hamah], вАЬto murmur; to roarвАЭ), referring to the busy, bustling place where the street branches off from the gate complex. The LXX reads ѕДќµќєѕЗќ≠ѕЙќљ (teiche≈Нn) which reflects вАП„Ч÷є„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО (khomot), вАЬwallsвАЭ (feminine plural noun from вАП„Ч„Х÷є„Ю÷Є„ФвАО [khomah], вАЬwallвАЭ): вАЬShe proclaims on the summits of the walls.вАЭ MT is preferred because it is the more difficult form. The LXX textual error was caused by simple omission of вАП„ЩвАО (yod). In addition, the LXX expands the verse to read, вАЬshe sits at the gates of the princes, at the gates of the city she boldly says.вАЭ The shorter MT reading is preferred.
n2000195: sn The phrase вАЬin the cityвАЭ further defines the area of the entrance just inside the gate complex, the business area. In an ancient Near Eastern city, business dealings and judicial proceedings would both take place in this area.
n2000197: tn Wisdom addresses three types of people: simpletons (вАП„§÷∞÷Љ„™÷Є„Щ÷і„ЭвАО, petayim), scoffers (вАП„Ь÷µ„¶÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, letsim) and fools (вАП„Ы÷∞÷Љ„°÷і„Щ„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, kesilim). For the term вАЬsimpletonвАЭ see note on 1:4. Each of these three types of people is satisfied with the life being led and will not listen to reason. See J. A. Emerton, вАЬA Note on the Hebrew Text of Proverbs 1:22-23, вАЭ JTS 19 (1968): 609-14.
n2000198: tn Heb вАЬsimplicityвАЭ (so KJV, NASB); NAB вАЬinanity.вАЭ The noun вАП„§÷ґ÷Љ„™÷і„ЩвАО (peti) means вАЬsimplicity; lack of wisdomвАЭ (BDB 834 s.v.; HALOT 989 s.v. II вАП„§÷ґ÷Љ„™÷і„ЩвАО). It is related to the term вАП„§÷∞÷Љ„™÷Є„Щ÷і„ЭвАО (petayim) вАЬsimpletonsвАЭ and so forms a striking wordplay. This lack of wisdom and moral simplicity is inherent in the character of the naive person.
n2000199: tn The words вАЬhow longвАЭ appear in the first line of the verse and are understood by implication in each line. English style requires repeating the question words because of the changing of the tense of the verbs in the three lines.
n20001100: sn The term вАП„Ь÷µ„¶÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (letsim, вАЬscoffers; mockersвАЭ) comes from the root вАП„Ь÷і„Щ„•вАО (lits, вАЬto scorn; to mock; to speak indirectlyвАЭ (BDB 539 s.v. вАП„Ь÷і„Щ„•вАО). They are cynical and defiant freethinkers who ridicule the righteous and all for which they stand (e.g., Ps 1:1).
n20001101: tn The Hebrew verb (вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„УвАО, khamad) is often translated in the future tense to match the other verbs in the verse. But вАЬwill loveвАЭ and вАЬwill hateвАЭ are both imperfect forms of stative verbs which must be future. In contrast, the verb вАЬto take pleasure; to delightвАЭ (вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„УвАО, khamad) is in the perfect conjugation and is morphologically dynamic (as indicated by its imperfect form вАПвАП„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷∞„Ю÷є„УвАО, yakhmod). Therefore it is past or perfective. By switching the time frame, the rebuke embedded in the question looks forward and back, both at what should not continue and what should not have been done.
n20001102: tn Heb вАЬfor themselves.вАЭ The ethical dative вАП„Ь÷Є„Ф÷ґ„ЭвАО (lahem, вАЬfor themselvesвАЭ) is normally untranslated. It is a rhetorical device emphasizing that they take delight in mockery for their own self-interests.
n20001103: tn The words вАЬhow longвАЭ appear in the first line of the verse and are understood by implication in each line. English style requires repeating the question words because of the changing of the tense of the verbs in the three lines.
n20001104: sn The term вАЬfoolвАЭ (вАП„Ы÷∞÷Љ„°÷і„Щ„ЬвАО, kesil) refers to the morally insensitive dullard (BDB 493 s.v.).
n20001105: tn The form of the verb вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б„Х÷Љ„С„Х÷ЉвАО (yashuvu) is an imperfect from вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv, вАЬto returnвАЭ). The translation depends on how it works with the preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed). Most frequently lamed means вАЬto, toward.вАЭ But it can also mean вАЬfromвАЭ (HALOT 508 s.v. 5). If it means вАЬto,вАЭ it says вАЬyou return to my rebukeвАЭ which is contrary to the context. Some translations (ESV, NIV) add вАЬifвАЭ to make sense of the context. Others (NASB, NRSV, KJV) translate as an imperative, e.g. вАЬturn to,вАЭ though they probably understood it as a jussive in meaning. If the preposition means вАЬfromвАЭ here, then it probably continues the series of questions in v. 22, вАЬHow long will you turn away from my rebuke?вАЭ Compare also Roland Murphy, Proverbs (WBC), 8, 10. While the precise formulation is in question, the rhetorical force is not. The translation is faithful to the rhetorical force of the clause.
n20001106: sn The noun вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„Ы÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (tokhakhat, вАЬrebukeвАЭ) is used in all kinds of disputes including rebuking, arguing, reasoning, admonishing, and chiding. The term is broad enough to include here warning and rebuke. Cf. KJV, NAB, NRSV вАЬreproofвАЭ; TEV вАЬwhen I reprimand youвАЭ; CEV вАЬcorrect you.вАЭ
n20001108: tn The Hiphil cohortative of вАП„†÷Є„С÷Ј„ҐвАО (nava њ, вАЬto pour outвАЭ) describes the speakerвАЩs resolution to pour out wisdom on those who respond.
n20001109: tn Heb вАЬmy spirit.вАЭ The term вАЬspiritвАЭ (вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО, ruakh) functions as a metonymy (= spirit) of association (= thoughts), as indicated by the parallelism with вАЬmy wordsвАЭ (вАП„У÷Љ÷∞„С÷Є„®÷Ј„ЩвАО, devaray). The noun вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (ruakh, вАЬspiritвАЭ) can have a cognitive nuance, e.g., вАЬspirit of wisdomвАЭ (Exod 28:3; Deut 34:9). It is used metonymically for вАЬwordsвАЭ (Job 20:3) and вАЬmindвАЭ (Isa 40:13; Ezek 11:5; 20:32; 1 Chr 28:12; see BDB 925 s.v. вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО 6). The вАЬspirit of wisdomвАЭ produces skill and capacity necessary for success (Isa 11:2; John 7:37-39).
n20001110: tn Here too the form is the cohortative, stressing the resolution of wisdom to reveal herself to the one who responds.
n20001111: tn The term вАЬhoweverвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the contrast between the offer in 1:23 and the accusation in 1:24-25. It is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n20001112: tn The particle вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЯвАО (ya њan, вАЬbecauseвАЭ) introduces a causal clause which forms part of an extended protasis; the apodosis is 1:26.
n20001113: tn The phrase вАЬto listenвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n20001114: tn The term вАЬbecauseвАЭ does not appear in this line but is implied by the parallelism; it is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n20001115: sn This expression is a metonymy of adjunct; it is a gesture that goes with the appeal for some to approach.
n20001116: tn The participle expresses the circumstances while she stretched out her hand.
n20001117: tn The verb III вАП„§÷Є÷Љ„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (para њ) means вАЬto let go; to let aloneвАЭ (BDB 828 s.v.). It can refer to unkempt hair of the head (Lev 10:6) or lack of moral restraint: вАЬto let things run freeвАЭ (Exod 32:25; Prov 28:19). Here it means вАЬto avoid, neglectвАЭ the offer of wisdom (BDB 829 s.v. 2). As a preterite, or vayyiqtol, this verb continues the response of the naive and the fools from the previous verse. The structure of 1:24-25 is: вАЬBecause I did X, but you did Y, and [because] I did A, but you did B and C, and D.вАЭ Verse 25 has the вАЬand C and DвАЭ portions.
n20001118: tn The verbs in this series are a participle, a preterite/vayyiqtol, and finally a perfect. вАЬNeglectedвАЭ is a preterite (matching the preterite вАЬrefusedвАЭ in 1:24) giving the contrasting response to what Lady Wisdom did. вАЬDid not complyвАЭ is a Hebrew perfect in order to expand on what вАЬneglectedвАЭ means, rather than give an additional response as another preterite would imply. For the word вАЬcomply, consent,вАЭ see 1:20.
n20001119: tn The conclusion or apodosis is now introduced.
n20001120: sn Laughing at the consequences of the foolвАЩs rejection of wisdom does not convey hardness against the fool; it reveals the folly of rejecting wisdom (e.g., Ps 2:4). It vindicates wisdom and the appropriateness of the disaster (D. Kidner, Proverbs [TOTC], 60).
n20001121: tn Heb вАЬat your disaster.вАЭ The second person masculine plural suffix is either (1) a genitive of worth: вАЬthe disaster due youвАЭ or (2) an objective genitive: вАЬdisaster strikes you.вАЭ The term вАЬdisasterвАЭ (вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„УвАО,  Њed) often refers to final life-ending calamity (Prov 6:15; 24:22; BDB 15 s.v. 3). The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) focuses upon time here.
n20001122: tn Heb вАЬyour dreadвАЭ (so NASB); KJV вАЬyour fearвАЭ; NRSV вАЬpanic.вАЭ The second person masculine plural suffix is a subjective genitive: вАЬthat which you dread.вАЭ
n20001124: sn The term вАЬwhirlwindвАЭ (NAB, NIV, NRSV; cf. TEV, NLT вАЬstormвАЭ) refers to a devastating storm and is related to the verb вАП„©„Б„Х÷є„РвАО (sho Њ, вАЬto crash into ruinsвАЭ; see BDB 996 s.v. вАП„©„Б„Х÷є„Р÷Є„ФвАО). Disaster will come swiftly and crush them like a devastating whirlwind.
n20001125: tn Heb вАЬyour disaster.вАЭ The second person masculine plural suffix is an objective genitive: вАЬdisaster strikes you.вАЭ
n20001126: tn Heb вАЬlike a storm.вАЭ The noun вАП„°„Х÷Љ„§÷Є„ФвАО (sufah, вАЬstormвАЭ) is often used in similes to describe sudden devastation (Isa 5:28; Hos 8:7; Amos 1:14).
n20001127: tn Heb вАЬdistress and trouble.вАЭ The nouns вАЬdistress and troubleвАЭ mean almost the same thing so they may form a hendiadys. The two similar sounding terms вАП„¶„Х÷Љ„І÷Є„ФвАО (tsuqah) and вАП„¶÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (tsarah) also form a wordplay (paronomasia) which also links them together.
n20001128: tn Heb вАЬlook to.вАЭ The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (shakhar, вАЬto lookвАЭ) is used figuratively of intensely looking (i.e., seeking) for deliverance out of trouble (W. L. Holladay, Concise Hebrew and Aramaic Lexicon, 366); cf. NLT вАЬanxiously search for.вАЭ It is used elsewhere in parallelism with вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„©„БвАО (baqash, вАЬto seek rescueвАЭ; Hos 5:15). It does not mean вАЬto seek earlyвАЭ (cf. KJV) as is popularly taught due to etymological connections with the noun вАП„©„Б÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (shakhar, вАЬdawnвАЭ; so BDB 1007 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„®вАО).
n20001129: sn The causal particle вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„™ „Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (takhat ki, вАЬfor the reason thatвАЭ) explains why Lady Wisdom will not respond to their calls in the future. Their past refusal to listen means she will not listen and they will have to bear the consequences of their choices. The content repeats the previous accusation in verse 22 of hating moral knowledge. And the two halves of verse 29 echo the two parts of 1:7a, emphasizing how completely they have missed the mark.
n20001130: tn Heb вАЬknowledge.вАЭ The noun вАП„У÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО (da њat, вАЬknowledgeвАЭ) refers to moral knowledge. See note on 1:7.
n20001131: tn Heb вАЬthe fear of the Lord.вАЭ The noun is an objective genitive; the Lord is to be the object of fear. See note on 1:7.
n20001132: tn The verb вАЬspurnedвАЭ (вАП„†÷Є„Р÷Ј„•вАО, na Њats) is parallel to вАЬcomply, accede to, be willingвАЭ (e.g., 1:10). This is how the morally stubborn fool acts (e.g., 15:5).
n20001133: tn The vav (вАП„ХвАО) prefixed to the verb вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷є„Р„Ы÷∞„Ь„Х÷ЉвАО (veyo Њkhelu) functions in a consecutive logical sense: вАЬtherefore.вАЭ
n20001134: sn The expression вАЬeat the fruit ofвАЭ is a figurative expression (hypocatastasis) that compares the consequences of sin to agricultural growth that culminates in produce. They will suffer the consequences of their sinful actions, that is, they will вАЬreapвАЭ what they вАЬsow.вАЭ
n20001135: sn The words вАЬwayвАЭ (вАП„У÷Љ÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО, derekh) and вАЬcounselвАЭ (вАП„Ю„Х÷є„Ґ÷µ„¶÷Є„ФвАО, mo њetsah) stand in strong contrast to the instruction of wisdom which gave counsel and rebuke to encourage a better way. They will bear the consequences of the course they follow and the advice they take (for that wrong advice, e.g., Ps 1:1).
n20001136: tn Heb вАЬto eat to oneвАЩs fill.вАЭ The verb вАП„©„В÷Є„С÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (savea њ) means (1) positive: вАЬto eat oneвАЩs fillвАЭ so that oneвАЩs appetite is satisfied and (2) negative: вАЬto eat in excessвАЭ as a glutton to the point of sickness and revulsion (BDB 959 s.v.). Fools will not only вАЬeatвАЭ the fruit of their own way (v. 31a), they will be force-fed this revolting вАЬmenuвАЭ which will make them want to vomit (v. 31b) and eventually kill them (v. 32).
n20001137: tn Heb вАЬturning awayвАЭ (so KJV). The term вАП„Ю÷∞„©„Б„Х÷Љ„С÷Ј„™вАО (meshuvat, вАЬturning awayвАЭ) refers to moral defection and apostasy (BDB 1000 s.v.; cf. ASV вАЬbackslidingвАЭ). The noun вАП„Ю÷∞„©„Б„Х÷Љ„С÷Ј„™вАО (вАЬturning awayвАЭ) which appears at the end of WisdomвАЩs speech in 1:32 is from the same root as the verb вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„©„Б„Х÷Љ„С„Х÷ЉвАО (tashuvu, вАЬturn!вАЭ) which appears at the beginning of this speech in 1:23. This repetition of the root вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv, вАЬto turnвАЭ) creates a wordplay: Because fools refuse to вАЬturn toвАЭ wisdom (1:23), they will be destroyed by their вАЬturning awayвАЭ from wisdom (1:32). The wordplay highlights the poetic justice of their judgment. But here they have never embraced the teaching in the first place; so it means turning from the advice as opposed to turning to it.
n20001138: sn The Hebrew verb вАЬto killвАЭ (вАП„Ф÷Є„®÷Ј„ТвАО, harag) is the end of the naive who refuse to change. The word is broad enough to include murder, massacre, killing in battle, and execution. Here it is judicial execution by God, using their own foolish choices as the means to ruin.
n20001139: tn Heb вАЬcomplacencyвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); NAB вАЬsmugness.вАЭ The noun вАП„©„Б÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Х÷Ј„ФвАО (shalvah) means (1) positively: вАЬquietness; peace; easeвАЭ and (2) negatively: вАЬself-sufficiency; complacency; careless securityвАЭ (BDB 1017 s.v.), which is the sense here. It is вАЬrepose gained by ignoring or neglecting the serious responsibilities of lifeвАЭ (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 29).
n20001140: tn The participle is used substantivally here: вАЬwhoever listensвАЭ will enjoy the benefits of the instruction.
n20001141: tn The noun вАП„С÷ґ÷Љ„Ш÷Ј„ЧвАО (betakh, вАЬsecurityвАЭ) functions as an adverbial accusative of manner: вАЬin security.вАЭ The phrase refers to living in a permanent settled condition without fear of danger (e.g., Deut 33:12; Ps 16:9). It is the antithesis of the dread of disaster facing the fool and the simple.
n20001142: tn The verb вАП„©÷Ј„Б„Р÷≤„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (sha Њanan) is a Palel perfect of вАП„©÷Є„Б„Р÷Ј„ЯвАО (sha Њan) which means вАЬto be at ease; to rest securelyвАЭ (BDB 983 s.v. вАП„©÷Є„Б„Р÷Ј„ЯвАО). Elsewhere it parallels the verb вАЬto be undisturbedвАЭ (Jer 30:10), so it means вАЬto rest undisturbed and quiet.вАЭ The combination of вАП„ХвАО (vav) plus perfect continues the framework of the verb preceding it, in this case the imperfect.
n200021: sn The chapter begins with an admonition to receive wisdom (1-4) and then traces the benefits: the knowledge of God and his protection (5-8), moral discernment for living (9-11), protection from evil men (12-15) and immoral women (16-19), and enablement for righteous living (20-22).
n200023: sn Verses 1-11 form one long conditional sentence in the Hebrew text: (1) the protasis (вАЬifвА¶вАЭ) encompasses vv. 1-4 and (2) the apodosis (вАЬthenвА¶вАЭ) consists of two parallel panels in vv. 5-8 and vv. 9-11 both of which are introduced by the particle вАП„Р÷Є„ЦвАО ( Њaz, вАЬthenвАЭ).
n200024: sn The verb вАЬto store upвАЭ (вАП„¶÷Є„§÷Ј„ЯвАО, tsafan; cf. NAB, NLT вАЬtreasureвАЭ) in the second colon qualifies the term вАЬreceiveвАЭ (вАП„Ь÷Є„І÷Ј„ЧвАО, laqakh) in the first, just as вАЬcommandsвАЭ intensifies вАЬwords.вАЭ This pattern of intensification through parallelism occurs throughout the next three verses. The verb вАЬto store up; to treasureвАЭ is used in reference to things of value for future use, e.g., wealth, dowry for a bride. Since proverbs will be useful throughout life and not always immediately applicable, the idea of storing up the sayings is fitting. They will form the way people think which in turn will influence attitudes (W. G. Plaut, Proverbs, 43).
n200025:
tn Heb вАЬstore up with yourself.вАЭ Most translations use вАЬtreasureвА¶ within youвАЭ (NASB, NKJV, NRSV), but also вАЬstore upвАЭ (NIV) or вАЬwith youвАЭ (ESV). NIDOTTE 837 s.v. вАП„¶÷Є„§÷Ј„ЯвАО says the verb вАЬtakes on the technical meaning of memorizing the commandments of God.вАЭ The instructions are to have these lessons stored up inside so that you can draw on them in need.
sn The idea here is to study to be prepared. It is the opposite of the idea of getting in a difficult situation and then looking for something in the Bible to apply to your life. This verse is about applying your life to biblical wisdom and being prepared for situations that may come your way.
n200026: tn The Hiphil infinitive construct вАП„Ь÷∞„Ф÷Ј„І÷∞„©„Б÷і„Щ„СвАО (lehaqshiv, вАЬby making attentiveвАЭ) functions as an epexegetical explanation of how one will receive the instruction.
n200027: sn The word вАЬearвАЭ is a metonymy of cause; the word is used as the instrument of hearing. But in parallelism with вАЬheartвАЭ it indicates one aspect of the mental process of hearing and understanding. A вАЬhearing earвАЭ describes an obedient or responsive person (BDB 24 s.v. вАП„Р÷є„Ц÷ґ„ЯвАО 2).
n200028: tn The conjunction вАЬandвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for the sake of smoothness.
n200029: tn The Hiphil imperfect (вАЬby turningвАЭ) continues the nuance introduced by the infinitive construct in the first colon (GKC 352 ¬І114.r). The verb вАП„†÷Є„Ш÷Є„ФвАО (natah) normally means вАЬto stretch outвАЭ and only occasionally вАЬto turnвАЭ or вАЬto inclineвАЭ oneвАЩs heart to something, as is the case here.
n2000210: tn Or вАЬmindвАЭ (the center of the will, the choice).
n2000211: tn Both particles retain their individual meanings, otherwise the verse would begin with a strong adversative and be a contrast to what has been said.
n2000212: sn The noun recalls the second purpose of the book (1:2). It is also cognate to the last word of 2:2, forming a transition. The two objects of the prepositions in this verse are actually personifications, as if they could be summoned.
n2000213: tn Heb вАЬgive your voice.вАЭ The expression means to shout loudly (BDB 679 s.v. вАП„†÷Є„™÷Ј„ЯвАО Qal 1.x, HALOT 735 s.v. вАП„†÷Є„™÷Ј„ЯвАО Qal 12), to make a sound that carries farther (e.g., Jer 2:15). J. H. Greenstone says, вАЬIf it [understanding] does not come at your first call, raise your voice to a higher pitch, put forth greater effortsвАЭ (Proverbs, 17).
n2000214: tn The conditional particle now reiterates the initial conditional clause of this introductory section (1-4); the apodosis will follow in v. 5.
n2000215: tn The verb вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„І÷Ј„©„БвАО (baqash) means вАЬto search for; to seek; to investigateвАЭ (BDB 134 s.v.). This calls for the same diligence one would have in looking for silver.
n2000216: sn The two similes affirm that the value placed on the object will influence the eagerness and diligence in the pursuit and development of wisdom (e.g., Job 28:9-11). The point is not only that the object sought is valuable, but that the effort will be demanding but rewarding.
n2000217: sn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„§÷Ј„©„ВвАО (khafas) means вАЬto dig; to searchвАЭ (BDB 344 s.v.; cf. NCV вАЬhunt for itвАЭ). The Arabic cognate means вАЬto dig for water.вАЭ It is used literally of Joseph searching his brothersвАЩ sacks (Gen 44:12) and figuratively for searching the soul (Ps 64:7). This is a more emphatic word than the one used in the first colon and again emphasizes that acquiring wisdom will be demanding.
n2000218: tn The verb вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (bin, вАЬto perceive; to understand; to discernвАЭ) refers to ability to grasp, discern or be sensitive to what it means to fear the Lord.
n2000219: tn Heb вАЬthe fear of the Lord.вАЭ The noun is an objective genitive; the Lord is to be the object of fear and reverence.
n2000221: tn The term вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО (da њat, вАЬknowledgeвАЭ) goes beyond cognition; it is often used metonymically (cause) for obedience (effect); see, e.g., Prov 3:6, вАЬin all your ways acknowledge him,вАЭ and BDB 395 s.v. This means that the disciple will follow GodвАЩs moral code; for to know God is to react ethically and spiritually to his will (e.g., J. H. Greenstone, Proverbs, 18).
n2000222: tn Heb вАЬknowledge of God.вАЭ The noun is an objective genitive.
n2000223: tn This is a causal clause. The reason one must fear and know the Lord is that he is the source of true, effectual wisdom.
n2000224: tn The verb is an imperfect tense which probably functions as a habitual imperfect describing a universal truth in the past, present and future.
n2000225: sn This expression is an anthropomorphism; it indicates that the Lord is the immediate source or author of the wisdom. It is worth noting that in the incarnation many of these вАЬanthropomorphismsвАЭ become literal in the person of the Logos, the Word, Jesus, who reveals the Father.
n2000226: tn The verb вАЬcomesвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity and smoothness.
n2000227:
tc The form is a Kethib/Qere reading, reflecting confusion between вАП„ЩвАО (yod) and вАП„ХвАО (vav). The Kethib вАП„Х÷∞„¶÷Є„§÷Ј„ЯвАО (vetsafan; Qal perfect with vav consecutive) is supported by the Syriac (but not by the LXX, contra the notes in BHS). The Qere вАП„Щ÷і„¶÷∞„§÷Љ÷є„ЯвАО (yitspon; Qal imperfect) is supported by the LXX, the Aramaic Targum of Prov 2:7 (the Aramaic translations of the Hebrew scriptures were called Targums), and Latin Vulgate. Internal evidence favors the imperfect. As in v. 6a, this Qal imperfect functions as a habitual imperfect, or general present.
sn The verbal root вАП„¶÷Є„§÷Ј„ЯвАО (tsafan, вАЬto store up; to treasure upвАЭ) is repeated in 2:1 and 2:7. In 2:1 it is the responsibility of man to вАЬstore upвАЭ wisdom, but in 2:7 it is God who вАЬstores upвАЭ wisdom for the wise person who seeks him.
n2000228: tn The noun вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷Љ„©÷і„Б„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (tushiyyah) has a two-fold range of meanings: (1) вАЬsound wisdomвАЭ (so KJV, NRSV); вАЬeffective counselвАЭ and (2) result (metonymy of effect): вАЬabiding successвАЭ (BDB 444 s.v.; W. L. Holladay, Concise Hebrew and Aramaic Lexicon, 388; cf. NIV вАЬvictoryвАЭ). It refers to competent wisdom and its resultant ability to achieve moral success (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 80).
n2000229: sn The Hebrew word translated вАЬuprightвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷Є„©÷Є„Б„®вАО, yashar) is one of the terms used for the righteous. It points to the right conduct of the believerвАФthat which is right or pleasing in the eyes of God. It stresses that the life of the individual is upright, straightforward, and just. It is paralleled with вАЬthose who walk in integrity.вАЭ
n2000230: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2000231: tn The word can be taken as in apposition explaining the subject of the first colonвАФthe Lord is a shield, the Lord stores up. The word then is a metaphor for the protection afforded by the Lord.
n2000232: tn Heb вАЬwalk.вАЭ The verb вАЬto walkвАЭ (вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО, halakh) is an idiom (based upon hypocatastasis: implied comparison) for habitual manner of life (BDB 234 s.v. 3.e).
n2000233: tn Heb вАЬthose who walk of integrity.вАЭ The noun вАП„™÷є„ЭвАО (tom, вАЬintegrityвАЭ) functions as a genitive of manner.
n2000234: tn The infinitive construct вАП„Ь÷і„†÷∞„¶÷є„®вАО (lintsor, вАЬto guardвАЭ) designates the purpose of the Lord giving вАЬeffective counselвАЭ and being a вАЬshieldвАЭ to the upright. The verb вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„®вАО (natsar, вАЬto guardвАЭ) has a broad range of meanings: (1) to watch over, guard or protect a vineyard from theft (Prov 27:18); (2) to guard oneвАЩs lips or heart from evil (Prov 4:23; 13:3); (3) to protect a person from moral or physical danger (Prov 2:8, 11; 4:6; 13:6; 20:28; 22:12; 24:12) and (4) to guard with fidelity = to observe commands, law or covenant (Prov 3:1, 21; 4:13; 5:2; 6:20; 28:7; see BDB 665-66 s.v.). Here God guards the way of the just, that is, the course and conduct of life from the influence of evil.
n2000235: tn Heb вАЬpaths of righteousness.вАЭ The word вАЬrighteousnessвАЭ is a possessive genitive, signifying the ways that the righteous take.
n2000236: tn The imperfect tense verb вАП„Щ÷і„©„Б÷∞„Ю÷є„®вАО (yishmor, вАЬto protectвАЭ) continues the syntactical nuance of the preceding infinitive construct of purpose.
n2000237:
tc The Kethib is the singular noun with third person masculine singular suffix вАП„Ч÷≤„°÷і„Щ„У„Х÷євАО (khasido) вАЬhis pious one.вАЭ The Qere reads the plural noun with third person masculine singular suffix вАП„Ч÷≤„°÷і„Щ„У÷Є„Щ„ХвАО (khasidayv) вАЬhis pious ones.вАЭ The LXX ќµбљРќїќ±ќ≤ќњѕЕќЉќ≠ќљѕЙќљ ќ±бљРѕДѕМќљ (eulaboumen≈Нn auton) supports the Qere reading.
tn The noun вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„УвАО (khesed, вАЬthe piousвАЭ) describes those who show вАЬcovenantal faithful loveвАЭ or вАЬloyal loveвАЭ to God and his people. The description of the righteous by this term indicates their active participation in the covenant, for which God has promised his protection.
n2000238: tn Heb вАЬdiscern.вАЭ See preceding note on вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (bin) in 2:5.
n2000239: tn The phrase вАЬevery good wayвАЭ functions appositionally to the preceding triad of righteous attributes, further explaining and defining them.
n2000240: tn Heb вАЬevery way of good.вАЭ The term вАП„Ш„Х÷є„СвАО (tov, вАЬgoodвАЭ) functions as an attributive genitive: вАЬgood way.вАЭ
n2000241: tn Heb вАЬtrackвАЭ; KJV, NIV, NRSV вАЬpath.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„Т÷Љ÷Ј„ЬвАО (ma њgal) is used (1) literally of вАЬwagon-wheel track; firm pathвАЭ and (2) figuratively (as a metaphor) to describe the course of life (Pss 17:5; 23:3; 140:6; Prov 2:9, 15, 18; 4:11, 26; 5:6, 21; Isa 26:7; 59:8; see BDB 722-23 s.v. 2; KBL 2:609). It is related to the feminine noun вАП„Ґ÷≤„Т÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО ( њagalah, вАЬcartвАЭ) and the verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„Т÷Ј„ЬвАО ( њagal) вАЬto be roundвАЭ (Qal) and вАЬto rollвАЭ (Niphal). As a wagon-wheel cuts a deep track in a much traversed dirt road, so a person falls into routines and habits that reveal his moral character. In Proverbs the вАЬpathsвАЭ of the righteous are characterized by uprightness and integrity.
n2000242: tn The noun вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬheartвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of association for вАЬmindвАЭ and вАЬthoughtsвАЭ (BDB 524 s.v. 3). It represents the center of the inner life where the volition and emotions join to bring about actions. It is used here in parallelism with вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, вАЬsoulвАЭ), for which see note.
n2000243: tn Heb вАЬknowledge.вАЭ For the noun вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО (da њat), see the note on 1:7.
n2000244: tn Heb вАЬpleasant.вАЭ The verb вАП„Щ÷і„†÷∞„Ґ÷Є„ЭвАО (yin њam, вАЬto be pleasantвАЭ) describes what is attractive. It is used of being physically attracted to oneвАЩs lover (Song 7:7) or to a close friendship (2 Sam 1:26). Here wisdom becomes attractive to the righteous, that is, the righteous desires to acquire it.
n2000245: tn Heb вАЬyour soul.вАЭ The term вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО (nafshekha, вАЬyour soulвАЭ) functions as a synecdoche of part (= soul) for the whole person (= you); see BDB 660 s.v. 4.a.2. It also might function as a metonymy of association for emotions and passions (BDB 660 s.v. 6) or mental cognition (BDB 660 s.v. 7).
n2000246: tn The word вАП„Ю÷∞„Ц÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (mezimmah, вАЬdiscretionвАЭ) is the ability to know the best course of action for achieving oneвАЩs goal. It is knowledge and understanding with a purpose. This kind of knowledge enables one to make the right choices that will protect him from blunders and their consequences (cf. NLT вАЬwise planningвАЭ; CEV вАЬsound judgmentвАЭ).
n2000248: tn The Hiphil infinitive construct of вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„ЬвАО (natsal, вАЬto deliverвАЭ) expresses the purpose of understanding right conduct: to protect a person from the wicked. The verb вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„ЬвАО (natsal) means вАЬto save; to deliver; to rescue,вАЭ as in snatching away prey from an animal, rescuing from enemies, plucking a brand from the fire, retrieving property, or the like. Here it portrays rescue from the course of action of the wicked.
n2000249: tn The term вАЬwickedвАЭ (вАП„®÷Ј„ҐвАО, ra њ) means вАЬbad, harmful, painful.вАЭ Rather than referring to the abstract concept of вАЬwickednessвАЭ in general, the term probably refers to wicked people because of the parallelism with вАЬthose speaking perversity.вАЭ
n2000250: tn Heb вАЬman.вАЭ The singular noun вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО ( Њish, вАЬmanвАЭ) here will be further defined in vv. 13-15 with plural forms (verbs, nouns and suffixes). So the singular functions in a collective sense which is rendered in a plural sense in the translation for the sake of clarification and smoothness.
n2000251: tn Heb вАЬperversities.вАЭ The plural form of вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ф÷∞„§÷Љ÷ї„Ы„Х÷є„™вАО (tahpukhot) may denote a plurality of number (вАЬperverse thingsвАЭ) or intensification: вАЬawful perversity.вАЭ As here, it often refers to perverse speech (Prov 8:13; 10:31, 32; 23:33). It is related to the noun вАП„Ф÷ґ„§÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (hefekh, вАЬthat which is contrary, perverseвАЭ) which refers to what is contrary to morality (Isa 29:16; Ezek 16:34; BDB 246 s.v. вАП„Ф÷ґ„§÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО). The related verb вАП„Ф÷Є„§÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (hafakh, вАЬto turn; to overturnвАЭ) is used (1) literally of turning things over, e.g., tipping over a bowl (2 Kgs 21:13) and turning over bread-cakes (Judg 7:13; Hos 7:8) and (2) figuratively of perverting things so that they are morally upside down, so to speak (Jer 23:36). These people speak what is contrary to morality, wisdom, sense, logic or the truth.
n2000252: tn The articular plural active participle functions as attributive adjective for вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО ( Њish, вАЬmanвАЭ) in v. 12b, indicating that вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО (вАЬmanвАЭ) is collective.
n2000253: tn Heb вАЬpaths of uprightness.вАЭ The noun вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Є„®вАО (yashar, вАЬuprightness; straightnessвАЭ) is an attributive genitive. The moral life is described in Proverbs as the smooth, straight way (2:13; 4:11). The wicked abandon the clear straight path for an evil, crooked, uncertain path.
n2000254: tn Heb вАЬways of darkness.вАЭ Darkness is often metaphorical for sinfulness, ignorance, or oppression. Their way of life lacks spiritual illumination.
n2000255: tn The articular plural active participle functions as the second attributive adjective for вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО ( Њish, вАЬmanвАЭ) in v. 12b.
n2000256: tn The Qal infinitive construct is the complementary use of the form, expressing the direct object of the participle.
n2000258: tn Heb вАЬthe perversities of evil.вАЭ The structure combines a plural noun in construct with a singular adjective. The most typical options for understanding this construction would be вАЬevil perversityвАЭ (plural for abstract concept with attributive genitive) or вАЬthe perversities of an evil man.вАЭ Possibly it could mean вАЬthe perverse aspects of evil.вАЭ
n2000259: tn The noun in this relative clause is an accusative of specification: The evil people are twisted with respect to their paths/conduct.
n2000260: tn Heb вАЬcrooked.вАЭ The adjective вАП„Ґ÷і„І÷Љ÷µ„©„БвАО ( њiqqesh, вАЬcrooked; twistedвАЭ) uses the morphological pattern of adjectives that depict permanent bodily defects, e.g., blindness, lameness. Their actions are morally defective and, apart from repentance, are permanently crooked and twisted.
n2000261: tn The Niphal participle of вАП„Ь„Х÷Љ„ЦвАО (luz, вАЬdevious; crookedвАЭ) describes conduct that is morally deceptive, crafty, and cunning (Isa 30:12).
n2000262: sn The same term, вАП„Ь÷∞„Ф÷Ј„¶÷і÷Љ„Щ„Ь÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО (lehatsilekha, вАЬto deliver youвАЭ), introduces twin purposes in vv. 12 and 16 of deliverance from the evil man and the wicked woman. Four poetic lines elaborate each: vv. 12-15 the evil man, vv. 16-19 the evil woman.
n2000263: tn Heb вАЬstrange womanвАЭ (so KJV, NASB); NRSV вАЬthe loose woman.вАЭ The root вАП„Ц„Х÷Љ„®вАО (zur, вАЬto be a strangerвАЭ) sometimes refers to people who are ethnically foreign to Israel (Isa 1:7; Hos 7:9; 8:7) but it often refers to what is morally estranged from God or his covenant people (Pss 58:4; 78:30; BDB 266 s.v.). Referring to a woman, it means adulteress or prostitute (Prov 2:16; 5:3, 20; 7:5; 22:14; 23:33; see BDB 266 s.v. 2.b). It does not mean that she is a foreigner but that she is estranged from the community with its social and religious values (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 285). It describes her as outside the framework of the covenant community (L. A. Snijders, вАЬThe Meaning of вАП„Ц„Х÷Љ„®вАО in the Old Testament: An Exegetical Study,вАЭ OTS 10 [1954]: 85-86). Here an Israelite woman is in view because her marriage is called a вАЬcovenant with God.вАЭ She is an adulteress, acting outside the legal bounds of the marriage contract.
n2000264: tn Heb вАЬalien woman.вАЭ The adjective вАП„†÷Є„Ы÷∞„®÷і„ЩвАО (nokhri, вАЬforeign; alienвАЭ) may refer to people who are non-Israelite, ethnically foreign, or someone who is unknown or unfamiliar, although an Israelite (see BDB 649 s.v.) It is perhaps used as a technical term in Proverbs for a harlot or promiscuous woman as someone who is morally alienated from God and moral society (Prov 2:16; 5:20; 6:24; 7:5; 23:27; see BDB 649 s.v. 2). Or perhaps the terms characterizing her as a stranger are chosen to underscore the danger of being naively taken in by someone unknown.
n2000265:
tn Heb вАЬhas made smooth.вАЭ The Hiphil of II вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ІвАО (khalaq, вАЬto be smooth; to be slipperyвАЭ) means (1) вАЬto make smoothвАЭ (metal with hammer) and (2) вАЬto use smooth words,вАЭ that is, to flatter (Pss 5:10; 36:3; Prov 2:16; 7:5; 28:23; 29:5; see BDB 325 s.v. 2; HALOT 322 s.v. I вАП„Ч„Ь„ІвАО hif.2). The related Arabic cognate verb means вАЬmake smooth, lie, forge, fabricate.вАЭ The seductive speech of the temptress is compared to olive oil (5:3) and is recounted (7:14-20).
sn As the perfect verb of a dynamic root, the verb reports what she has done not what she is doing (the way the participle in 2:12 describes the men speaking). While it is likely true that she would regularly flatter every man who crossed her path, we are given the picture of the young man carrying on his mind what she has said to him. Part of succumbing to temptation often involves becoming narrowly focused on something potentially pleasurable and blocking out the consequences. Compare Eve in Genesis 3. The man has been flatteredвАФhow will he let that sit in his mind?
n2000266: sn For descriptions of seductive speech, see Prov 5:3 where it is compared to olive oil, and 7:14-20 where such speech is recorded.
n2000267: tn Or вАЬthe husband-abandoner.вАЭ The construction is the active participle of вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ц÷Ј„СвАО ( њazav) with the article, serving as an attributive adjective. The verb means вАЬto forsake; to leave; to abandon.вАЭ Presumably this woman left her husband for good some time ago in the past. Understanding the participle as a label continues to assign the character to her. By comparison God is called the Maker of the earth (Isa 45:18), using the participle вАП„Щ÷є„¶÷µ„®вАО (yotser). The label persists even though creation was in the past.
n2000268: tn Heb вАЬcompanionвАЭ (so NAB, NASB); NIV вАЬpartner.вАЭ The term вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Љ„Х÷Љ„£вАО ( Њalluf, вАЬcompanionвАЭ) is from the root вАП„Р÷Є„Ь÷Ј„£вАО ( Њalaf, вАЬto be familiar with; to cleave toвАЭ) and refers to a womanвАЩs husband (Prov 2:17; Jer 3:4; see BDB 48 s.v. вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Љ„Х÷Љ„£вАО 2). This noun follows the passive adjectival formation and so signifies one who is well-known.
n2000269: tn Heb вАЬof her youth.вАЭ The noun вАП„†÷∞„Ґ„Х÷Љ„®÷ґ„Щ„Ф÷ЄвАО (ne њureha, вАЬher youthвАЭ) functions as a temporal genitive. The plural form is characteristic of nouns that refer to long periods of duration in the various stages of life. The time of вАЬyouthвАЭ encompasses the entire formative period within marriage.
n2000270: tn The verb вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ы÷Ј„ЧвАО (shakhakh) is often translated вАЬforgetвАЭ but can also mean to вАЬignoreвАЭ or вАЬneglect.вАЭ Rather than being unable to remember that she entered into a covenant, she has dismissed its relevance. The form is a Hebrew perfect and the perfect in English captures this well. She made a past decision to ignore the covenant, a condition which continues. The vowel pointing of pausal forms of the Qal perfect of this verb usually has an i-class vowel (tsere), suggesting the root may be stative, which would allow a past or present tense translation, вАЬshe ignores.вАЭ
n2000271: tn Heb вАЬthe covenant.вАЭ This could refer to the Mosaic covenant that prohibits adultery, or more likely, as in the present translation, the marriage covenant (cf. also TEV, CEV). The lexicons list this use of вАЬcovenantвАЭ (вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„®÷і„Щ„™вАО, berit) among other referents to marriage (Prov 2:17; Ezek 16:8; Mal 2:14; BDB 136 s.v. 1.5; HALOT 157 s.v. A.9).
n2000272: tn Heb вАЬcovenant of God.вАЭ The genitive-construct could mean вАЬcovenant made before God.вАЭ The woman and her husband had made a marriage-covenant in which God was invoked as witness. Her sin is against her solemn pledge to her husband, as well as against God.
n2000273: tc The MT reads вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ч÷Є„ФвАО (shakhah) from вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (shuakh) or the biform вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЧвАО (shakhakh): вАЬshe sinks down to death her house.вАЭ However most English versions take вАП„С÷µ÷Љ„Щ„™÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (betah) вАЬher houseвАЭ (masculine singular noun with third person feminine singular suffix) as the subject (e.g., KJV, RSV, NASB, NIV, NRSV, CEV): вАЬher house sinks down to death.вАЭ The LXX reflects вАП„©÷Є„Б„™÷Є„ФвАО (shatah) from вАП„©÷і„Б„Щ„™вАО (shit): вАЬShe has placed her house near death.вАЭ This is a matter of simple orthographic confusion between вАП„ЧвАО (khet) and вАП„™вАО (tav). The MT preserves the more difficult reading, which is often to be preferred. The question is whether the reading is too difficult because the syntax is unworkable. The MT and LXX both read the verb as Qal perfect third person feminine singular. Contextually the subject would be the вАЬloose womanвАЭ of 2:16-17. But the MTвАЩs reading from вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (вАЬto sink downвАЭ) does not expect a direct object, leaving no role for the masculine noun вАЬhouse.вАЭ K&D 16:83 suggests that вАП„С÷µ÷Љ„Щ„™÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (вАЬher houseвАЭ) is a permutative noun that qualifies the subject: вАЬshe together with all that belongs to her [her house] sinks down to deathвАЭ (GKC 425 ¬І131.k). D. Kidner suggests that вАЬher houseвАЭ is in apposition to вАЬdeathвАЭ (e.g., Job 17:13; 30:23; Prov 9:18; Eccl 12:5), meaning that death is her house: вАЬshe sinks down to death, which is her houseвАЭ (Proverbs [TOTC], 62). However the verb also has to operate in the next line where the verb is understood again though the technique of ellipsis and double duty. The parallelism should expect the same role for вАЬher pathsвАЭ as for вАЬher house.вАЭ But this is unworkable for the second half of the line. Further the picture of вАЬsinking downвАЭ in English may be misleading. The Arabic cognate may suggest sinking into the ground, but the Akkadian cognate suggests вАЬcrumblingвАЭ (of a building) or вАЬwasting awayвАЭ (of health). The Hebrew root вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЧвАО (shakhakh) seems to mean вАЬcrouch downвАЭ elsewhere (e.g. Job 38:40; Ps 10:10). The BHS editors attempt to resolve this syntactical problem by suggesting a conjectural emendation of MT вАП„С÷µ÷Љ„Щ„™÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (betah вАЬher houseвАЭ) to the feminine singular noun вАП„†÷∞„™÷і„Щ„С÷Є„™÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (netivatah, вАЬher pathвАЭ) which appears in the plural in 7:25 (though they cite 7:27), to recover a feminine subject for the verb: вАЬher path sinks down to death.вАЭ This would solve the problem of subject-verb agreement, but may not resolve whether this verb can really be modified by the prepositional phrase вАЬto death.вАЭ It also seems problematic to propose a difficult conjectural emendation for the sake of keeping a syntactically difficult text. Most of the versions follow the MT, trying to make the picture of вАЬsinking down to deathвАЭ work. However the LXX reading is simple to explain textually (confusion of two similar looking letters) and restores reasonable syntax, although the preposition вАП„Р÷ґ„ЬвАО ( Њel) is more typical of another verb meaning вАЬto set, to place,вАЭ вАП„©÷і„В„Щ„ЭвАО (sim).
n2000274: tn Heb вАЬto the departed spiritsвАЭ or вАЬto the Rephaim.вАЭ The term вАП„®÷∞„§÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (refa њim, вАЬRephaimвАЭ) refers to spirits of the dead who are inhabitants of Sheol (BDB 952 s.v.; HALOT 1274-75 s.v. I вАП„®÷∞„§÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО). It is used in parallelism with вАП„Ю÷µ„™÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (metim, вАЬthe deadвАЭ) to refer to the departed spirits of the dead in Sheol (Ps 88:11; Isa 26:14). The Rephaim inhabit вАП„Ю÷Є„Х÷ґ„™вАО (mavet, вАЬ[place of] deathвАЭ; Prov 2:18), вАП„©÷∞„Б„Р„Х÷є„ЬвАО (she Њol, вАЬSheolвАЭ; Job 26:5; Prov 9:18; Isa 14:9), вАЬdarkness and the land of forgetfulnessвАЭ (Ps 88:14), and вАЬthe land of the RephaimвАЭ (Isa 26:19). Scholars debate whether вАП„®÷∞„§÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО is derived from the root (1) вАП„®÷Є„§÷Є„РвАО (rafa Њ, вАЬto healвАЭ), meaning вАЬthe healersвАЭ or (2) вАП„®÷Є„§÷Є„ФвАО (rafah, вАЬto be weak; to sink downвАЭ), meaning вАЬthe powerless onesвАЭ or вАЬthose who sink down (to Sheol)вАЭ (BDB 952 s.v.; HALOT 1274-75 s.v.). The related term occurs in Phoenician and Neo-Punic meaning вАЬspirits of the deadвАЭ (DISO 282) and in Ugaritic referring to вАЬspirits of the deadвАЭ who inhabited the underworld and were viewed as healers (UT 2346; WUS 2527). The Hebrew term is often translated вАЬthe shadesвАЭ as a description of the shadowy existence of those who dwelling in Sheol who have lost their vitality (R. F. Schnell, IDB 4:35). Used here in parallelism with вАП„Ю÷Є„Х÷ґ„™вАО (вАЬ[place of] deathвАЭ), вАП„®÷∞„§÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (вАЬthe RephaimвАЭ) probably functions as a synecdoche of inhabitants (= the departed spirits of the dead) for the place inhabited (= Sheol). The point of this line is that those who fall prey to an adulteress will end up among the departed spirits in the realm of the dead. This might mean (1) physical death: he will get himself killed by her zealous husband (e.g., Prov 5:23; 6:32-35; 7:23-27) or (2) spiritual death: he will find himself estranged from the community, isolated from the blessings of God, a moral leper, living a shadowy existence of вАЬdeathвАЭ in the land of no return (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 288).
n2000275: tn Heb вАЬall who go in to her will not return.вАЭ
n2000276: sn The phrase вАЬreach the paths of lifeвАЭ is a figurative expression for experiencing joy and fullness of blessing (BDB 673 s.v. вАП„†÷Є„©„В÷Ј„ТвАО 2.a).
n2000277: tn The conjunction вАП„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЯвАО (lema њan, вАЬso; as a resultвАЭ) introduces the concluding result (BDB 775 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЯвАО 2; HALOT 614 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЯвАО 2.c) of heeding the admonition to attain wisdom (2:1-11) and to avoid the evil men and women and their destructive ways (2:12-19).
n2000278: tn The noun вАЬgoodвАЭ (вАП„Ш„Х÷є„С÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, tovim) does not function as an attributive genitive (вАЬthe good wayвАЭ) because it is a plural noun and the term вАЬwayвАЭ (вАП„У÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО, derekh) is singular. Rather it functions as a genitive of possession identifying the people who walk on this path: вАЬthe way of the good people.вАЭ
n2000279: tn In the light of the parallelism, the noun вАЬrighteousвАЭ (вАП„¶÷Ј„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„І÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, tsaddiqim) functions as a genitive of possession rather than an attributive genitive.
n2000280: tn Heb вАЬthe blamelessвАЭ (so NASB, NIV); NAB вАЬthe honestвАЭ; NRSV вАЬthe innocent.вАЭ The term вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (temimim, вАЬthe blamelessвАЭ) describes those who live with integrity. They are blameless in that they live above reproach according to the requirements of the law.
n2000281: tn Heb вАЬthe guilty.вАЭ The term вАП„®÷∞„©÷Є„Б„Ґ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (resha њim, вАЬthe wickedвАЭ) is from the root вАП„®÷Є„©÷Ј„Б„ҐвАО (rasha њ, вАЬto be guiltyвАЭ) and refers to those who are (1) guilty of sin: moral reprobates or (2) guilty of crime: criminals deserving punishment (BDB 957 s.v. вАП„®÷Є„©÷Є„Б„ҐвАО). This is the person who is probably not a covenant member and manifests that in the way he lives, either by sinning against God or committing criminal acts. The noun sometimes refers to guilty criminals who deserve to die (Num 16:26; 35:31; 2 Sam 4:11). Here they will be вАЬcut offвАЭ and вАЬtorn awayвАЭ from the land.
n2000282: tn Heb вАЬcut off.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„®÷Ј„™вАО (karat, вАЬto be cut offвАЭ) indicates either that the guilty will (1) die prematurely, (2) be excommunicated from the community or (3) be separated eternally in judgment. The Mishnah devoted an entire tractate (m. Keritot) to this topic. The context suggests that the guilty will be вАЬremovedвАЭ from the land where the righteous dwell in security either through death or expulsion.
n2000283: tn The word вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Т÷Ј„УвАО (bagad) means вАЬto act treacherouslyвАЭ (BDB 93 s.v.; HALOT 108 s.v. вАП„С„Т„УвАО). It describes those who deal treacherously, unfaithfully or deceitfully in marriage relations, matters of property or personal rights, in violating covenants, and in their words and general conduct.
n2000284: tn The consonantal form вАП„Щ„°„Ч„ХвАО (yskhv) is vocalized in the MT as вАП„Щ÷і„°÷∞÷Љ„Ч„Х÷ЉвАО (yissekhu, Qal imperfect third person masculine plural from вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ЧвАО, nasakh, вАЬto tear awayвАЭ) but this produces an awkward sense: вАЬthey [= the righteous in vv. 20-21] will tear away the treacherous from itвАЭ (BDB 650 s.v. вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ЧвАО). Due to the parallelism, the BHS editors suggest emending the form to вАП„Щ÷і„†÷Љ÷Є„°÷∞„Ч„Х÷ЉвАО (yinnasekhu, Niphal imperfect third person masculine plural): вАЬthe treacherous will be torn away from it.вАЭ However, Tg. Prov 2:22 points the form as вАП„Щ÷ї„°÷∞„Ч„Х÷ЉвАО (yuskhu) which reflects an old Qal passive vocalizationвАФprobably the best solution to the problem: вАЬthe treacherous will be torn away from it.вАЭ
n200031: sn The chapter begins with an introductory exhortation (1-4), followed by an admonition to be faithful to the Lord (5-12). Wisdom is commended as the most valuable possession (13-18), essential to creation (19-20), and the way to a long and safe life (21-26). There then follows a warning to avoid unneighborliness (27-30) and emulating the wicked (31-35).
n200033: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷і„¶÷Љ÷є„®вАО (yitsor) is a Qal jussive and the noun вАП„Ь÷і„С÷Љ÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (libbekha, вАЬyour heartвАЭ) functions as the subject: вАЬlet your heart keep my commandments.вАЭ
n200034: tn Heb вАЬThey will add to you.вАЭ The verb appears in the second half of the Hebrew line but has been moved to the beginning in the translation for the sake of smoothness.
n200035: tn Heb вАЬlength of days and years of lifeвАЭ (so NASB, NRSV). The idiom вАЬlength of daysвАЭ refers to a prolonged life and вАЬyears of lifeвАЭ signifies a long time full of life, a life worth living (T. T. Perowne, Proverbs, 51). The term вАЬlifeвАЭ refers to вАЬearthly felicity combined with spiritual blessednessвАЭ (BDB 313 s.v. вАП„Ч÷Ј„Щ÷і÷Љ„Щ„ЭвАО).
n200036: tn The noun вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО (shalom, вАЬpeaceвАЭ) here means вАЬwelfare, health, prosperityвАЭ (BDB 1022 s.v. 3). It can be used of physical health and personal well-being. It is the experience of positive blessing and freedom from negative harm and catastrophe.
