n200037: tn The term вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„УвАО (khesed) can mean вАЬmercy, loyal love, covenant love.вАЭ The two words вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„У „Х÷ґ„Р÷±„Ю÷ґ„™вАО (khesed ve Њemet, вАЬmercy and truthвАЭ) are used together over two dozen times. Sometimes they are treated in parallel clauses and sometimes they are paired in a hendiadys, to be understood as вАЬfaithful mercy.вАЭ Here they are pictured as concrete objects to tie around oneвАЩs neck, so at that level they are distinct even though they complement each other.
n200038: sn The picture of tying mercy and truth on the neck (also at 6:21) is similar to tying GodвАЩs commands on the hand or letting them be frontlets for the eyes (Deut 6:8; 11:18). To live by these characteristics is compared to wearing them like clothes, to have these qualities adorn the вАЬwearer.вАЭ The picture of writing them on the heart emphasizes the inward appropriation of the teachings as a complement to their outward manifestation.
n200039: tn The form вАП„Х÷Љ„Ю÷∞„¶÷Є„РвАО (umetsa Њ, вАЬfindвАЭ) is the imperative but it functions as a purpose/result statement. Following a string of imperatives (v. 3), the imperative with a prefixed vav introduces a volitive sequence expressing purpose or result (v. 4).
n2000310: tn The noun вАП„©„В÷µ„Ы÷ґ„ЬвАО (sekhel, вАЬunderstandingвАЭ) does not seem to parallel вАП„Ч÷µ„ЯвАО (khen, вАЬfavorвАЭ). The LXX attaches the first two words to v. 3 and renders v. 4: вАЬand devise excellent things in the sight of the Lord and of men.вАЭ Tg. Prov 3:4 and Syriac Peshitta list all three words separately: вАЬfavor and good and understanding.вАЭ C. H. Toy (Proverbs [ICC], 59) suggests emending the MTвАЩs вАП„©„В÷µ„Ы÷ґ„Ь÷Њ„Ш„Х÷є„СвАО (sekhel tov, вАЬgood understandingвАЭ) to вАП„©„Б÷µ„Э÷Њ„Ш„Х÷є„СвАО (shem tov, вАЬa good nameвАЭ). It is also possible to take the two words as a hendiadys: the favor of good understanding, meaning, a reputation for good understanding.
n2000312: sn The word вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ш÷Ј„ЧвАО (betakh, вАЬtrustвАЭ) is used in the OT in (1) literal physical sense: to physically lean upon something for support and (2) figurative sense: to rely upon someone or something for help or protection (BDB 105 s.v. I вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ш÷Ј„ЧвАО; HALOT 120 s.v. I вАП„С„Ш„ЧвАО). The verb is often used with false securities, people trusting in things that prove to be worthless. But here the object of the secure trust is the Lord who is a reliable object of confidence.
n2000313: sn The вАЬheartвАЭ functions as a metonymy of subject encompassing mind, emotions and will (BDB 524 s.v. вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО 2).
n2000314: tn Heb вАЬdo not lean.вАЭ The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ЯвАО (sha њan, вАЬto lean; to relyвАЭ) is used in (1) literal physical sense of leaning upon something for support and (2) figurative sense of relying upon someone or something for help or protection (BDB 1043 s.v.). Here it functions figuratively (hypocatastasis: implied comparison); relying on oneвАЩs own understanding is compared to leaning on something that is unreliable for support (e.g., Isa 10:20).
n2000315: tn Heb вАЬyour understanding.вАЭ The term вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„†÷Є„ФвАО (binah, вАЬunderstandingвАЭ) is used elsewhere in this book of insight given by God from the instructions in Proverbs (Prov 2:3; 7:4; 8:14; 9:6, 10; 23:23). Here it refers to inherent human understanding that functions in relative ignorance unless supplemented by divine wisdom (Job 28:12-28; 39:26). The reflexive pronoun вАЬownвАЭ is supplied in the translation to clarify this point. It is dangerous for a person to rely upon mere human wisdom (Prov 14:12; 16:25).
n2000316: tn Heb вАЬknow him.вАЭ The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ) includes the meanings вАЬto know (a fact, idea, or person), to learn or realize (to come to know something), to experience (to come to know a circumstance), to acknowledge or care for (to act in a way consistent with a personвАЩs station, whether authority or need). That knowing, or acknowledging, God means to obey him (live in a way consistent with his authority) is clear in negative formulations; those who do not know him do not obey (Exod 5:2; 1 Sam 2:12; Ps 79:6; Jer 4:22). Other passages emphasize knowing his characteristics, and not just his authority (Jer 9:23-24). The sage is calling for a life of trust and obedience in which the disciple sees the Lord in every event, submits to, and trusts him.
n2000317: tn The term вАП„У÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (derekh, вАЬwayвАЭ) is figurative (hypocatastasis: implied comparison) referring to a personвАЩs course of life, actions and undertakings (Prov 2:8; 3:6, 23; 11:5; 20:24; 29:27; 31:3; BDB 203 s.v. 5; cf. TEV вАЬin everything you doвАЭ; NCV, NLT вАЬin all you doвАЭ). This is a call for total commitment in trust for obedience in all things.
n2000318: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„©÷Є„Б„®вАО (yashar) means вАЬto make smooth; to make straightвАЭ (BDB 444 s.v.). This phrase means вАЬto make the way free from obstacles,вАЭ that is, to make it successful (e.g., Isa 40:3). The straight, even road is the right road; God will make the way smooth for the believer.
n2000319: tn Heb вАЬin your own eyesвАЭ (so NAB, NIV, NRSV); NLT вАЬDonвАЩt be impressed with your own wisdom.вАЭ
n2000320: sn The second colon clarifies the first. If one fears the Lord and turns away from evil, then he is depending on the Lord and not wise in his own eyes. There is a higher source of wisdom than human insight.
n2000321: tn Heb вАЬit will be.вАЭ The form is Qal jussive of вАП„Ф÷Є„Щ÷Є„ФвАО (hayah) and is one of the rare uses of the volitive to express purpose or result, even though there is no vav prefixed to it. This indicates that v. 8 is the outcome of v. 7. If a person trusts in the Lord and fears him (vv. 5-7), God will bless him (v. 8).
n2000322: tc Heb вАЬyour navelвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV). MT reads вАП„©„Б÷Є„®÷Љ÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (sharrekha, вАЬyour navelвАЭ) which functions as a synecdoche of part (= navel) for the whole (= body), meaning вАЬyour bodyвАЭ (BDB 1057 s.v. вАП„©„В„®вАО). The geminate noun вАП„©„В÷є„®вАО (sor, вАЬnavel; navel-string [= umbilical cord]вАЭ) occurs only two other times in OT (Ezek 16:4; Song 7:3). The LXX reads ѕДбњЈ ѕГбљљќЉќ±ѕДќѓ ѕГќњѕЕ (t≈Н s≈Нmati sou, вАЬyour bodyвАЭ). So the BHS editors suggest emending MT to the more commonly used terms вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„©„В÷Є„®÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (besarekha, вАЬyour fleshвАЭ) or вАП„©„Б÷∞„Р÷µ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (she Њerekha, вАЬyour bodyвАЭ). But this kind of emendation runs counter to the canons of textual criticism; normally the more difficult reading or rarer term is preferred as original rather than a smooth reading or common term. Since вАЬnavelвАЭ occurs only twice elsewhere, it is difficult to imagine that it would have been confused for these two more common terms and that a scribe would mistakenly write вАЬyour navelвАЭ instead. If MT вАЬyour navelвАЭ is a synecdoche for вАЬyour body,вАЭ the LXX is not pointing to a different textual tradition but is merely interpreting MT accordingly. In similar fashion, the English versions which read вАЬyour bodyвАЭ are not rejecting the MT reading; they are merely interpreting the term as a figure (synecdoche) for вАЬyour body.вАЭ
n2000323: tn Heb вАЬdrink.вАЭ The noun вАП„©„Б÷і„І÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЩвАО (shiqquy, вАЬdrinkвАЭ) is a figure: metonymy of cause (= drink) for the effect (= refreshment); see BDB 1052 s.v. Just as a drink of water would bring physical refreshment to oneвАЩs body, trusting in God and turning away from evil will bring emotional refreshment to oneвАЩs soul.
n2000324: tn Heb вАЬyour bones.вАЭ The term вАП„Ґ÷Ј„¶÷∞„Ю„Х÷є„™÷ґ„Щ„Ъ÷ЄвАО ( њatsmotekha, вАЬyour bonesвАЭ) functions as a synecdoche of part (= bones) for the whole person (= physical and moral aspects); cf. Pss 6:3; 35:10; Prov 3:8; 14:30; 15:30; 16:24; Isa 66:14 and BDB 782 s.v. вАП„Ґ÷ґ„¶÷ґ„ЭвАО 1.d. Scripture often uses the body to describe the inner person (A. R. Johnson, The Vitality of the Individual in the Thought of Ancient Israel, 67-8).
n2000325: tn The imperative вАП„Ы÷Ј÷Љ„С÷µ÷Љ„УвАО (kabbed, вАЬhonorвАЭ) functions as a command, instruction, counsel or exhortation. To honor God means to give him the rightful place of authority by rendering to him gifts of tribute. One way to acknowledge God in oneвАЩs ways (v. 6) is to honor him with oneвАЩs wealth (v. 9).
n2000326: tn Heb вАЬproduce.вАЭ The noun вАП„™÷∞÷Љ„С„Х÷Љ„Р÷Є„ФвАО (tevu Њah) has a two-fold range of meaning: (1) вАЬproduct; yieldвАЭ of the earth (= crops; harvest) and (2) вАЬincome; revenueвАЭ in general (BDB 100 s.v.). The imagery in vv. 9-10 is agricultural; however, all IsraelitesвАФnot just farmersвАФwere expected to give the best portion (= firstfruits) of their income to the Lord.
n2000327:
tn Heb вАЬwith plentyвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NRSV); NIV вАЬto overflowing.вАЭ The noun вАП„©÷Є„В„С÷Є„ҐвАО (sava њ, вАЬplenty; satietyвАЭ) functions as an adverbial accusative of manner or contents: вАЬcompletely.вАЭ
tc The LXX reads вАЬgrain,вАЭ implying вАП„©÷ґ„Б„С÷ґ„®вАО (shever, вАЬgrain) instead of вАП„©÷Є„В„С÷Є„ҐвАО (sava њ, вАЬplentyвАЭ), but the ideas are similar.
n2000328: sn This pictures the process of pressing grapes in which the upper receptacle is filled with grapes and the lower one catches the juice. The harvest of grapes will be so plentiful that the lower vat will overflow with grape juice. The pictures in v. 10 are metonymies of effect for cause (= the great harvest that God will provide when they honor him).
n2000329: tn Heb вАЬburst open.вАЭ The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„•вАО (parats, вАЬto burst openвАЭ) functions as hyperbole here to emphasize the fullness of the wine vats (BDB 829 s.v. 9).
n2000330:
tn The word вАП„™÷Љ÷і„®„Х÷є„©„БвАО (tirosh) appears to be a loanword that refers to unfermented grape juice or sometimes to fresh wine (HALOT 1727-28).
sn Most of the economy of ancient Israel was agricultural. The Lord commanded that Israel give the firstfruits of the land (e.g. Deut 26:1-3) and promised to bless Israel with the produce of the land when she would obey God (e.g. Deut 28:1-13).
n2000331: tn Heb вАЬthe discipline of the Lord.вАЭ Or вАЬinstruction,вАЭ see the note at 1:2.
n2000332: tn The verb вАП„І„Х÷Љ„•вАО (quts) has a two-fold range of meaning: (1) вАЬto feel a loathing; to abhorвАЭ and (2) вАЬto feel a sickening dreadвАЭ (BDB 880 s.v.). The parallelism with вАЬdo not despiseвАЭ suggests the former nuance here. The common response to suffering is to loathe it; however, the righteous understand that it refines oneвАЩs moral character and that it is a means to the blessing.
n2000333: tn Heb вАЬchastens.вАЭ The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЧвАО (yakhakh) here means вАЬto chasten; to punishвАЭ (HALOT 410 s.v. вАП„Щ„Ы„ЧвАО 1) or вАЬto correct; to rebukeвАЭ (BDB 407 s.v. 6). The context suggests some kind of corporeal discipline rather than mere verbal rebuke or cognitive correction. This verse is quoted in Heb 12:5-6 to show that suffering in the service of the Lord is a sign of membership in the covenant community (i.e., sonship).
n2000334: tc MT reads вАП„Х÷Љ„Ы÷∞„Р÷Є„СвАО (ukhe Њav, вАЬand like a fatherвАЭ) but the LXX reflects the Hiphil verb вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷Ј„Ы÷∞„Р÷і„СвАО (veyakh Њiv, вАЬand scourges every son he receivesвАЭ). Both readings fit the parallelism; however, it is unnecessary to emend MT which makes perfectly good sense. The fact that the writer of Hebrews quotes this passage from the LXX and it became part of the inspired NT text does not mean that the LXX reflects the original Hebrew reading here.
n2000335: tn The verb вАЬdisciplinesвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the parallelism; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2000336: tn Although the word вАП„Р÷Ј„©÷∞„Б„®÷µ„ЩвАО ( Њashre, вАЬblessedвАЭ) is frequently translated вАЬhappyвАЭ here (so KJV, ASV, NAB, NCV, NRSV, TEV, NLT), such a translation can be somewhat misleading. The core meaning of the word conveys вАЬbenefitвАЭ or вАЬadvantage,вАЭ sometimes meaning security. Though feeling glad about the benefit is often in view, вАЬhappyвАЭ is too narrow a translation. For example, Job 5:17 says the one whom God corrects is вАП„Р÷Ј„©÷∞„Б„®÷µ„ЩвАО. Clearly the correction is a benefit; less clearly does it prompt inner happiness. On the other hand вАЬblessedвАЭ should not be confused with вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (barakh) вАЬto bless,вАЭ used in pronouncing blessings.
n2000337: tn Heb вАЬthe manвАЭ (also again in the following line).
n2000338: tn The verb вАП„Ю÷Є„¶÷Є„РвАО (matsa Њ) is a dynamic root in the perfect conjugation, best represented here by the English perfect вАЬhas found.вАЭ The formula with вАП„Р÷Ј„©÷∞„Б„®÷µ„ЩвАО ( Њashre, вАЬblessedвАЭ) most commonly uses the imperfect or participle for future or present activities. The perfect identifies an action in the past with continuing results. The present focus of the continuing results is sufficient to be in parallel with the following imperfect verb, which may be understood as present or future. The blessings, or benefits, that accrue to someone who has found wisdom are identified in the following verses.
n2000339: tn The precise meaning of the word вАП„§÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ІвАО (puq) is unclear. It occurs only 7 times in the Bible and the meanings of cognates in other languages are disputed. It involves вАЬobtainingвАЭ as is clear from its parallelism with вАП„Ю÷Є„¶÷Є„РвАО (matsa Њ; вАЬto findвАЭ) here and elsewhere. But it is not clear whether it considers the process of obtaining or just the final achievement of obtaining (compare getting a wife in Prov 18:22). The lexical meaning affects oneвАЩs understanding of the imperfect form of the verb. If its lexical meaning focuses on the achievement, then it should probably be understood as future. This would work with the perfect verb in the first line to include those who will yet pursue and obtain understanding in receiving the benefits of wisdom. It could also be understood as a general present. If the lexical meaning includes the process of obtaining, that opens the possibilities of using the imperfect tense for progressive or habitual meaning.
n2000340: tn Heb вАЬher profit.вАЭ The third person feminine singular suffix on the noun is probably a genitive of source: вАЬfrom her.вАЭ
n2000341: tn Heb вАЬprofit.вАЭ The noun вАП„°÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (sakhar, вАЬtrading profitвАЭ) often refers to the financial profit of traveling merchants (Isa 23:3, 18; 45:14; HALOT 750 s.v.). The related participle describes a traveling вАЬtrader, dealer, wholesaler, merchantвАЭ (e.g., Gen 37:28; Prov 31:14; Isa 23:2; Ezek 27:36; HALOT 750 s.v. вАП„°„Ч„®вАО qal.2). Here the noun is used figuratively to describe the moral benefit of wisdom.
n2000342: tn The noun вАП„°÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (вАЬprofitвАЭ) is repeated in this line for emphasis. The two usages draw upon slightly different nuances, creating a polysemantic wordplay. The moral вАЬbenefitвАЭ of wisdom is more вАЬprofitableвАЭ than silver.
n2000343: tn Heb вАЬher yield.вАЭ The third person feminine singular suffix on the noun is probably a genitive of source: вАЬfrom her.вАЭ
n2000344: tn Heb вАЬyield.вАЭ The noun вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„С„Х÷Љ„Р÷Є„ФвАО (tevu Њah, вАЬproduct; yieldвАЭ) is normally used of crops and harvests (BDB 100 s.v. 1). Here it is figurative for the moral benefit of wisdom (BDB 100 s.v. 2.b).
n2000345: tn The phrase вАЬis betterвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the parallelism; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of smoothness.
n2000346: tn The imperfect verb from вАП„Щ÷Є„°÷Ј„УвАО (yasad, вАЬto establish be like; to resembleвАЭ) has a modal nuance here: вАЬcan [not] compare with.вАЭ
n2000347: tn Heb вАЬAll of your delights cannot compare with her.вАЭ
n2000349: tn Heb вАЬher ways are ways of pleasantnessвАЭ (so KJV, NRSV). The present translation contracts this expression for the sake of smoothness. The plural of вАП„У÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (derekh, вАЬwayвАЭ) is repeated for emphasis. The noun вАП„†÷є„Ґ÷Ј„ЭвАО (no њam, вАЬpleasantnessвАЭ) functions as an attributive genitive: вАЬpleasant ways.вАЭ
n2000350: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor.
n2000351: sn The metaphor compares wisdom to the symbol of vitality and fullness of life. This might be an allusion to Gen 3:22, suggesting that what was lost as a result of the Fall may be recovered through wisdom: long and beneficial life (R. Marcus, вАЬThe Tree of Life in Proverbs,вАЭ JBL 62 [1943]: 117-20).
n2000352: tn The singular participle вАП„Ю÷∞„Р÷ї„©÷Є÷Љ„Б„®вАО (me Њushar, literally, вАЬhe will be blessedвАЭ) functions as a distributive singular for a plural subject (GKC 464 ¬І145.l): вАЬeach and everyone will be blessed.вАЭ Not recognizing this point of syntax, the BHS editors unnecessarily suggest emending this singular form to the plural.
n2000353: tn Heb вАЬfounded the earth.вАЭ The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„°÷Ј„УвАО (yasad, вАЬto establish; to foundвАЭ) describes laying the foundation of a building (1 Kgs 5:31 HT [5:17 ET]; 7:10; 2 Chr 3:3; Ezra 3:10-12; Zech 4:9) and God laying the foundation of the earth (Job 38:4; Pss 24:2; 89:12; 102:26; 104:5; Isa 48:13; 51:13, 16; Zech 12:1).
n2000354: sn The theme of GodвАЩs use of wisdom in creation is developed in Prov 8:22-31. Because God established the world to operate according to the principle of wisdom it is impossible for anyone to live successfully in his world apart from the wisdom that only God can give.
n2000355: sn The word вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ф„Х÷є„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО (tehomot, вАЬprimordial seaвАЭ) alludes to the chaotic вАЬdeepвАЭ in Gen 1:2 (BDB 1063 s.v. вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ф„Х÷є„ЭвАО 3). This was viewed in the ancient world as a force to be reckoned with. However, God not only formed it but controls it (see J. Emerton, вАЬSpring and Torrent in Ps 74:15, вАЭ VT 15 [1965]: 125).
n2000356: sn This might refer to GodвАЩs action of dividing the waters to form the dry ground on the third day (Gen 1:9-10) or, less likely, to the breaking up of the fountains of the deep at the flood (Gen 7:11).
n2000357:
tn The verb is a prefixed form and follows three perfect verbs describing past time events. The form may be understood as an archaic preterite (which normally begins with a waw consecutive). In that case it is simple past time. Or it may be taken as an imperfect to show result, вАЬso that the clouds drip down dew.вАЭ
sn The two colons form a merism: The wisdom of God is behind all forces of nature, whether the violent breaking forth of its watery forces at creation or the provision of the gentle rain and dew throughout history (T. T. Perowne, Proverbs, 55).
n2000358: tn The object of the verb вАЬescapeвАЭ is either (1) wisdom, knowledge, and understanding in vv. 13-20 or (2) вАЬwisdom and discretionвАЭ in the second colon of this verse. Several English versions transpose the terms вАЬwisdom and discretionвАЭ from the second colon into the first colon for the sake of clarity and smoothness (e.g., RSV, NRSV, NIV, TEV, CEV). NIV takes the subject from the second colon and reverses the clauses to clarify that.
n2000360: tn The noun вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©÷ґ„Б„Ъ÷ЄвАО (nafshekha, вАЬyour soulвАЭ) is a synecdoche of part (= inner soul) for the whole person (= you); see BDB 600 s.v. 4.a.2.
n2000362: sn Heb вАЬYou will not strike your foot.вАЭ What the foot strikes (cf. Ps 91:12 вАЬagainst a stoneвАЭ) is omitted but something is implied. This is a figure (hypocatastasis) comparing stumbling on a stone in the path to making serious mistakes in life that bring harm.
n2000363: tn The particle вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim, вАЬifвАЭ) here functions in its rare temporal sense (вАЬwhenвАЭ) followed by an imperfect tense (e.g., Num 36:4; BDB 50 s.v. 1.b.4.b).
n2000364: tc The LXX reads вАЬsit down,вАЭ presumably from вАП„™÷Љ÷µ„©„Б÷µ„СвАО (teshev) while the MT reads вАП„™÷і÷Љ„©÷∞„Б„Ы÷Ј÷Љ„СвАО (tishkav, вАЬlie downвАЭ). Either the LXX translator worked from a text which had lost the вАП„ЫвАО (kaf) or the MT copyist had a damaged text and restored a verb from the root вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ы÷Ј„СвАО (shakav, вАЬto lie downвАЭ) based on the following verb. The text restored from the LXX would present a progression from walking (v. 23), to sitting, to lying down: вАЬWhen you sit down, you will not fear, then you will lie down and your sleep will be pleasant.вАЭ
n2000365: tn Heb вАЬwill not have dread.вАЭ The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„УвАО (pakhad, вАЬtremble, shake with fearвАЭ) describes emotion that is stronger than mere fearвАФit is dread.
n2000366: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷∞„С÷Є„ФвАО ( њarevah) is from III вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„СвАО ( њarav, вАЬto be sweet; to be pleasing; to be pleasantвАЭ; BDB 787 s.v. III вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„СвАО). It should not be confused with the other five homonymic roots that are also spelled вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„СвАО (see BDB 786-88).
n2000367: sn The negative exhortation with the jussive verb вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„™÷і÷Љ„Щ„®÷Є„РвАО ( Њal tira Њ, вАЬdo not be afraidвАЭ) is based in part on the assurances given in vv. 23-24 but is directly tied to v. 26.
n2000368: tn Heb вАЬterror of suddenness.вАЭ The noun вАП„§÷і÷Љ„™÷∞„Р÷є„ЭвАО (pit Њom, вАЬsuddenвАЭ) functions as an attributive genitive: вАЬsudden terrorвАЭ (e.g., Job 22:10; BDB 837 s.v.).
n2000369: tn Heb вАЬterror.вАЭ The noun вАП„§÷Љ÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„УвАО (pakhad, вАЬterrorвАЭ) is a metonymy of effect for cause (= disaster); see BDB 808 s.v. 2. This is suggested by the parallelism with the noun вАП„Ю÷і„©÷є÷Љ„Б„Р÷Ј„™вАО (misho Њat, вАЬdestructionвАЭ) in the following colon. The term вАП„§÷Ј÷Љ„Ч÷Ј„УвАО (вАЬterrorвАЭ) often refers to the object (or cause) of terror (e.g., Job 3:25; 15:21; 22:10; 31:23; Pss 31:12; 36:2; Isa 24:18; Jer 48:44).
n2000370: tn Heb вАЬor the destruction of the wicked when it comes.вАЭ The noun вАП„®÷∞„©÷Є„Б„Ґ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (resha њim, вАЬwicked onesвАЭ) probably functions as an objective genitive (the destruction that comes on the wicked) or a genitive of source (the destruction that the wicked bring on others).
n2000371: tn Heb вАЬyour confidenceвАЭ (so NAB, NIV, NRSV) or вАЬat your side.вАЭ BDB (492) lists both meanings under one entry for вАП„Ы÷ґ÷Љ„°÷ґ„ЬвАО (kesel). HALOT (489) sees two homonyms, I вАП„Ы÷ґ÷Љ„°÷ґ„ЬвАО (вАЬloin, sideвАЭ) and II вАП„Ы÷ґ÷Љ„°÷ґ„ЬвАО (вАЬconfidenceвАЭ). The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) either functions locatively meaning вАЬat your side,вАЭ or as a bet essentiae (GKC 379 ¬І119.i, BDB 88 s.v. 7 вАП„С÷∞÷ЉвАО; HALOT 104 s.v. 3 вАП„С÷∞÷ЉвАО), emphasizing the quality or nature of the noun (but which typically cannot be rendered in English) meaning here вАЬyour confidence.вАЭ It may then be viewed as a metonymy standing either for the object or the source of your confidence. The Vulgate reads вАЬat your side (latus).вАЭ The LXX appears to have read вАП„Ы÷є÷Љ„Ь „Ю÷∞„°÷і„Ь÷є÷Љ„™÷ґ„Щ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (kol mesillotekha, вАЬ[over] all your ways.вАЭ
n2000372: tn The term вАЬfootвАЭ functions as a synecdoche, where the part stands for the whole (вАЬyour footвАЭ stands for вАЬyouвАЭ). This device helps build a comparison between a hunterвАЩs snare and calamity that afflicts the wicked.
n2000373: tn Heb вАЬfrom capture,вАЭ a figure for the calamity of v. 25.
n2000374: tn The MT has вАЬfrom its possessorsвАЭ and the LXX simply has вАЬfrom the poor.вАЭ C. H. Toy (Proverbs [ICC], 77) suggests emending the text to read вАЬneighborsвАЭ (changing вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Є„Щ„ХвАО [be њalayv] to вАП„®÷µ„Ґ÷ґ„Щ„Ъ÷ЄвАО, re њekha) but that is gratuitous. The idea can be explained as being those who need to possess it, or as BDB 127 s.v. вАП„С÷Ј÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО has it with an objective genitive, вАЬthe owner of itвАЭ = the one to whom it is due.
n2000375:
tc The Kethib is the dual form вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷ґ„Щ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (yadekha, вАЬyour handsвАЭ); the Qere is the singular вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО (yadekha, вАЬyour handвАЭ). Normally the Qere is preferred because it represents an alternate textual tradition that the Masoretes viewed as superior to the received text.
tn Heb вАЬyour hand.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО (yadekha, вАЬyour handвАЭ) functions as a synecdoche of part (= your hand) for the whole person (= you).
n2000376: tn Heb вАЬit is to the power of your hand.вАЭ This expression is idiomatic for вАЬit is in your powerвАЭ or вАЬyou have the abilityвАЭ (Gen 31:29; Deut 28:23; Neh 5:5; Mic 2:1). The noun вАП„Р÷µ„ЬвАО ( Њel) means вАЬpowerвАЭ (BDB 43 s.v. 7), and вАП„Щ÷Є„УвАО (yad, вАЬhandвАЭ) is used figuratively to denote вАЬabilityвАЭ (BDB 390 s.v. 2). Several translations render this as вАЬwhen it is in your power to do itвАЭ (KJV, RSV, NRSV, NASB) or вАЬwhen it is in your power to actвАЭ (NIV). W. McKane suggests, вАЬwhen it is in your power to confer itвАЭ (Proverbs [OTL], 215).
n2000378: tn Heb вАЬBut there it is with you.вАЭ The circumstantial clause introduces a condition which true at the same time as as an earlier clause. The words вАЬat the timeвАЭ were for clarity.
n2000379: sn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Ј„©„БвАО (kharash) means вАЬto cut in; to engrave; to plough; to devise.вАЭ The idea of plotting is metaphorical for working, practicing or fabricating (BDB 360 s.v.).
n2000380: tn Heb вАЬAnd he dwells trustingly (or securely) with you.вАЭ The vav (вАП„ХвАО) prefixed to the pronoun introduces a circumstantial clause which further defines the neighbor.
n2000381: sn The term вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv) can mean вАЬquarrelвАЭ or вАЬlegal accusationвАЭ (BDB 936 s.v.). Both ideas would work but the more technical legal accusation fits the context better. This is a warning to not bring legal accusations against anyone without a legitimate reason.
n2000383: tn Heb вАЬgratuitouslyвАЭ; NIV, TEV вАЬfor no (+ good NCV) reason.вАЭ The adverb вАП„Ч÷і„†÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (khinam) means вАЬwithout cause, undeservedly,вАЭ especially of groundless hostility (HALOT 334 s.v. 3; BDB 336 s.v. c).
n2000384: tn Heb вАЬa man of violence.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Є„°вАО (khamas, вАЬviolenceвАЭ) functions as an attributive genitive. The word itself means вАЬviolence, wrongвАЭ (HALOT 329 s.v.) and refers to physical violence, social injustice, harsh treatment, wild ruthlessness, injurious words, hatred, and general rudeness (BDB 329 s.v.).
n2000385: tn The basic meaning of the verb вАП„Ь„Х÷Љ„ЦвАО (luz) is вАЬto turn aside; to departвАЭ (BDB 531 s.v.). The Niphal stem is always used figuratively of moral apostasy from the path of righteousness: (1) вАЬto go astrayвАЭ (Prov 2:15; 3:32; 14:2) and (2) вАЬcrookednessвАЭ in action (Isa 30:12; see HALOT 522 s.v. вАП„Ь„Х„ЦвАО nif; BDB 531 s.v. Niph).
n2000386: tn Heb вАЬabomination of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) functions as a genitive of respect: вАЬabomination to the Lord.вАЭ It is loathsome or detestable to him. Things that are repugnant to the Lord are usually the most heinous of crimes and gross violations of rituals.
n2000387: tn Heb вАЬbut with the upright is his intimate counsel.вАЭ The phrase вАЬhe revealsвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for the sake of smoothness and clarity.
n2000388: tn Heb вАЬhis counsel.вАЭ The noun вАП„°„Х÷є„УвАО (sod) can refer to (1) вАЬintimate circleвАЭ of friends and confidants, (2) вАЬconfidential discussionвАЭ among friends and confidants, or вАЬsecret counselвАЭ revealed from one confidant to another and kept secret and (3) relationship of вАЬintimacyвАЭ with a person (BDB 691 s.v.; HALOT 745 s.v.). God reveals his secret counsel to the heavenly assembly (Job 15:8; Jer 23:18, 22) and his prophets (Amos 3:7). God has brought the angels into his вАЬintimate circleвАЭ (Ps 89:8). Likewise, those who fear the Lord enjoy an intimate relationship with him (Job 29:4; Ps 25:14; Prov 3:32). The perverse are repugnant to the Lord, but he takes the upright into his confidence and brings him into his intimate circle.
n2000389: tn Or вАЬthe curse of the Lord,вАЭ a subjective genitive indicating the curse that the Lord makes. The noun вАП„Ю÷∞„Р÷µ„®÷Ј„ФвАО (me Њerah, вАЬcurseвАЭ) is the opposite, or antonym, of вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„®÷Є„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (berakhah, вАЬblessingвАЭ). It is associated with poverty and affliction. The curse of God brings ruin and failure to crops, land in general, an individual, or the nation (Deut 28:20; Mal 2:2; 3:9; see BDB 76 s.v.; HALOT 541 s.v.).
n2000390: tn Heb вАЬhouse.вАЭ The term вАП„С÷µ÷Љ„Щ„™вАО (bet, вАЬhouseвАЭ) functions as a synecdoche of container (= house) for the persons contained (= household). See, e.g., Exod 1:21; Deut 6:22; Josh 22:15 (BDB 109 s.v. 5.a).
n2000391: sn The term вАЬwickedвАЭ is singular; the term вАЬrighteousвАЭ in the second half of the verse is plural. In scripture such changes often hint at GodвАЩs reluctance to curse, but eagerness to bless (e.g., Gen 12:3).
n2000392: sn The term вАЬblessвАЭ (вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО, barakh) is the antithesis of вАЬcurse.вАЭ A blessing is a gift, enrichment, or endowment. The blessing of God empowers one with the ability to succeed, and brings vitality and prosperity in the material realm, but especially in oneвАЩs spiritual relationship with God.
n2000393: tn Heb вАЬhabitation.вАЭ The noun вАП„†÷Є„Х÷ґ„ФвАО (naveh, вАЬhabitation; abodeвАЭ), which is the poetic parallel to вАП„С÷µ÷Љ„Щ„™вАО (bet, вАЬhouseвАЭ), usually refers to the abode of a shepherd in the country: вАЬhabitationвАЭ in the country (BDB 627 s.v. вАП„†÷Є„Х÷ґ„ФвАО). It functions as a synecdoche of container (= habitation) for the contents (= people in the habitation and all they possess).
n2000394: tn The Hebrew is structured chiastically (AB:BA): вАЬThe curse of the Lord / is on the house of the wicked // but the home of the righteous / he blesses.вАЭ The word order in the translation is reversed for the sake of smoothness and readability.
n2000395: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim, вАЬifвАЭ) and the syntax is вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim) plus imperfect verb followed by vav plus perfect consecutive. This particle can introduce a realizable or unrealizable condition, or a concessive clause (HALOT 60-61 s.v.). A realizable condition presents the circumstance in which the apodosis is realized, вАЬif/when he is scornfulвА¶, then he will show favor.вАЭ An unrealizable condition or a concessive clause should be rendered вАЬeven ifвАЭ or вАЬalthough [X would be],вАЭ referring to something that is not the case, as in, вАЬeven if he would be scornfulвА¶, thenвА¶вАЭ (cf. Num 22:18, 1 Kgs 13:8; Job 9:15; Jer 15:1). Neither of these options fit the context well. The content of the second half of the verse does not depend on the first half. And the first half is not to be understood as an unrealizable or unexpected condition, rather both are truisms. An alternative is to read the similarly sounding term вАП„Ґ÷і„ЭвАО ( њim, вАЬwithвАЭ), вАЬwith the scorners he is scornfulвАЭ (cf. Ps 18:25-26). The LXX does not have a conditional particle, so it may not have read вАП„Р÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim, вАЬifвАЭ), but also it does not have ќЉќµѕДбљ∞ (meta, вАЬwithвАЭ) so it is not clear that it read вАП„Ґ÷і„ЭвАО ( Њim, вАЬwithвАЭ). The translation presumes the particle вАП„Ґ÷і„ЭвАО.
n2000396: tn Heb вАЬwith those who mock he will mock.вАЭ The repetition of the root вАП„Ь÷і„Щ„•вАО (lits, вАЬto scorn; to mockвАЭ) connotes poetic justice; the punishment fits the crime. Scoffers are characterized by arrogant pride (e.g., Prov 21:24), as the antithetical parallelism with вАЬthe humbleвАЭ here emphasizes.
n2000397: tc The Kethib reads вАП„Х÷∞„Ь÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„†÷і„Щ÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (vela њaniyyim) from вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷і„ЩвАО ( њani, вАЬafflictedвАЭ), followed by the NASB. The Qere, supported by the LXX, Targum, and Latin Vulgate reads вАП„Х÷∞„Ь÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„†÷Є„Х÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (vela њanavim) from вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷Є„ХвАО ( њanav, вАЬmeek, humbleвАЭ) followed by the NIV, ESV, NRSV and others.
n2000398: tc MT reads вАП„Ю÷µ„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (merim, вАЬhe lifts upвАЭ): singular Hiphil participle of вАП„®„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО (rum, вАЬto rise; to exaltвАЭ), functioning verbally with the Lord as the implied subject: вАЬbut he lifts up fools to shame.вАЭ The LXX and Vulgate reflect the plural вАП„Ю÷∞„®÷і„Щ„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (merimim, вАЬthey exaltвАЭ) with вАЬfoolsвАЭ (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„°÷і„Щ„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, kesilim) as the explicit subject: вАЬbut fools exalt shame.вАЭ The textual variant was caused by haplography or dittography of вАП„Щ„ЭвАО (yod and mem), depending on whether MT or the alternate tradition is original.
n2000399: tn The noun вАП„І÷Є„Ь„Х÷є„ЯвАО (qalon, вАЬignominy; dishonor; contemptвАЭ) is from вАП„І÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (qalah) which is an alternate form of вАП„І÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (qalal) which means (1) вАЬto treat something lightly,вАЭ (2) вАЬto treat with contempt [or, with little esteem]вАЭ or (3) вАЬto curse.вАЭ The noun refers to personal disgrace or shame. While the wise will inherit honor, fools will be made a public display of dishonor. God lets fools entangle themselves in their folly in a way for all to see.
n200041: sn The chapter includes an exhortation to acquire wisdom (1-4a), a list of the benefits of wisdom (4b-9), a call to pursue a righteous lifestyle (10-13), a warning against a wicked lifestyle (14-19), and an exhortation to righteousness (20-27).
n200044: tn Heb вАЬin order to come to know.вАЭ As a stative verb, вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ) can mean вАЬto knowвАЭ or вАЬto come to know,вАЭ the latter essentially meaning вАЬto learn.вАЭ The infinitive indicates the purpose of the earlier imperatives.
n200045: tn The perfect tense has the nuance of instantaneous (or performative) perfect; the sage is now calling the disciples to listen. It could also be a perfect of resolve, indicating what he is determined to do, or a present perfect reflecting his proven past history, вАЬIndeed I have given you X.вАЭ
n200046: tn Or вАЬa boy with my father.вАЭ
n200047:
tc The LXX introduces the ideas of вАЬobedientвАЭ and вАЬbelovedвАЭ for these two terms. This seems to be a free rendering, if not a translation of a different Hebrew textual tradition. The MT makes good sense and requires no emendation.
tn Heb вАЬtender and only one.вАЭ The phrase вАП„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞ „Х÷∞„Щ÷Є„Ч÷і„Щ„УвАО (rakh veyakhid, вАЬtender and only oneвАЭ) is a hendiadys meaning вАЬtender only child.вАЭ The adjective вАП„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (rakh) means вАЬtender; delicateвАЭ (BDB 940 s.v. вАП„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО), and describes a lad who is young and undeveloped in character (e.g., 2 Sam 3:39). The adjective вАП„Щ÷Є„Ч÷і„Щ„УвАО (yakhid) means вАЬonly oneвАЭ (BDB 402 s.v. вАП„Щ÷Є„Ч÷і„Щ„УвАО) and refers to a beloved and prized only child (e.g., Gen 22:2).
n200048: tn The vav plus imperative вАЬand liveвАЭ expresses purpose, as is common with volitional verbs in a series.
n200049:
tn Heb вАЬfrom the words of my mouthвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NRSV); TEV, CEV вАЬwhat I say.вАЭ
sn The verse repeats the imperative вАЬacquireвАЭ to underscore the importance of getting wisdom.
n2000410: tn Heb вАЬher.вАЭ The referent of the pronoun is personified вАЬwisdom,вАЭ which has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2000411: tn The absolute and construct state of вАП„®÷µ„Р„©„Б÷і„Щ„™вАО (re Њshit) are identical (BDB 912 s.v.). Some treat вАП„®÷µ„Р„©„Б÷і„Щ„™ „Ч÷Є„Ы÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (re Њshit khokhmah) as a genitive-construct phrase: вАЬthe beginning of wisdomвАЭ (cf. NAB, NASB, NRSV). Others take вАП„®÷µ„Р„©„Б÷і„Щ„™вАО as an absolute functioning as predicate and вАП„Ч÷Є„Ы÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО as the subject: вАЬwisdom is the first/chief thingвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV). The context here suggests the predicate.
n2000412: tn The term вАЬsoвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is supplied in the translation for the sake of smoothness and style.
n2000413: tn The noun вАП„І÷і„†÷∞„Щ÷Є„ЯвАО (qinyan) means вАЬthing got or acquired; acquisitionвАЭ (BDB 889 s.v.). With the preposition that denotes price, it means вАЬwith (or at the price of) all that you have acquired.вАЭ The point is that no price is too high for wisdomвАФgive everything for it (K&D 16:108).
n2000414: tc The verse is not in the LXX; some textual critics delete the verse as an impossible gloss that interrupts vv. 6 and 8 (e.g., C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 88).
n2000415: tn The verb is the Pilpel imperative from вАП„°÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (salal). In its ten Qal uses, it means to pile up, usually in building a road. The two uses in the doubling stems (here and Exod 9:17) are resultative or plurative, meaning вАЬto raise up, to exalt.вАЭ To grant her a high position implies esteeming her.
n2000416: sn The personification of wisdom continues with the bestowal of a wreath for the head (e.g., 1:9). The point is that grace will be given to the individual like a wreath about the head.
n2000417: sn This verse uses wedding imagery: The wife (wisdom) who is embraced by her husband (the disciple) will place the wedding crown on the head of her new bridegroom. Wisdom, like a virtuous wife, will crown the individual with honor and grace. In vv. 4-9 Murphy points out the four fold repetition of вАЬacquireвАЭ (вАП„І÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО, qanah), the same term used of Boaz taking Ruth as a wife (Ruth 4:8, 10), and the calls to love her (Prov 4:6) and embrace her (4:8) (R. Murphy, Proverbs [WBC], 27). This section personifies wisdom and portrays the pursuit of wisdom as a paramount romantic pursuit.
n2000419: tn Following an imperative, a vav plus imperfect verb can depict purpose or result.
n2000420: tn Heb вАЬand the years of life will be many for you.вАЭ
n2000421:
tn The form вАП„Ф÷є„®÷µ„™÷і„Щ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (horetikha) is the Hiphil perfect with a suffix from the root вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (yarah, вАЬto guideвАЭ). This and the parallel verb should be taken as instantaneous (or performative) perfects, translated as an English present tense: The sage is now instructing or pointing the way.
sn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (yarah) means вАЬto teach; to instruct; to guide.вАЭ This is from the same root as the Hebrew word for вАЬlawвАЭ (torah). See G. R. Driver, вАЬHebrew Notes,вАЭ VT 1 (1951): 241-50; and J. L. Crenshaw, вАЬThe Acquisition of Knowledge in Israelite Wisdom Literature,вАЭ WW 7 (1986): 9.
n2000422: tn Heb вАЬin the tracks of uprightnessвАЭ; cf. NAB вАЬon straightforward paths.вАЭ Both the verb and the object of the preposition make use of the idiomвАФthe verb is the Hiphil perfect from вАП„У÷Љ÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (derekh, related to вАЬroad; wayвАЭ) and the object is вАЬwagon tracks, paths.вАЭ
n2000423: sn The noun вАП„¶÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„У÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (tsa њadekha, вАЬyour stepsвАЭ) and the temporal infinitive вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Ь÷ґ„Ы÷∞„™÷Љ÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО (belekhtekha, вАЬwhen you walkвАЭ) use the idiom of walking to represent the course of life. On that course there will be no obstacles; the вАЬpathвАЭ will be straightвАФmorally and practically.
n2000424: sn The verb вАП„¶÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО (tsarar, вАЬto be narrow; to be constrictedвАЭ) refers to that which is narrow or constricted, signifying distress, trouble, adversity; that which was wide-open or broad represents freedom and deliverance.
n2000425: sn The progression from walking to running is an idiom called вАЬanabasis,вАЭ suggesting that as greater and swifter progress is made, there will be nothing to impede the progress (e.g., Isa 40:31).
n2000427: tn The form вАП„†÷і„¶÷∞÷Љ„®÷ґ„Ф÷ЄвАО (nitsereha, from вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„®вАО, natsar) has an anomalous doubled letter (see GKC 73 ¬І20.h).
n2000428: tn The verb вАП„Р÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„®вАО ( Њashar, вАЬto walkвАЭ) is not to be confused with the identically spelled homonym вАП„Р÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„®вАО вАЬto pronounce happyвАЭ as in BDB 80 s.v. вАП„Р÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„®вАО.
n2000429: sn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„С÷Ј„®вАО ( њavar, вАЬto cross over; to travel throughвАЭ) ends both cola. In the first it warns against going on wrong paths; in the second it means вАЬto go your own way,вАЭ but may hint that the way will cross over the wrong way. The rapid sequence of commands stresses the urgency of the matter.
n2000430: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„ЯвАО (yashan) вАЬto be asleepвАЭ is a stative root. In the imperfect it can be future вАЬwill not sleepвАЭ or modal, in this case abilitive, вАЬare not able to sleep.вАЭ
n2000431: sn The verb is вАП„®÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ҐвАО (ra њa њ), which means вАЬto do evil; to harm.вАЭ The verse is using the figure of hyperbole to stress the preoccupation of some people with causing trouble. R. L. Alden says, вАЬHow sick to find peace only at the price of another manвАЩs misfortuneвАЭ (Proverbs, 47).
n2000432: sn Heb вАЬtheir sleep is robbed/seizedвАЭ; these expressions are metonymical for their restlessness in plotting evil.
n2000433: sn The Hiphil imperfect (Kethib) means вАЬcause to stumble.вАЭ This idiom (from hypocatastasis) means вАЬbring injury/ruin to someoneвАЭ (BDB 505-6 s.v. вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„ЬвАО Hiph.1).
n2000434: tn The verb вАПвАП„Ь÷Є„Ч÷≤„Ю„Х÷ЉвАО (lakhamu) is a perfect form of a dynamic root, and therefore past or perfective. This serves as verification of the description in the previous verse.
n2000435: tn Heb вАЬthe bread of wickednessвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV). There are two ways to take the genitives: (1) genitives of apposition: wickedness and violence are their food and drink (cf. TEV, CEV, NLT), or (2) genitives of source: they derive their livelihood from the evil they do (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 93).
n2000436: tn The verb is an imperfect, either present or future, saying what they do or forecasting what they will do. Being paired with a perfect verb in the beginning of the verse, the description combines their past pattern with what they continue to do.
n2000437: tn Heb вАЬthe wine of violenceвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV). This is a genitive of source, meaning that the wine they drink was plundered from their violent crime. The Hebrew is structured in an AB:BA chiasm: вАЬFor they eat the bread of wickedness, and the wine of violence they drink.вАЭ The word order in the translation is reversed for the sake of smoothness and readability.
n2000438: tn Heb вАЬlike light of brightness.вАЭ This construction is an attributive genitive: вАЬbright light.вАЭ The word вАЬlightвАЭ (вАП„Р„Х÷є„®вАО,  Њor) refers to the early morning light or the dawn (BDB 21 s.v.). The point of the simile is that the course of life that the righteous follow is like the clear, bright morning light. It is illumined, clear, easy to follow, and healthy and safeвАФthe opposite of what darkness represents.
n2000439: tn A literal rendering would appear to be вАЬwalking and becoming bright.вАЭ When the Qal active participle of вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (halakh) is paired with another participle (or adj.) it can express the intensification of an event, that is, the second element in the pairing continues on and increases in character. Cf. Jonah 1:11 and see HALOT 246-247 s.v. вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО 5).
n2000440: tn Heb вАЬuntil the day is established.вАЭ This expression refers to the coming of the full day or the time of high noon.
n2000441: sn The image of paths, brightness or darkness, and stumbling illustrate the contrast of lifestyles. When acting with righteousness oneвАЩs course becomes clearer and more sure, while the wicked are caught in their ways, ignorant of why they fall.
n2000442:
tn Heb вАЬincline your ear.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ф÷Ј„ШвАО (hat) is the Hiphil imperative from вАП„†÷Є„Ш÷Є„ФвАО (natah, Hiphil: вАЬto turn to; to inclineвАЭ). The idiom вАЬto incline the earвАЭ gives the picture of вАЬlean over and listen closely.вАЭ
sn Commentators note the use of the body in this section: ear (v. 20), eyes (v. 21), flesh (v. 22), heart (v. 23), lips (v. 24), eyes (v. 25), feet (v. 26), and hands and feet (v. 27). Each is a synecdoche of part representing the whole; the total accumulation signifies the complete person in the process.
n2000443: tn The Hiphil form вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ц„Х÷ЉвАО (yallizu) follows the Aramaic with gemination. The verb means вАЬto turn aside; to departвАЭ (intransitive Hiphil or inner causative).
n2000444: tn Or вАЬkeepвАЭ (so KJV, NIV, NRSV and many others).
n2000445: sn The words вАЬeyesвАЭ and вАЬheartвАЭ are metonymies of subject representing the faculties of each. Cf. CEV вАЬthink about it all.вАЭ
n2000447: tn Anatomically the Hebrew word вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev) refers to the вАЬheart.вАЭ But abstractly it can refer to oneвАЩs inner self, will, understanding, or mind. They did not see the heart and mind in opposition, such that the advice here includes both oneвАЩs thinking and feelings.
n2000448: tn Heb вАЬmore than any guard.вАЭ The preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) has its comparative sense вАЬmore than.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю÷і„©÷∞„Б„Ю÷Є„®вАО (mishmar) refers here to the act of guarding, protection, or vigilance (BDB 1038 s.v. вАП„Ю÷і„©÷∞„Б„Ю÷Є„®вАО; HALOT 649 s.v. вАП„Ю÷і„©÷∞„Б„Ю÷Є„®вАО).
n2000449: sn The word вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„¶÷∞„Р„Х÷є„™вАО (totse Њot, from вАП„Щ÷Є„¶÷Є„РвАО, yatsa Њ) means вАЬoutgoings; extremities; sources.вАЭ It is used here for starting points, like a fountainhead, and so the translation вАЬsourcesвАЭ works well.
n2000450: tn Heb вАЬcrookedness.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ґ÷і„І÷∞÷Љ„©„Б„Х÷Љ„™вАО ( њiqqeshut) refers to what is morally twisted or perverted. Here it refers to things that are said (cf. NAB вАЬdishonest talkвАЭ; NRSV вАЬcrooked speechвАЭ). The term вАЬmouthвАЭ functions as a metonymy of cause for perverse speech. Such perverse talking could be subtle or blatant.
n2000452: tn Heb вАЬdeviousness of lips put far from you.вАЭ
n2000453: tn The jussives in this verse are both Hiphil, the first from the verb вАЬto gaze; to look intently [or, carefully],вАЭ (вАП„†÷Є„С÷Ј„ШвАО, navat) and the second from the verb вАЬto be smooth, straightвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Є„®вАО, yashar).
n2000454: tn Heb вАЬyour eyelids.вАЭ The term вАЬeyelidsвАЭ is often a poetic synonym for вАЬeyeвАЭ (it is a metonymy of adjunct, something connected with the eye put for the eye that sees); it may intensify the idea as one might squint to gain a clearer look.
n2000456: sn The verb is a denominative Piel from the word вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„°вАО (peles), вАЬbalance; scale.вАЭ In addition to telling the disciple to keep focused on a righteous life, the sage tells him to keep his path level, which is figurative for living the righteous life.
n2000457: tn Following an imperative, a vav plus imperfect verb can depict purpose or result.
n2000458: tn The Niphal jussive from вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (kun, вАЬto be fixed; to be established; to be steadfastвАЭ) continues the idiom of walking and ways for the moral sense in life.
n2000459: sn The two verbs in this verse are from different roots, but nonetheless share the same semantic domain. The first verb is вАП„™÷Љ÷µ„ШвАО (tet), a jussive from вАП„†÷Є„Ш÷Є„ФвАО (natah), which means вАЬto turn asideвАЭ (Hiphil); the second verb is the Hiphil imperative of вАП„°„Х÷Љ„®вАО (sur), which means вАЬto cause to turn to the sideвАЭ (Hiphil). The disciple is not to leave the path of righteousness, but to stay on the path he must leave evil.
n2000460: tn Heb вАЬyour footвАЭ (so NAB, NIV, NRSV). The term вАП„®÷Ј„Т÷∞„Ь÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО (raglekha, вАЬyour footвАЭ) is a synecdoche of part (= foot) for the whole person (= вАЬyourselfвАЭ).
n2000461: tc The LXX adds, вАЬFor the way of the right hand God knows, but those of the left hand are distorted; and he himself will make straight your paths and guide your goings in peace.вАЭ The ideas presented here are not out of harmony with Proverbs, but the section clearly shows an expansion by the translator. For a brief discussion of whether this addition is Jewish or early Christian, see C. H. Toy, Proverbs (ICC), 99.
n200051: sn In this chapter the sage/father exhorts discretion (1, 2) then explains how to avoid seduction (3-6); this is followed by a second exhortation to prevention (7, 8) and an explanation that obedience will avoid ruin and regret (9-14); finally, he warns against sharing love with strangers (15-17) but to find it at home (18-23). For an analysis of the chapter, see J. E. Goldingay, вАЬProverbs V and IX,вАЭ RB 84 (1977): 80-93.
n200052: tn The text again has вАЬmy son.вАЭ In this passage perhaps вАЬsonвАЭ would be the most fitting because of the warning against going to the adulterous woman. However, since the image of the adulterous woman probably represents all kinds of folly (through personification), and since even in this particular folly the temptation works both ways, the general address to either young men or women should be retained. The text was certainly not intended to convey that only women could seduce men.
n200053: tn Heb вАЬincline your earвАЭ (so NAB, NRSV); NLT вАЬlisten carefully.вАЭ
n200055: sn This вАЬdiscretionвАЭ is the same word in 1:4; it is wise, prudential consideration, careful planning, or the ability to devise plans with a view to the best way to carry them out. If that ability is retained then temptations to digress will not interfere.
n200056: sn вАЬLipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause, referring to her words. Dripping honey is compared by implication to her words, which are flattering and smooth (cf. Song 4:11). See M. Dahood, вАЬHoney That Drips. Notes on Proverbs 5:2-3, вАЭ Bib 54 (1973): 65-66.
n200057: tn Heb вАЬher palate.вАЭ The word вАП„Ч÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО (khekh, вАЬpalate; roof of the mouth; gumsвАЭ) is a metonymy of cause (= organ of speech) for what is said (= her seductive speech). The present translation clarifies this metonymy with the phrase вАЬher seductive words.вАЭ
n200058: sn Heb вАЬher endвАЭ (so KJV). D. Kidner notes that Proverbs does not allow us to forget that there is an afterward (Proverbs [TOTC], 65).
n200059: sn The verb вАЬto be bitterвАЭ (вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО, marar) describes things that are harmful and destructive for life, such as the death of the members of the family of Naomi (Ruth 1:20) or finding water that was undrinkable (Exod 15:22-27). The word indicates that the sweet talking will turn out badly.
n2000510: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬwormwoodвАЭ refers to the aromatic plant that contrasts with the sweetness of honey. Some follow the LXX and translate it as вАЬgallвАЭ (cf. NIV). The point is that there was sweetness when the tryst had alluring glamour, but afterward it had an ugly ring (W. G. Plaut, Proverbs, 74).
n2000511: sn The Hebrew has вАЬlike a sword of [two] mouths,вАЭ meaning a double-edged sword that devours/cuts either way. There is no movement without damage. There may be a wordplay here with this description of the вАЬsword with two mouths,вАЭ and the subject of the passage being the words of her mouth which also have two sides to them. The irony is cut by the idiom.
n2000512:
tn The term вАП„©„Б÷∞„Р„Х÷є„ЬвАО (she Њol, вАЬgraveвАЭ) is paralleled to вАЬdeath,вАЭ so it does not refer here to the realm of the unblessed.
sn The terms death and grave could be hyperbolic of a ruined life, but probably refer primarily to the mortal consequences of a life of debauchery.
n2000513:
tc The LXX and other versions read вАЬnotвАЭ instead of вАЬlest.вАЭ This may be an effort to make sense of the unusual syntax, or perhaps the MT has been corrupted. The general sense is the same in either case.
tn Heb вАЬThe path of life lest she clear the way.вАЭ This is the only occasion where the particle вАП„§÷ґ÷Љ„ЯвАО (pen, вАЬlestвАЭ) appears to occur in the middle of its clause rather than at the beginning. The particle implies some action has been taken to avert or avoid what follows. The translation treats the вАЬpath of lifeвАЭ as the object and links the clause to the previous verse. One may note, however, that if the two halves of this verse reversed, normal syntax and good sense are also restored. вАЬHer paths have wandered. She is not able to discernвАФthe path leading to life, lest she make it clear.вАЭ (Or вАЬlest she examine it.вАЭ See note on the verb.)
n2000514: tn Two roots are proposed for the verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„°вАО (palas), вАЬto clear/make a wayвАЭ or вАЬto observe; to examine.вАЭ If the latter root, then it would mean вАЬlest she examine the path of life.вАЭ
n2000515: tn Heb вАЬthe path of life.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ч÷Ј„Щ÷і÷Љ„Щ„ЭвАО (khayyim, вАЬof lifeвАЭ) functions as a genitive of direction (вАЬleading toвАЭ).
n2000516: tn The verb вАП„†„Х÷Љ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (nua њ) means вАЬto quiver; to waver; to roam around.вАЭ As the perfect form of a dynamic verb, it is past or perfective. Here it should be perfective to recognize the continuing results. Some translations treat the verb as stative and so present tense (cf. KJV вАЬher ways are moveableвАЭ), but it is morphologically dynamic as revealed by its imperfect form. Others treat it as an imperfect, rendering it in future tense (NAB вАЬher paths will rambleвАЭ) or general present (NLT вАЬShe staggers down a crooked trail.вАЭ).
n2000517: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ, вАЬto knowвАЭ) is a stative verb in the imperfect form, which can be either future or modal. Here it is a modal abilitive: she is unable to know. DCH includes вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО II, вАЬbe quiet, at rest; be submissiveвАЭ citing Jer 4:18 and Job 21:19. See also D. W. Thomas, вАЬA Note on вАП„Ь„Р „™„У„ҐвАО in Proverbs v 6, вАЭ JTS 37 (1936): 59, proposing вАЬshe is not tranquil.вАЭ
n2000519: tn Heb вАЬthe words of my mouthвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NRSV).
n2000521: sn There is a contrast made between вАЬkeep far awayвАЭ (вАП„Ф÷Ј„®÷∞„Ч÷µ„ІвАО, harkheq) and вАЬdo not draw nearвАЭ (вАП„Х÷∞„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„™÷Љ÷і„І÷∞„®÷Ј„СвАО, ve Њal tiqrav).
n2000522: sn The term вАП„Ф„Х÷є„УвАО (hod, вАЬvigor; splendor; majestyвАЭ) in this context means the best time of oneвАЩs life (cf. NIV вАЬyour best strengthвАЭ), the full manly vigor that will be wasted with licentiousness. Here it is paralleled by вАЬyears,вАЭ which refers to the best years of that vigor, the prime of life. Life would be ruined by living this way, or the revenge of the womanвАЩs husband would cut it short.
n2000523: tn Heb вАЬeat their fill ofвАЭ or вАЬbecome sated fromвАЭ your strength.
n2000524: tn The word вАП„Ы÷є÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (koakh, вАЬstrengthвАЭ) refers to what laborious toil would produce (so a metonymy of cause). Everything that this person worked for could become the property for others to enjoy.
n2000526: tn The term вАЬbenefitвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity and smoothness.
n2000528:
tn The form is the perfect tense with the vav consecutive; it is equal to a specific future within this context.
sn The verb means вАЬto growl, groan.вАЭ It refers to a lion when it devours its prey, and to a sufferer in pain or remorse (e.g., Ezek 24:23).
n2000529: tn Heb вАЬin the finishing of your flesh and your body.вАЭ The construction uses the Qal infinitive construct of вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (kalah) in a temporal clause; the verb means вАЬbe complete, at an end, finished, spent.вАЭ
n2000530: tn The vav that introduces this clause functions in an explanatory sense.
n2000531: tn Heb вАЬdid not listen to the voice of.вАЭ The picture is that of treating the teacherвАЩs instruction as background noise instead of paying attention to it or obeying it.
n2000532: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Ю„Х÷є„®÷Ј„ЩвАО (moray) is the nominal form based on the Hiphil plural participle with a suffix, from the root вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (yarah). The verb is вАЬto teach,вАЭ the common noun is вАЬinstruction, law [torah],вАЭ and this participle form is teacher (вАЬmy teachersвАЭ).
n2000533: tn The idiom is based on attentiveness: вАЬdid not incline my ear to.вАЭ
n2000534: tn The form is the Piel plural participle of вАП„Ь÷Є„Ю÷Ј„УвАО (lamad) used substantivally.
n2000535: tn The expression вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Ю÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„ШвАО (kim њat) is вАЬlike a little.вАЭ It means вАЬalmost,вАЭ and is used of unrealized action (BDB 590 s.v. 2). Cf. NCV вАЬI came close toвАЭ; NLT вАЬI have come to the brink of.вАЭ
n2000536: tn Heb вАЬI was in all evilвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV).
n2000537: tn The text uses the two words вАЬcongregation and assemblyвАЭ to form a hendiadys, meaning the entire assembly.
n2000538: sn Paul Kruger develops this section as an allegory consisting of a series of metaphors. He suggests that what is at issue is private versus common property. The images of the cistern, well, or fountain are used of a wife (e.g., Song 4:15) because she, like water, satisfies desires. Streams of water in the street would then mean sexual contact with a lewd woman. According to 7:12 she never stays home but is in the streets and is the property of many (P. Kruger, вАЬPromiscuity and Marriage Fidelity? A Note on Prov 5:15-18, вАЭ JNSL 13 [1987]: 61-68).
n2000539: tn The verb means вАЬto be scattered; to be dispersedвАЭ; here the imperfect takes a deliberative nuance in a rhetorical question.
n2000540: tc The verse is usually understood as a rhetorical question, expecting a вАЬnoвАЭ answer (e.g. NIV, NASB, ESV, NKJV). The LXX records a negative volitional statement вАЬLet them not flow out.вАЭ
n2000541: tn The вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) preposition denotes possession: вАЬfor youвАЭ = вАЬyours.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷∞„С÷Ј„У÷Љ÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (levadekha) is appositional, underscoring the possession as exclusive.
n2000542: sn The point is that what is private is not to be shared with strangers; it belongs in the home and in the marriage. The water from that cistern is not to be channeled to strangers or to the public.
n2000543: sn The positive instruction is now given: Find pleasure in a fulfilling marriage. The вАЬfountainвАЭ is another in the series of implied comparisons with the sexual pleasure that must be fulfilled at home. That it should be blessed (the passive participle of вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО, barakh) indicates that sexual delight is God-given; having it blessed would mean that it would be endowed with fruitfulness, that it would fulfill all that God intended it to do.
n2000544: tn The form is a Qal imperative with a vav (вАП„ХвАО) of sequence; after the jussive of the first half this colon could be given an equivalent translation or logically subordinated.
n2000545: tn Or вАЬyour young wifeвАЭ; Heb вАЬin the wife of your youthвАЭ (so NIV, NLT, HCSB). The genitive may function as an attributive adjective: вАЬyoung wifeвАЭ or вАЬyouthful wife.вАЭ Or the genitive may be temporal in that it refers to the age in which a man married his wife: вАЬthe wife you married in your youthвАЭ (cf. NCV, CEV). The temporal genitive is supported by parallel passages with similar constructions in Prov 2:17 and Mal 2:14.
n2000546:
tn The construct expression вАЬa doe of lovesвАЭ is an attributive genitive, describing the doe with the word вАЬloves.вАЭ The plural noun may be an abstract plural of intensification (but this noun only occurs in the plural). The same construction follows with a вАЬdeer of graceвАЭвАФa graceful deer.
sn The imagery for intimate love in marriage is now employed to stress the beauty of sexual fulfillment as it was intended. The doe and deer, both implied comparisons, exhibit the grace and love of the wife.
n2000547: sn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО (shagah) means вАЬto swerve; to meander; to reelвАЭ as in drunkenness; it signifies a staggering gait expressing the ecstatic joy of a captivated lover. It may also mean вАЬto be always intoxicated with her loveвАЭ (cf. NRSV).
n2000548: tn In the interrogative clause the imperfect has a deliberative nuance.
n2000549: tn Heb вАЬforeignerвАЭ (so ASV, NASB), but this does not mean that the woman is non-Israelite. This term describes a woman who is outside the moral boundaries of the covenant communityвАФshe is another manвАЩs wife, but since she acts with moral abandonment she is called вАЬforeign.вАЭ
n2000551: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ; the referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2000552: tn BDB 814 s.v. вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„°вАО 2 suggests that the participle вАП„Ю÷∞„§÷Љ÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷µ„°вАО (mepalles) means вАЬto make level [or, straight].вАЭ As oneвАЩs ways are in front of the eyes of the Lord, they become straight or right. It could be translated вАЬweighsвАЭ since it is a denominative from the noun for вАЬbalance, scaleвАЭ; the Lord weighs or examines the actions.
n2000553: tn Heb вАЬall hisвАЭ; the referent (the person mentioned in the first half of the verse) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2000554: tn The suffix on the verb is the direct object suffix; вАЬthe wickedвАЭ is a second object by apposition: They capture him, the wicked. Since вАЬthe wickedвАЭ is not found in the LXX, it could be an old scribal error; or the Greek translator may have simply smoothed out the sentence. C. H. Toy suggests turning the sentence into a passive idea: вАЬThe wicked will be caught in his iniquitiesвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 117).
n2000555: tn The word is the subject of the clause, but the pronominal suffix has no clear referent. The suffix is proleptic, referring to the wicked.
n2000556: tn Heb вАЬhis own iniquities will capture the wicked.вАЭ The translation shifts the syntax for the sake of smoothness and readability.
n2000557: sn The lack of discipline and control in the area of sexual gratification is destructive. The one who plays with this kind of sin will become ensnared by it and led to ruin.
n2000558: tn The Hebrew is structured chiastically: вАЬhis own iniquities will capture the wicked, by the cords of his own sin will he be held.вАЭ
n2000559: tn The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) is used in a causal sense: вАЬbecauseвАЭ (cf. NCV, TEV, CEV).
n2000560: sn The word вАП„Р÷і„Х÷Љ÷Ј„Ь÷∞„™÷Љ„Х÷євАО ( Њivvalto, вАЬhis follyвАЭ) is from the root вАП„Р„Х„ЬвАО and is related to the noun вАП„Р÷ґ„Х÷і„Щ„ЬвАО ( Њevil, вАЬfoolish; foolвАЭ). The noun вАП„Р÷і„Х÷Љ÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„™вАО ( Њivvelet, вАЬfollyвАЭ) describes foolish and destructive activity. It lacks understanding, destroys what wisdom builds, and leads to destruction if it is not corrected.
n2000561: sn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО (shagah, вАЬto swerve; to reelвАЭ) is repeated in a negative sense. If the young man is not captivated by his wife but is captivated with a stranger in sinful acts, then his own iniquities will captivate him and he will be led to ruin.
n200061: sn The chapter advises release from foolish indebtedness (1-5), admonishes avoiding laziness (6-11), warns of the danger of poverty (9-11) and deviousness (12-15), lists conduct that the Lord hates (16-19), and warns about immorality (20-35).
n200063: sn It was fairly common for people to put up some kind of financial security for someone else, that is, to underwrite anotherвАЩs debts. But the pledge in view here was foolish because the debtor was someone who was not well known (вАП„Ц÷Є„®вАО, zar). The one who pledged security for this one was simply gullible.
n200064: tn A neighbor (вАП„®÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО, rea њ) does not mean a person who lives next door or on your block, but someone whom you are brought into contact with, or live or work with, because of lifeвАЩs circumstances. Since this person is also called a stranger (вАП„Ц÷Є„®вАО, zar) at the end of the verse, вАЬneighborвАЭ should be understood in the broadest sense of a social contact.
n200065: tn The conjunction вАЬifвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text. It applies from the previous line and is supplied in the translation for the sake of smoothness.
n200066: tn Heb вАЬstruck your handsвАЭ; NIV вАЬhave struck hands in pledgeвАЭ; NASB вАЬhave given a pledge.вАЭ The guarantee of a pledge was signaled by a handshake (e.g., 11:15; 17:18; 22:26).
n200067: tn Heb вАЬstranger.вАЭ The term вАП„Ц÷Є„®вАО (zar, вАЬstrangerвАЭ) can refer to a stranger who is outside the family, a non-Israelite foreigner, or an unauthorized or prohibited person (like the strange/prohibited woman in Prov 2:16 and 5:3). The person is either not well known or off-limits and represents a high financial risk and/or an undesirable association.
n200068: tn The term вАЬifвАЭ does not appear in this line but is implied by the parallelism. It is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n200069: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„І÷Ј„©„БвАО (yaqash) means вАЬto lay a bait; to lure; to lay snares.вАЭ In the Niphal it means вАЬto be caught by bait; to be ensnaredвАЭвАФhere in a business entanglement.
n2000610: tn Heb вАЬby the words of your mouth.вАЭ The same expression occurs at the end of the following line (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB). Many English versions vary the wording slightly, presumably for stylistic reasons, to avoid redundancy (e.g., NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2000611: tn The syntactical construction of imperative followed by an imperative with vav consecutive denotes purpose: вАЬin order to be delivered.вАЭ The verb means вАЬto deliver oneself, be deliveredвАЭ in the Niphal. The image is one of being snatched or plucked quickly out of some danger or trouble, in the sense of a rescue, as in a вАЬbrand snatched [Hophal stem] from the fireвАЭ (Zech 3:2).
n2000612: tn Heb вАЬhave come into the hand of your neighborвАЭ (so NASB; cf. KJV, ASV). The idiom using the вАЬhandвАЭ means that the individual has come under the control or the power of someone else. This particular word for hand is used to play ironically on its first occurrence in v. 1.
n2000613: tn In the Hitpael the verb вАП„®÷Є„§÷Ј„°вАО (rafas) means вАЬto stamp oneself downвАЭ or вАЬto humble oneselfвАЭ (cf. KJV, NASB, NIV). BDB 952 s.v. Hithp suggests вАЬbecome a suppliant.вАЭ G. R. Driver related it to the Akkadian cognate rapasu, вАЬtrample,вАЭ and interpreted as trampling oneself, swallowing pride, being unremitting in effort (вАЬSome Hebrew Verbs, Nouns, and Pronouns,вАЭ JTS 30 [1929]: 374).
n2000614: tn Heb вАЬbe bold.вАЭ The verb вАП„®÷Є„Ф÷Ј„СвАО (rahav) means вАЬto act stormily; to act boisterously; to act arrogantly.вАЭ The idea here is a strong one: storm against (beset, importune) your neighbor. The meaning is that he should be bold and not take no for an answer. Cf. NIV вАЬpress your pleaвАЭ; TEV вАЬbeg him to release you.вАЭ
n2000615: tn Heb вАЬdo not give sleep to your eyes.вАЭ The point is to go to the neighbor and seek release from the agreement immediately (cf. NLT вАЬDonвАЩt rest until you doвАЭ).
n2000616: tn Heb вАЬfrom the hand.вАЭ Most translations supply вАЬof the hunter.вАЭ The word вАЬhandвАЭ can signify power, control; so the meaning is that of a gazelle freeing itself from a snare or a trap that a hunter set.
n2000617: tc Heb вАЬhandвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NRSV). Some mss and versions have it as вАЬtrap,вАЭ which may very well represent an interpretation too.
n2000618: sn The sluggard (вАП„Ґ÷Є„¶÷µ„ЬвАО,  њatsel) is the lazy or sluggish person (cf. NCV вАЬlazy person,вАЭ and NRSV and NLT вАЬlazybonesвАЭ).
n2000619: sn A fact seemingly unknown until recent centuries is that although worker ants are sterile, they are female. The gender of the word вАЬantвАЭ in Hebrew is feminine.
n2000620: tn The conjunction vav (вАП„ХвАО) here has the classification of alternative, вАЬorвАЭ (R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 71, ¬І433).
n2000621: tn The Hebrew imperfect verb occurs here in the sense of past habitual action.
n2000622:
tc The LXX adds a lengthy section at the end of the verse on the lesson from the bee: вАЬOr, go to the bee and learn how diligent she is and how seriously she does her workвАФher products kings and private persons use for healthвАФshe is desired and respected by allвАФthough feeble in body, by honoring wisdom she obtains distinction.вАЭ The Greek translator thought the other insect should be mentioned (see C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 124).
tn Heb вАЬits food.вАЭ
n2000623: sn The use of the two rhetorical questions is designed to rebuke the lazy person in a forceful manner. The sluggard is spending too much time sleeping.
n2000624: sn The writer might in this verse be imitating the words of the sluggard who just wants to take вАЬa little nap.вАЭ The use is ironic, for by indulging in this little rest the lazy one comes to ruin.
n2000625: tn Heb вАЬlike a wayfarerвАЭ or вАЬlike a travelerвАЭ (cf. KJV). The LXX has вАЬswiftness like a traveler.вАЭ It has also been interpreted as a вАЬhighwaymanвАЭ (cf. NAB) or a вАЬdangerous assailant.вАЭ W. McKane suggests вАЬvagrantвАЭ (Proverbs [OTL], 324); cf. NASB вАЬvagabond.вАЭ Someone traveling swiftly would likely be a robber.
n2000626: tn The Hebrew word for вАЬarmedвАЭ is probably connected to the word for вАЬshieldвАЭ and вАЬdeliverвАЭ (s.v. вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО). G. R. Driver connects it to the Arabic word for вАЬbold; insolent,вАЭ interpreting its use here as referring to a beggar or an insolent man (вАЬStudies in the Vocabulary of the Old Testament, IV,вАЭ JTS 33 [1933]: 38-47).
n2000627: sn The terms describe one who is both worthless and wicked. Some suggest that вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО (beliyya њal) is a compound of the negative вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„ЩвАО (beli) and a noun вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО (ya њal, вАЬprofit; worthвАЭ). Others suggest that the root is from вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО (ba њal, вАЬlord [of goats]вАЭ) or a derivative of вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ҐвАО (bala њ) with reduplication (вАЬconfusionвАЭ or вАЬengulfing ruinвАЭ), or a proper name from Babylonian Bililu. See B. Otzen, TDOT 2:131-36; and D. W. Thomas, вАЬвАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ь÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО in the Old Testament,вАЭ Biblical and Patristic Studies in Memory of Robert Pierce Casey, 11-19. Whatever the etymology, usage shows that the word describes people who violate the law (Deut 15:9; Judg 19:22; 1 Kgs 21:10, 13; Prov 16:27; et al.) or act in a contemptuous and foolish manner against cultic observance or social institutions (1 Sam 10:27; 25:17; 30:22); cf. NRSV вАЬa scoundrel and a villainвАЭ (NAB and NIV similar). The present instruction will focus on the devious practice of such wicked and worthless folk.
n2000628: tn Heb вАЬcrookedвАЭ or вАЬtwisted.вАЭ This term can refer to something that is physically twisted or crooked, or something morally perverse. Cf. NAB вАЬcrooked talkвАЭ; NRSV вАЬcrooked speech.вАЭ
n2000629: tn Heb вАЬwalks around with a perverse mouth.вАЭ The term вАЬmouthвАЭ is a metonymy of cause, an organ of speech put for what is said. This is an individual who says perverted or twisted things.
n2000630: sn The sinister sign language and gestures of the perverse individual seem to indicate any kind of look or gesture that is put on and therefore a form of deception if not a way of making insinuations. W. McKane suggests from the presence of вАП„Ч÷є„®÷µ„©„БвАО (khoresh) in v. 14 that there may be some use of magic here (Proverbs [OTL], 325).
n2000631: tn The noun is an adverbial accusative of manner, explaining the circumstances that inform his evil plans.
n2000632: tn The word вАЬcontentionвАЭ is from the root вАП„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (din); the noun means вАЬstrife, contention, quarrel.вАЭ The normal plural form is represented by the Qere, and the contracted form by the Kethib.
n2000633: tn This word is a substantive that is used here as an adverbial accusativeвАФwith suddenness, at an instant.
n2000634: tn The conjunction has the explicative use here (R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 71, ¬І434).
n2000635: sn This saying involves a numerical ladder, paralleling six things with seven things (e.g., also 30:15, 18, 21, 24, 29). The point of such a numerical arrangement is that the number does not exhaust the list (W. M. Roth, вАЬThe Numerical Sequence x / x +1 in the Old Testament,вАЭ VT 12 [1962]: 300-311; and his вАЬNumerical Sayings in the Old Testament,вАЭ VT 13 [1965]: 86).
n2000637: sn The expression вАЬhigh/ lofty [вАП„®÷Є„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО, ramot] eyesвАЭ refers to a proud look suggesting arrogant ambition (cf. NCV вАЬa proud lookвАЭ). The use of вАЬeyesвАЭ is a metonymy of adjunct, the look in the eyes accompanying the attitude. This term вАЬhighвАЭ is used in Num 15:30 for the sin of the вАЬhigh hand,вАЭ i.e., willful rebellion or defiant sin. The usage of вАЬhaughty eyesвАЭ may be illustrated by its use with the pompous Assyrian invader (Isa 10:12-14) and the proud king of the book of Daniel (11:12). God does not tolerate anyone who thinks so highly of himself and who has such ambition.
n2000638: tn Heb вАЬa tongue of deception.вАЭ The genitive noun functions attributively. The term вАЬtongueвАЭ functions as a metonymy. The term is used of false prophets who deceive (Jer 14:14), and of a deceiver who betrays (Ps 109:2). The Lord hates deceptive speech because it is destructive (26:28).
n2000639: sn The hands are the instruments of murder (metonymy of cause), and God hates bloodshed. Gen 9:6 prohibited shedding blood because people are the image of God. Even David being a man of blood (in war mostly) was not permitted to build the Temple (1 Chr 22:8). But shedding innocent blood was a greater crimeвАФit usually went with positions of power, such as King Manasseh filling the streets with blood (2 Kgs 21:16), or princes doing it for gain (Ezek 22:27).
n2000640: tn Heb вАЬheart that devises plans of wickedness.вАЭ The latter term is an attributive genitive. The heart (metonymy of subject) represents the will; here it plots evil schemes. The heart is capable of evil schemes (Gen 6:5); the heart that does this is deceitful (Prov 12:20; 14:22).
n2000641:
tc The MT reads вАЬmake haste to run,вАЭ that is, be eager to seize the opportunity. The LXX omits вАЬrun,вАЭ that is, feet hastening to do evil. It must have appeared to the LXX translator that the verb was unnecessary; only one verb occurs in the other cola.
sn The word вАЬfeetвАЭ is here a synecdoche, a part for the whole. Being the instruments of movement, they represent the swift and eager actions of the whole person to do some harm.
n2000642: sn The Lord hates perjury and a lying witness (e.g., Ps 40:4; Amos 2:4; Mic 1:4). This is a direct violation of the law (Exod 20).
n2000643: sn Dissension is attributed in Proverbs to contentious people (21:9; 26:21; 25:24) who have a short fuse (15:8).
n2000644:
tn Heb вАЬbrothers,вАЭ although not limited to male siblings only. Cf. NRSV, CEV вАЬin a familyвАЭ; TEV вАЬamong friends.вАЭ
sn These seven things the Lord hates. To discover what the Lord desires, one need only list the opposites: humility, truthful speech, preservation of life, pure thoughts, eagerness to do good, honest witnesses, and peaceful harmony. In the NT the Beatitudes present the positive opposites (Matt 5). It has seven blessed things to match these seven hated things; moreover, the first contrasts with the first here (вАЬpoor in spiritвАЭ of 5:5 with вАЬhaughty eyesвАЭ), and the seventh (вАЬpeacemakersвАЭ of 5:7) contrasts with the seventh here (вАЬsows dissensionвАЭ).
n2000645: tn The text again has вАЬmy son.вАЭ In this passage perhaps вАЬsonвАЭ would be the most fitting because of the warning against the adulterous woman. However, since even in this particular folly the temptation works both ways, the general address to either young men or women is retained. Similar warnings would apply to daughters to be warned of smooth-talking, seductive men.
n2000646: sn The figures used here are hypocatastases (implied comparisons). There may also be an allusion to Deut 6 where the people were told to bind the law on their foreheads and arms. The point here is that the disciple will never be without these instructions. See further, P. W. Skehan, Studies in Israelite Poetry and Wisdom (CBQMS), 1-8.
n2000647: sn The Hebrew word вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev) вАЬheartвАЭ includes the mind. Hebrew does not separate вАЬheart knowledgeвАЭ and вАЬhead knowledge.вАЭ While вАЬheartвАЭ may convey a deep commitment, the вАЬmindвАЭ is crucial to considering and adopting the instruction. To have the instruction вАЬon your mindвАЭ is critical to the deliberate talking to oneself needed to conform to the instruction, to meditating on it and assimilating it into oneвАЩs world view.
n2000648: tn The verbal form is the Hitpael infinitive construct with a preposition and a suffixed subjective genitive to form a temporal clause. The term вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (halakh) in this verbal stem means вАЬto go about; to go to and fro.вАЭ The use of these terms in v. 22 also alludes to Deut 6:7.
n2000649: tn Heb вАЬit will guide you.вАЭ The verb is singular and the instruction is the subject.
n2000650: tn Heb вАЬThen you will wake up. It (the instruction) will talk to you.вАЭ In both of the preceding cola an infinitive construct was used for the temporal clauses. Now the construction uses a perfect tense with vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive. The verb verb вАЬwake upвАЭ is consecutive to вАЬlie down [to sleep].вАЭ But it is also the circumstance for the following verb вАЬwill talk,вАЭ so it is has been subordinated here as a temporal clause.
n2000651: sn The meaning of the verb вАП„©÷і„В„Щ„Ч÷ЈвАО (siakh) has been understood variously as meditating, considering, whispering, or talking (in praise or complaint); cf. TEV, NLT вАЬadvise you.вАЭ The picture here is that the person has been so dedicated to the instruction that it is the first thing that comes to mind upon waking. The words of instruction вАЬbound on the mind/heartвАЭ in 6:21 become, in todayвАЩs terminology, the words of oneвАЩs self-talk.
n2000653: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2000654: sn The terms вАЬlamp,вАЭ вАЬlight,вАЭ and вАЬwayвАЭ are all metaphors. The positive teachings and commandments will illumine or reveal to the disciple the way to life; the disciplinary correctives will provide guidance into fullness of life.
n2000655: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2000656: tn Heb вАЬthe way of lifeвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NRSV); NIV, NLT вАЬthe way to life.вАЭ The noun вАЬlifeвАЭ is a genitive following the construct вАЬway.вАЭ It could be an attributive genitive modifying the kind of way/course of life that instruction provides, but it could also be objective in that the course of life followed would produce and lead to life.
n2000657: tn The infinitive construct is epexegetical here, explaining how these teachings function as lights: вАЬby keeping you.вАЭ This verse is the transition from the general admonition about heeding the teachings to the practical application.
n2000658: tc The word translated вАЬwomanвАЭ is modified by вАП„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (ra њ, вАЬevilвАЭ) in the sense of violating the codes of the community and inflicting harm on others. The BHS editors propose changing it to read вАЬstrange womanвАЭ as before, but there is not support for that. Some commentaries follow the LXX and read вАП„®÷Ј„ҐвАО as вАЬwife of a neighborвАЭ (cf. NAB; also NRSV вАЬthe wife of anotherвАЭ; CEV вАЬsomeone elseвАЩs wifeвАЭ) but that seems to be only a clarification.
n2000659: tn The word вАЬtongueвАЭ is not in construct; the word вАЬforeign womanвАЭ is in apposition to вАЬsmooth of tongue,вАЭ specifying whose it is. The word вАЬsmoothвАЭ then is the object of the preposition, вАЬtongueвАЭ is the genitive of specification, and вАЬforeign womanвАЭ in apposition.
n2000660: sn The description of the woman as a вАЬstrange womanвАЭ and now a вАЬloose [Heb вАЬforeignвАЭ] womanвАЭ is within the context of the people of Israel. She is a вАЬforeignerвАЭ in the sense that she is a nonconformist, wayward, and loose. It does not necessarily mean that she is not ethnically an Israelite.
n2000661:
tn The negated jussive gives the young person an immediate warning. The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„УвАО (khamad) means вАЬto desire,вАЭ and here in the sense of lust. The word is used in the Decalogue of Deut 5:21 for the warning against coveting.
sn Lusting after someone in the heart, according to Jesus, is a sin of the same kind as the act, not just the first step toward it (Matt 5:28). Playing with temptation in the heartвАФthe seat of the will and the emotionsвАФis only the heart reaching out after the sin.
n2000662: tn Heb вАЬher eyelidsвАЭ (so KJV, NASB); NRSV вАЬeyelashesвАЭ; TEV вАЬflirting eyesвАЭ). This term is a synecdoche of part (eyelids) for the whole (eyes) or a metonymy of association for painted eyes and the luring glances that are the symptoms of seduction (e.g., 2 Kgs 9:30). The term вАЬalluringвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarification.
n2000663: tn The word вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„УвАО (be њad) may be taken either as вАЬon account ofвАЭ (= by means of a) prostitute (cf. ASV, NASB), or вАЬfor the price ofвАЭ a prostitute (cf. NAB). Most expositors take the first reading, though that use of the preposition is unattested, and then must supply вАЬone is brought to.вАЭ The verse would then say that going to a prostitute can bring a man to poverty, but going to another manвАЩs wife can lead to death.
