If the second view were taken, it would mean that one had a smaller price than the other. It is not indicating that one is preferable to the other; both are to be avoided.
n2000665: tn These two lines might be an example of synthetic parallelism, that is, вАЬA, whatвАЩs more B.вАЭ The A-line describes the detrimental moral effect of a man going to a professional prostitute; the B-line heightens this and describes the far worse effectвАФmoral and mortal!вАФof a man committing adultery with another manвАЩs wife. When a man goes to a prostitute, he lowers himself to become nothing more than a вАЬmeal ticketвАЭ to sustain the life of that woman; however, when a man commits adultery, he places his very life in jeopardyвАФthe rage of the husband could very well kill him.
n2000666: tn The Qal imperfect (with the interrogative) here has a potential nuanceвАФвАЬIs it possible to do this?вАЭ The sentence is obviously a rhetorical question making an affirmation that it is not possible.
n2000667: sn вАЬFireвАЭ provides the analogy for the sageвАЩs warning: Fire represents the sinful woman (hypocatastasis) drawn close, and the burning of the clothes the inevitable consequences of the liaison. See J. L. Crenshaw, вАЬImpossible Questions, Sayings, and Tasks,вАЭ Semeia 17 (1980): 19-34. The word вАЬfireвАЭ (вАП„Р÷µ„©„БвАО,  Њesh) plays on the words вАЬmanвАЭ (вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО, Њish) and вАЬwomanвАЭ (вАП„Р÷і„©„Б÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО,  Њishah); a passage like this probably inspired R. GamalielвАЩs little explanation that what binds a man and a woman together in a holy marriage is вАП„ЩвАО (yod) and вАП„ФвАО (he), the two main letters of the holy name Yah. But if the Lord is removed from the relationship, that is, if these two letters are removed, all that is left is the вАП„Р÷µ„©„БвАОвАФthe fire of passion. Since Gamaliel was the teacher of Paul, this may have influenced PaulвАЩs advice that it was better to marry than to burn (1 Cor 7:9).
n2000668: tn Heb вАЬsnatch up fire into his bosom.вАЭ
n2000669: tn The second colon begins with the vav (вАП„ХвАО) disjunctive on the noun, indicating a disjunctive clause; here it is a circumstantial clause.
n2000670: tn The particle indicates that this is another rhetorical question like that in v. 27.
n2000672: tn Heb вАЬapproaches.вАЭ The verb вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ) with the preposition вАП„Р÷ґ„ЬвАО ( Њel) means вАЬcome toвАЭ or вАЬapproach,вАЭ but is also used as a euphemism for sexual relations.
n2000673: tn Heb вАЬanyone who touches her will not.вАЭ
n2000674: sn The verb вАЬtouchesвАЭ is intended here to be a euphemism for illegal sexual contact (e.g., Gen 20:6).
n2000675: tn Heb вАЬwill be exempt fromвАЭ; NASB, NLT вАЬwill not go unpunished.вАЭ
n2000676: tn The verb is вАП„Щ÷і„†÷Љ÷Є„І÷ґ„ФвАО (yinnaqeh), the Niphal imperfect from вАП„†÷Є„І÷Є„ФвАО (naqah, вАЬto be empty; to be cleanвАЭ). From it we get the adjectives вАЬclean,вАЭ вАЬfree from guilt,вАЭ вАЬinnocent.вАЭ The Niphal has the meanings (1) вАЬto be cleaned outвАЭ (of a plundered city; e.g., Isa 3:26), (2) вАЬto be clean; to be free from guilt; to be innocentвАЭ (Ps 19:14), (3) вАЬto be free; to be exempt from punishmentвАЭ [here], and (4) вАЬto be free; to be exempt from obligationвАЭ (Gen 24:8).
n2000678: tn Heb вАЬhimselfвАЭ or вАЬhis life.вАЭ Since the word вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, traditionally вАЬsoulвАЭ) refers to the whole person, body and soul, and since it has a basic idea of the bundle of appetites that make up a person, the use here for satisfying his hunger is appropriate.
n2000679: tn The term вАЬyetвАЭ is supplied in the translation.
n2000680: tn Heb вАЬis found out.вАЭ The perfect tense with the vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive may continue or advance from a previous verbвАЩs framework. Here it advances from вАЬstealsвАЭ in 6:30 and serves as the condition for the following imperfect verb.
n2000681: tn The imperfect tense has an obligatory nuance. The verb in the Piel means вАЬto repay; to make restitution; to recompenseвАЭ; cf. NCV, TEV, CEV вАЬmust pay back.вАЭ
n2000682: tn This final clause in the section is somewhat cryptic. The guilty thief must pay back sevenfold what he stole, even if it means he must use the substance of his whole house. The verb functions as an imperfect of possibility: вАЬhe might even give.вАЭ
n2000683: tn The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) here represents thinking by means of metonymy (i.e., the mind stands for what the mind does). The overstatement, вАЬlacking the ability to think,вАЭ means lacking discernment, wisdom, good sense. Cf. NAB вАЬis a foolвАЭ; NIV вАЬlacks judgmentвАЭ; NKJV вАЬlacks understanding,вАЭ NCV, NRSV вАЬhas no sense.вАЭ
n2000684: tn Heb вАЬsoul.вАЭ The noun вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, вАЬsoulвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of association for вАЬlifeвАЭ (BDB 659 s.v. 3.c).
n2000685: tn Heb вАЬHe will find (or obtain) a wound and contempt.вАЭ
n2000686: sn Even though the text has said that the man caught in adultery ruins his life, it does not mean that he was put to death, although that could have happened. He seems to live on in ignominy, destroyed socially and spiritually. He might receive blows and wounds from the husband and shame and disgrace from the spiritual community. D. Kidner observes that in a morally healthy society the adulterer would be a social outcast (Proverbs [TOTC], 75).
n2000687: tn The word вАЬkindlesвАЭ was supplied in the translation; both вАЬrageвАЭ and вАЬjealousyвАЭ have meanings connected to heat.
n2000689: tn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЬвАО (khamal) means вАЬto show mercy; to show compassion; to show pity,вАЭ usually with the outcome of sparing or delivering someone. The idea here is that the husband will not spare the guilty man any of the punishment (cf. NRSV вАЬhe shows no restraintвАЭ).
n2000690: tn Heb вАЬlift up the face of,вАЭ meaning вАЬregard.вАЭ
n2000691:
tc The MTвАЩs вАЬhe will not lift the face of all of compensationвАЭ is probably a case of wrong word division. If the two letters of the word вАП„Ы÷є„ЬвАО (kol, вАЬallвАЭ) are divided and joined to the previous and following words, the text reads вАП„Ь÷є„Р „Щ÷і„©÷Є÷Љ„В„Р „§÷Є„†÷ґ„Щ„Ъ÷Є „Ь÷∞„Ы÷є„§÷ґ„®вАО (lo Њ yissa Њ faneka lekofer) вАЬhe will not lift your face for [any] compensation.вАЭ
tn The word rendered вАЬcompensationвАЭ is вАП„Ы÷є÷Љ„§÷ґ„®вАО (kofer); it is essentially a ransom price, a sum to be paid to deliver another from debt, bondage, or crime. The husband cannot accept payment as a ransom for a life, since what has happened cannot be undone so easily.
n2000692: tn BDB 1005 s.v. вАП„©÷є„Б„Ч÷Ј„УвАО suggests that this term means вАЬhush moneyвАЭ or вАЬbribeвАЭ (cf. NIV, NRSV, NLT). C. H. Toy takes it as legal compensation (Proverbs [ICC], 142).
n200071: sn The chapter begins with the important teaching of the father (1-5), then it focuses on the seduction: first the victim (6-9), then the temptress (10-12), then the persuasion (13-20), and the capitulation (21-23); the chapter concludes with the deadly results of adultery (24-27).
n200072: tn The text again has вАЬmy son.вАЭ In this passage perhaps вАЬsonвАЭ would be the most fitting because of the warning against the adulterous woman. However, since even in this particular folly the temptation works both ways, the general address to either young men or women is retained. Similar warnings would apply to daughters to be warned of smooth-talking, seductive men.
n200073:
tn Heb вАЬstore up with yourself.вАЭ Most translations either use вАЬstoreвАЭ (NIV, NRSV) or вАЬtreasureвАЭ (NASB, ESV, NKJV) and вАЬwith youвАЭ (ESV, NRSV, KJV) or вАЬwithin youвАЭ (NIV, NASB, NKJV). BDB 860 s.v. вАП„¶÷Є„§÷Ј„ЯвАО Qal.1 suggests that вАЬwithin youвАЭ means вАЬin your own keeping.вАЭ HALOT 1049 s.v. describes the verb as вАЬto keep in oneвАЩs heart.вАЭ NIDOTTE 837 s.v. вАП„¶÷Є„§÷Ј„ЯвАО says the verb вАЬtakes on the technical meaning of memorizing the commandments of God.вАЭ The instructions are to have these lessons stored up inside so that you can draw on them in need.
sn The idea here is to study to be prepared. It is the opposite of the idea of getting in a difficult situation and then looking for something in the Bible to apply to your life. This verse is about applying your life to biblical wisdom and being prepared for situations that may come your way.
n200074: tc Before v. 2 the LXX inserts: вАЬMy son, fear the Lord and you will be strong, and besides him, fear no other.вАЭ Although this addition has the precedent of 3:7 and 9 and harmonizes with 14:26, it does not fit here; the advice is to listen to the teacher.
n200075: tn The construction of an imperative with the vav (вАП„ХвАО) of sequence after another imperative denotes a logical sequence of purpose or result: вАЬthat you may live,вАЭ or вАЬand you will live.вАЭ
n200076: tn The term вАЬobeyвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the parallelism; it is supplied for the sake of clarity and smoothness. Some English versions, in light of the second line of v. 1, supply вАЬguardвАЭ (e.g., NIV, NCV, NLT).
n200077: tn The Hebrew phrase refers to the pupil of the eye, perhaps by the idiom вАЬthe little man in [the] eye.вАЭ The term вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„Б„Х÷є„ЯвАО ( Њishon, вАЬpupilвАЭ) appears to be a diminutive from вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО ( Њish, вАЬmanвАЭ). The saying may have arisen because the pupil will make a small reflection of the person looking into anotherвАЩs eyes. Because of the importance of protecting the eye from harm, the вАЬpupilвАЭ of the eye вАЬhas the idea of something precious that was to be guarded jealouslyвАЭ (NIDOTTE 386 s.v. вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„Б„Х÷є„ЯвАО). Traditionally this Hebrew idiom is translated into English as вАЬthe apple of your eyeвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV); a more contemporary rendering would be вАЬas your most prized possession.вАЭ The point is that the teaching must be the central focus of the discipleвАЩs vision and attention.
n200078: tn Heb вАЬfingersвАЭ (so KJV and many other English versions). In light of Deut 6:8, вАЬfingersвАЭ appears to be a metonymy for the lower part of the arm or for the hands.
n200079: sn This is an allusion to Deut 6:8. Binding the teachings on the fingers and writing them on the tablets here are implied comparisons for preserving the teaching in memory so that it can be recalled and used with ease.
n2000710: sn The metaphor is meant to signify that the disciple will be closely related to and familiar with wisdom and understanding, as close as to a sibling. Wisdom will be personified in the next two chapters, and so referring to it as a sister in this chapter certainly prepares for that personification.
n2000711: tn Heb вАЬstrangeвАЭ (so KJV, ASV). See the note at 2:16, which is identical to this verse, except for using a synonym for the beginning verb.
n2000712: tn Heb вАЬstrange woman.вАЭ This can be interpreted as a вАЬwayward wifeвАЭ (so NIV) or an вАЬunfaithful wifeвАЭ (so NCV). As discussed earlier, the designations вАЬstrange womanвАЭ and вАЬforeign womanвАЭ could refer to Israelites who stood outside the community in their lawlessness and loose moralsвАФan adulteress or wayward woman. H. Ringgren and W. Zimmerli, however, suggest that she is also a promoter of a pagan cult, but that is not entirely convincing (Spruche/Prediger [ATD], 19).
n2000713:
tn The Hiphil of вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ІвАО (khalaq, вАЬto be smooth/slipperyвАЭ) means вАЬto use smooth words,вАЭ that is, to flatter (Pss 5:10; 36:3; Prov 2:16; 28:23; 29:5). The seductive speech of the temptress is as sweet as honey and smooth as oil (5:3).
sn As the perfect verb of a dynamic root, the verb reports what she has done. She probably flatters every man who crosses her path, but this advice is given to the young man who would have on his mind what she has said to him. Part of succumbing to temptation often involves becoming narrowly focused on something perceived as pleasurable and blocking out any thought of the consequences. (Compare Eve in Gen 3.) The sage goes on to tell a story in order to make the trap and the consequences vivid.
n2000714: tn The term вАЬyouвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for the sake of smoothness.
n2000715:
tn Heb вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„™÷і„ЩвАО (peti, вАЬnaive, simpletonвАЭ).
sn This naive young man who lacked wisdom is one of the вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„™÷і„ЩвАО (peti) simpletons, lacking keen judgment, one void of common sense (cf. NAB, NASB, NRSV, NLT) or understanding (cf. KJV, ASV). He is young, inexperienced, featherbrained (so D. Kidner, Proverbs [TOTC], 75).
n2000716: tc Heb вАЬsons.вАЭ The MT reads вАП„С÷Ј„С÷Є÷Љ„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (vabbanim) вАЬamong the sons,вАЭ perhaps meaning вАЬyoung menвАЭ (cf. Song 2:3; HALOT 138, s. v. I „С÷µ÷Љ„Я). Based on the Syriac, Targum, and Vulgate, perhaps the text should read вАП„С÷Ј„†÷∞„С÷Є„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (vanevalim, вАЬamong the foolsвАЭ).
n2000718: tn The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) is used as a metonymy of association for what one does with the mind (thinking), and so refers to discernment, wisdom, good sense.
n2000719: tn The verb вАП„¶÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„УвАО (tsa њad) means вАЬto step; to march.вАЭ It suggests that the youth was intentionally making his way to her house. The verb is the imperfect tense; it stresses continual action parallel to the active participle that began the verse, but within a context that is past time.
n2000720: tn Heb вАЬway of her house.вАЭ The term вАЬwayвАЭ is an adverbial accusative telling where he was marching. It is described by the genitive вАЬher houseвАЭ identifying where the way goes by or to.
n2000721: tn Heb вАЬin the evening of the day.вАЭ
n2000722: tn Heb вАЬin the middle of the night, and darkвАЭ; KJV вАЬin the black and dark nightвАЭ; NRSV вАЬat the time of night and darkness.вАЭ
n2000723: tn The particle вАП„Х÷∞„Ф÷і„†÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО (vehinneh) introduces a dramatic sense of the immediate to the narrative; it has a deictic force, вАЬand look!вАФthere was a woman,вАЭ or вАЬall of a sudden this womanвА¶.вАЭ
n2000724: tn Heb вАЬwith the garment of a prostitute.вАЭ The noun вАП„©„Б÷і„Щ„™вАО (shit, вАЬgarmentвАЭ) is an adverbial accusative specifying the appearance of the woman. The words вАЬshe wasвАЭ are supplied in the translation to make a complete English sentence.
n2000725: tn Heb вАЬkept secret of heartвАЭ; cf. ASV, NRSV вАЬwily of heart.вАЭ The verbal form is the passive participle from вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„®вАО (natsar) in construct. C. H. Toy lists the suggestions of the commentators: false, malicious, secret, subtle, excited, hypocritical (Proverbs [ICC], 149). The LXX has вАЬcauses the hearts of the young men to fly away.вАЭ The verb means вАЬto guard; to watch; to keepвАЭ; to be guarded of heart means to be wily, to have secret intentвАФshe has locked up her plans and gives nothing away (e.g., Isaiah 48:6 as well). Interestingly enough, this contrasts with her attire which gives everything away.
n2000726: tn Heb вАЬher feet.вАЭ This is a synecdoche, a part for the whole; the point is that she never stays home, but is out and about all the time.
n2000727: tn Heb вАЬdwellвАЭ or вАЬsettleвАЭ; NAB вАЬher feet cannot rest.вАЭ
n2000728: tn The repetition of the noun вАП„§÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЭвАО (pa њam, вАЬstep, occasionвАЭ) is an idiom indicating different occasions. It could be rendered idiomatically in English as вАЬnow [here], now [there],вАЭ вАЬonce [here], then [there],вАЭ or вАЬat one timeвА¶at another timeвАЭ (BDB 822 s.v. вАП„§÷Ј÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„ЭвАО 3.e).
n2000729: tn Heb вАЬshe made her face bold.вАЭ The Hiphil perfect of вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ц÷Ј„ЦвАО ( њazaz, вАЬto be strongвАЭ) means she has an impudent face (cf. KJV, NAB, NRSV), a bold or brazen expression (cf. NASB, NIV, NLT).
n2000730: tn Heb вАЬpeace offerings are with me.вАЭ The peace offerings refer to the meat left over from the votive offering made at the sanctuary (e.g., Lev 7:11-21). Apparently the sacrificial worship meant little to this woman spiritually. By expressing that she has peace offerings, she could be saying that she has fresh meat for a meal at home, or that she was ceremonially clean, perhaps after her period. At any rate, it is all probably a ruse for winning a customer.
n2000731: tn Heb вАЬto look diligently for your face.вАЭ
n2000732: tn Heb вАЬwith spreads I have spread my bed.вАЭ The rare noun is a cognate to the verb.
n2000733: tn The feminine noun means вАЬdark-hued stuffsвАЭ (BDB 310 s.v. вАП„Ч÷≤„Ш÷ї„С„Х÷є„™вАО). The form is a passive participle from a supposed root II вАП„Ч÷Є„Ш÷Ј„СвАО (khatav), which in Arabic means to be of a turbid, dusky color mixed with yellowish red. Its Aramaic cognate means вАЬvariegatedвАЭ; cf. NAB вАЬwith brocaded cloths of Egyptian linen.вАЭ BDBвАЩs translation of this colon is unsatisfactory: вАЬwith dark hued stuffs of yarn from Egypt.вАЭ
n2000734: tn The verb means вАЬto be saturated; to drink oneвАЩs fill,вАЭ and can at times mean вАЬto be intoxicated with.вАЭ
n2000735: tn Heb вАЬloves.вАЭ The word вАП„У÷Љ„Х÷є„УвАО (dod) means physical love or lovemaking. It is found frequently in the Song of Solomon for the loved one, the beloved.
n2000736: tn The form is the Hitpael cohortative of вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„°вАО ( њalas), which means вАЬto rejoice.вАЭ Cf. NIV вАЬletвАЩs enjoy ourselves.вАЭ
n2000737: tn Heb вАЬthe man.вАЭ The LXX interpreted it as вАЬmy husband,вАЭ taking the article to be used as a possessive. Many English versions do the same.
n2000740: tn Heb вАЬhe will come back to his home at.вАЭ
n2000741: tn Heb вАЬnew moon.вАЭ Judging from the fact that the husband took a purse of money and was staying away until the next full moon, the woman implies that they would be safe in their escapade. If v. 9 and v. 20 are any clue, he could be gone for about two weeksвАФuntil the moon is full again.
n2000742:
tn Heb вАЬshe turned him aside.вАЭ This expression means that she persuaded him.
sn While this verb is a Hebrew perfect (and so past tense in English) the next verb is an imperfect (past progressive). The sage is taking us inside the transition in the manвАЩs mind. He is hooked but not yet reeled in. He has turned and maybe taken a step in her direction, but not really committed yet inside. The second half of the verse points to her continuing enticement to keep him coming until he commits; she is close to closing the deal.
n2000743: sn The term вАП„Ь÷ґ„І÷Ј„ЧвАО (leqakh) was used earlier in Proverbs for wise instruction; now it is used ironically for enticement to sin (see D. W. Thomas, вАЬTextual and Philological Notes on Some Passages in the Book of Proverbs,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 280-92).
n2000744: tn Heb вАЬsmoothness of her lipsвАЭ; cf. NAB вАЬsmooth lipsвАЭ; NASB вАЬflattering lips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause representing what she says. The noun вАП„Ч÷µ„Ь÷ґ„ІвАО (kheleq) вАЬsmoothnessвАЭ is the counterpart to the verb вАЬflatterвАЭ is 7:5.
n2000745: tn The basic meaning of the verb вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„ЧвАО (nadakh) is вАЬto go/be led astray.вАЭ In the causative Hiphil form it means вАЬto drive away, to entice, to seduce.вАЭ As an imperfect verb in a past time setting it is progressive: she turned him aside and was leading him astray.
n2000746: tn The participle with вАЬsuddenlyвАЭ gives a vivid picture. It depicts the inner change in the man. She had turned him and been enticing him along, but he was still like an ox deciding whether to really follow the call after turning in its direction. Then suddenly, like a switch has been thrown inside, he goes on under his own will power, just like the dumb ox he has become.
n2000747: tn The present translation follows R. B. Y. Scott (Proverbs, Ecclesiastes [AB], 64). This third colon of the verse would usually be rendered, вАЬfetters to the chastening of a foolвАЭ (KJV, ASV, and NASB are all similar). But there is no support that вАП„Ґ÷ґ„Ы÷ґ„°вАО ( њekhes) means вАЬfetters.вАЭ It appears in Isaiah 3:16 as вАЬanklets.вАЭ The parallelism here suggests that some animal imagery is required. Thus the ancient versions have вАЬas a dog to the bonds.вАЭ
n2000748: sn The figure of an arrow piercing the liver (an implied comparison) may refer to the pangs of a guilty conscience that the guilty must reap along with the spiritual and physical ruin that follows (see on these expressions H. W. Wolff, Anthropology of the Old Testament).
n2000749: tn The expression that it is вАЬfor/about/over his lifeвАЭ means that it could cost him his life (e.g., Num 16:38). Alternatively, the line could refer to moral corruption and social disgrace rather than physical deathвАФbut this would not rule out physical death too.
n2000750: tn The literal translation вАЬsonsвАЭ works well here in view of the warning. Cf. KJV, NAB, NRSV вАЬchildren.вАЭ
n2000753: tn Heb вАЬnumerousвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NRSV, NLT) or вАЬcountless.вАЭ
n2000754:
tn The noun вАЬSheolвАЭ in parallelism to вАЬthe chambers of deathвАЭ probably means the grave. The noun is a genitive of location, indicating the goal of the road(s). Her house is not the grave; it is, however, the sure way to it. Cf. 2:18.
sn Her house is the way to the grave. The young manвАЩs life is not destroyed in one instant; it is taken from him gradually as he enters into a course of life that will leave him as another victim of the wages of sin. The point of the warning is to prevent such a course from starting. Sin can certainly be forgiven, but the more involvement in this matter the greater the alienation from the healthy community.
n200081: sn In this chapter wisdom is personified. In 1:20-33 wisdom proclaims her value, and in 3:19-26 wisdom is the agent of creation. Such a personification has affinities with the wisdom literature of the ancient Near East, and may have drawn on some of that literature, albeit with appropriate safeguards (Claudia V. Camp, Wisdom and the Feminine in the Book of Proverbs, 23-70). Wisdom in Proverbs 8, however, is not a deity like EgyptвАЩs Ma'at or the Assyrian-Babylonian Ishtar. It is simply presented as if it were a self-conscious divine being distinct but subordinate to God, but in reality it is the personification of the attribute of wisdom displayed by God (R. B. Y. Scott, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes [AB], 69-72; and R. Marcus, вАЬOn Biblical Hypostases of Wisdom,вАЭ HUCA 23 [1950-1951]: 157-71). Many have equated wisdom in this chapter with Jesus Christ. This connection works only in so far as Jesus reveals the nature of the Father, just as Proverbs presents wisdom as an attribute of God. JesusвАЩ claims included wisdom (Matt 12:42) and a unique knowledge of God (Matt 11:25-27). He even personified wisdom in a way that was similar to Proverbs (Matt 11:19). Paul saw the fulfillment of wisdom in Christ (Col 1:15-20; 2:3) and affirmed that Christ became our wisdom in the crucifixion (1 Cor 1:24, 30). So this personification in Proverbs provides a solid foundation for the similar revelation of wisdom in Christ. But because wisdom is a creation of God in Proverbs 8, it is unlikely that wisdom here is to be identified with Jesus Christ. The chapter unfolds in three cycles: After an introduction (1-3), wisdom makes an invitation (4, 5) and explains that she is noble, just, and true (6-9); she then makes another invitation (10) and explains that she is valuable (11-21); and finally, she tells how she preceded and delights in creation (22-31) before concluding with the third invitation (32-36).
n200082: tn Heb вАЬhead.вАЭ The word вАП„®÷є„Р„©„БвАО (ro Њsh, вАЬheadвАЭ) refers to the highest area or most important place in the elevated area. The contrast with chapter 7 is striking. There the wayward woman lurked at the corners in the street at night; here wisdom is at the highest point in the open places in view of all.
n200083: tn Heb вАЬat the house of the paths.вАЭ The вАЬhouseвАЭ is not literal here, but refers to where the paths meet (cf. ASV, NIV), that is, the вАЬcrossroadsвАЭ (so NAB, NRSV, NLT).
n200085: tn The cry is a very loud ringing cry that could not be missed. The term вАП„®÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (ranan) means вАЬto give a ringing cry.вАЭ It is often only a shrill sound that might come with a victory in battle, but its use in the psalms for praise shows that it also can have clear verbal content, as it does here. For wisdom to stand in the street and give such a ringing cry would mean that it could be heard by all. It was a proclamation.
n200086: tn Heb вАЬmen.вАЭ Although it might be argued in light of the preceding material that males would be particularly addressed by wisdom here, the following material indicates a more universal appeal. Cf. TEV, NLT вАЬto all of you.вАЭ
n200087: tn The verb вАЬcallsвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for the sake of style.
n200088: tn Heb вАЬsons of man.вАЭ Cf. NAB вАЬthe children of menвАЭ; NCV, NLT вАЬall peopleвАЭ; NRSV вАЬall that live.вАЭ
n200089: tn The imperative of вАП„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (bin) means вАЬto understand; to discern.вАЭ The call is for the simple to understand what wisdom is, not just to gain it.
n2000810: tn Heb вАЬheart.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬheartвАЭ) often functions metonymically for wisdom, understanding, discernment.
n2000811: tc The MT reads вАП„†÷∞„Т÷і„Щ„У÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (negidim) вАЬnobles.вАЭ HALOT interprets this as the plural form of the noun that lies behind the preposition вАП„†÷ґ„Т÷ґ„УвАО (neged), meaning вАЬcorrect, proper expressionsвАЭ (HALOT 667, s.v. вАП„†÷ґ„Т÷ґ„УвАО). The translation follows BHS in reading вАП„†÷∞„Т÷Є„У÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (negadim) вАЬnoble thingsвАЭ as a substantival adjective based on the same root.
n2000812: tn Heb вАЬopening of my lipsвАЭ (so KJV, NASB). The noun вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause, with the organ of speech put for what is said.
n2000813: tn Heb вАЬroof of the mouth.вАЭ This expression is a metonymy of cause for the activity of speaking.
n2000814: tn The word вАЬtruthвАЭ (вАП„Р÷±„Ю÷ґ„™вАО,  Њemet) is derived from the verbal root вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЯвАО ( Њaman) which means вАЬto be firm, trustworthy.вАЭ There are a number of derived nouns that have the sense of reliability: вАЬpillars,вАЭ вАЬmaster craftsman,вАЭ вАЬnurse,вАЭ вАЬguardian.вАЭ Modifiers related to this group of words include things like вАЬfaithful,вАЭ вАЬsurely,вАЭ вАЬtrulyвАЭ ( Њamen). In the derived stems the verb develops various nuances: The Niphal has the meanings of вАЬreliable, faithful, sure, steadfast,вАЭ and the Hiphil has the meaning вАЬbelieveвАЭ (i.e., consider something dependable). The noun вАЬtruthвАЭ means what is reliable or dependable, firm or sure.
n2000815: sn Wise lips detest wickedness; wisdom hates speaking wicked things. In fact, speaking truth results in part from detesting wickedness.
n2000816: tn Heb вАЬwickedness is an abomination to my lipsвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NRSV).
n2000817: tn The phrase could be rendered with an understood ellipsis: вАЬall the words of my mouth [are said] in righteousnessвАЭ; or the preposition could be interpreted as a beth essentiae: вАЬall the words of my mouth are righteousness.вАЭ
n2000818: sn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„™÷Ј„ЬвАО (patal) means вАЬto twist.вАЭ In the Niphal it means вАЬto wrestleвАЭ (to twist oneself). It was used in Gen 30:8 for the naming of Naphtali, with the motivation for the name from this verb: вАЬwith great struggling.вАЭ Here it describes speech that is twisted. It is a synonym for the next word, which means вАЬtwisted; crooked; perverse.вАЭ
n2000819: tn Heb вАЬfront of.вАЭ Describing the sayings as вАЬright in frontвАЭ means they are open, obvious, and clear, as opposed to words that might be twisted or perverse. The parallel word вАЬuprightвАЭ means вАЬstraight, smooth, right.вАЭ WisdomвАЩs teachings are in plain view and intelligible for those who find knowledge.
n2000820: tn Heb вАЬdiscipline.вАЭ The term refers to instruction that trains with discipline (e.g., Prov 1:2).
n2000821: tn Heb вАЬand notвАЭ (so KJV, NASB); NAB вАЬin preference to.вАЭ
n2000822: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷і„©÷∞„Б„Х„Х÷ЉвАО (yishevu, from вАП„©÷Є„Б„Х÷Є„ФвАО, shavah) can be rendered вАЬare not comparableвАЭ or with a modal nuance, вАЬcannot be comparedвАЭ with her.
n2000823: tn The verb вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ы÷Ј„†÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (shakanti) is a perfect form which should normally be past or perfective. Some of this rootвАЩs perfect forms follow stative morphology (though the imperfect forms consistently use the morphology of dynamic verbs). The meanings of some verbs drift across the stative vs. dynamic boundary over time. If interpreted as a stative verb, it would be present tense.
n2000824: tn The noun is вАЬshrewdness,вАЭ i.e., the right use of knowledge in special cases (see also the discussion in 1:4); cf. NLT вАЬgood judgment.вАЭ The word in this sentence is an adverbial accusative of specification.
n2000825:
tn This verb form is an imperfect, showing habitual action.
tc It has been reasonably proposed, based on Greek witnesses, that the verb can be read as a Niphal rather than a Qal. The proposal keeps the same consonants for this verb (but reads different vowels), however the Greek implies that the noun вАЬknowledgeвАЭ should be emended to a participle (requires adding a вАП„ЮвАО, [mem]). The meaning of this reading is вАЬI reveal myself (or вАЬam foundвАЭ) making discretion known.
n2000826:
tn The verb вАП„©÷Є„В„†÷µ„РвАО (sane Њ) means вАЬto hate.вАЭ In this sentence it functions nominally as the predicate. Fearing the Lord is hating evil.
sn The verb translated вАЬhateвАЭ has the basic idea of rejecting something spontaneously. For example, вАЬJacob have I loved, but Esau have I hatedвАЭ (Mal 1:2b, 3a). It frequently has the idea of disliking or loathing (as English does), but almost always with an additional aspect of rejection. To вАЬhate evilвАЭ is not only to dislike it, but to reject it and have nothing to do with it.
n2000827: tn Since вАП„Т÷µ÷Љ„Р÷Є„ФвАО (ge Њah, вАЬprideвАЭ) and вАП„Т÷Є÷Љ„Р„Х÷є„ЯвАО (ga Њon, вАЬarrogance; prideвАЭ) are both from the same verbal root вАП„Т÷Є÷Љ„Р÷Є„ФвАО (ga Њah, вАЬto rise upвАЭ), they should here be interpreted as one idea, forming a nominal hendiadys: вАЬarrogant pride.вАЭ
n2000828: tn Heb вАЬand a mouth of perverse things.вАЭ The word вАЬmouthвАЭ is a metonymy of cause for what is said; and the noun вАП„™÷Ј„Ф÷∞„§÷Љ÷ї„Ы„Х÷є„™вАО (tahpukhot, вАЬperverse thingsвАЭ) means destructive things (the related verb is used for the overthrowing of Sodom).
n2000829:
tc In the second half of v. 14 instead of вАП„Р÷≤„†÷і„ЩвАО ( Њani) the editors propose reading simply вАП„Ь÷і„ЩвАО (li) as the renderings in the LXX, Latin, and Syriac suggest. Then, in place of the вАП„Ь÷і„ЩвАО that comes in the same colon, read вАП„Х÷∞„Ь÷і„ЩвАО (veli). While the MT is a difficult reading, it can be translated as it is. It would be difficult to know exactly what the ancient versions were reading, because their translations could have been derived from either text. They represent an effort to smooth out the text.
tn Heb вАЬTo me [belong] counsel and sound wisdom.вАЭ The second colon in the verse has: вАЬI, understanding, to me might.вАЭ
sn In vv. 14-17 the pronouns come first and should receive greater prominenceвАФalthough it is not always easy to do this with English.
n2000830: tn The words вАЬby meвАЭ are understood to apply from the first line through the technique of ellipsis and double duty.
n2000831: tn The verb вАП„®÷Є„Ц÷Ј„ЯвАО (razan) means вАЬto be weighty; to be judicious; to be commanding.вАЭ It only occurs in the Qal active participle in the plural as a substantive, meaning вАЬpotentates; rulersвАЭ (e.g., Ps 1:1-3). Cf. KJV, ASV вАЬprincesвАЭ; NAB вАЬlawgivers.вАЭ
n2000832: sn This verb вАП„Щ÷∞„Ч÷є„І÷∞„І„Х÷ЉвАО (yekhoqequ) is related to the noun вАП„Ч÷є„ІвАО (khoq), which is a вАЬstatute; decree.вАЭ The verb is defined as вАЬto cut in; to inscribe; to decreeвАЭ (BDB 349 s.v. вАП„Ч÷Є„І÷Ј„ІвАО). The point the verse is making is that when these potentates decree righteousness, it is by wisdom. History records all too often that these rulers acted as fools and opposed righteousness (cf. Ps 2:1-3). But people in power need wisdom to govern the earth (e.g., Isa 11:1-4 which predicts how Messiah will use wisdom to do this very thing). The point is underscored with the paronomasia in v. 15 with вАЬkingsвАЭ and вАЬwill reignвАЭ from the same root, and then in v. 16 with both вАЬprincesвАЭ and вАЬruleвАЭ being cognate. The repetition of sounds and meanings strengthens the statements.
n2000833: tn The term вАЬandвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for the sake of smoothness and readability.
n2000834: tc Many of the MT mss read вАЬsovereigns [princes], all the judges of the earth.вАЭ The LXX has вАЬsovereignsвА¶rule the earth.вАЭ But the MT manuscript in the text has вАЬjudges of righteousness.вАЭ C. H. Toy suggests that the Hebrew here has assimilated Ps 148:11 in its construction (Proverbs [ICC], 167). The expression вАЬjudges of the earthвАЭ is what one would expect, but the more difficult and unexpected reading, the one scribes might change, would be вАЬjudges of righteousness.вАЭ If that reading stands, then it would probably be interpreted as using an attributive genitive.
n2000835:
tn The verb вАП„Р÷Є„Ф÷µ„СвАО ( Њahev, вАЬto loveвАЭ) is stative, so in the imperfect form it is future tense. It still states a general truth.
sn In contrast to the word for вАЬhateвАЭ (вАП„©÷Є„В„†÷µ„РвАО, sane Њ), the verb вАЬloveвАЭ (вАП„Р÷Є„Ф÷µ„СвАО,  Њahev) includes within it the idea of choosing spontaneously. So in this line вАЬlovingвАЭ and вАЬseekingвАЭ point out the means of finding wisdom.
n2000836: tn The two synonyms, вАЬthan gold, than fine goldвАЭ probably form a hendiadys here to express вАЬthe very finest gold.вАЭ
n2000837: tn The noun вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„С„Х÷Љ„Р÷Є„ФвАО (tevu Њah) means вАЬharvest, yield of crops, produceвАЭ and by extension вАЬprofitвАЭ (HALOT 1679, s.v.). The agricultural imagery is an implied metaphor (hypocatastasis) for the gains that wisdom produces in oneвАЩs life.
n2000838: tn The infinitive construct expressing the purpose of the preceding вАЬwalkвАЭ in the way of righteousness. These verses say that wisdom is always on the way of righteousness for the purposes of bestowing the same to those who find her. If sin is involved, then wisdom has not been followed.
n2000839: tn Heb вАЬand their treasuries I fill.вАЭ The imperfect verb expresses purposive modality because of the parallelism with the infinitive beginning the verse.
n2000840: tc The LXX adds at the end of this verse: вАЬIf I declare to you the things of daily occurrence, I will remember to recount the things of old.вАЭ
n2000841: tn There are two roots вАП„І÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО (qanah) in Hebrew, one meaning вАЬto possess,вАЭ and the other meaning вАЬto create.вАЭ The earlier English versions did not know of the second root, but suspected in certain places that a meaning like that was necessary (e.g., Gen 4:1; 14:19; Deut 32:6). Ugaritic confirmed that it was indeed another root. The older versions have the translation вАЬpossessвАЭ because otherwise it sounds like God lacked wisdom and therefore created it at the beginning. They wanted to avoid saying that wisdom was not eternal. Arius liked the idea of Christ as the wisdom of God and so chose the translation вАЬcreate.вАЭ Athanasius translated it, вАЬconstituted me as the head of creation.вАЭ The verb occurs twelve times in Proverbs with the meaning of вАЬto acquire,вАЭ but the Greek and the Syriac versions have the meaning вАЬcreate.вАЭ Although the idea is that wisdom existed before creation, the parallel ideas in these verses (вАЬappointed,вАЭ вАЬgiven birthвАЭ) argue for the translation of вАЬcreateвАЭ or вАЬestablishвАЭ (R. N. Whybray, вАЬProverbs 8:22-31 and Its Supposed Prototypes,вАЭ VT 15 [1965]: 504-14; and W. A. Irwin, вАЬWhere Will Wisdom Be Found?вАЭ JBL 80 [1961]: 133-42).
n2000842: tn Verbs of creation often involve double accusatives; here the double accusative involves the person (i.e., wisdom) and an abstract noun in construct (IBHS 174-75 ¬І10.2.3c).
n2000843:
tn Heb вАЬhis wayвАЭ (so KJV, NASB). The word вАЬwayвАЭ is an idiom (implied comparison) for the actions of God.
sn The claim of wisdom in this passage is that she was foundational to all that God would do.
n2000844: tn The MT reads вАП„†÷і„°÷Ј÷Љ„Ы÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (nissakhti), which would come from one of the homonymous roots вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (nasakh). The LXX reads бЉРќЄќµќЉќµќїќѓѕЙѕГќ≠ќљ ќЉќµ (ethemeli≈Нsen me, вАЬhe founded meвАЭ) suggesting вАП„†„Х÷є„°÷Ј„У÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (nosadti, вАЬI was founded, establishedвАЭ) from вАП„Щ÷Є„°÷Ј„УвАО (yasad, see HALOT 417, s.v.). BHS proposes вАП„†÷∞„°÷Ј„Ы÷є÷Љ„™÷і„ЩвАО (nesakkoti, вАЬI was shaped, wovenвАЭ), which uses the same consonants as the MT but is from the root вАП„°÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЪвАО (sakhakh). BDB created an entry for Ps 2:6 and Prov 8:23 (BDB s.v. III вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ЪвАО), citing the Akkadian verb nasaku, which it then related to the noun nasiku, вАЬprince.вАЭ BDBвАЩs proposed meaning is вАЬset, install,вАЭ however, this is not the meaning of the Akkadian verb and the noun is a West Semitic word brought into Akkadian (see CAD N2 15, s.v. nasaku A and 27, s.v. nasiku). HALOT lists this verb under II вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ЪвАО, вАЬto be woven, shapedвАЭ in the Niphal, and also calls II вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ЪвАО a by-form of вАП„°÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЪвАО (sakhakh) (see HALOT 703, s.v. II вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ЪвАО and 754, s.v. 2 вАП„°÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЪвАО). The Concise DCH suggests the possibilities that it is related to I вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ЪвАО (nasakh) вАЬbe poured out, i.e., emanateвАЭ or II вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ЪвАО (nasakh) вАЬbe woven, fashionedвАЭ (See The Concise Dictionary of Classical Hebrew 275, s.v. I вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ЪвАО and II вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ЪвАО). The root вАП„°÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЪвАО (sakhakh) is used in Ps 139:13 where it is parallel to вАП„І÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО (qanah, вАЬto createвАЭ) just as the verb here is parallel to вАП„І÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО in 8:22. The translation attempts to capture the notion of being вАЬcreated, fashioned, formedвАЭ in the two parallel verbs whether this verb is from II вАП„†÷Є„°÷Ј„ЪвАО or вАП„°÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЪвАО. Note that the parallel in 8:24 is being born, another verb of making.
n2000845: tn The verb вАЬexistedвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied in the translation in the light of the context.
n2000846: sn The summary statements just given are now developed in a lengthy treatment of wisdom as the agent of all creation. This verse singles out вАЬwatery deepsвАЭ (вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ф÷є„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО, tehomot) in its allusion to creation because the word in Genesis signals the condition of the world at the very beginning, and because in the ancient world this was something no one could control. Chaos was not there firstвАФwisdom was.
n2000847: tn The third parallel verb is вАП„Ч„Х÷є„Ь÷Є„Ь÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (kholalti), вАЬI was birthed (through labor pains).вАЭ Some (e.g., KJV, NAB, NASB, NRSV) translate it вАЬbrought forthвАЭвАФnot in the sense of being presented, but in the sense of being вАЬbegotten, given birth to.вАЭ Here is the strongest support for the translation of вАП„І÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО (qanah) as вАЬcreatedвАЭ in v. 22. The verb is not literal; it continues the perspective of the personification.
n2000849: tn This is not the common verb for being born (Niphal of вАП„Щ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„УвАО, yalad). The nuance of вАП„Ч„Х÷є„Ь÷Є„Ь÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (kholalti, the Polal of вАП„Ч÷і„Щ„ЬвАО, [khil]) emphasizes being birthed through labor pains.
n2000851:
tn Heb вАЬthe head of the soil of the world.вАЭ The noun вАП„®„Р÷є„©„БвАО (ro Њsh, вАЬheadвАЭ) can refer to the topmost of something or the first of something. The noun вАП„Ґ÷Є„§÷Є„®вАО ( њafar, вАЬdirt clodsвАЭ) can refer to dust (вАЬfine dry top soilвАЭ), loose earth, or soil (HALOT 862, s.v.).
tc BHS proposes вАП„У÷ґ„©÷ґ„Б„РвАО (deshe Њ, вАЬgrassвАЭ) instead of вАП„®„Р÷є„©„БвАО which assumes both the common confusion of вАП„УвАО (dalet) and вАП„®вАО (resh), as well as the reversal of the final two letters. This would mean вАЬthe vegetation of the worldвАЩs soil.вАЭ
n2000852: sn The infinitive construct вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ч„Х÷Љ„І„Х÷євАО (bekhuqo, вАЬto cut; to engrave; to markвАЭ) and the noun вАП„Ч„Х÷Љ„ТвАО (khug, вАЬhorizon; circleвАЭ) form a paronomasia in the line.
n2000853: tc The MT has the Qal infinitive вАП„С÷Ј÷Љ„Ґ÷≤„Ц„Х÷є„ЦвАО (ba њazoz), вАЬwhen [they] grew strongвАЭ (cf. NASB вАЬwhen [they] became fixedвАЭ). The LXX, supported by the Syriac, Targum, and Vulgate, implies the Piel infinitive plus pronominal suffix вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„Ц÷∞÷Љ„Ц„Х÷євАО (be њazzezo) вАЬwhen he made [them] strong.вАЭ The proposed reading suggests metathesis (switching positions) of the last two consonants. In addition the parallel to the infinitive beginning the verse supports the pronominal suffix and the meaning of the verb (cf. NIV вАЬwhen heвА¶ fixed securelyвАЭ; NLT вАЬwhen he establishedвАЭ).
n2000854: tn Heb вАЬwhen he set his decree on the sea.вАЭ
n2000856: tn This preterite verb provides the concluding statement for the temporal clauses as well as the parallel to v. 27 вАЬI was there.вАЭ
n2000857: tn Or вАЬI was beside him faithfully,вАЭ or вАЬI was beside him, the master craftsman.вАЭ The interpretation of this line depends on вАП„Р÷Є„Ю„Х÷є„ЯвАО ( Њamon) for which there are three main proposals. The majority of translations understand II вАП„Р÷Є„Ю„Х÷є„ЯвАО to be a craftsman (HALOT 62, s.v.), referring to wisdom (cf. ASV, NASB, NIV [1973], ESV, NRSV, NKJV). C. Z. Rogers has argued that вАЬcraftsmanвАЭ is in apposition to вАЬhim,вАЭ describing the Lord (C. Z. Rogers, вАЬThe Meaning and Significance of the Hebrew Word вАП„Р÷Є„Ю„Х÷є„ЯвАО in Prov 8, 30вАЭ ZAW 109, [1997] 208-21). It is also understood as вАЬnursing childвАЭ (cf. NCV, Darby, KJV), assuming it to be вАП„Р÷Є„Ю„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО ( Њamun) a passive participle of II вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЯвАО ( Њaman, see HALOT 24, s.v.). The image of a child is consistent with the previous figure of being вАЬgiven birth toвАЭ (vv. 24, 25). It may also derive from I вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЯвАО ( Њaman) meaning вАЬfaithfulвАЭ (see HALOT 63, s.v. I вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЯвАО, and 62 s.v. I вАП„Р÷µ„Ю„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО or вАП„Р÷Є„Ю„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО) (cf. NIV 2011 вАЬconstantlyвАЭ). R. B. Y. Scott chooses вАЬfaithfulвАЭ (вАЬWisdom in Creation: The вАШAmon of Proverbs 8:30, вАЭ VT 10 [1960]: 213-23). However, вАЬcraftsmanвАЭ has the most support (LXX, Vulgate, Syriac, Tg. Prov 8:30, Song 7:1; Jer 52:15; also P. W. Skehan, вАЬStructures in Poems on Wisdom: Proverbs 8 and Sirach 24, вАЭ CBQ 41 [1979]: 365-79).
n2000858: tn The word is a plural of intensification for вАЬdelightвАЭ; it describes wisdom as the object of delight. The LXX has the suffix; the Hebrew does not.
n2000859: tn The two words are synonymous in general and so could be taken to express a superlative ideaвАФthe вАЬwhole worldвАЭ (cf. NIV, NCV). But вАП„™÷Љ÷µ„С÷µ„ЬвАО (tevel) also means the inhabited world, and so the construct may be interpreted as a partitive genitive.
n2000864: tn The construction uses two imperatives joined with the vav (вАП„ХвАО); this is a volitive sequence in which result or consequence is being expressed.
n2000866: tn The form вАП„Ь÷і„©„Б÷∞„І÷є„УвАО (lishqod) is the infinitive construct serving epexegetically in the sentence. It explains how the person will listen to wisdom.
n2000868: tn Heb вАЬat the posts of my doorsвАЭ (so KJV, ASV).
n2000869: tc The Kethib reads the verb as a plural participle: вАЬthe one who finds me are finders of life.вАЭ The LXX reads a plural subject: вАЬthose who find me.вАЭ But the Hebrew Qere reads a singular perfect verb. The next verb is a preterite, which commonly follows the perfect but very rarely a participle. The perfect form of a dynamic verb should be translated as past or perfective.
n2000870:
tn The preterite with vav (вАП„ХвАО) consecutive continues the time frame of the perfect verb that came before it.
sn The sage uses these verb forms in contrast with the following verse, which is present tense. The antithetic parallelism contrasts not just the subject (who finds vs. who misses) and the verb (to find vs. to harm) but also the state of the outcome. This person found life and continues in the benefit: вАЬhad found life.вАЭ
n2000871: tn Heb вАЬthe one sinning [against] me.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„Ш÷Є„РвАО (khata Њ, вАЬto sin, to errвАЭ) forms a contrast with вАЬfindвАЭ in the previous verse, and so has its basic meaning of вАЬfailing to find, miss.вАЭ
n2000872:
tn The Qal active participle functions verbally here. The word stresses both social and physical harm and violence.
sn Brings harm. While the previous verse used past time verbs, the sage employs the participle here as an ongoing activity. Whoever tries to live without wisdom is inviting all kinds of disaster into his life.
n2000874: tn The basic idea of the verb вАП„©÷Є„В„†÷µ„РвАО (sane Њ, вАЬto hateвАЭ) is that of rejection. Its antonym is also used in the line, вАЬlove,вАЭ which has the idea of choosing. So not choosing (i.e., hating) wisdom amounts to choosing (i.e., loving) death.
n200091: sn Chapter 9 forms the conclusion of the lengthy introduction to the book. Both wisdom and folly will make their final appeals; and both appeal to the simpletons. Wisdom offers life with no mention of pleasure; folly offers pleasure with no mention of death. The first twelve verses concern accepting wisdom: the invitation of wisdom (1-6), the description of the responses (7-11), and the consequence (12). Verses 13-18 concern accepting folly: the invitation (13-17) and the consequence (18).
n200092: sn Wisdom is personified as a wise woman. She has prepared a house and established it on seven pillars. This is a reference to the habitable world (e.g., 8:31). For the equation of the house and the world, e.g., 8:29; Job 38:6; Ps 104:5 (also G. Bostr√ґm, Proverbiastudien [LU√Е], 1-14). The вАЬseven pillarsвАЭ have been variously interpreted, but since seven is a number for completeness and sacredness, the idea seems to be that wisdom produced a perfect world.
n200093: tn Heb вАЬshe has slaughtered her slaughter [animals].вАЭ English does not prefer to use a cognate verb and noun for butchering an animal in food preparation. Cf. KJV вАЬhath killed her beastsвАЭ; NAB вАЬhas dressed her meatвАЭ; NASB вАЬhas prepared her food.вАЭ
n200094: sn Wisdom has prepared a sumptuous banquet in this house and sends out her maids to call the simple to come and eat (M. Lichtenstein, вАЬThe Banquet Motif in Keret and in Proverbs 9, вАЭ JANESCU 1 [1968/69]: 19-31). The figures of meat and wine represent the good teaching of wisdom that will be palatable and profitable (implied comparisons). Cf. Isaiah 55:1-2 and John 6:51, 55 for similar uses of the figures. The idea of mixing wine could refer to the practice of mixing wine with spices or with water (as the LXX text assumes; e.g., Prov 23:30; Isa 5:22). Mixed wine was the most intoxicating; thus, her wisdom is attractive. All the imagery lets the simple know that what wisdom has to offer is marvelous.
n200095: tn The Hebrew imperfect can be a general present. The prior perfect verbs tell what she has done in preparation, while the imperfect tells what she now does. But it is also possible, given the past time context of the previous verbs, that the imperfect should be understood as past habitual, вАЬshe would call,вАЭ or as a preterite (without the vav consecutive), вАЬshe called.вАЭ This would be in line with the next verse which uses a perfect, вАЬshe has said.вАЭ
n200096: tn The text uses two synonymous terms in construct to express the superlative degree.
n200097: tn Heb вАЬ[As for one] lacking of mindвАФshe has said to him.вАЭ The pronominal suffix is a resumptive pronoun, meaning, вАЬshe has said to the [person] lacking of mind.вАЭ
n200098: tn The Hebrew word вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev) means both the вАЬheartвАЭ and the вАЬmind.вАЭ By metonymy, the mind stands for understanding or judgment.
n200099:
tn The Hebrew switches to the perfect verb form to introduce the speech in the following verses. It lets us know what her message has been. It is possible that the imperfect verb in the previous verse should be understood as a past habitual, вАЬshe would callвАЭ or as a preterite (without the vav consecutive), вАЬshe called.вАЭ
tc The LXX supports the reading of the verb as a perfect. But at the similar construction in 9:16, the LXX reads a participle, which would be present time. The consonants are the same for both forms. It is possible that the verb should be read as a participle in both verses. The present tense could certainly fit the context.
n2000910: tn The construction features a cognate accusative (verb and noun from same root). The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) has the partitive use вАЬsomeвАЭ (GKC 380 ¬І119.m).
n2000911:
tn The final verb actually stands in a relative clause although the relative pronoun is not present; it modifies вАЬwine.вАЭ
sn The expressions вАЬeatвАЭ and вАЬdrinkвАЭ carry the implied comparison forward; they mean that the simple are to appropriate the teachings of wisdom.
n2000912: tn There are two ways to take this word: either as вАЬfoolsвАЭ or as вАЬfoolish ways.вАЭ The spelling for вАЬfoolishnessвАЭ in v. 13 differs from this spelling, and so some have taken that as an indicator that this should be вАЬfools.вАЭ But this could still be an abstract plural here as in 1:22. Either the message is to forsake fools (i.e., bad company; cf. KJV, TEV) or forsake foolishness (cf. NAB, NASB, NIV, NCV, NRSV, NLT).
n2000913: tn The two imperatives are joined with vav; this is a volitive sequence in which result or consequence is expressed.
n2000914: tn The verb means вАЬgo straight, go on, advanceвАЭ or вАЬgo straight on in the way of understandingвАЭ (BDB 80 s.v. вАП„Р÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„®вАО).
n2000915: tn The active participle вАП„Щ÷є„°÷µ„®вАО (yoser) describes one who tries to correct by means of instruction and discipline; it is paralleled by the Hiphil participle which refers to someone who rebukes or reproves another. Anyone trying this on these types of people would be inviting trouble.
n2000917: tn The word means вАЬdishonorвАЭ or вАЬdisgrace.вАЭ It is paralleled with вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„Ю„Х÷євАО (mumo), translated вАЬabuse.вАЭ The latter term means вАЬblemish,вАЭ although some would emend the text to read вАЬreproach.вАЭ The MT is figurative but not impossible to interpret: Whoever tries to rebuke a wicked person will receive only insults and perhaps physical attack.
n2000918: tn The verb вАЬreceivesвАЭ is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity and smoothness.
n2000919: tn In view of the expected response for reproof, the text now uses a negated jussive to advise against the attempt. This is paralleled antithetically by the imperative in the second colon. This imperative is in an understood conditional clause: вАЬif you reprove a wise person.вАЭ
n2000920: tn Heb вАЬlest he hate you.вАЭ The particle вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ЯвАО (pen, вАЬlestвАЭ) expresses fear or precaution (R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 79, ¬І476). The antonyms вАЬloveвАЭ and вАЬhateвАЭ suggest that the latter means вАЬrejectвАЭ and the former means вАЬchoosing and embracing.вАЭ
n2000921: tn Heb вАЬGive to the wise.вАЭ The object, вАЬinstructionвАЭ (or possibly rebuke), is implied. Most translations either supply the object (NIV, NLT, Holman) or change the verb to вАЬinstructвАЭ (NASB, ESV, NKJV, NRSV).
n2000922: sn The parallelism shows what Proverbs will repeatedly stress, that the wise person is the righteous person.
n2000923: tn The Hiphil verb normally means вАЬto cause to know, make known,вАЭ but here the context suggests вАЬto teachвАЭ (so many English versions).
n2000924: tn The term вАЬhisвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied for the sake of smoothness and clarity.
n2000925: sn The difference between вАП„™÷∞÷Љ„Ч÷і„Ь÷Ј÷Љ„™вАО (tekhillat) here and вАП„®÷µ„Р„©÷і„Б„Щ„™вАО (re Њshit) of 1:7, if there is any substantial difference, is that this term refers to the starting point of wisdom, and the earlier one indicates the primary place of wisdom (K&D 16:202).
n2000927: tn Heb вАЬknowledge of the Holy OneвАЭ (so ASV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV). Knowledge of God implies an understanding of his character and ways and acknowledging or adopting his values. The noun вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО (da њat), traditionally taken as вАЬknowledgeвАЭ from the root вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ, вАЬto knowвАЭ), may also be related to the root вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (da њah, вАЬto seek, request,вАЭ see NIDOTTE 959, s.v.). The homonym of вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО (da њat) is not widely recognized, but the meaning of seeking or having concern for God (and his ways) would fit the context well. Cf. Hos 6:3 for an example of the verb вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО.
n2000928: tn The word is in the plural in the Hebrew (literally вАЬholy onesвАЭ; KJV вАЬthe holyвАЭ). It was translated вАЬholy menвАЭ in Tg. Prov 9:10. But it probably was meant to signify the majestic nature of the Lord. As J. H. Greenstone says, he is вАЬall-holyвАЭ (Proverbs, 94). This is an example of the plural of majesty, one of the honorific uses of the plural (see IBHS 122-23 ¬І7.4.3b).
n2000929: tn The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) here may have the causal sense (R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 45, ¬І247), although it could also be means (Williams, 44, ¬І243).
n2000930: tn The verb вАП„Х÷∞„Щ„Х÷є„°÷і„Щ„§„Х÷ЉвАО (veyosifu) is the Hiphil imperfect, third masculine plural, but because there is no expressed subject the verb may be taken as a passive.
n2000931: tn The text simply has the preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) with a suffix, but this will be the use of the preposition classified as вАЬinterest,вАЭ either for advantage or disadvantage (R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 48-49, ¬І271).
n2000932: tn The word вАЬifвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is understood from the first half of the line through the convention of ellipsis and double duty.
n2000933: tn Here the conjunction vav begins a second conditional sentence, laying down an antithetical condition. It uses the perfect form of a dynamic verb in contrast to the first verb (the stative perfect for present time). While it is advantageous to be or become wise at any time, once you have mocked, there will be a consequence for it.
n2000934: tn The use of the imperfect tense here could be the simple future tense (cf. NASB, NRSV вАЬyouвА¶will bear itвАЭ), but the obligatory nuance is more appropriateвАФвАЬyou must bear it.вАЭ These words anticipate JamesвАЩ warnings that the words we speak will haunt us through life (e.g., James 3:1-12).
n2000935: tc The LXX has an addition: вАЬForsake folly, that you may reign forever; and seek discretion and direct understanding in knowledge.вАЭ
n2000936: tn Heb вАЬa woman of foolishness.вАЭ This could be translated as вАЬfoolish woman,вАЭ taking the genitive as attributive (cf. KJV, ASV, NRSV). But in view of the contrast with the personification of wisdom, this word probably also represents a personification and so can be taken as a genitive of apposition, the woman who is folly, or вАЬthe woman, FollyвАЭ (cf. NIV). For clarity and stylistic reasons the word вАЬcalledвАЭ has been supplied in the translation.
n2000937: tn The meaning of the word comes close to вАЬriotous.вАЭ W. McKane describes her as restless and rootless (Proverbs [OTL], 366).
n2000938: tn The noun means вАЬfoolishnessвАЭ (cf. KJV вАЬsimpleвАЭ; NAB вАЬinaneвАЭ). Here it could be classified as a metonymy of adjunct, or as a predictive apposition (when a substantive is used in place of a noun; see R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 15, ¬І67).
n2000939: tn The ignorance here in Proverbs must be moral ignorance. But see D. W. Thomas for the idea that the verb means вАЬbecome still,вАЭ вАЬbe at rest,вАЭ yielding here the idea of restless (вАЬA Note on вАП„С÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„Щ÷Є„У÷∞„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО in Proverbs 913,вАЭ JTS 4 [1953]: 23-24).
n2000940: tc The text of v. 13 has been difficult for translators. The MT has, вАЬThe foolish woman is boisterous, simplicity, and knows not what.вАЭ The LXX reads, вАЬA foolish and impudent woman comes to lack a morsel, she who knows not shame.вАЭ The Syriac has, вАЬa woman lacking in discretion, seductive.вАЭ Tg. Prov 9:13 translates it, вАЬa foolish woman and a gadabout, ignorant, and she knows not good.вАЭ The Vulgate has, вАЬa woman foolish and noisy, and full of wiles, and knowing nothing at all.вАЭ
n2000941: tn The infinitive construct вАЬcalling outвАЭ functions epexegetically in the sentence, explaining how the previous action was accomplished.
n2000942: tn The term вАЬherвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied for the sake of clarity and smoothness.
n2000943: tn The noun is a genitive of location after the construct participle. Its parallel word is also an adverbial accusative of location.
n2000944: tn The participle modifies the participle in the first colon. To describe the passers-by in this context as those вАЬwho go straightвАЭ means that they are quiet and unwary.
n2000945: tn The word вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev; вАЬheart, mindвАЭ). By metonymy, the mind stands for understanding or judgment.
n2000946:
tc The LXX reads вАЬshe exhorts sayingвАЭ a present indicative plus a participle. This implies a verb missing in the Hebrew and reading the vav plus perfect verb вАП„Х÷∞„Р÷Є„Ю÷∞„®÷Є„ФвАО (ve Њamerah, вАЬand has saidвАЭ) as a participle вАП„Х÷∞„Р÷є„Ю÷∞„®÷Є„ФвАО (ve Њomerah, вАЬand saysвАЭ). The participle would be present time. The consonants are the same for both forms and the present tense could certainly fit the context. The loss of another verb might explain the presence of the conjunction vav beginning the form.
tn Heb вАЬAnd [as for one] lacking of mindвАФand she has said to him.вАЭ The expression is almost identical to v. 4, except this verse adds the conjunction vav twice. The parallel is deliberate, showing the competing appeals for those passing by.
n2000947: sn The offer is not wine and meat (which represented wisdom), but water that is stolen. The вАЬwaterвАЭ will seem sweeter than wine because it is stolenвАФthe idea of getting away with something exciting appeals to the baser instincts. In Proverbs the water imagery was introduced earlier in 5:15-19 as sexual activity with the adulteress, which would seem at the moment more enjoyable than learning wisdom. Likewise bread will be drawn into this analogy in 30:20. So the вАЬcalling outвАЭ is similar to that of wisdom, but what is being offered is very different.
n2000948: tn Heb вАЬbread of secrecies.вАЭ It could mean вАЬbread [eaten in] secret places,вАЭ a genitive of location; or it could mean вАЬbread [gained through] secrets,вАЭ a genitive of source, the secrecies being metonymical for theft. The latter makes a better parallelism in this verse, for bread (= sexually immoral behavior) gained secretly would be like stolen water.
n2000950: sn The вАЬdeadвАЭ are the Rephaim, the вАЬshadesвАЭ or dead persons who lead a shadowy existence in Sheol (e.g., Prov 2:18-19; Job 3:13-19; Ps 88:5; Isa 14:9-11). This approximates an вАЬas-ifвАЭ motif of wisdom literature: The ones ensnared in folly are as good as in Hell. See also Ptah-hotepвАЩs sayings (ANET 412-414).
n2000951:
tc The LXX adds to the end of v. 18: вАЬBut turn away, linger not in the place, neither set your eye on her: for thus will you go through alien water, but abstain from alien water, drink not from an alien fountain, that you may live long, that years of life may be added to you.вАЭ
sn The text has вАЬin the depths of SheolвАЭ (вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷і„Ю÷∞„І÷µ„Щ „©„Б÷∞„Р„Х÷є„ЬвАО, be њimqe she Њol). The parallelism stresses that those who turn to this way of life are ignorant and doomed. It may signal a literal death lying ahead in the not too distant future, but it is more likely an analogy. The point is that the life of folly, a life of undisciplined, immoral, riotous living, runs counter to GodвАЩs appeal for wisdom and leads to ruin. That is the broad way that leads to destruction.
n200101: sn Beginning with ch. 10 there is a difference in the form of the material contained in the book of Proverbs. No longer are there long admonitions, but the actual proverbs, short aphorisms dealing with right or wrong choices. Other than a few similar themes grouped together here and there, there is no arrangement to the material as a whole. It is a long collection of approximately 400 proverbs.
n200103: tn The imperfect tense describes progressive or habitual action, translated here with an English present tense. These fit the nature of proverbs which are general maxims, and not necessarily absolutes or universal truths. One may normally expect to find what the proverb notes, and one should live according to its instructions in the light of those expectations, but one should not be surprised if from time to time there is an exception. The fact that there may be an exception does not diminish the need to live by the sayings.
n200105: tn Heb вАЬgrief of his mother.вАЭ The noun вАЬgriefвАЭ is in construct, and вАЬmotherвАЭ is an objective genitive. The saying declares that the consequences of wisdom or folly affects the parents.
n200106: tn Heb вАЬtreasures of wickednessвАЭ (so KJV, ASV); NASB вАЬIll-gotten gainsвАЭ; TEV вАЬWealth that you get by dishonesty.вАЭ
n200107: sn The term вАЬrighteousnessвАЭ here means honesty (cf. TEV). Wealth has limited value even if gained honestly, but honesty delivers from mortal danger.
n200108: tn Heb вАЬdeath.вАЭ This could refer to literal death, but it is probably figurative here for mortal danger or ruin.
n200109: tn Heb вАЬdoes not allowвА¶to go hungry.вАЭ The expression вАЬThe Lord does not allow the appetite of the righteous to go hungryвАЭ is an example of tapeinosisвАФa figurative expression stated in the negative to emphasize the positive: The Lord satisfies the appetite of the righteous.
n2001010: tn The term вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) means вАЬsoulвАЭ but its root meaning is вАЬthroatвАЭ and it has a broad range of meanings; here it denotes вАЬappetiteвАЭ (BDB 660 s.v. 5.a; see, e.g., Pss 63:6; 107:9; Prov 27:7; Isa 56:11; 58:10; Jer 50:19; Ezek 7:19). The term could denote вАЬdesireвАЭ (BDB 660 s.v. 6.a) which would include the inner urge for success. By contrast, the wicked live unfulfilled livesвАФas far as spiritual values are concerned.
n2001011: tn Heb вАЬthrusts awayвАЭ (cf. ASV, NASB); NLT вАЬrefuses to satisfy.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ф÷Є„У÷Ј„£вАО (hadaf) means вАЬto thrust away; to push; to drive,вАЭ either to depose or reject (BDB 213 s.v.).
n2001012: tn This verse contrasts the вАЬappetiteвАЭ of the righteous with the вАЬcravingвАЭ of the wicked. This word вАП„Ф÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷Ј„ФвАО (havvah, вАЬcravingвАЭ) means вАЬdesireвАЭ often in a bad sense, as вАШthe desire of the wicked,вАЭ which could not be wholesome (Ps 52:9).
n2001013: tn Heb вАЬa palm of slackness.вАЭ The genitive noun вАП„®÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (remiyyah, вАЬslacknessвАЭ) functions as an attributive adjective: вАЬa slack palmвАЭ (BDB 941 s.v.). The term вАП„Ы÷Ј„£вАО (khaf, вАЬpalmвАЭ) is a synecdoche of part (= palm) for the whole person (= one who works with his hands). The hand is emphasized because it is the instrument of physical labor. The вАЬslack handвАЭ is contrasted with the вАЬdiligent hand.вАЭ A slack hand refers to a lazy worker or careless work that such hands produce. See N. C. Habel, вАЬWisdom, Wealth, and Poverty Paradigms in the Book of Proverbs,вАЭ BiBh 14 (1988): 28-49.
n2001014:
tc The MT reads вАП„®÷Є„Р„©„БвАО (ra Њsh, вАЬpoorвАЭ) which is the plene spelling of вАП„®÷Є„©„БвАО (rash, вАЬpoor [person]вАЭ; HALOT 1229-30 s.v. вАП„®÷µ„Щ„©„БвАО). Both Tg. Prov 10:4 and LXX reflect an alternate vocalization вАП„®÷і„Щ„©„БвАО (rish, вАЬpovertyвАЭ) which is from the same root, and essentially means the same thing.
tn Heb вАЬcauses poverty.вАЭ The expression is literally, вАЬthe palm of slackness causes poverty.вАЭ
n2001015: tn Heb вАЬbut the hand of the diligentвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NRSV). The genitive noun вАП„Ч÷Є„®„Х÷Љ„¶÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (kharutsim, вАЬdiligenceвАЭ) functions as an attributive adjective: вАЬa diligent hand.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ч÷Є„®„Х÷Љ„¶÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (kharutsim) uses the plural form because the plural is often used for abstract moral qualities. The term вАП„Щ÷Є„УвАО (yad, вАЬhandвАЭ) is a synecdoche of part (= вАЬhandвАЭ) for the whole person (= вАЬthe one who works with his handsвАЭ). The hand is emphasized because it is the instrument of physical labor.
n2001016: tn Heb вАЬmakes richвАЭ (so NASB, NRSV). The Hiphil verb is used in a causative sense; literally, вАЬthe hand of the diligent makes rich.вАЭ
n2001017: tn The direct object вАЬcropsвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is implied by the verb; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of smoothness.
n2001018: tn Heb вАЬprudent.вАЭ The term вАП„Ю÷Ј„©÷∞„В„Ы÷і÷Љ„Щ„ЬвАО (maskil) refers to a wise and so successful person. He seizes the opportunity, knowing the importance of the season.
n2001019: sn The term вАЬsleepsвАЭ is figurative, an implied comparison that has become idiomatic (like the contemporary English expression вАЬasleep on the jobвАЭ). It means that this individual is lazy or oblivious to the needs of the hour.
n2001021: sn The word вАЬblessingsвАЭ has the sense of gifts, enrichments, that is, the rewards or the results of being righteous. The blessings come either from the people the righteous deal with, or from God. CEV understands the blessings as praise for good behavior (вАЬEveryone praises good peopleвАЭ).
n2001022: tn Heb вАЬthe mouth.вАЭ The term вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (peh, вАЬmouthвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of cause for speech.
n2001023: tn Heb вАЬcovers.вАЭ Behind the speech of the wicked is aggressive violence (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 422).
n2001024: tn The syntax of this line is ambiguous. The translation takes вАЬthe mouth of the wickedвАЭ as the nominative subject and вАЬviolenceвАЭ as the accusative direct object; however, the subject might be вАЬviolence,вАЭ hence: вАЬviolence covers the mouth of the wickedвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV, NIV).
n2001025: sn вАЬMemoryвАЭ (вАП„Ц÷µ„Ы÷ґ„®вАО, zekher) and вАЬnameвАЭ are often paired as synonyms. вАЬMemoryвАЭ in this sense has to do with reputation, fame. OneвАЩs reputation will be good or bad by righteousness or wickedness respectively.
n2001026: tn Heb вАЬname.вАЭ The term вАЬnameвАЭ often functions as a metonymy of association for reputation (BDB 1028 s.v. вАП„©„Б÷µ„ЭвАО 2.b).
n2001027:
tn The editors of BHS suggest a reading вАЬwill be cursedвАЭ to make a better parallelism, but the reading of the MT is more striking as a metaphor.
sn To say the wickedвАЩs name will rot means that the name will be obliterated from memory (Exod 17:14; Deut 25:19), leaving only a bad memory for a while.
n2001028: tn Heb вАЬthe wise of mind.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) functions as a genitive of specification: вАЬwise in the mindвАЭ or вАЬwise-minded.вАЭ Cf. вАЬwise heartedвАЭ NASB, ESV, NRSV; вАЬwise in heartвАЭ KJV, NIV. The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev) represents the person in this case (a synecdoche of part for the whole) because it is the seat of thinking and wisdom.
n2001030: tn Heb вАЬfool of lips.вАЭ The phrase is a genitive of specification: вАЬa fool in respect to lips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause (= lips) for effect (= speech). This person talks foolishness; he is too busy talking to pay attention to instruction.
n2001031: tn The Niphal verb вАП„Ь÷Є„С÷Ј„ШвАО (lavat) means вАЬto be thrust down [or, away]вАЭ; that is, вАЬto be ruined; to fallвАЭ or вАЬto stumbleвАЭ (e.g., Hos 4:14). The fool who refuses to listen to adviceвАФbut abides by his own standards which he freely expressesвАФwill suffer the predicaments that he creates.
n2001032: tn Heb вАЬhe who walks.вАЭ The idiom is used widely in both OT and NT for conduct, behavior, or lifestyle.
n2001033: sn вАЬIntegrityвАЭ here means вАЬblamelessвАЭ in conduct. Security follows integrity, because the lifestyle is blameless. The righteous is certain of the course to be followed and does not fear retribution from man or God.
n2001035: tn Heb вАЬhe who perverts his waysвАЭ (so NASB); NIV вАЬwho takes crooked pathsвАЭ (NLT similar). The Piel participle вАП„Ю÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„І÷Љ÷µ„©„БвАО (me њaqqesh) means вАЬmake crooked; twisted; perverse.вАЭ It is stronger than simply taking crooked paths; it refers to perverting the ways. The one who is devious will not get away with it.
n2001036: tn The participle (вАП„І÷Є„®÷Ј„•вАО, qarats) describes a person who habitually вАЬwinksвАЭ his eye maliciously as a secretive sign to those conspiring evil (Prov 6:13). This is a comparison rather than a contrast. Devious gestures are grievous, but not as ruinous as foolish talk. Both are to be avoided.
n2001040: tn Heb вАЬthe fool of lipsвАЭ; cf. NASB вАЬa babbling fool.вАЭ The phrase is a genitive of specification: вАЬa fool in respect to lips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause (= lips) for effect (= speech). The word for fool (вАП„Р÷ґ„Х÷і„Щ„ЬвАО,  Њevil) refers to someone who despises knowledge and discernment.
n2001041: tn Heb вАЬmouth.вАЭ The word вАЬmouthвАЭ is metonymy of cause, representing what the righteous say and teach.
n2001042: tn Heb вАЬa fountain of life is the mouth of the righteousвАЭ (NAB similar). The subject (вАЬa fountain of lifeвАЭ) and the predicate (вАЬthe mouth of the righteousвАЭ) in the Hebrew text are reversed in the present translation (as in most English versions) for the sake of clarity and smoothness. The idea of this metaphor, вАЬthe fountain of life,вАЭ may come from Ps 36:9 (e.g., also Prov 13:14; 14:27; 16:22). What the righteous say is beneficial to life or life-giving. Their words are life-giving but the words of the wicked are violent. See R. B. Y. Scott, вАЬWise and Foolish, Righteous and Wicked,вАЭ VT 29 (1972): 145-65.
n2001043: tn Heb вАЬthe mouth.вАЭ The term вАП„§÷ґ÷Љ„ФвАО (peh, вАЬmouthвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of cause for speech.
n2001044: tn Heb вАЬcovers.вАЭ Behind the speech of the wicked is aggressive violence (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 422).
n2001045: tn The syntax of this line is ambiguous. The translation takes вАЬthe mouth of the wickedвАЭ as the nominative subject and вАЬviolenceвАЭ as the accusative direct object; however, the subject might be вАЬviolence,вАЭ hence: вАЬviolence covers the mouth of the wicked.вАЭ
n2001046: sn This contrasts the wicked motivated by hatred (animosity, rejection) with the righteous motivated by love (kind acts, showing favor).
n2001047: sn Love acts like forgiveness. Hatred looks for and exaggerates faults, but love seeks ways to make sins disappear (e.g., 1 Pet 4:8).
n2001048: tn Heb вАЬon the lipsвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NRSV). The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause for the words spoken by the lips.
n2001049: tn Heb вАЬthe one who is discerning.вАЭ The term вАЬdiscerningвАЭ describes someone who is critically perceptive and has understanding. He can be relied on to say things that are wise.
n2001050: tn Heb вАЬthe one lacking of mind.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) is used as a metonymy of association for what one does with the mind (i.e., thinking), and so refers to discernment, wisdom, good sense.
n2001051: tn Heb вАЬa rod is for the back of the one lacking heart.вАЭ The term вАП„©÷µ„Б„С÷ґ„ШвАО (shevet, вАЬrodвАЭ) functions figuratively: synecdoche of specific (= rod of discipline) for general (= discipline in general). The term вАП„Т÷Љ÷µ„ХвАО (gev, вАЬbackвАЭ) is a synecdoche of part (= back) for the whole (= person as a whole). The back is emphasized because it was the object of physical corporeal discipline. This proverb is not limited in its application to physical corporeal punishment because the consequences of foolishness may come in many forms, physical corporeal discipline being only one form.
n2001053: sn The verb вАП„¶÷Є„§÷Ј„ЯвАО (tsafan, вАЬto store up; to treasureвАЭ) may mean (1) the wise acquire and do not lose wisdom (cf. NAB, NIV, TEV), or (2) they do not tell all that they know (cf. NCV), that is, they treasure it up for a time when they will need it. The fool, by contrast, talks without thinking.
n2001054: tn Heb вАЬthe mouth of foolishnessвАЭ; cf. NRSV, NLT вАЬthe babbling of a fool.вАЭ The term вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (peh, вАЬmouthвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of cause for speech. The genitive вАП„Р÷ґ„Х÷і„Щ„ЬвАО ( Њevil, вАЬfoolishnessвАЭ) functions as an attributive adjective: вАЬa foolish mouthвАЭ = foolish speech.
n2001055: tn Heb вАЬnear destruction.вАЭ The words of the fool that are uttered without wise forethought may invite imminent ruin (e.g., James 3:13-18). See also Ptah-hotep and Amenemope in ANET 414 and 423.
n2001056: tn Heb вАЬis.вАЭ This expression, вАЬa rich manвАЩs wealth is his strong city,вАЭ is a metaphor. The comparative particle вАЬlikeвАЭ is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity and smoothness.
n2001057: tn Heb вАЬa city of his strength.вАЭ The genitive вАП„Ґ÷є„ЦвАО ( њoz, вАЬstrengthвАЭ) functions as an attributive genitive: вАЬstrong cityвАЭ = вАЬfortified city.вАЭ This phrase is a metaphor; wealth protects its possessors against adversity like a fortified city. Such wealth must be attained by diligence and righteous means (e.g., 13:8; 18:23; 22:7).
n2001058: tn Heb вАЬthe ruin of the poor.вАЭ The term вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ь÷і÷Љ„Щ„ЭвАО (dallim, вАЬof the poorвАЭ) functions as an objective genitive. Poverty leads to the ruin of the poor. The term вАЬruinвАЭ includes the shambles in which the person lives. This provides no security but only the fear of ruin. This proverb is an observation on life.
n2001060: tn Heb вАЬrecompenseвАЭ (so NAB); NASB, NIV вАЬwages.вАЭ The noun вАП„§÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷ї„Ь÷Љ÷Ј„ФвАО (pe њullah) has a two-fold range of meanings: (1) вАЬwork; deedвАЭ and (2) вАЬreward; recompenseвАЭ (BDB 821 s.v.). There is a clear correlation between a personвАЩs conduct and its consequences. Rewards are determined by moral choices. What one receives in life depends on the use of gifts and a righteous character.
n2001062: tn Heb вАЬharvest.вАЭ The term вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„С„Х÷Љ„Р÷Ј„™вАО (tevu Њat, вАЬharvest; yieldвАЭ) is used figuratively here (hypocatastasis), drawing an implied comparison between the agricultural yield of a farmerвАЩs labors with the consequences of the actions of the wicked. They will вАЬreapвАЭ (= judgment) what they вАЬsowвАЭ (= sin).
n2001064: tn Heb вАЬsin.вАЭ The term вАП„Ч÷Ј„Ш÷Љ÷Є„Р„™вАО (khatta Њt, вАЬsinвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of cause (= sin) for effect (= punishment). In contrast to the righteous who receive a reward, the wicked receive punishment for their sin (cf. NASB, NIV, NCV). See D. W. Thomas, вАЬThe Meaning of вАП„Ч÷Ј„Ш÷Љ÷Є„Р„™вАО in Proverbs X.16, вАЭ JTS 15 (1964): 295-96.
n2001065: tn Heb вАЬdiscipline.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„°÷Є„®вАО (musar) has a basic two-fold range of meanings: (1) вАЬdisciplineвАЭ (so NIV; NAB вАЬadmonitionвАЭ; NCV, NLT вАЬcorrectionвАЭ) and (2) вАЬinstructionвАЭ (BDB 416 s.v.; so KJV, NASB, NRSV). The wise person listens to instruction (first colon); however, the fool will not even take discipline to heart (second colon).
n2001066: tn The term is a genitive of location indicating the goal (IBHS 147-48 ¬І9.5.2f).
n2001067: sn The contrast with the one who holds fast to discipline is the one who forsakes or abandons reproof or correction. Whereas the first is an example, this latter individual causes people to wander from the true course of life, that is, causes them to err.
n2001068: tn Heb вАЬlips of falsehood.вАЭ The genitive noun вАП„©„Б÷Є„І÷ґ„®вАО (shaqer, вАЬfalsehoodвАЭ) functions as an attributive genitive. The noun вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause for speech produced by lips. The one who shows friendliness while concealing hatred is a liar (e.g., Ps 28:3).
n2001069: tn Heb вАЬcauses to go out.вАЭ The Hiphil of вАП„Щ÷Є„¶÷Є„РвАО (yatsa Њ) literally means вАЬto cause to go outвАЭ (BDB 424 s.v. Hiph.1). This may refer to speech (вАЬto utterвАЭ) in the sense of causing words to go out of oneвАЩs mouth, or it may refer to slander (вАЬto spreadвАЭ) in the sense of causing slander to go out to others.
n2001070:
tn The word вАП„У÷і„С÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (dibbah) means вАЬwhispering; defamation; evil reportвАЭ (BDB 179 s.v.). Cf. NAB вАЬaccusationsвАЭ; TEV вАЬgossip.вАЭ
sn The one who spreads slander is a fool because it not only destroys others but comes back on the guilty. See also the sayings of Amenemope and Ahiqar on these subjects (ANET 423, 429).
n2001071: tn Heb вАЬhe is a fool.вАЭ The independent personal pronoun вАП„Ф„Х÷Љ„РвАО (hu Њ, вАЬheвАЭ) is used for emphasis. This is reflected in the translation as вАЬcertainly.вАЭ
n2001072: tn Heb вАЬdoes not cease.вАЭ It is impossible to avoid sinning in an abundance of wordsвАФsooner or later one is bound to say something wrong.
n2001073: tn Or вАЬholds his lips under control.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„©„В÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (khasakh) means вАЬto withhold; to restrain; to hold in checkвАЭ (BDB 362 s.v.). The related Arabic term is used in reference to placing a piece of wood in the mouth of a goat to prevent it from sucking (HALOT 359 s.v. вАП„Ч„©„В„Ъ÷∞вАО).
n2001074: tn Heb вАЬhis lipsвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB); NIV вАЬhis tongue.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause for speech.
n2001075: tn Heb вАЬthe lips of the righteous.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ functions as a metonymy of cause for speech. This contrasts the tongue (metonymy of cause for what they say) with the heart (metonymy of subject for what they intend). What the righteous say is more valuable than what the wicked intend.
n2001076: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2001078: tn Heb вАЬthe heart of the wickedвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NIV). The term вАЬheartвАЭ functions as a metonymy of cause for thoughts. The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬheartвАЭ) often refers to the seat of thoughts, will and emotions (BDB 524 s.v. 3-4).
n2001079: tn Heb вАЬlike little.вАЭ This expression refers to what has little value: вАЬlittle worthвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NRSV; cf. BDB 590 s.v. вАП„Ю÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„ШвАО 2.d). The point of the metaphor is clarified by the parallelism: Silver is valuable; the heart of the wicked is worth little. Tg. Prov 10:20 says it was full of dross, a contrast with choice silver.
n2001080: tn Heb вАЬlips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ functions as a metonymy of cause for what is said (or in this case taught).
n2001081: tn The verb вАП„®÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (ra њah) means вАЬto feedвАЭ or вАЬto shepherdвАЭ (e.g., Gen 48:15). What they say will meet the needs of many.
n2001082: tn In what sense the fool вАЬdiesвАЭ is unclear. Fools ruin their lives and the lives of others by their lack of discipline and knowledge. The contrast is between enhancing life and ruining life.
n2001083: tn The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) is used as a metonymy of association for what one does with the mind (i.e., thinking), and so refers to discernment, wisdom, good sense.
n2001084: tn The term вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„®÷Є„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (berakhah, вАЬblessingвАЭ) refers to a gift, enrichment or endowment from the Lord.
n2001085: tn Heb вАЬof the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) functions here as a genitive of source.
n2001086: tn Heb вАЬmakes richвАЭ (so NASB); NAB вАЬbrings wealth.вАЭ The direct object вАЬa personвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is implied by the Hiphil verb; it is supplied in the translation.
n2001087: tn Heb вАЬtoil.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ґ÷ґ„¶÷ґ„СвАО ( њetsev) has a basic two-fold range of meanings: (1) вАЬtoil; laborвАЭ which produces pain and sorrow, and (2) вАЬpain; sorrowвАЭ which is the result of toil and labor (BDB 780 s.v.). This is the word used of the curse of вАЬtoilвАЭ in manвАЩs labor (Gen 3:17) and the вАЬpainвАЭ in the womanвАЩs child-bearing (Gen 3:16). GodвАЩs blessing is pure and untarnishedвАФit does not bring physical pain or emotional sorrow.
n2001089: tn Heb вАЬdoing a plan.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ц÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (zimmah, вАЬplanвАЭ) is often used pejoratively of a scheme to do wickedness. It is used elsewhere for planning lewdness, murder, incest, adultery, idolatry, and licentiousness. Any planned gross impropriety gives the fool pleasure.
