The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„©„В÷Є„ФвАО ( њasah, вАЬto doвАЭ) here means вАЬto carry out (a plan)вАЭ (BDB 794 s.v.).
n2001090: tn Heb вАЬlike sportвАЭ (so NASB, NRSV). The noun вАП„©„В÷∞„Ч„Х÷є„ІвАО (sekhoq, вАЬsportвАЭ) is used elsewhere to refer to what is exhilarating and pleasurable (BDB 966 s.v.). As W. G. Plaut says, it is like childвАЩs play (Proverbs, 132). For the fool evil brings such enjoyment; for the discerning wisdom does.
n2001092: tn Heb вАЬthe dread of the wicked.вАЭ The noun вАП„®÷Є„©„Б÷Є„ҐвАО (rasha њ, вАЬwickedвАЭ) is a subjective genitive. The noun вАП„Ю÷∞„Т„Х÷є„®÷Ј„™вАО (megorat) refers to вАЬthe feared thing,вАЭ that is, what the wicked dread. The wicked are afraid of the consequences of their sinful actions; however, they cannot escape these consequences.
n2001093: tn Heb вАЬthe desire of the righteous.вАЭ The noun вАП„¶÷Ј„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ІвАО (tsadiq, вАЬrighteousвАЭ) is a subjective genitive.
n2001094: tn Heb вАЬit will give.вАЭ When used without an expressed subject, the verb вАП„Щ÷і„™÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (yitten) has a passive nuance: вАЬit will be granted.вАЭ
n2001095: sn The word for вАЬstorm windвАЭ comes from the root вАП„°„Х÷Љ„£вАО (suf, вАЬto come to an end; to ceaseвАЭ). The noun may then describe the kind of storm that makes an end of things, a вАЬwhirlwindвАЭ (so KJV, NASB; NLT вАЬcycloneвАЭ). It is used in prophetic passages that describe swift judgment and destruction.
n2001096: tn Heb вАЬthe wicked are notвАЭ; ASV, NAB, NASB вАЬis no more.вАЭ
n2001097:
tn Heb вАЬa foundation foreverвАЭ; NLT вАЬhave a lasting foundation.вАЭ
sn The metaphor compares the righteous to an everlasting foundation to stress that they are secure when the catastrophes of life come along. He is fixed in a covenantal relationship and needs not to fear passing misfortunes. The wicked has no such security.
n2001098: sn Two similes are used to portray the aggravation in sending a lazy person to accomplish a task. Vinegar to the teeth is an unpleasant, irritating experience; and smoke to the eyes is an unpleasant experience that hinders progress.
n2001099: tn The participle is plural, and so probably should be taken in a distributive sense: вАЬto each one who sends him.вАЭ
n20010100: tn Heb вАЬthe fear of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) functions as an objective genitive.
n20010102: tn Heb вАЬyears.вАЭ The term вАЬyearsвАЭ functions as a synecdoche of part (= years) for the whole (= lifespan).
n20010103: sn This general saying has to be qualified with the problem of the righteous suffering and dying young, a problem that perplexed the sages of the entire ancient world. But this is the general principle: The righteous live longer because their life is the natural one and because God blesses them.
n20010104: sn This proverb contrasts the hopes of the righteous and the wicked. The righteous will see their hopes fulfilled. The saying is concerned with GodвАЩs justice. The words вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„Ч÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„™вАО (tokhelet, from вАП„Щ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЬвАО, yakhal) and вАП„™÷і÷Љ„І÷∞„Х÷Ј„™вАО (tiqvat, from вАП„І÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО, qavah) are synonyms, both emphasizing eager expectations, longings, waiting in hope.
n20010105:
tc The LXX reads вАЬthe fear of the Lord.вАЭ
sn The вАЬway of the LordвАЭ is an idiom for GodвАЩs providential administration of life; it is what the Lord does (вАЬwayвАЭ being a hypocatastasis).
n20010106: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n20010107: tc Heb вАЬfor the one with integrityвАЭ (вАП„Ь÷Ј„™÷є÷Љ„ЭвАО, lattom). The versions appear to read вАП„Ь÷∞„™÷Ј„ЭвАО (letam) вАЬfor the blameless.вАЭ
n20010110: sn This proverb concerns the enjoyment of covenant blessingsвАФdwelling in the land of Israel. It is promised to the righteous for an eternal inheritance, and so the wicked cannot expect to settle thereвАФthey will be exiled.
n20010111: tn Heb вАЬthe mouth.вАЭ The term вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (peh, вАЬmouthвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of cause for speech.
n20010112: tn Heb вАЬbears wisdom.вАЭ The verb вАП„†„Х÷Љ„СвАО (nuv) means вАЬto bear fruit.вАЭ It is used figuratively of the righteous; they produce wisdom and righteousness. The term вАП„Ч÷Є„Ы÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (khokhmah, вАЬwisdomвАЭ) represents the вАЬfruitвАЭ that the righteous bear: вАЬthey bear the fruit of wisdomвАЭ (BDB 626 s.v.).
n20010113: tn Heb вАЬthe tongue of perversions.вАЭ The noun вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ф÷∞„§÷Љ÷ї„Ы„Х÷є„™вАО (tahpukhot, вАЬperversionsвАЭ) functions as a genitive of content; it refers to what the tongue saysвАФperverse things. The plural form depicts a plural of character. The term вАП„Ь÷Є„©„Б„Х÷є„ЯвАО (lashon, вАЬtongueвАЭ) functions as a synecdoche of part (= tongue) for the whole person (= the speaker). The tongue is emphasized because this person is characterized by perverse speech. The term вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ф÷∞„§÷Љ÷ї„Ы„Х÷є„™вАО (вАЬperversionsвАЭ) refers to those who turn things upside down, overthrow, or pervert what is right.
n20010114: tn Heb вАЬwill be cut offвАЭ (so NAB, NRSV, NLT); cf. KJV, NASB, NIV вАЬcut out.вАЭ Their tongue will be cut off, a hyperbole meaning to bring to an end the evil that they speak.
n20010115: sn The verb вАЬknowвАЭ applied to вАЬlipsвАЭ is unusual. вАЬLipsвАЭ is a metonymy for what the righteous say; and their words вАЬknowвАЭ (a personification) what is pleasing, i.e., they are acquainted with.
n20010116: sn The righteous say what is pleasing, acceptable, or delightful, but the wicked say perverse and destructive things.
n20010117: tn Heb вАЬlips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause for what is said.
n200111: tn Heb вАЬan abomination of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) is a subjective genitive.
n200112: tn Heb вАЬscales of deception.вАЭ The genitive is attributive: вАЬdeceptive scales.вАЭ This refers to dishonesty in the market where silver was weighed in the scales. God condemns dishonest business practices (Deut 25:13-16; Lev 19:35-36), as did the ancient Near East (ANET 388, 423).
n200113: tn Heb вАЬa perfect stone.вАЭ вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ь÷µ„ЭвАО (shalem) can mean вАЬintact, whole, perfect.вАЭ Stones were used for measuring amounts of silver on the scales and so were critical to the integrity of economic translations. Someone might cheat by tampering with the scale or the stones. The Lord is pleased with a proper stone that has not been tampered with because it represents integrity of process in the marketplace.
n200114: tn Heb вАЬpresumptuousness, over-confidence.вАЭ This term is from the root вАП„Ц÷і„Щ„УвАО (zid) which means вАЬto be presumptuous, arrogant, impudentвАЭ in the Qal, or to behave with such characteristics in the Hiphil.
n200115: tn Heb вАЬPride came, then shame came.вАЭ The verbs are a perfect and a preterite with vav consecutive of вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„РвАО (bo Њ, вАЬto enter; to comeвАЭ). Because the second verb is sequential to the first, the first may be subordinated as a temporal clause. Proverbs in Hebrew utilize the past tense as a prototypical example. English also does so, although less frequently, as in вАЬcuriosity killed the cat.вАЭ
n200116: sn This proverb does not state how the disgrace will come, but affirms that it will follow pride. The proud will be brought down.
n200117: tn The term вАЬcameвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew, but is supplied in the translation from parallelism.
n200118: tn Heb вАЬmodestyвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬthe lowly.вАЭ The adjective вАП„¶÷∞„†„Х÷Љ„Ґ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (tsenu њim, вАЬmodestвАЭ) is used as a noun; this is an example of antimeria in which one part of speech is used in the place of another (see E. W. Bullinger, Figures of Speech, 491-506), e.g., вАЬLet the dry [adjective] appear!вАЭ = dry land (Gen 1:9). The root вАП„¶÷Є„†÷Ј„ҐвАО (tsana њ, вАЬto be modest; to be humbleвАЭ) describes those who are reserved, retiring, modest. The plural form is used for the abstract idea of humility.
n200119: sn This contrasts two lifestyles, affirming the value of integrity. The upright live with integrityвАФblamelessnessвАФand that integrity leads them in success and happiness. Those who use treachery will be destroyed by it.
n2001110: tn The verb вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Т÷Ј„УвАО (bagad) means вАЬto act treacherously, with duplicity, or to betray.вАЭ
n2001111: tc The form is a Kethib/Qere reading. The Qere, вАП„Щ÷∞„©÷Є„Б„У÷Љ÷µ„ЭвАО (yeshoddem), is a Qal imperfect of вАП„©÷Є„Б„У÷Ј„УвАО (shadad, вАЬto devastateвАЭ) and is supported by the versions. The syntactically difficult Kethib, вАП„Х÷∞„©÷Ј„Б„У÷Љ÷Є„ЭвАО (veshaddam), is a Qal perfect consecutive prefixed with the conjunction vav.
n2001112: sn The вАЬday of wrathвАЭ refers to divine punishment in this life (R. N. Whybray, Proverbs [CBC], 67; e.g., also Job 21:30; Ezek 7:19; Zeph 1:18). Righteousness and not wealth is more valuable in anticipating judgment.
n2001113: tn The Piel form of the verb вАП„Щ÷Є„©÷Ј„Б„®вАО (yashar) means вАЬto make straight, smooth or easy.вАЭ The concrete image is about making a road level and smooth; it represents an easier way of life. It does not mean an easy life in all respects, but means that integrity removes some obstacles and hardships in life, and integrity is the right choice for how to live.
n2001114:
sn The wicked may think that they can make their way through life easier by their wickedness, but instead it will at some point bring them down.
tn The masculine singular pronouns have been replaces with third person plural pronouns for the sake of style.
n2001115: sn The contrast is between being rescued or delivered (вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„ЬвАО, natsal) and being captured (вАП„Ь÷Є„Ы÷Ј„УвАО, lakhad). Righteousness is freeing; [evil] desires are enslaving.
n2001116: tn The verb вАП„Ь÷Є„Ы÷Ј„УвАО (lakhad) means вАЬto capture, trap, overpower.вАЭ Here it is passive; cf. NIV, TEV вАЬare trapped,вАЭ NASB, NKJV вАЬcaught,вАЭ ESV, NRSV вАЬtaken captive.вАЭ
n2001117: tc The Hebrew text has the singular construct form вАП„Х÷Љ„С÷∞„Ф÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷Ј„™вАО (uvehavvat) which may be from I вАП„Ф÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (havvah, вАЬdesire ofвАЭ) or II вАП„Ф÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (havvah, вАЬdisaster of, destruction ofвАЭ). The line would read вАЬbut in the desire ofвА¶вАЭ (cf. NLT вАЬthe ambition ofвА¶ entraps themвАЭ) or вАЬbut in the disaster of the treacherous they will be caught.вАЭ The BHS editors propose repointing the word to the plural absolute form вАП„Х÷Љ„С÷∞„Ф÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷є„™вАО (uvehavvot) resulting in вАЬthe treacherous will be ensnared in [their] desiresвАЭ or вАЬthe treacherous will be caught in disasters.вАЭ The LXX has a singular form, but it does not represent a Hebrew construct form and not necessarily the same word as the MT: вАЬungodliness will fall into [the hands of] unrighteousnessвАЭ or вАЬencounters injustice.вАЭ
n2001118:
tc The LXX alters the proverb to speak first of the righteous: вАЬWhen the righteous dies, hope does not perish; but the boasting by the ungodly perishes.вАЭ The spirit of the saying is similar to the Hebrew. Perhaps the LXX translators wanted to see the hope of the righteous fulfilled in the world to come. However, they may have tried to address the conceptual problem that arises from a literal reading of the Hebrew, вАЬwhen a wicked person dies, hope perishes.вАЭ The LXX has вАЬhope does not perish.вАЭ If the Hebrew text they used read вАЬnot,вАЭ they may have inferred that the proverb should talk about the righteous. If a вАЬnotвАЭ were restored to the Hebrew, it would then contrast true hope from hope in power: вАЬWhen a wicked person dies, hope (itself) does not perish; but expectation based on power has perished.вАЭ But note that the LXX text of Proverbs is generally loose as a translation and sometimes has apparent substitutions.
tn The pronoun вАЬhisвАЭ does not occur in the Hebrew text, but has been added to help make sense of the Masoretic text.
n2001119:
tn The imperfect verb can be present or future tense. It states a general truth which typically occurs in the given circumstances.
sn The subject of this proverb is the hope of the wicked. His expectations die with him (Ps 49). Any hope for long life and success borne of wickedness will be disappointed.
n2001120:
tc There are several suggested changes for this word вАП„Р„Х÷є„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( Њonim, вАЬvigorвАЭ or вАЬstrengthвАЭ). Rashi, a Jewish scholar who lived a.d. 1040-1105, suggests that the word refers to children, a meaning implied from Gen 49:3. This would mean that even his children would not benefit from his wickedness. Tg. Prov 11:7 rendered it вАЬwho practice crookedness,вАЭ deriving it from a root which means вАЬwickedness.вАЭ A similarly spelled word вАП„Р÷Є„Х÷ґ„ЯвАО ( Њaven) and a similarly sounding word вАП„Ґ÷Є„Х÷є„ЯвАО ( њavon) can each refer to sin or wickedness. However the first does not occur in the plural and the second is feminine, no neither are likely to stand behind this masculine plural noun.
tn Heb вАЬthe hope of strength,вАЭ meaning hope based on power, is a genitive of cause or source.
n2001121: tn The use of the Hebrew perfect verb as a perfective, showing the continuing results of an event in the past, emphasizes the finality of the situation. The hope associated with the wicked person is now gone.
n2001122: tn The verb is the Niphal perfect from the first root вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Ј„•вАО (khalats), meaning вАЬto draw off; to withdraw,вАЭ and hence вАЬto be delivered.вАЭ The saying uses a perfect verb for past time followed by a preterite with vav consecutive. In so doing, the perspective of the proverb is that of a sage telling the student of a situation which has happened, and is prototypical of what will happen again.
n2001123: tn Heb вАЬThe wicked came [= arrived] in his place,вАЭ meaning the place of trouble that the righteous was delivered from. Cf. NASB вАЬthe wicked takes his placeвАЭ; NRSV вАЬthe wicked get into it insteadвАЭ; NIV вАЬit comes on the wicked instead.вАЭ The verb is a preterite with vav consecutive and should be past time. On the one hand the sage has seen this take place and the student should expect it to happen again. From another angle, the proverb says that the trouble, which a righteous person appears to be headed for, could actually be prepared for the wicked.
n2001124: tn Heb вАЬwith his mouth.вАЭ The term вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (peh, вАЬmouthвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of cause for speech.
n2001125: sn The Hebrew word originally meant вАЬimpious, godless, polluted, profane.вАЭ It later developed the idea of a вАЬhypocriteвАЭ (Dan 11:32), one who conceals his evil under the appearance of godliness or kindness. This one is a false flatterer.
n2001126: sn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (shakhat) means вАЬto destroy; to ruinвАЭ (e.g., the destruction of Sodom in Gen 13:10). The imperfect tense is probably not an habitual imperfect (because the second colon shows exceptions), but probably a progressive imperfect (вАЬthis goes onвАЭ) or potential imperfect (вАЬthey can do thisвАЭ).
n2001127: sn The antithetical proverb states that a righteous person can escape devastating slander through knowledge. The righteous will have sufficient knowledge and perception to see through the hypocrisy and avoid its effect.
n2001128: tn The text has вАЬin the good [вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ш„Х÷є„СвАО, betov] of the righteous,вАЭ meaning when they do well, when they prosper. Cf. NCV, NLT вАЬsucceedвАЭ; TEV вАЬhave good fortune.вАЭ
n2001129: sn The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„Ь÷є„•вАО (ta њalots, вАЬto rejoice; to exultвАЭ) is paralleled with the noun вАП„®÷і„†÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (rinnah, вАЬringing cryвАЭ). The descriptions are hyperbolic, except when the person who dies is one who afflicted society (e.g., 2 Kgs 11:20; Esth 8:15). D. Kidner says, вАЬHowever drab the world makes out virtue to be, it appreciates the boon of it in public lifeвАЭ (Proverbs [TOTC], 91).
n2001130: tn Heb вАЬthe blessing of the upright.вАЭ This expression features either an objective or subjective genitive. It may refer to the blessing God gives the upright (which will benefit society) or the blessing that the upright are to the city. The latter fits the parallelism best: The blessings are the beneficent words and deeds that the righteous perform.
n2001131: tn Heb вАЬmouth.вАЭ The term вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (peh, вАЬmouthвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of cause for counsel, as the parallelism suggests.
n2001132: sn What the wicked say has a disastrous effect on society, endangering, weakening, demoralizing, and perverting with malicious and slanderous words. Wicked leaders, in particular, can bring destruction on a city by their evil counsel.
n2001133:
tn Heb вАЬdespisesвАЭ (so NASB) or вАЬbelittlesвАЭ (so NRSV). The participle вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„ЦвАО (baz, from вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЦвАО, buz) means вАЬto despise; to show contempt forвАЭ someone. It reflects an attitude of pride and judgmentalism. In view of the parallel line, in this situation it would reflect perhaps some public denunciation of another person.
sn According to Proverbs (and the Bible as a whole) how one treats a neighbor is an important part of righteousness. One was expected to be a good neighbor, and to protect and safeguard the life and reputation of a neighbor.
n2001134: tn Heb вАЬlacking of mind.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) refers by metonymy to what one does with the mind (i.e., thinking), and so refers to discernment, wisdom, good sense.
n2001136: sn The verb translated вАЬkeeps silenceвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷≤„®÷і„Щ„©„БвАО, yakharish) means вАЬholds his peace.вАЭ Rather than publicly denouncing another personвАЩs mistake or folly, a wise person will keep quiet about it (e.g., 1 Sam 10:27). A discerning person realizes that the neighbor may become an opponent and someday retaliate.
n2001137: tn Heb вАЬgoing about in slander.вАЭ This expression refers to a slanderer. The noun means вАЬslanderвАЭ and so вАЬtale-bearerвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NASB), вАЬinformer.вАЭ The related verb (вАП„®÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЬвАО, rakhal) means вАЬto go aboutвАЭ from one person to another, either for trade or for gossip.
n2001138:
tn The participle вАП„Ю÷∞„Т÷Ј„Ь÷ґ÷Љ„ФвАО (megalleh) means вАЬuncoveringвАЭ or вАЬrevealingвАЭ secrets.
sn This is the intent of a person who makes disparaging comments about othersвАФhe cannot wait to share secrets that should be kept.
n2001139: tn Heb вАЬfaithful of spirit.вАЭ This phrase describes the inner nature of the person as faithful and trustworthy. This individual will not rush out to tell whatever information he has heard, but will conceal it.
n2001140: tn The word вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ч÷∞„С÷Љ÷ї„Ь„Х÷є„™вАО (takhbulot, вАЬguidance; directionвАЭ) is derived from the root I вАП„Ч÷Є„С÷Ј„ЬвАО (khaval, вАЬrope-pullingвАЭ and вАЬsteeringвАЭ or вАЬdirectingвАЭ a ship; BDB 286 s.v.). Thus spiritual guidance is like steering a ship, here the ship of state (R. N. Whybray, Proverbs [CBC], 68; Prov 1:5). Advice is necessary for the success of a nation.
n2001141: tn Heb вАЬvictory.вАЭ This term вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„©„Б„Х÷Љ„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (teshu њah) means вАЬsalvationвАЭ or вАЬvictoryвАЭ (BDB 448 s.v.); cf. NAB, TEV вАЬsecurityвАЭ; NRSV, NLT вАЬsafety.вАЭ Here, it connotes вАЬsuccessвАЭ as the antithesis of the nation falling. The setting could be one of battle or economics. Victory or success will be more likely with good advice. This assumes that the counselors are wise.
n2001142: sn The вАЬstrangerвАЭ could refer to a person from another country or culture, as it often does, but it could also refer to an unknown Israelite, with the idea that the individual stands outside the known and respectable community.
n2001143: tn The sentence begins with the Niphal imperfect and the cognate (вАП„®÷Ј„Ґ÷Њ„Щ÷µ„®„Х÷є„Ґ÷ЈвАО, ra њ yeroa њ), stressing that whoever does this вАЬwill certainly suffer hurt.вАЭ The hurt in this case will be financial responsibility for a bad risk.
n2001144: tn Heb вАЬhates.вАЭ The term вАП„©÷є„В„†÷µ„РвАО (sone Њ) means вАЬto reject,вАЭ and here вАЬto avoid.вАЭ The participle is substantival, functioning as the subject of the clause. The next participle, вАП„™÷є„І÷∞„Ґ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (toqe њim, вАЬstriking handsвАЭ), is its object, telling what is hated. The third participle вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„Ш÷µ„Ч÷ЈвАО (boteach, вАЬis secureвАЭ) functions verbally.
n2001145: tn Heb вАЬstriking.вАЭ The term вАЬhandsвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied. The imagery here is shaking hands to seal a contract. It does not refer to greeting people with a handshake or exclude all business agreements.
n2001146: tn The participle вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„Ш÷µ„Ч÷ЈвАО (boteakh) means to вАЬbe secure, confident, safe, or care free.вАЭ In this verse it applies specifically to the issue of putting up security for another, not all the rest of life. A person who avoids this bad decision has no worries about its consequences.
n2001147: tn Heb вАЬa woman of grace.вАЭ The genitive вАП„Ч÷µ„ЯвАО (khen, вАЬgraceвАЭ) functions as an attributive adjective. The contrast is between вАЬa gracious womanвАЭ (вАП„Р÷µ„©„Б÷ґ„™÷Њ„Ч÷µ„ЯвАО,  Њeshet khen), a woman who is not only graceful but generous, and вАЬpowerful men,вАЭ a term usually having a bad sense, such as tyrants or ruthless men.
n2001148: tn Heb вАЬthose who are terrifying.вАЭ The term вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷і„Щ„•вАО ( њarits) refers to a person who strikes terror into the hearts of his victims. The term refers to a ruthless person who uses violence to overcome his victims (BDB 792 s.v.). Cf. ASV, NASB, NLT вАЬviolent menвАЭ; NRSV вАЬthe aggressive.вАЭ
n2001149:
tc The LXX adds: вАЬShe who hates virtue makes a throne for dishonor; the idle will be destitute of means.вАЭ This reading is followed by several English versions (e.g., NAB, NEB, NRSV, TEV). C. H. Toy concludes that MT provides remnants of the original, but that the LXX does not provide the full meaning (Proverbs [ICC], 229).
sn The implication is that the ruthless men will obtain wealth without honor, and therefore this is not viewed as success by the writer.
n2001150:
tn Heb вАЬman of kindness,вАЭ вАЬof loyalty,вАЭ or вАЬof loyal love.вАЭ
sn This contrasts the вАЬkind personвАЭ and the вАЬcruel personвАЭ (one who is fierce, cruel), showing the consequences of their dispositions.
n2001151: tn The term вАП„Т÷є÷Љ„Ю÷ґ„ЬвАО (gomel) means вАЬto deal fully [or вАЬadequatelyвАЭ] withвАЭ someone or something. The kind person will benefit himself.
n2001152: tn Heb вАЬhis own soul.вАЭ The term вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, вАЬsoulвАЭ) is used as a synecdoche of part (= soul) for the whole (= person): вАЬhimselfвАЭ (BDB 660 s.v. 4).
n2001153:
tn Heb вАЬbrings trouble to his flesh.вАЭ
sn There may be a conscious effort by the sage to contrast вАЬsoulвАЭ and вАЬbodyвАЭ: He contrasts the benefits of kindness for the вАЬsoulвАЭ (translated вАЬhimselfвАЭ) with the trouble that comes to the вАЬflesh/bodyвАЭ (translated вАЬhimselfвАЭ) of the cruel.
n2001154: tn The form is the masculine singular adjective used as a substantive.
n2001156:
tn Heb вАЬwages of deception.вАЭ
sn Whatever recompense or reward the wicked receive will not last, hence, it is deceptive (R. B. Y. Scott, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes [AB], 88).
n2001157: sn The participle вАЬsowingвАЭ provides an implied comparison (the figure is known as hypocatastasis) with the point of practicing righteousness and inspiring others to do the same. What is sown will yield fruit (1 Cor 9:11; 2 Cor 9:6; Jas 3:18).
n2001158: tn The term вАЬreapsвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but has been supplied in the translation from context for the sake of smoothness.
n2001159: tn Heb вАЬtrueвАЭ (so NASB, NRSV); KJV, NAB, NIV вАЬsure.вАЭ
n2001160: sn A wordplay (paronomasia) occurs between вАЬdeceptiveвАЭ (вАП„©„Б÷Є„І÷ґ„®вАО, shaqer) and вАЬrewardвАЭ (вАП„©„В÷ґ„Ы÷ґ„®вАО, sekher), underscoring the contrast by the repetition of sounds. The wages of the wicked are deceptive; the reward of the righteous is sure.
n2001161: tn Heb вАЬthe veritable of righteousness.вАЭ The adjective вАП„Ы÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (ken, вАЬright; honest; veritableвАЭ) functions substantivally as an attributive genitive, meaning вАЬveritable righteousnessвАЭ = true righteousness (BDB 467 s.v. 2; HALOT 482 s.v. I вАП„Ы÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО 2.b). One medieval Hebrew ms, LXX, and Syriac read вАП„С÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (ben), вАЬson of righteousness.вАЭ That idiom, however, usually introduces bad qualities (вАЬson of worthlessnessвАЭ). Others interpret it as вАЬrighteousness is the foundation of life.вАЭ KB identifies the form as a participle and reads it as вАЬsteadfast in righteousness,вАЭ but the verb does not otherwise exist in the Qal. W. McKane reads it as вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„ЯвАО (kan, from вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО, kun) and translates it вАЬstrive afterвАЭ life (Proverbs [OTL], 435).
n2001162: tn Heb вАЬis to life.вАЭ The expression вАЬleads toвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but the idiom implies it; it is supplied in the translation for smoothness.
n2001163: tn The phrase вАЬpursues itвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but has been supplied in the translation from context.
n2001164: sn вАЬLifeвАЭ and вАЬdeathвАЭ describe the vicissitudes of this life but can also refer to the situation beyond the grave. The two paths head in opposite directions.
n2001165: tn Heb вАЬan abomination of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) functions as a subjective genitive. Cf. NIV вАЬdetestsвАЭ; NCV, TEV, CEV, NLT вАЬhates.вАЭ
n2001166: sn The word вАП„Ґ÷і„І÷∞÷Љ„©÷µ„Б„ЩвАО ( њiqqeshe, вАЬcrooked; twisted; pervertedвАЭ) describes the wicked as having вАЬtwisted minds.вАЭ Their mentality is turned toward evil things.
n2001167: tn Heb вАЬthose who are blameless of way.вАЭ The noun вАП„У÷Љ÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (derekh, вАЬwayвАЭ) is a genitive of specification: вАЬblameless in their way.вАЭ
n2001168: sn The noun means вАЬgoodwill, favor, acceptance, willвАЭ; it is related to the verb вАП„®÷Є„¶÷Ј„ФвАО (ratsah) which means вАЬto be pleased with; to accept favorably.вАЭ These words are used frequently in scripture to describe what pleases the Lord, meaning, what he accepts. In particular, sacrifices offered properly find acceptance with God (Ps 51:19). Here the lifestyle that is blameless pleases him.
n2001169: tn The expression вАЬhand to handвАЭ refers the custom of striking hands to confirm an agreement (M. Anbar, вАЬProverbes 11:21; 16:15; вАП„Щ„У „Ь„Щ„УвАО, ¬Ђsur le champ¬ї,вАЭ Bib 53 [1972]: 537-38). Tg. Prov 11:21 interprets it differently: вАЬhe who lifts up his hand against his neighbor will not go unpunished.вАЭ
n2001170: tn Heb The verb вАП„†÷Є„І÷Є„ФвАО (naqah) means вАЬto be clean; to be empty.вАЭ In the Niphal it means вАЬto be free of guilt; to be clean; to be innocent,вАЭ and therefore вАЬto be exempt from punishmentвАЭ (BDB 667 s.v. Niph). The phrase вАЬwill not go unpunishedвАЭ (cf. NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV) is an example of tapeinosis (a negative statement that emphasizes the positive opposite statement): вАЬwill certainly be punishedвАЭ (cf. TEV, CEV, NLT).
n2001171: tn Heb вАЬthe seed of the righteous.вАЭ This is an idiom that describes a class of people who share the nature of righteousness (e.g., Isa 1:4; 65:23). The word вАЬseedвАЭ (hypocatastasis) means вАЬoffspring.вАЭ Some take it literally, as if it meant that the children of the righteous will escape judgment (Saadia, a Jewish scholar who lived a.d. 882-942). The LXX translates it in a different sense: вАЬhe that sows righteousness will receive a faithful reward.вАЭ
n2001172:
tn The verb вАП„†÷і„Ю÷∞„Ь÷Є„ШвАО (nimlat) is a Niphal, which usually has a reflexive meaning вАЬto escape,вАЭ but can also have a passive meaning вАЬto be delivered.вАЭ By implication the person escapes from harm, whether the threat of harm or the harmful situation he or she is already in. The verb form could be either a perfect or a participle (because the pausal accent makes them look identical). The perfect means вАЬhave escaped/been delivered,вАЭ while the participle would be present tense, вАЬescape/are delivered.вАЭ
sn This proverb uses antithetic parallelism, presenting opposite people with opposite outcomes described by opposite verb forms. In contrast to how things may look at the moment, the sage assures the student about the future of the wicked using the imperfect verb. They may look like they are getting away free, but in the end they will not. On the other hand, using the perfect verb, he assures the student of the benefit that he has seen for the righteousвАФthey have escaped. This is something that really has occurred and is prototypical of what can be expected. Further, by contrasting the evil person with the descendants of the righteous, the sage expands the range of benefit received from righteous living.
n2001173: tn The proverb makes a comparison by means of a verbless clause; the words вАЬlikeвА¶ isвА¶вАЭ are added in English for the sake of style.
n2001175:
tn Heb вАЬtaste.вАЭ The term can refer to physical taste (Exod 16:31), intellectual discretion (1 Sam 25:33), or ethical judgment (Ps 119:66). Here it probably means that she has no moral sensibility, no propriety, no good taste.
sn By means of the parallelism, one who rejects discretion is like a swine. If that person has beauty, its value is wasted on and overshadowed by their вАЬpiggishness.вАЭ
n2001176: tc The MT reads вАП„Ґ÷ґ„С÷∞„®÷Є„ФвАО ( њevrah, вАЬwrathвАЭ) implying that whatever the wicked hope it turns out that they receive wrath. The LXX reads бЉАѕАќњќїќµбњЦѕДќ±ќє (apoleitai, вАЬwill perishвАЭ) which might reflect an underlying Hebrew of вАП„Р÷Є„С÷∞„У÷Є„ФвАО ( Њavedah) вАЬit has perished,вАЭ which is also attested in at least one Medieval manuscript. The difference involves two letters similar in sound, вАП„РвАО and вАП„ҐвАО ( Њaleph and  њayin), and two similar in appearance, вАП„УвАО and вАП„®вАО (dalet and resh). This would be similar to Prov 10:28, which uses the imperfect of the same root, вАЬthe expectation of the wicked perishes.вАЭ
n2001177: tn Heb вАЬThere is one who scatters.вАЭ The participle вАП„Ю÷∞„§÷Ј„Ц÷Љ÷µ„®вАО (mefazzer, вАЬone who scattersвАЭ) refers to charity rather than farming or investments (and is thus a hypocatastasis). Cf. CEV вАЬbecome rich by being generous.вАЭ
n2001178: tn Heb вАЬincreases.вАЭ The verb means that he grows even more wealthy. This is a paradox: Generosity determines prosperity in GodвАЩs economy.
n2001179: tn Heb вАЬmore than what is right.вАЭ This one is not giving enough, but saving for himself.
n2001180: tn Heb вАЬcomes to lack.вАЭ The person who withholds will come to the diminishing of his wealth. The verse uses hyperbole to teach that giving to charity does not make anyone poor, and neither does refusal to give ensure prosperity.
n2001181: tn Heb вАЬthe soul of blessing.вАЭ The genitive functions attributively. вАЬBlessingвАЭ refers to a gift (Gen 33:11) or a special favor (Josh 15:19). The term вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, вАЬsoulвАЭ) functions as a synecdoche of part (= soul) for the whole (= person); see BDB 660 s.v. 4.
n2001182: tn Heb вАЬwill grow fat.вАЭ Drawing on the standard comparison of fatness and abundance (Deut 32:15), the term means вАЬbecome rich, prosperous.вАЭ
n2001183: tn The verb вАП„Ю÷Ј„®÷∞„Х÷ґ„ФвАО (marveh, вАЬto be saturated; to drink oneвАЩs fillвАЭ) draws a comparison between providing water for others with providing for those in need (e.g., Jer 31:25; Lam 3:15). The kind act will be reciprocated.
n2001184: tn The phrase вАЬfor othersвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is implied by the causative Hiphil verb which normally takes a direct object; it is elided in the Hebrew for the sake of emphasis. It is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity and smoothness.
n2001185: tn This verb also means вАЬto pour water,вАЭ and so continues the theme of the preceding participle: The one who gives refreshment to others will be refreshed. BDB 924 s.v. вАП„®÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО lists the form вАП„Щ„Х÷є„®÷ґ„РвАО (yore Њ) as a Hophal imperfect of вАП„®÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО (ravah) and translates it вАЬwill himself also be wateredвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB). HALOT notes that some manuscripts have вАП„Щ„Х÷є„®÷ґ„ФвАО (yoreh) and treats it as вАЬan alternate form of I вАП„®÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАОвАЭ (see HALOT 436 s.v. II вАП„Щ„®„ФвАО). The editors of BHS cite the Syriac evidence and suggest the line should read вАЬthe one who curses will be cursed,вАЭ taking the verbs as forms of вАП„Р÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО ( Њarar, вАЬto curseвАЭ).
n2001186: tn The direct object suffix on the verb picks up on the emphatic absolute phrase: вАЬthey will curse himвАФthe one who withholds grain.вАЭ
n2001187: sn The proverb refers to a merchant who holds back his grain from the free market to raise prices when there is a great need for the produce. It is assumed that merchants are supposed to have a social conscience.
n2001188: tn Heb вАЬbut a blessing is for the head of the one who sells.вАЭ The parallelism with вАЬcurseвАЭ suggests that вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„®÷Є„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (berakhah) вАЬblessingвАЭ means вАЬpraise.вАЭ
n2001189: tn Heb вАЬfor the head of the one who sells.вАЭ The term вАЬheadвАЭ functions as a synecdoche of part (= head) for the whole (= person). The head is here emphasized because it is the вАЬcrowningвАЭ point of praise. The direct object (вАЬitвАЭ) is not in the Hebrew text but is implied.
n2001190: tn Two separate words are used here for вАЬseek.вАЭ The first is вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (shakhar, вАЬto seek diligentlyвАЭ) and the second is вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„©„БвАО (baqash, вАЬto seek after; to look forвАЭ). Whoever is seeking good is in effect seeking favorвАФfrom either God or man (e.g., Ps 5:12; Isa 49:8).
n2001191: tn The participle вАП„У÷Љ÷є„®÷µ„©„БвАО (doresh) means вАЬto seek; to inquire; to investigate.вАЭ A person generally receives the consequences of the kind of life he seeks.
n2001192: tn The verb is the imperfect tense, third feminine singular, referring to вАЬevil,вАЭ the object of the participle.
n2001193: sn The implication from the parallelism is that the righteous do not trust in their own riches, but in the Lord.
n2001194: tn Heb вАЬleafageвАЭ or вАЬleafвАЭ (cf. KJV вАЬas a branchвАЭ); TEV вАЬleaves of summerвАЭ; NLT вАЬleaves in spring.вАЭ The simile of a leaf is a figure of prosperity and fertility throughout the ancient Near East.
n2001195: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО ( њakhar, вАЬto troubleвАЭ) refers to actions which make life difficult for oneвАЩs family (BDB 747 s.v.). He will be cut out of the family inheritance.
n2001196: tn Heb вАЬhis house.вАЭ The term вАП„С÷µ÷Љ„Щ„™вАО (bet, вАЬhouseвАЭ) is a synecdoche of container (= house) for its contents (= family, household).
n2001197: tn Heb вАЬthe windвАЭ (so KJV, NCV, NLT); NAB вАЬempty air.вАЭ The word вАЬwindвАЭ (вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО, ruakh) refers to what cannot be grasped (Prov 27:16; Eccl 1:14, 17). The figure is a hypocatastasis, comparing wind to what he inheritsвАФnothing he can put his hands on. Cf. CEV вАЬwonвАЩt inherit a thing.вАЭ
n2001198: sn The вАЬfoolвАЭ here is the вАЬtroublerвАЭ of the first half. One who mismanages his affairs so badly so that there is nothing for the family may have to sell himself into slavery to the wise. The ideas of the two halves of the verse are complementary.
n2001199: tn Heb вАЬthe wise of mind.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) functions as a genitive of specification: вАЬwise in the mindвАЭ or вАЬwise-minded.вАЭ Cf. вАЬwiseheartedвАЭ NASB; вАЬwise of heartвАЭ ESV, NKJV. The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО represents the person in this case (a synecdoche of part for the whole) because it is the seat of thinking and wisdom; see BDB 525 s.v. 7.
n20011100: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n20011101: tn Heb вАЬtree of lifeвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV). The noun вАП„Ч÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (khayyim, вАЬlifeвАЭ) is genitive of product. What the righteous produce (вАЬfruitвАЭ) is like a tree of lifeвАФa long and healthy life as well as a life-giving influence and provision for others.
n20011102: tc The Leningrad Codex, one of the most authoritative witnesses to the Hebrew text, mistakenly vocalized вАП„©вАО as вАП„©„ВвАО (sin) instead of вАП„©„БвАО (shin). The result, вАП„†÷∞„§÷Є„©„В„Х÷є„™вАО (nefasot), is not a word. Early printed editions of the Masoretic Text, other medieval Hebrew mss, read correctly вАП„†÷∞„§÷Є„©„Б„Х÷є„™вАО (nefashot, вАЬsoulsвАЭ).
n20011103: tc The MT reads вАП„Ч÷Є„Ы÷Є„ЭвАО (khakham, вАЬwiseвАЭ) and seems to refer to capturing (вАП„Ь÷Є„І÷Ј„ЧвАО, laqakh; вАЬto lay hold of; to seize; to captureвАЭ) people with influential ideas (e.g., 2 Sam 15:6). An alternate textual tradition reads вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Є„°вАО (khamas) вАЬviolentвАЭ (reflected in the LXX and Syriac) and refers to taking away lives: вАЬbut the one who takes away lives (= kills people) is violentвАЭ (cf. NAB, NRSV, TEV). The textual variant was caused by orthographic confusion of вАП„°вАО (samek) and вАП„ЫвАО (kaf), and metathesis of вАП„ЮвАО (mem) between the second and third consonants. If the parallelism is synonymous, the MT reading fits; if the parallelism is antithetical, the alternate tradition fits. See D. C. Snell, вАЬвАШTaking SoulsвАЩ in Proverbs 11:30, вАЭ VT 33 (1083): 362-65.
n20011104: tc The LXX introduces a new idea: вАЬIf the righteous be scarcely savedвАЭ (reflected in 1 Pet 4:18). The Greek translation вАЬscarcelyвАЭ could have come from a Vorlage of вАП„С÷Ј÷Љ„¶÷Є÷Љ„®÷Є„ФвАО (batsarah, вАЬdeficiencyвАЭ or вАЬwantвАЭ) or вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„¶÷Ј÷Љ„®вАО (batsar, вАЬto cut off; to shortenвАЭ) perhaps arising from confusion over the letters вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Р÷Є„®÷ґ„•вАО (ba Њarets, вАЬon the land/earthвАЭ). The verb вАЬreceive dueвАЭ could only be translated вАЬsavedвАЭ by an indirect interpretation. See J. Barr, вАЬвАП„С„Р„®„•вАО ~ ќЬќЯќЫќЩќ£: Prov. XI.31, I Pet. IV.18, вАЭ JSS 20 (1975): 149-64.
n20011105: tn This construction is one of the вАЬhow much moreвАЭ argumentsвАФif this be true, how much more this (arguing from the lesser to the greater). The point is that if the righteous suffer for their sins, certainly the wicked will as well.
n20011106: tn Heb вАЬthe wicked and the sinner.вАЭ The two terms may form a hendiadys with the first functioning adjectivally: вАЬthe wicked sinner.вАЭ
n200121: sn Those who wish to improve themselves must learn to accept correction; the fool hates/rejects any correction.
n200122: tn The word вАП„С÷Ј÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (ba њar, вАЬstupid, brutishвАЭ) comes from вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Ґ÷і„Щ„®вАО (be њir, вАЬbeast, cattle). It refers to a lack of rationality (Ps 49:10; 73:22; 92:7; 30:2). The verbal derivative is used to convey вАЬdeficiency in moral and religious, rather than intellectual aspectsвАЭ (NIDOTTE 679 s.v. вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО).
n200123: tn Heb вАЬbut he condemns.вАЭ The referent (the Lord) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n200124: tn Heb вАЬa man of wicked plans.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ю÷∞„Ц÷і„Ю÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО (mezimmot, вАЬevil plansвАЭ) functions as an attributive genitive: вАЬan evil-scheming man.вАЭ Cf. NASB вАЬa man who devises evilвАЭ; NAB вАЬthe schemer.вАЭ
n200126: tn The Niphal imperfect of вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (cun, вАЬto be establishedвАЭ) refers to finding permanent вАЬsecurityвАЭ (so NRSV, TEV, CEV) before God. Only righteousness can do that.
n200127: tn Heb вАЬa root of righteousness.вАЭ The genitive вАП„¶÷Ј„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„І÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (tsaddiqim, вАЬrighteousnessвАЭ) functions as an attributive adjective. The figure вАЬrootвАЭ (вАП„©÷є„Б„®÷ґ„©„БвАО, shoresh) stresses the security of the righteous; they are firmly planted and cannot be uprooted (cf. NLT вАЬthe godly have deep rootsвАЭ). The righteous are often compared to a tree (e.g., 11:30; Pss 1:3; 92:13).
n200128: tn Heb вАЬa wife of virtueвАЭ; NAB, NLT вАЬa worthy wife.вАЭ This noble woman (вАП„Р÷µ„©÷ґ„Б„™÷Њ„Ч÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЬвАО,  Њeshet khayil) is the subject of Prov 31. She is a вАЬvirtuous womanвАЭ (cf. KJV), a capable woman of noble character. She is contrasted with the woman who is disgraceful (вАП„Ю÷∞„С÷і„Щ„©÷Є„Б„ФвАО, mevishah; вАЬone who causes shameвАЭ) or who lowers his standing in the community.
n200129: sn The metaphor of the вАЬcrownвАЭ emphasizes that such a wife is a symbol of honor and glory.
n2001210: tn Heb вАЬsheвАЭ; the referent (the wife) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2001211: sn The simile means that the shameful acts of such a woman will eat away her husbandвАЩs strength and influence and destroy his happiness.
n2001212: tn Heb вАЬthoughts.вАЭ This term refers not just to random thoughts, however, but to what is planned or devised.
n2001213: sn The plans of good people are directed toward what is right. Advice from the wicked, however, is deceitful and can only lead to trouble.
n2001214: tn Heb вАЬare to ambush blood.вАЭ The infinitive construct вАП„Р÷±„®÷Є„СвАО ( Њerov, вАЬto lie in waitвАЭ) expresses the purpose of their conversations. The proverb either compares their words to an ambush (cf. NAB, NRSV вАЬare a deadly ambushвАЭ) or states what the content of their words is about.
n2001215: tn Heb вАЬfor blood.вАЭ The term вАЬbloodвАЭ is a metonymy of effect, the cause being the person that they will attack and whose blood they will shed. After the construct вАЬbloodвАЭ is also an objective genitive.
n2001216: tn Heb вАЬmouth.вАЭ The term вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (peh, вАЬmouthвАЭ) is a metonymy of cause, signifying what the righteous say. The righteous can make a skillful defense against false accusations that are intended to destroy. The righteous, who have gained wisdom, can escape the traps set by the words of the wicked.
n2001217:
tn The MT has an infinitive absolute вАЬas to the overthrow of the wickedвАФthey are [then] no more.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ф÷Є„§÷Ј„ЪвАО (haphakh) can mean вАЬto turnвАЭ (change directions), вАЬto turn something into something,вАЭ or вАЬto overthrowвАЭ (particularly said of cities). The LXX interprets as вАЬwherever the wicked turns he disappears.вАЭ
sn This proverb is about the stability of the righteous in times of trouble.
n2001219: tn Heb вАЬthe house of the righteous.вАЭ The genitive вАП„¶÷Ј„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„І÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (tsaddiqim) functions as an attributive adjective: вАЬrighteous house.вАЭ The noun вАП„С÷µ÷Љ„Щ„™вАО (bet, вАЬhouseвАЭ) functions as a synecdoche of container (= house) for the contents (= family, household; perhaps household possessions). Cf. NCV вАЬa good personвАЩs familyвАЭ; NLT вАЬthe children of the godly.вАЭ
n2001221: tn Heb вАЬto the mouth of.вАЭ This idiom means вАЬaccording toвАЭ (BDB 805 s.v. вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО 6.b.(b); cf. KJV, NAB, NIV). The point is that praise is proportionate to wisdom.
n2001222: tn Heb вАЬbent of mind.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„Х÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО ( њavah) occurs four times in the Niphal. In Isa 21:3 and Ps 38:6 it describes someone who is dazed or bewildered; in 1 Sam 20:30 it is derogatory, probably meaning moral perversity. Here it contrasts wisdom, so вАЬbewilderedвАЭ is likely, but it may also mean вАЬperverseвАЭ (NASB, NRSV, NKJV), вАЬwarpedвАЭ (NIV, NLT), вАЬtwistedвАЭ (ESV). The noun вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) is a genitive of specification. It functions as a metonymy of association for вАЬmind; thoughtsвАЭ (BDB 524 s.v. 3) and вАЬwill; volitionвАЭ (BDB 524 s.v. 4). This person does not perceive things as they are, and so makes wrong choices. His thinking is all wrong.
n2001223: tn Heb вАЬone who is lightly regarded.вАЭ The verb вАП„І÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (qalah) means вАЬto be lightly esteemed; to be dishonored; to be degradedвАЭ (BDB 885 s.v.).
n2001224:
tn Or вАЬwho accomplishes [something] for himself.вАЭ This is another possible meaning of the Hebrew underlying the LXX (see below). All of the possible options suggest that this person still has something of their own in contrast to the pretentious person in the second half of the saying.
tc The MT reads вАП„Х÷∞„Ґ÷ґ„С÷ґ„У „Ь„Х÷євАО (ve њeved lo), which may mean вАЬhas a servantвАЭ or вАЬis a servant for himself.вАЭ The LXX, Syriac, Vulgate and at least one Medieval Hebrew manuscript read the consonants as вАП„Х÷∞„Ґ÷є„С÷µ„У „Ь„Х÷євАО (ve њoved lo) вАЬwho serves (works for) himself.вАЭ The editors of BHS suggest a slight emendation to вАП„Х÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„С„Х÷Љ„® „Ь„Х÷євАО (va њavur lo) вАЬand the produce belongs to him.вАЭ The meaning produce (cf. Josh 5:11) is a fitting parallel to вАЬfoodвАЭ and the end of the verse, but the suggestion has no textual support.
n2001225:
tn Heb вАЬwho makes himself out to be important,вАЭ вАЬwho feigns importance,вАЭ or вАЬto boast.вАЭ The verb is a Hitpael participle from вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„С÷µ„УвАО (kaved), вАЬto be weighty; to be honored; to be importantвАЭ). See BDB 458 s.v. вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„С÷µ„УвАО Hitp.2 and HALOT 456 s.v. вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„С÷µ„УвАО.
sn This individual lives beyond his financial means in a vain show to impress other people and thus cannot afford to put food on the table.
n2001226: tn Heb вАЬknowsвАЭ; NLT вАЬconcerned for the welfare of.вАЭ For вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ) meaning вАЬto care forвАЭ see HALOT 391 s.v. Qal 4 and 7, NIDOTTE 401 s.v., and compare Job 9:21; Ps 1:6.
n2001227: tn Heb вАЬbut the mercies.вАЭ The additional words appear in the translation for the sake of clarification. The line can be interpreted in two ways: (1) when the wicked exhibit a kind act, they do it in a cruel way, or (2) even the kindest of their acts is cruel by all assessments.
n2001228: sn In the biblical period agriculture was the most common occupation for the people; so вАЬworking a fieldвАЭ describes a substantial occupation, but also represents working in general. Diligent work, not get-rich-quick schemes, is the key to ensuring income.
n2001229: tn Heb вАЬwill have his fill ofвАЭ or вАЬwill be satisfied with.вАЭ
n2001230: tn Heb вАЬempty thingsвАЭ or вАЬvain things.вАЭ The term вАП„®÷µ„Щ„І÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (reqim) refers to worthless pursuits in an effort to make money. The fact that the participle used is вАЬchase afterвАЭ shows how elusive these are. Cf. NIV вАЬfantasiesвАЭ; NCV вАЬempty dreamsвАЭ; TEV вАЬuseless projects.вАЭ
n2001231: tn Heb вАЬlacking of mind.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) refers by metonymy to thinking, and by extension to discernment, wisdom, good sense.
n2001232: sn The contrast includes a contrast of verb forms, here the perfect verb вАЬhas desired,вАЭ next the imperfect verb вАЬwill yield [fruit].вАЭ The perfect verb leaves the wicked at the point of desire for a goal. He or she has [only] desired, but there is no implication of achievement. In contrast the righteous are described not in terms of their goal or desire, but their root, implying their foundation or character. Their focus is different but their root will yield fruit or be productive.
n2001233: tn This line is difficult to interpret. BDB connects the term вАП„Ю÷∞„¶„Х÷є„УвАО (metsod) to II вАП„Ю÷Є„¶„Х÷є„УвАО which means (1) вАЬsnare; hunting-netвАЭ and (2) what is caught: вАЬpreyвАЭ (BDB 844-45 s.v. II вАП„Ю÷Є„¶„Х÷є„УвАО). This would function as a metonymy of cause for what the net catches: the prey. Or it may be saying that the wicked get caught in their own net, that is, reap the consequences of their own sins. On the other hand, HALOT 622 connects вАП„Ю÷∞„¶„Х÷є„УвАО (metsod) to II вАП„Ю÷∞„¶„Х÷Љ„У÷Є„ФвАО (metsudah, вАЬmountain strongholdвАЭ; cf. NAB вАЬthe stronghold of evil men will be demolishedвАЭ). The LXX translated it as: вАЬThe desires of the wicked are evil.вАЭ The Syriac has: вАЬThe wicked desire to do evil.вАЭ The Latin expands it: вАЬThe desire of the wicked is a defense of the worst [things, or persons].вАЭ C. H. Toy suggests emending the text to read вАЬwickedness is the net of bad menвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 250).
n2001234:
tc The MT reads вАП„Щ÷і„™÷µ÷Љ„ЯвАО (yitten, вАЬwill give; gives,вАЭ without a direct object: вАЬthe root of the righteous gives.вАЭ The LXX reads вАЬthe root of the righteous enduresвАЭ (cf. NAB). This suggests a Hebrew Vorlage of вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„™÷Є„ЯвАО ( Њetan, вАЬconstant; continualвАЭ; HALOT 44-45 s.v. I вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„™÷Є„ЯвАО 2) which would involve the omission of вАП„РвАО ( Њalef) in the MT.
tn Heb вАЬwill give/yield.вАЭ The verb вАП„†÷Є„™÷Ј„ЯвАО (natan) is used elsewhere in the phrase to вАЬproduce fruitвАЭ (e.g. Lev 25:19 of the land; Zech 8:12 of the vine). Yielding fruit can be a natural implication of healthy roots (cf. Ps. 1:3; Isa 11:1; Jer 12:2). The sage has probably left out specific mention of the word вАЬfruitвАЭ to heighten the contrast between desiring a goal and receiving a result which is the byproduct of good character. However the omission may imply a text critical problem.
n2001235:
tc MT reads the noun вАП„Ю„Х÷є„І÷µ„©„БвАО (moqesh, вАЬbait; lureвАЭ). The LXX, Syriac and Tg. Prov 12:13 took it as a passive participle (вАЬis ensnaredвАЭ). The MT is the more difficult reading and so is preferred. The versions appear to be trying to clarify a difficult reading.
tn Heb вАЬsnare of a man.вАЭ The word вАЬsnareвАЭ is the figurative meaning of the noun вАП„Ю„Х÷є„І÷µ„©„БвАО (вАЬbait; lureвАЭ from вАП„Щ÷Є„І÷Ј„©„БвАО [yaqash, вАЬto lay a bait, or lureвАЭ]).
n2001236: tn Heb вАЬtransgression of the lips.вАЭ The noun вАЬlipsвАЭ is a genitive of specification and it functions as a metonymy of cause for speech: sinful talk or sinning by talking. J. H. Greenstone suggests that this refers to litigation; the wicked attempt to involve the innocent (Proverbs, 131).
n2001237: sn J. H. Greenstone suggests that when the wicked become involved in contradictions of testimony, the innocent is freed from the trouble. Another meaning would be that the wicked get themselves trapped by what they say, but the righteous avoid that (Proverbs, 131).
n2001238: tn Heb вАЬfruit of the lips.вАЭ The term вАЬfruitвАЭ is the implied comparison, meaning what is produced; and вАЬlipsвАЭ is the metonymy of cause, referring to speech. Proper speech will result in good things.
n2001239: tn Heb вАЬthe work of the hands of a man.вАЭ
n2001240: tc The Kethib has the Qal imperfect, вАЬwill returnвАЭ to him (cf. NASB); the Qere preserves a Hiphil imperfect, вАЬhe/one will restore/renderвАЭ to him (cf. KJV, ASV). The Qere seems to suggest that someone (God or people) will reward him in kind. Since there is no expressed subject, it may be translated as a passive voice.
n2001241: sn The way of a fool describes a headlong course of actions (вАЬwayвАЭ is an idiom for conduct) that is not abandoned even when wise advice is offered.
n2001242: sn The fool believes that his own plans and ideas are perfect or вАЬrightвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Є„®вАО, yashar); he is satisfied with his own opinion.
n2001244: tn Or вАЬa wise person listens to adviceвАЭ (cf. NIV, NRSV, TEV, CEV, NLT).
n2001245: tn Heb вАЬThe fool, at once his vexation is known.вАЭ This rhetorically emphatic construction uses an independent nominative absolute, which is then followed by the formal subject with a suffix. The construction focuses attention on вАЬthe fool,вАЭ then states what is to be said about him.
n2001246:
tn Heb вАЬon the dayвАЭ or вАЬthe same day.вАЭ
sn The fool is impatient and unwise, and so flares up immediately when anything bothers him. W. McKane says that the foolвАЩs reaction is вАЬlike an injured animal and so his opponent knows that he has been woundedвАЭ (Proverbs [OTL], 442).
n2001248:
tn The range of meanings for the verb and the object suggest several possible interpretations of the last line. The verb вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„°÷Є„ФвАО (kasah) means вАЬto coverвАЭ and may indicate hiding or ignoring something. The noun вАП„І÷Є„Ь„Х÷є„ЯвАО (qalon) means вАЬshameвАЭ and may refer to disgrace (something to be ashamed of) or to contempt or an insult given (shaming words). Several English translations view it as ignoring or overlooking an insult (NIV, ESV, NRSV). Others more ambiguously render it as covering or concealing dishonor or shame, where it is less clear whether the person conceals their own shame or someone elseвАЩs. And the LXX reads вАЬa clever person conceals his own dishonor.вАЭ But these entail the three main possibilities: to ignore an insult given to you, to ignore something that could shame others, or to conceal something of your own that could be shameful. In a similar phrase in 12:23, the verb does not mean to ignore something.
sn The contrast in this proverb could be that the prudent person overlooks the insult made by the fool in part one, bypasses the opportunity to expose something that would shame another (in contrast to the fool), or doesnвАЩt give the opportunity for the fool to see what might be embarrassing. In contrast the fool cannot handle criticism well and/or announces dissatisfaction instinctively and quickly, without appropriate thought for others.
n2001249: tn The text has вАЬhe pours out faithfullyвАЭ; the word rendered вАЬfaithfullyвАЭ or вАЬreliablyвАЭ (вАП„Р÷±„Ю„Х÷Љ„†÷Є„ФвАО,  Њemunah) is used frequently for giving testimony in court, and so here the subject matter is the reliable witness.
n2001251: tn Heb вАЬwitness of falsehoods.вАЭ The genitive noun functions attributively, and the plural form depicts habitual action or moral characteristic. This describes a person who habitually lies. A false witness cannot be counted on to help the cause of justice.
n2001252: tn The term вАЬspeaksвАЭ does not appear in this line but is implied by the parallelism; it is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2001253: tn The term вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„Ш÷ґ„ФвАО (boteh) means вАЬto speak rashly [or, thoughtlessly]вАЭ (e.g., Lev 5:4; Num 30:7).
n2001254: tn Heb вАЬthe tongueвАЭ (so NAB, NIV, NRSV). The term вАП„Ь÷Є„©„Б„Х÷є„ЯвАО (lashon, вАЬtongueвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of cause for what is said.
n2001255: tn Heb вАЬ[is] healing.вАЭ The term вАЬbringsвАЭ is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity and smoothness.
n2001256: sn Healing is a metonymy of effect. Healing words are the opposite of the cutting, irresponsible words. What the wise say is faithful and true, gentle and kind, uplifting and encouraging; so their words bring healing.
n2001257: tn Heb вАЬa lip of truth.вАЭ The genitive вАП„Р÷±„Ю÷ґ„™вАО ( Њemet, вАЬtruthвАЭ) functions as an attributive adjective: вАЬtruthful lip.вАЭ The term вАП„©÷∞„В„§÷Ј„™вАО (sefat, вАЬlipвАЭ) functions as a synecdoche of part (= lip) for the whole (= person): вАЬtruthful person.вАЭ The contrast is between вАЬthe lip of truthвАЭ and the вАЬtongue of lying.вАЭ
n2001258: tn Heb вАЬa tongue of deceit.вАЭ The genitive вАП„©÷Є„Б„І÷ґ„®вАО (shaqer, вАЬdeceitвАЭ) functions as an attributive genitive. The noun вАП„Ь÷Є„©„Б„Х÷є„ЯвАО (lashon, вАЬtongueвАЭ) functions as a synecdoche of part (= tongue) for the whole (= person): вАЬlying person.вАЭ
n2001259: tn The verb вАП„Р÷Ј„®÷∞„Т÷і„Щ„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО ( Њargi њah) is the Hiphil of the root вАП„®÷Є„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО (raga њ). The number of homonyms of this root in Hebrew is debated. BDB lists it as a denominative of вАП„®÷ґ„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО (rega њ, вАЬa momentвАЭ), with the Hiphil meaning вАЬto make a twinklingвАЭ (BDB 920 s.v. I вАП„®÷Є„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО). HALOT lists only one verbal root with a base meaning вАЬto look for peaceвАЭ and this phrase with the Hiphil meaning вАЬas long as I grant restвАЭ (HALOT 1188, s.v. вАП„®÷Є„Т÷Ј„ҐвАО). Gesenius considers it to refer to blinking the eyes (GKC 321 ¬І 108h). In any case it is agreed that this expression is an idiom for brevity, вАЬonly for a moment.вАЭ
n2001260: tc Rather than the MTвАЩs вАП„Ю÷і„®÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (mirmah, вАЬdeceitвАЭ), the BHS editors suggest вАП„Ю÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО (morah, вАЬbitterness, sorrowвАЭ) as a contrast to joy in the second half.
n2001261: sn The contrast here is between вАЬevilвАЭ (= pain and calamity) and вАЬpeaceвАЭ (= social wholeness and well-being); see, e.g., Pss 34:14; 37:37.
n2001262: tn Heb вАЬthose who are counselors of peace.вАЭ The term вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ь„Х÷є„ЭвАО (shalom, вАЬpeaceвАЭ) is an objective genitive, so the genitive-construct вАЬcounselors of peaceвАЭ means those who advise, advocate or promote peace (cf. NAB, NIV).
n2001263: tn Hebrew places the negative with the verb (вАЬall harm will not beвА¶вАЭ), while English prefers to negate the noun (вАЬno harm willвА¶вАЭ). The proper nuance of вАП„Р÷Є„Х÷ґ„ЯвАО ( Њaven) is debated. The noun can refer to disaster, injustice, or iniquity. It is not clear how neutrally the term may refer to disaster or how tightly it is tied to the consequence or result of wickedness. There is some question as to whether it can have a magical connotation, as in a spell or a curse. In Job, Eliphaz declares that вАП„Р÷Є„Х÷ґ„ЯвАО ( Њaven) doesnвАЩt come out of the dust (just happen); on the other hand, humankind is born to trouble (Job 5:6-7). Rashi, a Jewish scholar who lived a.d. 1040-1105, took the term as вАЬwickedness,вАЭ and the clause as вАЬthe righteous will not be caught up in wickedness.вАЭ
n2001264:
tn The pual imperfect verb вАПвАП„Щ÷∞„Р÷ї„†÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (ye Њunneh) is from a rare root occurring only 4 times in the Bible. In the only piel case, Exod 21:13, God makes something happen for someone. This implies that the pual is not simply вАЬto happen toвАЭ but вАЬmade to happen to.вАЭ Some propose вАЬbe allowed to happen to.вАЭ
sn Proverbial sayings are often general and not absolute. Clearly Job was a righteous person to whom harm happened (or was permitted to happen). And being righteous does not mean an exemption from all hardships that are not related to punishment. The proper nuance also depends on the understanding of вАЬharm.вАЭ Perhaps the correct nuance is that God does not direct harms of punishment at the righteous. In the surrounding polytheistic countries, they believed that a god might make a mistake and punish the wrong person.
n2001265: tn The expression вАП„Ю÷Є„Ь÷∞„Р„Х÷Љ „®÷Є„ҐвАО (male Њu ra њ, вАЬto be full of calamity/evilвАЭ) means (1) the wicked do much evil or (2) the wicked experience much calamity (cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2001266: tn Heb вАЬan abomination of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) is a subjective genitive.
n2001267: tn Heb вАЬlips of lying.вАЭ The genitive вАП„©„Б÷Є„І÷ґ„®вАО (shaqer, вАЬlyingвАЭ) functions as an attributive genitive: вАЬlying lips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ functions as a synecdoche of part (= lips) for the whole (= person): вАЬa liar.вАЭ
n2001268: tn Heb вАЬbut doers of truthfulness.вАЭ The term вАЬtruthfulnessвАЭ is an objective genitive, meaning: вАЬthose who practice truthвАЭ or вАЬthose who act in good faith.вАЭ Their words and works are reliable.
n2001269: sn The contrast between вАЬdelight/pleasureвАЭ and вАЬabominationвАЭ is emphatic. What pleases the Lord is acting truthfully or faithfully.
n2001270:
tn Heb вАЬa shrewd manвАЭ (so NAB); KJV, NIV вАЬa prudent manвАЭ; NRSV вАЬOne who is clever.вАЭ
sn A shrewd person knows how to use knowledge wisely, and restrains himself from revealing all he knows.
n2001271: sn The term вАП„Ы÷є÷Љ„°÷ґ„ФвАО (koseh, вАЬcovers; hidesвАЭ) does not mean that he never shares his knowledge, but discerns when it is and is not appropriate to speak, cf. 10:14; 17:27.
n2001272: tn Heb вАЬthe mind of fools.вАЭ The вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) is the place of thinking and so it is the both source of what is said and the place of discernment for what to say aloud.
n2001273:
tn Or вАЬspeak out foolishly.вАЭ The noun may be a direct object (folly) or an adverbial accusative (foolishly).
sn The noun вАП„Р÷і„Х÷ґ÷Љ„Ь÷ґ„™вАО ( Њivvelet, вАЬfoolishness; follyвАЭ) is the antithesis of perception and understanding. It is related to the noun вАП„Р÷±„Х÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЬвАО ( Њevvil, вАЬfoolвАЭ), one who is morally bad because he despises wisdom and discipline, mocks at guilt, is licentious and quarrelsome, and is almost impossible to rebuke. W. McKane says that the more one speaks, the less he is able to speak effectively (Proverbs [OTL], 422). Cf. TEV вАЬstupid people advertise their ignorance;вАЭ NLT вАЬfools broadcast their folly.вАЭ
n2001274: sn By their diligent work they succeed to management. The diligent rise to the top, while the lazy sink to the bottom.
n2001275: tn Heb вАЬthe hand of the diligent.вАЭ The term вАЬhandвАЭ is a synecdoche of part (= hand) for the whole (= person): diligent person. The hand is emphasized because it is the instrument of physical labor; it signifies the actions and the industry of a diligent personвАФwhat his hand does.
n2001276: tn The term вАП„®÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (remiyyah) can mean вАЬslack, negligent, deceptiveвАЭ (HALOT 1243 s.v.). By the feature of ellipsis and double duty we should probably understand it as вАЬthe hand of the negligent,вАЭ as a way of referring to a negligent person. The term refers to one who is not diligent, who perhaps tries deceive his employer about his work, which he has neglected.
n2001277: tn The term вАП„Ю÷Ј„°вАО (mas) refers to forced or conscripted labor and is sometimes translated as вАЬslave laborвАЭ (NIV, cf. NLT вАЬslaveвАЭ) but it is far from clear that it means slavery (see NIDOTTE 984 s.v.). The term certainly describes imposed work requirements. For Israelites within Israel it is elsewhere used only in connection to conscription to work on royal building projects making it like a form of taxation (forced labor has often been used in world history as taxation instead of money). The precise use of the term here is unclear because of general lack of information, but perhaps the lazy person will not earn enough money to meet obligations and be required to pay via forced labor.
n2001278: tn The word вАЬanxietyвАЭ (вАП„У÷Љ÷∞„Р÷Є„Т÷Є„ФвАО, de Њagah) combines anxiety and fearвАФanxious fear (e.g., Jer 49:23; Ezek 4:16; for the related verb see Ps 38:18; Jer 17:8).
n2001279: tn Heb вАЬbows it [= his heart] down.вАЭ Anxiety weighs heavily on the heart, causing depression. The spirit is brought low.
n2001280: tn Heb вАЬgood.вАЭ The Hebrew word вАЬgoodвАЭ (вАП„Ш„Х÷є„СвАО, tov) refers to what is beneficial for life, promotes life, creates life or protects life. The вАЬgood wordвАЭ here would include encouragement, kindness, and insightвАФthe person needs to regain the proper perspective on life and renew his confidence.
n2001281: tn Heb вАЬmakes it [= his heart] glad.вАЭ The similarly sounding terms вАП„Щ÷Ј„©÷∞„Б„Ч÷ґ„†÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (yashkhennah, вАЬweighs it downвАЭ) and вАП„Щ÷∞„©÷Ј„В„Ю÷∞÷Љ„Ч÷ґ„†÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (yesammekhennah, вАЬmakes it gladвАЭ) create a wordplay (paronomasia) that dramatically emphasizes the polar opposite emotional states: depression versus joy.
n2001282: tn The line has several possible translations: (1) The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„™÷µ„®вАО (yater) can mean вАЬto spy out; to examine,вАЭ which makes a good contrast to вАЬlead astrayвАЭ in the parallel colon. So perhaps, it means вАЬsearches out his neighbor/friend,вАЭ though this bypasses the preposition вАЬfrom.вАЭ (2) вАП„Щ÷Є„™÷µ„®вАО could be the Hophal of вАП„†÷Є„™÷Ј„®вАО (natar, Hiphil вАЬto set freeвАЭ; Hophal вАЬto be set freeвАЭ): вАЬthe righteous is delivered from harmвАЭ [reading вАП„Ю÷µ„®÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО, mera њah] (J. A. Emerton, вАЬA Note on Proverbs 12:26, вАЭ ZAW 76 [1964]: 191-93). (3) Another option is, вАЬthe righteous guides his friend arightвАЭ (cf. NRSV, NLT).
n2001283:
tc The MT reads вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷≤„®÷є„Ъ÷∞вАО (yakharokh) from II вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (kharakh, вАЬto roastвАЭ?). On the other hand, several versions (LXX, Syriac, Vulgate) reflect a Hebrew Vorlage of вАП„Щ÷Ј„У÷∞„®÷і„Щ„Ъ÷∞вАО (yadrikh) from вАП„У÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (darakh, вАЬto gainвАЭ), meaning: вАЬa lazy person cannot catch his preyвАЭ (suggested by Gemser; cf. NAB). The MT is the more difficult reading, being a hapax legomenon, and therefore should be retained; the versions are trying to make sense out of a rare expression.
tn The verb II вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (kharakh) is a hapax legomenon, appearing in the OT only here. BDB suggests that it means вАЬto start; to set in motionвАЭ (BDB 355 s.v.). The related Aramaic and Syriac verb means вАЬto scorch; to parch,вАЭ and the related Arabic verb means вАЬto roast; to scorch by burningвАЭ; so it may mean вАЬto roast; to fryвАЭ (HALOT 353 s.v. I вАП„Ч„®„ЪвАО). The lazy person canвАЩt be bothered cooking what he has hunted. The Midrash sees an allusion to Jacob and Esau in Genesis 25. M. Dahood translates it: вАЬthe languid man will roast no game for himself, but the diligent will come on the wealth of the steppeвАЭ (вАЬThe Hapax harak in Proverbs 12:27, вАЭ Bib 63 [1982]: 60-62). This hyperbole means that the lazy person does not complete a project.
n2001284: tn Heb вАЬthe precious possession of a man, diligent.вАЭ The LXX reads вАЬbut a valuable possession [is] a pure manвАЭ while Rashi, a highly esteemed 11th century Rabbi, interpreted it as вАЬa precious possession of a man is to be diligentвАЭ (R. Murphy, Proverbs [WBC] 88). The translation assumes that the word вАП„Щ÷Є„І÷Є„®вАО (yaqar, вАЬpreciousвАЭ) should either be a construct form or transposed into predicate position. The implication is not to desire or overvalue possessions themselves but to take care of what one has.
n2001285: tc The MT has вАП„У÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ „†÷∞„™÷і„Щ„С÷Є„ФвАО (derekh netivah) вАЬa way, a path.вАЭ The duplication of meaning is awkward. If the first word is repointed as a Qal participle (вАП„У÷Љ÷є„®÷µ„ЪвАО, dorekh) it could be understood as вАЬtreading a path [that leads toвА¶].вАЭ The editors of BHS propose that the second word be emended to вАП„Ю÷∞„©„Б„Х÷Љ„С÷Є„ФвАО (meshuvah, вАЬ[way of] apostacyвАЭ) or вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„Ґ÷µ„С÷Є„ФвАО (to њevah, вАЬ[way of] abominationвАЭ). The LXX reads вАЬthe ways of the revengeful [lead] to death.вАЭ
n2001286: tc The consonants вАП„Р„Ь÷Њ„Ю„Х„™вАО ( Њl mvt) are vocalized by the MT as вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„Ю÷Є„Х÷ґ„™вАО ( Њal mavet, вАЬno deathвАЭ), perhaps meaning immortality (вАЬthe journey of [her] path is no-deathвАЭ). M. Dahood suggests that it means permanence (вАЬImmortality in Proverbs 12:28, вАЭ Bib 41 [1960]: 176-81). However, many medieval Hebrew mss and all the versions vocalize it as вАП„Р÷ґ„Ь÷Њ„Ю÷Є„Х÷ґ„™вАО ( Њel mavet), meaning вАЬleads to deathвАЭ (cf. NAB, NCV). W. McKane adopts this reading, and suggests that MT is a scribal change toward eternal life (Proverbs [OTL], 451-52). Others adopt this reading because they do not find the term вАЬlifeвАЭ used in Proverbs for eternal life, nor do they find references to immortality elsewhere in Proverbs.
n200131: tc The MT reads вАЬa wise son, discipline of a father.вАЭ Instead of вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„°÷Є„®вАО (musar, вАЬdisciplineвАЭ), G. R. Driver suggested reading this word as вАП„Ю÷∞„Щ÷ї„°÷Ј÷Љ„®вАО (meyussar, вАЬallows himself to be disciplinedвАЭ); see his вАЬHebrew Notes on Prophets and Proverbs,вАЭ JTS 41 (1940): 174. A few Medieval Hebrew manuscripts, the LXX, and the Syriac read вАП„Щ÷і„©÷∞„Б„Ю÷Ј„ҐвАО (yishma њ) вАЬa wise son listens to/obeys his father.вАЭ The translation, вАЬacceptsвА¶discipline,вАЭ reflects the notion intended by either.
n200132: sn The вАЬscofferвАЭ is the worst kind of fool. He has no respect for authority, reviles worship of God, and is unteachable because he thinks he knows it all. The change to a stronger word in the second colonвАФвАЬrebukeвАЭ (вАП„Т÷Є÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО, ga њar)вАФshows that he does not respond to instruction on any level. Cf. NLT вАЬa young mocker,вАЭ taking this to refer to the opposite of the вАЬwise sonвАЭ in the first colon.
n200133: tn Heb вАЬhas not listened.вАЭ The perfect verb has been chosen to emphasize the past pattern of the scoffer.
n200134: tn Heb вАЬlipsвАЭ (so NIV); KJV вАЬmouth.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause for what the lips produce: speech.
n200136: tn Heb вАЬthe desire of the treacherous.вАЭ The verb вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Т÷Ј„УвАО (bagad), here a participle, means вАЬto act treacherously, with duplicity, or to betray.вАЭ
n200137: tn The noun вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, traditionally вАЬsoulвАЭ) has a broad range of meanings, and here denotes вАЬappetiteвАЭ (e.g., Ps 17:9; Prov 23:3; Eccl 2:24; Isa 5:14; Hab 2:5; BDB 660 s.v. 5.c) or (2) вАЬdesireвАЭ (e.g., Deut 12:20; Prov 13:4; 19:8; 21:10; BDB 660 s.v. 6.a).
n200138:
tn Heb вАЬviolence.вАЭ The phrase вАЬthe fruit ofвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is implied by the parallelism. The term вАЬviolenceвАЭ is probably a metonymy of cause: вАЬviolenceвАЭ represents what violence gainsвАФill-gotten gains resulting from violent crime. The wicked desire what does not belong to them.
tc The LXX reads вАЬthe souls of the wicked perish untimely.вАЭ The MT makes sense as it stands.
n200139: tn Heb вАЬmouthвАЭ (so KJV, NAB). The term вАП„§÷ґ÷Љ„ФвАО (peh, вАЬmouthвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of cause for speech.
n2001310: tn Heb вАЬopens wide his lips.вАЭ This is an idiom meaning вАЬto be talkativeвАЭ (BDB 832 s.v. вАП„§÷Є÷Љ„©÷Ј„В„ІвАО Qal). Cf. NIV вАЬspeaks rashlyвАЭ; TEV вАЬa careless talkerвАЭ; CEV вАЬtalk too much.вАЭ
n2001311:
tn Heb вАЬruin belongs to him.вАЭ
sn Tight control over what one says prevents trouble (e.g., Prov 10:10; 17:28; Jas 3:1-12; Sir 28:25). Amenemope advises to вАЬsleep a night before speakingвАЭ (5:15; ANET 422, n. 10). The old Arab proverb is appropriate: вАЬTake heed that your tongue does not cut your throatвАЭ (O. Zockler, Proverbs, 134).
n2001312: tn The noun вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, traditionally вАЬsoulвАЭ) has a broad range of meanings, and here denotes вАЬappetiteвАЭ (e.g., Ps 17:9; Prov 23:3; Eccl 2:24; Isa 5:14; Hab 2:5; BDB 660 s.v. 5.c) or вАЬdesireвАЭ (e.g., Deut 12:20; Prov 19:8; 21:10; BDB 660 s.v. 6.a).
n2001313: sn The contrast is between the вАЬsoul (= appetite) of the sluggardвАЭ (вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б„Х÷є „Ґ÷Є„¶÷µ„ЬвАО, nafsho  њatsel) and the вАЬsoul (= desire) of the diligentвАЭ (вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„Б „Ч÷Є„®÷ї„¶÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, nefesh kharutsim)вАФwhat they each long for.
n2001314: tn The Hitpael verb means вАЬto lust after; to crave.вАЭ A related verb is used in the DecalogueвАЩs prohibition against coveting (Exod 20:17; Deut 5:21).
n2001315: tn Heb вАЬwill be made fatвАЭ (cf. KJV, NASB); NRSV вАЬis richly supplied.вАЭ
n2001316: tn Heb вАЬwill hate.вАЭ The verb вАП„©÷Є„В„†÷µ„РвАО (sane Њ, вАЬto hateвАЭ) can express a range of feelings of dislike or the implications of such. It can, then, have the connotation вАЬto reject, spurnвАЭ (see NIDOTTE 1254 s.v.).
n2001317: tn Heb вАЬa word of falsehood.вАЭ The genitive вАЬfalsehoodвАЭ functions as an attributive genitive. The construct noun вАП„У÷∞÷Љ„С÷Ј„®вАО (devar) means either вАЬwordвАЭ or вАЬthing.вАЭ Hence, the phrase means вАЬa false wordвАЭ or вАЬa false thing.вАЭ
n2001318:
tc The versions render this phrase variously: вАЬis ashamed and without confidenceвАЭ (LXX); вАЬis ashamed and put to the blushвАЭ (Tg. Prov 13:5); вАЬconfounds and will be confoundedвАЭ (Vulgate). The variety is due in part to confusion of вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Р÷Ј„©„БвАО (ba Њash, вАЬto stinkвАЭ) and вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„©„БвАО (bosh, вАЬto be ashamedвАЭ). Cf. NASB вАЬacts disgustingly and shamefully.вАЭ
tn Heb вАЬacts shamefully and disgracefully.вАЭ The verb вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Р÷Ј„©„БвАО (ba Њash) literally means вАЬto cause a stink; to emit a stinking odorвАЭ (e.g., Exod 5:21; Eccl 10:1) and figuratively means вАЬto act shamefullyвАЭ (BDB 92 s.v.). The verb вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷∞„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„®вАО (veyakhpir) means вАЬto display shame.вАЭ Together, they can be treated as a verbal hendiadys: вАЬto act in disgraceful shame,вАЭ or more colorfully вАЬto make a shameful smell,вАЭ or as W. McKane has it, вАЬspread the smell of scandalвАЭ (Proverbs [OTL], 460). W. G. Plaut says, вАЬUnhappily, the bad odor adheres not only to the liar but also to the one about whom he liesвАФespecially when the lie is a big oneвАЭ (Proverbs, 152).
n2001319: sn Righteousness refers to that which conforms to law and order. One who behaves with integrity will be safe from consequences of sin.
n2001320: tn Heb вАЬintegrity of way.вАЭ The term вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞вАО (darekh) is a genitive of specification: вАЬintegrity in respect to his way.вАЭ This means living above reproach in their course of life. Cf. NASB вАЬwhose way is blamelessвАЭ; NAB вАЬwho walks honestly.вАЭ
n2001321: sn Righteousness and wickedness are personified in this proverb to make the point of security and insecurity for the two courses of life.
n2001322: tn The Hitpael of вАП„Ґ÷Є„©÷Ј„Б„®вАО ( њashar, вАЬto be richвАЭ) means вАЬto pretend to be richвАЭ (BDB 799 s.v. вАП„Ґ÷Є„©÷Ј„Б„®вАО Hithp). Here the Hitpael means to show or present oneself in a state (cf. GKC 1256 ¬І54e, Jo√Љon 147 ¬І53i, IBHS 431 ¬І26.2f).
n2001323: tn The Hitpolel of вАП„®„Х÷Љ„©„БвАО (rush, вАЬto be poorвАЭ) means вАЬto pretend to be poorвАЭ (BDB 930 s.v. Hithpolel). The Hitpolel forms of hollow root verbs are the equivalent of Hitpael forms; this Hitpolel functions like the Hitpael in the first part of the verse.
n2001324: sn The proverb seems to be a general observation on certain people in life, but it is saying more. Although there are times when such pretending may not be wrong, the proverb is instructing people to be honest. An empty pretentious display or a concealing of wealth can come to no good.
n2001325: sn As the word вАЬransomвАЭ (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷є„§÷ґ„®вАО, cofer) indicates, the rich are susceptible to kidnapping and robbery. But the poor man pays no attention to blackmailвАФhe does not have money to buy off oppressors. So the rich person is exposed to legal attacks and threats of physical violence and must use his wealth as ransom.
n2001327: tn Heb вАЬhas not heard.вАЭ The perfect verb form has been chosen to emphasize the pattern that has been known from past experience. It implies that the pattern is unlikely to change.
n2001328: tn The term вАП„Т÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (ge њarah) may mean (1) вАЬrebukeвАЭ (so KJV, NASB) or (2) вАЬthreatвАЭ (so NIV; cf. ASV, NRSV, NLT). If вАЬrebukeвАЭ is the sense here, it means that the burdens of society fall on the rich as well as the dangers. But the sense of вАЬthreatвАЭ better fits the context: The rich are threatened with extortion, but the poor are not (cf. CEV вАЬthe poor donвАЩt have that problemвАЭ).
n2001329: sn The images of вАЬlightвАЭ and вАЬdarknessвАЭ are used frequently in scripture. Here вАЬlightвАЭ is an implied comparison: вАЬlightвАЭ represents life, joy, and prosperity; вАЬdarknessвАЭ signifies adversity and death. So the вАЬlight of the righteousвАЭ represents the prosperous life of the righteous.
n2001330: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷і„©÷∞„В„Ю÷Є„ЧвАО (yismakh) is normally translated вАЬto make glad; to rejoice.вАЭ But with вАЬlightвАЭ as the subject, it has the connotation вАЬto shine brightlyвАЭ (see G. R. Driver, вАЬProblems in the Hebrew Text of Proverbs,вАЭ Bib 32 [1951]: 180).
n2001331: sn The lamp is an implied comparison as well, comparing the life of the wicked to a lamp that is going to be extinguished.
n2001332:
tc The LXX adds, вАЬDeceitful souls go astray in sins, but the righteous are pitiful and merciful.вАЭ
tn The verb вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (da њakh) means вАЬto go out [in reference to a fire or lamp]; to be extinguished.вАЭ The idea is that of being made extinct, snuffed out (cf. NIV, NLT). The imagery may have been drawn from the sanctuary where the flame was to be kept burning perpetually. Not so with the wicked.
n2001333: sn The parallelism suggests pride here means contempt for the opinions of others. The wise listen to advice rather than argue out of stubborn pride.
n2001334: tn The particle вАП„®÷Ј„ІвАО (raq, вАЬonlyвАЭ) modifies the noun вАЬcontentionвАЭвАФonly contention can come from such a person.
n2001335: tn The Niphal of вАП„Щ÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„•вАО (ya њats, вАЬto advise; to counselвАЭ) means вАЬto consult together; to take counsel.вАЭ It means being well-advised, receiving advice or consultation (cf. NCV вАЬthose who take advice are wiseвАЭ).
n2001336: tc The MT reads вАП„Ю÷µ„Ф÷µ„С÷ґ„ЬвАО (mehevel) вАЬfrom vanityвАЭ and is followed by KJV and ASV. The word вАП„Ф÷µ„С÷ґ„ЬвАО (hevel) means вАЬvaporвАЭ and figuratively refers to that which is unsubstantial, fleeting, or amounts to nothing (BDB 210 s.v.). The Greek and Latin versions, followed by RSV, reflect вАП„Ю÷∞„С÷є„Ф÷Є„ЬвАО (mevohal, вАЬin hasteвАЭ) which exhibits metathesis. A different pointing of the MT has also been proposed: вАП„Ю÷∞„Ф÷ї„С÷Є÷Љ„ЬвАО (mehubbal) вАЬobtained by fraudвАЭ (HALOT 236 s.v. вАП„Ф„С„ЬвАО), cf. NASB, NIV, CEV. The proverb favors steady disciplined work and saving over get-rich-quick schemes, be they by fraud or by empty dreams (cf. Prov 20:21; 28:20, 22).
n2001337: tn Heb вАЬwill become small.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ю÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ШвАО (ma њat) means вАЬto become small; to become diminished; to become few.вАЭ Money gained without work will diminish quickly, because it was come by too easily. The verb forms a precise contrast with вАП„®÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО (ravah), вАЬto become much; to become many,вАЭ but in the Hiphil, вАЬto multiply; to make much many; to cause increase.вАЭ
n2001338: tn Heb вАЬby handвАЭ; cf. KJV, ASV, NASB вАЬby labor.вАЭ
n2001340: sn The word вАЬhopeвАЭ (вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„Ч÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„™вАО [tokhelet] from вАП„Щ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЬвАО [yakhal]) also has the implication of a tense if not anxious wait.
n2001341: tn The verb is the Pual participle from вАП„Ю÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (mashakh, вАЬto draw; to dragвАЭ).
n2001342: sn Failure in realizing oneвАЩs hopes can be depressing or discouraging. People can bear frustration only so long (W. G. Plaut, Proverbs, 153).
n2001343: tn Heb вАЬa desire that comesвАЭ; cf. CEV вАЬa wish that comes true.вАЭ
n2001344: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2001345: tn Heb вАЬthe word.вАЭ Both the term вАЬwordвАЭ (вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„С÷Є„®вАО, davar) and its parallel вАЬcommandвАЭ (вАП„Ю÷і„¶÷∞„Х÷Є„ФвАО, mitzvah) are used at times for scripture, but probably here for the sageвАЩs teaching. Here the second term gives more specificity to the first.
n2001346: tc The MT reads вАП„Щ÷µ„Ч÷Є„С÷ґ„ЬвАО (yekhavel) the Niphal imperfect of вАП„Ч÷Є„С÷Ј„ЬвАО (khaval). This root may be one of two homonyms, meaning either вАЬa pledge will be seized from himвАЭ or вАЬhe will fare badlyвАЭ (see HALOT 285 s.v. II вАП„Ч„С„ЬвАО and III вАП„Ч„С„ЬвАО). But the BHS editors suggest revocalizing the text to вАП„Щ÷∞„Ч÷ї„С÷Є÷Љ„ЬвАО (yekhubbal, вАЬhe will be broken [for it]вАЭ; cf. NRSV вАЬbring destruction on themselvesвАЭ).
n2001347: tn Heb вАЬfears a commandmentвАЭ; NIV вАЬrespects a command.вАЭ
n2001348: tn Heb вАЬheвАЭ or вАЬthat oneвАЭ [will be rewarded].
n2001349: tc The MT reads вАП„Щ÷∞„©÷ї„Б„Ь÷Є÷Љ„ЭвАО (yeshullam, вАЬwill be rewardedвАЭ); the LXX implies a different vocalization вАП„Щ÷і„©÷∞„Б„Ь÷Є„ЭвАО (yishlam, вАЬwill stay well/healthyвАЭ). Also the LXX then adds: вАЬA crafty son will have no good thing, but the affairs of a wise servant will be prosperous; and his path will be directed rightly.вАЭ
n2001350: tn The term вАП„™„Х÷є„®÷Є„ФвАО (torah) in legal literature means вАЬlaw,вАЭ but in wisdom literature often means вАЬinstruction; teachingвАЭ (BDB 435 s.v.); cf. NAV, NIV, NRSV вАЬteachingвАЭ; NLT вАЬadvice.вАЭ
n2001351: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor.
n2001352: tn Heb вАЬfountain of lifeвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV). The genitive вАП„Ч÷Ј„Щ÷і÷Љ„Щ„ЭвАО (khayyim) functions as a genitive of material, similar to the expression вАЬfountain of water.вАЭ The metaphor means that the teaching of the wise is life-giving. The second colon is the consequence of the first, explaining this metaphor.
n2001353: tn The infinitive construct with preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) gives the result (or, purpose) of the first statement. It could also be taken epexegetically, вАЬby turning.вАЭ
n2001354: tn The term вАЬpersonвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity and smoothness.
n2001355: tn Heb вАЬsnares of deathвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT). The genitive вАП„Ю÷Є„Х÷ґ„™вАО (mavet) functions as an attributive adjective. The term вАЬsnaresвАЭ makes an implied comparison with hunting; death is like a hunter. W. McKane compares the idea to the Ugaritic god Mot, the god of death, carrying people off to the realm of the departed (Proverbs [OTL], 455). The expression could also mean that the snares lead to death.
n2001356: tn Heb вАЬgood insight.вАЭ The expression вАП„©÷µ„В„Ы÷ґ„Ь÷Њ„Ш„Х÷є„СвАО (sekhel tov) describes a person who has good sense, sound judgment, or wise opinions (BDB 968 s.v. вАП„©÷µ„В„Ы÷ґ„ЬвАО).
