n2001358: tn Heb вАЬway,вАЭ frequently for conduct, behavior, or lifestyle.
n2001359: tn The Hebrew has a nominal clause: вАЬthe way [conduct] of the treacherous [is] destruction.вАЭ The verb вАЬends [in]вАЭ is supplied for style and in keeping with the image of a pathway leading to a destination.
n2001360: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„™÷Є„ЯвАО ( Њetan, вАЬenduring; permanent; perennialвАЭ; BDB 450 s.v. вАП„Щ„™„ЯвАО 1), which gives a meaning not consistent with the teachings of Proverbs. Several scholars suggest that the text here needs revision. G. R. Driver suggested that вАП„Ь÷є„РвАО (lo Њ, вАЬnotвАЭ) was dropped before the word by haplography and so the meaning would have been not вАЬenduringвАЭ but вАЬpassing awayвАЭ (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew Text of Proverbs,вАЭ Bib 32 [1951]: 181). The LXX reads вАЬthe ways of the contemptuous [lead] to destructionвАЭ which, supported by the Syriac, may reflect an underlying text of вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„У÷Є„ЭвАО ( Њedam) вАЬtheir calamityвАЭ or just вАП„Р÷µ„Щ„УвАО ( Њed, вАЬcalamity, distressвАЭ; BDB 15 s.v.). The Targum reflects a text of вАП„™÷є„Р„С÷µ„УвАО (to Њved) вАЬwill perish, be destroyed.вАЭ
n2001361: sn The shrewd person knows the circumstances, dangers and pitfalls that lie ahead. So he deals with them wisely. This makes him cautious.
n2001362: tn Heb вАЬspreads openвАЭ [his folly]. W. McKane suggests that this is a figure of a peddler displaying his wares (Proverbs [OTL], 456; cf. NAB вАЬthe fool peddles follyвАЭ). If given a chance, a fool will reveal his foolishness in public. But the wise study the facts and make decisions accordingly.
n2001364: tn The RSV changes this to a Hiphil to read, вАЬplunges [men] into trouble.вАЭ But the text simply says the wicked messenger вАЬfalls into trouble,вАЭ perhaps referring to punishment for his bad service.
n2001366: tn Heb вАЬan envoy of faithfulness.вАЭ The genitive вАП„Р÷±„Ю„Х÷Љ„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО ( Њemunim, вАЬfaithfulnessвАЭ) functions as an attributive adjective: вАЬfaithful envoy.вАЭ The plural form вАП„Р„Ю„Х„†„Щ„ЭвАО (literally, вАЬfaithfulnessesвАЭ) is characteristic of abstract nouns. The term вАЬenvoyвАЭ (вАП„¶÷і„Щ„®вАО, tsir) suggests that the person is in some kind of government service (e.g., Isa 18:2; Jer 49:14; cf. KJV, ASV вАЬambassadorвАЭ). This individual can be trusted to вАЬbring healingвАЭвАФbe successful in the mission. The wisdom literature of the ancient Near East has much to say about messengers.
n2001367: tn The verb вАЬbringsвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the parallelism; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of smoothness.
n2001368: tn The verb III вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (para њ) normally means вАЬto let go; to let aloneвАЭ and here вАЬto neglect; to avoid; to rejectвАЭ (BDB 828 s.v.).
n2001369: tn The phrase вАЬends up inвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the parallelism; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of smoothness.
n2001370: sn Honor and success are contrasted with poverty and shame; the key to enjoying the one and escaping the other is discipline and correction. W. McKane, Proverbs (OTL), 456, notes that it is a difference between a man of weight (power and wealth, from the idea of вАЬheavyвАЭ for вАЬhonorвАЭ) and the man of straw (lowly esteemed and poor).
n2001371: tn The verb III вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„СвАО ( њarav, вАЬto be sweetвАЭ) is stative. The imperfect form of a stative verb should be future tense or modal, not present tense as in most translations.
n2001372: tn Heb вАЬan abomination of fools.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ы÷∞÷Љ„°÷і„Щ„Ь÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (kesilim, вАЬfoolsвАЭ) functions as a subjective genitive: вАЬfools hate to turn away from evilвАЭ (cf. NAB, TEV, CEV). T. T. Perowne says: вАЬIn spite of the sweetness of good desires accomplished, fools will not forsake evil to attain itвАЭ (Proverbs, 103). Cf. Prov 13:12; 29:27.
n2001373: tn The word вАП„®÷Є„ҐвАО (ra њ, вАЬbadвАЭ) can refer to evil and is traditionally taken as such in this verse. However, вАП„®÷Є„ҐвАО can also refer to something of little worth. Perhaps the verse comments on the foolвАЩs unwillingness to forego what is inferior or to avoid cutting corners and to persevere for what is truly desirable.
n2001374: tn Heb вАЬwalks.вАЭ When used with the preposition вАП„Р÷ґ„™вАО ( Њet, вАЬwithвАЭ), the verb вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (halakh, вАЬto walkвАЭ) means вАЬto associate withвАЭ someone (BDB 234 s.v. вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО II.3.b; e.g., Mic 6:8; Job 34:8). The active participle of вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (вАЬto walkвАЭ) stresses continual, durative action. One should stay in close association with the wise, and move in the same direction they do.
n2001375: tc The Kethib of the MT and two important Greek witnesses read вАЬWalk with the wise and become wise.вАЭ The Qere and other important Greek witnesses along with the Syriac, Targum and Vulgate read вАЬOne who walks with the wise will become wise.вАЭ
n2001376: tn The verb form вАП„Щ÷µ„®„Х÷є„Ґ÷ЈвАО (yeroa њ) is the Niphal imperfect of вАП„®÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ҐвАО (ra њa њ), meaning вАЬto suffer hurt.вАЭ Several have attempted to parallel the repetition in the wordplay of the first colon. A. Guillaume has вАЬhe who associates with fools will be left a foolвАЭ (вАЬA Note on the Roots вАП„®÷і„Щ„ҐвАО, вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Ј„ҐвАО, and вАП„®÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ҐвАО in Hebrew,вАЭ JTS 15 [1964]: 294). Knox translated the Vulgate thus: вАЬFool he ends that fool befriendsвАЭ (cited by D. Kidner, Proverbs [TOTC], 104).
n2001377: tn Heb вАЬevil.вАЭ The term вАП„®÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (ra њah, вАЬevilвАЭ) here functions in a metonymical sense meaning вАЬcalamity.вАЭ вАЬGoodвАЭ is the general idea of good fortune or prosperity; the opposite, вАЬevil,вАЭ is likewise вАЬmisfortuneвАЭ (cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV) or calamity.
n2001378: sn This statement deals with recompense in generalizing terms. It is this principle in an absolute sense, without allowing for any of the exceptions that Proverbs itself acknowledges, that JobвАЩs friends applied (incorrectly) to his suffering.
n2001379: sn In ancient Israel the idea of leaving an inheritance was a sign of GodвАЩs blessing; blessings extended to the righteous and not the sinners.
n2001380: sn In the ultimate justice of God, the wealth of the wicked goes to the righteous after death (e.g., Ps 49:10, 17).
n2001381: tn Heb вАЬabundance of food, fallow ground of вАП„®÷Є„Р„©÷і„Б„Щ„ЭвАО (ra Њshim).вАЭ The line has several difficulties. The word вАП„®÷Є„Р„©÷і„Б„Щ„ЭвАО is spelled as if from вАП„®„Р÷є„©„БвАО (ro Њsh, вАЬheadвАЭ) meaning a tribal head, leader, chief (HALOT 1166 s.v.). It is usually assumed however that it is a participle from вАП„®„Х÷Љ„©„БвАО (rush) meaning вАЬto be poorвАЭ (HALOT 1209 s.v.). The lack of a verb also poses a problem. Some translations assume that the food is in the field or the field produces the food (NASB, KJV, BBE, NIV [2011], Holman), but this runs counter to the notion of fallow ground. If it is full of crops, it isnвАЩt fallow (if indeed вАП„†÷і„Щ„®вАО [nir] means prepared unplanted soil). Other translations are modal, stating that the field вАЬmayвАЭ or вАЬwouldвАЭ produce much food (NIV [1973], ESV, NLT, NRSV). Perhaps it is fallow after a harvest; or perhaps the saying is about presuming the crops before they are actually there (like counting your chickens before they are hatched). BDB proposes the possibility: вАЬabundant food [yields] the fallow ground of poor menвАЭ (BDB 644 s.v. вАП„†÷і„Щ„®вАО). If food leads to fallow ground, it may imply not seeing a need to plant all the fields, which later results in poverty. Any of these options seems equally speculative.
n2001382: tc The MT reads вАЬthere is what is swept away without justiceвАЭ (вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷µ„©„Б „†÷і„°÷∞„§÷Љ÷ґ„Ф „С÷∞÷Љ„Ь÷є„Р „Ю÷і„©÷∞„Б„§÷Љ÷Є„ШвАО, veyesh nispeh belo Њ mishpat). The LXX reads вАЬthe great enjoy wealth many years, but some men perish little by little.вАЭ The Syriac reads вАЬthose who have no habitation waste wealth many years, and some waste it completely.вАЭ Tg. Prov 13:23 reads вАЬthe great man devours the land of the poor, and some men are taken away unjustly.вАЭ The Vulgate has вАЬthere is much food in the fresh land of the fathers, and for others it is collected without judgment.вАЭ C. H. Toy says that the text is corrupt (Proverbs [ICC], 277). Nevertheless, the MT makes sense: The ground could produce enough food for people if there were no injustice in the land.
n2001383: sn R. N. Whybray cites an Egyptian proverb that says that вАЬboys have their ears on their backsides; they listen when they are beatenвАЭ (Proverbs [CBC], 80). Cf. Prov 4:3-4, 10-11; Eph 6:4; Heb 12:5-11.
n2001384:
tn Or вАЬis hating.вАЭ Most stative verbs by nature cannot have participle forms. But some do, including вАП„©÷Є„В„†÷µ„РвАО (sane Њ, вАЬto hateвАЭ), in contexts where they take on dynamic overtones. So the nuance may be less on the overall felt emotion, and more on the particular action: the rod-sparer is hating his child (in this regard).
sn The importance of parental disciplining is stressed by the verbs вАЬhateвАЭ and вАЬlove.вАЭ вАЬHatingвАЭ a child in this sense means in essence abandoning or rejecting him; вАЬlovingвАЭ a child means embracing and caring for him. Failure to discipline a child is tantamount to hating himвАФnot caring about his character.
n2001386: tn Heb вАЬhimвАЭ; the referent (his child) is specified in the translation for clarity.
n2001387: tn Heb вАЬseeks him.вАЭ The verb вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (shakhar, вАЬto be diligent; to do something earlyвАЭ; BDB 1007 s.v.) could mean вАЬto be diligent to discipline,вАЭ or вАЬto be early or prompt in disciplining.вАЭ See G. R. Driver, вАЬHebrew Notes on Prophets and Proverbs,вАЭ JTS 41 (1940): 170.
n2001388: tn The noun вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„°÷Є„®вАО (musar, вАЬdisciplineвАЭ) functions as an adverbial accusative of reference: вАЬhe is diligent in reference to discipline.вАЭ
n2001390: tn The noun вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (traditionally вАЬsoulвАЭ; cf. KJV, ASV) here means вАЬappetiteвАЭ (BDB 660 s.v. 5.a).
n2001391:
tn The imperfect verb вАП„™÷Љ÷ґ„Ч÷∞„°÷Є„®вАО (tekhsar) is from the stative root вАП„°÷Є„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (sakhar, вАЬto be devoid of, to decrease, to be emptyвАЭ) and so should be future tense.
sn The wicked may go hungry, or lack all they desire, just as the first colon may mean that what the righteous acquire proves satisfying to them.
n200141:
tc The verb вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„†÷∞„™÷Є„ФвАО (banetah) is singular, while the noun вАП„†÷Є„©÷і„Б„Щ„ЭвАО (nashim) is plural. Because of the lack of agreement between the apparent subject and verb and because of the similar thought in Prov 9:1, the BHS editors suggest two possibilities: (1) to delete the word вАЬwomen/wivesвАЭ and read the line identically as Prov 9:1, or (2) to read вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„©÷і„В„Щ„Э „С÷∞÷Љ„Р÷µ„Щ„™÷Є„ЯвАО (tasim be Њetan) in place of вАП„†÷Є„©÷і„Б„Щ„Э „С÷Є÷Љ„†÷∞„™÷Є„ФвАО (nashim banetah), meaning вАЬWisdom sets up her house with strength.вАЭ Without emending the consonants, the text may also be read as вАЬwivesвАЩ wisdom has built her house,вАЭ or вАЬshe built her house with wivesвАЩ wisdom,вАЭ by reading the noun вАП„Ч÷Є„Ы÷∞„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО (khokhmot) instead of the adjective вАП„Ч÷Ј„Ы÷∞„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО (khakhmot). The personification of Folly in the second half of the verse implies the personification of Wisdom at the beginning.
tn Heb вАЬwise ones of women.вАЭ The construct phrase вАП„Ч÷Ј„Ы÷∞„Ю„Х÷є„™ „†÷Є„©÷і„Б„Щ„ЭвАО (khakhmot nashim) features a wholistic genitive: вАЬwise women.вАЭ The plural functions in a distributive sense: вАЬevery wise woman.вАЭ The contrast is between wise and foolish women (e.g., Prov 7:10-23; 31:10-31).
n200142: tn The perfect tense verb in the first colon and the imperfect verb in the second colon accent the antithetic parallelism. The verse contrasts Lady Wisdom and Lady Folly by painting the picture of what Wisdom has done (and by implication still benefits from) in contrast to what Folly keeps doing (to her own detriment).
n200143: tn Heb вАЬhouse.вАЭ This term functions as a synecdoche of container (= house) for contents (= household, family).
n200144: tn Heb вАЬfear of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) functions as an objective genitive.
n200145: tn Heb вАЬcrooked of waysвАЭ; NRSV вАЬdevious in conduct.вАЭ This construct phrase features a genitive of specification: вАЬcrooked in reference to his ways.вАЭ The term вАЬwaysвАЭ is an idiom for moral conduct. The evidence that people fear the Lord is uprightness; the evidence of those who despise him is the devious ways.
n200146: tn The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) may denote (1) exchange: вАЬin exchange forвАЭ foolish talk there is a rod; or (2) cause: вАЬbecause ofвАЭ foolish talk.
n200147: sn The noun вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (peh, вАЬmouthвАЭ) functions as a metonymy of cause for what is said (вАЬspeech, words, talkвАЭ).
n200148:
tc The MT reads вАП„Т÷Ј÷Љ„Р÷≤„Х÷Є„ФвАО (ga Њavah, вАЬprideвАЭ) which creates an awkward sense вАЬin the mouth of a fool is a rod/shoot of prideвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV), perhaps meaning that pride sprouts from his mouth. The BHS editors suggest emending the form to вАП„Т÷µ÷Љ„Х÷є„ФвАО (gevoh, вАЬdisciplining-rodвАЭ) to create tighter parallelism and irony: вАЬin the mouth of a fool is a rod for the backвАЭ (e.g., Prov 10:13). What the fool says will bring discipline.
tn Heb вАЬa rod of back.вАЭ The noun вАП„Т÷µ÷Љ„Х÷є„ФвАО functions as a genitive of specification: вАЬa rod for his back.вАЭ The fool is punished because of what he says.
n200149: tn Heb вАЬlips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause, meaning what they say. The wise by their speech will find protection.
n2001410: tn Heb вАЬthe strength of oxen.вАЭ The genitive вАП„©„Б„Х÷є„®вАО (shor, вАЬoxenвАЭ) functions as an attributed genitive: вАЬstrong oxen.вАЭ Strong oxen are indispensable for a good harvest, and for oxen to be strong they must be well-fed. The farmer has to balance grain consumption with the work oxen do.
n2001411: tn Heb вАЬa witness of faithfulness.вАЭ The genitive functions in an attributive sense: вАЬfaithful witnessвАЭ (so KJV, NRSV); TEV вАЬreliable witness.вАЭ
n2001412: tn Heb вАЬa witness of falsehood.вАЭ The genitive functions in an attributive sense: вАЬfalse witness.вАЭ
n2001413: sn This saying addresses the problem of legal testimony: A faithful witness does not lie, but a false witness does lieвАФnaturally. The first colon uses the verb вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ц÷Ј„СвАО (kazav, вАЬto lieвАЭ) and the second colon uses the noun вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ц÷Є„СвАО (kazav, вАЬlie; falsehoodвАЭ).
n2001414: sn The вАЬscornerвАЭ (вАП„Ь÷µ„•вАО, lets) is intellectually arrogant; he lacks any serious interest in knowledge or religion. He pursues wisdom in a superficial way so that he can appear wise. The acquisition of wisdom is conditioned by oneвАЩs attitude toward it (J. H. Greenstone, Proverbs, 149).
n2001415: sn It is not that wisdom was unavailable (as if in contradiction to Prov 8). Instead the proverb enters the point of view of the person characterized by derision and scoffing. From their perspective it wasnвАЩt there. As observers we see that the scorner did not find wisdom because of a haughty attitude. Perhaps the proverb is given in a past time reference because it also pictures a person is done with seeking wisdom. They looked. It wasnвАЩt there. They stopped looking.
n2001416: tn The verb вАП„І÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (qalal) is a stative verb meaning to вАЬbe small, insignificant, quick, easy.вАЭ Stative verbs seldom appear in the Niphal, as the verb does here. But in the Niphal they are often ingressive, вАЬknowledge (be)came easy for the discerning.вАЭ
n2001417:
tn The general meaning of the proverb is clear, to avoid association with a fool who is not a source of wisdom. But the precise way that the proverb says it is unclear. The Hebrew in the first colon has the imperative вАП„Ь÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО (lekh) вАЬwalkвАЭ followed by the compound preposition вАП„Ю÷і„†÷Љ÷ґ„Т÷ґ„УвАО (minneged) вАЬacross from,вАЭ вАЬopposite of,вАЭ or rarely вАЬ[away] from in front of [someoneвАЩs eyes].вАЭ The most common use of the preposition yields, вАЬWalk across/abreast from a foolish person and you do not [come to] know knowledgeable lips.вАЭ Many translations interpret it to say вАЬgo/stay away fromвА¶вАЭ (e.g. NIV, ESV, NAS, KJV) while others say вАЬenter into the presence ofвА¶вАЭ (ASV, ERV).
tc Instead of вАП„Ь÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО (lekh) вАЬwalk,вАЭ the LXX reads вАЬall,вАЭ implying the reversal of the two consonants as вАП„Ы÷є÷Љ„ЬвАО (kol). The Hebrew would mean вАЬeverything is opposite of the foolish person.вАЭ This is perhaps an idiomatic way of saying that from the foolвАЩs perspective, everything is opposed to him.
n2001419:
tn As the perfect form of a stative verb, вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„™÷Љ÷ЄвАО (yada њta) may be understood as present or past: thus as the result вАЬyou do not come to knowвАЭ or the basis вАЬyou have not known.вАЭ
tc The MT reads вАП„Х÷Љ„С÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„™÷Љ÷ЄвАО (uval yada њta, вАЬyou do not know [the lips of knowledge]).вАЭ The LXX reflects a Hebrew Vorlage of вАП„Х÷Љ„Ы÷∞„Ь÷µ„Щ÷Њ„У÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО (ukhele da њat) вАЬinstruments of knowledge/discretion.вАЭ The textual variant involves wrong word division and orthographic confusion between вАП„СвАО (bet) and вАП„ЫвАО (kaf). The LXX reading here makes sense if its reading of the first colon is accepted (see earlier note) or if вАП„Ю÷і„†÷Љ÷ґ„Т÷ґ„УвАО (minneged) is separative (вАЬwalk away fromвА¶вАЭ). Both would contrast the value of being with a fool and value of wise lips. The LXX of Proverbs can be loose, but this case seems to be the faithful rendering of a slightly different Hebrew copy. Either the LXX or the MT text could just as easily give rise to the other. Both readings are workable and both give the same general advice. Tg. Prov 14:7 freely interprets the verse: вАЬfor there is no knowledge on his lips.вАЭ C. H. Toy emends the text: вАЬfor his lips do not utter knowledgeвАЭ as in 15:7 (Proverbs [ICC], 285).
n2001420: tn Heb вАЬlips of knowledgeвАЭ (so KJV, ASV). вАЬLipsвАЭ is the metonymy of cause, and вАЬknowledgeвАЭ is an objective genitive (speaking knowledge) or attributive genitive (knowledgeable speech): вАЬwise counsel.вАЭ
n2001421: tn Or вАЬthe prudent [person]вАЭ (cf. KJV, NASB, NIV).
n2001422: tn The Hiphil infinitive construct denotes purpose. Those who are shrewd will use it to give careful consideration to all their ways.
n2001423: tn The word means вАЬdeception,вАЭ but some suggest вАЬself-deceptionвАЭ here (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 466; and D. W. Thomas, вАЬTextual and Philological Notes on Some Passages in the Book of Proverbs,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 286); cf. NLT вАЬfools deceive themselves.вАЭ The parallelism would favor this, but there is little support for it. The word usually means вАЬcraft practiced on others.вАЭ If the line is saying the fool is deceitful, there is only a loose antithesis between the cola.
n2001424: tn The noun вАЬfoolsвАЭ is plural but the verb вАЬmockвАЭ is singular. This has led some to reverse the line to say вАЬguilty/guilt offering mocks foolsвАЭ (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 287); see, e.g., Isa 1:14; Amos 5:22. But lack of agreement between subject and verb is not an insurmountable difficulty.
n2001425:
tc The LXX reads вАЬhouses of transgressors will owe purification.вАЭ Tg. Prov 14:9 has вАЬguilt has its home among fools,вАЭ apparently reading вАП„Щ÷Є„Ь÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (yalin, вАЬto spend the night, dwellвАЭ) instead of вАП„Щ÷Є„Ь÷і„Щ„•вАО (yalits, вАЬto scoff atвАЭ).
tn Heb вАЬguilt.вАЭ The word вАП„Р÷Є„©÷Є„Б„ЭвАО ( Њasham) has a broad range of meanings: вАЬguilt, restitution, guilt-offering.вАЭ According to Leviticus, when someone realized he was guilty he would bring a вАЬreparation offering,вАЭ a sin offering with an additional tribute for restitution (Lev 5:1-6). It would be left up to the guilty to come forward; it was for the kind of thing that only he would know, for which his conscience would bother him. Fools mock any need or attempt to make things right, to make restitution (cf. NIV, NRSV, NCV, TEV).
n2001426: tn The word вАП„®÷Є„¶„Х÷є„ЯвАО (ratson) means вАЬfavor; acceptance; pleasing.вАЭ It usually means what is pleasing or acceptable to God. In this passage it either means that the upright try to make amends, or that the upright find favor for doing so.
n2001429: tn The verb is the Hitpael of II вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„СвАО ( њarav), which means вАЬto take in pledge; to give in pledge; to exchange.вАЭ Here it means вАЬto share [in].вАЭ The proverb is saying that there are joys and sorrows that cannot be shared. No one can truly understand the deepest feelings of another.
n2001430:
tn Heb вАЬhouse.вАЭ The term вАЬhouseвАЭ is a metonymy of subject, referring to their contents: families and family life.
sn Personal integrity ensures domestic stability and prosperity, while lack of such integrity (= wickedness) will lead to the opposite.
n2001431: tn The term вАЬtentвАЭ is a metonymy here referring to the contents of the tent: families.
n2001432:
tn Heb вАЬwhich is straight before a man.вАЭ
sn The proverb contrasts the roadway with the roadвАЩs destination. The pathway immediately ahead is straight and smooth, easy to travel. So it would seem like a good path to follow, except that itвАЩs destination is destruction. One view of the proverb is that the straight road represents wickedness that is disguised or rationalized. Another is that the sage recognizes the ambiguities of life; even when good judgment is used with regard to what a person can see, things may may still turn out quite badly.
n2001433:
tn Or вАЬbut after it are the ways of death.вАЭ The phrase вАЬways of deathвАЭ features the result of these paths. Here death means ruin (cf. Prov 7:27). Possibly the proverb envisions that the initial path which seemed good leads to other paths whose outcomes are all ruinous.
tc The LXX seems to take вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„®÷∞„Ы÷µ„ЩвАО (darkhe, вАЬways ofвАЭ) as вАП„Щ÷Ј„®÷∞„Ы÷∞÷Љ„™÷µ„ЩвАО (yarkete, вАЬdepths/recesses ofвАЭ) and renders вАЬthe depths of Hades,вАЭ but the verse seems to be concerned with events of this life.
n2001434: sn No joy is completely free of grief. There is a joy that is superficial and there is underlying pain that will remain after the joy is gone.
n2001435: tc Heb вАЬand its end, joy, is grief.вАЭ The suffix may be regarded as an Aramaism, a proleptic suffix referring to вАЬjoy.вАЭ Or it may be considered a case of wrong word division, moving the вАП„ФвАО (he) to read вАП„Р÷Ј„Ч÷≤„®÷і„Щ„™ „Ф÷Ј„©÷і÷Љ„В„Ю÷∞„Ч÷Є„ФвАО ( Њakharit hassimkhah, вАЬafter the joy [may be] griefвАЭ) rather than вАП„Р÷Ј„Ч÷≤„®÷і„Щ„™÷Є„Ф÷Љ „©÷і„В„Ю÷∞„Ч÷Є„ФвАО ( Њakharitah simkhah, вАЬafter it, joy, griefвАЭ).
n2001436: tn The phrase вАЬmay beвАЭ is not in the Hebrew but is supplied from the parallelism, which features an imperfect of possibility.
n2001437: tn Heb вАЬa backslidden heart.вАЭ The term вАП„°„Х÷Љ„ТвАО (sug) means вАЬto move away; to move backwards; to depart; to backslideвАЭ (BDB 690 s.v. I вАП„°„Х÷Љ„ТвАО). This individual is the one who backslides, that is, who departs from the path of righteousness.
n2001438: tn Heb вАЬwill be filledвАЭ; cf. KJV, ASV. The verb (вАЬto be filled, to be satisfiedвАЭ) here means вАЬto be repaid,вАЭ that is, to partake in his own evil ways. His faithlessness will come back to haunt him.
n2001439: tn The phrase вАЬwill be rewardedвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is supplied based on the parallelism for the sake of clarity and smoothness.
n2001440: sn The contrast is with the simpleton and the shrewd. The simpleton is the young person who is untrained morally or intellectually, and therefore gullible. The shrewd one is the prudent person, the one who has the ability to make critical discriminations.
n2001441: tn Heb вАЬhis stepвАЭ; cf. TEV вАЬsensible people watch their step.вАЭ
n2001442: tn Heb вАЬfears.вАЭ Since the holy name (Yahweh, translated вАЬthe LordвАЭ) is not used, it probably does not here mean fear of the Lord, but of the consequences of actions.
n2001443: tn The Hitpael of вАП„Ґ÷Є„С÷Ј„®вАО ( њavar, вАЬto pass overвАЭ) means вАЬto pass over the bounds of propriety; to act insolentlyвАЭ (BDB 720 s.v.; cf. ASV вАЬbeareth himself insolentlyвАЭ).
n2001444: tn The verb вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ш÷Ј„ЧвАО here denotes self-assurance or overconfidence. Fools are not cautious and do not fear the consequences of their actions.
n2001445: sn The proverb discusses two character traits that are distasteful to othersвАФthe quick tempered person (вАЬshort of angerвАЭ or impatient) and the crafty person (вАЬman of devicesвАЭ). C. H. Toy thinks that the proverb is antithetical and renders it вАЬbut a wise man enduresвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 292). In other words, the quick-tempered person acts foolishly and loses peopleвАЩs respect, but the wise man does not.
n2001447:
tn The verb вАП„©÷Є„В„†÷µ„РвАО (sane Њ) is stative and as a Niphal is ingressive (вАЬbecome hatedвАЭ); its imperfect form should be future rather than present.
tc The LXX reads вАЬenduresвАЭ (from вАП„†÷Є„©÷Є„В„РвАО, nasa Њ) rather than вАЬis hatedвАЭ (from вАП„©÷Є„В„†÷µ„РвАО, sane Њ). This change seems to have arisen on the assumption that a contrast was needed. It has: вАЬa man of thought endures.вАЭ Other versions take вАП„Ю÷∞„Ц÷і„Ю÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО (mezimmot) in a good sense, but antithetical parallelism is unwarranted here.
n2001448:
tc G. R. Driver proposed reading the verb as вАЬare adornedвАЭ from a supposed verb вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (khalah) derived from the noun вАП„Ч÷≤„Ь÷і„ЩвАО (khali, вАЬornamentвАЭ) (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew Text of Proverbs,вАЭ Bib 32 [1951]: 181). A similar reading is followed by a number of English versions (e.g., NAB, NRSV, NLT).
tn Or вАЬhave taken possession of.вАЭ The verb вАП„†÷Є„Ч÷≤„Ь„Х÷ЉвАО (nakhalu) is a Qal perfect form of вАП„†÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЬвАО (nakhal) вАЬto inherit, to take possession, to maintain as a possession.вАЭ The tense of the translation depends on whether the verb is stative or dynamic. Morphologically it is ambiguous. Based on its lexical meaning, it appears to be a dynamic verb, though it does not occur enough times in the Qal to be certain based on its usage. (All other perfect forms are past and all its imperfect forms could be future. However, Ps 82:8 and Prov 3:35; 11:29; 28:10 could be cases of the present and these all use the imperfect, as dynamic verbs can for present tense.) As a dynamic verb, its perfect form should be understood as past time or perfective. As such the antithetic parallelism of the verse contrasts the verb tenses as well as the subjects and results. The naive have gotten folly and continue in it (unless they change). But the prudent are in a process of putting on knowledge in which they will be crowned with it. If the root is stative it could be understood as present, вАЬThe naive inherit folly.вАЭ
n2001449: tn Or вАЬprudentвАЭ (KJV, NASB, NIV); NRSV, TEV вАЬclever.вАЭ
n2001450: tn The meaning of вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ы÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„®„Х÷ЉвАО (yaktiru, Hiphil imperfect of вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„™÷Ј„®вАО, katar) is elusive. The noun from the same root, вАП„Ы÷ґ÷Љ„™÷ґ„®вАО (keter), refers to a headdress, e.g. a turban or crown in Esther 1:11. The verbal root relates to surrounding. So the picture of wrapping on a turban, or вАЬcrowningвАЭ oneself, may be correct. However it may mean вАЬto encompass knowledge,вАЭ i.e., possess it (parallel to the verb вАЬinheritвАЭ).
n2001451: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЧвАО (shakhakh) means вАЬto crouch, cower, bowвАЭ whether from weariness or in submission. As a dynamic verb in the perfect conjugation form, it is past or perfective. Here the sage takes the viewpoint of assuring the learner of what has happened in the past, asserting it to be prototypical of what will continue to happen. Some translations emphasize the future implication (NIV, NASB, CEV, NLT) while others opt to portray the lesson as a characteristic present (ESV, KJV, Holman).
n2001452: tn The phrase вАЬhave bowedвАЭ does not appear in this line but is implied by the parallelism; it is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2001453: sn J. H. Greenstone suggests that this means that they are begging for favors (Proverbs, 154).
n2001454: tn The adjective is singular. A plurality of people crouching before a single person portrays an even greater extent of difference in power between them.
n2001455: tn Heb вАЬhated.вАЭ The verse is just a statement of fact. The verbs вАЬloveвАЭ and вАЬhateвАЭ must be seen in their connotations: The poor are rejected, avoided, shunnedвАФthat is, hated, but the rich are sought after, favored, embracedвАФthat is, loved.
n2001456: tn Heb вАЬMany are the friends of the rich.вАЭ The participle of the verb вАП„Р÷Є„Ф÷µ„СвАО ( Њahev, вАЬto loveвАЭ) can mean friend.
n2001457: sn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Ј„©„БвАО (kharash) means (1) literally: вАЬto cut in; to engrave; to plow,вАЭ describing the work of a craftsman; and (2) figuratively: вАЬto devise,вАЭ describing the mental activity of planning evil (what will harm people) in the first colon, and planning good (what will benefit them) in the second colon.
n2001458: tn The term вАЬexhibitвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew, but is implied by the antithetic parallelism and supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2001459: tn Heb вАЬloyal-love and truth.вАЭ The two terms вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„У „Х÷ґ„Р÷±„Ю÷ґ„™вАО (khesed ve Њemet) often form a hendiadys: вАЬfaithful loveвАЭ or better вАЬfaithful covenant love.вАЭ
n2001460: sn The Hebrew term вАП„Ґ÷ґ„¶÷ґ„СвАО ( њetsev, вАЬpainful toil; laborвАЭ) is first used in scripture in Gen 3:19 to describe the effects of the Fall. The point here is that people should be more afraid of idle talk than of hard labor.
n2001461: tn Heb вАЬword of lips.вАЭ This construct phrase features a genitive of source (вАЬa word from the lipsвАЭ) or a subjective genitive (вАЬspeaking a wordвАЭ). Talk without work (which produces nothing) is contrasted with labor that produces something.
n2001462: tn The term вАЬbringsвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2001463: sn The noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ч÷∞„°„Х÷є„®вАО (makhsor, вАЬneed; thing needed; povertyвАЭ) comes from the verb вАЬto lack; to be lacking; to decrease; to need.вАЭ A person given to idle talk rather than industrious work will have needs that go unmet.
n2001464:
tc The LXX reads ѕАќ±ќљќњбњ¶ѕБќ≥ќњѕВ (panourgos, вАЬcraftyвАЭ) which suggests deleting the вАП„©„БвАО (shin) from вАП„Ґ÷Є„©÷∞„Б„®÷Є„ЭвАО ( њoshram, вАЬtheir richesвАЭ) and reading a noun or adj. derived from the verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷є„ЭвАО ( њarom, вАЬbe crafty). If the first case of вАЬfollyвАЭ in the second half is also emended, the proverb would read вАЬThe crown of the wise is their craftiness, but the garland of fools is folly.вАЭ
sn C. H. Toy suggests that this line probably means that wealth is an ornament to those who use it well (Proverbs [ICC], 269). J. H. Greenstone suggests that it means that the wisdom of the wise, which is their crown of glory, constitutes their wealth (Proverbs, 155).
n2001465: tc The MT reads вАП„Р÷і„Х÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„™вАО ( Њivelet, вАЬfollyвАЭ). The editors of BHS propose emending the text to вАП„Х÷∞„Ь÷і„Х÷∞„Щ÷Ј„™вАО (velivyat) from вАП„Ь÷і„Х÷∞„Щ÷Є„ФвАО (livyah, вАЬwreath, garlandвАЭ). This would provide the same parallelism (вАЬgarlandвАЭ and вАЬcrownвАЭ) as Prov 4:9. The LXX reads ќіќєќ±ѕДѕБќєќ≤бљі (diatribƒУ, вАЬlifestyleвАЭ). See M. Rotenberg, вАЬThe Meaning of вАП„Р÷і„Х÷ґ÷Љ„Ь÷ґ„™вАО in Proverbs,вАЭ Les≈Т 25 (1960-1961): 201. A similar emendation is followed by NAB (вАЬthe diademвАЭ) and NRSV (вАЬthe garlandвАЭ).
n2001466: tn Heb вАЬa witness of truthвАЭ; cf. CEV вАЬan honest witness.вАЭ
n2001467:
tn The noun вАП„†÷∞„§÷Є„©„Б„Х÷є„™вАО (nefashot) often means вАЬsouls,вАЭ but here вАЬlivesвАЭвАФit functions as a metonymy for life (BDB 659 s.v. вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО 3.c).
sn The setting of this proverb is the courtroom. One who tells the truth вАЬsavesвАЭ (вАП„Ю÷Ј„¶÷і÷Љ„Щ„ЬвАО [matsil, вАЬrescues; deliversвАЭ]) the lives of those falsely accused.
n2001468: tc In the MT the verb lacks agreement with the two nouns in either gender or number so that there is no clear subject: вАЬbut he breathes lies, deceit.вАЭ Revocalizing the consonants from вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷Є„§÷і„Ч÷ЈвАО (veyafiakh, вАЬand he breathes/testifiesвАЭ) to вАП„Х÷і„Щ„§÷µ„Ч÷ЈвАО (vifeakh, вАЬand a witness [of lies]вАЭ) and from вАП„Ю÷і„®÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (mirmah, вАЬdeceitвАЭ) to the Piel participle вАП„Ю÷∞„®÷Ј„Ю÷ґ÷Љ„ФвАО (merammeh, вАЬbetrays, deceives, leads astrayвАЭ) produces a grammatically acceptable text. One may also supply by parallelism вАЬвА¶betrays lives.вАЭ
n2001469: tn Heb вАЬIn the fear of the Lord [there is] confidence of strength.вАЭ The verb вАЬone hasвАЭ is supplied in the translation for the Hebrew nominal clause for the sake of smoothness.
n2001470: tn Heb вАЬconfidence of strength.вАЭ This construct phrase features an attributive genitive: вАЬstrong confidenceвАЭ (so most English versions; NIV вАЬa secure fortressвАЭ).
n2001471: sn The fear of the Lord will not only provide security for the parent but will also be a refuge for children. The line recalls Exod 20:5-6 where children will reap the benefits of the righteous parents. The line could also be read as вАЬhe [= God] will be a refuge for the children.вАЭ
n2001472: sn The verse is similar to Prov 13:14 except that вАЬthe fear of the LordвАЭ has replaced вАЬthe teaching of the wise.вАЭ
n2001473: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2001475: tn The infinitive construct with prefixed вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) indicates the purpose/result of the first line; it could also function epexegetically, explaining how fear is a fountain: вАЬby turningвА¶.вАЭ
n2001476: tn The term вАЬpeopleвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is supplied in the translation for the sake of smoothness.
n2001477: tn Heb вАЬsnares of deathвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT); CEV вАЬdeadly traps.вАЭ
n2001478: tn The preposition serves as the beth essentiaeвАФthe glory is the abundant population, not in it.
n2001479: tn Heb вАЬpeople.вАЭ Cf. NLT вАЬa dwindling nation.вАЭ
n2001480: sn The word means вАЬruin; destruction,вАЭ but in this context it could be a metonymy of effect, the cause being an attack by more numerous people that will bring ruin to the ruler. The proverb is purely a practical and secular saying, unlike some of the faith teachings in salvation history passages.
n2001481: tn Or вАЬSomeone who is slow to anger [has] great understanding.вАЭ The translation treats the Hebrew nominal clause as having predicate-subject word order, similar to predicate position for adjectival clauses. But the issue of basic word order is debated.
n2001482: tn Heb вАЬhasty of spiritвАЭ (so KJV, ASV); NRSV, NLT вАЬa hasty temper.вАЭ One who has a quick temper or a short fuse will be evident to everyone, due to his rash actions.
n2001483: sn The participle вАЬexaltsвАЭ (вАП„Ю÷µ„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, merim) means that this person brings folly to a full measure, lifts it up, brings it to the full notice of everybody.
n2001484: tn Heb вАЬheart of calmnessвАЭ or вАЬheart of healing.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬheart, mindвАЭ) is a metonymy for the emotional state of a person (BDB 660 s.v. 6). The genitive noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„®÷∞„§÷µ÷Љ„РвАО (marpe Њ) functions as an attributive adjective and may be one of two homonyms. 1 вАП„Ю÷Ј„®÷∞„§÷Љ÷µ„РвАО (вАЬhealingвАЭ) derives from the root вАП„®÷Є„§÷Є„РвАО (rapha Њ, вАЬto healвАЭ) and 2 вАП„Ю÷Ј„®÷∞„§÷Љ÷µ„РвАО (вАЬcalmness, gentlenessвАЭ) derives from the root вАП„®÷Є„§÷Є„ФвАО (raphah, вАЬto be slack, looseвАЭ). A healthy spirit is tranquil, bringing peace to the body (J. H. Greenstone, Proverbs, 158).
n2001485: tn Heb вАЬis the life of the fleshвАЭ (so KJV, ASV); NAB, NIV вАЬgives life to the body.вАЭ
n2001486: tn The term вАП„І÷і„†÷∞„Р÷Є„ФвАО (qin Њah, вАЬenvyвАЭ) refers to passionate zeal or вАЬjealousyвАЭ (so NAB, NCV, TEV, NLT), depending on whether the object is out of bounds or within oneвАЩs rights. In the good sense one might be consumed with zeal to defend the institutions of the sanctuary. But as envy or jealousy the word describes an intense and sometimes violent excitement and desire that is never satisfied.
n2001487: tn Heb вАЬrottenness of bones.вАЭ The term вАЬbonesвАЭ may be a synecdoche representing the entire body; it is in contrast with вАЬfleshвАЭ of the first colon. One who is consumed with envy finds no tranquility or general sense of health in body or spirit.
n2001488: tn Heb вАЬan oppressor of the poor.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„©÷Ј„Б„ІвАО ( њashaq) normally means вАЬto oppressвАЭ (as in many English versions). However, here it might mean вАЬto slander.вАЭ See J. A. Emerton, вАЬNotes on Some Passages in the Book of Proverbs,вАЭ JTS 20 (1969): 202-22.
n2001489: sn In the Piel this verb has the meaning of вАЬto reproach; to taunt; to say sharp things againstвАЭ someone (cf. NIV вАЬshows contempt forвАЭ). By oppressing the poor one taunts or mistreats God because that person is in the image of GodвАФhence the reference to his/her maker, or вАЬCreator.вАЭ To ridicule what God made is to ridicule God himself.
n2001490:
tn Or вАЬwhoever shows favor to the needy honors himвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, and most translations, except KJV). While being an вАЬoppressorвАЭ contrasts вАЬshowing favorвАЭ and to вАЬhave insultedвАЭ contrasts вАЬhonoringвАЭ the Creator, the proverb may also make its contrast by switching which element is the subject and which is verbal. In the first half вАЬthe oppressorвАЭ occurs first; the second half begins with vav (and/but) plus вАЬthe one who honors him. When the second half of a proverb begins with vav plus a participle, the overwhelming trend is that the participle is the subject, or occasionally the object of the sentence. On the rare occasions that a participle is the subject of a finite verb in a clause where the verb comes first, eight occur in the A-line and only one in the B-line (6:29 where the A-line is verbless and the B-line places focus on the verb). In some cases it is ambiguous whether the participle is verbal or substantival (11:17; 12:16; 16:2; 17:3; 21:2; 29:13) but these probably act as a predicate nominative. In other cases where another participle supplies the verbal element in the B-line (10:19; 11:13, 15; 16:28; 17:9; 19:2) the lead participle is the subject and the verbal participle is second. Thus standard syntax expects to read the line with вАЬthe one who honors himвАЭ as the subject. Showing favor to the needy is an outgrowth of the character of honoring the Lord. This may also elucidate the contrast between the verb forms. What act(s) it took to be вАЬan oppressor of the poorвАЭ qualify as having insulted (perfect verb) the Maker. Insult has been given; that mark continues. But the one who honors him [the Maker] keeps (participle) being gracious to the needy.
sn The phrase вАЬshows favorвАЭ is contrasted with the term вАЬoppresses.вАЭ To вАЬshow favorвАЭ means to be gracious to (or treat kindly) those who do not deserve it or cannot repay it. It is treatment that is gratis. This honors God because he commanded it to be done (Prov 14:21; 17:5; 19:17).
n2001491: tn Or вАЬduring his troubleвАЭ (i.e., when catastrophe comes). The noun вАП„®÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (ra њah) can refer to evil (so KJV, NASB, ESV, NRSV) or to calamity (CEV, NIV, NLT).
n2001492: tc The MT reads вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Ю„Х÷є„™„Х÷євАО (bemoto, вАЬin his deathвАЭ). The LXX reads вАЬin his integrity,вАЭ implying the switching of two letters to вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„™„Х÷Љ„Ю÷Љ„Х÷євАО (betummo). The LXX is followed by some English versions (e.g., NAB вАЬin his honesty,вАЭ NRSV вАЬin their integrity,вАЭ and TEV вАЬby their integrityвАЭ). For all other cases of the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„°÷Є„ФвАО (khasah, вАЬto take refugeвАЭ), the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) indicates what the person relies on, not what they take refuge through, and it is unlikely that the righteous rely on death or see death as a refuge.
n2001493: tc The MT says вАЬit [wisdom] is known,вАЭ but this runs counter to the rest of ProverbsвАЩ teaching, making it sound sarcastic at best. The LXX and the Syriac negate the clause, saying it is вАЬnot known in the heart foolsвАЭ (cf. NAB, NRSV, TEV, NLT), which suggests the word вАП„Ь÷є„РвАО (lo Њ, вАЬnotвАЭ) has dropped out. The Targum supports reading вАП„Р÷і„Х÷Љ÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„™вАО ( Њivvelet) вАЬfolly is in the heart of fools.вАЭ Thomas connects the verb to the Arabic root wd њ and translates it вАЬin fools it is suppressed.вАЭ See D. W. Thomas, вАЬThe Root вАП„Щ„У„ҐвАО in Hebrew,вАЭ JTS 35 (1934): 302-3.
n2001494: tn Heb вАЬin the inner partвАЭ; ASV вАЬin the inward partвАЭ; NRSV вАЬin the heart of fools.вАЭ
n2001495: sn The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„®„Х÷є„Ю÷µ„ЭвАО (teromem, translated вАЬexaltsвАЭ) is a Polel imperfect; it means вАЬto lift up; to raise up; to elevate.вАЭ Here the upright dealings of the leaders and the people will lift up the people. The peopleвАЩs condition in that nation will be raised.
n2001496: tn The term is the homonymic root II вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„УвАО (khesed, вАЬshame; reproachвАЭ; BDB 340 s.v.), as reflected by the LXX translation. Rabbinic exegesis generally took it as I вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„УвАО (вАЬloyal love; kindnessвАЭ) as if it said, вАЬeven the kindness of some nations is a sin because they do it only for a showвАЭ (so Rashi, a Jewish scholar who lived a.d. 1040-1105).
n2001497: tn Heb вАЬthe favor of a king.вАЭ The noun вАЬkingвАЭ functions as a subjective genitive: вАЬthe king shows favorвА¶.вАЭ
n2001498: sn The wise servant is shown favor, while the shameful servant is shown anger. Two Hiphil participles make the contrast: вАП„Ю÷Ј„©„В÷∞„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЬвАО (maskil, вАЬwiseвАЭ) and вАП„Ю÷µ„С÷і„Щ„©„БвАО (mevish, вАЬone who acts shamefullyвАЭ). The wise servant is a delight and enjoys the favor of the king because he is skillful and clever. The shameful one botches his duties; his indiscretions and incapacity expose the master to criticism (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 470).
n200151: tn Heb вАЬsoft answer.вАЭ The adjective вАП„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (rakh, вАЬsoft; tender; gentleвАЭ; BDB 940 s.v.) is more than a mild response; it is conciliatory, an answer that restores good temper and reasonableness (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 477). Gideon illustrates this kind of answer (Judg 8:1-3) that brings peace.
n200152: tn Heb вАЬword of harshnessвАЭ; KJV вАЬgrievous words.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ґ÷ґ„¶÷ґ„СвАО ( њetsev, вАЬpain, hurtвАЭ) functions as an attributive genitive. The term вАП„Ґ÷ґ„¶÷ґ„СвАО refers to something that causes pain (BDB 780 s.v. I вАП„Ґ÷ґ„¶÷ґ„СвАО). For example, JephthahвАЩs harsh answer led to war (Judg 12:1-6).
n200153: tn Heb вАЬraises anger.вАЭ A common response to painful words is to let oneвАЩs temper flare up.
n200154: sn The contrast is between the вАЬtongue of the wiseвАЭ and the вАЬmouth of the fool.вАЭ Both expressions are metonymies of cause; the subject matter is what they say. How wise people are can be determined from what they say.
n200155: tn Or вАЬmakes knowledge acceptableвАЭ (so NASB). The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷µ„Щ„Ш÷і„Щ„СвАО (tetiv, Hiphil imperfect of вАП„Щ÷Є„Ш÷Ј„СвАО [yatav, вАЬto be goodвАЭ]) can be translated вАЬto make goodвАЭ or вАЬto treat in a good [or, excellent] wayвАЭ (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 303). M. Dahood, however, suggests emending the text to вАП„™÷Љ÷µ„Щ„Ш÷і„Щ„£вАО (tetif) which is a cognate of вАП„†÷Є„Ш÷Ј„£вАО (nataf, вАЬdripвАЭ), and translates вАЬtongues of the sages drip with knowledgeвАЭ (Proverbs and Northwest Semitic Philology, 32-33). But this change is gratuitous and unnecessary.
n200156: sn The Hiphil verb вАП„Щ÷Ј„С÷і÷Љ„Щ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (yabbia њ) means вАЬto pour out; to emit; to cause to bubble; to belch forth.вАЭ The fool bursts out with reckless utterances (cf. TEV вАЬspout nonsenseвАЭ).
n200157: sn The proverb uses anthropomorphic language to describe GodвАЩs exacting and evaluating knowledge of all people.
n200158: tn The form вАП„¶÷є„§„Х÷є„™вАО (tsofot, вАЬwatchingвАЭ) is a feminine plural participle agreeing with вАЬeyes.вАЭ GodвАЩs watching eyes comfort good people but convict evil.
n200159: tn Heb вАЬa tongue.вАЭ The term вАЬtongueвАЭ is a metonymy of cause for what is produced: speech.
n2001510: tn Heb вАЬa tongue of healing.вАЭ A healing tongue refers to speech that is therapeutic or soothing. It is a source of vitality.
n2001511: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2001513: tn Heb вАЬperversion in it.вАЭ The referent must be the tongue, representing speech, from the first line; so this has been supplied in the translation for clarity. A tongue that is twisted, perverse, or deceitful is a way of describing deceitful speech. Such words will crush the spirit.
n2001514: tn Heb вАЬis a fracture in the spirit.вАЭ
n2001515: tn Heb вАЬis prudentвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NRSV); NCV, NLT вАЬis wise.вАЭ Anyone who accepts correction or rebuke will become prudent in life.
n2001516: sn The Hebrew noun вАП„Ч÷є„°÷ґ„ЯвАО (khosen) means вАЬwealth; treasure.вАЭ Prosperity is the reward for righteousness. This is true only in so far as a proverb can be carried in its application, allowing for exceptions. The Greek text for this verse has no reference for wealth, but talks about amassing righteousness.
n2001517: tn Heb вАЬbeing ruined.вАЭ The Niphal participle of вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО ( њakhar; вАЬto disturb, trouble, ruinвАЭ) may be understood substantivally, meaning вАЬdisturbance, calamityвАЭ (BDB 747 s.v. вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО) or a вАЬthing troubled,вАЭ thus perhaps вАЬ[it] is ruined/ruinous.вАЭ Or it may be viewed verbally, вАЬwill be ruinedвАЭ (HALOT 824 s.v. вАП„Ґ„Ы„®вАО nif). The LXX translates вАЬwill be ruined.вАЭ
n2001518: tc The verb of the first colon, вАП„Щ÷∞„Ц÷Є„®„Х÷ЉвАО (yezaru, вАЬthey scatterвАЭ) is difficult because it does not fit the second very wellвАФa heart does not вАЬscatterвАЭ or вАЬspreadвАЭ knowledge. SymmachusвАЩ Greek translation uses ѕЖѕЕќїќђѕГѕГѕЙ (phulass≈Н, вАЬto guard, keepвАЭ) suggesting his text read вАП„Щ÷і„¶÷∞÷Љ„®„Х÷ЉвАО (yitseru) from вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„®вАО (natsar, вАЬto guard, keep watch, comply withвАЭ). The LXX uses a form of ќіќ≠ѕЙ (de≈Н, вАЬto bindвАЭ). Although binding (often being bound as a prisoner) might be related to guarding, ќіќ≠ѕЙ does not otherwise represent вАП„†÷Є„¶÷Ј„®вАО in the LXX. Still the editors of BHS and C. H. Toy (Proverbs [ICC], 305) suggest reading вАП„Щ÷і„¶÷∞÷Љ„®„Х÷ЉвАО (yitseru, вАЬthey guardвАЭ).
n2001519:
tn The Hebrew вАП„Ь÷є„Р÷Њ„Ы÷µ„ЯвАО (lo Њ khen) could be вАЬnot soвАЭ (HALOT 482 s.v. II вАП„Ы÷µ÷Љ„ЯвАО) or вАЬnot right, incorrect, wrongвАЭ (HALOT 482 s.v. I вАП„Ы÷µ÷Љ„ЯвАО), which is supported by the LXX: вАЬhearts of fools are unstable.вАЭ If вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬheart, mindвАЭ) is understood to represent thinking, then, accepting the emendation in the first line, the proverb may say, вАЬThe lips of the wise preserve knowledge, but the thoughts of fools are incorrect.вАЭ
sn The phrase вАЬthe heart of foolsвАЭ emphasizes that fools do not comprehend knowledge. Cf. NCV вАЬthere is no knowledge in the thoughts of fools.вАЭ
n2001520: tn Heb вАЬan abomination of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) functions as a subjective genitive: вАЬthe Lord abhors.вАЭ Cf. NIV вАЬthe Lord detestsвАЭ; NCV, NLT вАЬthe Lord hatesвАЭ; CEV вАЬthe Lord is disgusted.вАЭ
n2001521: sn The sacrifices of the wicked are hated by the Lord because the worshipers are insincere and blasphemous (e.g., Prov 15:29; 21:3; 28:9; Ps 40:6-8; Isa 1:10-17). In other words, the spiritual condition of the worshiper determines whether or not the worship is acceptable to God.
n2001522: sn J. H. Greenstone notes that if God will accept the prayers of the upright, he will accept their sacrifices; for sacrifice is an outer ritual and easily performed even by the wicked, but prayer is a private and inward act and not usually fabricated by unbelievers (Proverbs, 162).
n2001523: tn Heb вАЬ[is] his pleasure.вАЭ The third person masculine singular suffix functions as a subjective genitive: вАЬhe is pleased.вАЭ God is pleased with the prayers of the upright.
n2001524: tn Heb вАЬan abomination of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) functions as a subjective genitive: вАЬthe Lord abhors.вАЭ
n2001525: tn The verb вАП„Р÷Є„Ф÷µ„СвАО ( Њahev, вАЬto loveвАЭ) is stative, so its imperfect form should be future; it still speaks of a general truth.
n2001527: sn God hates the way of the wicked, that is, their lifestyle and things they do. God loves those who pursue righteousness, the Piel verb signifying a persistent pursuit. W. G. Plaut says, вАЬHe who loves God will be moved to an active, persistent, and even dangerous search for justiceвАЭ (Proverbs, 170).
n2001528: tn The two lines are parallel synonymously, so the вАЬsevere disciplineвАЭ of the first colon is parallel to вАЬwill dieвАЭ of the second. The expression вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„°÷Є„® „®÷Є„ҐвАО (musar ra њ, вАЬsevere disciplineвАЭ) indicates a discipline that is catastrophic or harmful to life.
n2001529: sn If this line and the previous line are synonymous, then the one who abandons the way also refuses any correction, and so there is severe punishment. To abandon the way means to leave the life of righteousness which is the repeated subject of the book of Proverbs.
n2001530: tn Heb вАЬSheol and AbaddonвАЭ (вАП„©÷∞„Б„Р„Х÷є„Ь „Х÷Ј„Р÷≤„С÷Ј„У÷Љ„Х÷є„ЯвАО (she Њol va Њavaddon); so ASV, NASB, NRSV; cf. KJV вАЬHell and destructionвАЭ; NAB вАЬthe nether world and the abyss.вАЭ These terms represent the remote underworld and all the mighty powers that reside there (e.g., Prov 27:20; Job 26:6; Ps 139:8; Amos 9:2; Rev 9:11). The Lord knows everything about this remote region.
n2001531: tn The construction вАП„Р÷Ј„£ „Ы÷і÷Љ„ЩвАО ( Њaf ki, вАЬhow much more!вАЭ) introduces an argument from the lesser to the greater: If all this is open before the Lord, how much more so human hearts. вАЬHeartsвАЭ here is a metonymy of subject, meaning the motives and thoughts (cf. NCV вАЬthe thoughts of the livingвАЭ).
n2001532: tn Heb вАЬthe hearts of the sons of man,вАЭ although here вАЬsons of manвАЭ simply means вАЬmenвАЭ or вАЬhuman beings.вАЭ
n2001533:
tn The verb вАП„Р÷Є„Ф÷µ„СвАО ( Њahev, вАЬto loveвАЭ) is stative, so its imperfect form should be future tense.
sn This is an understatement, the opposite being intended (a figure called tapeinosis). A scorner rejects any efforts to reform him.
n2001534: tn The form вАП„Ф„Х÷є„Ы÷µ„Ч÷ЈвАО (hokheakh) is a Hiphil infinitive absolute. It could function as the object of the verb (cf. NIV, NRSV) or as a finite verb (cf. KJV, NASB, NLT). The latter has been chosen here because of the prepositional phrase following it, although that is not a conclusive argument.
n2001535: tc The MT has вАП„Р÷ґ„ЬвАО ( Њel, вАЬto [the wise]вАЭ), suggesting seeking the advice of the wise. The LXX, however, has вАЬwith the wise,вАЭ suggesting вАП„Р÷ґ„™вАО ( Њet).
n2001536: tn The contrast in this proverb is between the вАЬjoyful heartвАЭ (Heb вАЬa heart of joy,вАЭ using an attributive genitive) and the вАЬpainful heartвАЭ (Heb вАЬpain of the heart,вАЭ using a genitive of specification).
n2001537: sn The verb вАП„Щ÷µ„Щ„Ш÷і„СвАО (yetiv) normally means вАЬto make good,вАЭ but here вАЬto make the face good,вАЭ that is, there is a healthy, favorable, uplifted expression. The antithesis is the pained heart that crushes the spirit. C. H. Toy observes that a broken spirit is expressed by a sad face, while a cheerful face shows a courageous spirit (Proverbs [ICC], 308).
n2001538: tn Or вАЬheart.вАЭ The Hebrew term is вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ).
n2001539: tc The idea expressed in the second colon does not make a strong parallelism with the first with its emphasis on seeking knowledge. Its poetic image of feeding (a hypocatastasis) would signify the acquisition of follyвАФthe fool has an appetite for it. D. W. Thomas suggests the change of one letter, вАП„®вАО (resh) to вАП„УвАО (dalet), to obtain a reading вАП„Щ÷і„У÷∞„Ґ÷ґ„ФвАО (yid њeh); this he then connects to an Arabic root da њa with the meaning вАЬsought, demandedвАЭ to form what he thinks is a better parallel (вАЬTextual and Philological Notes on Some Passages in the Book of Proverbs,вАЭ VTSup 3 [1955]: 285). But even though the parallelism is not as precise as some would prefer, there is insufficient warrant for such a change.
n2001540: sn The вАЬdaysвАЭ represent what happens on those days (metonymy of subject).
n2001541:
tn The contrast is between the вАЬafflictedвАЭ and the вАЬgood of heartвАЭ (a genitive of specification, вАЬcheerful/healthy heart/spirit/attitudeвАЭ).
sn The parallelism suggests that the afflicted is one afflicted within his spirit, for the proverb is promoting a healthy frame of mind.
n2001544: sn The image of a continual feast signifies the enjoyment of what life offers (cf. TEV вАЬhappy peopleвА¶enjoy lifeвАЭ). The figure is a hypocatastasis; among its several implications are joy, fulfillment, abundance, pleasure.
n2001545: sn One of the frequent characteristics of wisdom literature is the вАЬbetterвАЭ saying; it is a comparison of different but similar things to determine which is to be preferred. These two verses focus on spiritual things being better than troubled material things.
n2001546: sn Turmoil refers to anxiety; the fear of the Lord alleviates anxiety, for it brings with it contentment and confidence.
n2001547: sn Not all wealth has turmoil with it. But the proverb is focusing on the comparison of two thingsвАФfear of the Lord with little and wealth with turmoil. Between these two, the former is definitely better.
n2001548: tn Heb вАЬand love there.вАЭ This clause is a circumstantial clause introduced with vav, that becomes вАЬwhere there is love.вАЭ The same construction is used in the second colon.
n2001549: sn Again the saying concerns troublesome wealth: Loving relationships with simple food are better than a feast where there is hatred. The ideal, of course, would be loving family and friends with a great meal in addition, but this proverb is only comparing two things.
n2001550: tn Heb вАЬa man of wrathвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬa wrathful man.вАЭ The term вАЬwrathвАЭ functions as an attributive genitive: вАЬan angry person.вАЭ He is contrasted with the вАЬslow of anger,вАЭ so he is a вАЬquick-tempered personвАЭ (cf. NLT вАЬa hotheadвАЭ).
n2001551: tn Heb вАЬslow of anger.вАЭ The noun вАЬangerвАЭ functions as a genitive of specification: slow in reference to anger, that is, slow to get angry, patient.
n2001552: tn The Hiphil verb вАП„Щ÷Ј„©„Б÷∞„І÷і„Щ„ШвАО (yashqit) means вАЬto cause quietness; to pacify; to allayвАЭ the strife or quarrel (cf. NAB вАЬallays discordвАЭ). This type of person goes out of his way to keep things calm and minimize contention; his opposite thrives on disagreement and dispute.
n2001553: sn The fact that вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv) is used for вАЬquarrel; strifeвАЭ strongly implies that the setting is the courtroom or other legal setting (the gates of the city). The hot-headed person is eager to turn every disagreement into a legal case.
n2001554: tn Heb вАЬlike an overgrowthвАЭ; NRSV вАЬovergrown with thornsвАЭ; cf. CEV вАЬlike walking in a thorn patch.вАЭ The point of the simile is that the path of life taken by the lazy person has many obstacles that are painfulвАФit is like trying to break through a hedge of thorns. The LXX has вАЬstrewn with thorns.вАЭ
n2001555: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2001556: sn The contrast to the вАЬthorny wayвАЭ is the highway, the Hebrew word signifying a well built-up road (вАП„°÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО, salal, вАЬto heap upвАЭ). The upright have no reason to swerve, duck, or detour, but may expect вАЬclear sailing.вАЭ Prov 28:19 pairs similar concepts while Prov 6:10 and 10:26 speak of the hardships associated with laziness.
n2001558: tn Heb вАЬa fool of a man,вАЭ a genitive of specification.
n2001559: sn The proverb is almost the same as 10:1, except that вАЬdespisesвАЭ replaces вАЬgrief.вАЭ This adds the idea of the callousness of the one who inflicts grief on his mother (D. Kidner, Proverbs [TOTC], 116).
n2001560: tn Heb вАЬlacking of mind.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) refers by metonymy to thinking, and by extension to discernment, wisdom, good sense (cf. NIV вАЬjudgmentвАЭ). The one who has not developed this ability to make proper choices finds great delight in folly.
n2001561: tn Heb вАЬa man of understandingвАЭ (so KJV, NIV); NLT вАЬa sensible person.вАЭ
n2001562: tn The Hebrew construction is вАП„Щ÷∞„Щ÷Ј„©÷ґ÷Љ„Б„®÷Њ„Ь÷Є„Ы÷ґ„™вАО (yeyasher lakhet, вАЬmakes straight [to] goвАЭ). This is a verbal hendiadys, in which the first verb, the Piel imperfect, becomes adverbial, and the second form, the infinitive construct of вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО, halakh, becomes the main verb: вАЬgoes straight aheadвАЭ (cf. NRSV).
n2001563: tn Heb вАЬgo wrongвАЭ (so NRSV, NLT). The verb is the Hiphil infinitive absolute from вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО, parar, which means вАЬto break; to frustrate; to go wrongвАЭ (HALOT 975 s.v. I вАП„§„®„®вАО 2). The plans are made ineffectual or are frustrated when there is insufficient counsel.
n2001564: sn The proverb says essentially the same thing as 11:14, but differently.
n2001565: tn Heb вАЬjoy to the manвАЭ or вАЬthe man has joy.вАЭ
n2001566: tn Heb вАЬin the answer of his mouthвАЭ (so ASV); NASB вАЬin an apt answer.вАЭ The term вАЬmouthвАЭ is a metonymy of cause for what he says. But because the parallelism is loosely synonymous, the answer given here must be equal to the good word spoken in season. So it is an answer that is proper or fitting.
n2001567: tn Heb вАЬin its season.вАЭ To say the right thing at the right time is useful; to say the right thing at the wrong time is counterproductive.
n2001568: tn There is disagreement over the meaning of the term translated вАЬupward.вАЭ The verse is usually taken to mean that вАЬupwardвАЭ is a reference to physical life and well-being (cf. NCV), and вАЬgoing down to SheolвАЭ is a reference to physical death, that is, the grave, because the concept of immortality is said not to appear in the book of Proverbs. The proverb then would mean that the wise live long and healthy lives. But W. McKane argues (correctly) that вАЬupwardsвАЭ in contrast to Sheol, does not fit the ways of describing the worldly pattern of conduct and that it is only intelligible if taken as a reference to immortality (Proverbs [OTL], 480). The translations вАЬupwardsвАЭ and вАЬdownwardsвАЭ are not found in the LXX. This has led some commentators to speculate that these terms were not found in the original, but were added later, after the idea of immortality became prominent. However, this is mere speculation.
n2001569: tn Heb вАЬto the wise [man],вАЭ because the form is masculine.
n2001570: tn The term вАП„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„ЯвАО (lema њan, вАЬin order toвАЭ) introduces a purpose clause; the path leads upward in order to turn the wise away from Sheol.
n2001571: tn Heb вАЬto turn from Sheol downwardвАЭ; cf. NAB вАЬthe nether world below.вАЭ
n2001572: sn The вАЬproudвАЭ have to be understood here in contrast to the widow, and their вАЬhouseвАЭ has to be interpreted in contrast to the widowвАЩs territory. The implication may be that the вАЬproudвАЭ make their gain from the needy, and so God will set the balance right.
n2001573: sn The Lord administers justice in his time. The Lord champions the widow, the orphan, the poor, and the needy. These people were often the prey of the proud, who would take and devour their houses and lands (e.g., 1 Kgs 21; Prov 16:19; Isa 5:8-10).
n2001574: tn Heb вАЬan abomination of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) functions as a subjective genitive: вАЬthe Lord abhors.вАЭ
n2001575: tn The noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ч÷∞„©„Б÷∞„С„Х÷є„™вАО (makhshevot) means вАЬthoughtsвАЭ (so KJV, NIV, NLT), from the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„СвАО (khashav, вАЬto think; to reckon; to deviseвАЭ). So these are intentions, what is being planned (cf. NAB вАЬschemesвАЭ).
n2001576: tn The word вАП„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (ra њ, вАЬevil, wickedвАЭ) is a genitive of source or subjective genitive, meaning the plans that the wicked deviseвАФвАЬwicked plans.вАЭ
n2001577: sn The contrast is between the вАЬthoughtsвАЭ and the вАЬwords.вАЭ The thoughts that are designed to hurt people the Lord hates; words that are pleasant (вАП„†÷є„Ґ÷Ј„ЭвАО, no њam), however, are pure (to him). What is pleasant is delightful, lovely, enjoyable.
n2001578: tc The MT simply has вАЬbut pleasant words are pureвАЭ (Heb вАЬbut pure [plural] are the words of pleasantnessвАЭ). Some English versions add вАЬto himвАЭ to make the connection to the first part (cf. NAB, NIV). The LXX has: вАЬthe sayings of the pure are held in honor.вАЭ The Vulgate has: вАЬpure speech will be confirmed by him as very beautiful.вАЭ The NIV has paraphrased here: вАЬbut those of the pure are pleasing to him.вАЭ
n2001579: tn Heb вАЬthe one who gains.вАЭ The phrase вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„¶÷µ„Ґ÷Ј „С÷Љ÷Є„¶÷Ј„ҐвАО (botsea њ batsa њ) is a participle followed by its cognate accusative. This refers to a person who is always making the big deal, getting the larger cut, or in a hurry to get rich. The verb, though, makes it clear that the gaining of a profit is by violence and usually unjust, since the root has the idea of вАЬcut off; break off; gain by violence.вАЭ The line is contrasted with hating bribes, and so the gain in this line may be through bribery.
n2001580: sn The participle вАЬtroublesвАЭ (вАП„Ґ÷є„Ы÷µ„®вАО,  њokher) can have the connotation of making things difficult for the family, or completely ruining the family (cf. NAB). In Josh 7:1 Achan took some of the вАЬbanned thingsвАЭ and was put to death: Because he вАЬtroubled Israel,вАЭ the Lord would вАЬtroubleвАЭ him (take his life, Josh 7:25).
n2001582: tn Heb вАЬgiftsвАЭ (so KJV). Gifts can be harmless enough, but in a setting like this the idea is that the вАЬgiftвАЭ is in exchange for some вАЬprofit [or, gain].вАЭ Therefore they are bribes (cf. ASV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT), and to be hated or rejected. Abram, for example, would not take anything that the king of Sodom had to offer, вАЬlest [he] say, вАЬI have made Abram richвАЭ (Gen 14:22-24).
n2001583: tn Or вАЬmind.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev) can refer to the вАЬmindвАЭ or вАЬheartвАЭ and represent a personвАЩs thinking, feeling, or will.
n2001584: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷ґ„Ф÷∞„Т÷ґ÷Љ„ФвАО (yehgeh) means вАЬto muse; to meditate; to consider; to study.вАЭ It also involves planning, such as with the wicked вАЬplanningвАЭ a vain thing (Ps 2:1, which is contrasted with the righteous who вАЬmeditateвАЭ in the law [1:2]).
n2001585: tn The word вАЬhowвАЭ is supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2001586:
tc The LXX reads: вАЬthe hearts of the righteous meditate faithfulness.вАЭ
sn The advice of the proverb is to say less but better things. The wiseвАФhere called the righteousвАФare cautious in how they respond to others. They think about it (heart = mind) before speaking.
n2001587: sn The form is plural. What they say (the вАЬmouthвАЭ is a metonymy of cause) is any range of harmful things.
n2001588: sn To say that the Lord is вАЬfarвАЭ from the wicked is to say that he has made himself unavailable to their appealвАФhe does not answer them. This motif is used by David throughout Ps 22 for the problem of unanswered prayerвАФвАЬWhy are you far off?вАЭ
n2001589: sn The verb вАЬhearвАЭ (вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ҐвАО, shama њ) has more of the sense of вАЬrespond toвАЭ in this context. If one вАЬlistens to the voice of the Lord,вАЭ for example, it means that he obeys the Lord. If one wishes God to вАЬhear his prayer,вАЭ it means he wishes God to answer it.
n2001590: sn GodвАЩs response to prayer is determined by the righteousness of the one who prays. A prayer of repentance by the wicked is an exception, for by it they would become the righteous (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 316).
n2001591:
tc The LXX has вАЬthe eye that sees beautiful things.вАЭ D. W. Thomas suggests pointing вАП„Ю÷∞„Р„Х÷є„®вАО (me Њor) as a Hophal participle, вАЬa fine sight cheers the mindвАЭ (вАЬTextual and Philological Notes,вАЭ 205). But little is to be gained from this change.
tn Heb вАЬlight of the eyesвАЭ (so KJV, NRSV). The expression may indicate the gleam in the eyes of the one who tells the good news, as the parallel clause suggests.
n2001592: tn Heb вАЬmakes fat the bones;вАЭ NAB вАЬinvigorates the bones;вАЭ NASB вАЬputs fat on the bones.вАЭ The word вАЬbonesвАЭ is a metonymy of subject, the bones representing the whole body. The idea of вАЬmaking fatвАЭ signifies by comparison (hypocatastasis) with fat things that the body will be healthy and prosperous (e.g., Prov 17:22; 25:25; Gen 45:27-28; Isa 52:7-8). Good news makes the person feel good in body and soul.
n2001593: tn Heb вАЬearвАЭ (so KJV, NRSV). The term вАЬearвАЭ is a synecdoche of part (= ear) for the whole (= person).
n2001594: tn вАЬLifeвАЭ is an objective genitive: Reproof brings or preserves life. Cf. NIV вАЬlife-giving rebukeвАЭ; NLT вАЬconstructive criticism.вАЭ
n2001595: tn Heb вАЬlodges.вАЭ This means to live with, to be at home with.
n2001596: sn The proverb is one full sentence; it affirms that a teachable person is among the wise.
n2001597: sn To вАЬdespise oneselfвАЭ means to reject oneself as if there was little value. The one who ignores discipline is not interested in improving himself.
n2001598: tn The nuances of вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ю÷Ј„ҐвАО (shama њ) include hearing and obeying or carrying out what was said. Cf. вАЬheedsвАЭ so NAB, NIV, NKJV, NRSV.
n2001599: tn The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) is used as a metonymy of association for what one does with the mind (thinking), and so refers to discernment, wisdom, good sense.
n20015100:
tn Heb вАЬ[is] instruction of wisdomвАЭ (KJV and NASB similar). The noun translated вАЬwisdomвАЭ is an attributive genitive: вАЬwise instruction.вАЭ
sn The idea of the first line is similar to Prov 1:7 and 9:10. Here it may mean that the fear of the Lord results from the discipline of wisdom, just as easily as it may mean that the fear of the Lord leads to the discipline of wisdom. The second reading harmonizes with the theme in the book that the fear of the Lord is the starting point.
n20015101: tn Heb вАЬ[is] humilityвАЭ (so KJV). The second clause is a parallel idea in that it stresses how one thing leads to anotherвАФhumility to honor. Humble submission in faith to the Lord brings wisdom and honor.
n200161:
tn Heb вАЬplans of the heartвАЭ (so ASV, NASB, NIV). The phrase вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„®÷∞„Ы÷µ„Щ÷Њ„Ь÷µ„СвАО (ma њarekhe lev) means вАЬthe arrangements of the mind.вАЭ
sn Humans may set things in order, plan out what they are going to say, but God sovereignly enables them to put their thoughts into words.
n200162: tn Here вАЬthe tongueвАЭ is a metonymy of cause in which the instrument of speech is put for what is said: the answer expressed.
n200163: sn The contrasting prepositions enhance the contrasting ideasвАФthe ideas belong to people, but the words come from the Lord.
n200164: sn There are two ways this statement can be taken: (1) what one intends to say and what one actually says are the same, or (2) what one actually says differs from what the person intended to say. The second view fits the contrast better. The proverb then is giving a glimpse of how God even confounds the wise. When someone is trying to speak [вАЬanswerвАЭ in the book seems to refer to a verbal answer] before others, the Lord directs the words according to his sovereign will.
n200166: sn The Hebrew term translated вАЬrightвАЭ (вАП„Ц÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО, zakh) means вАЬpure, clear, cleanвАЭ (cf. KJV, NASB вАЬclean;вАЭ NIV вАЬinnocent;вАЭ ESV, NKJV, NLT, NRSV вАЬpure.вАЭ It is used in the Bible for pure (uncontaminated) oils or undiluted liquids. Here it means uncontaminated actions and motives. It address how people naively conclude or rationalize that their actions are fine.
n200167: tn Heb вАЬin his eyes.вАЭ Physical sight is used figuratively for insight, or oneвАЩs intellectual point of view.
n200168: tn The figure (a hypocatastasis) of вАЬweighingвАЭ signifies вАЬevaluationвАЭ (e.g., Exod 5:8; 1 Sam 2:3; 16:7; Prov 21:2; 24:12). There may be an allusion to the Egyptian belief of weighing the heart after death to determine righteousness. But in Hebrew thought it is an ongoing evaluation as well, not merely an evaluation after death.
n200169:
tn Heb вАЬspiritsвАЭ (so KJV, ASV). This is a metonymy for the motives, the intentions of the heart (e.g., 21:2 and 24:2).
sn Humans deceive themselves rather easily and so appear righteous in their own eyes, but the proverb says that God evaluates motives and so he alone can determine if the personвАЩs ways are innocent.
n2001610:
tc The MT reads вАП„Т÷є÷Љ„ЬвАО (gol, вАЬcommitвАЭ) from the root вАП„Т÷Є÷Љ„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (galal, вАЬto rollвАЭ). The LXX and Tg. Prov 16:3 have вАЬrevealвАЭ as if the root were вАП„Т÷Є÷Љ„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (galah, вАЬto revealвАЭ).
tn Heb вАЬroll.вАЭ The figure of rolling (an implied comparison or hypocatastasis), as in rolling oneвАЩs burdens on the Lord, is found also in Pss 22:8 [9]; 37:5; 55:22. It portrays complete dependence on the Lord. This would be accomplished with a spirit of humility and by means of diligent prayer, but the plan must also have GodвАЩs approval.
n2001611: tn The suffix on the plural noun would be a subjective genitive: вАЬthe works you are doing,вАЭ or here, вАЬthe works that you want to do.вАЭ
n2001612: tn The syntax of the second clause shows that there is subordination: The vav on вАП„Х÷∞„Щ÷і„Ы÷є÷Љ„†„Х÷ЉвАО (veyikkonu) coming after the imperative of the first clause expresses that this clause is the purpose or result. People should commit their works in order that the Lord may establish them. J. H. Greenstone says, вАЬTrue faith relieves much anxiety and smoothens many perplexitiesвАЭ (Proverbs, 172).
n2001613: tn The Hebrew verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО (pa њal) means вАЬto work out; to bring about; to accomplish.вАЭ As the perfect form of a dynamic root, it is past or perfective. By using a past nuance, the proverb asserts that this is not just something that will work out some day. It affirms that God has done so and views this action as prototypical of what God does. Elsewhere with this verb, the preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) indicates the purpose of the work (when followed by an infinitive, e.g. Exod 15:7), or who the action was for/against (when followed by a person, e.g. Isa 26:12). In the only other case where the verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО (pa њal) has a direct object and the preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) it means to make, or modify, the thing into something else (Ps 7:13). Applying that same syntax here could mean вАЬGod has turned everything to his own purpose.вАЭ God has done so by turning what was meant to harm into good (as with Joseph, Gen 50:20) and here by preparing the wicked for disaster. If it means to turn one thing into another, then the verse affirms GodвАЩs sovereignty while not making him directly responsible for evil acts chosen by the wicked.
n2001614:
tc The Latin Vulgate implies the form вАП„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„†÷µ„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (lema њanehu) вАЬfor his/its sake/purposeвАЭ rather than the Masoretic textвАЩs вАП„Ь÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„†÷µ„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (lamma њanehu) вАЬfor his/its purpose/answer.вАЭ Both are reading the same consonantal text but understanding the vowels differently.
tn At the core of the phrase вАП„Ь÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„†÷µ„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (lamma њanehu) in the Masoretic text, lies the word вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„†÷ґ„ФвАО (ma њaneh), which is proposed to mean вАЬanswerвАЭ or вАЬpurpose.вАЭ HALOT proposes that they are two homonyms (HALOT I, 614) going back to different homonymous verbal roots. If this is the noun meaning вАЬanswer,вАЭ it may imply the consequence. God ensures that everyoneвАЩs actions and the consequences of those actions correspondвАФcertainly the wicked for the day of calamity. In GodвАЩs order there is just retribution for every act.
n2001615: sn This is an example of synthetic parallelism (вАЬA, whatвАЩs more BвАЭ). The A-line affirms a truth, and the B-line expands on it with a specific application about the wickedвАФwhatever disaster comes their way is an appropriate correspondent for their life.
n2001616: tn Heb вАЬan abomination of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) is a subjective genitive: вАЬthe Lord abhors.вАЭ
n2001617: tn Heb вАЬeveryone of proud of heart.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬheart, mindвАЭ) represents what one does with the вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО, i.e., thinking and feeling, and here refers to the attitude of someoneвАЩs spirit. It acts as a genitive of specification, вАЬproud in the heart.вАЭ The phrase refers to that class of people who are arrogant, those who set themselves presumptuously against God (e.g., 2 Chr 26:16; Ps 131:1; Prov 18:12).
n2001618: tn Heb вАЬhand to hand.вАЭ This idiom means вАЬyou can be assuredвАЭ (e.g., Prov 11:21).
n2001619:
tn The B-line continues the A-line, stating the eventual outcome of the LordвАЩs abhorrence of arroganceвАФhe will punish them. вАЬWill not go unpunishedвАЭ is an understatement (tapeinosis) to stress first that they will certainly be punished; whereas those who humble themselves before God in faith will not be punished.
tc The LXX has inserted two couplets here: вАЬThe beginning of a good way is to do justly, // and it is more acceptable with God than to do sacrifices; // he who seeks the Lord will find knowledge with righteousness, // and they who rightly seek him will find peace.вАЭ C. H. Toy reminds the reader that there were many proverbs in existence that sounded similar to those in the book of Proverbs; these lines are in the Greek OT as well as in Sirach (Proverbs [ICC], 321-22).
n2001620: sn These two words are often found together to form a nominal hendiadys: вАЬfaithful loyal love.вАЭ The couplet often characterizes the Lord, but here in parallel to the fear of the Lord it refers to the faithfulness of the believer. Such faith and faithfulness bring atonement for sin.
n2001621: tn Heb вАЬis atonedвАЭ; KJV вАЬis purgedвАЭ; NAB вАЬis expiated.вАЭ The verb is from I вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„§÷Ј„®вАО (kafar, вАЬto atone; to expiate; to pacify; to appeaseвАЭ; HALOT 493-94 s.v. I вАП„Ы„§„®вАО). This root should not be confused with the identically spelled Homonym II вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„§÷Ј„®вАО (kafar, вАЬto cover overвАЭ; HALOT 494 s.v. II *вАП„Ы„§„®вАО). Atonement in the OT expiated sins, it did not merely cover them over (cf. NLT). C. H. Toy explains the meaning by saying it affirms that the divine anger against sin is turned away and manвАЩs relation to God is as though he had not sinned (Proverbs [ICC], 322). Genuine repentance, demonstrated by loyalty and truthfulness, appeases the anger of God against oneвАЩs sin.
n2001622: tn Heb вАЬfear of the Lord.вАЭ The term вАП„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО (yehvah, вАЬthe LordвАЭ) functions as an objective genitive: вАЬfearing the Lord.вАЭ
n2001623: tn Heb вАЬturns away fromвАЭ; NASB вАЬkeeps away from.вАЭ
n2001624: sn The Hebrew word translated вАЬevilвАЭ (вАП„®÷Ј„ҐвАО, ra њ) can in some contexts mean вАЬcalamityвАЭ or вАЬdisaster,вАЭ but here it seems more likely to mean вАЬevilвАЭ in the sense of sin. Faithfulness to the Lord brings freedom from sin. The verse uses synonymous parallelism with a variant: One half speaks of atonement for sin because of the life of faith, and the other of avoidance of sin because of the fear of the Lord.
n2001626: tn The first line uses an infinitive in a temporal clause, followed by its subject in the genitive case: вАЬin the taking pleasure of the LordвАЭ = вАЬwhen the Lord is pleased with.вАЭ So the condition set down for the second colon is a lifestyle that is pleasing to God.
n2001627: tn The referent of the verb in the second colon is unclear. The straightforward answer is that it refers to the person whose ways please the LordвАФit is his lifestyle that disarms his enemies. W. McKane comments that the righteous have the power to mend relationships (Proverbs [OTL], 491); see, e.g., 10:13; 14:9; 15:1; 25:21-22). The life that is pleasing to God will be above reproach and find favor with others. Some would interpret this to mean that God makes his enemies to be at peace with him (cf. KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NLT). This is workable, but in this passage it would seem God would do this through the pleasing life of the believer (cf. NCV, TEV, CEV).
n2001628: tn Heb вАЬeven his enemies he makes to be at peace with him.вАЭ
n2001629: sn The lines contrast the modest income with the abundant income, but the real contrast is between righteousness and the lack of justice (or injustice). вАЬJusticeвАЭ is used for both legal justice and ethical conduct. It is contrasted with righteousness in 12:5 and 21:7; it describes ethical behavior in 21:3. Here the point is that unethical behavior tarnishes the great gain and will be judged by God.
n2001630: sn This is another вАЬbetterвАЭ saying; between these two things, the first is better. There are other optionsвАФsuch as righteousness with wealthвАФbut the proverb is not concerned with that. A similar saying appears in Amenemope 8:19-20 (ANET 422).
n2001631: tn Heb вАЬthe mind of a man.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) represents the person in this case (a synecdoche of a part for the whole) but highlights that faculty most relevant to the verb for planning.
n2001633: tn The verb вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (kun, вАЬto establish; to confirmвАЭ) with вАП„¶÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„УвАО (tsa њad, вАЬstepвАЭ) means вАЬto directвАЭ (e.g., Ps 119:133; Jer 10:23). This contrasts what people plan and what actually happensвАФGod determines the latter.
n2001634: sn вАЬStepsвАЭ is an implied comparison, along with вАЬway,вАЭ to indicate the events of the plan as they work out.
n2001635: tn Heb вАЬoracleвАЭ (so NAB, NIV) or вАЬdecisionвАЭ; TEV вАЬthe king speaks with divine authority.вАЭ The term вАП„І÷ґ„°÷ґ„ЭвАО (qesem) is used in the sense of вАЬoracle; decision; verdictвАЭ (HALOT 1115-16 s.v.). The pronouncements of a king form an oracular sentence, as if he speaks for God; they are divine decisions (e.g., Num 22:7; 23:23; 2 Sam 14:20).
n2001636: tn Heb вАЬon the lips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause referring to what the king saysвАФno doubt what he says officially.
n2001637: tn Heb вАЬhis mouth.вАЭ The term вАЬmouthвАЭ is a metonymy of cause for what the king says: his pronouncements and legal decisions.
n2001638: sn The second line gives the effect of the first: If the king delivers such oracular sayings (вАП„І÷ґ„°÷ґ„ЭвАО, qesem, translated вАЬdivine verdictвАЭ), then he must be careful in the decisions he makes. The imperfect tense then requires a modal nuance to stress the obligation of the king not to act treacherously against justice. It would also be possible to translate the verb as a jussive: Let the king not act treacherously against justice. For duties of the king, see Ps 72 and Isa 11. For a comparison with Ezek 21:23-26, see E. W. Davies, вАЬThe Meaning of qesem in Prov 16:10, вАЭ Bib 61 (1980): 554-56.
n2001639: tn Heb вАЬa scale and balances of justice.вАЭ This is an attributive genitive, meaning вАЬjust scales and balances.вАЭ The law required that scales and measures be accurate and fair (Lev 19:36; Deut 25:13). Shrewd dishonest people kept light and heavy weights to make unfair transactions.
n2001641: sn The вАЬwickednessвАЭ mentioned here (вАП„®÷ґ„©„Б÷Ј„ҐвАО, resha њ) might better be understood as a criminal act, for the related word вАЬwickedвАЭ can also mean the guilty criminal. If a king is trying to have a righteous administration, he will detest any criminal acts.
n2001642: tn The вАЬthroneвАЭ represents the administration, or the decisions made from the throne by the king, and so the word is a metonymy of adjunct (cf. NLT вАЬhis ruleвАЭ).
n2001643: tc The MT has the plural. Two Hebrew mss, the LXX, Syriac, and Targum read вАЬa king.вАЭ
