n2001644: tn Heb вАЬlips of righteousnessвАЭ; cf. NAB, NIV вАЬhonest lips.вАЭ The genitive вАЬrighteousnessвАЭ functions as an attributive adjective. The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause for what is said: вАЬrighteous speechвАЭ or вАЬrighteous counsel.вАЭ
n2001645: tn The MT has the singular participle followed by the plural adjective (which is here a substantive). The editors of BHS wish to follow the ancient versions in making the participle plural, вАЬthose who speak uprightly.вАЭ
n2001646: sn The verse is talking about righteous kings, of courseвАФthey love righteousness and not flattery. In this proverb вАЬrighteousвАЭ and вАЬuprightвАЭ referring to what is said means вАЬwhat is right and straight,вАЭ i.e., the truth (cf. NCV).
n2001647: sn This proverb introduces the danger of becoming a victim of the kingвАЩs wrath (cf. CEV вАЬif the king becomes angry, someone may dieвАЭ). A wise person knows how to pacify the unexpected and irrational behavior of a king. The proverb makes the statement, and then gives the response to the subject.
n2001648: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2001649: tn The expression uses an implied comparison, comparing вАЬwrathвАЭ to a messenger because it will send a message. The qualification is вАЬdeath,вАЭ an objective genitive, meaning the messenger will bring death, or the message will be about death. E.g., 1 Kgs 2:25, 29-34 and 46. Some have suggested a comparison with the two messengers of Baal to the god Mot (вАЬDeathвАЭ) in the Ugaritic tablets (H. L. Ginsberg, вАЬBaalвАЩs Two Messengers,вАЭ BASOR 95 [1944]: 25-30). If there is an allusion, it is a very slight one. The verse simply says that the kingвАЩs wrath threatens death.
n2001650: tn The verb is вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„§÷Ј„®вАО (kafar), which means вАЬto pacify; to appeaseвАЭ and вАЬto atone; to expiateвАЭ in Levitical passages. It would take a wise person to know how to calm or pacify the wrath of a kingвАФespecially in the ancient Near East.
n2001651:
tn Heb вАЬthe light of the face of the king.вАЭ This expression is a way of describing the kingвАЩs brightened face, his delight in what is taking place. This would mean life for those around him.
sn The proverb is the antithesis of 16:14.
n2001653: tn Heb вАЬlatter rainвАЭ (so KJV, ASV). The favor that this expression represents is now compared to the cloud of rain that comes with the вАЬlatterвАЭ rain or harvest rain. The point is that the rain cloud was necessary for the successful harvest; likewise the kingвАЩs pleasure will ensure the success and the productivity of the people under him. E.g., also Psalm 72:15-17; the prosperity of the land is portrayed as a blessing on account of the ideal king.
n2001654: tn The form вАП„І÷∞„†÷є„ФвАО (qenoh) is an infinitive; the Greek version apparently took it as a participle, and the Latin as an imperativeвАФboth working with an unpointed вАП„І„†„ФвАО, the letter вАП„ФвАО (he) being unexpected in the form if it is an infinitive construct (the parallel clause has вАП„І÷∞„†„Х÷є„™вАО [qenot] for the infinitive, but the ancient versions also translate that as either a participle or an imperative).
n2001655: tn The form is a Niphal participle, masculine singular. If it is modifying вАЬunderstandingвАЭ it should be a feminine form. If it is to be translated, it would have to be rendered вАЬand to acquire understanding is to be chosen more than silverвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB). Many commentaries consider it superfluous. NIV and NCV simply have вАЬto choose understanding rather than silver!вАЭ
n2001656: sn The point of righteous living is made with the image of a highway, a raised and well-graded road (a hypocatastasis, implying a comparison between a highway and the right way of living).
n2001657: tn The form вАП„°„Х÷Љ„®вАО (sur) is a Qal infinitive; it indicates that a purpose of the righteous life is to turn away from evil. вАЬEvilвАЭ here has the sense of sinful living. So the first line asserts that the well-cared-for life avoids sin.
n2001658: sn The second half of the verse uses two different words for вАЬguardвАЭ; this one is вАП„†÷є„¶÷µ„®вАО (notser) вАЬthe one who guards his way,вАЭ and the first is вАП„©„Б÷є„Ю÷µ„®вАО (shomer) вАЬthe one who guards his lifeвАЭ (the order of the words is reversed in the translation). The second colon then explains further the first (synthetic parallelism), because to guard oneвАЩs way preserves life.
n2001659: tc The LXX adds three lines after 17a and one after 17b: вАЬThe paths of life turn aside from evils, and the ways of righteousness are length of life; he who receives instruction will be prosperous, and he who regards reproofs will be made wise; he who guards his ways preserves his soul, and he who loves his life will spare his mouth.вАЭ
n2001660: sn The two lines of this proverb are synonymous parallelism, and so there are parasynonyms. вАЬPrideвАЭ is paired with вАЬhaughty spiritвАЭ (вАЬspiritвАЭ being a genitive of specification); and вАЬdestructionвАЭ is matched with вАЬa tottering, falling.вАЭ
n2001662: sn Many proverbs have been written in a similar way to warn against the inevitable disintegration and downfall of pride. W. McKane records an Arabic proverb: вАЬThe nose is in the heavens, the seat is in the mireвАЭ (Proverbs [OTL], 490).
n2001663: tn Heb вАЬlow of spiritвАЭ; KJV вАЬof an humble spirit.вАЭ This expression describes the person who is humble and submissive before the Lord and therefore inoffensive. It is always necessary to have a humble spirit, whether there is wealth or not.
n2001664: tn Heb вАЬthan to divide plunder.вАЭ The word вАЬplunderвАЭ implies that the wealth taken by the proud was taken violently and wrongfullyвАФspoils are usually taken in warfare. R. N. Whybray translates it with вАЬlootвАЭ (Proverbs [CBC], 95). The proud are in rebellion against God, overbearing and oppressive. One should never share the вАЬlootвАЭ with them.
n2001665: tn Heb вАЬhe who is prudentвАЭ or вАЬhe who deals wiselyвАЭ (cf. KJV). The proverb seems to be referring to wise business concerns and the reward for the righteous. One who deals wisely in a matter will find good results. R. N. Whybray sees a contrast here: вАЬThe shrewd man of business will succeed well, but the happy man is he who trusts the LordвАЭ (Proverbs [CBC], 92). Synonymous parallelism is more appropriate.
n2001666: tn Or вАЬhe who gives heed to a word,вАЭ that is, вАЬwho listens to instructionвАЭ (cf. NIV, NLT).
n2001668: tn Although traditionally this word is translated вАЬhappyвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV, NAB, NRSV, NLT), such a translation can be misleading because the word means far more than that. It describes the heavenly bliss that comes from knowing one is right with God and following GodвАЩs precepts. The вАЬblessedвАЭ could be at odds with the world (Ps 1:1-3).
n2001669: tn Heb вАЬand the one who trusts in the LordвАФblessed is he.вАЭ
n2001671: tn Heb вАЬto the wise of heart it will be called discerning.вАЭ This means that the wise of heart, those who make wise decisions (вАЬheartвАЭ being the metonymy), will gain a reputation of being the discerning ones.
n2001672: tn Heb вАЬsweetness of lips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause, meaning what is said. It is a genitive of specification. The idea of вАЬsweetnessвАЭ must be gracious and friendly words. The teaching will be well-received because it is both delightful and persuasive (cf. NIV вАЬpleasant words promote instructionвАЭ).
n2001673: tn Heb вАЬteachingвАЭ or вАЬreceptivityвАЭ; KJV вАЬlearningвАЭ; NIV вАЬinstruction.вАЭ
n2001674: tn The Hebrew noun вАП„©„В÷µ„Ы÷µ„ЬвАО (sekhel, вАЬprudence; insightвАЭ; cf. KJV, NASB, NIV вАЬunderstandingвАЭ; NAB, CEV вАЬgood senseвАЭ) is related to the verb that means вАЬto have insight; to give attention to; to act circumspectly [or, prudently],вАЭ as well as вАЬto prosper; to have success.вАЭ These words all describe the kind of wise action that will be successful.
n2001675: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2001676: tn Heb вАЬfountain of life.вАЭ The point of the metaphor is that like a fountain this wisdom will be a constant provision for living in this world.
n2001677: tn Heb вАЬthe discipline of fools [is] folly.вАЭ The вАЬdisciplineвАЭ (вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„°÷Є„®вАО, musar) in this proverb is essentially a requital for sin (hence вАЬpunishment,вАЭ so NIV, NCV, NRSV); discipline which is intended to correct is normally rejected and despised by fools. So the line is saying that there is very little that can be done for or with the fool (cf. NLT вАЬdiscipline is wasted on foolsвАЭ).
n2001679: tn Heb вАЬmakes wise his mouth,вАЭ with вАЬmouthвАЭ being a metonymy of cause for what is said: вАЬspeech.вАЭ
n2001680: sn Those who are wise say wise things. The proverb uses synthetic parallelism: The first line asserts that the wise heart ensures that what is said is wise, and the second line adds that such a person increases the reception of what is said.
n2001681: tn Heb вАЬto his lips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ functions as a metonymy of cause for what is said.
n2001682: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2001683: sn The metaphor of honey or the honeycomb is used elsewhere in scripture, notably Ps 19:10 [11]. Honey was used in Israel as a symbol of the delightful and healthy products of the landвАФвАЬa land flowing with milk and honeyвАЭ (Deut 6:3).
n2001684: sn Two predicates are added to qualify the metaphor: The pleasant words are вАЬsweetвАЭ and вАЬhealing.вАЭ вАЬSoulвАЭ includes in it the appetites, physical and spiritual; and so sweet to the вАЬsoulвАЭ would summarize all the ways pleasant words give pleasure. вАЬBonesвАЭ is a metonymy of subject, the boney framework representing the whole person, body and soul. Pleasant words, like honey, will enliven and encourage the whole person. One might recall, in line with the imagery here, how JonathanвАЩs eyes brightened when he ate from the honeycomb (1 Sam 14:27).
n2001685: tn Heb вАЬwhich is straight before a man.вАЭ
n2001686: tn Heb вАЬthe ways of death.вАЭ See note at the identical saying in 14:12.
n2001687: sn The words for вАЬlaborerвАЭ (вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ю÷µ„ЬвАО;  њamel) and вАЬlaboredвАЭ (вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЬвАО;  њamal) are less common Hebrew terms for work. The root idea refers to exertion, or toil, and sometimes to anxiety. In Eccl. 2:11 the Preacher refers to all his many building projects with this verb for hard work. This proverb pictures hard work being done of necessity rather than for the satisfaction of doing the work itself.
n2001688: tn Heb вАЬsoul.вАЭ The term вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) here means вАЬappetite,вАЭ functioning as a metonymy; the вАЬinner soulвАЭ of a person representing his appetite (BDB 660 s.v. 5a; see, e.g., Pss 63:6; 107:9; Prov 13:25; 16:24; 27:7; Isa 56:11; 58:10; Jer 50:19; Ezek 7:19). This is suggested by the parallelism with вАЬhunger.вАЭ
n2001689: tn Heb вАЬhis mouthвАЭ (so KJV, NAB). The term вАЬmouthвАЭ is a metonymy for hunger or eating. The idea of the proverb is clearвАФthe need to eat drives people to work.
n2001690: tn This verb, вАП„Р÷Є„Ы÷Ј„£вАО ( Њakaf), occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible. Cognate languages associate it with saddling an animal or pressing. A similar English idiom might be вАЬto spur on.вАЭ The verbs in the proverb are past time, noting a pattern which has been observed and is prototypical.
n2001691:
tc The LXX has apparently misread вАП„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (pihu) and inserted the idea of вАЬruinвАЭ for the laborer: вАЬhe drives away ruin.вАЭ This influenced the Syriac to some degree; however, its first clause understood вАЬsufferingвАЭ instead of вАЬlaborвАЭ: вАЬthe person who causes suffering suffers.вАЭ
sn This theme is taught elsewhere (e.g., Eccl 6:7; Eph 4:28; 6:7; 2 Thess 3:10-12).
n2001692: tn Heb вАЬa man of belial.вАЭ This phrase means вАЬwicked scoundrel.вАЭ Some translate вАЬworthlessвАЭ (so ASV, NASB, CEV), but the phrase includes deep depravity and wickedness (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 125-26).
n2001693: tn Heb вАЬdigs upвАЭ (so NASB). The вАЬwicked scoundrelвАЭ finds out about evil and brings it to the surface (Prov 26:27; Jer 18:20). What he digs up he spreads by speech.
n2001694: tn Heb вАЬon his lipsвАЭ (so NAB) The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause. To say that вАЬevilвАЭ is on his lips means that he talks about the evil he has dug up.
n2001695: sn The simile stresses the devastating way that slander hurts people. W. McKane says that this one вАЬdigs for scandal andвА¶propagates it with words which are ablaze with misanthropyвАЭ (Proverbs [OTL], 494).
n2001696: tn Heb вАЬa man of perverse thingsвАЭ; NAB вАЬan intriguer.вАЭ This refers to someone who destroys lives. The parallelism suggests that he is a вАЬslandererвАЭ or вАЬgossipвАЭвАФone who whispers and murmurs (18:8; 26:20, 22).
n2001697: tn The term вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Љ„Х÷Љ„£вАО ( Њalluf) refers to a вАЬfriendвАЭ or вАЬan intimate associate.вАЭ The word has other possible translations, including вАЬtameвАЭ or вАЬdocileвАЭ when used of animals. Rashi, a Jewish scholar who lived a.d. 1040-1105, took it in the later sense of вАЬprince,вАЭ saying that such speech alienates the Prince, namely God. But that is a forced interpretation of the line.
n2001698: tn Heb вАЬman of violence.вАЭ He influences his friends toward violence. The term вАП„Ч÷Є„Ю÷Є„°вАО (khamas, вАЬviolenceвАЭ) often refers to sins against society, social injustices, and crimes.
n2001699: tn Or вАЬwill entice.вАЭ The verb in the first colon is an imperfect, and the form in the second is a vav plus perfect consecutive. The imperfect verb may be either present or future and implies customary or habitual behavior. The perfect consecutive continues the habitual force of the first verb. The first verb, вАЬto persuade, seduce, entice,вАЭ is the metonymy of cause; the second verb, вАЬto lead,вАЭ is the metonymy of effect, the two together forming the whole process.
n20016100: tn Heb вАЬnot goodвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV); NLT вАЬa harmful path.вАЭ The expression вАЬa way that is not goodвАЭ is an example of tapeinosisвАФa deliberate understatement for the sake of emphasis: It is terrible. This refers to crime and violence. The understatement is used to warn people away from villains and to remind them to follow a good path.
n20016101: tn Or вАЬwho shuts.вАЭ HALOT suggests the idiom вАЬto screw up the eyesвАЭ (HALOT I, 866) for this hapax legomenon (word which only occurs one time). The precise gesture is not certain.
n20016102: sn The participle вАП„Ґ÷є„¶÷ґ„ФвАО ( њotseh) describes one as shutting his eyes (cf. KJV, ASV). This could mean simply вАЬclosing the eyes,вАЭ or it could refer to вАЬwinkingвАЭ (so many English versions). The proverb is saying that facial expressions often reveal if someone is plotting evil (e.g., 6:13-14).
n20016103: tc Heb вАЬto devise perversity.вАЭ The Hebrew text implies a verbal element before the infinitive вАЬ[does so] to devise perversity,вАЭ while the LXX uses a finite verb, which suggests an imperfect verb.
n20016104: tn The conjunction вАЬandвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is implied by the synonymous parallelism.
n20016105: tn The participle вАП„І÷є„®÷µ„•вАО (qorets) indicates that the person involved is pinching, compressing, or biting his lips (cf. NIV вАЬpurses his lipsвАЭ).
n20016106: tn The verb is a Piel perfect; it means вАЬto complete, finish, bring to an end.вАЭ A full understanding of the proverb requires certainty about what the gestures are and how they functioned in that culture. BDB classifies the use of this verb, вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„Ь÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (killah), as вАЬto accomplish in thoughtвАЭ meaning вАЬto determineвАЭ something (BDB 478). The proverb appears to advise how to spot if someone is devising evil or if someone has either finished plotting evil or has finished doing evil.
n20016107: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n20016108: sn The proverb presents the ideal, for it is not concerned with old people who may be evil. The KJV tried to qualify the interpretation by making the second half of the verse a conditional clause (вАЬif it be found in the way of righteousnessвАЭ). This is acceptable but unnecessary. The book of Proverbs is simply laying out the equity of longevity for righteousness and premature death for wicked people. In this line вАЬgray hairвАЭ is a metonymy of adjunct/effect, representing old age; and the вАЬglorious crownвАЭ (taking the genitive as attributive) provides a fitting metaphor to compare the hair on the head with a crown.
n20016109: tn Heb вАЬit is foundвАЭ (so NASB) or вАЬit will be found.вАЭ
n20016110: sn While the proverb presents a general observation, there is a commendable lesson about old people who can look back on a long walk with God through life and can anticipate unbroken fellowship with him in glory.
n20016111: tn One who is вАЬslow to angerвАЭ is a patient person (cf. NAB, NIV, NLT). This is explained further in the parallel line by the description of вАЬone who rules his spiritвАЭ (вАП„Х÷Љ„Ю÷є„©„Б÷µ„Ь „С÷Љ÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„Ч„Х÷євАО, umoshel berukho), meaning вАЬcontrols his temper.вАЭ This means the person has the emotions under control and will not вАЬfly off the handleвАЭ quickly.
n20016113: tn The phrase вАЬis better thanвАЭ does not appear in this line in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the parallelism.
n20016114: sn The saying would have had greater impact when military prowess was held in high regard. It is harder, and therefore better, to control oneвАЩs passions than to do some great exploit on the battlefield.
n20016115:
tn Heb вАЬthe lot is cast.вАЭ Because the ancient practice of вАЬcasting lotsвАЭ is unfamiliar to many modern readers, the imagery has been updated to вАЬthrowing dice.вАЭ
sn The proverb concerns the practice of seeking divine leading through casting lots. For a similar lesson, see Amenemope (18, 19:16-17, in ANET 423).
n20016117: sn The point concerns seeking GodвАЩs will through the practice. The Lord gives guidance in decisions that are submitted to him.
n200171: tn The phrase вАЬa dry piece of breadвАЭ is like bread without butter, a morsel of bread not dipped in vinegar mix (e.g., Ruth 2:14). It represents here a simple, humble meal.
n200172:
tn Heb вАЬand quietness in itвАЭ; the construction functions as a circumstantial clause: вАЬin which there is quietnessвАЭ or вАЬwith quietness.вАЭ
sn The Hebrew word means вАЬquietnessвАЭ or вАЬease.вАЭ It represents a place where there can be carefree ease because of the sense of peace and security. The Greek rendering suggests that those translators read it as вАЬpeace.вАЭ Even if the fare is poor, this kind of setting is to be preferred.
n200173: tn The house is described as being full of вАЬsacrifices of strifeвАЭ (вАП„Ц÷і„С÷∞„Ч÷µ„Щ÷Њ„®÷і„Щ„СвАО, zivkhe riv). The use of вАЬsacrificesвАЭ suggests a connection with the temple (as in 7:14) in which the people may have made their sacrifices and had a large amount meat left over. It is also possible that the reference is simply to a sumptuous meal (Deut 12:15; Isa 34:6; Ezek 39:17). It would be rare for Israelites to eat meat apart from festivals, however. In the construction the genitive could be classified as a genitive of effect, the feast in general вАЬbringing about strife,вАЭ or it could simply be an attributive genitive, вАЬa feast characterized by strife.вАЭ Abundance often brings deterioration of moral and ethical standards as well as an increase in envy and strife.
n200174: sn The setting is in the ancient world where a servant rarely advanced beyond his or her station in life. But there are notable exceptions (e.g., Gen 15:3 where the possibility is mentioned, 1 Chr 2:35 where it changed through marriage, and 2 Sam 16:1-4; 19:24-30, with the story of Ziba the servant of Mephibosheth). This proverb focuses on a servant who is wise, one who uses all his abilities effectivelyвАФa Joseph figure.
n200175: sn The parallelism indicates that вАЬruling overвАЭ and вАЬsharing in the inheritanceвАЭ means that the disgraceful son will be disinherited.
n200177: tn The form вАП„Ю÷µ„С÷і„Щ„©„БвАО (mevish) is a Hiphil participle, modifying вАП„С÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (ben). This original heir would then be one who caused shame or disgrace to the family, probably by showing a complete lack of wisdom in the choices he made.
n200178: tn Heb вАЬin the midst of the brothersвАЭ; NIV вАЬas one of the brothers.вАЭ
n200179: sn The noun вАП„Ю÷Ј„¶÷∞„®÷µ„£вАО (matsref) means вАЬa place or instrument for refiningвАЭ (cf. ASV, NASB вАЬthe refining potвАЭ). The related verb, which means вАЬto melt, refine, smelt,вАЭ is used in scripture literally for refining and figuratively for the LordвАЩs purifying and cleansing and testing people.
n2001710: tn The term вАЬrefiningвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the parallelism; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2001711: sn The term вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„®вАО (kur) describes a вАЬfurnaceвАЭ or вАЬsmelting pot.вАЭ It can be used figuratively for the beneficial side of affliction (Isa 48:10).
n2001712: tn Heb вАЬand.вАЭ Most English versions treat this as an adversative (вАЬbutвАЭ).
n2001713: sn The participle вАП„С÷є÷Љ„Ч÷µ„ЯвАО (bokhen, вАЬtestsвАЭ) in this emblematic parallelism takes on the connotations of the crucible and the furnace. When the Lord вАЬtestsвАЭ human hearts, the test, whatever form it takes, is designed to improve the value of the one being tested. Evil and folly will be removed when such testing takes place.
n2001714: tn The Hiphil participle вАП„Ю÷µ„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (mera њ) indicates one who is a doer of evil. The line affirms that a person of this nature will eagerly listen to evil talkвАФit is part of his nature.
n2001715: tn Heb вАЬto the lip of evilвАЭ; ASV, NAB, NASB, NRSV вАЬwicked lips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipвАЭ is a metonymy of cause for speech (what is said); the term вАЬevilвАЭ is an attributive genitive. The same will be true in the parallel line where the expression вАЬto the tongue of destructionвАЭ (NASB вАЬa destructive tongueвАЭ) means things that are said that destroy others.
n2001716: tc The verb вАП„Ю÷µ„Ц÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (mezin) would a Hiphil participle from вАП„Ц„Х„ЯвАО (zwn, вАЬto feedвАЭ). The suggested emendation is вАП„Ю÷Ј„Р÷≤„Ц÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (ma њazin), derive it from the denominative verb вАП„Р„Ц„ЯвАО ( Њzn, вАЬto give ear, listenвАЭ). Two Hebrew mss have this variant.
n2001717: sn Wicked, self-serving people find destructive speech appealing. They should be rebuked and not tolerated (Lev 19:17).
n2001718:
tn Or вАЬA mocker of the poor.вАЭ
sn The parallelism helps define the subject matter: The one who вАЬmocks the poorвАЭ (NAB, NASB, NIV) is the one who вАЬrejoices [NIV gloats] over disaster,вАЭ where the disaster resulted in the poverty of others. The topic of the parable is the person who mocks others by making fun of their misfortune.
n2001719: sn The Hebrew word translated вАЬinsultsвАЭ (вАП„Ч÷µ„®÷µ„£вАО, kheref) means вАЬreproach; tauntвАЭ (as with a cutting taunt); it describes words that show contempt for or insult God. The idea of reproaching the Creator may be mistaking and blaming GodвАЩs providential control of the world (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 337). W. G. Plaut, however, suggests that mocking the poor means holding up their poverty as a personal failure and thus offending their dignity and their divine nature (Proverbs, 187). Cf. Prov 14:31.
n2001721: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2001722: sn The metaphor signifies that grandchildren are like a crown, that is, they are the вАЬcrowning gloryвАЭ of life. The proverb comes from a culture that places great importance on the family in society and that values its heritage.
n2001723: tn The noun вАП„™÷Љ÷і„§÷∞„Р÷Є„®÷Є„™вАО (tif Њarat) means вАЬbeauty; gloryвАЭ (BDB 802 s.v.). In this passage вАЬgloryвАЭ seems to be identified with вАЬglorying; boastingвАЭ; so a rendering that children are proud of their parents would be in order. Thus, вАЬglory of childrenвАЭ would be a subjective genitive, the glorying that children do.вАЭ
n2001724:
tc The LXX has inserted: вАЬTo the faithful belongs the whole world of wealth, but to the unfaithful not an obulus.вАЭ It was apparently some popular sentiment at the time.
tn Heb вАЬtheir fathers.вАЭ
n2001725: tn The word вАП„Щ÷ґ„™÷ґ„®вАО (yeter) could be rendered either вАЬarrogantвАЭ (cf. NIV) or вАЬexcellentвАЭ (cf. KJV, NASB; NLT вАЬeloquentвАЭ) because the basic idea of the word is вАЬremainder; excess,вАЭ from the verb вАЬbe left over.вАЭ It describes вАЬloftyвАЭ speech (arrogant or excellent) that is not suited for the fool. The Greek version, using pista, seems to support the idea of вАЬexcellent,вАЭ and makes a contrast: вАЬwords that are excellent do not fit a fool.вАЭ The idea of arrogance (NIV) fits if it is taken in the sense of lofty, heightened, or excessive language.
n2001726: tn вАЬa lip of excess.вАЭ The term вАЬlipвАЭ is a metonymy for what is said.
n2001727: sn The вАЬfoolвАЭ proper, described by the term вАП„†÷Є„С÷Є„ЬвАО (naval), occurs only here, in v. 21, and in 30:22 in the book. It describes someone who is godless and immoral in an overbearing way (e.g., 1 Sam 25:25; Ps 14:1). A fool should restrain his words lest his foolishness spew out.
n2001728: tn Heb вАЬspeech of falsehoodвАЭ; NRSV вАЬfalse speech.вАЭ
n2001729: sn This вАЬrulerвАЭ (KJV, NASB вАЬprinceвАЭ; NAB вАЬnobleвАЭ) is a gentleman with a code of honor, to whom truthfulness is second nature (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 507). The word describes one as вАЬinclined, generous, nobleвАЭ (BDB 622 s.v. вАП„†÷Є„У÷і„Щ„СвАО). It is cognate to the word for the вАЬfree will offering.вАЭ So for such a noble person lies are not suited. The argument is from the lesser to the greaterвАФif fools shouldnвАЩt speak lofty things, then honorable people should not lie (or, lofty people should not speak base things).
n2001730: tn The phrase вАЬworks likeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2001731: tn Heb вАЬa stone of favorsвАЭ; NAB, NRSV вАЬa magic stone.вАЭ The term вАП„©„Б÷є„Ч÷Ј„УвАО (shokhad, вАЬbribeвАЭ) could be simply translated as вАЬa gift,вАЭ but the second half of the verse says that the one who offers it is successful. At best it could be a gift that opens doors; at worst it is a bribe. The word вАП„©„Б÷є„Ч÷Ј„УвАО is never used of a disinterested gift, so there is always something of the bribe in it (e.g., Ps 15:5; Isa 1:23). Here it is вАЬa stone that brings favor,вАЭ the genitive being the effect or the result of the gift. In other words, it has magical properties and вАЬworks like a charm.вАЭ
n2001732: tn Heb вАЬin the eyes of its owner.вАЭ
n2001733: tn Heb вАЬin all that he turnsвАЭ; NASB, NIV вАЬwherever he turns.вАЭ
n2001734: sn As C. H. Toy points out, the sage is merely affirming a point without making a commentвАФthose who use bribery meet with widespread success (Proverbs [ICC], 341). This does not amount to an endorsement of bribery.
n2001735: tn Heb вАЬcoversвАЭ (so NASB); NIV вАЬcovers over.вАЭ How people respond to the faults of others reveals whether or not they have love. The contrast is between one who вАЬcoversвАЭ (forgives, cf. NCV, NRSV) the fault of a friend and one who repeats news about it. The former promotes love because he cares about the person; the latter divides friends.
n2001736: sn The participle вАП„Ю÷∞„С÷Ј„І÷Љ÷µ„©„БвАО (mevaqqesh) means вАЬseeksвАЭ in the sense of seeking to secure or procure or promote love. There can be no friendship without such understanding and discretion.
n2001737: sn W. G. Plaut notes that harping on the past has destroyed many friendships and marriages (Proverbs, 188). W. McKane observes that this line refers to the person who breaks up friendships by his scandalous gossip, even if it is done with a kind of zeal for the welfare of the community, for it will destroy love and trust (Proverbs [OTL], 508-9).
n2001738: tn Heb вАЬgoes in deeperвАЭ (cf. NASB, NRSV). The verb вАП„†÷Є„Ч÷µ„™вАО (nakhet) вАЬto go down; to descendвАЭ with the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) means вАЬto descend into; to make an impression onвАЭ someone.
n2001739: tn The form is the Hiphil infinitive of вАП„†÷Є„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (nakhah) with the comparative вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО, min. The word вАЬfoolвАЭ then would be an objective genitiveвАФmore than blows to/on a fool.
n2001740:
tc The LXX reads бЉАќљѕДќєќїќњќ≥ќѓќ±ѕВ (antilogias, вАЬcontentionвАЭ), which usually stands for the Hebrew words вАП„Ю÷∞„®÷і„Щ„С÷Є„ФвАО (merivah), вАП„Ю÷і„У÷∞„Щ÷Є„ЯвАО (midyan), or вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv). These words all refer to вАЬstrife, contention, disputingвАЭ and are all somewhat graphically similar to each other and the word вАП„Ю÷∞„®÷і„ЩвАО (meri, вАЬrebellionвАЭ). Since the next Hebrew word starts with yod and bet, (вАП„Щ÷∞„С÷Ј„І÷µ÷Љ„©„БвАО, yevaqqesh) it is possible that something dropped out between the two yods and the text originally read вАП„Ю÷∞„®÷і„Щ„С÷Є„Ф „Щ÷∞„С÷Ј„І÷Љ÷µ„©„БвАО or вАП„Ю÷і„У÷∞„Щ÷Є„Я „Щ÷∞„С÷Ј„І÷Љ÷µ„©„БвАО.
sn The proverb is set up in a cause and effect relationship. The cause is that evil people seek rebellion. The term вАП„Ю÷∞„®÷і„ЩвАО (meri) means вАЬrebellion.вАЭ It is related to the verb вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (marah, вАЬto be contentious, to be rebellious, to be refractoryвАЭ). BDB 598 s.v. вАП„Ю÷∞„®÷і„ЩвАО translates the line вАЬa rebellious man seeketh only evilвАЭ (so NASB).
n2001741: tn The parallelism seems to be formal, with the idea simply continuing to the second line; the conjunction is therefore translated to reflect this. However, the proverb could be interpreted as antithetical just as easily.
n2001742: sn Those bent on rebellion will meet with retribution. The messenger could very well be a merciless messenger from the king, but the expression could also figuratively describe something God sendsвАФstorms, pestilence, or any other misfortune.
n2001743: tn Heb вАЬLet a man meetвАЭ (so NASB); NLT вАЬIt is safer to meet.вАЭ The infinitive absolute вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Т„Х÷є„©„БвАО (pagosh, вАЬto meetвАЭ) functions as a jussive of advice. The bear meeting a man is less dangerous than a fool in his folly. It could be worded as a вАЬbetterвАЭ saying, but that formula is not found here.
n2001744: tn The second colon begins with вАП„Х÷∞„Р÷Ј„ЬвАО (ve Њal), вАЬand not.вАЭ This negative usually appears with volitives, so the fuller expression of the parallel line would be вАЬand let not a fool in his folly [meet someone].вАЭ
n2001745: tn The words вАЬto meetвАЭ are not in the Hebrew text, but are implied by the parallelism and are supplied in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n2001746: sn The human, who is supposed to be rational and intelligent, in such folly becomes more dangerous than the beast that in this case acts with good reason. As R. L. Alden comments, вАЬConsider meeting a fool with a knife, or gun, or even behind the wheel of a carвАЭ (Proverbs, 134). See also E. Loewenstamm, вАЬRemarks on Proverbs 17:12 and 20:27, вАЭ VT 37 (1967): 221-24. For a slightly different nuance cf. TEV вАЬsome fool busy with a stupid project.вАЭ
n2001747: tn The sentence begins with the participle вАП„Ю÷µ„©„Б÷і„Щ„СвАО (meshiv, вАЬthe one who repaysвАЭ). The whole first colon may be taken as an independent nominative absolute, with the formal sentence to follow. Some English versions have made the first colon a condition by supplying вАЬifвАЭ (NAB, NIV, TEV, NLT).
n2001748: tn The verb вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„©„БвАО (mush) means вАЬto depart; to remove.вАЭ The Kethib is a Hiphil, which would yield a meaning of вАЬto take awayвАЭ; so the Qere, which is the Qal, makes more sense in the line.
n2001749: sn The proverb does not explain whether God will turn evil back on him directly or whether people will begin to treat him as he treated others.
n2001750: tn Heb вАЬthe beginning of a quarrelвАЭ; TEV, CEV вАЬThe start of an argument.вАЭ
n2001751:
tn Heb вАЬSomeone who releases water is the beginning of a quarrel.вАЭ The Hebrew order typically places the subject, вАЬbeginning,вАЭ second. This phrase is a metaphor, but most English versions have made it a simile (supplying вАЬlikeвАЭ or вАЬasвАЭ). R. N. Whybray makes it the subject of the clause: вАЬstealing water starts a quarrelвАЭ (Proverbs [CBC], 100). But this treats the participle as an infinitive and there is no indication that the verb means вАЬto steal,вАЭ for which there are clearer words. Keil and Delitzsch interpret it personally вАЬone who breaks through a water-damвА¶ opens a sluiceвАЭ (Keil and Delitzsch, Commentary on the Old Testament 6, 362). вАЬOpening such a sluice lets loose more than one can predict, control or retreiveвАЭ (Kidner, Proverbs [TOTC], 125).
tc The LXX has вАЬGiving authority to words is the beginning of strife.вАЭ This would make it a warning against thoughtless talk.
n2001752: tn Or вАЬForsake [it] before strife breaks out.вАЭ The Masoretic tradition takes вАЬstrifeвАЭ as the object of the imperative verb, but it could also be a subjective genitive following the infinitive вАЬto break out.вАЭ The verb вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ҐвАО (gala њ) occurs only in the Hitpael, meaning вАЬto quarrel; to break out.вАЭ
n2001753: tn Heb вАЬhe who justifies the wicked and he who condemns the righteousвАЭ (so NASB). The first colon uses two Hiphil participles, вАП„Ю÷Ј„¶÷∞„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ІвАО (matsdiq) and вАП„Ю÷Ј„®÷∞„©„Б÷і„Щ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (marshia њ). The first means вАЬto declare righteousвАЭ (a declarative Hiphil), and the second means вАЬto make wicked [or, guilty]вАЭ or вАЬto condemnвАЭ (i.e., вАЬto declare guiltyвАЭ). To declare someone righteous who is a guilty criminal, or to condemn someone who is innocent, are both abominations for the Righteous Judge of the whole earth.
n2001755: tn Heb вАЬwhy is this thatвА¶?вАЭ The combination of вАП„Ь÷Є„Ю÷Є÷Љ„ФвАО (lammah, вАЬfor what?, why?вАЭ) and вАП„Ц÷ґ÷Љ„ФвАО (zeh, вАЬthisвАЭ) does not simply seek a reason, but can add an incredulous tone to the question. Cf. Gen 25:32; Exod 2:20; 5:22; 17:3; Num 11:20; 1 Sam 17:28; 20:8; 26:18. Colloqially this might be expressed as вАЬWhy in the worldвА¶?вАЭ or вАЬWhy ever wouldвА¶?вАЭ
n2001756: tn Heb вАЬthat there is a price in the hand of a fool.вАЭ This is not the common word for money, which would simply be вАЬsilver.вАЭ The word вАП„Ю÷∞„Ч÷і„Щ„®вАО (mekhir) means вАЬprice, cost, value, hire, wagesвАЭ (see HALOT 569 s.v. and Concise DCH 214 s.v.).
n2001757:
tn Heb вАЬand a mind of nothing.вАЭ The word вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev) can refer to the mind and so represent thinking or refer to the heart and represent feeling or will. This can extend to refer to inclination, determination, reason, or common sense (see HALOT 514 s.v.). The construction is circumstantial вАЬwhen the вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО is empty/absent.вАЭ Due to the range of meaning of вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО, it could have several nuances. It could focus on thought, вАЬwhen [or since] he is empty-headedвАЭ or вАЬbrainless,вАЭ as if to say, вАЬwhy does he have money to buy a wisdom program when he doesnвАЩt have the hardware to run it on?вАЭ Cf. NASB вАЬhe has no senseвАЭ (TEV вАЬno common senseвАЭ) and NRSV вАЬhave no mind [to learn].вАЭ It could focus on the will, вАЬwhen he has no motivationвАЭ or вАЬis not of a mind [to use it].вАЭ Cf. NLT вАЬhas no heart for wisdomвАЭ and NIV вАЬhas no desire.вАЭ It could also be a comment on the rhetorical question, вАЬit is an empty-headed [thing to do],вАЭ perhaps a way to assert that it is senseless to try to buy wisdom.
sn W. McKane envisions a situation where the fool comes to a sage with a fee in hand, supposing that he can acquire a career as a sage, and this gives rise to the biting comment here: Why does the fool have money in his hands? To buy wisdom when he has no brains? (Proverbs [OTL], 505). According R. Murphy, вАЬThe price for acquiring wisdom is only metaphorical; the fool does not have the вАШheart,вАЩ i.e., the sense or desire to pursue the goal. See also v 24. According to 26:7 the fool is not able to implement a proverb, even though he вАШmouthsвАЩ itвАЭ (Proverbs [WBC], 130).
n2001758: sn The verse uses synonymous parallelism, so вАЬfriendвАЭ and вАЬrelativeвАЭ are equated. Others, however, will take the verse with antithetical parallelism: W. G. Plaut argues that friendship is a spiritual relationship whereas a brotherвАЩs ties are based on a blood relationshipвАФoften adversity is the only thing that brings brothers together (Proverbs, 189).
n2001760: tn Heb вАЬis born for adversity.вАЭ This is not referring to sibling rivalry but to the loyalty a brother shows during times of calamity. This is not to say that a brother only shows loyalty when there is trouble, nor that he always does in these times (e.g., 18:19, 24; 19:7; 27:10). The true friend is the same as a brotherly relationвАФin times of greatest need the loyal love is displayed.
n2001761: tn Heb вАЬlacking of mind.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) refers by metonymy to thinking, and by extension to discernment, wisdom, good sense. Cf. KJV, ASV вАЬa man void of understandingвАЭ; NIV вАЬa man lacking in judgment.вАЭ
n2001762: tn The phrase вАЬin pledgeвАЭ is supplied for the sake of clarification.
n2001763: tn The line uses the participle вАП„Ґ÷є„®÷µ„СвАО ( њorev) with its cognate accusative вАП„Ґ÷≤„®÷ї„С÷Є÷Љ„ФвАО ( њarubbah), вАЬwho pledges a pledge.вАЭ
n2001764: sn It is foolish to pledge security for someoneвАЩs loans (e.g., Prov 6:1-5).
n2001765: tn Heb вАЬthe one who loves transgression the one who loves a quarrel.вАЭ There is some ambiguity in the first line. The meaning would not differ greatly if either were taken as the subject, but the parallelism suggests that the proverb is about a quarrelsome and arrogant person who loves sin and invites destruction.
n2001767: tn Some have taken this second line literally and interpreted it to mean he has built a pretentious house. Probably it is meant to be figurative: The gate is the mouth (the figure would be hypocatastasis) and so to make it high is to say lofty thingsвАФhe brags too much (e.g., 1 Sam 2:3; Prov 18:12; 29:23); cf. NCV, TEV, NLT. C. H. Toy (Proverbs [ICC], 348) wishes to emend вАП„§÷Љ÷і„™÷∞„Ч„Х÷євАО (pitkho, вАЬhis gateвАЭ) to вАП„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„ХвАО (piv, вАЬhis mouthвАЭ), but that is unnecessary since the idea can be obtained by interpretation.
n2001768: tn The verse parallels two descriptions of the wicked person: вАЬcrooked/perverse of heartвАЭ (genitive of specification), and вАЬturned away in his tongueвАЭ (deceitful). The first phrase describes twisted intentions. The second, using the Niphal participle (вАЬone turned awayвАЭ) with вАЬtongue,вАЭ the metonymy of cause, describes one who has turned away from speaking truth. Cf. NLT вАЬthe twisted tongue tumbles into trouble.вАЭ
n2001769: tn The phrase вАЬdoes not find goodвАЭ is a figure (tapeinosis) meaning, вАЬwill experience calamity.вАЭ The wicked person can expect trouble ahead.
n2001770: tn Heb вАЬtongueвАЭ; NIV вАЬwhose tongue is deceitful.вАЭ
n2001771: sn Here the Hebrew terms вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„°÷і„Щ„ЬвАО (kesil) and вАП„†÷Є„С÷Є„ЬвАО (naval) are paired. The first one, which occurs about fifty times in the book, refers to a dullard, whether it be in spiritual, intellectual, or moral matters. The second word, rare in the book, primarily focuses on religious follyвАФit refers to the practical atheist, the one who lives as if there is no God.
n2001772: tn The form simply means вАЬbearsвАЭ or вАЬgives birth to,вАЭ but since it is masculine it could be rendered вАЬfathersвАЭ (cf. NASB вАЬhe who begets a foolвАЭ; NIV вАЬTo have a fool for a sonвАЭ). The form for вАЬfoolвАЭ is masculine, but the proverb is not limited only to male children (cf. NCV вАЬIt is sad to have a foolish childвАЭ).
n2001773: tn The phrase вАЬdoes soвАЭ is supplied for the sake of clarification.
n2001774: sn Parents of fools, who had hoped for children who would be a credit to the family, find only bitter disappointment (cf. TEV вАЬnothing but sadness and sorrowвАЭ).
n2001775: sn Heb вАЬa heart of rejoicingвАЭ; KJV вАЬa merry heartвАЭ; NAB, NASB вАЬa joyful heart.вАЭ This attributive genitive refers to the mind or psyche. A happy and healthy outlook on life brings healing.
n2001776:
tc The word вАЬhealingвАЭ is a hapax legomenon; some have suggested changes, such as to Arabic jihatu (вАЬfaceвАЭ) or to вАП„Т÷∞÷Љ„Х÷і„Щ÷Є÷Љ„ФвАО (geviyyah, вАЬbodyвАЭ) as in the Syriac and Tg. Prov 17:22, but the MT makes sense as it is and should be retained.
tn Heb вАЬit causes good a healing.вАЭ This means it promotes healing.
n2001777: sn The вАЬcrushed spiritвАЭ refers to one who is depressed (cf. NAB вАЬa depressed spiritвАЭ). вАЬCrushedвАЭ is figurative (an implied comparison) for the idea that oneвАЩs psyche or will to go on is beaten down by circumstances.
n2001778: sn The вАЬbonesвАЭ figuratively represent the whole body encased in a boney framework (metonymy of subject). вАЬFat bonesвАЭ in scripture means a healthy body (3:8; 15:30; 16:24), but вАЬdried upвАЭ bones signify unhealthiness and lifelessness (cf. Ezek 37:1-4).
n2001779: sn The fact that the вАЬgiftвАЭ is given secretly (Heb вАЬfrom the bosomвАЭ [вАП„Ю÷µ„Ч÷µ„Щ„ІвАО, mekheq]; so NASB) indicates that it was not proper. Cf. NRSV вАЬa concealed bribeвАЭ; TEV, CEV, NLT вАЬsecret bribes.вАЭ
n2001780: tn The form вАП„Ь÷∞„Ф÷Ј„Ш÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО (lehattot) is the Hiphil infinitive construct of вАП„†÷Є„Ш÷Є„ФвАО (natah), meaning вАЬto thrust away,вАЭ i.e., to вАЬpervert.вАЭ This purpose clause clarifies that the receiving of the вАЬgiftвАЭ is for evil intent.
n2001781: tn The verse begins with вАП„Р÷ґ„™÷Њ„§÷Љ÷∞„†÷µ„Щ „Ю÷µ„С÷і„Щ„ЯвАО ( Њet pene mevin), вАЬbefore the discerningвАЭ or вАЬthe face of the discerning.вАЭ The particle вАП„Р÷ґ„™вАО here is simply drawing emphasis to the predicate (IBHS 182-83 ¬І10.3.2b). Cf. NIV вАЬA discerning man keeps wisdom in view.вАЭ
n2001782: tn The term вАЬrunвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is supplied for the sake of clarification.
n2001783: sn To say that вАЬthe eyes of the fool run to the ends of the earthвАЭ means that he has no power to concentrate and cannot focus his attention on anything. The language is hyperbolic. Cf. NCV вАЬthe mind of a fool wanders everywhere.вАЭ
n2001784: sn The Hebrew noun means вАЬvexation, anger, grief.вАЭ
n2001785:
tn Heb вАЬto the one who bore him.вАЭ Because the participle is feminine singular in Hebrew, this has been translated as вАЬthe mother who bore him.вАЭ
sn The proverb is similar to v. 21, 10:1, and 15:20.
n2001786: tn Heb вАЬnot good.вАЭ This is an example of tapeinosisвАФan understatement that implies the worst-case scenario: вАЬit is terrible.вАЭ
n2001787: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷Ј„©„БвАО ( њanash), here a Qal infinitive construct, properly means вАЬto fineвАЭ (cf. NAB, NRSV, NLT) but is taken here to mean вАЬto punishвАЭ in general. The infinitive functions as the subject of the clause.
n2001788: tn The form is the Hiphil infinitive construct from вАП„†÷Є„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (nakhah, вАЬto strike; to smiteвАЭ). It may well refer to public beatings, so вАЬflogвАЭ is used in the translation, since вАЬstrikeвАЭ could refer to an individualвАЩs action and вАЬbeatвАЭ could be taken to refer to competition.
n2001789:
tn Heb вАЬ[is] against uprightness.вАЭ The expression may be rendered вАЬcontrary to what is right.вАЭ
sn The two lines could be synonymous parallelism, but the second part is being used to show how wrong the first act would beвАФpunishing the righteous makes about as much sense as beating an official of the court for doing what is just.
n2001790: tn Heb вАЬthe one knowing knowledge.вАЭ The cognate accusative underscores the meaning of the participleвАФthis is a truly knowledgeable person.
n2001791: sn The participle вАП„Ч„Х÷є„©„В÷µ„Ъ÷∞вАО (khosekh) means вАЬwithholds; restrains; refrains; spares; holds in check,вАЭ etc. One who has knowledge speaks carefully.
n2001792: tn Heb вАЬcool of spirit.вАЭ This genitive of specification describes one who is вАЬcalmвАЭ (so NCV, TEV, CEV) or вАЬeven-temperedвАЭ (so NIV, NLT); he is composed.
n2001793: tn The imperfect tense here denotes possibility: One who holds his tongue [may be considered] discerning.
n2001794: tn The Niphal participle is used in the declarative/estimative sense with stative verbs: вАЬto be discerningвАЭ (Qal) becomes вАЬto be declared discerningвАЭ (Niphal). The proverb is teaching that silence is one evidence of wisdom, and that even a fool can thereby appear wise. D. Kidner says that a fool who takes this advice is no longer a complete fool (Proverbs [TOTC], 127). He does not, of course, become wiseвАФhe just hides his folly.
n200181: tn The Niphal participle functions substantively and has a reflexive nuance: вАЬone who has separated himselfвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB). He is not merely anti-social; he is a problem for society since he will defy sound judgment. The Mishnah uses the verse to teach the necessity of being part of a community because people have social responsibilities and need each other (m. Avot 2:4).
n200182: tc The MT has вАЬseeks [his own] desire[s].вАЭ The translation in the LXX represents a Hebrew Vorlage of вАП„Ь÷∞„™÷є„Р÷≤„†÷Є„ФвАО (leto Њanah) instead of вАП„Ь÷∞„™÷Ј„Р÷≤„Х÷Є„ФвАО (leta Њavah); this could be translated вАЬseeks his own occasion,вАЭ that is, вАЬhis own pretextвАЭ (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 354; cf. NAB). The MT makes sense as it stands and the emendation is not really necessary.
n200183: tn Heb вАЬbreaks outвАЭ; NRSV вАЬshowing contempt forвАЭ; NLT вАЬsnarling at.вАЭ This individual breaks out in contention against sound judgment. He is in opposition to society (e.g., Prov 17:14; 20:3).
n200184: sn This expression forms an understatement (tapeinosis); the opposite is the pointвАФhe detests understanding or discernment.
n200185: tn The Hitpael infinitive construct вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Ф÷і„™÷∞„Т÷Ј÷Љ„Ь÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО (behitgallot) functions nominally as the object of the preposition. The term means вАЬreveal, uncover, betray.вАЭ So the fool takes pleasure вАЬin uncoveringвАЭ his heart.
n200186: tn Heb вАЬhis heart.вАЭ This is a metonymy meaning вАЬwhat is on his mindвАЭ (cf. NAB вАЬdisplaying what he thinksвАЭ; NRSV вАЬexpressing personal opinionвАЭ). This kind of person is in love with his own ideas and enjoys spewing them out (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 515). It is the kind of person who would ask a question, not to learn, but to show everyone how clever he is (cf. TEV).
n200187: tc The MT has вАЬa wicked [person].вАЭ Many commentators emend the text to вАП„®÷ґ„©÷Ј„Б„ҐвАО (resha њ, вАЬwickednessвАЭ) which makes better parallelism with вАЬshameвАЭ (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 521; R. B. Y. Scott, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes [AB], 112; C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 355; cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV). However, there is no external evidence for this emendation.
n200188: sn вАЬContemptвАЭ (вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЦвАО, buz) accompanies the wicked; вАЬreproachвАЭ (вАП„Ч÷ґ„®÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО, kherpah) goes with shame. This reproach either further characterizes the behaviors expected of the wicked or possibly the critical rebukes and taunts of the community against a wicked person.
n200189: tn Heb вАЬcontempt also comes/has come.вАЭ The verb form вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„РвАО (ba Њ) may either be a perfect verb вАЬhas comeвАЭ (cf. Prov 11:2) or a participle вАЬcomes.вАЭ
n2001810: tn The term вАЬcomesвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity and smoothness.
n2001811: tc The LXX reads вАЬin a personвАЩs heart,вАЭ probably conforming to the near parallel in Prov 20:5.
n2001812: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2001813: sn The metaphor вАЬdeep watersвАЭ indicates either that the words have an inexhaustible supply or that they are profound. Keil and Delitzsch see the second line as two more characteristics of the manвАЩs words rather than as a second sentence, i.e., a personвАЩs words are: deep waters, a bubbling brook, a fountain of wisdom. The вАЬbubbling brookвАЭ would refer to the supply and вАЬdeep watersвАЭ to their insightfulness, or what is beneath the surface. See also Prov 20:5 for the metaphor вАЬdeep waters.вАЭ
n2001814: tn There is debate about the nature of the parallelism between lines 4a and 4b. The major options are: (1) synonymous parallelism, (2) antithetical parallelism (e.g., NAB, NIV, NCV) or (3) formal parallelism. Normally a vav (вАП„ХвАО) would begin an antithetical clause; the structure and the ideas suggest that the second colon continues the idea of the first half, but in a parallel way rather than as additional predicates. The metaphors used in the proverb elsewhere describe the wise.
n2001815: sn This is an implied comparison (hypocatastasis), the fountain of wisdom being the person who speaks. The Greek version has вАЬfountain of lifeвАЭ instead of вАЬwisdom,вАЭ probably influenced from 10:11.
n2001816: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2001817: sn The point of this metaphor is that the wisdom is a continuous source of refreshing and beneficial ideas.
n2001818: tn Heb вАЬnot good.вАЭ This is a figure known as tapeinosis, a deliberate understatement to emphasize a worst-case scenario: вАЬit is terrible!вАЭ
n2001819: tn The idiom вАЬlifting up the face ofвАЭ (вАП„©÷∞„В„Р÷µ„™ „§÷Љ÷∞„†÷µ„ЩвАО, se Њet pene) means вАЬto show partialityвАЭ in decisions (e.g., Deut 10:17; Mal 2:9); cf. CEV, NLT вАЬto favor.вАЭ The verbal form is the Qal infinitive construct from вАП„†÷Є„©÷Є„В„РвАО (nasa Њ), which functions as the subject of the clause.
n2001820: tn Or вАЬthe guilty,вАЭ since in the second colon вАЬrighteousвАЭ can also be understood in contrast as вАЬinnocentвАЭ (cf. NRSV, TEV, NLT).
n2001821: tn Heb вАЬto turn asideвАЭ (so ASV); NASB вАЬto thrust aside.вАЭ The second half of the verse may illustrate this reprehensible action. The Hiphil infinitive construct вАП„Ь÷∞„Ф÷Ј„Ш÷Љ„Х÷є„™вАО (lehattot) may serve either (1) as result, вАЬshowing partialityвА¶so that the righteous are turned away,вАЭ or (2) as epexegetical infinitive, вАЬshowing partialityвА¶by turning the righteous away.вАЭ The second is preferred in the translation. Depriving the innocent of their rights is a perversion of justice.
n2001822: sn The вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause, meaning what the fool says. The вАЬmouthвАЭ in the second colon is likewise a metonymy for speech, what comes out of the mouth.
n2001823: sn вАЬStrifeвАЭ is a metonymy of cause, it is the cause of the beating or flogging that follows; вАЬfloggingвАЭ in the second colon is a metonymy of effect, the flogging is the effect of the strife. The two together give the whole picture.
n2001824: tn Heb вАЬcalls for.вАЭ This is personification: What the fool says вАЬcalls forвАЭ a beating or flogging. The fool deserves punishment, but does not actually request it.
n2001825: tn Heb вАЬblows.вАЭ This would probably be physical beatings, either administered by the father or by society (e.g., also 19:25; Ps 141:5; cf. NAB, NIV, TEV, NLT). Today, however, вАЬa beatingвАЭ could be associated with violent criminal assault, whereas the context suggests punishment. Therefore вАЬa floggingвАЭ is used in the translation, since that term is normally associated with disciplinary action.
n2001826:
tn Heb вАЬhis soulвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV).
sn What a fool says can ruin him. Calamity and misfortune can come to a person who makes known his lack of wisdom by what he says. It may be that his words incite anger, or merely reveal stupidity; in either case, he is in trouble.
n2001827: tn Or вАЬslandererвАЭ; KJV, NAB вАЬtalebearerвАЭ; ASV, NRSV вАЬwhisperer.вАЭ
n2001828: tn The word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„Ю÷і„™÷∞„Ь÷Ј„Ф÷≤„Ю÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (kemitlahamim) occurs only here (and 26:22 where the verse is repeated verbatim). It is related to a cognate verb meaning вАЬto swallow greedily,вАЭ so here вАЬthings swallowed greedily,вАЭ meaning food delicacies. Earlier English versions took it from a Hebrew root вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЭвАО (halam, see the word вАП„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Ј„Ф÷≤„Ь÷ї„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО [lemahalumot] in v. 6) meaning вАЬwoundsвАЭ (so KJV) or reflexively for the Hitpael as вАЬself-inflicted wounds.вАЭ But the translation of вАЬchoice morselsвАЭ seems to fit the next image of going into the belly better. But that could also show the extent of wounds.
n2001829:
tn Heb вАЬthey have gone down [into] the dark/inner chambers of the bellyвАЭ; NASB вАЬof the body.вАЭ
sn When the choice morsels of gossip are received, they go down like delicious foodвАФinto the innermost being; they have been too easily believed. R. N. Whybray says, вАЬThere is a flaw in human nature that assures slander will be listened toвАЭ (Proverbs [CBC], 105).
n2001830: tn Heb вАЬAlso, the one who.вАЭ Many commentators and a number of English versions omit the word вАЬalso.вАЭ
n2001831: tn The form вАП„Ю÷і„™÷∞„®÷Ј„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (mitrappeh) is the Hitpael participle, вАЬshowing oneself slack.вАЭ The verb means вАЬto sink; to relax,вАЭ and in the causative stem вАЬto let dropвАЭ the hands. This is the lazy person who does not even try to work.
n2001832: sn These two troubling types, the slacker and the destroyer, are closely related.
n2001833: tn Heb вАЬpossessor of destruction.вАЭ This idiom means вАЬdestroyerвАЭ (so ASV); KJV вАЬa great wasterвАЭ; NRSV вАЬa vandal.вАЭ
n2001834: sn The вАЬname of the LordвАЭ is a metonymy of subject. The вАЬnameвАЭ here signifies not the personal name вАЬYahweh,вАЭ for that would be redundant in the expression вАЬthe name of Yahweh,вАЭ but the attributes of the Lord (cf. Exod 34:5-7)вАФhere his power to protect.
n2001835: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2001836: tn Heb вАЬa tower of strength,вАЭ with вАЬstrengthвАЭ regarded as attributive by most English versions. The metaphor вАЬstrong towerвАЭ indicates that God is a secure refuge. The figure is qualified in the second colon.
n2001837: sn The metaphor of вАЬrunningвАЭ to the Lord refers to a whole-hearted and unwavering trust in GodвАЩs protection (e.g., Isa 40:31).
n2001838: tn Heb вАЬis highвАЭ or вАЬis inaccessible.вАЭ This military-type expression stresses the effect of the trustвАФsecurity, being out of danger (see HALOT 1305 s.v. вАП„©„В„Т„СвАО). Other scriptures will supply the ways that God actually protects people who trust him.
n2001839: sn This proverb forms a contrast with the previous one. The rich, unlike the righteous, trust in wealth and not in God.
n2001840: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2001841: tn Heb вАЬcity of his strengthвАЭ; NIV вАЬfortified city.вАЭ This term refers to their place of refuge, what they look to for security and protection in time of trouble.
n2001842:
tc The MT reads вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Ю÷Ј„©÷∞„В„Ы÷і÷Љ„Щ„™„Х÷євАО (bemaskito, вАЬin his imaginationsвАЭ). The LXX, Tg. Prov 18:11, and the Latin reflect вАП„С÷і÷Љ„Ю÷∞„©÷ї„В„Ы÷Є÷Љ„™„Х÷євАО (bimsukkato, вАЬlike a fence [or, high wall]вАЭ) that is, wealth provides protection. The MT reading, on the other hand, suggests that this security is only in the mind.
tn The proverb is an observation saying, reporting a common assumption without commenting on it. The juxtaposition with the last verse is a loud criticism of this misguided faith. The final word вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Ю÷Ј„©÷∞„В„Ы÷і÷Љ„Щ„™„Х÷євАО (bemaskito, вАЬin his imaginationsвАЭ) indicates that oneвАЩs wealth is a futile place of refuge.
n2001843: sn The term вАЬheartвАЭ is a metonymy of subject, referring to the seat of the spiritual and intellectual capacitiesвАФthe mind, the will, the motivations and intentions. Proud ambitions and intentions will lead to a fall.
n2001844: tn Heb вАЬ[is] before honorвАЭ; cf. CEV вАЬhumility leads to honor.вАЭ
n2001845: sn The way to honor is through humility (e.g., Prov 11:2; 15:33; 16:18). The humility and exaltation of Jesus provides the classic example (Phil 2:1-10).
n2001846: tn Heb вАЬreturns a wordвАЭ; KJV вАЬHe that answereth a matter.вАЭ
n2001847: sn Poor listening and premature answering indicate that the person has a low regard for what the other is saying, or that he is too absorbed in his own ideas. The Mishnah lists this as the second characteristic of the uncultured person (m. Avot 5:7).
n2001848: tn Heb вАЬit is folly to him and shame.вАЭ The verse uses formal parallelism, with the second colon simply completing the thought of the first.
n2001849: tn Heb вАЬthe spirit of a man.вАЭ Because the verb of this clause is a masculine form, some have translated this line as вАЬwith spirit a man sustains,вАЭ but that is an unnecessary change.
n2001850: sn This is a rhetorical question, asserting that very few can cope with depression.
n2001851: sn The figure of a вАЬcrushed spiritвАЭ (ASV, NAB, NCV, NRSV вАЬa broken spirit,вАЭ comparing depression to something smashed or crushed) suggests a broken will, a loss of vitality, despair, and emotional pain. In physical sickness one can fall back on the will to live, but in depression even the will to live is gone.
n2001852: tn Heb вАЬdiscerning mind.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) is a synecdoche of part (= heart/mind) for the whole (= person); cf. TEV, NLT вАЬintelligent people.вАЭ Placing вАЬheartвАЭ and вАЬearвАЭ in parallel encompasses more of the process of acquiring knowledge. The ear listens for and to instruction, and the mind considers what is heard to acquire knowledge.
n2001853:
tn Heb вАЬthe ear of the wise.вАЭ The term вАЬearвАЭ is a synecdoche of part (= ear) for the whole (= person): вАЬwise person.вАЭ
sn The wise continually seek more knowledge. D. Kidner says, вАЬThose who know most know best how little they knowвАЭ (Proverbs [TOTC], 129).
n2001854: sn This line features a mixed metaphor: The вАЬearвАЭ is pictured вАЬseeking.вАЭ The wise person is listening for (on the lookout for) things worth listening to in the pursuit of knowledge.
n2001855: sn The Hebrew term translated вАЬgiftвАЭ is a more general term than вАЬbribeвАЭ (вАП„©÷є„Б„Ч÷Ј„УвАО, shokhad), used in 17:8, 23. But it also has danger (e.g., 15:27; 21:14), for by giving gifts one might learn how influential they are and use them for bribes. The proverb simply states that a gift can expedite matters.
n2001856: sn The two verbs here show a progression, helping to form the synthetic parallelism. The gift first вАЬmakes roomвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷Ј„®÷∞„Ч÷і„Щ„СвАО, yarkhiv) for the person, that is, extending a place for him, and then вАЬushers him inвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷Ј„†÷∞„Ч÷µ„†÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО, yankhennu) among the greats.
n2001857: tn Heb вАЬin his legal caseвАЭ; NAB вАЬwho pleads his case first.вАЭ
n2001858: tn The term вАЬseemsвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is supplied in the translation for the sake of smoothness (cf. KJV вАЬseemethвАЭ).
n2001860: tc The Kethib is the imperfect вАП„Щ÷Є„С÷є„РвАО (yavo Њ), вАЬhis opponent comes andвА¶.вАЭ The Qere is the conjunction with the participle/perfect tense form вАП„Х÷Љ„С÷Є„РвАО (uva Њ), вАЬ[but] then his opponent comes andвА¶.вАЭ The latter is reflected in most of the ancient versions. There is not an appreciable difference in the translation.
n2001861: sn The proverb is a continuous sentence teaching that there must be cross-examination to settle legal disputes. There are two sides in any disputes, and so even though the first to present his case sounds right, it must be challenged. The verb вАП„Ф÷Є„І÷Ј„®вАО (haqar, translated вАЬcross-examinesвАЭ) is used for careful, diligent searching and investigating to know something (e.g., Ps 139:1).
n2001862: tn Heb вАЬcasting the lot.вАЭ Because modern readers are not familiar with the ancient practice of casting lots, the image of the coin toss to decide an issue has been employed in the translation (cf. CEV вАЬdrawing strawsвАЭ). Although the casting of lots is often compared to throwing dice, the translation вАЬthrowing dice ends disputesвАЭ in this context could be misunderstood to mean вАЬparticipating in a game of dice ends disputes.вАЭ
n2001863: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Ј„©„Б÷∞„С÷Љ÷і„Щ„™вАО (yashbit) is the Hiphil imperfect from вАП„©„Б÷Є„С÷Ј„™вАО (shavat), meaning вАЬto cause to cease; to bring to an end; to endвАЭ; cf. NIV вАЬsettles disputes.вАЭ The assumption behind this practice and this saying is that providence played the determining role in the casting of lots. If both parties accepted this, then the issue could be resolved.
n2001864: tn Heb вАЬmakes a separationвАЭ or вАЬdecides.вАЭ In the book of Proverbs this verb often has a negative connotation, such as separating close friends (e.g., 16:9). But here it has a positive nuance: Opponents are вАЬseparatedвАЭ by settling the issue.
n2001865: tn The word is the adjective, вАЬmightyвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB) used here substantivally as the object of the preposition.
n2001866: tn Heb вАЬbrother,вАЭ but this is not limited to actual siblings (cf. NRSV вАЬan allyвАЭ; CEV, NLT вАЬfriendвАЭ).
n2001867: tn The Niphal participle from вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„ҐвАО (pasha њ) modifies вАЬbrotherвАЭ: a brother transgressed, offended, sinned against.
n2001868:
tc The LXX has a clear antithetical proverb here: вАЬA brother helped is like a stronghold, but disputes are like bars of a citadel.вАЭ Accordingly, the editors of BHS propose вАП„Ю„Х÷є„©„Б÷і„Щ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (moshia њ) instead of вАП„†÷і„§÷∞„©„Б÷Є„ҐвАО (nifsha њ, so also the other versions and the RSV). But since both lines use the comparison with a citadel (fortified/barred), the antithesis is problematic.
tn The phrase вАЬis harder to reachвАЭ is supplied in the translation on the basis of the comparative вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min). It is difficult to get into a fortified city; it is more difficult to reach an offended brother.
n2001869: tn Heb вАЬbars,вАЭ but this could be understood to mean вАЬtaverns,вАЭ so вАЬbarred gatesвАЭ is employed in the translation.
n2001870: sn The proverb is talking about changing a friend or a relative into an enemy by abuse or strifeвАФthe bars go up, as it were. And the вАЬwallsвАЭ that are erected are not easily torn down.
n2001871: sn Two harvest images, fruit (from trees) and produce (from field crops), are applied to speech, represented by the mouth and lips. The вАЬmouthвАЭ and the вАЬlipsвАЭ are metonymies of cause, with both lines indicating that speech is productive. The following verse about harvest of the tongue may be part of this proverb.
n2001872: tn The noun вАП„С÷ґ÷Љ„Ш÷ґ„ЯвАО (beten) can refer to the stomach, womb, or internal organs. In Prov 20:30 and 22:18 it appears to be metaphorical for the inner person, or soul. Given the references to the mouth, lips, and being satisfied, on one level it refers to the stomach. But it probably functions on a spiritual level as well, especially when read with the following verse.
n2001873: tn Or вАЬis satisfied.вАЭ The translation understands вАП„©÷Є„В„С÷Ј„ҐвАО (sava њ) as stative вАЬto be satisfied; be filledвАЭ rather than fientive, вАЬto satisfy oneself,вАЭ so that the imperfect form is future. An imperfect verb may be future for both stative and dynamic verbs, and may be present for dynamic verbs. It is not possible to tell by morphological criteria whether the verb вАП„©„В÷Є„С÷Ј„ҐвАО is stative or dynamic, but elsewhere it behaves similarly to a stative.
n2001875: sn What people say can lead to life or death. The Midrash on Psalms shows one way the tongue [what is said] can cause death: вАЬThe evil tongue slays three, the slanderer, the slandered, and the listenerвАЭ (Midrash Tehillim 52:2). See J. G. Williams, вАЬThe Power of Form: A Study of Biblical Proverbs,вАЭ Semeia 17 (1980): 35-38.
n2001876: tn The referent of вАЬitвАЭ must be the tongue, i.e., what the tongue says (= вАЬits useвАЭ). So those who enjoy talking, indulging in it, must вАЬeatвАЭ its fruit, whether good or bad. The expression вАЬeating the fruitвАЭ is an implied comparison; it means accept the consequences of loving to talk (cf. TEV).
n2001877: tn The verb вАП„Ю÷Є„¶÷Є„РвАО (matsa Њ, translated вАЬhas foundвАЭ) is used twice in the first colon. As the perfect form of a dynamic root, the verb should be understood as past or perfective. The first verb sets the premiseвАФthe case where a man has found a good wife. The second verb makes an evaluative comment about the premise.
n2001878:
tc Some Hebrew manuscripts, the LXX, the Syriac, the Targum, and some Latin witnesses include the adjective вАЬgoodвАЭ (вАП„Ш„Х÷Ї„С÷Є„ФвАО; tovah). Its omission in the MT resulted from the common scribal mistake of homoeoteleuton, omitting a word when two successive words have a similar ending.
tn The adjective вАЬgoodвАЭ has a broad meaning and may mean вАЬvirtuous,вАЭ вАЬkind,вАЭ вАЬcheerful,вАЭ or вАЬcontent.вАЭ
sn The significance of the adjective is affirmed by realizing that this proverb should not contradict Prov 19:13; 21:9; 25:24; and 27:15. These verses do not paint the contentious wife as a benefit.
n2001879:
tc Heb вАП„Ш„Х÷є„СвАО (tov) вАЬa good [thing]вАЭ or вАЬ[what is] good.вАЭ The LXX translates with a noun вАЬgrace/favorвАЭ which may imply the Hebrew noun вАП„Ш„Х÷є„СвАО (tov), or the noun вАП„Ш„Х÷Љ„СвАО (tuv), a different reading of the same consonants. Both nouns mean вАЬgoodness,вАЭ вАЬwell-being;вАЭ вАЬhappiness.вАЭ
sn The term вАП„Ш„Х÷є„СвАО (tov, вАЬgood; enjoyable; favorable; virtuousвАЭ) might be an allusion to Gen 2:18, which affirms that it is not good for man to be alone. The word describes that which is pleasing to God, beneficial for life, and abundantly enjoyable.
n2001880: tn Heb вАЬwhat is pleasing; what brings delight.вАЭ The noun вАП„®÷Є„¶„Х÷є„ЯвАО (ratson, вАЬwhat is pleasingвАЭ). This is not the specific religious sense of finding acceptance before the Lord (a when bringing a sacrifice, e.g. Lev 1:3) but the general sense of delight. Yet this fortunate condition of having a virtuous, cheerful wife is described as providentially from God, cf. CEV вАЬshe is a gift from the Lord.вАЭ
n2001881: tc The LXX adds this embellishment to complete the thought: вАЬWhoever puts away a good wife puts away good, and whoever keeps an adulteress is foolish and ungodly.вАЭ
n2001882: tn Heb вАЬspeaks supplicationsвАЭ; NIV вАЬpleads for mercy.вАЭ The poor man has to ask for help because he has no choice (cf. CEV). The Hebrew term вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ч÷≤„†„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (takhanun) is a вАЬsupplication for favorвАЭ (related to the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО [khanan], вАЬto be gracious; to show favorвАЭ). So the poor man speaks, but what he speaks is a request for favor.
n2001883: sn The rich person responds harshly to the request. He has hardened himself against such appeals because of relentless demands. The proverb is an observation saying; it simply describes the way the world generally works, rather than setting this out as the ideal.
n2001884: tn The word is spelled вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО ( Њish), typical of the word for вАЬman, person,вАЭ and is often so translated (KJV, NIV, NASB, ESV). It is probably a synonym or alternate form of вАП„Щ÷µ„©„БвАО (yesh, вАЬthere isвАЭ), which begins the second line of the verse. The Ugaritic and Aramaic cognates of вАП„Щ÷µ„©„БвАО (yesh) are  Њt and вАП„Р÷і„Щ„™вАО ( Њith) respectively. A regular phonetic change in the history of the languages accounts for the Ugaritic and Aramaic tav (вАП„™вАО, вАЬtвАЭ) where Hebrew has a shin (вАП„©„БвАО, вАЬshвАЭ). It is spelled without the yod as вАП„Р÷і„©„БвАО ( Њish, вАЬthere isвАЭ) in 2 Sam 14:19 and Mic 6:10 (see HALOT 92, s.v. вАП„Р÷і„©„БвАО). C. H. Toy suggested reading вАП„Щ÷µ„©„БвАО (yesh) instead of вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО ( Њish), along with some of the Greek mss, the Syriac, and Tg. Prov 18:24 (Proverbs [ICC], 366) but the emendation is unnecessary in light of the cognate.
n2001885: tn The noun вАП„®÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (rea њ) refers to a вАЬcompanion, associate, friend, neighbor.вАЭ It has a wide range of meaning depending on context, but generally вАЬthose persons with whom one is brought into contact and with whom one must live on account of the circumstances of lifeвАЭ (HALOT 1253 s.v. II вАП„®÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО). Some translations employ the word вАЬfriendвАЭ in both halves of the verse, obscuring the distinction between them. This term speaks of association, not necessarily friendship.
n2001886: tn The text lacks a main verb and simply has an infinitive construct, вАП„Ь÷∞„Ф÷і„™÷∞„®÷є„Ґ÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (lehitro њea њ), a hitpolel of the verb вАП„®„Ґ„ҐвАО (ra њa њ). Based on the noun вАП„®÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (rea њ, вАЬcompanion, associate, friend, neighborвАЭ), the KJV had postulated a cognate, an otherwise unattested root вАП„®„Ґ„ҐвАО meaning вАЬshow oneself friendlyвАЭ in the Hitpolel. This would be reasonable if there was a root вАП„®„Ґ„ҐвАО that means вАЬto be a friendвАЭ in the Qal, but the noun вАП„®÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (rea њ) is actually associated with a root вАП„®„Ґ„ФвАО (ra њah). Instead the infinitive points toward a result and the Hitpolel of вАП„®„Ґ„ҐвАО (ra њa њ) means вАЬto smash one anotherвАЭ (HALOT 1269 s.v. II вАП„®„Ґ„ҐвАО). If the first word of the verse is maintained to be вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО ( Њish, вАЬmanвАЭ), it might mean вАЬa man of companions may be crushed by them.вАЭ
n2001887: tn This term for friend (вАП„Р÷є„Ф÷µ„СвАО,  Њohev) is based on the root meaning вАЬto love. It speaks of a bond or commitment that is not true of the term for вАЬcompanionвАЭ in the first line.
n200191: sn People should follow honesty even if it leads to poverty (e.g., Prov 18:23; 19:22).
n200192: tn Heb вАЬlips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy for what one says with his lips. The expression вАЬperverse in his lipsвАЭ refers to speech that is morally perverted. Some medieval Hebrew mss, the Syriac, and Tg. Prov 19:1 have вАЬhis waysвАЭ rather than вАЬhis lipsвАЭ (e.g., Prov 28:6); cf. NAB.
n200193: tc The Syriac and Tg. Prov 19:1 read вАЬrichвАЭ instead of MT вАЬfool.вАЭ This makes tighter antithetical parallelism than MT and is followed by NAB. However, the MT makes sense as it stands; this is an example of metonymical parallelism. The MT reading is also supported by the LXX. The Hebrew construction uses вАП„Х÷∞„Ф„Х÷Љ„РвАО (vehu Њ), вАЬand he [is],вАЭ before вАЬfool.вАЭ This may be rendered вАЬone who is perverse while a foolвАЭ or вАЬa fool at the same time.вАЭ
n200194: tn Heb вАЬnot good.вАЭ This is a figure known as tapeinosis (a deliberate understatement to emphasize a worst-case scenario): вАЬit is dangerous!вАЭ
n200195: tn The interpretation of this line depends largely on the meaning of вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) which has a broad range of meanings: (1) the breathing substance of man, (2) living being, (3) life, (4) person, (5) seat of the appetites, (6) seat of emotions and passions, (7) activities of intellect, emotion and will, (8) moral character, etc. (BDB 659-61 s.v.). In light of the synonymous parallelism, the most likely nuance here is вАЬzeal, passionвАЭ (HALOT 713 s.v. 8). NIV takes the word in the sense of вАЬvitalityвАЭ and вАЬdriveвАЭвАФвАЬit is not good to have zeal without knowledgeвАЭ (cf. NCV, TEV, and NLT which are all similar).
n200196: tn Heb вАЬhe who is hasty with his feet.вАЭ The verb вАП„Р„Х÷Љ„•вАО ( Њuts) means вАЬto be pressed; to press; to make haste.вАЭ The verb is followed by the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) which indicates that with which one hastensвАФhis feet. The word вАЬfeetвАЭ is a synecdoche of part for the whole personвАФbody and mind working together (cf. NLT вАЬa person who moves too quicklyвАЭ).
n200197:
tn Heb вАЬmisses the goal.вАЭ The participle вАП„Ч„Х÷є„Ш÷µ„РвАО (khote Њ) can be translated вАЬsinsвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV), but in this context it refers only to actions without knowledge, which could lead to sin, or could lead simply to making poor choices (cf. NAB вАЬblundersвАЭ; NASB вАЬerrsвАЭ; NCV вАЬmight make a mistakeвАЭ).
sn The basic meaning of the verb is вАЬto miss a goal or the way.вАЭ D. Kidner says, вАЬHow negative is the achievement of a man who wants tangible and quick rewardsвАЭвАФhe will miss the way (Proverbs [TOTC], 132).
n200199:
tn The verb вАП„°÷Є„Ь÷Ј„£вАО (salaf) normally means вАЬto twist; to pervert; to overturn,вАЭ but in this context it means вАЬto subvertвАЭ (BDB 701 s.v.); cf. ASV вАЬsubverteth.вАЭ
sn J. H. Greenstone comments: вАЬManвАЩs own failures are the result of his own folly and should not be attributed to GodвАЭ (Proverbs, 201).
n2001910: tn The clause begins with vav on the nonverb phrase вАЬagainst the Lord.вАЭ While clause structure and word order is less compelling in a book like Proverbs, this fits well as a circumstantial clause indicating concession.
n2001911: sn The вАЬheart ragingвАЭ is a metonymy of cause (or adjunct); it represents the emotions that will lead to blaming God for the frustration. Genesis 42:28 offers a calmer illustration of this as the brothers ask what God was doing to them.
n2001912: tn The Niphal imperfect probably should be taken in the passive sense (the poor person is deserted by his вАЬfriend,вАЭ cf. NAB, NIV) rather than as a direct middle (the poor person deserted his friend).
n2001913: sn This proverb simply makes an observation on life: People pursue wealthy folk hoping that they can gain something from the rich, but the poor are deserted even by friends, who fear that the poor will try to gain something from them.
n2001914: tn Heb вАЬa witness of lies.вАЭ This expression is an attributive genitive: вАЬa lying witnessвАЭ (cf. CEV вАЬdishonest witnessesвАЭ). This is paralleled by вАЬthe one who pours out lies.вАЭ
n2001915: tn Heb вАЬbreathes outвАЭ; NAB вАЬuttersвАЭ; NIV вАЬpours out.вАЭ
n2001916:
tn Heb вАЬwill not escapeвАЭ (so NAB, NASB); NIV вАЬwill not go free.вАЭ Here вАЬpunishmentвАЭ is implied, and has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
sn This proverb is a general statement, because on occasion there are false witnesses who go unpunished in this life (e.g., Prov 6:19; 14:5, 25; 19:9). The Talmud affirms, вАЬFalse witnesses are contemptible even to those who hire themвАЭ (b. Sanhedrin 29b).
n2001917:
tn The verb вАП„Щ÷∞„Ч÷Ј„Ь÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (yekhallu) is a Piel imperfect of вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (khalah) meaning вАЬto seek favor; to entreat favor; to mollify; to appeaseвАЭ; cf. NIV вАЬcurry favor.вАЭ It literally means вАЬmaking the face of someone sweet or pleasant,вАЭ as in stroking the face. To вАЬentreat the favorвАЭ of someone is to induce him to show favor; the action aims at receiving gifts, benefits, or any other kind of success.
sn The Hebrew verb translated вАЬentreat the favorвАЭ is often used to express prayer when God is the one whose favor is being sought; here it is the prince who can grant requests.
n2001918: tn Heb вАЬthe face of a generous manвАЭ; ASV вАЬthe liberal man.вАЭ The term вАЬfaceвАЭ is a synecdoche of part (= face) for the whole (= person).
n2001919: sn The proverb acknowledges the fact of life, but it also reminds people of the value of gifts in life, especially in business or in politics.
n2001920: tn Heb вАЬa man of gifts.вАЭ This could be (1) attributive genitive: a man characterized by giving gifts or (2) objective genitive: a man who gives gifts (IBHS 146 ¬І9.5.2b).
n2001921: tn Heb вАЬbrothers,вАЭ but not limited only to male siblings in this context.
n2001922: tn Heb вАЬhate him.вАЭ The verb вАП„©„В÷Є„†÷µ„РвАО (sane Њ) may be nuanced вАЬrejectвАЭ here (metonymy of effect, cf. CEV). The kind of вАЬdislikeвАЭ or вАЬhatredвАЭ family members show to a poor relative is to have nothing to do with him (NIV вАЬis shunnedвАЭ). If relatives do this, how much more will the poor personвАЩs friends do so.
n2001923: tn Heb вАЬhis friends are far from him.вАЭ
n2001924:
tc The section titled вАЬproverbs of SolomonвАЭ (10:1-22:16) has 375 proverbs. 374 are two-line proverbs, while this three-line proverb has a difficult and awkward third line. The LXX has three two-line proverbs where this one verse is in the Hebrew text. The second proverb in the Greek text is separate and self contained; the third has some correlation to the stray third line in the Hebrew Masoretic text. Assuming the LXX points to an original two-line Hebrew proverb, Delitzsch proposed two Hebrew texts possibly lying behind the Greek. The reconstructed text would begin with вАЬthe friend of many is repaid with harmвАЭ and end with either (1) вАЬthe one who pursues words [=rumors] will not escapeвАЭ or (2) вАЬchasing words which are not [=nothing]вАЭ (Delitzsch, Proverbs I, 15; Proverbs II, 25). The first option best reflects the Greek, while the second option reflects the existing Hebrew. Besides the issue raised by the LXX, the Hebrew itself differs in tradition, with the Kethib reading the final two words as вАЬthey are notвАЭ (вАП„Ь÷є„Р „Ф÷µ„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО; lo Њ hemmah) and the Qere reading вАЬthey are hisвАЭ (вАП„Ь„Х÷є „Ф÷µ„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО; lo hemmah). Unless other manuscript evidence comes to light, the text cannot be recovered with certainty.
tn Different solutions have been proposed for the problematic last line of the verse. One perspective is that his attempts at friendship result only in empty words (words which are not). Another that he pursues words (spoken by family and friends) but only the words belong to him (they are his). Another supplies missing (but implied?) elements, вАЬhe pursues [them with] words, but they [do] not [respond].вАЭ Since they are far off, he has to look for them вАЬwith wordsвАЭ (adverbial accusative), that is, вАЬsend wordвАЭ for help. But they вАЬare nowhere to be foundвАЭ (so NIV). The basic idea is of his family and friends rejecting the poor person, revealing how superficial they are, and making themselves scarce.
n2001925: tn The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) is used as a metonymy of association for what one does with the mind (thinking), and so refers to discernment, wisdom, good sense. Most English versions translate as вАЬwisdomвАЭ or вАЬsenseвАЭ but cf. NAB вАЬintelligence.вАЭ
n2001926: tn Heb вАЬhis own soul.вАЭ The expression вАЬloves his soulвАЭ means that he is paying attention to his needs or taking care of his life (cf. NAB вАЬis his own best friendвАЭ). This expression works with its parallel to provide the whole idea: вАЬloving the soulвАЭ is the metonymy of the cause for prospering, and вАЬprosperingвАЭ is the metonymy of the effect (of loving).
n2001927: tn Heb вАЬfinds goodвАЭ (similar KJV, NASB); NCV вАЬwill succeed.вАЭ The MT reads вАП„Ь÷і„Ю÷∞„¶÷є„РвАО (limtso Њ), a Qal infinitive construct. The LXX (as well as the other major early versions) renders it as a future, which reflects a Vorlage of вАП„Щ÷і„Ю÷∞„¶÷Є„РвАО (yimtsa Њ). The infinitive is used here in a modal sense, meaning вАЬis destined toвАЭ or вАЬis certain ofвАЭ finding good in life.
n2001928: tn Heb вАЬbreathes outвАЭ; NAB вАЬuttersвАЭ; NIV вАЬpours out.вАЭ
n2001929: sn The verse is the same as v. 5, except that the last word changes to the verb вАЬwill perishвАЭ (cf. NCV вАЬwill dieвАЭ; CEV, NLT вАЬwill be destroyedвАЭ; TEV вАЬis doomedвАЭ).
n2001930: tn The form вАП„†÷Є„Р„Х÷ґ„ФвАО (na Њveh) is an adjective meaning вАЬseemly; comelyвАЭ in the older English versions like KJV, ASV, вАЬfittingвАЭ in more recent ones (e.g., NASB, NIV, NRSV). The verbal root вАП„†„Х÷є„ФвАО (noh) only occurs in the Pilel stem, but it also has the basic meaning of вАЬbeing fitting; being comely.вАЭ In this sentence the form is a predicate adjective.
