n2001931: sn The verse is simply observing two things that are misfits. It is not concerned with a fool who changes and can handle wealth, or a servant who changes to become a nobleman. It is focused on things that are incongruous.
n2001932: sn In the ancient world the prince would be trained for his rule (hence, one of the original purposes of Proverbs). A slave ruling over princes would be arrogant and cruel, or foolish and unwise. For other unbearable things, e.g., 11:22; 17:7; 26:1; 30:21-23.
n2001933: tn Or вАЬprudence,вАЭ the successful use of wisdom in discretion. Cf. NAB, NRSV, NLT вАЬgood sense.вАЭ
n2001934: tn Heb вАЬhas slowed his anger.вАЭ The Hiphil perfect of вАП„Р÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО ( Њarakh, вАЬto be longвАЭ) means вАЬto make long; to prolong.вАЭ As the perfect form of a dynamic verb it should understood as past or perfective. Having developed an insightful perspective has resulted in not being quick to respond in anger.
n2001935: sn вАЬGloryвАЭ signifies the idea of beauty or adornment. D. Kidner explains that such patience вАЬbrings out here the glowing colours of a virtue which in practice may look drably unassertiveвАЭ (Proverbs [TOTC], 133).
n2001936:
tn Heb вАЬto pass overвАЭ (so KJV, ASV); NCV, TEV вАЬignore.вАЭ The infinitive construct вАП„Ґ÷≤„С÷є„®вАО ( њavor) functions as the formal subject of the sentence. This clause provides the cause, whereas the former gave the effectвАФif one can pass over an offense there will be no anger.
sn W. McKane says, вАЬThe virtue which is indicated here is more than a forgiving temper; it includes also the ability to shrug off insults and the absence of a brooding hypersensitivityвА¶. It contains elements of toughness and self-discipline; it is the capacity to stifle a hot, emotional rejoinder and to sleep on an insultвАЭ (Proverbs [OTL], 530).
n2001937: sn The verse contrasts the вАЬrageвАЭ of the king with his вАЬfavorвАЭ by using two similes. The first simile presents the king at his most dangerousвАФhis anger (e.g., 20:2; Amos 3:4). The second simile presents his favor as beneficial for life (e.g., 16:14-15; 28:15).
n2001938: tn Heb вАЬis a roaring like a lion.вАЭ
n2001939: sn The proverb makes an observation about a kingвАЩs power to terrify or to refresh. It advises people to use tact with a king.
n2001940: tn Heb вАЬa foolish sonвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NIV, CEV); NRSV вАЬa stupid child.вАЭ
n2001941: tn Heb вАЬthe contentions of a wifeвАЭ (so KJV, NASB); NAB вАЬthe nagging of a wife.вАЭ The genitive could be interpreted (1) as genitive of source or subjective genitiveвАФshe is quarreling; or (2) it could be a genitive of specification, making the word вАЬcontentionsвАЭ a modifier, as in the present translation.
n2001942: tn Heb вАЬis a constant dripping.вАЭ The term вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity. The metaphor pictures water dropping (perhaps rain through the roof, cf. NRSV, CEV) in a continuous flow: It is annoying and irritating (e.g., Prov 27:15-16).
n2001943: tc The LXX makes this moralistic statement for 13b: вАЬvows paid out of hire of a harlot are not pure.вАЭ It is not based on the MT and attempts to reconstruct a text using this have been unsuccessful.
n2001944: tn Heb вАЬinheritance of fathersвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NASB).
n2001945: sn This statement describes a wife who has a skillful use of knowledge and discretion that proves to be successful. This contrasts with the preceding verse. The proverb is not concerned about unhappy marriages or bad wives (both of which exist); it simply affirms that when a marriage works out well one should credit it as a gift from God.
n2001946: tn Heb вАЬcauses to fallвАЭ or вАЬcastsвАЭ; NAB вАЬplungesвА¶into.вАЭ
n2001947: tn Or вАЬcomplete inactivityвАЭ; the word вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„®÷∞„У÷Љ÷µ„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (tardemah) can refer to a physical вАЬdeep sleepвАЭ (e.g., Gen 2:21; Jonah 1:5, 6), but it can also be used figuratively for complete inactivity, as other words for вАЬsleepвАЭ can. Here it refers to lethargy or debility and morbidness.
n2001948: tn The expression вАП„Х÷∞„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„Б „®÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (venefesh remiyyah) can be translated вАЬthe soul of deceitвАЭ or вАЬthe soul of slackness.вАЭ There are two identical feminine nouns, one from the verb вАЬbeguile,вАЭ and the other from a cognate Arabic root вАЬgrow loose.вАЭ The second is more likely here in view of the parallelism (cf. NIV вАЬa shiftless manвАЭ; NAB вАЬthe sluggardвАЭ). One who is slack, that is, idle, will go hungry.
n2001949: sn The two lines are related in a metonymical sense: вАЬdeep sleepвАЭ is the cause of going hungry, and вАЬgoing hungryвАЭ is the effect of deep sleep.
n2001950: tn The verb вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ю÷Ј„®вАО (shamar) is repeated twice in this line but with two different senses, creating a polysemantic wordplay: вАЬhe who obeys/keeps (вАП÷є÷є„©÷є„Ю÷µ„®вАО, shomer) the commandment safeguards/keeps (вАП„©÷є„Б„Ю÷µ„®вАО, shomer) his life.вАЭ
n2001951: sn The expression his ways could refer either (1) to the conduct of the individual himself, or (2) to the commandments as the LordвАЩs ways. If the latter is the case, then the punishment is more certain.
n2001952: tc The Kethib is вАП„Щ„Х÷Љ„Ю÷Є„™вАО (yumat), вАЬwill be put to death,вАЭ while the Qere reads вАП„Щ÷Є„Ю„Х÷Љ„™вАО (yamut, вАЬwill dieвАЭ). The Qere is the preferred reading and is followed by most English versions.
n2001953: sn The participle вАП„Ч„Х÷є„†÷µ„ЯвАО (khonen, вАЬshows favor toвАЭ) is related to the word for вАЬgrace.вАЭ The activity here is the kind favor shown poor people for no particular reason and with no hope of repayment. It is literally an act of grace.
n2001954: tn The form вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Х÷µ„ФвАО (malveh) is the Hiphil participle from вАП„Ь÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО (lavah) in construct; it means вАЬto cause to borrow; to lend.вАЭ The expression here is вАЬlender of the Lord.вАЭ The person who helps the poor becomes the creditor of God.
n2001955: tn Heb вАЬhe.вАЭ The referent of the third person masculine singular pronoun is вАЬthe LordвАЭ in the preceding line, which has been supplied here in the translation for clarity.
n2001956: sn The promise of reward does not necessarily mean that the person who gives to the poor will get money back; the rewards in the book of Proverbs involve life and prosperity in general.
n2001957: tn Heb вАЬand his good deed will repay him.вАЭ The word вАП„Т÷Љ÷∞„Ю÷ї„Ь„Х÷євАО (gemulo) could be (1) the subject or (2) part of a double accusative of the verb. Understanding it as part of the double accusative makes better sense, for then the subject of the verb is God. How вАЬhis deedвАЭ could repay him is not immediately obvious.
n2001958: tn The translation understands вАП„Ы÷і÷Љ„ЩвАО (ki) as causal. Some prefer to take вАП„Ы÷і÷Љ„ЩвАО as temporal and translate, вАЬwhile there is hopeвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NCV, NRSV, NLT), meaning that discipline should be administered when the child is young and easily guided. In the causal reading of вАП„Ы÷і÷Љ„ЩвАО, the idea seems to be that children should be disciplined because change is possible due to their youth and the fact that they are not set in their ways.
n2001959:
tc The word вАП„Ф÷≤„Ю÷і„Щ„™„Х÷євАО (hamito) is the Hiphil infinitive construct of вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„™вАО (mut, вАЬto dieвАЭ) plus third masculine singular suffix, вАЬto cause/allow his death.вАЭ The LXX gives вАЬdo not lift up your soul to excess,вАЭ perhaps having read вАП„Ч÷µ„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО (khemot, вАЬanger, rageвАЭ) with a вАП„ЧвАО (het) instead of a вАП„ФвАО (he) and without the suffix. The KJV rendered as вАЬlet not thy soul spare for his crying.вАЭ Perhaps they read as if from the similar sounding root вАП„Ю„Х÷Љ„ШвАО (mut, вАЬto shudder,вАЭ as in вАЬat making him shudderвАЭ) or from the verb вАП„Ф÷Є„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (hamah, вАЬto murmur, be in commotionвАЭ), whose Qal infinitive construct with suffix would be вАП„Ф÷≤„Ю„Х÷є„™„Х÷євАО (hamoto). It is not clear that either of these latter roots should be associated with crying.
tn The expression вАЬlift up your soulвАЭ is unclear. It may mean вАЬto set your heart on somethingвАЭ as in determining to do it, perhaps even determining a course of action that leads to unintended results. Or it may mean вАЬto remove your soul from something,вАЭ as in withdrawing from a course of action. Several possibilities arise for understanding this verse. The two most likely are to вАЬnot set your heart on causing (i.e., contributing to) his deathвАЭ or to вАЬnot withdraw your soul [from disciplining as you should] resulting in causing his death.вАЭ These have the same effect of warning against failing to discipline to the ruin of the undisciplined child. T. Longman calls this the most natural reading, consistent with Prov 23:13-14 (Proverbs 370). Less likely, it may warn against being extreme in punishment (any capital punishment should go before the elders, see Deut 21:18-21).
n2001960: sn The Hebrew word means вАЬindemnity, fineвАЭ; this suggests that the trouble could be legal, and the angry person has to pay for it.
n2001961: tn The second colon of the verse is very difficult, and there have been many proposals as to its meaning: (1) вАЬIf you save [your enemy], you will add [good to yourself]вАЭ; (2) вАЬIf you save [your son by chastening], you may continue [chastisement and so educate him]вАЭ; (3) вАЬIf you deliver [him by paying the fine for him once], you will have to do it againвАЭ; (4) вАЬIf you save [him this time], you will have to increase [the punishment later on].вАЭ All interpretations have to supply a considerable amount of material (indicated by brackets). Many English versions are similar to (3).
n2001962: sn The advice refers in all probability to the teachings of the sages that will make one wise.
n2001963: tn The proverb is one continuous thought, but the second half of the verse provides the purpose for the imperatives of the first half.
n2001964: tn The imperfect tense has the nuance of a final imperfect in a purpose clause, and so is translated вАЬthat you may become wiseвАЭ (cf. NAB, NRSV).
n2001965: tn Heb вАЬbecome wise in your latter endвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV) which could obviously be misunderstood.
n2001966: sn The plans (from the Hebrew verb вАП„Ч÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„СвАО [khashav], вАЬto think; to reckon; to deviseвАЭ) in the human heart are many. But only those which God approves will succeed.
n2001967: tn Heb вАЬin the heart of a manвАЭ (cf. NAB, NIV). Here вАЬheartвАЭ is used for the seat of thoughts, plans, and reasoning, so the translation uses вАЬmind.вАЭ In contemporary English вАЬheartвАЭ is more often associated with the seat of emotion than with the seat of planning and reasoning.
n2001968: tn Heb вАЬbut the counsel of the Lord, it will stand.вАЭ The construction draws attention to the вАЬcounsel of the LordвАЭ; it is an independent nominative absolute, and the resumptive independent pronoun is the formal subject of the verb.
n2001969:
tn The antithetical parallelism pairs вАЬcounselвАЭ with вАЬplans.вАЭ вАЬCounsel of the LordвАЭ (вАП„Ґ÷≤„¶÷Ј„™ „Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО,  њatsat yehvah) is literally вАЬadviceвАЭ or вАЬcounselвАЭ with the connotation of вАЬplanвАЭ in this context (cf. NIV, NRSV, NLT вАЬpurposeвАЭ; NCV вАЬplanвАЭ; TEV вАЬthe LordвАЩs willвАЭ).
sn The point of the proverb is that the human being with many plans is uncertain, but the Lord with a sure plan gives correct counsel.
n2001970: tn Heb вАЬthe desire of a manвАЭ (so KJV). The noun in construct is вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Р÷≤„Х÷Ј„™вАО (ta Њavat), вАЬdesire [of].вАЭ Here it refers to вАЬthe desire of a man [= person].вАЭ Two problems surface here, the connotation of the word and the kind of genitive. вАЬDesireвАЭ can also be translated вАЬlust,вАЭ and so J. H. Greenstone has вАЬThe lust of a man is his shameвАЭ (Proverbs, 208). But the sentence is more likely positive in view of the more common uses of the words. вАЬManвАЭ could be a genitive of possession or subjective genitiveвАФthe man desires loyal love. It could also be an objective genitive, meaning вАЬwhat is desired for a man.вАЭ The first would be the more natural in the proverb, which is showing that loyal love is better than wealth.
n2001971: tn Heb вАЬ[is] his loyal loveвАЭ; NIV вАЬunfailing loveвАЭ; NRSV вАЬloyalty.вАЭ
n2001972: sn The second half of the proverb presents the logical inference: The liar would be without вАЬloyal loveвАЭ entirely, and so poverty would be better than this. A poor person who wishes to do better is preferable to a person who makes promises and does not keep them.
n2001973: tn Heb вАЬthe fear of the Lord.вАЭ This expression features an objective genitive: вАЬfearing the Lord.вАЭ
n2001974: tn The term вАЬleadsвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity and style.
n2001975: tn Here вАЬlifeвАЭ is probably a metonymy of subject for вАЬblessings and prosperity in life.вАЭ The plural form often covers a personвАЩs вАЬlifetime.вАЭ
n2001976: tn The subject of this verb is probably the one who fears the Lord and enjoys life. So the proverb uses synthetic parallelism; the second half tells what this life is likeвАФit is an abiding contentment that is not threatened by calamity (cf. NCV вАЬunbothered by troubleвАЭ).
n2001977: tn Heb вАЬhe will not be visitedвАЭ (so KJV, ASV). The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„І÷Ј„УвАО (paqad) is often translated вАЬvisit.вАЭ It describes intervention that will change the destiny. If God вАЬvisitsвАЭ it means he intervenes to bless or to curse. To be вАЬvisited by troubleвАЭ means that calamity will interfere with the course of life and change the direction or the destiny. Therefore this is not referring to a minor trouble that one might briefly experience. A life in the Lord cannot be disrupted by such major catastrophes that would alter oneвАЩs destiny.
n2001978: tn Heb The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЯвАО (taman) means вАЬto buryвАЭ (so many English versions) or вАЬto hideвАЭ (so KJV). As the perfect form of a dynamic verb it should be understood as past or perfective. The proverb presents a scene where the sluggard has not just reached to the food in the dish but buried his hand in it. The second comment reveals that this is not a frozen frame, but a continuing scene revealing the extent of his laziness.
n2001979: sn This humorous portrayal is an exaggeration, but the point is that laziness opposes common sense and can thwart basic needs. It would have a wider application for anyone who would start a project and then lack the interest or energy to finish it (R. N. Whybray, Proverbs [CBC], 111). Ibn Ezra proposes that the dish was empty, because the sluggard was too lazy to provide for himself.
n2001980: tn The Hiphil imperfect вАП„™÷Ј÷Љ„Ы÷ґ÷Љ„ФвАО (takkeh) is followed by another imperfect. It could be rendered: вАЬstrike a scorner [imperfect of instruction] and a simpleton will become prudent.вАЭ But the first of the parallel verbs can also be subordinated to the second as a temporal or conditional clause. Some English versions translate вАЬbeatвАЭ (NAB вАЬif you beat an arrogant manвАЭ), but this could be understood to refer to competition rather than physical punishment. Therefore вАЬflogвАЭ has been used in the translation, since it is normally associated with punishment or discipline.
n2001981: sn Different people learn differently. There are three types of people in this proverb: the scorner with a closed mind, the simpleton with an empty mind, and the discerning person with an open mind (D. Kidner, Proverbs [TOTC], 135). The simpleton learns by observing a scoffer being punished, even though the punishment will have no effect on the scoffer.
n2001982: sn The word is related to вАЬshrewdnessвАЭ (cf. 1:4). The simpleton will learn at least where the traps are and how to avoid them.
n2001983:
tn The second half begins with вАП„Ф„Х÷є„Ы÷і„Щ„Ч÷ЈвАО (hokhiakh), the Hiphil infinitive construct. This parallels the imperfect tense beginning the first half; it forms a temporal or conditional clause as well, so that the main verb is вАЬhe will understand.вАЭ
sn The discerning person will learn from verbal rebukes. The contrast is caught in a wordplay in the Midrash: вАЬFor the wise a hint [r Њmizo], for the fool a fist [kurmezo]вАЭ (Mishle 22:6).
n2001984: tn The construction joins the Piel participle вАП„Ю÷∞„©÷Ј„Б„У÷Љ÷ґ„УвАО (meshadded, вАЬone who robsвАЭ) with the Hiphil imperfect вАП„Щ÷Ј„С÷∞„®÷і„Щ„Ч÷ЈвАО (yavriakh, вАЬcauses to fleeвАЭ = chases away). The imperfect given a progressive imperfect nuance matches the timeless description of the participle as a substantive.
n2001985: sn вАЬFatherвАЭ and вАЬmotherвАЭ here represent a stereotypical word pair in the book of Proverbs, rather than describing separate crimes against each individual parent. Both crimes are against both parents.
n2001986: tn The more generic вАЬchildвАЭ does not fit the activities described in this verse and so вАЬsonвАЭ is retained in the translation. In the ancient world a вАЬsonвАЭ was more likely than a daughter to behave as stated. Such behavior may reflect the son wanting to take over his fatherвАЩs lands prematurely.
n2001987: tn Heb вАЬStop listeningвА¶!вАЭ The infinitive construct вАП„Ь÷і„©„Б÷∞„Ю÷є„Ґ÷ЈвАО (lishmoa њ) functions as the direct object of the imperative: вАЬstop heeding [or, listening to].вАЭ Of course in this proverb which shows the consequences of doing so, this is irony. The sage is instructing not to stop. The conditional protasis construction does not appear in the Hebrew but is supplied in the translation.
n2001988: tn The second line has an infinitive construct вАП„Ь÷і„©„Б÷∞„Т„Х÷є„™вАО (lishgot), meaning вАЬto stray; to go astray; to err.вАЭ It indicates the result of the instructionвАФstop listening, and as a result you will go astray. The LXX took it differently: вАЬA son who ceases to attend to discipline is likely to stray from words of knowledge.вАЭ RSV sees the final clause as the purpose of the instructions to be avoided: вАЬdo not listen to instructions to err.вАЭ
n2001989:
tn Heb вАЬa witness who is worthless and wickedвАЭ (вАП„Ґ÷µ„У „С÷∞÷Љ„Ь÷і„Щ÷Ј÷Љ„Ґ÷Ј„ЬвАО,  њed beliyya њal). Cf. KJV вАЬan ungodly witnessвАЭ; NAB вАЬan unprincipled witnessвАЭ; NCV вАЬan evil witnessвАЭ; NASB вАЬa rascally witness.вАЭ
sn These are crooked or corrupt witnesses who willfully distort the facts and make a mockery of the whole legal process.
n2001990: tn The parallel line says the mouth of the wicked вАЬgulps downвАЭ or вАЬswallowsвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷∞„С÷Ј„Ь÷Ј÷Љ„ҐвАО, yevalla њ) iniquity. The verb does not seem to fit the line (or the proverb) very well. Some have emended the text to вАП„Щ÷Ј„С÷і÷Љ„Щ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (yabbia њ, вАЬgushesвАЭ) as in 15:28 (cf. NAB вАЬpours outвАЭ). Driver followed an Arabic balaga to get вАЬenunciates,вАЭ which works well with the idea of a false witness (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 529). As it stands, however, the line indicates that in what he says the wicked person accepts evilвАФand that could describe a false witness.
n2001991: tc The MT reads вАП„©÷∞„Б„§÷Є„Ш÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (shefatim from вАП„©÷ґ„Б„§÷ґ„ШвАО, shephet), meaning вАЬpenalties; judgments.вАЭ The text might be вАП„Ю÷і„©÷∞„Б„§÷Љ÷Є„Ш÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (mishpatim) restoring a mem lost by haplography (the previous word ends with mem), and meaning вАЬjudicial decisionsвАЭ (by extension вАЬpenaltiesвАЭ). The LXX reads вАЬscourges,вАЭ a gloss it uses for вАП„©„Б„Х÷є„Ш÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (shotim; cf. Prov 26:3), while some propose emending to вАП„©÷∞„Б„С÷Є„Ш÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (shevatim) вАЬrodsвАЭ (cf. 23:14). Rods might be the instrument of the flogging mentioned in the second half of the verse, but any of the proposals conforms to the convention of parallelism. The main choice is between the MT as it stands and the LXX.
n2001992: tn The verb is a Niphal perfect of вАП„Ы„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (kun) and may be past, as in вАЬhave been prepared,вАЭ or focused on the resulting state, as in вАЬare ready.вАЭ
n200201: sn The drinks are wine and barley beer (e.g., Lev 10:9; Deut 14:26; Isa 28:7). These terms here could be understood as personifications, but better as metonymies for those who drink wine and beer. The inebriated person mocks and brawls.
n200202: tn The two participles вАП„Ь÷µ„•вАО (lets, вАЬmockerвАЭ) and вАП„Ф÷є„Ю÷ґ„ФвАО (homeh, вАЬbrawlerвАЭ) are substantives; they function as predicates in the sentence. Excessive use of intoxicants excites the drinker to boisterous behavior and aggressive attitudesвАФit turns them into mockers and brawlers.
n200203: sn The proverb does not prohibit the use of wine or beer; in fact, strong drink was used at festivals and celebrations. But intoxication was considered out of bounds for a member of the covenant community (e.g., 23:20-21, 29-35; 31:4-7). To be led astray by their use is not wise.
n200204: tn Heb вАЬthe terror of a kingвАЭ (so ASV, NASB); The term вАЬterrorвАЭ is a metonymy of effect for cause: the anger of a king that causes terror among the people. The term вАЬkingвАЭ functions as a possessive genitive: вАЬa kingвАЩs angerвАЭ (cf. NIV вАЬA kingвАЩs wrathвАЭ; NLT вАЬThe kingвАЩs furyвАЭ).
n200205: tn The verb вАП„Ю÷і„™÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„С÷Љ÷∞„®„Х÷євАО (mit њabbero) is problematic; in the MT the form is the Hitpael participle with a pronominal suffix, which is unusual, for the direct object of this verb usually takes a preposition first: вАЬis angry with.вАЭ The LXX rendered it вАЬangers [or, irritates].вАЭ
n200206: sn The expression вАЬsins against himselfвАЭ has been taken by some to mean вАЬforfeits his lifeвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV) or вАЬendangers his lifeвАЭ (cf. NCV, NLT). That may be the implication of getting oneself in trouble with an angry king (cf. TEV вАЬmaking him angry is suicideвАЭ).
n200208:
tn Heb вАЬcessationвАЭ (вАП„©„Б÷ґ„С÷ґ„™вАО, shevet); NAB вАЬto shun strifeвАЭ; NRSV вАЬrefrain from strife.вАЭ
sn One cannot avoid conflict altogether, but the proverb is instructing that at the first sign of conflict the honorable thing to do is to find a way to end it.
n200209: tn Heb вАЬbreaks out.вАЭ The Hitpael of the verb вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ҐвАО (gala њ, вАЬto expose; to lay bareвАЭ) means вАЬto break out; to disclose oneself,вАЭ and so the idea of flaring up in a quarrel is clear. But there are also cognate connections to the idea of вАЬshowing the teeth; snarlingвАЭ and so quarreling viciously.
n2002010: sn The act of plowing is put for the whole process of planting a crop.
n2002011: tn Heb вАЬfrom winter.вАЭ The preposition вАП„Ю÷і„ЯвАО (min) may designate the starting point вАЬfrom winter [onward]вАЭ or the cause вАЬdue toвАЭ (so ASV вАЬby reason of the winterвАЭ). The noun вАЬwinterвАЭ refers to the time for sowing seed and having the early growth of crops. The right time for planting was after the autumn harvest and the rainy season of autumn and winter began.
n2002012:
tc The Kethib reads a Qal imperfect, while the Qere reads a vav plus Qal perfect consecutive. Both forms would be future; the Qere more overtly states this as a consequence.
tn The basic meaning of the Qal verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Р÷Ј„ЬвАО (sha Њal) is вАЬto ask;вАЭ by extension it sometimes means вАЬto wish for; to desire; to borrowвАЭ and perhaps вАЬto beg.вАЭ The Piel can mean вАЬto begвАЭ and does not require emending the consonantal text. Because he did not plant, or did not do it at the right time, he is reduced to begging and will have nothing (cf. KJV, ASV; NASB вАЬhe begs during the harvestвАЭ).
n2002013: tn The phrase вАЬfor grainвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is implied; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2002014: sn The noun means вАЬadvice, counselвАЭ; it can have the connotation of planning or making decisions. Those with understanding can sort out plans.
n2002015: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Ь÷ґ„СвАО (lev) refers to the вАЬmindвАЭ (NRSV) as well as the вАЬheartвАЭ (KJV, NIV, NASB). The expression refers to unspoken thoughts.
n2002016: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2002017: sn The motives or plans of a person are вАЬdifficult to fathomвАЭ; it takes someone with understanding to discover and surface them (the verb in the last colon continues the figure with the sense of bringing the plans to the surface and sorting them out).
n2002018: tn Heb вАЬa man of understandingвАЭ; TEV вАЬsomeone with insightвАЭ; NLT вАЬthe wise.вАЭ
n2002019: tn Heb вАЬmany a man calls/proclaims a man of his loyal love.вАЭ The Syriac and Tg. Prov 20:6 render the verb as passive: вАЬmany are called kind.вАЭ Other suggestions include: вАЬmost men meet people who will do them occasional kindnessesвАЭ (RSV); вАЬmany men profess friendshipвАЭ (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 384); вАЬmany men invite only the one who has shown them kindness.вАЭ The simplest interpretation in this context is вАЬmany proclaim [themselves to be] a kind person (= a loyal friend).вАЭ The contrast is between many who claim to be loyal friends and the one who actually proves to be faithful.
n2002020: tn The shift to the expression вАЬa man of faithfulness[es]вАЭ in the second line indicates that of all those who claim to show faithful love, it is rare to find one who is truly reliable (as the word вАП„Р÷±„Ю„Х÷Љ„†÷і„Щ„ЭвАО [ Њemunim] indicates clearly); cf. NAB, NRSV вАЬone worthy of trust.вАЭ
n2002021: sn The point of the rhetorical question is that a truly faithful friend is very difficult to find.
n2002022: sn Two terms describe the subject of this proverb: вАЬrighteousвАЭ and вАЬintegrity.вАЭ The first describes the person as a member of the covenant community who strives to live according to GodвАЩs standards; the second emphasizes that his lifestyle is blameless.
n2002023: tn Heb вАЬwalks in his integrityвАЭ (so NASB); cf. NIV вАЬleads a blameless life.вАЭ The Hitpael participle of вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (halakh) means вАЬto walk about; to walk to and fro.вАЭ The idiom of walking representing living is intensified here in this stem. This verbal stem is used in scripture to describe people вАЬwalking withвАЭ God.
n2002024: sn The nature and the actions of parents have an effect on children (e.g., Exod 20:4-6); if the parents are righteous, the children will enjoy a blessingвАФthe respect and the happiness which the parent reflects on them.
n2002025: tn The infinitive construct вАП„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (din, вАЬto judgeвАЭ) indicates purpose (so NIV, NCV), even though it does not have a preposition with it.
n2002026: tn The second line uses the image of winnowing (cf. NIV, NRSV) to state that the kingвАЩs judgment removes evil from the realm. The verb form is вАП„Ю÷∞„Ц÷Є„®÷ґ„ФвАО (mezareh), the Piel participle. It has been translated вАЬto sift; to winnow; to scatterвАЭ and вАЬto separateвАЭвАФi.e., separate out evil from the land. The text is saying that a just government roots out evil (cf. NAB вАЬdispels all evilвАЭ), but few governments have been consistently just.
n2002027: sn The phrase with his eyes indicates that the king will closely examine or look into all the cases that come before him.
n2002028: sn The verse is a rhetorical question; it is affirming that no one can say this because no one is pure and free of sin.
n2002029: tn The noun вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev) commonly translated вАЬheartвАЭ includes the вАЬmindвАЭ and embraces both motives and thoughts.
n2002030: tn In the Qal this verb, вАП„Ц÷Є„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (zakhah), means to be (morally) вАЬclean; pure.вАЭ Here in the Piel it is factitive to вАЬmake cleanвАЭ (so NRSV) or вАЬkeep cleanвАЭ (so NIV). This verb only appears 8 times in the Bible, but this phrase вАЬto cleanse the heart/mindвАЭ also occurs in Ps 73:13, where Asaph despairs of having cleansed his heart (or kept it clean). Ps 119:9 remarks that one can keep your path clean by carefully observing GodвАЩs word. And Isa 1:16 advises cleansing oneself by putting away and ceasing to do evil. In an ultimate sense, no one has kept a clean heart in every regard. However these other passages suggest that one can repent in order to cleanse the heart and attend to GodвАЩs word to keep it clean. The question thus points out the inherent lack of purity and poses the obligation to take steps to safeguard purity. In other words, since my heart is not (natively) pure, what do I need to do to keep it pure (as in being true to God not in a sense of works adding up to purity)?
n2002031: sn The Hebrew verb (вАП„Ш÷Є„Ф÷µ„®вАО, taher) means to вАЬbe clean; pureвАЭ and may refer to physical cleanliness or the absence of disease, mildew, infectants, or blemishes. As a Levitical term it normally refers to cleanness from infectants or religious ceremonial cleanness (though often the two are related). The term is applied morally (specifically вАЬclean from sinвАЭ as in this verse) in Lev 16:30 as part of the Day of Atonement. After the High Priest confesses the sins of the people and makes the sacrifice on their behalf, the people are considered вАЬclean.вАЭ So on the one hand, the question sounds rhetoricalвАФno one can claim to be pure on their own merit. On the other hand, the cultic answer would be those who have confessed sin and offered a sacrifice are cleansed.
n2002032: tn The construction simply uses repetition to express different kinds of weights and measures: вАЬa stone and a stone, an ephah and an ephah.вАЭ
n2002033:
tn Heb вАЬan abomination of the Lord.вАЭ The phrase features a subjective genitive: вАЬthe Lord abhors.вАЭ
sn Behind this proverb is the image of the dishonest merchant who has different sets of weights and measures which are used to cheat customers. The Lord hates dishonesty in business transactions.
n2002034: sn In the first nine chapters of the book of Proverbs the Hebrew term вАП„†÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (na њar) referred to an adolescent, a young person whose character was being formed in his early life.
n2002035: sn The Hebrew verb вАП„†÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО (nakhar) means вАЬto recognizeвАЭ more than simply вАЬto know.вАЭ Certain character traits can be recognized in a child by what he does (cf. NCV вАЬby their behaviorвАЭ).
n2002036: sn Character is demonstrated by actions at any age. But the emphasis of the book of Proverbs would also be that if the young child begins to show such actions, then the parents must try to foster and cultivate them; if not, they must try to develop them through teaching and discipline.
n2002037: sn The first half of the verse refers to two basic senses that the Lord has given to people. C. H. Toy, however, thinks that they represent all the faculties (Proverbs [ICC], 388). But in the book of Proverbs seeing and hearing come to the fore. By usage, вАЬhearingвАЭ also means obeying (15:31; 25:12), and вАЬseeingвАЭ also means perceiving and understanding (Isa 6:9-10).
n2002038: sn The verse not only credits God with making these faculties of hearing and sight and giving them to people, but it also emphasizes their spiritual use in GodвАЩs service.
n2002039: sn The proverb uses antithetical parallelism to teach that diligence leads to prosperity. It contrasts loving sleep with opening the eyes, and poverty with satisfaction. Just as вАЬsleepвАЭ can be used for slothfulness or laziness, so opening the eyes can represent vigorous, active conduct. The idioms have caught on in modern usage as wellвАФthings like вАЬopen your eyesвАЭ or вАЬasleep on the job.вАЭ
n2002040: tn The second line uses two imperatives in a sequence (without the vav [вАП„ХвАО]): вАЬopen your eyesвАЭ and then (or, in order that) you will вАЬbe satisfied.вАЭ
n2002041: tn Heb вАЬbreadвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NRSV), although the term often serves in a generic sense for food in general.
n2002042: tn Heb вАЬ[It is] bad, [it is] bad.вАЭ Since вАЬbadвАЭ can be understood in some modern contexts as a descriptive adjective meaning вАЬgood,вАЭ the translation uses вАЬworthlessвАЭ insteadвАФthe real point of the prospective buyerвАЩs exclamation.
n2002043: sn This proverb reflects standard procedure in the business world. When negotiating the transaction the buyer complains how bad the deal is for him, or how worthless the prospective purchase, but then later brags about what a good deal he got. The proverb will alert the inexperienced as to how things are done.
n2002044: tn The Hitpael imperfect of вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (halal) means вАЬto praiseвАЭвАФto talk in glowing terms, excitedly. In this stem it means вАЬto praise oneself; to boast.вАЭ
n2002045: tn The verse is usually taken as antithetical parallelism: There may be gold and rubies but the true gem is knowledge. However, C. H. Toy arranges it differently: вАЬstore of gold and wealth of corals and precious vesselsвАФall are wise lipsвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 388). But this uses the gems as metaphors for wise speech, and does not stress the contrast between wealth and wisdom.
n2002046: tn Heb вАЬlips of knowledge.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy for speaking, and вАЬknowledgeвАЭ could be either an attributive genitive or objective genitive: вАЬknowledgeable lips.вАЭ Lips that impart knowledge are the true jewel to be sought.
n2002047: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2002049: sn Taking a garment was the way of holding someone responsible to pay debts. In fact, the garment was the article normally taken for security (Exod 22:24-26; Deut 24:10-13). People normally had few changes of clothes, so a garment represented giving a necessity as collateral. (In the case of a poor person the cloak should be returned for the nighttime to keep them warm.)
n2002050:
tc The Kethib has the masculine plural form, вАП„†÷Є„Ы÷∞„®÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (nokhrim), suggesting a reading вАЬstrangers.вАЭ But the Qere has the feminine form вАП„†÷Є„Ы÷∞„®÷і„Щ÷Є÷Љ„ФвАО (nokhriyyah), вАЬstrange womanвАЭ or вАЬanother manвАЩs wifeвАЭ (e.g., 27:13). The parallelism would suggest вАЬstrangersвАЭ is the correct reading, although theories have been put forward for the interpretation of вАЬstrange womanвАЭ (see below).
tn M. Dahood argues that the cloak was taken in pledge for a harlot (cf. NIV вАЬa wayward womanвАЭ). Two sins would then be committed: taking a cloak and going to a prostitute (вАЬTo Pawn OneвАЩs Cloak,вАЭ Bib 42 [1961]: 359-66; also Snijders, вАЬThe Meaning of вАП„Ц÷Є„®вАО,вАЭ 85-86). In the MT the almost identical proverb in 27:13 has a feminine singular form here.
sn The one for whom the pledge is taken is called вАЬa strangerвАЭ and вАЬforeign.вАЭ These two words do not necessarily mean that the individual or individuals are non-IsraeliteвАФjust outside the community and not well known.
n2002052: tn Heb вАЬbread of deceitвАЭ (so KJV, NAB). This refers to food gained through dishonest means. The term вАЬbreadвАЭ is a synecdoche of specific for general, referring to anything obtained by fraud, including food.
n2002054: sn The image of food and eating is carried throughout the proverb. Food taken by fraud seems sweet at first, but afterward it is not. To end up with a mouth full of gravel (a mass of small particles; e.g., Job 20:14-15; Lam 3:16) implies by comparison that what has been taken by fraud will be worthless and useless and certainly in the way (like food turning into sand and dirt).
n2002055: tn The noun form is plural, but the verb is singular, suggesting either an abstract plural or a collective plural is being used here.
n2002056: tn The clause begins with vav (вАП„ХвАО) on вАЬwith guidance.вАЭ But the clause has an imperative for its main verb. One could take the imperfect tense in the first colon as an imperfect of injunction, and then this clause would be also instructional. But the imperfect tense is a Niphal, and so it is better to take the first colon as the foundational clause and the second colon as the consequence (cf. NAB): If that is true, then you should do this.
n2002057: sn There have been attempts by various commentators to take вАЬwarвАЭ figuratively to mean lifeвАЩs struggles, litigation, or evil inclinations. But there is no need and little justification for such interpretations. The proverb simply describes the necessity of taking counsel before going to war.
n2002058: sn The word describes a slanderer (NASB), a tale-bearer (KJV, ASV), or an informer. BDB 940 s.v. вАП„®÷Є„Ы÷і„Щ„ЬвАО says the Hebrew expression вАЬgoers of slanderвАЭ means slanderous persons. However, W. McKane observes that these people are not necessarily maliciousвАФthey just talk too much (Proverbs [OTL], 537).
n2002059: tn The form is the Hitpael imperfect (of prohibition or instruction) from вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„СвАО ( њarav). BDB 786-88 lists six roots with these radicals. The first means вАЬto mix,вАЭ but only occurs in derivatives. BDB 786 lists this form under the second root, which means вАЬto take on a pledge; to exchange.вАЭ The Hitpael is then defined as вАЬto exchange pledges; to have fellowship with [or, share].вАЭ The proverb is warning people to have nothing to do with gossips.
n2002060: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷є„™÷ґ„ФвАО (poteh) is a homonym, related to I вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„™÷Є„ФвАО (patah, вАЬto be naive; to be foolishвАЭ; HALOT 984-85 s.v. I вАП„§„™„ФвАО) or II вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„™÷Є„ФвАО (вАЬto open [the lips]; to chatterвАЭ; HALOT 985 s.v. II вАП„§„™„ФвАО). So the phrase вАП„Х÷Љ„Ь÷∞„§÷є„™÷ґ„Ф „©„В÷∞„§÷Є„™÷Є„Щ„ХвАО may be understood either (1) as HALOT 985 s.v. II вАП„§„™„ФвАО suggests, вАЬone opens his lipsвАЭ = he is always talking/gossiping, or (2) as BDB suggests, вАЬone who is foolish as to his lipsвАЭ (he lacks wisdom in what he says; see BDB 834 s.v. II вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„™÷Є„ФвАО, noted in HALOT 984 s.v. I вАП„§„™„ФвАО 1). The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause for what is said: gossip. If such a person is willing to talk about others, he will be willing to talk about you, so it is best to avoid him altogether.
n2002061: tn The form is the Piel participle of вАП„І÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (qalal), which means вАЬto be lightвАЭ; in the Piel stem it means вАЬto take lightly; to treat as worthless; to treat contemptuously; to curse.вАЭ Under the Mosaic law such treatment of parents brought a death penalty (Exod 21:17; Lev 20:9; Deut 27:16).
n2002062:
tn вАЬHis lampвАЭ is a figure known as hypocatastasis (an implied comparison) meaning вАЬhis life.вАЭ Cf. NLT вАЬthe lamp of your lifeвАЭ; TEV вАЬyour life will end like a lamp.вАЭ
sn For the lamp to be extinguished would mean death (e.g., 13:9) and possibly also the removal of posterity (R. N. Whybray, Proverbs [CBC], 115).
n2002063: tc The Kethib, followed by the LXX, Syriac, and Latin, has вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Р÷і„Щ„©„Б„Х÷є„ЯвАО (be Њishon), вАЬin the pupil of the eye darkness,вАЭ the dark spot of the eye. But the Qere has вАП„С÷Љ÷ґ„Р÷±„©„Б„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (be Њeshun), probably to be rendered вАЬpitchвАЭ or вАЬblackest,вАЭ although the form occurs nowhere else. The meaning with either reading is approximately the sameвАФdeep darkness, which adds vividly to the figure of the lamp being snuffed out. This individualвАЩs destruction will be total and final.
n2002064:
tc The Kethib reads вАП„Ю÷∞„С÷ї„Ч÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„™вАО (mebukhelet), вАЬgotten by greedвАЭ (based on a cognate Syriac verb, вАЬto be greedyвАЭ), but the Qere is вАП„Ю÷∞„С÷є„Ф÷ґ„Ь÷ґ„™вАО (mevohelet), вАЬgotten hastily [or, quickly].вАЭ A large number of mss and the ancient versions read with the Qere (cf. KJV, ASV вАЬgotten hastilyвАЭ; NAB вАЬgained hastilyвАЭ; NIV вАЬquickly gainedвАЭ; NRSV вАЬquickly acquiredвАЭ).
sn If the inheritance is obtained quickly, it could mean prematurely (e.g., Luke 15:12) or cruelly (Prov 19:26). The inheritance is gained without labor or without preparation.
n2002065:
tn The form is the Pual imperfect, вАЬwill not be blessed,вАЭ suggesting that divine justice is at work.
sn The Hebrew verb means вАЬenriched, made fruitful, prospered.вАЭ Whatever the inheritance was, it will not reach its full potential or even remain permanent.
n2002066: tn Heb вАЬin its endвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬthe end thereof.вАЭ
n2002067: tn The verse is directly instructive; it begins with the negated jussive in the first colon, and follows with the imperative in the second. It warns that the righteous should not take vengeance on the wicked, for only God can do that.
n2002068: tn The form is the Piel cohortative of resolveвАФвАЬI am determined to pay back.вАЭ The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷µ„ЭвАО (shalem) means вАЬto be complete; to be sound.вАЭ In this stem, however, it can mean вАЬto make complete; to make good; to requite; to recompenseвАЭ (KJV, ASV). The idea is вАЬgetting evenвАЭ by paying back someone for the evil done.
n2002069: sn To вАЬwaitвАЭ (вАП„І÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО, qavveh) on the Lord requires faith in him, reliance on divine justice, and patience. It means that the wrongs done to a person will have to be endured for a time.
n2002070: tn After the imperative, the jussive is subordinated in a purpose or result clause: вАЬwait for the Lord so that he may deliver you.вАЭ The verb вАП„Щ÷є„©„Б÷Ј„ҐвАО (yosha њ) means вАЬto save (KJV, ASV, NASB); to deliver (NIV); to give victoryвАЭ; in this context it means вАЬdeliver from the evil done to you,вАЭ and so вАЬvindicateвАЭ is an appropriate connotation. Cf. NCV вАЬhe will make things right.вАЭ
n2002071: tn Heb вАЬan abomination of the Lord.вАЭ This expression features a subjective genitive: вАЬthe Lord abhors.вАЭ
n2002072: tn Heb вАЬnot good.вАЭ This is a figure known as tapeinosisвАФa deliberate understatement to emphasize a worst-case scenario: вАЬit is wicked!вАЭ (e.g., 11:1; 20:10).
n2002073: tn Heb вАЬthe steps of a man,вАЭ but вАЬmanвАЭ is the noun вАП„Т÷Љ÷ґ„С÷ґ„®вАО (gever, in pause), indicating an important, powerful person. BDB 149-50 s.v. suggests it is used of men in their role of defending women and children; if that can be validated, then a translation of вАЬmanвАЭ would be appropriate here. But the line seems to have a wider, more general application. The вАЬstepsвАЭ represent (by implied comparison) the course of life (cf. NLT вАЬthe road we travelвАЭ).
n2002074:
tn Heb вАЬfrom the LordвАЭ; NRSV вАЬordered by the LordвАЭ; NIV вАЬdirected by the Lord.вАЭ
sn To say that oneвАЩs steps are ordained by the Lord means that oneвАЩs course of actions, oneвАЩs whole life, is divinely prepared and sovereignly superintended (e.g., Gen 50:26; Prov 3:6). Ironically, man is not actually in control of his own steps.
n2002075: tn The verse uses an independent nominative absolute to point up the contrast between the mortal and the immortal: вАЬand man, how can he understand his way?вАЭ The verb in the sentence would then be classified as a potential imperfect; and the whole question rhetorical. It is affirming that humans cannot understand very much at all about their lives.
n2002076: tn Heb вАЬhis way.вАЭ The referent of the third masculine singular pronoun is unclear, so the word вАЬownвАЭ was supplied in the translation to clarify that the referent is the human individual, not the Lord.
n2002077: sn It would be a вАЬsnareвАЭ because it would lead people into financial difficulties; Leviticus 27 talks about foolish or rash vows.
n2002079:
tn The verb is from вАП„Ь„Х÷Љ„ҐвАО (lu њ) or вАП„Ь÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„ҐвАО (la њa њ); it means вАЬto talk wildlyвАЭ (not to be confused with the homonym вАЬto swallowвАЭ). It occurs here and in Job 6:3.
sn This refers to speaking rashly in dedicating something to the sanctuary by calling it вАЬHoly.вАЭ
n2002080: tn Heb вАЬreflect on.вАЭ The person is to consider the vows before making them, to ensure that they can be fulfilled. Too many people make their vow or promise without thinking, and then later worry about how they will fulfill their vows.
n2002082: tn Heb вАЬwinnowsвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NRSV). The sage draws on the process of winnowing to explain how the king uncovers and removes wickedness. The verb from which the participle вАП„Ю÷∞„Ц÷Є„®÷ґ„ФвАО (mezareh) is derived means вАЬto separate; to winnow; to scatterвАЭ; the implied comparison means that the king will separate good people from bad people like wheat is separated from chaff. The image of winnowing is also used in divine judgment. The second line of the verse uses a detail of the process to make the point. Driving a wheel over the wheat represents the threshing process; the sharp iron wheels of the cart would easily serve the purpose (e.g., Isa 28:27-28).
n2002083: tn The king has the wisdom/ability to destroy evil from his kingdom. See also D. W. Thomas, вАЬProverbs 20:26, вАЭ JTS 15 (1964): 155-56.
n2002084: sn The expression translated вАЬthe human spiritвАЭ is the Hebrew term вАП„†÷і„©„Б÷∞„Ю÷Ј„™вАО (nishmat), a feminine noun in construct. This is the inner spiritual part of human life that was breathed in at creation (Gen 2:7) and that constitutes humans as spiritual beings with moral, intellectual, and spiritual capacities.
n2002085: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2002086: tn The вАЬlampвАЭ is the metaphor in the line; it signifies that the human spirit functions as a conscience, enabling people to know and please God, and directing them in choices that will be life-giving. E. Loewenstamm unnecessarily reads вАП„†÷і„Щ„®вАО (nir, вАЬto plowвАЭ) instead of вАП„†÷µ„®вАО (ner, вАЬlampвАЭ) to say that God plows and examines the soul (вАЬRemarks on Proverbs 17:12 and 20:27, вАЭ VT 37 [1967]: 233). The NIV supplies a verb (вАЬsearchesвАЭ) from the second half of the verse, changing the emphasis somewhat.
n2002087: tn Heb вАЬall the chambers of the belly.вАЭ This means вАЬthe inner parts of the bodyвАЭ (BDB 293 s.v. вАП„Ч÷ґ„У÷ґ„®вАО); cf. NASB вАЬthe innermost parts of his being.вАЭ
n2002088: tn The first line uses two Hebrew words, вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„У „Х÷ґ„Р÷±„Ю÷ґ„™вАО (khesed ve Њemet, вАЬloyal love and truthвАЭ), to tell where security lies. The first word is the covenant term for вАЬloyal love; loving-kindness; mercyвАЭ; and the second is вАЬtruthвАЭ in the sense of what is reliable and dependable. The two words often are joined together to form a hendiadys: вАЬfaithful love.вАЭ That a hendiadys is intended here is confirmed by the fact that the second line uses only the critical word вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„УвАО.
n2002089: sn The emphasis is on the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7:11-16; Ps 89:19-37). It is the Lord and his faithful love for his covenant that ultimately makes the empire secure. But the enjoyment of divine protection requires the king to show loyal love as well.
n2002090: tn The Hebrew term вАП„™÷Љ÷і„§÷∞„Р÷ґ„®÷ґ„™вАО (tif Њeret) means вАЬbeauty; gloryвАЭ; in a context like this it means вАЬhonorвАЭ in the sense of glorying or boasting (BDB 802 s.v. 3.b).
n2002091: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Ф÷≤„У÷Ј„®вАО (hadar), the noun in construct, means вАЬsplendor; honor; ornament.вАЭ The latter sense is used here, since grey hair is like a crown on the head.
n2002092: sn Gray hair is a metonymy of adjunct; it represents everything valuable about old ageвАФdignity, wisdom, honor, experience, as well as worry and suffering of life. At the very least, since they survived, they must know something. At the most, they were the sages and elders of the people.
n2002093: tc The verb вАП„Ю÷Є„®÷Ј„ІвАО (maraq) means вАЬto polish; to scourвАЭ; in the Hiphil it means вАЬto cleanse away,вАЭ but it is only attested here, and that in the Kethib reading of вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ю÷∞„®÷і„Щ„ІвАО (tamriq). The Qere has вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ю÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„ІвАО (tamruq, вАЬare a means of cleansingвАЭ). The LXX has вАЬblows and contusions fall on evil men, and stripes penetrate their inner beingsвАЭ; the Latin has вАЬthe bruise of a wound cleanses away evil things.вАЭ C. H. Toy suggests emending the text to read вАЬstripes cleanse the body, and blows the inward partsвАЭ or вАЬcosmetics purify the body, and blows the soulвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 397). Cf. CEV вАЬcan knock all of the evil out of you.вАЭ
n2002094: tn The term вАЬcleanseвАЭ does not appear in this line but is supplied in the translation in the light of the parallelism.
n2002095: sn Physical punishment may prove spiritually valuable. Other proverbs say that some people will never learn from this kind of punishment, but in general this may be the only thing that works for some cases.
n200211: sn вАЬHeartвАЭ is a metonymy of subject; it signifies the ability to make decisions, if not the decisions themselves.
n200212: sn вАЬHandвАЭ in this passage is a personification; the word is frequently used idiomatically for вАЬpower,вАЭ and that is the sense intended here.
n200213:
tn вАЬChannels of waterвАЭ (вАП„§÷Љ÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Т÷µ„ЩвАО, palge) is an adverbial accusative, functioning as a figure of comparisonвАФвАЬlike channels of water.вАЭ Cf. NAB вАЬLike a streamвАЭ; NIV вАЬwatercourseвАЭ; NRSV, NLT вАЬa stream of water.вАЭ
sn The farmer channels irrigation ditches where he wants them, where they will do the most good; so does the Lord with the king. No king is supreme; the Lord rules.
n200214: tn Heb вАЬin his own eyes.вАЭ The term вАЬeyesвАЭ is a metonymy for estimation, opinion, evaluation. Physical sight is used figuratively for oneвАЩs point of view intellectually.
n200215: tn Heb вАЬweighsвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, NRSV); NLT вАЬexaminesвАЭ; NCV, TEV вАЬjudges.вАЭ Weighing on scales is an act of examining and verifying weight for market purposes (2 Kgs 12:11) but can be used metaphorically for evaluating other things (e.g. the Lord weighs actions in (1 Sam 2:3).
n200216:
tn Heb вАЬthe minds.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) is used as a metonymy of association for thoughts and motives (BDB 660-61 s.v. 6-7).
sn It is easy to rationalize oneвАЩs point of view and deceive even oneself. But the Lord evaluates our thinking and motives as well (cf. Prov 16:2).
n200217: tn The Niphal participle вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (bakhar, вАЬto chooseвАЭ) means вАЬchoice to the LordвАЭ or вАЬchosen of the Lord,вАЭ meaning вАЬacceptable to the LordвАЭ; cf. TEV вАЬpleases the Lord more.вАЭ
n200218: sn The Lord prefers righteousness above religious service (e.g., Prov 15:8; 21:29; 1 Sam 15:22; Ps 40:6-8; Isa 1:11-17). This is not a rejection of ritual worship; rather, religious acts are without value apart from righteous living.
n200219:
tc The MT reads вАЬthe tillage (вАП„†÷і„®вАО, nir) of the wicked.вАЭ The LXX, other versions, and some Hebrew manuscripts read вАЬthe lamp (вАП„†÷µ„®вАО, ner) of the wickedвАЭ (so ASV, NASB, NIV, NRSV). Both are difficult in the context.
tn The MTвАЩs вАП„†÷і„®вАО (nir) refers to вАЬground that has recently been cultivatedвАЭ (HALOT 723, s.v.), hence вАЬtillageвАЭ (NAB), or вАЬplowingвАЭ (KJV). This image goes one step behind the metaphor of planting to the cultivating the ground, but it may still have the harvest from such work in view. What the wicked cultivate (and produce) is sin. The saying perhaps looks at their haughty attitude as the groundwork for of their actions which are sinful. The LXXвАЩs вАЬlampвАЭ does not solve the difficulty of the relationship between the expressions. We would somehow need to equate haughtiness with what the wicked consider a (guiding?) light, so that it is evaluated as sin.
n2002110: tn The word вАЬdiligentвАЭ is an adjective used substantivally. The related verb means вАЬto cut, sharpen, decideвАЭ; so the adjective describes one who is вАЬsharpвАЭвАФone who acts decisively. The word вАЬhastyвАЭ has the idea of being pressed or pressured into quick actions. So the text contrasts calculated expeditiousness with unproductive haste. C. H. Toy does not like this contrast, and so proposes changing the latter to вАЬlazyвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 399), but W. McKane rightly criticizes that as unnecessarily forming a pedestrian antithesis (Proverbs [OTL], 550).
n2002111: tn The term вАЬleadвАЭ is supplied in the translation.
n2002112: tn The Hebrew noun translated вАЬplentyвАЭ comes from the verb вАП„Щ÷Є„™÷Ј„®вАО (yatar), which means вАЬto remain over.вАЭ So the calculated diligence will lead to abundance, prosperity.
n2002113: tn Heb вАЬlack; need; thing neededвАЭ; NRSV вАЬto want.вАЭ
n2002114: tn The first word of the verse is the noun meaning вАЬdoing, deed, work.вАЭ The BHS editors suggest reading with the LXX an active participleвАФвАЬthe one who makesвАЭ (cf. NAB вАЬHe who makesвАЭ). The second word means вАЬtreasure,вАЭ from the verb вАЬlay up, store up.вАЭ It is an objective genitive here.
n2002115: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2002116: tn The Hebrew вАП„Ф÷ґ„С÷ґ„Ь „†÷і„У÷Љ÷Є„£вАО (hevel niddaf) is properly вАЬa driven vaporвАЭ (вАЬdrivenвАЭ = the Niphal participle). The point of the metaphor is that the ill-gotten gains will vanish into thin air. The LXX has вАЬpursuesвАЭ (as if reading вАП„®÷є„У÷µ„£вАО, rodef); cf. NAB вАЬchasing a bubble over deadly snares.вАЭ
n2002117: tn The Hebrew has вАЬseekers of death,вАЭ meaning вАЬ[they that seek them] are seekers of death,вАЭ or that the fortune is вАЬa fleeting vapor for those who seek death.вАЭ The sense is not readily apparent. The Greek and the Latin versions have вАЬsnares of deathвАЭ; the form вАП„Ю„Х÷є„І÷∞„©÷µ„Б„ЩвАО (moqeshe) was read instead of вАП„Ю÷∞„С÷Ј„І÷∞„©÷µ„Б„ЩвАО (mevaqqeshe). This reading does not make a more credible metaphor, and one must explain the loss of the letter вАП„СвАО (bet) in the textual variant. It is, however, slightly easier to interpret in the verse, and is followed by a number of English versions (e.g., NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT). But whether the easier reading is the correct one in this case would be difficult to prove.
n2002118: tn The вАЬviolenceвАЭ (вАП„©„Б÷є„УвАО, shod) drags away the wicked, probably either to do more sin or to their punishment. вАЬViolenceвАЭ here is either personified, or it is a metonymy of cause, meaning вАЬthe outcome of their violenceвАЭ drags them away.
n2002119: tn Heb вАЬviolence of the wicked.вАЭ This is a subjective genitive: вАЬviolence which the wicked do.вАЭ
n2002120: tn The second colon of the verse is the causal clause, explaining why they are dragged away. They are not passive victims of their circumstances or their crimes. They have chosen to persist in their violence and so it destroys them.
n2002121: tn Heb вАЬthey refused to do вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„ШвАО (mishpat; justice). The noun may be an accusative of direct object вАЬdo justiceвАЭ (so ASV) or an adverbial accusative of manner вАЬact justlyвАЭ or вАЬact with justiceвАЭ (so NASB).
n2002122: tn The first line of the proverb is difficult. Since вАП„Х÷Є„Ц÷Є„®вАО (vazar) occurs only here it has been given much attention. The translation of вАЬguiltyвАЭ is drawn from an Arabic cognate meaning вАЬto bear a burdenвАЭ and so вАЬto be sin ladenвАЭ or вАЬguiltyвАЭ (cf. NASB, NIV, NCV, NRSV, NLT). G. R. Driver prefers to read the line as вАЬa man crooked of ways is false [zar]вАЭ (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew Text of Proverbs,вАЭ Bib 32 [1951]: 185). C. H. Toy adopts the meaning of вАЬproudвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 400). Whatever the reading, вАЬguiltyвАЭ or вАЬproudвАЭ or вАЬfalse,вАЭ the idea is that such people are devious. Bad people are underhanded; good people are aboveboard (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 400). Another way to analyze the line is to read it with the definition вАЬstrange, strangerвАЭ: вАЬThe way of a man and a stranger is perverse.вАЭ But this is unclear, and would form no satisfactory contrast to 8b. Another suggestion is вАЬthe way of (usual) man is changeable and strange, but the pure fellow leads a straight and even courseвАЭ (J. H. Greenstone, Proverbs, 244); cf. NLT вАЬthe innocent travel a straight road.вАЭ
n2002123: tn The form вАП„Ф÷≤„§÷Ј„Ы÷∞„§÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (hafakhpakh) is an adjective with an intensified meaning due to the duplication of the second and third radicals; it means вАЬvery devious; crookedвАЭ (from the verb вАЬto overturnвАЭ).
n2002124: tn If this translation stands, then the construction is formed with an independent nominative absolute, resumed by the suffixed noun as the formal subject. It draws attention to the вАЬpureвАЭ or вАЬinnocentвАЭ person in contrast to the previously mentioned wicked.
n2002125:
tn English versions which translate the Hebrew term as вАЬroofвАЭ here sometimes produce amusing images for modern readers: TEV вАЬBetter to live on the roofвАЭ; CEV вАЬItвАЩs better to stay outside on the roof of your house.вАЭ
sn The reference is probably to a small room that would be built on the flat housetop primarily for guests (e.g., 1 Kgs 17:19; 2 Kgs 4:10). It would be cramped and lonelyвАФbut peaceful in avoiding strife.
n2002126: tn The phrase вАЬthan a house of companyвАЭ has received numerous interpretations. The word вАЬcompanyвАЭ or вАЬcompanionshipвАЭ would qualify вАЬhouseвАЭ as a place to be shared. The BHS editors propose вАЬspacious house,вАЭ which would call for a transposition of letters (cf. NAB вАЬa roomy houseвАЭ; NLT вАЬa lovely homeвАЭ). Such an emendation makes good sense, but has no external support.
n2002127: tn Heb вАЬa wife of contentionsвАЭ; KJV вАЬa brawling womanвАЭ; TEV, CEV вАЬa nagging wife.вАЭ The Greek version has no reference to a quarrelsome wife, but instead mentions justice in a common house.
n2002128: tn Heb вАЬsoul.вАЭ The Hebrew text uses вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, traditionally, вАЬsoulвАЭ) as the formal subject of the sentenceвАФвАЬthe soul of a wicked man desires.вАЭ This term has at its core the idea of appetites, and so its use here underscores that the cravings are deep-seated (BDB 660 s.v. 5), and the translation вАЬappetiteвАЭ reflects this.
n2002129: tn The verb вАП„Р÷і„Х÷Љ÷∞„™÷Є„ФвАО ( Њivvetah) is a Piel perfect. Categorically, Piel verbs are dynamic rather than stative, so the perfect form should be understood as past or perfective. In the Qal, some verbs for вАЬdesireвАЭ are stative and some dynamic; so semantically the question could be raised whether this is a rare, or lone, stative in the Piel. If stative, it could be understood as present tense, as rendered in most translations. But it is doubtful that more recent developments in linguistics and biblical Hebrew influenced any of the translations. However, as perfective we should understand that this is what they have set their desire on, and that is ongoing, so a present time relevance is appropriate. In this proverb the first colon provides the setting as a basis, and the second colon gives the result. We may understand it as вАЬbecause [he/she] has desired evil, his/her neighbor will not be shown favor.вАЭ
n2002130: tn The form вАП„Щ÷ї„Ч÷Ј„ЯвАО (yukhan) is a Hophal imperfect from вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (khanan); it means вАЬto be shown mercyвАЭвАФhere negated to mean вАЬhe will not be shown mercy.вАЭ The person who lives to satisfy his own craving for evil will not be interested in meeting the needs of others.
n2002131: sn The contrast here is between the simple and the wise. The simple gain wisdom when they see the scorner punished; the wise gains knowledge through instruction. The scorner does not change, but should be punished for the benefit of the simple (e.g., Prov 19:25).
n2002132: tn Heb вАЬin the instructing of the wise.вАЭ The construction uses the Hiphil infinitive construct вАП„Ф÷Ј„©„В÷∞„Ы÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЬвАО (haskil) with a preposition to form a temporal clause (= вАЬwhenвАЭ). The word вАЬwiseвАЭ (вАП„Ч÷Є„Ы÷Є„ЭвАО, khakham) after it is the subjective genitive. The preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) on the form is probably dittography from the ending of the infinitive.
n2002133: tn In the book of Proverbs, the Hebrew term вАП„¶÷Ј÷Љ„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ІвАО (tsaddiq) normally refers to a human being, and that is a possible translation here (cf. KJV, ASV, NAB), although it would have to refer to a righteous person who was a judge or a ruler with the right to destroy the wicked. Many commentators and English versions simply interpret this as a reference to God (cf. NIV, NRSV, TEV, NLT).
n2002134: tn The form вАП„Ю÷Ј„©÷∞„В„Ы÷і÷Љ„Щ„ЬвАО (maskil) is now used with the meaning вАЬto consider; to give attention to; to ponder.вАЭ It is the careful scrutiny that is given to the household of the wicked before judgment is poured out on them.
n2002135: tn Heb вАЬhouse.вАЭ This term probably means вАЬhouseholdвАЭ hereвАФthe family. One way to read the line is that the righteous judge (human or divine) takes into consideration the wicked personвАЩs family before judging the wicked person. The otherвАФand more plausibleвАФinterpretation is that the judge considers the household of the wicked and then on the basis of what was observed judges them.
n2002136: tn Heb вАЬto evilвАЭ (i.e., catastrophe); cf. NLT вАЬto disaster.вАЭ
n2002137: sn The imagery means вАЬpay no attention toвАЭ the cry for help or вАЬrefuse to help,вАЭ so it is a metonymy of cause for the effect.
n2002138: sn вАЬCryвАЭ here would be a metonymy of effect for the cause, the cause being the great needs of the poor.
n2002139: sn The proverb is teaching that those who show mercy will receive mercy. It involves the principle of talionic justiceвАФthose who refuse the needs of others will themselves be refused when they need help (so Luke 16:19-31).
n2002140: sn The synonymous parallelism joins the more neutral term вАЬgiftвАЭ with the more specific вАЬbribe.вАЭ D. Kidner notes that this underscores how hard it is to tell the difference between them, especially since they accomplish similar things (Proverbs [TOTC], 143).
n2002141: tn The word вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„§÷Є„ФвАО (kafah) occurs only here; it means вАЬto subdue,вАЭ but in New Hebrew it means вАЬto overturn; to compel.вАЭ The BHS editors suggest a change to вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО (kavah), вАЬto be quenched,вАЭ based on Symmachus and Tg. Prov 21:14, but there is no substantial improvement in the textвАЩs meaning with such a change.
n2002142: tn Heb вАЬa bribe in the bosomвАЭ (so NASB). This refers to a gift hidden in the folds of the garment, i.e., given secretly (cf. NIV вАЬa bribe concealed in the cloakвАЭ).
n2002143: tn The repetition of the term вАЬsubduesвАЭ in the second line is supplied in the translation.
n2002144: tc The LXX offers a moralizing translation not too closely tied to the MT: вАЬhe who withholds a gift stirs up violent wrath.вАЭ
n2002145: tn The Qal infinitive construct вАП„Ґ÷≤„©„В„Х÷є„™вАО ( њasot) functions as the subject of the sentence.
n2002146: tn The term вАЬbringsвАЭ is supplied in the translation; many English versions supply a simple copula (вАЬisвАЭ).
n2002147: sn The noun means вАЬterror (NAB, NASB, NIV), destruction (KJV, ASV), ruin (cf. NCV).вАЭ Its related verb means вАЬbe shattered, dismayed.вАЭ The idea of вАЬdismayвАЭ (NRSV) or вАЬterrorвАЭ would make the better choice to contrast with вАЬjoyвАЭ in the first line, but вАЬruinвАЭ is also possible. Whenever justice prevails, whether in the courts or simply in society, the people who practice iniquity may be shaken into reality by fear (cf. CEV вАЬcrooks are terrifiedвАЭ).
n2002148: tn The text uses вАЬmanвАЭ as the subject and the active participle вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„Ґ÷ґ„ФвАО (to њeh) as the predicate. The image of вАЬwandering off the pathвАЭ signifies leaving a life of knowledge, prudence, and discipline.
n2002149: tn Or вАЬprudenceвАЭ; KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV вАЬunderstandingвАЭ; NLT вАЬcommon sense.вАЭ
n2002150: tn Heb вАЬwill remainвАЭ or вАЬwill rest.вАЭ The Hebrew word вАП„†„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (nuakh) does not here carry any of the connotations of comforting repose in death that the righteous enjoy; it simply means вАЬto remain; to reside; to dwell.вАЭ The choice of this verb might have an ironic twist to it, reminding the wicked what might have been.
n2002151: sn The departed are the Shades (the Rephaim). The literal expression вАЬwill rest among the ShadesвАЭ means вАЬwill be numbered among the dead.вАЭ So once again physical death is presented as the punishment for folly.
n2002152: sn The participle вАЬlovesвАЭ (вАП„Р÷є„Ф÷µ„СвАО,  Њohev) indicates in this context that more is involved than the enjoyment of pleasure, for which there is no problem. The proverb is looking at вАЬloveвАЭ in the sense of needing and choosing, an excessive or uncontrolled indulgence in pleasure.
n2002153: sn вАЬPleasureвАЭ is actually the Hebrew word вАЬjoyвАЭ (вАП„©„В÷і„Ю÷∞„Ч÷Є„ФвАО, simkhah). It is a metonymy of effect, the cause being the good life that brings the joy. In the second colon, вАЬwineвАЭ and вАЬoilвАЭ would be metonymies of cause, the particular things in life that bring joy. Therefore the figures in the lines work together to give the complete picture.
n2002154: tn The phrase вАЬwill beвАЭ is supplied in the translation.
n2002155: tn Heb вАЬa man of povertyвАЭ; NRSV вАЬwill suffer want.вАЭ
n2002156: sn In elaborate feasts and celebrations the wine was for drinking but the oil was for anointing (cf. NAB, NCV вАЬperfumeвАЭ). Both of these characterize the luxurious life (e.g., Pss 23:5; 104:15; Amos 6:6).
n2002157: sn The Hebrew word translated вАЬransomвАЭ (вАП„Ы÷є÷Љ„§÷ґ„®вАО, kofer) normally refers to a penalty paid in place of some other punishment or the price paid to free a prisoner. But since it seems out of place to suggest that the just face a punishment that they need a ransom for, the proverb remains obscure. Similar wording is reflected in Isa 43:3-4 where God substitutes Egpyt as JudahвАЩs вАЬransomвАЭ and Ethiopia and Seba вАЬin place ofвАЭ Judah. In that passage Judah is not just, but has been punished and is now being redeemed. Another application reported by R. Murphy is that punishment intended for a group may take the wicked from that group, who then serve metaphorically as a ransom for the righteous (R. Murphy, Proverbs [WBC] 161), but as Murphy points out this is an application rather than the assertion of the proverb. R. N. Whybray (Proverbs [CBC], 121) similarly suggests it may taken to mean that the wicked suffers the evil he has prepared for the righteous, which harmonizes with Proverbs elsewhere (e.g., 11:8). When Haman is taken in place of Mordecai (Esth 7:9-10) would illustrate an application where the righteous escape and the wicked suffer in their place.
n2002158: tn The verb вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Т÷Ј„УвАО (bagad), here a participle, means вАЬto act treacherously, with duplicity, or to betray.вАЭ
n2002159: tn The phrase вАЬare takenвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is implied by the parallelism; it is supplied in the translation for smoothness.
n2002160: tn The Hebrew form вАП„©„Б÷ґ„С÷ґ„™вАО (shevet) is the infinitive construct of вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„СвАО (yashav), functioning as the subject of the sentence.
n2002161: sn A wilderness (вАП„Ю÷і„У÷∞„С÷Љ÷Є„®вАО, midbar) is too dry for permanent settlements since it receives less than twelve inches of rain annually. It may be able to support enough vegetation for bedouin to use on a temporary basis. In this context it represents a harsh environment, but a quiet place. Cf. 21:9 and 25:24.
n2002162: tn The Hebrew noun вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„°вАО (ka њas) means вАЬvexation; anger.вАЭ The woman is not only characterized by a quarrelsome spirit, but also angerвАФshe is easily vexed (cf. NAB вАЬvexatiousвАЭ; NASB вАЬvexingвАЭ; ASV, NRSV вАЬfretfulвАЭ). The translation вАЬeasily-provokedвАЭ conveys this idea well.
n2002163: tn The mention of вАЬolive oilвАЭ (вАП„©„Б÷ґ„Ю÷ґ„ЯвАО, shemen) is problematic in the lineвАФhow can a fool devour it? Several attempts have been made to alleviate the problem. The NIV interprets вАЬtreasureвАЭ as вАЬchoice food,вАЭ so that food and oil would make more sense being swallowed. C. H. Toy (Proverbs [ICC], 406) suggests dropping вАЬoilвАЭ altogether based on the reading in the LXX, but the Greek is too general for any support: It has вАЬprecious treasure will rest on the mouth of the sage.вАЭ W. McKane wants to change вАЬoilвАЭ to an Arabic word вАЬexpensiveвАЭ to read вАЬdesirable and rare wealthвАЭ (Proverbs [OTL], 552), but this idea does not match the metaphor any better. The figure of вАЬdevouringвАЭ in the second line simply means the fool uses up whatever he has.
n2002165: tn Heb вАЬhe swallows it.вАЭ The imagery compares swallowing food with consuming oneвАЩs substance. The fool does not prepare for the future.
n2002166: sn These two attributes, вАЬrighteousnessвАЭ (вАП„¶÷∞„У÷Є„І÷Є„ФвАО, tsedaqah) and вАЬloyal loveвАЭ (вАП„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„УвАО, khesed) depict the life style of the covenant-believer who is pleasing to God and a blessing to others. The first term means that he will do what is right, and the second means that he will be faithful to the covenant community.
n2002167: sn The Hebrew term translated вАЬbountyвАЭ is вАП„¶÷∞„У÷Є„І÷Є„ФвАО (tsedaqah) again, so there is a wordplay on the term in the verse. The first use of the word had the basic meaning of вАЬconduct that conforms to GodвАЩs standardвАЭ; this second use may be understood as a metonymy of cause, indicating the provision or reward (вАЬbountyвАЭ) that comes from keeping righteousness (cf. NIV вАЬprosperityвАЭ; NCV вАЬsuccessвАЭ). The proverb is similar to Matt 5:6, вАЬBlessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they will be satisfied.вАЭ
n2002168: tn This proverb uses the perfect verb form in the first colon and the preterite form in the second colon. These are past time references. The proverb portrays something which has happened as prototypical, as in the English proverb вАЬcuriosity killed the cat.вАЭ That wisdom can prevail over brute strength or difficult odds is not an abstract concept but a proven fact.
n2002169:
tn Heb вАЬthe strength of its confidenceвАЭ or вАЬits trusted strength.вАЭ The word вАЬstrengthвАЭ may refer by metonymy to the place of strength, i.e., вАЬthe stronghold.вАЭ
sn In a war the victory is credited not so much to the infantry as to the tactician who plans the attack. Brilliant strategy wins wars, even over apparently insuperable odds (e.g., Prov 24:5-6; Eccl 9:13-16; 2 Cor 10:4).
n2002170: sn вАЬMouthвАЭ and вАЬtongueвАЭ are metonymies of cause, signifying what one says (cf. NCV, TEV, CEV).
n2002171: tn This part could also be translated вАЬkeeps himselfвАЭ (so NIV), for вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) often simply means вАЬthe whole person.вАЭ The participle вАП„©„Б÷є„Ю÷µ„®вАО (shomer) is repeated from the first line in the parallelismвАФto guard what is said is to guard against difficulty.
n2002172: sn The вАЬtroublesвАЭ (вАП„¶÷Є„®„Х÷є„™вАО, tsarot) here could refer to social and legal difficulties into which careless talk might bring someone (e.g., 13:3; 18:21). The word means вАЬa strait, a bind, difficulty.вАЭ Careless and free talking could get the person into a tight spot.
n2002173: tn The word вАП„Ц÷µ„УвАО (zed, вАЬproudвАЭ) comes from the verb вАП„Ц÷і„Щ„УвАО (zid, вАЬto boil up; to seethe; to act proudly [or, presumptuously].вАЭ Just as water boiling up in a pot will boil over, so the presumptuous person вАЬoverstepsвАЭ the boundaries.
n2002174: tn The word вАП„Щ÷Є„Ф÷і„Щ„®вАО (yahir) means вАЬhaughty,вАЭ that is, to be or show oneself to be presumptuous or arrogant.
n2002175: tn Heb вАЬproud haughty scorner his nameвАЭ (KJV similar). There are several ways that the line could be translated: (1) вАЬProud, arrogantвАФhis name is scofferвАЭ or (2) вАЬA proud person, an arrogant personвАФвАШScofferвАЩ is his name.вАЭ BDB 267 s.v. вАП„Ц÷µ„УвАО suggests, вАЬA presumptuous man, [who is] haughty, scoffer is his name.вАЭ
n2002176: tn Heb вАЬdoes.вАЭ The Qal active participle вАЬdoesвАЭ serves as the main verb, and the subject is вАЬproud personвАЭ in the first line.
n2002177:
tn The expression вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷ґ„С÷∞„®÷Ј„™ „Ц÷Є„У„Х÷є„ЯвАО (be њevrat zadon) means вАЬin the overflow of insolence.вАЭ The genitive specifies what the overflow is; the proud deal in an overflow of pride. Cf. NIV вАЬoverweening prideвАЭ; NLT вАЬboundless arrogance.вАЭ
sn The portrait in this proverb is not merely of one who is self-sufficient, but one who is insolent, scornful, and arrogant.
n2002178: tn Heb вАЬthe desire of the sluggardвАЭ (so ASV, NASB). This phrase features a subject genitive: вАЬwhat the sluggard desires.вАЭ The term вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Р÷≤„Х÷Ј„™вАО (ta Њavat, вАЬdesire; cravingвАЭ) is a metonymy of cause. The craving itself will not destroy the sluggard, but what will destroy him is what the craving causes him to do or not to do. The lazy come to ruin because they desire the easy way out.
n2002179: tn The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ю÷і„Щ„™÷ґ„†÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (temitennu) is the Hiphil imperfect with a suffix: вАЬwill kill him.вАЭ It is probably used hyperbolically here for coming to ruin (cf. NLT), although it could include physical death.
n2002180: sn вАЬHandsвАЭ is figurative for the whole person, but вАЬhandsвАЭ is retained in the translation because it is often the symbol to express oneвАЩs ability of action.
n2002181: tn The construction uses the Hitpael perfect tense вАП„Ф÷і„™÷∞„Р÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (hit Њavvah) followed by the cognate accusative вАП„™÷Ј„Р÷≤„Х÷Є„ФвАО (ta Њavah). While the Piel verb means вАЬto desire, wish for,вАЭ the reflexive meaning of the Hitpael appears to mean to encourage or build oneвАЩs desire. An English idiom might be to fan the flames of desire. It is not inherently immoral (the king will build desire for his bride in Ps 45:11) but often more often refers to a greedy craving or lust. This verse has been placed with the preceding because of the lexical connection with вАЬdesire/craving.вАЭ
n2002182: sn The additional clause, вАЬand does not hold back,вАЭ emphasizes that when the righteous gives he gives freely, without fearing that his generosity will bring him to poverty. This is the contrast with the one who is self-indulgent and craves for more.
n2002183: tn Heb вАЬthe sacrifice of the wickedвАЭ (so KJV, NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV). This is a subjective genitive. The foundational clause states that ritual acts of worship brought by the wicked (thus a subjective genitive) are detestable to God. The вАЬwickedвАЭ refers here to people who are not members of the covenant (no faith) and are not following after righteousness (no acceptable works). But often they participate in sanctuary ritual, which amounts to hypocrisy.
n2002184: sn This rhetorical device shows that if the act is abomination, the wicked heart is an even greater sin. It argues from the lesser to the greater.
n2002185: tn The noun вАП„Ц÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (zimmah) means вАЬplan; device; wickednessвАЭ; here it indicates that the person is coming to the ritual with вАЬsinful purpose.вАЭ Some commentators suggest that this would mean he comes with the sacrifice as a bribe to pacify his conscience for a crime committed, over which he has little remorse or intent to cease (cf. NLT вАЬwith ulterior motivesвАЭ). In this view, people in ancient Israel came to think that sacrifices could be given for any reason without genuine submission to God.
n2002186: tn Heb вАЬa witness of lies,вАЭ an attributive genitive.
n2002187: tn The Hebrew verb translated вАЬwill perishвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷є„Р„С÷µ„УвАО, yo Њved) could mean that the false witness will die, either by the hand of God or by the community. But it also could be taken in the sense that the false testimony will be destroyed. This would mean that вАЬfalse witnessвАЭ would be a metonymy of causeвАФwhat he says will perish (cf. NCV вАЬwill be forgottenвАЭ).
n2002188: tn Heb вАЬbut a man who listens speaks forever.вАЭ The first part of it may mean (1) a true witness, one who reports what he actually hears. But it may also refer to (2) someone who listens to the false testimony given by the false witness. The NIV follows the suggestion of a homonym for the Hebrew word with the meaning вАЬwill perish/be destroyedвАЭ: вАЬwill be destroyed forever.вАЭ This suggests a synonymous pair of ideas rather than a contrast. Others accept antithetical parallelism. C. H. Toy suggested an idea like вАЬbe establishedвАЭ to contrast with вАЬwill perishвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 411). W. McKane suggested it meant the truthful witness вАЬwill speak to the endвАЭ without being put down (Proverbs [OTL], 556). It is simpler to interpret the words that are here in the sense of a contrast. The idea of speaking forever/to the end would then be hyperbolic.
n2002190: tn Heb вАЬhe has strengthened his face.вАЭ The Hifil of вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ц÷Ј„ЦвАО ( њazaz) вАЬto cause to be strongвАЭ is used idiomatically with вАЬfaceвАЭ meaning to show boldness. Similarly the seductress in Prov 7:13 had put on a bold/impudent face. This person makes a show of confidence, either to be persuasive or to divert their own attention from the substance of a matter. Their confidence is not backed up by reality.
n2002191: tn The вАЬuprightвАЭ is an independent nominative absolute; the pronoun becomes the formal (emphatic) subject of the verb.
n2002192: tc The Kethib is the imperfect of вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (kun), вАЬhe establishes.вАЭ This reading has the support of the Syriac, Latin, and Tg., and is followed by ASV and NASB. The Qere is the imperfect tense of вАП„С÷і÷Љ„Щ„ЯвАО (bin), вАЬhe understands; he discerns.вАЭ It has the support of the LXX and is followed by NIV, NCV, NRSV, NLT. The difficulty is that both make good sense in the passage and both have support. The contrast is between the wicked who [merely] puts up a bold front and the upright who either [actually] discerns his ways (Qere) or makes his ways solid (Kethib). And whichever reading is chosen, the meaning of the other is implied. It would not make sense for the verse to talk about someone who understands but does not act accordingly; on the other hand, to make his/her way solid, the upright person must understand it.
n2002193: tn The form вАП„Ь÷∞„†÷ґ„Т÷ґ„УвАО (leneged) means вАЬagainst; over against; in opposition to.вАЭ The line indicates they cannot in reality be in opposition, for human wisdom is nothing in comparison to the wisdom of God (J. H. Greenstone, Proverbs, 232).
n2002194: sn The verse uses a single sentence to state that all wisdom, understanding, and advice must be in conformity to the will of God to be successful. It states it negativelyвАФthese things cannot be in defiance of God (e.g., Job 5:12-13; Isa 40:13-14).
n2002195: tn Heb вАЬof the Lord.вАЭ The victory being вАЬof the LordвАЭ means that it is accomplished by him. Ultimate success comes from the Lord and not from human efforts. The faithful have acknowledged this down through the ages, even though they have been responsible and have prepared for the wars. Without this belief there would have been no prayer on the eve of battle (e.g., Pss 20:7; 33:17).
n200221: tn Heb вАЬa name.вАЭ The idea of the name being вАЬgoodвАЭ is implied; it has the connotation here of a reputation (cf. TEV, CEV, NLT).
n200222: tn вАЬTo be chosen rather thanвАЭ is a translation of the Niphal participle with the comparative degree taken into consideration. Cf. CEV вАЬworth much more than.вАЭ
n200223: tn Heb вАЬfavor of goodness.вАЭ This is a somewhat difficult expression. Some English versions render the phrase вАЬfavor is better than silver or goldвАЭ (so NASB, NRSV) making it parallel to the first colon. But if вАЬgoodвАЭ is retained as an attributive modifier, then it would mean one was well thought of, or one had engaging qualities (cf. ASV вАЬloving favor; NLT вАЬhigh esteemвАЭ). This fits with the idea of the reputation in the first colon, for a good name would bring with it the favor of others.
n200224: tn The form of the verb is the Niphal perfect of вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Т÷Ј„©„БвАО (pagash). The perfect verb form can be understood as perfective вАЬhave met togetherвАЭ but the Niphal may focus on the result вАЬare met together.вАЭ Having different economic status does not affect that they are met together (cf. NAB, NASB вАЬhave a common bond,вАЭ NIV, NLV вАЬhave this in commonвАЭ) in having the same Maker. Some commentators have taken this to mean that they should live together because they are part of GodвАЩs creation, but the verb form will not sustain that meaning.
n200225: tn Heb вАЬall of them.вАЭ The proverb may be emphasizing that everyone has the same creator regardless of their financial status, or be pointing out that God is the one who makes rich or poor. Either way it advises treating all people with respect, and not thinking too much, or too little, of oneself.
