n200226: sn The contrast is between the вАЬshrewdвАЭ (prudent) person and the вАЬsimpleton.вАЭ The shrewd person knows where the dangers and pitfalls are in life and so can avoid them; the naive person is unwary, untrained, and gullible, unable to survive the dangers of the world and blundering into them.
n200227: tn All the verbs in this verse are perfect forms, so past tense in English. They portray events that have happened as prototypical of what commonly happens.
n200228: tn Heb вАЬevil,вАЭ a term that is broad enough to include (1) вАЬsinвАЭ as well as (2) any form of вАЬdangerвАЭ (NIV, NCV, NRSV, NLT) or вАЬtroubleвАЭ (TEV, CEV). The second option is more likely what is meant here: The naive simpleton does not see the danger to be avoided and so suffers for it.
n200229: tc The Kethib is a Niphal imperfect, while the Qere is a Niphal perfect. The perfect form matches the rest of the verbs in the verse and is followed here.
n2002210: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷Ј„©„БвАО ( њanash) means вАЬto fineвАЭ specifically. In the Niphal stem it means вАЬto be fined,вАЭ or more generally, вАЬto be punished.вАЭ In this line the punishment is the consequence of blundering into troubleвАФthey will pay for it.
n2002211: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Ґ÷µ„І÷ґ„СвАО ( њeqev, вАЬrewardвАЭ) is related to the term meaning вАЬheelвАЭ; it refers to the consequences or the reward that follows (akin to the English expression вАЬon the heels ofвАЭ).
n2002212: tn вАЬHumilityвАЭ is used here in the religious sense of вАЬpietyвАЭ; it is appropriately joined with вАЬthe fear of the Lord.вАЭ Some commentators, however, make вАЬthe fear of the LordвАЭ the first in the series of rewards for humility, but that arrangement is less likely here.
n2002213: tn Heb вАЬthe fear of the Lord.вАЭ This is an objective genitive; the Lord is the object of the fear.
n2002214:
tc Because MT reading вАП„¶÷і„†÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (tsinnim, вАЬthornsвАЭ) does not make a very good match with вАЬtraps,вАЭ it has created some difficulty for interpreters. The word вАЬthornsвАЭ may be obscure, but it is supported by the LXX (вАЬprickly plantsвАЭ) and an apparent cognate вАЬthornsвАЭ in Num 33:55 and Josh 23:13. But some (including the editors of BHS) suggest changing it to вАП„¶÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (tsammim, вАЬtrapsвАЭ changing a вАП„†вАО [nun] to a вАП„ЮвАО [mem]). But BDB 855 s.v. вАП„¶÷Ј„Ю÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЭвАО acknowledges that this word is a doubtful word, attested only a couple of times in Job (e.g., 18:9). W. McKane traces a development from the idea of вАП„¶÷µ„ЯвАО (tsen, вАЬbasket; trapвАЭ) to support this change (Proverbs [OTL], 565). The present translation (like many other English versions) has retained вАЬthorns,вАЭ even though the parallelism with вАЬtrapsвАЭ is not very good; as the harder reading it is preferred. The variant readings have little textual or philological support, and simplify the line.
sn вАЬThorns and snaresвАЭ represent the dangers and threats to life. They would be implied comparisons (hypocatastasis): As a path strewn with thorns and traps, life for the wicked will be filled with dangers and difficulties.
n2002215: tn The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (khanakh) means вАЬto train up; to dedicateвАЭ (BDB 335 s.v.; HALOT 334 s.v. вАП„Ч„†„ЪвАО). The verb is used elsewhere to refer to dedicating a house (Deut 20:5; 1 Kgs 8:63; 2 Chr 7:5). The related noun вАП„Ч÷≤„†÷ї„Ы÷Є„ФвАО (khanukhah) means вАЬdedication; consecrationвАЭ (BDB 335 s.v.; HALOT 334 s.v.), and is used in reference to the dedication or consecration of altars (Num 7:10; 2 Chr 7:9), the temple (Ps 30:1), and town walls (Neh 12:27). The related adjective вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷і„Щ„Ъ÷∞вАО (khanikh) describes вАЬtrained, tried, experiencedвАЭ men (BDB 335 s.v.; Gen 14:14). In the related cognate languages the verb has similar meanings: Aramaic вАЬto train,вАЭ Ethiopic вАЬto initiate,вАЭ and Arabic IV вАЬto learn; to make experiencedвАЭ (HALOT 334 s.v.). This proverb pictures a child who is dedicated by parents to the Lord and morally trained to follow him. On the other hand, a popular expositional approach suggests that it means вАЬto motivate.вАЭ This view is based on a cognate Arabic root II which (among many other things) refers to the practice of rubbing the palate of a newborn child with date juice or olive oil to motivate the child to suck. While this makes an interesting sermon illustration, it is highly unlikely that this concept was behind this Hebrew verb. The Arabic meaning is late and secondaryвАФthe Arabic term did not have this meaning until nearly a millennium after this proverb was written.
n2002216: tn The term вАП„†÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (na њar) is traditionally translated вАЬchildвАЭ here (so almost all English versions), but might mean вАЬyouth.вАЭ The noun can refer to a broad range of ages (see BDB 654-55 s.v.; HALOT 707 s.v.): infant (Exod 2:6), weaned child (1 Sam 1:24), young child (Jer 1:6), lad (Gen 22:12), adolescent (Gen 37:2), or young man of marriageable age (Gen 34:19). The context focuses on the childвАЩs young, formative years. The Talmud says this would be up to the age of twenty-four.
n2002217: tn The expression in Hebrew is вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„§÷Љ÷і„Щ „У÷Љ÷Ј„®÷∞„Ы÷Љ„Х÷євАО ( њal pi darko), which can be rendered вАЬaccording to his wayвАЭ; NEB вАЬStart a boy on the right road.вАЭ The expression вАЬhis wayвАЭ is вАЬthe way he should goвАЭ; it reflects the point the book of Proverbs is making that there is a standard of life to which he must attain. Saadia, a Jewish scholar who lived a.d. 882-942, first suggested that this could mean the child should be trained according to his inclination or bent of mind. This may have some merit in practice, but it is not likely what the proverb had in mind. In the book of Proverbs there are only two ways that a person can go, the way of the wise or righteousness, and the way of the fool. One takes training, and the other does not. Ralbag, in fact, offered a satirical interpretation: вАЬTrain a child according to his evil inclinations (let him have his will) and he will continue in his evil way throughout lifeвАЭ (J. H. Greenstone, Proverbs, 234). C. H. Toy says the expression means вАЬin accordance with the manner of life to which he is destined (Proverbs [ICC], 415). W. McKane says, вАЬThere is only one right wayвАФthe way of lifeвАФand the educational discipline which directs young men along this way is uniformвАЭ (Proverbs [OTL], 564). This phrase does not describe the concept perpetuated by a modern psychological interpretation of the verse: Train a child according to his personality trait.
n2002218: sn The expected consequence of such training is that it will last throughout life. The sages were confident of the character-forming quality of their training. However, proverbs are not universal truths. One can anticipate positive results from careful child-trainingвАФbut there may be an occasional exception.
n2002219: sn The proverb is making an observation on life. The synonymous parallelism matches вАЬrule overвАЭ with вАЬservantвАЭ to show how poverty makes people dependent on, or obligated to, others.
n2002220: tn Or вАЬslaveвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NRSV, TEV, CEV). This may refer to the practice in Israel of people selling themselves into slavery to pay off debts (Exod 21:2-7).
n2002221: sn The verse is making an implied comparison (a figure of speech known as hypocatastasis) between sowing and sinning. One who sins is like one who sows, for there will be a вАЬharvestвАЭ or a return on the sinвАФtrouble.
n2002222: tc There is a variant reading in the LXX; instead of вАЬthe rod of his wrathвАЭ it reads вАЬthe punishment of his deeds.вАЭ C. H. Toy wishes to emend вАП„©„Б÷µ„С÷ґ„ШвАО (shevet) to вАП„©„Б÷ґ„С÷ґ„®вАО (shever), вАЬthe produce of his workвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 416). But the Hebrew text is not obscure, and вАП„©„Б÷ґ„С÷ґ„®вАО does not exactly mean вАЬproduce.вАЭ The expression вАЬrod of his wrathвАЭ may not follow the imagery of 8a very closely, but it is nonetheless understandable. The вАЬrodвАЭ is a symbol of power; вАЬwrathвАЭ is a metonymy of cause indicating what wrath will do, and an objective genitive. The expression signifies that in reaping trouble for his sins this person will no longer be able to unleash his fury on others. The LXX adds: вАЬA man who is cheerful and a giver God blessesвАЭ (e.g., 2 Cor 9:7).
n2002223: tn Heb вАЬgood of eye.вАЭ This expression is an attributive genitive meaning вАЬbountiful of eyeвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV вАЬHe that hath a bountiful eyeвАЭ). This is the opposite of the вАЬevil eyeвАЭ which is covetous and wicked. The вАЬeyeвАЭ is a metonymy representing looking well to peopleвАЩs needs. So this refers to the generous person (cf. NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2002224: tn The form вАП„Щ÷∞„С÷є„®÷Є„Ъ÷∞вАО (yevorakh) is a Pual imperfect (here in pause) from вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (barakh); the word means вАЬblessedвАЭ in the sense of вАЬenriched,вАЭ implying there is a practical reward for being generous to the poor.
n2002225: sn It is from his own food that he gives to the poor. Of the many observations that could be made, it is worth noting that in blessing this kind of person God is in fact providing for the poor, because out of his blessing he will surely continue to share more. Also, the blessing is not for those who take the resources of others and redistribute that to the poor.
n2002226: sn This proverb, written in loose synonymous parallelism, instructs that the scorner should be removed because he causes strife. The вАЬscornerвАЭ is вАП„Ь÷µ„•вАО (lets), the one the book of Proverbs says cannot be changed with discipline or correction, but despises and disrupts anything that is morally or socially constructive.
n2002227: tc The LXX freely adds вАЬwhen he sits in council (бЉРќљ ѕГѕЕќљќµќіѕБќѓбњ≥, en sunedri≈Н), he insults everyone.вАЭ The MT does not suggest that the setting is in a court of law; so the LXX addition is highly unlikely.
n2002228: sn The вАЬheartвАЭ is a metonymy of subject; it represents the intentions and choices that are made. вАЬPure of heartвАЭ uses вАЬheartвАЭ as a genitive of specification. The expression refers to someone who has honest and clear intentions.
n2002229:
tn Heb вАЬgrace of his lipsвАЭ (so KJV, ASV). The вАЬlipsвАЭ are a metonymy of cause representing what is said; it also functions as a genitive of specification.
sn This individual is gracious or kind in what he says; thus the verse is commending honest intentions and gracious words.
n2002230: tn The syntax of the line is somewhat difficult, because вАЬgrace of his lipsвАЭ seems to be intruding on the point of the verse with little explanation. Therefore the LXX rendered it вАЬThe Lord loves the pure in heart; all who are blameless in their ways are acceptable to him.вАЭ This has very little correspondence with the Hebrew; nevertheless commentators attempt to reconstruct the verse using it, and the NAB follows the first clause of the LXX here. Some have suggested taking вАЬkingвАЭ as the subject of the whole verse (вАЬthe king lovesвА¶вАЭ), but this is forced.
n2002231: sn The вАЬeyes of the LordвАЭ is an anthropomorphic expression; the omniscience of God is the intended meaning. When scripture uses the вАЬeyesвАЭ of the Lord, it usually means evaluation, superintending, or safeguarding.
n2002232: tn The first verb is the Hebrew perfect form and the second is a preterite, successive actions in past time. The proverb presents something God has done as prototypical.
n2002233: tn The common Hebrew word вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО (da њat), with the abstract meaning вАЬknowledge, ability, insight,вАЭ is an awkward direct object for this subject and verb. The verb is used one other time with this object but the expression is different (Prov 5:2 вАЬthat your lips preserve knowledge,вАЭ that is, to say what is wise and not need words of regret). вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО does occur in Prov 29:7 in parallelism to вАП„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (din), a cause or legal claim, which suggests a technical use for вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО, such as the facts of a case. HALOT (I, 229) follows D. W. Thomas (VTSupp 3, 285) in proposing a homonym for вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО meaning вАЬclaim, rightвАЭ based on an Arabic cognate. (See also D. W. Thomas вАЬA Note on вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„™вАО in Proverbs 22:12, вАЭ JTS 14 [1963]: 93-94). The second half of the proverb supports the idea of ensuring that the truth comes out.
n2002234:
tn The participle вАП„С÷є„Т÷µ„УвАО (voged) means вАЬone who acts treacherously, a traitor;вАЭ cf. NASB вАЬthe treacherous man;вАЭ ESV вАЬtraitor;вАЭ NIV вАЬunfaithful;вАЭ KJV вАЬtransgressor.вАЭ What treacherous people say is treachery, that which would distort or undermine a just cause.
sn The proverb affirms that God in safeguarding true knowledge will frustrate deception from faithless peopleвАФwhat they say will not have its intended effect.
n2002235: tn The verb вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО ( Њamar) can mean вАЬto sayвАЭ or вАЬto think.вАЭ The proverb uses the Hebrew perfect form of the verb for the past tense, giving the reason the sluggard is still in the house rather than out working. It is an example of the sorts of excuses he has made.
n2002236: sn The proverb humorously describes the sluggard as making ridiculous excuses for not workingвАФhe might be eaten by a lion (e.g., 26:13). It is possible that вАЬlionвАЭ is figurative, intended to represent someone who is like a lion, but this detracts from the humor of the exaggeration.
n2002237: tc The LXX changes the phrase to read вАЬmurderers in the streetвАЭ to form a better parallelism, possibly because the verb вАП„®÷Є„¶÷Ј„ЧвАО (ratsakh) is used only of humans, not wild animals. The NIV attempts to solve the problem by making the second line a separate claim by the sluggard: вАЬor, вАШI will be murdered in the streets!вАЩвАЭ
n2002238: sn The word вАЬmouthвАЭ is a metonymy of cause; it refers to the seductive speech of the strange woman (e.g., 2:16-22; and chs. 5, 7).
n2002239: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2002240: sn The point of the metaphor is that what the adulteress says is like a deep pit. The pit is like the hunterвАЩs snare; it is a trap that is difficult to escape. So to succumb to the adulteressвАФor to any other folly this representsвАФis to get oneself into a difficulty that has no easy escape.
n2002241: tn Heb вАЬthe one who is cursed by the LordвАЭ (cf. NASB). The construction uses the passive participle in construct with Yahweh. The вАЬLordвАЭ is genitive of agency after the passive form. The verb means вАЬbe indignant, express indignation.вАЭ So it is talking about one against whom the Lord is angry.
n2002242:
tn Heb вАЬwill fall there.вАЭ The вАЬfallingвАЭ could refer to the curse itself or to the result of the curse.
sn The proverb is saying that the Lord will use the seductive, deceptive words of the adulteress to bring about the downfall of one who is inclined to such folly.
n2002243: sn The passive participle is figurative (implied comparison with вАЬbindingвАЭ); it means that folly forms part of a childвАЩs nature (J. H. Greenstone, Proverbs, 238).
n2002244: tn The вАЬheart of a childвАЭ (вАП„Ь÷ґ„С÷Њ„†÷Є„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО, lev na њar) refers here to the natural inclination of a child to foolishness. The younger child is meant in this context, but the word can include youth. R. N. Whybray suggests that this idea might be described as a doctrine of вАЬoriginal follyвАЭ (Proverbs [CBC], 125). Cf. TEV вАЬChildren just naturally do silly, careless things.вАЭ
n2002245: tn The word вАЬrodвАЭ is a metonymy of adjunct; it represents physical chastening for direction or punishment, to suppress folly and develop potential. The genitive (вАЬdisciplineвАЭ) may be taken as an attributive genitive (вАЬa chastening rodвАЭ) or an objective genitive, (вАЬa rod [= punishment] that brings about correction/disciplineвАЭ).
n2002246: tn Heb вАЬoppressing the poor, it is gain; giving to the rich, it is loss.вАЭ The Hebrew is cryptic, but two sins are mentioned here that will be punished by poverty: extortion and bribery. Perhaps the proverb is simply saying it is easy to oppress the poor for gain, but it is a waste of money to try to buy or bribe a patron (D. Kidner, Proverbs [TOTC], 149).
n2002247: sn A new collection of sayings begins here, forming the fourth section of the book of Proverbs. This collection is not like that of 1:1-9:18; here the introductory material is more personal than 1:1-7, and the style differs, showing great similarity to the Instruction of Amenemope in Egypt (especially the thirty precepts of the sages in 22:17-24:22). Verses 17-21 form the introduction, and then the sayings begin in v. 22. After the thirty sayings are given, there are further sayings in 24:23-34. There is much literature on this material: see W. K. Simpson, ed., Literature of Ancient Egypt; ANET 412-425; and A. Cody, вАЬNotes on Proverbs 22:21 and 22:23b,вАЭ Bib 61 (1980): 418-26.
n2002248: sn To вАЬincline the earвАЭ means to вАЬlisten carefullyвАЭ (cf. NCV); the expression is metonymical in that the ear is the instrument for hearing. It is like telling someone to lean over to hear better.
n2002249: tn Or вАЬheart.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev) can refer to the вАЬmindвАЭ or the вАЬheartвАЭ and represent a personвАЩs thinking, feeling, or will.
n2002250: tn Heb вАЬknowledgeвАЭ (so KJV, NASB); in this context it refers to the knowledge that is spoken by the wise, hence вАЬinstruction.вАЭ
n2002252: tn Heb вАЬkeep them,вАЭ referring to the words of the wise expressed in these sayings. The referent has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n2002253: tn The term вАЬandвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is supplied in the translation.
n2002254: sn If the teachings are preserved in the heart/mind of the disciple, then that individual will always be ready to speak what was retained.
n2002255: tn The form вАП„Ь÷і„Ф÷∞„Щ„Х÷є„™вАО (liheyot, вАЬto beвАЭ) is the infinitive construct indicating the purpose (or result) of the teaching (cf. NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2002256: tn The verb вАП„Ф„Х÷є„У÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„™÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (hoda њti; from вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО, yada њ) is a Hiphil perfect form. The Hiphil is factitive вАЬto make know,вАЭ i.e., вАЬto inform.вАЭ The Hebrew perfect should be understood either as perfective вАЬI have informed youвАЭ or performative вАЬI hereby inform you.вАЭ Either is appropriate for вАЬtodayвАЭ since the thirty sayings it refers to have been written down (v. 20), but it appears to be part of introducing the sayings rather than a recap. However if the вАЬthirty [sayings]вАЭ mentioned in v. 20 should be understood as the word вАЬday before yesterdayвАЭ then the perfective translation should be preferred.
n2002257: tn Older English versions and a few more recent ones render this phrase as either вАЬexcellent thingsвАЭ following the Qere (so KJV, ASV, NASB, NKJV), вАЬofficers,вАЭ or вАЬheretoforeвАЭ [day before yesterday], following the Kethib. However (as in most recent English versions) the Qere should be rendered вАЬthirty,вАЭ referring to the number in the collection (cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2002258: tn The term вАЬsayingsвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text but is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2002259: tn Heb вАЬto cause you to know the truth of words of truthвАЭ (NASB similar).
n2002260: tn Heb вАЬto return true wordsвАЭ; NAB вАЬa dependable reportвАЭ; NIV вАЬsound answers.вАЭ
n2002261:
tn Two negated jussives form the instruction here: вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„™÷Љ÷і„Т÷∞„Ц÷Є„ЬвАО ( Њal tigzal, вАЬdo not exploitвАЭ) and вАП„Х÷∞„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„™÷Љ÷∞„У÷Ј„Ы÷Љ÷µ„РвАО (ve Њal tedakke Њ, вАЬdo not crushвАЭ).
sn Robbing or oppressing the poor is easy because they are defenseless. But this makes the crime tempting as well as contemptible. What is envisioned may be in bounds legally (just) but out of bounds morally.
n2002262: tn Heb вАЬin the gateвАЭ (so KJV); NAB, NASB, NRSV вАЬat the gate.вАЭ The вАЬgateвАЭ of the city was the center of activity, the place of business as well as the place for settling legal disputes. The language of the next verse suggests a legal setting, so вАЬcourtвАЭ is an appropriate translation here.
n2002263: tn The construction uses the verb вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (yariv) with its cognate accusative. It can mean вАЬto strive,вАЭ but here it probably means вАЬto argue a case, plead a caseвАЭ (cf. KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV). How the Lord will do this is not specifiedвАФeither through righteous people or by direct intervention.
n2002264: tn The expression вАЬrob the lifeвАЭ occurs only here. Possibly it means to kill (similar to вАЬseek the life [of someone]вАЭ) or perhaps вАЬto take away vitalityвАЭ by some means.
n2002265: tn The verb вАП„І÷Є„С÷Ј„ҐвАО (qava њ, вАЬto rob; to spoil; to plunderвАЭ) is used here in both places to reflect the principle of talionic justice. What the oppressors did to the poor will be turned back on them by the Lord.
n2002266: tn Heb вАЬpossessor of anger.вАЭ This expression is an idiom for вАЬwrathful personвАЭ or вАЬan angry personвАЭ (cf. NAB вАЬa hotheaded manвАЭ; NLT вАЬshort-tempered peopleвАЭ). These are people characterized by anger, meaning the anger is not a rare occurrence with them.
n2002267: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„Я÷Њ„™÷Љ÷ґ„Р÷±„Ь÷Ј„£вАО (pen te Њelaf) is translated вАЬlest you learn.вАЭ The idea is more precisely вАЬbecome familiar with his ways.вАЭ The construction indicates that if one associates with such people he will become like them (cf. TEV вАЬyou might learn their habitsвАЭ).
n2002268: sn The warning in this proverb is to avoid associating with a hothead because his influence could be fatal (a similar idea is found in the Instruction of Amenemope, chap. 9, 11:13-14 [ANET 423]).
n2002269: tn The вАЬbedвАЭ may be a metonymy of adjunct, meaning the garment that covers the bed (e.g., Exod 22:26). At any rate, it represents the individualвАЩs last possession (like the English expression вАЬthe shirt off his backвАЭ).
n2002270: tn Heb вАЬIf you cannot pay, why should he take the bed from under you?вАЭ This rhetorical question is used to affirm the statement. The rhetorical interrogative вАП„Ь÷Є„Ю÷Є÷Љ„ФвАО (lammah, вАЬwhy?вАЭ) appears in MT but not in the ancient versions; it may be in the Hebrew text by dittography.
n2002271: sn The third saying deals with rash vows: If people foolishly pledge what they have, they could lose everything (e.g., 6:1-5; 11:15; 17:18; 20:16; there is no Egyptian parallel).
n2002272: sn Moving a boundary stone was (and still is) a major problem. The boundaries that were established by the forefathers were to be preserved, but no law would stop such violations if people lacked integrity (e.g., Deut 19:14; 27:17; 1 Kgs 21:16-19). Boundaries in Israel were sacred because God owned the land and he apportioned the property to the tribes. To extend oneвАЩs property illegally by moving a neighborвАЩs boundary marker was a violation of covenant and oath. Of course, disputes could arise when both sides claim their ancestors established a boundary.
n2002273:
tn Heb вАЬyour fathersвАЭ (so NAB, NASB).
sn The fourth saying deals with respect for property that belongs to other people (cf. Instruction of Amenemope, chap. 6, 7:12-13 [ANET 422]).
n2002274: tn Most translations render the verse as a question (вАЬdo you seeвА¶?вАЭ, so NIV, NASB, RSV, ESV) or as a condition (вАЬif youвА¶, so CEV), but the Hebrew has a perfect verb form (вАП„Ч÷Є„Ц÷і„Щ„™÷ЄвАО, khazita) without an interrogative or conditional marker. Hebrew proverbs can use the past tense to set the topic or opening premise of a proverb (to present a case, e.g. вАЬtake this situation where X occurredвАЭ), and then comment on it in the second half of the proverb. English translators of proverbial sayings tend to want to make the past time verbs in Hebrew into present tense in English. But this convention is difficult with second person verb forms, so the translations tend to take the tactic of changing the nature of the sentence to interrogative or conditional.
n2002275: sn The word translated вАЬskilledвАЭ is general enough to apply to any craft, but it may refer to a scribe or an official (R. N. Whybray, Proverbs [CBC], 134).
n2002276: tn The verb form used twice here is вАП„Щ÷і„™÷∞„Щ÷Ј„¶÷µ÷Љ„СвАО (yityatsev), the Hitpael imperfect of вАП„Щ÷Є„¶÷Ј„СвАО (yatsav), which means вАЬto set or station oneself; to take oneвАЩs standвАЭ in this stem. With the form вАП„Ь÷і„§÷∞„†÷µ„ЩвАО (lifne) it means вАЬto present oneself beforeвАЭ someone; so here it has the idea of serving as a courtier in the presence of a king.
n2002277: sn The fifth saying affirms that true skill earns recognition and advancement (cf. Instruction of Amenemope, chap. 30, 27:16-17 [ANET 424]).
n200231: tn The construction uses the imperfect tense of instruction with the infinitive absolute to emphasize the careful discernment required on such occasions. Cf. NIV вАЬnote wellвАЭ; NLT вАЬpay attention.вАЭ
n200232: tn Or вАЬwho,вАЭ referring to the ruler (so ASV, NAB, TEV).
n200233: sn The expression вАЬput a knife to your throatвАЭ is an idiom that means вАЬcurb your appetiteвАЭ or вАЬcontrol yourselfвАЭ (cf. TEV). The instruction was from a time when people dealt with all-powerful tyrants. To enter the presence of such a person and indulge oneвАЩs appetites would be to take a very high risk.
n200234: tn Heb вАЬan owner of appetite.вАЭ The idiom вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„Ь „†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (ba њal nefesh) refers to someone who possesses a large appetite (cf. NAB вАЬa ravenous appetiteвАЭ). A person with a big appetite is in danger of taking liberties when invited to court.
n200235: tn Heb вАЬhisвАЭ; the referent (the ruler mentioned in v. 1) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n200236: sn The final line gives the causal clause: The impressive feast is not what it appears to be; the king is not doing you a favor, but rather wants something from you or is observing you (K&D 17:104); cf. TEV вАЬhe may be trying to trick you.вАЭ
n200237: sn Verses 1-3 form the sixth saying about being cautious before rulers (cf. Instruction of Amememope, chap. 23, 23:13-18). One should not get too familiar with rulers, for they always have ulterior motives. The Mishnah cites Gamaliel as warning that a ruler only draws someone into his court for his purpose, but in their day of trouble he will not be there to help them (m. Avot 2:3).
n200238: tn Heb вАЬfrom your understanding cease.вАЭ In the context this means that the person should have enough understanding to stop wearing himself out trying to be rich (cf. NRSV вАЬbe wise enough to desistвАЭ).
n200239:
tc The Kethib is вАП„Ф÷≤„™÷Є„Ґ„Х÷Љ„£вАО (hata њuf), вАЬdo your eyes fly [light] on it?вАЭ The Qere is the Hiphil, вАП„Ф÷≤„™÷Є„Ґ÷і„Щ„£вАО (hata њif) вАЬdo you cause your eyes to fly on it?вАЭ But the line is difficult. The question may be indirect: If you cast your eyes on it, it is goneвАФwhen you think you are close, it slips away.
tn The term вАЬrichesвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation based on the previous verse.
n2002310: sn This seventh saying warns people not to expend all their energy trying to get rich because riches are fleeting (cf. Instruction of Amememope, chap. 7, 9:10-11 which says, вАЬthey have made themselves wings like geese and have flown away to heavenвАЭ). In the ancient world the symbol of birds flying away signified fleeting wealth.
n2002311: tn Heb вАЬan evil eye.вАЭ This is the opposite of the вАЬgood eyeвАЭ which meant the generous man. The вАЬevil eyeвАЭ refers to a person who is out to get everything for himself (cf. NASB, NCV, CEV вАЬselfishвАЭ). He is ill-mannered and inhospitable (e.g., Prov 28:22). He is up to no goodвАФeven though he may appear to be a host.
n2002312: tc The line is difficult; multiple options are possible. As vocalized, the Hebrew says вАЬFor, as he has calculated in his soul, so he is.вАЭ As it appears in the MT, the line appears to mean that the miser is the kind of person who has calculated the cost of everything in his mind as he offers the food. The LXX has: вАЬEating and drinking with him is as if one should swallow a hair; do not introduce him to your company nor eat bread with him.вАЭ A somewhat free rendering is common in the LXX of Proverbs, but we can infer a Hebrew text which says вАЬFor, like a hair in his throat, so he is.вАЭ The issue revolves around the letters вАП„©„Ґ„®вАО (shin/sin,  њayin, and resh). The MT reads вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (sha њar) вАЬto calculateвАЭ while the LXX has read вАП„©÷µ„В„Ґ÷Є„®вАО (se њar) вАЬhair.вАЭ The choice here affects which meaning of вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) вАЬsoul, throat, breath, life, desireвАЭ that translators apply. However verbs of thinking typically relate to the mind (вАП„Ь÷µ„С÷Є„СвАО/вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО; levav/lev, also translated вАЬheartвАЭ) and not to the вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО. The consonants could also be vocalized as вАП„©÷є„Б„Ґ÷Є„®вАО (sho њar) вАЬsomething rotten [in oneвАЩs throat]вАЭ or вАП„©÷Ј„Б„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (sha њar) вАЬa gate [in oneвАЩs throat].вАЭ The readings taking вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО to mean вАЬthroatвАЭ would picture an irritating experience. The Instruction of Amenemope uses вАЬblocking the throatвАЭ in a similar saying (chapt. 11, 14:7 [ANET 423]). Most translations follow the MT, while the NRSV accepts the reading вАЬhair.вАЭ
n2002313: tn The phrase вАЬthe costвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew but is implied by the verb; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2002315: sn Eating and drinking with a selfish miser would be irritating and disgusting. The line is hyperbolic; the whole experience turns the stomach.
n2002316:
tn Or вАЬyour complimentsвАЭ (so NASB, NIV); cf. TEV вАЬyour flattery.вАЭ
sn This is the eighth saying; it claims that it would be a mistake to accept hospitality from a stingy person. He is always thinking about the cost, his heart is not in it, and any attempt at pleasant conversation will be lost.
n2002317: sn The mention of вАЬthe earsвАЭ emphasizes the concerted effort to get the personвАЩs undivided attention. However, a fool rejects instruction and discipline.
n2002318: sn Saying number nine indicates that wisdom is wasted on a fool. The literature of Egypt has no specific parallel to this one.
n2002319: tn Or вАЬencroach onвАЭ (NIV, NRSV); Heb вАЬgo into.вАЭ
n2002320:
tn The participle вАП„Т÷Љ÷є„Р÷µ„ЬвАО (go Њel) describes a вАЬkinsman redeemer.вАЭ Some English versions explicitly cite вАЬGodвАЭ (e.g., NCV, CEV) or вАЬthe LordвАЭ (e.g. TEV).
sn The Hebrew term describes a вАЬkinsman-redeemer.вАЭ That individual would be a rich or powerful relative who can protect the family; he does this by paying off the debts of a poor relative, buying up the property of a relative who sells himself into slavery, marrying the widow of a deceased relative to keep the inheritance in the family, or taking vengeance on someone who harms a relative, that vengeance often resulting in delivering (вАЬredeemingвАЭ) the relative from bondage. If there was no human вАЬkinsman redeemer,вАЭ then the defenseless had to rely on God to perform these actions (e.g., Gen 48:16; Exod 6:6; Job 19:25; Isa 41-63). In the prophetic literature God is presented as the Redeemer in that he takes vengeance on the enemies (the Babylonians) to deliverer his people (kin). In this proverb the Lord is probably the Protector of these people who will champion their cause and set things right.
n2002321: sn This is the tenth saying; once again there is a warning not to encroach on other peopleвАЩs rights and property, especially the defenseless (see v. 10; 22:22-23, 28).
n2002322: tn Heb вАЬbring.вАЭ The Hiphil imperative вАЬcome; enterвАЭ means вАЬto apply the heart,вАЭ to use the heart or mind in the process. The same would be true in the second half: вАЬto bring the earsвАЭ would mean to listen very carefully. Cf. TEV вАЬPay attention.вАЭ
n2002323: tn Or вАЬpunishвАЭ (NIV). The syntax of these two lines suggests a conditional clause (cf. NCV, NRSV).
n2002324: tn Heb вАЬhis soul.вАЭ The term вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, вАЬsoulвАЭ) functions as a synecdoche of part (= soul) for the whole (= person); see BDB 660 s.v. 4.
n2002325: tn The term вАП„©„Б÷∞„Р„Х÷є„ЬвАО (she Њol, вАЬSheolвАЭ) in this context probably means вАЬdeathвАЭ (so NIV, NCV, NLT) and not the realm of the departed (wicked) spirits (cf. NAB вАЬthe nether worldвАЭ). In the wisdom of other lands, Ahiqar 6:82 says, вАЬIf I strike you, my son, you will not die.вАЭ The idea is that discipline helps the child to a full life; if the child dies prematurely, it would be more than likely a consequence of not being trained by discipline. In the book of Proverbs the вАЬdeathвАЭ mentioned here could be social as well as physical.
n2002326: tn Heb вАЬmy son,вАЭ although the context does not limit this exhortation to male children.
n2002327: tn Heb вАЬmy kidneysвАЭ; in biblical Hebrew the term was used for the innermost being, the soul, the central location of the passions. Cf. NASB, NIV вАЬmy inmost being.вАЭ
n2002328: sn This twelfth saying simply observes that children bring joy to their parents when they demonstrate wisdom. The quatrain is arranged in a chiastic structure (AB:B'A'): The first line (A) speaks of wisdom in the child, and it is paired with the last line (A') which speaks of the childвАЩs saying what is right. In between these brackets are two lines (B and B') concerning joy to the parent.
n2002329: tn The verb in this line is вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„Щ÷∞„І÷Ј„†÷Љ÷µ„РвАО ( Њal yeqanne Њ), the Piel jussive negated. The verb means вАЬto be jealous, to be zealousвАЭ; it describes passionate intensity for something. In English, if the object is illegitimate, it is called вАЬenvyвАЭ; if it is correct, it is called вАЬzeal.вАЭ Here the warning is not to envy the sinners. The second colon could use the verb in the positive sense to mean вАЬbut rather let your passion burn for the fear of the Lord.вАЭ
n2002330: tn Heb вАЬthe fear of the Lord.вАЭ This expression features an objective genitive: вАЬfearing the Lord.вАЭ
n2002332: sn The saying is an understatement; far from being cut off, the вАЬhopeвАЭ will be realized in the end. So this saying, the thirteenth, advises people to be zealous for the fear of the Lord, their religion, rather than for anything that sinners have to offer.
n2002333: tn Heb вАЬmy son,вАЭ but the immediate context does not limit this to male children.
n2002334: tn Heb вАЬdo not be among,вАЭ but in the sense of вАЬassociate withвАЭ (TEV); вАЬjoinвАЭ (NIV); вАЬconsortвА¶withвАЭ (NAB).
n2002335: tn The verb вАП„°÷Є„С÷Є„РвАО (sava Њ) means вАЬto imbibe; to drink largely.вАЭ The participial construction here, вАП„°÷є„С÷∞„Р÷µ„Щ÷Њ„Щ÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЯвАО (sove Њe yayin), describes вАЬdrunkardsвАЭ (cf. NLT) which is somewhat stronger than saying it refers to вАЬpeople who drink too muchвАЭ (cf. NIV, TEV).
n2002336: tn The verb вАП„Ц÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (zalal) means вАЬto be light; to be worthless; to make light of.вАЭ Making light of something came to mean вАЬto be lavish with; to squander,вАЭ especially with regard to food. So it describes вАЬgluttonsвАЭ primarily, but in the expression there is also room for the person who wastes a lot of food as well.
n2002337: tn Here вАЬdrowsinessвАЭ is a metonymy of effect or adjunct, put for the drunkenness and gluttony that causes it. So all of it, the drunkenness and the drowsiness that comes from it, brings on the ruin (cf. CEV вАЬyou will end up poorвАЭ). Likewise, вАЬragsвАЭ is a metonymy of adjunct, associated with the poverty brought on by a dissolute lifestyle.
n2002338: sn This is the fourteenth saying, warning about poor associations. Drunkenness and gluttony represent the epitome of the lack of discipline. In the Mishnah they are used to measure a stubborn and rebellious son (m. Sanhedrin 8). W. G. Plaut notes that excessive drinking and eating are usually symptoms of deeper problems; we usually focus more on the drinking because it is dangerous to others (Proverbs, 241-42).
n2002339:
tn Heb вАЬbuyвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NLT); CEV вАЬInvest in truth.вАЭ
sn The sixteenth saying is an instruction to buy/acquire the kind of life that pleases God and brings joy to parents. вАЬGetting truthвАЭ would mean getting training in the truth, and getting wisdom and understanding would mean developing the perception and practical knowledge of the truth.
n2002340: tc The Qere reading has the imperfect вАП„Щ÷Є„Т÷і„Щ„ЬвАО (yagil) with the cognate accusative вАП„Т÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЬвАО (gil) which intensifies the meaning and the specific future of this verb.
n2002341: tn The term вАЬchildвАЭ is supplied for the masculine singular adjective here.
n2002342: tn The form вАП„™÷Є„Т÷µ„ЬвАО (tagel) is clearly a short form and therefore a jussive (вАЬmay sheвА¶rejoiceвАЭ); if this second verb is a jussive, then the parallel вАП„Щ÷і„©„В÷∞„Ю÷Ј„ЧвАО (yismakh) should be a jussive also (вАЬmay your father and your mother have joyвАЭ).
n2002343: tn Heb вАЬmy sonвАЭ; the reference to a вАЬsonвАЭ is retained in the translation here because in the following lines the advice is to avoid women who are prostitutes.
n2002344: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied for the sake of clarity.
n2002345: tn Heb вАЬforeign womanвАЭ (so ASV). The term вАП„†÷Є„Ы÷∞„®÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (nokhriyyah, вАЬforeign womanвАЭ) often refers to a prostitute (e.g., Prov 2:6; 5:20; 6:24; 7:5). While not all foreign women in Israel were prostitutes, their prospects for economic survival were meager and many turned to prostitution to earn a living. Some English versions see this term referring to an adulteress as opposed to a prostitute (cf. NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2002346: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2002347: sn In either case, whether a prostitute or an adulteress wife is involved, the danger is the same. The metaphors of a вАЬdeep pitвАЭ and a вАЬnarrow wellвАЭ describe this sin as one that is a trap from which there is no escape. The вАЬpitвАЭ is a gateway to Sheol, and those who enter are as good as dead, whether socially or through punishment physically.
n2002348: tn The noun вАП„Ч÷ґ„™÷ґ„£вАО (khetef) is defined by BDB 369 s.v. as вАЬprey,вАЭ while HALOT 365 s.v. defines it as вАЬrobber.вАЭ This is the only occurrence of the word in the OT, but HALOT also cites two uses in Ben Sirah. The related verb (вАП„Ч÷Є„™÷Ј„£вАО, khataf) means вАЬto snatch awayвАЭ according to both dictionaries (BDB 368-69 s.v.; HALOT 365 s.v.), while the cognates assembled by HALOT have a different nuance: Akkadian вАЬto slaughter,вАЭ Syriac вАЬto break to pieces,вАЭ and an Arabic noun meaning вАЬdeath.вАЭ Like the noun, the verb only occurs once in the OT, Job 9:12. In this passage the noun could have either a passive sense (what is seized = prey), or an active sense (the one who seizes = a robber, bandit). The traditional rendering is вАЬpreyвАЭ (KJV); most modern English versions have the active sense (вАЬrobberвАЭ or similar; cf. NIV вАЬlike a banditвАЭ). Since the prepositional phrase (the simile) is modifying the woman, the active sense works better in the translation.
n2002349: tn The verb вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Т÷Ј„УвАО (bagad), here a participle, means вАЬto act treacherously, with duplicity, or to betray.вАЭ Such a woman induces men to prove unfaithful to their wives and to the law of God. Dahood repoints it as вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Т÷Є„У÷і„Щ„ЭвАО (begadim, вАЬgarmentsвАЭ), saying that she collects garments in pledge for her service (M. Dahood, вАЬTo Pawn OneвАЩs Cloak,вАЭ Bib 42 [1961]: 359-66). But that is far-fetched; it might have happened on occasion, but as a common custom it is unlikely. Besides that, the text in the MT makes perfectly good sense without such a change.
n2002350: sn Verses 26-28 comprise the seventeenth saying; it warns the young person to follow the instructions about temptations because there are plenty of temptresses lurking about.
n2002351: sn The eighteenth saying is about excessive drinking. The style changes here as the sage breaks into a vivid use of the imagination. It begins with a riddle describing the effects of drunkenness (v. 29) and gives the answer in v. 30; instructions follow in v. 31, with the consequences described in v. 32; the direct address continues in vv. 33 and 34; and the whole subject is concluded with the drunkardвАЩs own words in v. 35 (M. E. Andrews, вАЬVariety of Expression in Proverbs 23:29-35, вАЭ VT 28 [1978]: 102-3).
n2002352: sn The Hebrew word translated вАЬdullnessвАЭ describes darkness or dullness of the eyes due to intoxication, perhaps вАЬrednessвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NRSV); NIV, NCV, NLT вАЬbloodshot eyes.вАЭ NAB understands the situation differently: вАЬblack eyes.вАЭ
n2002353: sn The answer to the question posed in v. 29 is obviously one who drinks too much, which this verse uses metonymies to point out. Lingering over wine is an adjunct of drinking more wine; and seeking mixed wine obviously means with the effect or the purpose of drinking it.
n2002354: tn Heb вАЬits eye gives.вАЭ With CEVвАЩs вАЬbubbling up in the glassвАЭ one might think champagne was in view.
n2002355: tn The expression is difficult. The imagery has some similarity to Song 7:9, although the parallel is not exact. The verb is the Hitpael imperfect of вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (halakh); and the prepositional phrase uses the word вАЬupright; equity; pleasing,вАЭ from вАП„Щ÷Є„©„Б÷Є„®вАО (yashar). KJV has вАЬwhen it moveth itself arightвАЭ; much more helpful is ASV вАЬwhen it goeth down smoothly.вАЭ Most recent English versions are similar to ASV. The phrase obviously refers to the pleasing nature of wine.
n2002356: tn Heb вАЬits endвАЭ; NASB вАЬAt the lastвАЭ; TEV (interpretively) вАЬThe next morning.вАЭ
n2002357: tn The feminine plural of вАП„Ц÷Є„®вАО (zar, вАЬstrange thingsвАЭ) refers to the trouble one has in seeing and speaking when drunk.
n2002358: tn Heb вАЬheart.вАЭ The idiom here means вАЬmiddleвАЭ; KJV вАЬin the midst.вАЭ
n2002359: sn The point of these similes is to compare being drunk with being seasick. One who tries to sleep when at sea, or even worse, when up on the ropes of the mast, will be tossed back and forth.
n2002360: tn The phrase вАЬYou will sayвАЭ is supplied in the translation to make it clear that the drunkard is now speaking.
n2002361: sn The line describes how one who is intoxicated does not feel the pain, even though beaten by others. He does not even remember it.
n2002362: tn The last line has only вАЬI will add I will seek it again.вАЭ The use of вАП„Р„Х÷є„°÷і„Щ„£вАО ( Њosif) signals a verbal hendiadys with the next verb: вАЬI will again seek it.вАЭ In this context the suffix on the verb refers to the wineвАФthe drunkard wants to go and get another drink.
n200241: tn Heb вАЬevil men,вАЭ although the context indicates a generic sense.
n200242: tn The Hitpael jussive is from the verb that means вАЬto crave; to desire.вАЭ This is more of a coveting, an intense desire.
n200243: sn This nineteenth saying warns against evil associations. Evil people are obsessed with destruction and trouble. See on this theme 1:10-19; 3:31 and 23:17. D. Kidner observes that a close view of sinners is often a good antidote to envying them (Proverbs [TOTC], 153).
n200244: tn The preposition вАП„СвАО (bet, вАЬby; throughвАЭ) in these two lines indicates means.
n200245: sn The twentieth saying, vv. 3-4, concerns the use of wisdom for domestic enterprises. In Prov 9:1 wisdom was personified as a woman who builds a house, but here the emphasis is primarily on the buildingвАФit is a sign of security and prosperity (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 442). One could still make a secondary application from this line for a household or вАЬfamilyвАЭ (cf. NCV, which sees this as a reference to the family).
n200246: sn The twenty-first saying seems to be concerned with the need for wisdom in warfare. In line with that, the word used here is вАП„Т÷Љ÷ґ„С÷ґ„®вАО (gever), вАЬmighty man; hero; warrior.вАЭ
n200247: tn The expression вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ„Х÷є„ЦвАО (ba њoz) employs a beth essentiae, meaning he вАЬis strong,вАЭ not вАЬin strength.вАЭ
n200248: sn The point of the saying is that wise counsel is necessary in war. Victory, strategy, and counsel are more important than mere military strengthвАФmany great armies have been destroyed because of their unwise leaders. See on this theme 11:14; 20:18; 21:22.
n200249: tc The MT reads вАП„®÷Є„Р„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО (ra Њmot, вАЬcoralsвАЭ)вАФwisdom to the fool is corals, i.e., an unattainable treasure. With a slight change in the text, removing the вАП„РвАО ( Њalef), the reading is вАП„®÷Є„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО (ramot, вАЬhighвАЭ), i.e., wisdom is too highвАФunattainableвАФfor a fool. The internal evidence favors the emendation, which is followed by most English versions including KJV.
n2002410: tn Heb вАЬ[city] gate,вАЭ a metonymy of subject, meaning what goes on in the gateвАФcourt cases and business transactions. So it is in these assemblies that the fool keeps quiet. The term вАЬcourtвАЭ has been used in the translation for clarity. Some English versions do not emphasize the forensic connotation here: NCV вАЬin a discussionвАЭ; NLT вАЬWhen the leaders gather.вАЭ
n2002411: sn The verse portrays a fool out of his element: In a serious moment in the gathering of the community, he does not even open his mouth (a metonymy of cause, meaning вАЬspeakвАЭ). Wisdom is too high for the foolвАФit is beyond his ability.
n2002412: tn Heb вАЬpossessor of schemesвАЭ; NAB вАЬan intriguer.вАЭ The picture of the wicked person is graphic: He devises plans to do evil and is known as a schemer. Elsewhere the вАЬschemesвАЭ are outrageous and lewd (e.g., Lev 18:7; Judg 20:6). Here the description portrays him as a cold, calculating, active person: вАЬthe fool is capable of intense mental activity but it adds up to sinвАЭ (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 399).
n2002413: tn Heb вАЬthe scheme of follyвАЭ (NIV similar). The genitive functions as an attributive genitive, meaning вАЬfoolish scheme.вАЭ But it could also be interpreted as a genitive of source, the scheme that comes from folly (or from the fool if вАЬfollyвАЭ were metonymical).
n2002414:
tn Heb вАЬto a manвАЭ; cf. CEV вАЬEveryone hates senseless fools.вАЭ
sn This describes evil people who flout all morality and goodness; sooner or later the public will have had enough of them.
n2002415: tn The verb вАП„Ф÷і„™÷∞„®÷Ј„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„™÷ЄвАО (hitrappita) is a Hitpael perfect form of вАП„®÷Є„§÷Є„ФвАО (rafah). Most translations render the verse as a conditional statement (вАЬif youвА¶вАЭ) though the Hebrew lacks the term вАЬif.вАЭ Hebrew proverbs can use the past tense to set the topic or opening premise of a proverb, and then comment on it in the second half of the proverb. English translators of proverbial sayings tend to want to make the Hebrew past time verbs into present tense in English. But this convention is difficult with second person verb forms, so the translations tend to change the sentence into an interrogative or conditional formula. The direct address in the Hebrew is more confrontational. In the Qal, the verb вАП„®÷Є„§÷Є„ФвАО (rafah) means вАЬto become slack, limp, to wither.вАЭ In the Hitpael it means вАЬto slack off,вАЭ вАЬbe lax,вАЭ possibly вАЬto discourage oneself.вАЭ It has also been rendered as вАЬgive upвАЭ (NCV, CEV); вАЬfailвАЭ (NLT); вАЬfalterвАЭ (NIV), вАЬfaintвАЭ (ASV, ESV).
n2002416: tn The verse employs a paronomasia to underscore the point: вАЬtroubleвАЭ is вАП„¶÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (tsarah), literally вАЬa bind; a strait [or, narrow] placeвАЭ; вАЬsmallвАЭ is вАП„¶÷Ј„®вАО (tsar), with the same idea of вАЬnarrowвАЭ or вАЬclose.вАЭ
n2002417: sn The test of strength is adversity, for it reveals how strong a person is. Of course a weak person can always plead adverse conditions in order to quit. This is the twenty-fourth saying.
n2002418:
tn The idea of вАЬslippingвАЭ (participle from вАП„Ю„Х÷є„ШвАО, mot) has troubled some commentators. G. R. Driver emends it to read вАЬat the point ofвАЭ (вАЬProblems in Proverbs,вАЭ ZAW 50 [1932]: 146). But the MT as it stands makes good sense. The reference would be general, viz., to help any who are in mortal danger or who might be tottering on the edge of such disasterвАФwhether through sin, or through disease, war, or danger. Several English versions (e.g., NASB, NIV, NRSV) render this term as вАЬstaggering.вАЭ
sn God holds people responsible for rescuing those who are in mortal danger. The use of вАЬdeathвАЭ and вАЬslaughterвАЭ seems rather strong in the passage, but they have been used before in the book for the destruction that comes through evil.
n2002419: tn Heb вАЬWill he not?вАЭ The verb is an imperfect stative and so should be understood as future or modal. Likewise the verb in the next line.
n2002420: tn Heb вАЬweighsвАЭ (so NASB, NIV, NRSV) meaning вАЬtestsвАЭ or вАЬevaluates.вАЭ
n2002421: tn The imperfect of the stative verb вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ, вАЬto knowвАЭ) means вАЬwill know/come to know,вАЭ thus вАЬwill learn, find out, realize.вАЭ
n2002422: sn The verse completes the saying by affirming that people will be judged responsible for helping those in mortal danger. The verse uses a series of rhetorical questions to affirm that God knows our hearts and we cannot plead ignorance.
n2002423: sn The twenty-sixth saying teaches that one should develop wisdom because it has a profitable future. The saying draws on the image of honey; its health-giving properties make a good analogy to wisdom.
n2002424: tn D. W. Thomas argues for a meaning of вАЬseekвАЭ in place of вАЬknowвАЭ (вАЬNotes on Some Passages in the Book of Proverbs,вАЭ JTS 38 [1937]: 400-403).
n2002425: tn The phrase вАЬis sweetвАЭ is supplied in the translation as a clarification.
n2002426: tn The term вАЬitвАЭ is supplied in the translation.
n2002427: tn Heb вАЬthere will be an вАП„Р÷Ј„Ч÷≤„®÷і„Щ„™вАО ( Њakharit), which means вАЬend, result, following period.вАЭ It suggests a future, which may imply posterity. It is sometimes connected with hope (Jer 29:11: 31:17; Prov 23:18).
n2002428: tn The word вАЬwickedвАЭ could be taken as a vocative (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB, вАЬO wicked manвАЭ), but since the next line refers to the wicked this is unlikely. It serves better as an adverbial accusative (вАЬlike the wickedвАЭ).
n2002429: sn The saying warns that it is futile and self-defeating to mistreat GodвАЩs people, for they surviveвАФthe wicked do not. The warning is against a deliberate, planned assault on their places of dwelling.
n2002430:
tn The clause beginning with вАП„Ы÷і÷Љ„ЩвАО (ki) could be interpreted as temporal, conditional, or emphatic. It may be viewed as concessive (вАЬalthoughвАЭ) but a concessive force would typically arise from its context and relationship to other independent clauses. In any case, the first half of the proverb assures that the righteous keep getting up and going again.
sn The righteous may suffer adversity or misfortune any number of timesвАФseven times hereвАФbut they will вАЬriseвАЭ for virtue triumphs over evil in the end (R. N. Whybray, Proverbs [CBC], 140).
n2002431: tn The verb is a Hebrew imperfect of вАП„†÷Є„§÷Ј„ЬвАО (nafal) which should be understood as future вАЬwill fallвАЭ or modal вАЬmay fall.вАЭ If it is future, it is exemplary and not predictive of the number of times a righteous person will metaphorically fall down. It is followed by a vav plus perfect consecutive, which either continues the force of the preceding verb, or advances it one logical step, like the apodosis of a condition.
n2002432: tn The Niphal of вАП„Ы÷Є÷Љ„©÷Ј„Б„ЬвАО (kashal; to stumble) is typically reflexive вАЬto collapse.вАЭ Intransitive verbs do not tend to have passive meanings, but the Niphal may refer to the resulting state, вАЬbe collapsed, fallen, brought down,вАЭ (although some take the Niphal unusually as вАЬcaused to staggerвАЭ). The imperfect verb form could be taken as a general present, but the future presents a better parallel to the first half of the proverb.
n2002433: sn The saying (vv. 17, 18) warns against gloating over the misfortune of oneвАЩs enemies. The prohibition is formed with two negated jussives вАЬdo not rejoiceвАЭ and вАЬlet not be glad,вАЭ the second qualified by вАЬyour heartвАЭ as the subject, signifying the inner satisfaction of such a defeat.
n2002434: tn Heb вАЬand [it is] evil in his eyes.вАЭ
n2002435: sn The judgment of God should strike a note of fear in the heart of people (e.g., Lev 19:17-18). His judgment is not to be taken lightly, or personalized as a victory. If that were to happen, then the Lord might take pity on the enemies in their calamity, for he champions the downtrodden and defeated. These are probably personal enemies; the imprecatory psalms and the prophetic oracles present a different set of circumstances for the downfall of GodвАЩs enemiesвАФeven the book of Proverbs says that brings joy to the community.
n2002436: tn Heb вАЬthere is no end [i.e., future] for the evil.вАЭ
n2002437: sn The saying warns against envying the wicked; v. 19 provides the instruction, and v. 20 the motivation. The motivation is that there is no future hope for themвАФnothing to envy, or as C. H. Toy explains, there will be no good outcome for their lives (Proverbs [ICC], 449). They will die suddenly, as the implied comparison with the lamp being snuffed out signifies.
n2002438: tn Heb вАЬmy son,вАЭ but there is no indication in the immediate context that this should be limited only to male children.
n2002439: tn Heb вАЬdo not get mixed up withвАЭ; cf. TEV вАЬHave nothing to do withвАЭ; NIV вАЬdo not join with.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„СвАО ( њarav) is used elsewhere meaning вАЬto exchange; to take on pledge.вАЭ In the Hitpael stem it means вАЬto have fellowship; to share; to associate with.вАЭ Some English versions (e.g., KJV) interpret as вАЬto meddleвАЭ in this context, because вАЬto have fellowshipвАЭ is certainly not what is meant.
n2002440: tn The form rendered вАЬrebelsвАЭ is difficult; it appears to be the Qal active participle, plural, from вАП„©„Б÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО (shanah), вАЬto changeвАЭвАФвАЬthose who change.вАЭ The RV might have thought of the idea of вАЬchangeвАЭ when they rendered it вАЬpolitical agitators.вАЭ The Syriac and Tg. Prov 24:21 have вАЬfools,вАЭ the Latin has вАЬdetractors,вАЭ and the LXX reads, вАЬdo not disobey either of them,вАЭ referring to God and the king in the first line. Accordingly the ruin predicted in the next line would be the ruin that God and the king can inflict. If the idea of вАЬchangersвАЭ is retained, it would have to mean people who at one time feared God and the king but no longer do.
n2002442:
tn Heb вАЬthe ruin of the two of them.вАЭ Judgment is sent on the rebels both by God and the king. The term вАП„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„УвАО (pid, вАЬruin; disasterвАЭ) is a metonymy of effect, the cause being the sentence of judgment (= вАЬruinous judgmentвАЭ in the translation; cf. NLT вАЬpunishmentвАЭ). The word вАЬtwo of themвАЭ is a subjective genitiveвАФthey two bring the disaster on the rebels. The referents (the Lord and the king) have been specified in the translation for clarity.
sn The reward for living in peace under God in this world is that those who do will escape the calamities that will fall on the rebellious. Verse 21a is used in 1 Peter 2:17, and v. 22 is used in Romans 13:1-7 (v. 4). This is the thirtieth and last of this collection.
n2002443: tn Heb вАЬto recognize facesвАЭ; KJV, ASV вАЬto have respect of personsвАЭ; NLT вАЬto show favoritism.вАЭ
n2002444: tn Heb вАЬnot good.вАЭ This is a figure known as tapeinosisвАФa deliberate understatement to emphasize a worst-case scenario: вАЬit is terrible!вАЭ
n2002445: tn The word means вАЬwicked; guiltyвАЭ or вАЬcriminalвАЭ; the contrast could be вАЬwickedвАФrighteousвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV, NAB, NASB) or вАЬinnocentвАФguiltyвАЭ (cf. NIV, TEV, CEV). Since this line follows the statement about showing partiality in judgment, it involves a forensic setting. Thus the statement describes one who calls a guilty person innocent or acquitted.
n2002446: tn Or вАЬrighteousвАЭ; the same Hebrew word may be translated either вАЬinnocentвАЭ or вАЬrighteousвАЭ depending on the context.
n2002447: tn The verb means вАЬto be indignant.вАЭ It can be used within the range of вАЬhave indignation,вАЭ meaning вАЬloatheвАЭ or вАЬabhor,вАЭ or express indignation, meaning вАЬdenounceвАЭ or вАЬcurse.вАЭ In this passage, in collocation with the previous term вАЬcurse,вАЭ the latter is intended (cf. NAB, NIV, NLT).
n2002448: tn The verb means вАЬto be pleasant; to be delightful.вАЭ The imperfect tense promises that there вАЬwill be delightвАЭ to those who rebuke the wicked.
n2002449: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЧвАО (yakhakh) means вАЬto decide; to adjudge; to prove.вАЭ This word occurs frequently in the book of Proverbs meaning вАЬto reproveвАЭ or вАЬto rebuke.вАЭ It deals with disputes, legal or otherwise. It can refer to a charge against someone or starting a dispute (and so rebuke); it can mean quarrel, argue; and it can mean settle a dispute. In this context the first or last use would work: (1) reproving the wicked for what they do (cf. KJV, NASB, NRSV), or (2) convicting them in a legal setting (cf. NAB, NIV, NLT). In light of the previous forensic context the second sense is preferred here.
n2002450: tn вАЬThe guiltyвАЭ is supplied in the translation for clarity based on the preceding context. See the previous note on the word вАЬconvictвАЭ: If a non-forensic context is preferred for vv. 23-25, вАЬwickedвАЭ would be supplied here.
n2002451: tn The expression is вАП„С÷і„®÷∞„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„™÷Њ„Ш„Х÷є„СвАО (birkat tov, вАЬblessing of goodвАЭ); the genitive вАЬgoodвАЭ has to be an attributive genitive modifying вАЬblessings.вАЭ The word is general enough to mean any number of thingsвАФrich, healthy, pleasing, etc. The parallelism here narrows the choice. Some English versions interpret the вАЬblessingвАЭ here as prosperity (cf. NAB, TEV, CEV).
n2002452: tn Heb вАЬthe one who returns right words kisses the lips.вАЭ This is an implied comparison for giving an honest answer. Honesty is like a kiss. The kiss would signify love, devotion, sincerity, and commitment (in that culture)вАФan outward expression of what is in the heart. It is an apt illustration of telling the truth. Some English versions now replace the figure to avoid cultural misunderstanding (cf. TEV, CEV вАЬa sign of true friendshipвАЭ; NLT вАЬan honorвАЭ).
n2002453: tn The perfect tense with vav following the imperatives takes on the force of an imperative here.
n2002454: sn If the term вАЬhouseвАЭ is understood literally, the proverb would mean that one should be financially secure before building a house (cf. NLT). If вАЬhouseвАЭ is figurative for household (metonymy of subject: children or family), the proverb would mean that one should have financial security and provision before starting a family. Some English versions suggest the latter meaning by using the word вАЬhomeвАЭ for вАЬhouseвАЭ (e.g., TEV, CEV).
n2002455: sn The legal setting of these sayings continues with this warning against being a false accuser. The вАЬwitnessвАЭ in this line is one who has no basis for his testimony. вАЬWithout causeвАЭ is the adverb from вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Є„ЯвАО (khanan), which means вАЬto be gracious.вАЭ The adverb means вАЬwithout a cause; gratis; free.вАЭ It is also cognate to the word вАП„Ч÷µ„ЯвАО (khen), вАЬgraceвАЭ or вАЬunmerited [or, undeserved] favor.вАЭ The connotation is that the opposite is due. So the adverb would mean that there was no cause, no justification for the witness, but that the evidence seemed to lie on the other side.
n2002456: tn Heb вАЬlips.вАЭ The term вАЬlipsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause; it means вАЬwhat is said.вАЭ Here it refers to what is said in court as a false witness.
n2002457: tn Heb вАЬrepay to the man.вАЭ The verb is вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv), which in the Hiphil stem means вАЬto restore; to repay; to returnвАЭ (cf. TEV, CEV, NLT вАЬIвАЩll get evenвАЭ). The idea is that of repaying someone for what he did.
n2002458: sn Rather than give in to the spirit of vengeance, one should avoid retaliation (e.g., Prov 20:22; Matt 5:43-45; Rom 12:9). According to the Talmud, Hillel said, вАЬDo not do to others what you would not have them do unto youвАЭ (b. Sanhedrin 31a).
n2002459: tn Heb вАЬlacking of heart.вАЭ The term вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev, вАЬmind, heartвАЭ) refers by metonymy to thinking, and by extension to discernment, wisdom, good sense.
n2002460: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Х÷∞„Ф÷і„†÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО (vehinneh, traditionally вАЬand, loвАЭ [KJV, ASV]) is a deictic particle that calls for attention. Frequently it shifts the point of view to that of the speaker. Translating with вАЬI sawвАЭ moves us into the sageвАЩs point of view and calls attention to the field that was noticed.
n2002462: tn The verb вАП„Х÷Є„Р÷ґ„Ч÷±„Ц÷ґ„ФвАО (va Њekhezeh) is a preterite of вАП„Р÷Є„Ч÷Ј„ЦвАО ( Њakhaz) вАЬto look at.вАЭ In context the sage looked with analytic thinking, hence вАЬscrutinized.вАЭ As a preterite verb, it gives a successive action in past time. This preterite links back to the perfect verb вАЬI passed byвАЭ in v. 30 (whereas the three perfect verbs in v. 31 were each controlled by the opening вАП„Ф÷і„†÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО [hinneh] as simultaneous conditions with the perfective nuance). The following verbs in this verse are simultaneous to this preterite and do not advance the timeline.
n2002463: tn The verb вАП„Р÷Є„©÷і„Б„Щ„™вАО ( Њashit) is an imperfect form of a dynamic root in a past time setting. The previous verb, a preterite, is part of establishing the past time setting. Because this is a long prefixed form (spelled with the mater lectionis yod), it is not written as a preterite and should be understood as imperfective. Typically an imperfect in a narrative background clause is habitual (which could not work in this context) or past progressive. It may however be an abilitive modal expression вАЬI was able to set my mind to it.вАЭ In either case this verb does not advance the timeline but expresses something happening while the sage scrutinized the field.
n2002464: tn The word вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev) is often translated вАЬheartвАЭ but the word also means the вАЬmind.вАЭ The Hebrew idiom вАЬto set/put the вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev)вАЭ transfers well to English when rendering with вАЬmindвАЭ and indicates careful consideration to what was observed.
n2002465: tn The verb вАП„®÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„™÷і„ЩвАО (ra Њiti) is a perfect verb meaning вАЬto seeвАЭ and by extension вАЬto understand.вАЭ It could refer to the looking that the sage was doing, or to realizing the lesson. Together with the previous imperfect and following perfect verb, it is part of the past tense time frame established by the preterite verb beginning the verse. If вАП„®÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„™÷і„ЩвАО refers to the looking, then within the preteriteвАЩs time frame this verb represents an onset while the next verb represents a conclusion to the act of pondering. If this verb refers to realizing, then together with the next verb it represents the conclusion of the act of pondering.
n2002466: sn The teacher makes several observations of the state of the sluggard that reveal that his continued laziness will result in poverty. The reminiscence used here may be a literary device to draw a fictional but characteristically true picture of the lazy person.
n2002467: tn Heb вАЬa man of shield.вАЭ This could refer to an armed warrior (so NRSV) but in this context, in collocation with the other word for вАЬrobberвАЭ in the previous line, it must refer to an armed criminal.
n200251: sn This section of the book of Proverbs contains proverbs attributed to Solomon but copied by HezekiahвАЩs sages (between 715 b.c. and 687 b.c.). Some scholars conclude that this has no historical value other than to report the later disposition that people thought they came from SolomonвАЩs time, but if that were the only consideration, then that in itself would have to be considered as a piece of historical information. But if the reference is an earlier note in the collection, then it becomes more valuable for consideration. The proverbs in these lines differ from the earlier ones in that these are multiple line sayings using more similes; chapters 28-29 are similar to 10-16, but chapters 25-27 differ in having few references to God.
n200252: sn The proverb provides a contrast between God and the king, and therein is the clue to the range of application involved. The interest of the king is ruling or administering his government; and so the subject matter is a contrast to the way God rules his kingdom.
n200253: sn The two infinitives form the heart of the contrastвАФвАЬto conceal a matterвАЭ and вАЬto search out a matter.вАЭ GodвАЩs government of the universe is beyond human understandingвАФhumans cannot begin to fathom the intentions and operations of it. But it is the glory of kings to search out matters and make them intelligible to the people. Human government cannot claim divine secrecy; kings have to study and investigate everything before making a decision, even divine government as far as possible. But kings who rule as GodвАЩs representatives must also try to represent his will in human affairsвАФthey must even inquire after God to find his will. This is their glorious nature and responsibility. For more general information on vv. 2-27, see G. E. Bryce, вАЬAnother Wisdom вАШBookвАЩ in Proverbs,вАЭ JBL 91 (1972): 145-57.
n200254: tn Heb вАЬheavens for height and earth for depth.вАЭ The proverb is clearly intending the first line to be an illustration of the secondвАФit is almost emblematic parallelism.
n200255: sn The proverb is affirming a simple fact: The kingвАЩs plans and decisions are beyond the comprehension of the common people. While the king would make many things clear to the people, there are other things that are вАЬabove their headsвАЭ or вАЬtoo deep for them.вАЭ They are unsearchable because of his superior wisdom, his caprice, or his need for secrecy. Inscrutability is sometimes necessary to keep a firm grip on power.
n200256: tn The Hebrew вАП„Ы÷Љ÷ґ„Ь÷і„ЩвАО (keli) means вАЬvessel; utensilвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB). But purging dross from silver does not produce a вАЬvesselвАЭ for the silversmith. Some versions therefore render it вАЬmaterialвАЭ (e.g., NIV, NRSV). The LXX says вАЬthat it will be entirely pure.вАЭ So D. W. Thomas reads вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷і„Щ„ЬвАО (kalil) and translates it вАЬpurified completelyвАЭ (вАЬNotes on Some Passages in the Book of Proverbs,вАЭ VT 15 [1965]: 271-79; cf. NAB). W. McKane simply rearranges the line to say that the smith can produce a work of art (Proverbs [OTL], 580; cf. TEV вАЬa thing of beautyвАЭ). The easiest explanation is that вАЬvesselвАЭ is a metonymy of effect, вАЬvesselвАЭ put for the material that goes into making it (such metonymies occur fairly often in Psalms and Proverbs).
n200257: sn These two verses present first an illustration and then the point (so it is emblematic parallelism). The passage uses imperatives to teach that the wicked must be purged from the kingdom.
n200258: sn вАЬThroneвАЭ is a metonymy of subject (or adjunct); it is the symbol of the government over which the king presides (cf. NCV, TEV).
n200259: sn When the king purges the wicked from his court he will be left with righteous counselors and his government therefore will be вАЬestablished in righteousnessвАЭвАФit will endure through righteousness (cf. NLT вАЬmade secure by justiceвАЭ). But as J. H. Greenstone says, вАЬThe king may have perfect ideals and his conduct may be irreproachable, but he may be misled by unscrupulous courtiersвАЭ (Proverbs, 264).
n2002510: tn The phrase вАЬfor himвАЭ is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2002511: sn This proverb, covering the two verses, is teaching that it is wiser to be promoted than to risk demotion by self-promotion. The point is clear: Trying to promote oneself could bring on public humiliation, but it would be an honor to have everyone in court hear the promotion by the king.
n2002512: tn The two infinitives construct form the contrast in this вАЬbetterвАЭ sayings; each serves as the subject of its respective clause.
n2002513: tc Most modern commentators either omit this last line or attach it to the next verse. But it is in the text of the MT as well as the LXX, Syriac, Vulgate, and most modern English versions (although some of them do connect it to the following verse, e.g., NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT).
n2002514:
tn Heb вАЬdo not go out hastily to striveвАЭ; the verb вАЬto striveвАЭ means dispute in the legal context. The last clause of v. 7, вАЬwhat your eyes have seen,вАЭ does fit very well with the initial clause of v. 8. It would then say: What you see, do not take hastily to court, but if the case was not valid, he would end up in disgrace.
sn The Hebrew verb вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv) is often used in legal contexts; here the warning is not to go to court hastily lest it turn out badly.
n2002515: tn The clause begins with вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ЯвАО (pen, вАЬlestвАЭ) which seems a bit out of place in this line. C. H. Toy suggests changing it to вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki, вАЬforвАЭ) to make a better connection, instead of supplying an ellipsis: вАЬlest it be said whatвА¶вАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 461).
n2002516: tn The verse begins with the direct object вАП„®÷і„Щ„С÷∞„Ъ÷ЄвАО (rivekha, вАЬyour caseвАЭ) followed by the imperative from the same root, вАП„®÷і„Щ„СвАО (riv, вАЬargueвАЭ). It is paralleled by the negated Piel jussive. The construction of the clauses indicates that the first colon is foundational to the second: вАЬArgueвА¶but do not reveal,вАЭ or better, вАЬWhen you argueвА¶do not reveal.вАЭ
n2002517: sn The concern is that in arguing with one person a secret about another might be divulged, perhaps deliberately in an attempt to clear oneself. The point then is about damaging a friendship by involving the friend without necessity or warrant in someone elseвАЩs quarrel.
n2002518: tn The noun вАП„У÷Љ÷і„С÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (dibbah, вАЬinfamy; defamation; evil report; whisperingвАЭ) is used of an evil report here (e.g., Gen 37:2), namely a true report of evil doing. So if a person betrays another personвАЩs confidence, he will never be able to live down the bad reputation he made as one who betrays secrets (cf. NIV).
n2002519: sn The verse uses emblematic parallelism, stating the simile in the first part and the point in the second. The meaning of the simile is not entirely clear, but it does speak of beauty, value, and artistry. The вАЬapples of goldвАЭ (possibly citrons, quinces, oranges, or apricots) may refer to carvings of fruit in gold on columns.
n2002520: tn Heb вАЬon its wheels.вАЭ This expression means вАЬaptly, fittingly.вАЭ The point is obviously about the immense value and memorable beauty of words used skillfully (R. N. Whybray, Proverbs [CBC], 148). Noting the meaning of the term and the dual form of the word, W. McKane suggests that the expression is metaphorical for the balancing halves of a Hebrew parallel wisdom saying: вАЬThe stichos is a wheel, and the sentence consisting of two wheels is a вАШwell-turnedвАЩ expressionвАЭ (Proverbs [OTL], 584). The line then would be describing a balanced, well-turned saying, a proverb; it is skillfully constructed, beautifully written, and of lasting value.
n2002521: sn This saying is another example of emblematic parallelism; the first half is the simile, and the second half makes the point from it: A wise rebuke that is properly received is of lasting value. The rebuke in the ear of an obedient student is like ornaments of fine jewelry.
n2002522: tn The вАЬear of the listenerвАЭ refers to the obedient disciple, the one who complies with the reproof he hears. Cf. KJV, ASV, NAB вАЬan obedient ear.вАЭ
n2002523: sn The emblem in the parallelism of this verse is the simile of the first line. Because snow at the time of harvest would be rare, and probably unwelcome, various commentators have sought to explain this expression. R. N. Whybray suggests it may refer to snow brought down from the mountains and kept cool in an ice hole (Proverbs [CBC], 148); this seems rather forced. J. H. Greenstone following Rashi, a Jewish scholar who lived a.d. 1040-1105, suggests it might refer to the refreshing breeze that comes from snow-capped mountains (Proverbs, 260). C. H. Toy suggests a snow-cooled drink (Proverbs [ICC], 464), and W. McKane an application of ice water to the forehead (Proverbs [OTL], 585). Some English versions replace вАЬsnowвАЭ with вАЬwaterвАЭ (cf. TEV вАЬcold waterвАЭ; CEV вАЬcool waterвАЭ). These all attempt to explain the simile, but the point is clear enough: A faithful servant is refreshing to his master. The analogy could be hypotheticalвАФas refreshing as the coolness of snow would be in harvest time.
n2002524: tn Heb вАЬhe restores the life [or, soul] of his masters.вАЭ The idea suggests that someone who sends the messenger either entrusts his life to him or relies on the messenger to resolve some concern. A faithful messenger restores his masterвАЩs spirit and so is вАЬrefreshing.вАЭ
n2002525: sn The emblem now is one of clouds and winds that would be expected to produce rain; they gain attention and raise peopleвАЩs expectations but prove to be disappointing when no rain is forthcoming, and hence could be thought of as deceitful.
n2002526: tn The form вАП„Ю÷і„™÷∞„Ф÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷µ„ЬвАО (mithallel) is the Hitpael participle of the well-known word for вАЬpraise,вАЭ but in this stem it means вАЬto praise oneselfвАЭ or вАЬto boast.вАЭ The description of вАЬwindbagвАЭ seems appropriate in this context.
n2002527: tn Heb вАЬa gift of falsehood.вАЭ This would mean that the individual brags about giving a gift, when there is no gift.
n2002528: tn Heb вАЬlong of angerвАЭ or вАЬforbearanceвАЭ (so NASB).
n2002529: tn The two imperfect verbs in this line may be nuanced as potential imperfects because what is described could happen, but does not do so as a rule.
n2002530: tn The вАЬtongueвАЭ is a metonymy of cause; and so the expression here refers to soft or gentle speech. This fits well with the parallel idea of patience (вАЬlong of angerвАЭ)вАФthrough a calm patient persuasion much can be accomplished. Some English versions relate this figure directly to the persuasion of a ruler in the previous line (cf. TEV вАЬcan even convince rulersвАЭ).
n2002531: sn The idea of breaking a bone uses the hardest and most firm part of the body in contrast to the вАЬsoftness of the tongue.вАЭ Both are figurative, forming a comparison. A gentle speech can break down any stiff opposition.
n2002532: tn Most translations render the verse as a question (вАЬHave you found honey?вАЭ so KJV, NASB, ASV) or as a condition (вАЬif/when you find honey,вАЭ so NIV, ESV, Holman). But the Hebrew has a perfect verb form (вАП„Ю÷Є„¶÷Є„Р„™÷ЄвАО, matsa Њta) without an interrogative or conditional marker. Hebrew proverbs can use the past tense to set the topic or opening premise of a proverb (to present a case, e.g. вАЬtake this situation where X occurredвАЭ), and then comment on it in the second half of the proverb. English translators of proverbial sayings tend to want to make the past time verbs in Hebrew into present tense in English. But this convention is difficult with second person verb forms, so the translations tend to take the tactic of changing the nature of the sentence to interrogative or conditional. We could also add вАЬLetвАЩs say [you have found honey].вАЭ See B. Webster вАЬThe Perfect Verb and the Perfect Woman in the Book of ProverbsвАЭ in Windows to the Ancient World of the Hebrew Bible, eds. B. Arnold, N. Erickson, J. Walton (Eisenbrauns, 2014).
n2002533: tn The verb means вАЬto be satisfied; to be sated; to be filled.вАЭ Here it means more than satisfied, since it describes one who overindulges and becomes sick. The English verb вАЬstuffedвАЭ conveys this idea well.
n2002534: sn The proverb warns that anything overindulged in can become sickening. The verse uses formal parallelism to express first the condition and then its consequences. It teaches that moderation is wise in the pleasures of life.
n2002535: tn Heb вАЬmake your foot rare.вАЭ The verb is вАП„Ф÷є„І÷Ј„®вАО (hoqar), the Hiphil imperative of вАП„Щ÷Є„І÷Ј„®вАО (yaqar, вАЬto be rare; to be preciousвАЭ). To вАЬmake oneвАЩs foot rareвАЭ would mean to keep the visits to a minimum as well as making them valuableвАФthings increase in value, according to the nuances of this word, when they are rare.
n2002536: tn Heb вАЬgets full.вАЭ This verb means вАЬto be sated; to be satisfied; to be filled.вАЭ It is often used with reference to food, but here it refers to frequent visits that wear out oneвАЩs welcome (cf. NLT).
n2002537: sn The first line identifies the emblem of the proverb: False witnesses are here compared to deadly weapons because they can cause the death of innocent people (e.g., Exod 20:16; Deut 5:20; Prov 14:5).
n2002538: tn The verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷Є„ФвАО ( њanah) followed by the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) with its object means вАЬto testify againstвАЭ (answer against someone). With the preposition вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) it would mean вАЬto testify forвАЭ someone. Here the false witness is an adversary, hence the comparison with deadly weapons.
n2002539: tn While вАП„Ґ÷µ„УвАО ( њed) could be interpreted as вАЬevidenceвАЭ (a meaning that came from a metonymyвАФwhat the witness gives in court), its normal meaning is вАЬwitness.вАЭ Here it would function as an adverbial accusative, specifying how he would answer in court.
n2002540: sn The similes in this emblematic parallelism focus on things that are incapable of performing certain activitiesвАФthey are either too painful to use or are ineffective.
n2002541: tc Heb вАЬConfidence, treacherous ones in a day of trouble.вАЭ Three possibilities require little change to the Hebrew text. (1) The noun вАП„Ю÷і„С÷∞„Ш÷Є„ЧвАО (mivtakh, вАЬconfidenceвАЭ) can be revocalized as a construct noun вАЬthe confidence of the treacherous.вАЭ This in turn could either refer to confidence that has been placed in the treacherous or to the confidence that the treacherous have. (2) It could be revocalized as вАП„Ю÷Ј„С÷∞„Ш÷і„ЧвАО (mavtikh) the Hiphil participle of вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Ш÷Ј„ЧвАО (batakh, вАЬto trustвАЭ) meaning вАЬto cause or inspire to rely on.вАЭ But a preposition is probably still to be expected. (3) One may suppose that a preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) was lost due to haplography before the following word (вАП„С÷є÷Љ„Т÷µ„УвАО, boged) so that the text read вАЬconfidence in a treacherous person.вАЭ Most of the possibilities point toward a reliance on someone who betrays, which is preferred in most English versions. C. H. Toy, however, argues it means that what the faithless person relies on will fail him in the time of trouble (Proverbs [ICC], 466).
n2002542: tn Heb вАЬin the day of troubleвАЭ; KJV, NASB вАЬin time of trouble.вАЭ
n2002543: tc The consonants of the Hebrew text of this verse are similar to the consonants in v. 19. The LXX has a much longer reading: вАЬLike vinegar is bad for a wound, so a pain that afflicts the body afflicts the heart. Like a moth in a garment, and a worm in wood, so the pain of a man wounds the heartвАЭ (NRSV follows much of the LXX reading; NAB follows only the second sentence of the LXX reading). The idea that v. 20 is a dittogram is not very convincing; and the Greek version is too far removed to be of help in the matter.
n2002544: tn The second simile mentions pouring vinegar on soda. The LXX has вАЬscab,вАЭ but that does not fit as a sensitive thing. The reference is to sodium carbonate (natural in Egypt) which can be neutralized with vinegar.
n2002545: sn It is inappropriate and counterproductive to sing songs to a heavy heart. One needs to be sensitive to others (e.g., 1 Sam 19:9).
n2002546: sn The imagery of the вАЬburning coalsвАЭ represents pangs of conscience, more readily effected by kindness than by violence.
