These coals produce the sharp pain of contrition through regret (e.g. 18:19; 20:22; 24:17; Gen 42-45; 1 Sam 24:18-20; Rom 12:20). The coals then would be an implied comparison with a searing conscience.
n2002547: sn The second consequence of treating enemies with kindness is that the Lord will reward the act. The fact that this is promised shows that the instruction here belongs to the religious traditions of Israel.
n2002548: sn One difficulty here is that it is the west wind that brings rain to Israel (e.g., 1 Kgs 18:41-44). C. H. Toy suggests that the expression is general, referring to a northwest windвАФunless it is an error (Proverbs [ICC], 468). J. P. M. van der Ploeg suggests that the saying originated outside the land, perhaps in Egypt (вАЬProv 25:23, вАЭ VT 3 [1953]: 189-92). But this would imply it was current in a place where it made no sense. R. N. Whybray suggests that the solution lies with the verb вАЬbrings forthвАЭ (вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„Ч„Х÷є„Ь÷µ„ЬвАО, tekholel); he suggests redefining it to mean вАЬrepels, holds backвАЭ (cf. KJV вАЬdriveth awayвАЭ). Thus, the point would be that the north wind holds back the rain just as an angry look holds back slander (Proverbs [CBC], 149). But the support for this definition is not convincing. Seeing this as a general reference to northerly winds is the preferred solution.
n2002549: tn Heb вАЬa tongue of secretвАЭ or вАЬa hidden tongue,вАЭ referring to someone who goes around whispering about people behind their backs (cf. KJV, NAB, NASB, NRSV вАЬa backbiting tongueвАЭ).
n2002550: tn The phrase вАЬbrings forthвАЭ does not appear in Hebrew in this line but is implied by the parallelism with the previous line; it is supplied here in the translation for clarity.
n2002551: sn The verse implies a comparison between the two parts to make the point that certain things automatically bring certain results. Gossiping words will infuriate people as easily as the northerly winds bring the cold rain.
n2002552: tn This proverb is identical with 21:9; see the notes there.
n2002553: tn Heb вАЬa weary [or, faint] soulвАЭ (so NASB, NIV); KJV, ASV, NRSV вАЬa thirsty soul,вАЭ but вАЬsoulвАЭ here refers to the whole person.
n2002554: sn The difficulty of getting news of any kind from a distant land made its reception all the more delightful when it was good (e.g., Gen 45:27; Prov 15:30).
n2002555: tn The Niphal participle is from вАП„®÷Є„§÷Ј„°вАО (rafas), which means вАЬto stamp; to tread; to foul by treading [or, by stamping].вАЭ BDB 952 s.v. defines it here as a вАЬfountain befouled.вАЭ The picture is one of a spring of water where men and beasts gather and muddy it by their trampling in and out of it.
n2002556: tn The Hophal participle from вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (shakhat, вАЬto ruin; to destroy; to corruptвАЭ) provides a general descriptionвАФthe well has been вАЬruinedвАЭ or вАЬcorruptedвАЭ (so ASV) and is therefore unusable.
n2002557: tn The verb вАП„Ю÷Є„ШвАО (mat) means вАЬto give way; to move.вАЭ This probably refers to the integrity of the righteous being lostвАФcomparing it to moving [off course]. T. T. Perowne writes, вАЬTo see a righteous man moved from his steadfastness through fear or favour in the presence of the wicked is as disheartening as to find the stream turbid and defiled at which you were longing to quench your thirstвАЭ (Proverbs, 161). But the line may refer to the loss of social standing and position by the righteous due to the plots of the wickedвАФjust as someone muddied the water, someone made the righteous slip from his place.
n2002558: sn This is a figure of speech known as tapeinosisвАФa deliberate understatement to emphasize a worst-case scenario: вАЬit is bad!вАЭ
n2002559: tn Heb вАЬand the investigation of their glory is not glory.вАЭ This line is difficult to understand but it forms an analogy to honeyвАФglory, like honey, is good, but not to excess. The LXX rendered this, вАЬit is proper to honor notable sayings.вАЭ A. A. MacIntosh suggests, вАЬHe who searches for glory will be distressedвАЭ (вАЬA Note on Prov 25:27, вАЭ VT 20 [1970]: 112-14). G. E. Bryce has вАЬto search out difficult things is gloriousвАЭ (вАЬAnother Wisdom вАШBookвАЩ in Proverbs,вАЭ JBL 91 (1972): 145-47). R. C. Van Leeuwen suggests, вАЬto seek difficult things is as gloryвАЭ (вАЬProverbs 25:27 Once Again,вАЭ VT 36 [1986]: 105-14). The Hebrew is cryptic, but not unintelligible: вАЬseeking their glory [is not] glory.вАЭ It is saying that seeking oneвАЩs own glory is dishonorable.
n2002560: tn Heb вАЬwhose spirit lacks restraintвАЭ (ASV similar). A person whose spirit (вАП„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО, ruakh) вАЬlacks restraintвАЭ is one who is given to outbursts of passion, who lacks self-control (cf. NIV, NRSV, CEV, NLT). This person has no natural defenses but reveals his true nature all the time. The proverb is stating that without self-control a person is vulnerable, like a city without defenses.
n200261: sn вАЬHonorвАЭ in this passage probably means respect, external recognition of worth, accolades, advancement to high position, etc. All of these would be out of place with a fool; so the sage is warning against elevating or acclaiming those who are worthless. See also J. A. Emerton, вАЬNotes on Some Passages in the Book of Proverbs,вАЭ VT 15 (1965): 271-79.
n200262: sn The first twelve verses of this chapter, Prov 26:1-12, are sometimes called вАЬthe Book of FoolsвАЭ because they deal with the actions of fools.
n200263:
tn Heb вАЬcauseless curseвАЭ (KJV similar) describes an undeserved curse (cf. NIV, NRSV). The Hebrew word translated вАЬcauselessвАЭ is the adverb from вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (khanan); it means вАЬwithout cause; gratuitous.вАЭ
sn This proverb is saying that a curse that is uttered will be powerless if that curse is undeserved. It was commonly believed in the ancient world that blessings and curses had power in themselves, that once spoken they were effectual. But scripture makes it clear that the power of a blessing or a curse depends on the power of the one behind it (e.g., Num 22:38; 23:8). A curse would only take effect if the one who declared it had the authority to do so, and he would only do that if the curse was deserved.
n200264: tc The MT has the negative with the verb вАЬto enter; to comeвАЭ to mean вАЬwill not comeвАЭ (вАП„Ь÷є„Р „™÷Є„С÷є„РвАО, lo Њ tavo Њ). This is interpreted to mean вАЬwill not come to restвАЭ or вАЬwill not come home.вАЭ Some commentators have taken the Qere reading of вАП„Ь„Х÷євАО (lo) instead, and read it as вАЬwill come home to him.вАЭ This is also a little difficult, but it gives the idea that an undeserved curse will come [back] to him [who gave it]. Just as a bird will fly around and eventually come home, so will the undeserved curse return on the one who gave it. This is plausible, but there is no referent for the suffix, making it syntactically difficult.
n200265: sn A fool must be disciplined by force like an animalвАФthere is no reasoning. The fool is as difficult to manage as the donkey or horse.
n200266: sn One should not answer a foolвАЩs foolish questions in line with the foolвАЩs mode of reasoning (J. H. Greenstone, Proverbs, 274).
n200267: sn The person who descends to the level of a fool to argue with him only looks like a fool as well.
n200268:
tc The editors of BHS suggest that the preposition in v. 5 should be вАП„СвАО (bet) which looks similar to the вАП„ЫвАО (kaf) in v. 4 but has a different meaning. The result would be that v. 4 says вАЬdo not answer a fool in conformity with his folly,вАЭ while the v. 5 might read вАЬanswer a fool in opposition to his folly.вАЭ In a practical sense this would mean that v. 4 speaks of not answering a fool at the level of his folly, perhaps thereby giving it validity, while v. 5 speaks of responding to a fool in opposition to his folly. Yet a similar meaning may be arrived at by maintaining вАП„ЫвАО (kaf) in each verse but reading different nuances based on the second half of each verse.
sn The apparent contradiction with the last verse has troubled commentators for some time. One approach is to assume the different proverbs apply in different settings. The Rabbis solved it by saying that v. 4 referred to secular things, but v. 5 referred to sacred or religious controversies. Another view is to ignore the fool in negligible issues, but to deal with the fool in significant matters, lest credence be given to what he says (W. G. Plaut, Proverbs, 266). Another approach is that the two proverbs present principles that must be held in tension at the same time. The second half of each verse advises, by reference to outcome, what is fitting or unsuited in making a response. (See B. Waltke, The Book of Proverbs [NICOT], 348-350.) Also consider the example of Paul, who talked like a вАЬfoolвАЭ to correct the foolish ideas of the Corinthians (2 Cor 11:16-17; 12:11).
n200269: tn Heb вАЬin his own eyesвАЭ (so NAB, NASB, NIV).
n2002610: sn Sending a messenger on a mission is like having another pair of feet. But if the messenger is a fool, this proverb says, not only does the sender not have an extra pair of feetвАФhe cuts off the pair he has. It would not be simply that the message did not get through; it would get through incorrectly and be a setback! The other simile uses вАЬviolence,вАЭ a term for violent social wrongs and injustice. The metaphorical idea of вАЬdrinkingвАЭ violence means suffering violenceвАФit is oneвАЩs portion. So sending a fool on a mission will have injurious consequences.
n2002611: tn The participle could be taken as the subject of the sentence: вАЬthe one who sendsвА¶cuts offвА¶and drinks.вАЭ
n2002612: sn The consequence is given in the first line and the cause in the second. It would be better not to send a message at all than to use a fool as messenger.
n2002613: tn The line does not start with the comparative preposition вАП„ЫвАО(kaf) вАЬlike,вАЭ but the proverb clearly invites comparison between the two lines.
n2002614: tn Heb вАЬthighs dangle from the lame.вАЭ The verb is вАП„У÷Љ÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Щ„Х÷ЉвАО (dalyu), from вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО (dalah) or вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (dalal) biforms which mean вАЬto hang downвАЭ and possibly by extension вАЬto let down/lower/be lowвАЭ and вАЬto draw [water]вАЭ i.e., lowering a bucket into a well and drawing it up. We might imagine paralyzed legs either as вАЬdanglingвАЭ or вАЬpulled upвАЭ to a stable position where a person sits, both indicating the uselessness of the legsвАФthey are there but cannot be used. Since the verb must function in both halves of the verse, вАЬdanglingвАЭ is the most likely picture. Luther gave the verse a fanciful but memorable rendering: вАЬLike dancing to a cripple, so is a proverb in the mouth of the fool.вАЭ
n2002615: tn Because of the analogy within the verse, indicated in translation by supplying вАЬlike,вАЭ the conjunction vav has been translated вАЬso.вАЭ
n2002616: sn As C. H. Toy puts it, the fool is a вАЬproverb-mongerвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 474); he handles an aphorism about as well as a lame man can walk. The fool does not understand, has not implemented, and cannot explain the proverb. It is useless to him even though he repeats it.
n2002617: tn The verb has been supplied from the first colon because of the convention of ellipsis and double duty (omitting a word in one line which is understood to apply from another line).
n2002618:
tn The translation вАЬlike tying a stone in a slingвАЭ seems to make the most sense, even though the word for вАЬslingвАЭ occurs only here.
sn The point is that only someone who does not know how a sling works would do such a stupid thing (R. N. Whybray, Proverbs [CBC], 152). So to honor a fool would be absurd; it would be counterproductive, for he would still be a fool.
n2002619: tn The line does not start with the comparative preposition вАП„ЫвАО (kaf) вАЬlike,вАЭ but the proverb clearly invites comparison between the two lines.
n2002620: sn The picture is one of seizing a thornbush and having the thorn pierce the hand (вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ь÷Є„Ф „С÷∞„Щ÷Ј„У÷ЊвАО,  њalah veyad). A drunk does not know how to handle a thornbush because he cannot control his movements and so gets hurt (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 599). C. H. Toy suggests that this rather means a half-crazy drunken man brandishing a stick (Proverbs [ICC], 475). In this regard cf. NLT вАЬa thornbush brandished by a drunkard.вАЭ
n2002621: tn Because of the analogy within the verse, indicated in translation by supplying вАЬlike,вАЭ the conjunction vav has been translated вАЬso.вАЭ
n2002622: tn The verb has been supplied from the first colon because of the convention of ellipsis and double duty (omitting a word in one line which is understood to apply from another line).
n2002623: sn A fool can read or speak a proverb but will be intellectually and spiritually unable to handle it; he will misapply it or misuse it in some way. In doing so he will reveal more of his folly. It is painful to hear fools try to use proverbs.
n2002624: tn The line does not start with the comparative preposition вАП„ЫвАО (kaf) вАЬlike,вАЭ but the proverb clearly invites comparison between the two lines.
n2002625: tn Or вАЬAn archer is one who wounds anyone; And the employer of a fool is (particularly) the employer of those just passing by.вАЭ This translation understands the participles substantivally rather than verbally. In a battle, archers are not initially taking aim to hit an individual bullвАЩs eye. They shoot as a group high in the air at the approaching enemy forces, who then find themselves in a hail of dangerous arrows. The individual archer is indiscriminate. When someone hires whoever is passing by, indiscriminately, that employer is more likely to end up with an incompetent or foolish employee. The words in the line have several possible meanings, making it difficult and often considered textually defective. The first line has вАП„®÷Ј„С „Ю÷∞„Ч„Х÷є„Ь÷µ„Ь÷Њ„Ы÷є÷Љ„ЬвАО (rav mekholel kol). The first word, вАП„®÷Ј„СвАО (rav), can mean вАЬarcher,вАЭ вАЬ master,вАЭ or вАЬmuch.вАЭ The verb вАП„Ю÷∞„Ч„Х÷є„Ь÷µ„ЬвАО (mekholel) can mean вАЬto woundвАЭ or вАЬto bring forth.вАЭ The possibilities are: вАЬa master performs [or, produces] all,вАЭ вАЬa master injures all,вАЭ вАЬan archer wounds all,вАЭ or вАЬmuch produces all.вАЭ The line probably should be stating something negative, so the idea of an archer injuring or wounding people [at random] is preferable. An undisciplined hireling will have the same effect as an archer shooting at anything and everything (cf. NLT вАЬan archer who shoots recklesslyвАЭ).
n2002626: tn Because of the analogy within the verse, indicated in translation by supplying вАЬlike,вАЭ the conjunction vav has been translated вАЬso.вАЭ
n2002627: tn The participle вАП„©÷є„В„Ы÷µ„®вАО (shokher) is rendered here according to its normal meaning вАЬhiresвАЭ or вАЬpays wages to.вАЭ Other suggestions include вАЬone who rewards a foolвАЭ (derived from the idea of wages) and вАЬone who stops a foolвАЭ (from a similar word).
n2002628: sn The simile is graphic and debasing (cf. 2 Peter 2:22).
n2002629: sn The point is clear: Fools repeat their disgusting mistakes, or to put it another way, whenever we repeat our disgusting mistakes we are fools. The proverb is affirming that no matter how many times a fool is warned, he never learns.
n2002630: tn Most translations render the verse as a question (вАЬHave you seenвА¶?вАЭ so KJV, NASB, NIV, ESV, Holman) while sometimes this construction is turned into a conditional sentence. But the Hebrew has a perfect verb form (вАП„®÷Є„Р÷і„Щ„™÷ЄвАО; ra Њita), expecting past time, without an interrogative or conditional marker. See the note at Prov 26:16.
n2002631: tn Heb вАЬin his own eyesвАЭ (so ESV, NASB, NIV).
n2002632: sn Previous passages in the book of Proverbs all but deny the possibility of hope for the fool. So this proverb is saying there is absolutely no hope for the self-conceited person, and there might be a slight hope for the foolвАФhe may yet figure out that he really is a fool.
n2002633: sn The Book of Fools covered vv. 1-12. This marks the beginning of what may be called the Book of Sluggards (vv. 13-16). Cf. this verse with 22:13.
n2002634: tn The verb вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„®вАО ( Њamar) can mean вАЬto sayвАЭ or вАЬto think.вАЭ The proverb uses the Hebrew perfect form of the verb for the past tense, giving the reason the sluggard is still in the house rather than out working. It is an example of the sorts of excuses he has made.
n2002635: tn Heb вАЬin the broad plazasвАЭ; NAB, NASB вАЬin the square.вАЭ This proverb makes the same point as 22:13, namely, that the sluggard uses absurd excuses to get out of work. D. Kidner notes that in this situation the sluggard has probably convinced himself that he is a realist and not a lazy person (Proverbs [TOTC], 163).
n2002636: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied from context in the translation.
n2002637: sn The sluggard is too lazy to get out of bedвАФalthough he would probably rationalize this by saying that he is not at his best in the morning. The humor of the verse is based on an analogy with a doorвАФit moves back and forth on its hinges but goes nowhere. Like the door to the wall, the sluggard is вАЬhingedвАЭ to his bed (e.g., Prov 6:9-10; 24:33).
n2002638: tn Because of the analogy within the verse, indicated in translation by supplying вАЬlike,вАЭ the conjunction vav has been translated вАЬso.вАЭ
n2002639: tn The term вАЬturnsвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation from the parallelism.
n2002640: tn Heb The verb вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЯвАО (taman) means вАЬto buryвАЭ (so many English versions) or вАЬto hideвАЭ (so KJV). As the perfect form of a dynamic verb it should be understood as past or perfective. The proverb presents a scene where the sluggard has not just reached to the food in the dish but has buried his hand in it. The second comment reveals that this is not a frozen frame, but a continuing scene revealing the extent of his laziness.
n2002641: tn The verb вАП„†÷і„Ь÷∞„Р÷Є„ФвАО (nil Њah) is a Niphal perfect of the root вАП„Ь÷Є„Р÷Є„ФвАО (la Њah) вАЬto be/grow weary.вАЭ The Niphal is typically reflexive, вАЬto wear oneself out.вАЭ Since the sluggard has not worked, the choice of this verb sounds like a jest. Perhaps it should be understood that, for the sluggard, merely reaching to the bowl is such effort as to become (or feel) to weary to bring his hand back.
n2002642: sn The proverb is stating that the sluggard is too lazy to eat; this is essentially the same point made in 19:24 (see the note there).
n2002643: tn Heb вАЬin his eyes.вАЭ The lazy person thinks that he has life all figured out and has chosen the wise course of actionвАФbut he is simply lazy. J. H. Greenstone says, for example, вАЬMuch anti-intellectualism may be traced to such rationalization for lazinessвАЭ (Proverbs, 269).
n2002644: tn The term means вАЬtaste; judgment.вАЭ The related verb means вАЬto taste; to perceive,вАЭ that is, вАЬto examine by tasting,вАЭ or examine by experiencing (e.g., Ps 34:9). Here the idea is expressed with the participle in construct, вАЬthose returners [of] good sense,вАЭ those who answer tastefully, with discretion. Cf. NIV вАЬwho (+ can NRSV) answer discreetly.вАЭ
n2002645: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ and the following вАЬsoвАЭ are not in the Hebrew text, but supplied from context in the translation. The Hebrew is a metaphor with the predicate first, rendered here as a simile to preserve the order.
n2002646:
tn Heb вАЬgrabs the ears of a dog. The word вАЬwildвАЭ has been supplied in the translation to make clear that these were not domesticated pets. CEV, to accomplish the same point, has вАЬa mad dog,вАЭ but there is no indication of that in context.
sn Someone who did this ran a serious risk of injury or harm. Dogs were not domestic pets in the ancient Near East; they were scavengers that ran in packs like jackals.
n2002647:
tn The word вАП„Ю÷і„™÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„С÷Љ÷µ„®вАО (mit њabber) means вАЬto put oneself in a furyвАЭ or вАЬbecome furiousвАЭ (BDB 720 s.v.). The Latin version apparently assumed the verb was вАП„Ґ÷Є„®÷Ј„СвАО ( њarav), for it has the sense of вАЬmeddleвАЭ (so also NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV). However, the MT reading could easily fit the verse, referring to anyone passing by who gets furious over a fight that is not his.
sn Perhaps the passerby who intrudes (likely not knowing all the facts of the matter) will become the target of both partiesвАЩ displeasure.
n2002648: tn The term вАП„Ы÷Љ÷∞„Ю÷і„™÷∞„Ь÷Ј„Ф÷∞„Ь÷µ„Ф÷Љ÷ЈвАО (kemitlahleah) is the Hitpalpel participle of the quadriliteral verbal root вАП„Ь÷і„Ф÷∞„Ь÷µ„Ф÷Љ÷ЈвАО (lihleah), which means вАЬto amaze; to startleвАЭ (BDB 529 s.v.). Here it functions as a substantiveвАФthe object of the prepositionвАФand has the meaning of вАЬmadmanвАЭ (cf. NRSV вАЬmaniacвАЭ). This is the only occurrence of the term.
n2002649: tn Heb вАЬarrows and deathвАЭ (so KJV, NASB). This expression can be understood as a nominal hendiadys: вАЬdeadly arrowsвАЭ (so NAB, NIV).
n2002651: sn The subject of this proverb is not simply a deceiver, but one who does so out of jest, or at least who claims he was joking afterward. (The LXX adds that he says this вАЬwhenever he is discovered.вАЭ) The participle вАП„Ю÷∞„©÷Ј„В„Ч÷µ„ІвАО (mesakheq) has the idea of вАЬlaughing, mockingвАЭ; in this context it might convey the idea of вАЬkiddingвАЭ or вАЬjoking.вАЭ The point is that such practical joking is immature and often dangerous. To the foolish deceiver it might all seem like fun, like sport, but it can destroy people. One cannot trifle with dangerous weapons, or put them in irresponsible hands; likewise one cannot trifle with human relationships. W. G. Plaut notes, вАЬThe only worthwhile humor is that which laughs with, not at othersвАЭ (Proverbs, 270).
n2002652: sn Gossip (that is, the one who goes around whispering and slandering) fuels contention just as wood fuels a fire. The point of the proverb is to prevent contentionвАФif one takes away the cause, contention will cease (e.g., 18:8).
n2002654: sn Heb вАЬa man of contentionsвАЭ; NCV, NRSV, NLT вАЬa quarrelsome person.вАЭ The expression focuses on the person who is contentious by nature. His quarreling is like piling fuel on a fire that would otherwise go out. This kind of person not only starts strife, but keeps it going.
n2002655: tn The Pilpel infinitive construct вАП„Ь÷∞„Ч÷Ј„®÷∞„Ч÷Ј„®вАО (lekharkhar) from вАП„Ч÷Є„®÷Ј„®вАО (kharar, вАЬto be hot; to be scorched; to burnвАЭ) means вАЬto kindle; to cause to flare up.вАЭ
n2002656: tn The proverb is identical to 18:8 (see notes there); it observes how appealing gossip is.
n2002657: tn The traditional translation of вАЬsilver drossвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NASB) never did make much sense because the parallel idea deals with hypocrisyвАФвАЬfervent lips with an evil heart.вАЭ But silver dross would not be used over earthenwareвАФinstead it is discarded. Yet the MT clearly has вАЬsilver drossвАЭ (вАП„Ы÷Љ÷ґ„°÷ґ„£ „°÷і„Щ„Т÷і„Щ„ЭвАО, kesef sigim). Ugaritic turned up a word spsg which means вАЬglaze,вАЭ and this found a parallel in Hittite zapzaga[y]a. H. L. Ginsberg repointed the Hebrew text to kвАЩsapsagim, вАЬlike glaze,вАЭ and this has been adopted by many commentators and recent English versions (e.g., NAB, NIV, NRSV, NLT). The final вАП„ЭвАО (mem) is then classified as enclitic. See, among others, K. L. Barker, вАЬThe Value of Ugaritic for Old Testament Studies,вАЭ BSac 133 (1976): 128-29.
n2002658: tn The word translated вАЬferventвАЭ actually means вАЬburning, glowingвАЭ; the LXX has вАЬflattering lipsвАЭ (as if from вАП„Ч÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ІвАО [khalaq] rather than вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ІвАО [dalaq]).
n2002659: sn The analogy fits the second line very well. Glaze makes a vessel look beautiful and certainly different from the clay that it actually is. So is one who has evil intent (вАЬheartвАЭ) but covers it with glowing speech.
n2002660: tn The Niphal imperfect from вАП„†÷Є„Ы÷Ј„®вАО (nakhar) means вАЬto act [or, treat] as a foreigner [or, stranger]; to misconstrue; to disguise.вАЭ The direct object (вАЬitвАЭ) is not present in the Hebrew text but is implied. In this passage it means that the hater speaks what is вАЬforeignвАЭ to his thought; in other words, he dissembles.
n2002661: tn Or вАЬplaces; puts; lays upвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB).
n2002662:
tn Heb вАЬwithin himвАЭ (so KJV, ASV) or вАЬin his midstвАЭ; NAB вАЬin his inmost being.вАЭ
sn Hypocritical words may hide a wicked heart. The proverb makes an observation: One who in reality despises other people will often disguise that with what he says.
n2002663: tn The particle вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki) is here interpreted with a temporal nuance. It is also possible that it could be read as concessive (so NIV, NLT вАЬThoughвАЭ).
n2002664: tn The meaning of the rare Piel form of вАП„Ч÷Є„†÷Ј„ЯвАО (khanan) is вАЬto make gracious; to make favorable.вАЭ The subject is вАП„І„Х÷є„Ь„Х÷євАО (qolo, вАЬhis voiceвАЭ), a metonymy of cause for what he says. The idea is that what he says is very gracious in its content and its effect.
n2002665: sn It may be that the placing of this proverb in this setting is designed to point out that the person speaking graciously is this wicked person who conceals an evil heart. Otherwise it may have in mind a person who has already proven untrustworthy but protests in order to conceal his plans. But even if that were not the connection, the proverb would still warn the disciple not to believe someone just because it sounded wonderful. It will take great discernment to know if there is sincerity behind the personвАЩs words.
n2002666: sn The number вАЬsevenвАЭ is used in scripture as the complete number. In this passage it is not intended to be literally seven; rather, the expression means that there is complete or total abomination in his heart. Cf. TEV вАЬhis heart is filled to the brim with hate.вАЭ
n2002667: sn вАЬAbominationвАЭ means something that is loathed. This is a description applied by the writer, for the hypocritical person would not refer to his plans this way.
n2002668: tn The referent is apparently the individual of vv. 24-25.
n2002669: tn The form вАП„™÷Љ÷і„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„°÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (tikkasseh) is the Hitpael imperfect (with assimilation); it is probably passive, meaning вАЬis concealed,вАЭ although it could mean вАЬconceals itselfвАЭ (naturally). Since the proverb uses antithetical parallelism, an imperfect tense nuance of possibility (вАЬmay be concealedвАЭ) works well here (cf. NIV, NLT).
n2002670: sn The Hebrew verb means вАЬto uncover,вАЭ here in the sense of вАЬto reveal; to make known; to expose.вАЭ The verse is promising that the evil the person has done will be exposed publicly. The common belief that righteousness will ultimately triumph informs this saying.
n2002671: sn The verse is teaching talionic justice (вАЬan eye for an eye,вАЭ etc.), and so the activities described should be interpreted as evil in their intent. вАЬDigging a pitвАЭ would mean laying a trap for someone (the figure of speech would be a metonymy of cause for the effect of ruining someone, if an actual pit is being dug; the figure would be hypocatastasis if digging a pit is being compared to laying a trap, but no pit is being dug). Likewise, вАЬrolling a stoneвАЭ on someone means to destroy that individual.
n2002672: tn Heb вАЬthe tongue of deception.вАЭ The subject matter of this proverb is deceptive speech. The вАЬtongue of deceptionвАЭ (using a metonymy of cause with an attributive genitive) means that what is said is false. Likewise the вАЬsmooth mouthвАЭ means that what is said is smooth, flattering.
n2002673: sn The verse makes it clear that only pain and ruin can come from deception. The statement that the lying tongue вАЬhates those crushed by itвАЭ suggests that the sentiments of hatred help the deceiver justify what he says about people. The ruin that he brings is probably on other people, but it could also be taken to include his own ruin.
n200271:
tn The form вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„™÷Љ÷і„™÷∞„Ф÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷µ„ЬвАО ( Њal tithallel) is the Hitpael jussive negated; it is from the common verb вАЬto praise,вАЭ and so in this setting means вАЬto praise oneselfвАЭ or вАЬto boast.вАЭ
sn The verse rules out oneвАЩs overconfident sense of ability to control the future. No one can presume on the future.
n200272: sn The word вАЬtomorrowвАЭ is a metonymy of subject, meaning what will be done tomorrow, or in the future in general.
n200273: sn The expression вАЬyou do not knowвАЭ balances the presumption of the first line, reminding the disciple of his ignorance and therefore his need for humility (e.g., Matt 6:34; Luke 12:20; Jas 4:13-16).
n200274: tn Heb вАЬa stranger.вАЭ This does not necessarily refer to a non-Israelite, as has been demonstrated before in the book of Proverbs, but these are people outside the familiar and accepted circles. The point is that such a person would be objective in speaking about your abilities and accomplishments.
n200275: sn вАЬMouthвАЭ and вАЬlipsвАЭ are metonymies of cause; they mean вАЬwhat is said.вАЭ People should try to avoid praising themselves. Self praise can easily become a form of pride, even if it begins with trivial things. It does not establish a reputation; reputation comes from what others think about you.
n200276: tn вАЬa foreignerвАЭ; KJV, ASV, NASB, NRSV вАЬa stranger.вАЭ
n200277:
tn The subject matter is the vexation produced by a fool. The term вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„°вАО (ka њas) means вАЬvexationвАЭ (ASV); provocationвАЭ (NAB, NASB, NIV, NRSV); вАЬangerвАЭ (KJV вАЬwrathвАЭ) and usually refers to undeserved treatment. Cf. NLT вАЬthe resentment caused by a fool.вАЭ
sn The same noun is used in 1 Sam 1:6, 16 for the вАЬprovocationвАЭ given to Hannah by Peninnah for being barren.
n200278: sn The contrast is made between dealing with the vexation of a fool and physical labor (moving stones and sand). More tiring is the vexation of a fool, for the mental and emotional effort it takes to deal with it is more draining than physical labor. It is, in the sense of this passage, almost unbearable.
n200279: tn Heb вАЬfierceness of wrath and outpouring [= flood] of anger.вАЭ A number of English versions use вАЬfloodвАЭ here (e.g., NASB, NCV, NLT).
n2002710: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬjealousyвАЭ here probably has the negative sense of вАЬenvyвАЭ rather than the positive sense of вАЬzeal.вАЭ It is a raging emotion (like вАЬangerвАЭ and вАЬwrath,вАЭ this word has nuances of heat, intensity) that defies reason at times and can be destructive like a consuming fire (e.g., 6:32-35; Song 8:6-7). The rhetorical question is intended to affirm that no one can survive a jealous rage. (Whether one is the subject who is jealous or the object of the jealousy of someone else is not so clear.)
n2002711: tn Heb вАЬrevealedвАЭ or вАЬuncoveredвАЭ (Pual participle from вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Є„ФвАО, galah). This would specify the reproof or rebuke as direct, honest, and frank, whether it was coming from a friend or an enemy.
n2002712: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬhiddenвАЭ (a Pual participle from вАП„°÷Є„™÷Ј„®вАО, satar) refers to a love that is carefully concealed; this is contrasted with the open rebuke in the first line. What is described, then, is someone too timid, too afraid, or not trusting enough to admit that reproof is a genuine part of love (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 610). It is a love that is not expressed in proper concern for the one loved. See also, e.g., 28:23 and 29:3.
n2002713: tn The Niphal participle of вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷Ј„ЯвАО ( Њaman) means вАЬfaithful; reliable; sure; trustworthy.вАЭ The word indicates that the wounds from a friend вАЬcan be trustedвАЭ (so NIV, NCV) because they are meant to correct and not to destroy (e.g., 25:12; Deut 7:9; Job 12:20).
n2002714: sn вАЬKissesвАЭ probably represents a metonymy of adjunct; the term describes any expressions or indications of affection. But coming from an enemy, they will be insincereвАФas indicated by their excessive number.
n2002715: tn The form is вАП„†÷Ј„Ґ÷∞„™÷Љ÷Є„®„Х÷є„™вАО (na њtarot), the Niphal participle of вАП„Ґ÷Є„™÷Ј„®вАО ( њatar, вАЬto be abundantвАЭ). Contemporary translations render this rare form in a number of different ways: вАЬdeceitfulвАЭ (NASB, NKJV); вАЬprofuseвАЭ (NRSV); вАЬmanyвАЭ (NLT). But the idea of вАЬexcessiveвАЭ or вАЬnumerousвАЭ fits very well. The kisses of an enemy cannot be trusted, no matter how often they are presented.
n2002716: tn Traditionally, вАЬsoulвАЭ (so KJV, ASV). The Hebrew text uses вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh) here for the subjectвАФthe full appetite [вАЬsoulвАЭ]. The word refers to the whole person with all his appetites. Here its primary reference is to eating, but it has a wider application than thatвАФpossession, experience, education, and the like.
n2002717: tn Here the term вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, traditionally, вАЬsoulвАЭ) is used again, now in contrast to describe the вАЬhungry appetiteвАЭ (cf. NRSV вАЬravenous appetiteвАЭ), although вАЬhungry mouthвАЭ might be more idiomatic for the idea. Those whose needs are great are more appreciative of things than those who are satisfied. The needy will be delighted even with bitter things.
n2002718: tn The form вАП„†„Х÷є„У÷ґ„У÷ґ„™вАО (nodedet) is the Qal participle from вАП„†÷Є„У÷Ј„УвАО (nadad), вАЬto wander; to stray; to flutter; to retreat; to departвАЭ; cf. NIV, NRSV, NLT вАЬstrays.вАЭ It will be directly paralleled with the masculine participle in the second colon.
n2002719:
tn Heb вАЬplaceвАЭ (so KJV, ASV); most other English versions translate as вАЬhome.вАЭ
sn The reason for the wandering from the nest/place is not given, but it could be because of exile, eviction, business, or irresponsible actions. The saying may be generally observing that those who wander lack the security of their home and cannot contribute to their community (e.g., the massive movement of refugees). It could be portraying the unhappy plight of the wanderer without condemning him over the reason for the flight.
n2002720: sn The first line of the proverb provides the emblem to the parallel point. The emblem is the joy that anointing oil (ointment) and incense bring, and the point is the value of the advice of a friend.
n2002721: tn Some think the MT is unintelligible as it stands: вАЬThe sweetness of his friend from the counsel of the soul.вАЭ The Latin version has вАЬthe soul is sweetened by the good counsels of a friend.вАЭ D. W. Thomas suggests, вАЬcounsels of a friend make sweet the soulвАЭ (вАЬNotes on Some Passages in the Book of Proverbs,вАЭ VT 15 [1965]: 275). G. R. Driver suggests, вАЬthe counsel of a friend is sweeter than oneвАЩs own adviceвАЭ (literally, вАЬmore than the counsel of the soulвАЭ). He also suggests вАЬmore than of fragrant wood.вАЭ See G. R. Driver, вАЬHebrew Notes,вАЭ ZAW 52 (1934): 54; idem, вАЬSuggestions and Objections,вАЭ ZAW 55 (1937): 69-70. The LXX reads вАЬand the soul is rent by misfortunes.вАЭ The MT, for want of better or more convincing readings, may be interpreted to mean something like вАЬ[Just as] ointment and incense brings joy to the heart, [so] the sweetness of oneвАЩs friend [comes] from his sincere counsel.вАЭ
n2002722: sn The meaning of the verse is very difficult, although the translation is rather straightforward. It may simply be saying that people should retain family relationships but will discover that a friend who is available is better than a relative who is not. But C. H. Toy thinks that the verse is made up of three lines that have no connection: 10a instructs people to maintain relationships, 10b says not to go to a brotherвАЩs house [only?] when disaster strikes, and 10c observes that a nearby friend is better than a far-away relative. C. H. Toy suggests a connection may have been there, but has been lost (Proverbs [ICC], 485-86). The conflict between 17:17 and 10b may be another example of presenting two sides of the issue, a fairly frequent occurrence in the book of Proverbs.
n2002723: tn Heb вАЬmy sonвАЭ; the reference to a вАЬsonвАЭ is retained in the translation here because in the following lines the advice is to avoid women who are prostitutes.
n2002724: tn The verb is the cohortative of вАП„©„Б„Х÷Љ„СвАО (shuv); after the two imperatives that provide the instruction, this form with the vav will indicate the purpose or result (indirect volitive sequence).
n2002725: sn The expression anyone who taunts me refers to those who would reproach or treat the sage with contempt, condemning him as a poor teacher. Teachers are often criticized for the faults and weaknesses of their students, but any teacher criticized that way takes pleasure in pointing to those who have learned as proof that he has not labored in vain (e.g., 1 Thess 2:19-20; 3:8).
n2002726: tn All of the verbs in this verse are Hebrew perfect forms that should be understood as past tense. The proverb presents its message as events which have occurred and are prototypical of the behavior of the shrewd and the inexperienced.
n2002727: tn This noun is plural, while the earlier substantival adjective вАЬshrewdвАЭ is singular. The contrast may suggest that the naive are in a group, each one doing what the others do, while insightful person had to go against the flow. That is, the naive go along with the bandwagon; but the shrewd person thinks for his/herself and makes good decisions accordingly.
n2002728: tn Heb вАЬpassed byвАЭ; the word вАЬrightвАЭ is supplied in the translation to clarify the meaning: The naive person, oblivious to impending danger, meets it head on.
n2002729: tn The Qal of the verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„†÷Є„©„БвАО ( њanash) means to impose a fine; here in the Niphal it means to have a fine imposed, or to have to pay for something. By extension it means to suffer a penalty. The English idiom вАЬto pay forвАЭ meaning вАЬto suffer the consequencesвАЭ conveys the idea while preserving the lexical base in Hebrew. Cf. NIV, ESV вАЬsuffer for it,вАЭ NASB, TNIV вАЬpay the penalty,вАЭ KJV, Holman вАЬare punished.вАЭ
n2002731:
tn Or вАЬfor a strange (= adulterous) woman.вАЭ Cf. KJV, ASV, NASB, NLT; NIV вАЬa wayward woman.вАЭ The first noun вАП„Ц÷Є„®вАО (zar) вАЬstranger,вАЭ вАЬforeignerвАЭ is masculine; the second term вАП„†÷Є„Ы÷∞„®÷і„Щ÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (nokhriyyah) вАЬforeigner,вАЭ вАЬstrangerвАЭ is feminine, thus whether the stranger is a man or a woman. The terms do not have to mean a non-Israelite, just someone from outside the community and not well-known.
sn This proverb is virtually identical to 20:16 which has a rare variant spelling of the initial imperative verb and has the masculine plural вАЬstrangersвАЭ as its Kethib reading, while matching 27:13 with the feminine singular вАЬstrangerвАЭ as its Qere reading.
n2002732: tn The verse begins with the Piel participle from вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„®÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (barakh). It could be taken as the subject, with the resulting translation: вАЬBlessingвА¶will be counted as a curse.вАЭ However, that would be rather awkward. So it is preferable to take the first line as the condition (вАЬif someone blessesвАЭ) and the second as the consequence (вАЬ[then] it will be countedвАЭ).
n2002733: tn Heb вАЬrising early in the morningвАЭ (so KJV, ASV). The infinitive explains the verb вАЬbless,вАЭ giving the circumstances of its action. The individual rises early to give his blessing.
n2002734: sn The point of the proverb is that loud and untimely greetings are not appreciated. What was given as a вАЬblessingвАЭ will be considered a вАЬcurseвАЭвАФthe two words being antonyms. The proverb makes the point that how, when, and why they say what they say is important too (D. Kidner, Proverbs [TOTC], 166).
n2002735: tn Heb вАЬa wife of contentionsвАЭ (an attributive genitive). Cf. NAB, NIV вАЬa quarrelsome wifeвАЭ; NLT вАЬa nagging wife.вАЭ
n2002736:
tc The form вАП„†÷і„©„Б÷∞„™÷Љ÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО (nishtavah) may be a rare Nitpael form or a scribal error for the Niphal of the root вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО (shavah, вАЬto be like, comparable to.вАЭ BDB classifies it as a Nitpael perfect from (BDB 1001 s.v. I вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷Є„ФвАО) with metathesis before the sibilant, a phonetic factor also known in the Hitpael. HALOT suggests that the metathesis is a scribal error such that the form should be вАП„†÷і„©„Б÷∞„Х÷Є„™÷Є„ФвАО (nishvatah), a standard Niphal perfect third feminine singular. Either form should be understood with a reflexive meaning. She вАЬmakes herself likeвАЭ an annoying dripping. The LXX concocts a further illustration: вАЬDrops drive a man out of his house on a wintry day; so a railing woman also drives him out of his own house.вАЭ
tn The word вАЬthatвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew. This is structured like other metaphorical proverbs (e.g. 26:7, 9, 10) whose first line begins without the word вАЬlike,вАЭ but still functions as a comparison for the second line which begins with the conjunction vav (вАЬandвАЭ). These are often translated as similes, using вАЬlikeвА¶ soвА¶.вАЭ In this case the verb has a semantic meaning of вАЬlike,вАЭ so that has not been added at the beginning to avoid redundancy in English.
n2002737: tn The participle and the verb are both from the root вАП„¶÷Є„§÷Є„ЯвАО (tsafan) вАЬto store up,вАЭ and by extension perhaps, вАЬto hideвАЭ (so KJV). To вАЬstore upвАЭ wind would entail вАЬrestrainingвАЭ it (so NASB, NIV, ESV, Holman) or вАЬcontainingвАЭ it, a gloss which is closer to the basic meaning of the term.
n2002738: sn A contentious woman is uncontrollable. The wind can gust at any moment; so too the contentious woman can nag or complain without warning.
n2002739: sn The verb is the Qal imperfect of вАП„І÷Є„®÷Є„РвАО (qara Њ); BDB 895 s.v. 5.b defines it here as вАЬcall for = demand, require,вАЭ but acknowledges that it probably needs revision. R. B. Y. Scott interprets it to mean вАЬgraspingвАЭ oil in the hand, an expression he compares to the modern вАЬbutterfingersвАЭ (Proverbs, Ecclesiastes [AB], 163). The imperfect form is interpreted as modal, вАЬcan grasp,вАЭ for this context. Others have interpreted it to mean вАЬbetraysвАЭвАФвАЬointment of his right hand betrays itself,вАЭ meaning its smell persists. However, the connection to the proverb does not seem obvious with that interpretation.
n2002740: tc The LXX took an etymologizing approach to the whole verse and translated it вАЬthe north wind is a severe wind, but by its name is termed auspicious.вАЭ In this rendering the Hebrew textвАЩs вАЬoilвАЭ became вАЬits name,вАЭ вАЬright handвАЭ became вАЬauspicious,вАЭ and вАЬgraspвАЭ became вАЬcalled.вАЭ
n2002741: tn The term вАЬasвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation to clarify the comparison.
n2002742: tn BDB classifies the verb in the first colon as a Qal apocopated jussive of I вАП„Ч÷Є„У÷Є„ФвАО (khadah, вАЬto grow sharpвАЭ; BDB 292 s.v.), and the verb in the second half of the verse (вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷Ј„УвАО, yakhad) as a Hiphil apocopated jussive. The difference would be: вАЬlet iron by means of iron grow sharp, and let a man sharpen the countenance of his friend.вАЭ But it makes more sense to take them both as Hiphil forms, the first being in pause. Other suggestions have been put forward for the meaning of the word, but the verb вАЬsharpensвАЭ fits the context the best, and is followed by most English versions. The verb may be a shortened form of the imperfect rather than a jussive.
n2002743: tn Heb вАЬand a man,вАЭ although the context does not indicate this should be limited to males only.
n2002744: tn Heb вАЬsharpens the face of his friend.вАЭ The use of the word вАЬfaceвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV вАЬcountenanceвАЭ) would here emphasize that it is the personality or character that is being sharpened. Constructive criticism sharpens character. Use of the wits in interaction that makes two people sharp as a razor (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 615); another example, from the Talmud, is that of two students sharpening each other in the study of the Torah (b. TaвАЩanit 7a).
n2002745: sn Tending fig trees requires closer attention than other plants; so the point here would be the diligent care that is required.
n2002746: sn The principle is established in the first line with the emblem: Those who faithfully serve will be rewarded in kind. The second half of the proverb makes the point from this illustration.
n2002747: sn The Hebrew participle translated вАЬtakes care ofвАЭ (вАП„©„Б÷є„Ю÷µ„®вАО, shomer) describes a careful watching over or looking after, a meticulous service, anticipating the needs and safeguarding the charge. Such a servant need not worry about his efforts going unrecognized and unrewarded (e.g., Prov 22:29; 2 Tim 2:6, 15).
n2002748: tn The verse is somewhat cryptic and so has prompted many readings. The first line in the MT has вАЬAs water the face to the face.вАЭ The simplest and most probable interpretation is that clear water gives a reflection of the face (cf. NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT). One creative but unconvincing suggestion is that of L. Kopf, who suggests the idea is вАЬwater of faceвАЭ (a construct) and that it means shame or modesty, i.e., a face is not really human without shame, and a man without a heart is not human (вАЬArabische Etymologien und Parallelen zum Bibelw√ґrterbuch,вАЭ VT 9 [1959]: 260-61).
n2002749:
tn The second line has вАЬso the heart of a man to a manвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV). The present translation (along with many English versions) supplies вАЬreflectsвАЭ as a verb in the second line to emphasize the parallelism.
sn In the parallelism this statement means that a personвАЩs heart is the true reflection of that person. It is in looking at the heart, the will, the choices, the loves, the decisions, the attitudes, that people come to self-awareness.
n2002750: tn The term вАЬasвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation in light of the analogy.
n2002752: sn Countless generations of people have gone into the world below; yet вАЬdeathвАЭ is never satisfiedвАФit always takes more. The line personifies Death and Destruction. It forms the emblem in the parallelism.
n2002753: tn Heb вАЬeyes of a man.вАЭ This expression refers to the desiresвАФwhat the individual looks longingly on. Ecclesiastes Rabbah 1:34 (one of the rabbinic Midrashim) says, вАЬNo man dies and has one-half of what he wanted.вАЭ
n2002754: tc The LXX contains a scribal addition: вАЬHe who fixes his eye is an abomination to the Lord, and the uninstructed do not restrain their tongues.вАЭ This is unlikely to be original.
n2002755: tn Heb вАЬA crucible is for silver. A furnace is for gold. A person is for his [word of] praise.вАЭ The analogy is implicit and represented in translation by the comparatives вАЬasвАЭ and вАЬso.вАЭ A crucible and furnace are used to melt the metals, refining them or verifying their purity. Likewise, every person should test their praise.
n2002756: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО ( Њish) often refers to a male, but can also mean a person, whether male or female.
n2002757: tn The Hebrew saying lacks a verb. The verbal phrase вАЬmust put to the testвАЭ was supplied in the translation in view of the analogy of testing or refining precious metals. Many English versions (NASB, NIV, ESV, NRSV) supply вАЬis tested byвАЭ for the same reason. Whether the person is testing his praise or being tested by it depends on the structure of the saying and the prepositional phrase. The prepositional phrase says that the person is вАП„Ь÷∞„§÷і„ЩвАО (lefi), вАЬfor the mouth ofвАЭ his praise. The noun вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (peh) вАЬmouthвАЭ can stand for вАЬword,вАЭ i.e., what is spoken by the mouth, hence вАЬthe [spoken] word of his praise.вАЭ But also, the combination of вАП„ЬвАО (lamed) with вАП„§÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО (peh) can mean вАЬaccording to.вАЭ The other translations take the concept of testing from the first part of the line and join it to the gloss for вАП„Ь÷∞„§÷і„ЩвАО вАЬaccording to.вАЭ However, вАП„Ь÷∞„§÷і„ЩвАО only means вАЬaccording toвАЭ in the sense of вАЬproportionate toвАЭ (cf. Exod 12:4; 16:16; Lev 27:16), not instrumentally, as in вАЬby [means of].вАЭ Additionally, the proverb has three parts, each with noun plus lamed plus noun (or noun phrase). It is structurally consistent to view the crucible, the furnace, and the person as all having the same role of testing what follows the preposition lamed.
n2002758: tn Heb вАЬhis praise.вАЭ The pronominal suffix could be an objective genitive (about him, i.e., the praise he receives) or a subjective genitive (by him, i.e., the praise he gives). If intended to be an objective genitive, the proverb could mean two things. A person must test, sift through and evaluate, the praise he or she receives. Or a person must prove, verify by being worthy of, the word of praise he or she receives. If it is a subjective genitive, it means a person must refine, make valuable, the praise that he or she gives. Some commentators interpret a subjective genitive to mean that people stand revealed by what or how they praise (D. Kidner, Proverbs [TOTC], 168). But the structure of the saying positions the person as one performing a test, along with the crucible and the furnace.
n2002759: tn The verb means вАЬto poundвАЭ in a mortar with a pestle (cf. NRSV вАЬCrushвАЭ; NLT вАЬgrindвАЭ). The imperfect is in a conditional clause, an unreal, hypothetical condition to make the point.
n2002760: tn The Hebrew term вАП„®÷і„Щ„§„Х÷є„™вАО (rifot) refers to some kind of grain spread out to dry and then pounded. It may refer to barley groats (coarsely ground barley), but others have suggested the term means вАЬcheesesвАЭ (BDB 937 s.v.). Most English versions have вАЬgrainвАЭ without being more specific; NAB вАЬgrits.вАЭ
n2002761: tn The LXX contains this paraphrase: вАЬIf you scourge a fool in the assembly, dishonoring him, you would not remove his folly.вАЭ This removes the imagery of mortar and pestle from the verse. Using the analogy of pounding something in a mortar, the proverb is saying even if a fool was pounded or pulverized, meaning severe physical punishment, his folly would not leave himвАФit is too ingrained in his nature.
n2002762: tn The sentence uses the infinitive absolute and the imperfect from вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ, вАЬto knowвАЭ). The imperfect here has been given the obligatory nuance, вАЬyou must know,вАЭ and that has to be intensified with the infinitive.
n2002764:
tn вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО (lev) means вАЬmind, heartвАЭ and by extension can refer to aspects of thinking or the will. The Hebrew idiom вАЬset the mind (вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО) onвАЭ or вАЬput the mind (вАП„Ь÷µ„СвАО) toвАЭ transfers easily to English and is another way of saying to pay careful attention to something.
sn The care of the flock must become the main focus of the will, for it is the livelihood. So v. 23 forms the main instruction of this lengthy proverb (vv. 23-27).
n2002765: tn Heb вАЬriches are not foreverвАЭ (so KJV, NASB); TEV вАЬwealth is not permanent.вАЭ The term вАЬlastвАЭ is supplied in the translation for clarity.
n2002766: tn The conjunction and the particle indicate that the same nuance continues here in the second colon, and so вАЬlastвАЭ has been supplied here as well.
n2002767: sn Verse 25 is the protasis and v. 26 the apodosis. The two verses say that when the harvest is taken in, then the grass will grow, and they can sell and use their livestock. The lambs will provide clothing, and the goats when sold will pay for land.
n2002768: sn This part of the proverb shows the proper interplay between human labor and divine provision. It teaches people to take care of what they have because it will not last forever.
n2002769: tn Heb вАЬlifeвАЭ; KJV, NAB вАЬmaintenanceвАЭ; NRSV вАЬnourishment.вАЭ
n200281:
tc The plural verb вАП„†÷Є„°„Х÷ЉвАО (nasu) вАЬthey fled,вАЭ should be read as singular to match the subject. It is a case of dittography: the vav beginning the next word was written twice, with the extra vav appended to this verb.
tn The verb вАП„†÷Є„°вАО (nas) is the perfect form of a dynamic root and should be understood as past or perfective. The proverb presents this portion as a past event which has happened and is prototypical of what can be expected. It contrasts what a wicked person felt compelled to do with what a righteous person can choose to do.
n200282: sn The line portrays the insecurity of a guilty personвАФhe flees because he has a guilty conscience, or because he is suspicious of others around him, or because he fears judgment.
n200283:
tn The verb вАП„С÷Є÷Љ„Ш÷Ј„ЧвАО (batakh) means вАЬto trust; to be secure; to be confident.вАЭ Cf. KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV, NLT вАЬbold.вАЭ As the imperfect form of a stative verb, it should be understood as future or modal. It has been translated as an abilitive modal вАЬcan be confident.вАЭ
sn The righteous, who seek to find favor with God and man, have a clear conscience and do not need to look over their shoulders for avengers or law enforcers. Their position is one of confidence, so that they do not flee.
n200284: sn The Hebrew word translated вАЬrebelliousвАЭ has rebellion as its basic meaning, and that is the idea here. The proverb is describing a time when sinfulness brings about social and political unrest.
n200285:
tn Heb вАЬmany are its princesвАЭ (so NASB).
sn In such a chaotic time there will be many rulers, either simultaneously or in a rapid sequence. The times of the judges or the days of the northern kings of Israel provide examples.
n200286: tn Heb вАЬa man who understands [and] knowsвАЭ; NRSV вАЬan intelligent rulerвАЭ; NLT вАЬwise and knowledgeable leaders.вАЭ
n200287:
tc The LXX reads (probably from a different underlying Hebrew text): вАЬIt is the fault of a violent man that quarrels start, but they are settled by a man of discernment.вАЭ For a survey of suggestions, see C. H. Toy, Proverbs (ICC), 495, and W. McKane, Proverbs (OTL), 630.
tn This last line is difficult. The MT has вАП„Ы÷Љ÷µ„Я „Щ÷Ј„Р÷≤„®÷і„Щ„Ъ÷∞вАО (ken ya Њarikh). The verb means вАЬto prolong,вАЭ but вАП„Ы÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (ken) is open to several possibilities for meaning. J. H. GreenstoneвАЩs interpretation of it as a noun from the Hollow root вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (kun) with a meaning of вАЬestablished orderвАЭ is what is expected here (Proverbs, 293).
sn For a study of the verses in chapters 28 and 29 concerning kings and governments, see B. V. Malchow, вАЬA Manual for Future Monarchs,вАЭ CBQ 47 (1985): 238-45.
n200288: tc The MT reads вАЬa poor man,вАЭ вАП„Т÷Љ÷ґ„С÷ґ„® „®÷Є„©„БвАО (gever rash); cf. KJV, NASB, NLT. The problem is that the poor in the book of Proverbs is not an oppressor and does not have the power to be such. So commentators assume the word is incorrect. By a slight change to вАП„®÷Є„©„Б÷Є„ҐвАО (rasha њ) the reading becomes вАЬa wicked rulerвАЭ [Heb вАЬa wicked mighty manвАЭ]. There is no textual support for this change. The LXX, however, reads, вАЬA courageous man oppresses the poor with impieties.вАЭ If вАЬa poor manвАЭ is retained, then the oppression would include betrayalвАФone would expect a poor man to have sympathy for others who are impoverished, but in fact that is not the case. It is a sad commentary on human nature that the truly oppressed people can also be oppressed by other poor people.
n200289: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2002810: sn вАЬFoodвАЭ is a metonymy of effect here. The picture is of the driving rain that should cause crops to grow so that food can be producedвАФbut does not (some English versions assume the crops are destroyed instead, e.g., NCV, TEV, CEV, NLT). The point the proverb is making is that a show of strength may not produce anything except ruin.
n2002811: sn Some commentators do not think that the word refers to the Mosaic law, but to вАЬinstructionвАЭ or вАЬteachingвАЭ in general (cf. NCV вАЬwho disobey what they have been taughtвАЭ). However, the expression вАЬkeep the lawвАЭ in the second line indicates that it is binding, which would not be true of teaching in general (J. Bright, вАЬThe Apodictic Prohibition: Some Observations,вАЭ JBL 92 [1973]: 185-204). Moreover, Proverbs 28:9 and 29:18 refer to the law, and this chapter has a stress on piety.
n2002812: sn The proverb gives the outcome and the evidence of those who forsake the lawвАФthey вАЬpraise the wicked.вАЭ This may mean (1) calling the wicked good or (2) justifying what the wicked do, for such people are no longer sensitive to evil.
n2002813: tn The verb is the Hitpael imperfect of вАП„Т÷Љ÷Є„®÷Є„ФвАО (garah), which means вАЬto stir up strifeвАЭ but in this stem means вАЬto engage in strifeвАЭ (cf. NIV вАЬresist themвАЭ). Tg. Prov 28:4 adds an explanatory expansion, вАЬso as to induce them to repent.вАЭ
n2002814: tn Heb вАЬmen of evilвАЭ; the context does not limit this to males only, however.
n2002815: tn The term translated вАЬjusticeвАЭ is вАП„Ю÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷Є„ШвАО (mishpat); it refers to the legal rights of people, decisions that are equitable in the community. W. G. Plaut observes that there are always those who think that вАЬjusticeвАЭ is that which benefits them, otherwise it is not justice (Proverbs, 282).
n2002816: sn The contrast (and the difference) is between the wicked and those who seek the Lord. Originally the idea of seeking the Lord meant to obtain an oracle (2 Sam 21:1), but then it came to mean devotion to GodвАФseeking to learn and do his will. Only people who are interested in doing the LordвАЩs will can fully understand justice. Without that standard, legal activity can become self-serving.
n2002817: sn This chapter gives a lot of attention to the contrast between the poor and the rich, assuming an integrity for the poor that is not present with the rich; the subject is addressed in vv. 6, 8, 11, 20, 22, 25, and 27 (G. A. Chutter, вАЬRiches and Poverty in the Book of Proverbs,вАЭ Crux 18 [1982]: 23-28).
n2002818: tn The Hebrew term translated вАЬwaysвАЭ is in the dual, suggesting that the person has double ways, i.e., he is hypocritical. C. H. Toy does not like this idea and changes the form to the plural (Proverbs [ICC], 497), but his emendation is gratuitous and should be rejected.
n2002819: tn Heb вАЬand he is rich.вАЭ Many English versions treat this as a concessive clause (cf. KJV вАЬthough he be richвАЭ).
n2002820: sn This is another вАЬbetterвАЭ saying, contrasting a poor person who has integrity with a rich person who is perverse. Of course there are rich people with integrity and perverse poor people, but that is not of interest here. If it came to the choices described here, honest poverty is better than corrupt wealth.
n2002821: tn The Hebrew word could refer (1) to вАЬinstructionвАЭ by the father (cf. NCV) or (2) the Mosaic law (so most English versions). The chapter seems to be stressing religious obedience, so the referent is probably the law. Besides, the fatherвАЩs teaching will be what the law demands, and the one who associates with gluttons is not abiding by the law.
n2002822: tn Heb вАЬson,вАЭ but the immediate context does not suggest limiting this only to male children.
n2002823: sn The companion of gluttons shames his father and his family because such a life style as he now embraces is both unruly and antisocial.
n2002824: tn Heb вАЬfather,вАЭ but the immediate context does not suggest limiting this only to the male parent.
n2002825: tn Heb вАЬby interest and increaseвАЭ (so ASV; NASB вАЬby interest and usuryвАЭ; NAB вАЬby interest and overcharge.вАЭ The two words seem to be synonyms; they probably form a nominal hendiadys, meaning вАЬby increasing [exorbitant] interest.вАЭ The law prohibited making a commission or charging interest (Exod 22:25; Lev 25:36-37; Deut 23:20; Ps 15:5). If the poor needed help, the rich were to help themвАФbut not charge them interest.
n2002826:
tn The term вАП„Ч„Х÷є„†÷µ„ЯвАО (khonen, вАЬsomeone who shows favorвАЭ) is the active participle.
sn The verse is saying that in GodвАЩs justice wealth amassed unjustly will eventually go to the poor. God will take the wealth away from them and give it to people who will distribute it better to the poor.
n2002827: sn The expression вАЬturn away the ear from hearingвАЭ uses a metonymy to mean that this individual will not listenвАФit indicates a deliberate refusal to follow the instruction of the law.
n2002828: sn It is hard to imagine how someone who willfully refuses to obey the law of God would pray according to the will of God. Such a person is more apt to pray for some physical thing or make demands on God. (Of course a prayer of repentance would be an exception and would not be an abomination to the Lord.)
n2002829: sn C. H. Toy says, вАЬIf a man, on his part, is deaf to instruction, then God, on his part, is deaf to prayerвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 499). And W. McKane observes that one who fails to attend to GodвАЩs law is a wicked person, even if he is a man of prayer (Proverbs [OTL], 623).
n2002830: sn The image of falling into a pit (a figure of speech known as hypocatastasis, involving implied comparison) is meant to say that the evil to which he guides people will ultimately destroy him.
n2002831: sn This proverb is teaching that those who corrupt others will be destroyed, usually by their own devices, but those who manage to avoid being corrupted will be rewarded. According to this proverb the righteous can be led astray (e.g., 26:27).
n2002832: tn Heb вАЬa rich man,вАЭ although the context does not indicate that this is limited only to males.
n2002833: sn The idiom вАЬin his own eyesвАЭ means вАЬin his own opinion,вАЭ that is, his self conceit. The rich person thinks he is wise because he is rich, that he has made all the right choices.
n2002834: tn The form вАП„Щ÷Ј„Ч÷∞„І÷∞„®÷ґ„†÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (yakhqerennu) means вАЬhe searches himвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV) or вАЬhe examines himвАЭ; a potential imperfect nuance fits well here to indicate that a discerning person, even though poor, can search the flaws of the rich and see through the pretension and the false assumptions (cf. NAB, NASB, NIV вАЬsees through himвАЭ). Several commentators have connected the word to the Arabic root hqr, which means вАЬdespiseвАЭ (D. W. Thomas, вАЬNotes on Some Passages in the Book of Proverbs,вАЭ JTS 38 [1937]: 400-403), but that would be both predictable and flat.
n2002835: tn The form вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷≤„Ь÷є„•вАО (ba њalots) is the infinitive construct with the preposition indicating a temporal clause (вАЬwhenвА¶вАЭ); the вАЬrighteousвАЭ are the subject of this clause (subjective genitive). The word may be taken as a metonymy of adjunctвАФthe righteous exult or rejoice because they are prosperous (cf. NLT вАЬsucceedвАЭ).
n2002836: sn вАЬGloryвАЭ here may have the sense of elation and praise.
n2002837: tn The meaning of вАЬsought outвАЭ (вАП„Щ÷∞„Ч÷ї„§÷Љ÷Ј„©„ВвАО, yekhuppas) indicates that people have gone into hiding. So the development of the ideas for this proverb requires in the first line that вАЬrejoiceвАЭ be connected with вАЬtriumphвАЭ that means they have come to power; and in the second line that вАЬare sought outвАЭ means people have gone into hiding (cf. ASV, NIV, NRSV, NLT). C. H. Toy thinks this is too strained; he offers this rendering: вАЬWhen the righteous are exalted there is great confidence, but when the wicked come into power men hide themselvesвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 500). For the verb G. R. Driver posits an Arabic cognate hafasa, вАЬprostrated; trampled onвАЭ (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew Text of Proverbs,вАЭ Bib 32 [1951]: 192-93), which gives a clearer result of wicked rule, but is perhaps unnecessary (e.g., Prov 28:28; 29:2). See J. A. Emerton, вАЬNotes on Some Passages in the Book of Proverbs,вАЭ JTS 20 (1969): 202-20.
n2002838: tn The Hebrew participles provide the subject matter in this contrast. On the one hand is the person who covers over (вАП„Ю÷∞„Ы÷Ј„°÷Љ÷ґ„ФвАО, mekhasseh) his sins. This means refusing to acknowledge them in confession, and perhaps rationalizing them away. On the other hand there is the one who both вАЬconfessesвАЭ (вАП„Ю„Х÷є„У÷ґ„ФвАО, modeh) and вАЬforsakesвАЭ (вАП„Ґ÷є„Ц÷µ„СвАО,  њozev) the sin. To вАЬconfessвАЭ sins means to acknowledge them, to say the same thing about them that God does.
n2002839: sn The verse contrasts the consequences of each. The person who refuses to confess will not prosper. This is an understatement (a figure of speech known as tapeinosis); the opposite is the truth, that eventually such a person will be undone and ruined. On the other hand, the penitent will find mercy. This expression is a metonymy of cause for the effectвАФalthough вАЬmercyвАЭ is mentioned, what mercy provides is intended, i.e., forgiveness. In other passages the verb вАЬconcealвАЭ is used of GodвАЩs forgivenessвАФhe covers over the iniquity (Ps 32:1). Whoever acknowledges sin, God will cover it; whoever covers it, God will lay it open.
n2002840: sn This verse is unique in the book of Proverbs; it captures the theology of forgiveness (e.g., Pss 32; 51). Every part of the passage is essential to the point: Confession of sins as opposed to concealing them, coupled with a turning away from them, results in mercy.
n2002841: tn Most commentators (and some English versions, e.g., NIV) assume that the participle вАП„Ю÷∞„§÷Ј„Ч÷µ„УвАО (mefakhed, вАЬfearsвАЭ) means вАЬfears the Lord,вАЭ even though вАЬthe LordвАЭ is not present in the text. Such an assumption would be more convincing if the word вАП„Щ÷і„®÷∞„Р÷Ј„™вАО (yir Њat) had been used. It is possible that the verse refers to fearing sin or its consequences. In other words, the one who is always apprehensive about the nature and consequences of sin will avoid sin and find GodвАЩs blessing. Of course the assumption that the phrase means вАЬfear the LordвАЭ could be correct as well. There would be little difference in the outcome; in either case sin would be avoided.
n2002842: sn The one who вАЬhardens his heartвАЭ in this context is the person who refuses to fear sin and its consequences. The image of the вАЬhard heartвАЭ is one of a stubborn will, unyielding and unbending (cf. NCV, TEV, NLT). This individual will fall into sin.
n2002843: tn The term вАЬlikeвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied in the translation for clarity and smoothness.
n2002844: sn The comparison uses animals that are powerful, terrifying, insensitive, and in search of prey. Because political tyrants are like this, animal imagery of this sort is also used in Dan 7:1-8 for the series of ruthless world powers.
n2002845: sn A poor nation under the control of political tyrants who are dangerous and destructive is helpless. The people of that nation will crumble under them because they cannot meet their demands and are of no use to them.
n2002846: tn Heb вАЬA prince lacking of understanding [is] also a great oppressorвАЭ (both KJV, ASV similar) The last clause, вАЬand a great oppressor,вАЭ appears to modify вАЬthe prince.вАЭ There is little difference in meaning, only in emphasis. The LXX has вАЬlacks incomeвАЭ (reading вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„С„Х÷Љ„Р„Х÷є„™вАО [tevu Њot] instead of вАП„™÷Љ÷∞„С„Х÷Љ„†„Х÷є„™вАО [tevunot]). C. H. Toy (Proverbs [ICC], 501) suggests deleting the word for вАЬprinceвАЭ altogether, but this emendation is gratuitous.
n2002847: tc This follows the Qere reading of the participle which is singular (as opposed to the plural). The implication is that this one is also a ruler, paralleling the first half. But since he вАЬhatesвАЭ (= rejects) unjust gain he will extend [his] days, meaning he will enjoy a long and happy life (cf. NIV, NRSV, CEV).
n2002848: tn The form is the Qal passive participle. The verb means вАЬto oppress; to wrong; to extortвАЭ; here the idea of being вАЬoppressedвАЭ would refer to the burden of a guilty conscience (hence вАЬtormentedвАЭ; cf. NAB, NRSV вАЬburdenedвАЭ). Some commentators have wanted to emend the text to read вАЬsuspected,вАЭ or вАЬcharged with,вАЭ or вАЬgiven to,вАЭ etc., but if the motive is religious and not legal, then вАЬoppressedвАЭ or вАЬtormentedвАЭ is preferred.
n2002849: sn The text has вАЬthe blood of a lifeвАЭ; blood will be the metonymy of effect for the murder, the shedding of blood.
n2002850: tn The verse is cryptic; it simply says that he will вАЬflee to the pit.вАЭ Some have taken the вАЬpitвАЭ to refer to the place of detention for prisoners, but why would he flee to that place? It seems rather to refer to death. This could mean that (1) since there is no place for him to go outside of the grave, he should flee to the pit (cf. TEV, NLT), or (2) he will be a fugitive until he goes to the grave (cf. NASB, NIV, NCV, NRSV, CEV). Neither one of these options is easily derived from the text. The verse seems to be saying that the one who is guilty of murder will flee, and no one should assist him. The meaning of вАЬthe pitвАЭ is unresolved.
n2002851: tn The form is the Niphal imperfect of вАП„Щ÷Є„©÷Ј„Б„ҐвАО (yasha њ, вАЬwill be savedвАЭ). In all probability this refers to deliverance from misfortune. Some render it вАЬkept safeвАЭ (NIV) or вАЬwill be safeвАЭ (NRSV, TEV). It must be interpreted in contrast to the corrupt person who will fall.
n2002852: tn The Qal imperfect вАП„Щ÷і„§÷Љ„Х÷є„ЬвАО (yippol) is given a future translation in this context, as is the previous verb (вАЬwill be deliveredвАЭ) because the working out of divine retribution appears to be coming suddenly in the future. The idea of вАЬfallingвАЭ could be a metonymy of adjunct (with the falling accompanying the ruin that comes to the person), or it may simply be a comparison between falling and being destroyed. Cf. NCV вАЬwill suddenly be ruinedвАЭ; NLT вАЬwill be destroyed.вАЭ
n2002853: tn The last word in the verse, вАП„С÷∞÷Љ„Р÷ґ„Ч÷Є„™вАО (be Њekhat), means вАЬin one [= at once (?)].вАЭ This may indicate a sudden fall, for falling вАЬin oneвАЭ (the literal meaning) makes no sense. W. McKane wishes to emend the text to read вАЬinto a pitвАЭ based on v. 10b (Proverbs [OTL], 622); this emendation is followed by NAB, NRSV.
n2002854: tn Or вАЬwill have plenty of foodвАЭ (Heb вАЬbreadвАЭ); so NAB, NASB, NCV.
n2002855:
tn Heb вАЬempty thingsвАЭ or вАЬvain thingsвАЭ; NRSV вАЬfollows worthless pursuits.вАЭ
sn Prosperity depends on diligent work and not on chasing empty dreams. The proverb is essentially the same as Prov 12:11 except for the last expression.
n2002856: tn The repetition of the verb strengthens the contrast. Both halves of the verse use the verb вАП„Щ÷і„©„В÷∞„С÷Љ÷Ј„ҐвАО (yisba њ, вАЬwill be satisfied; will be filled with; will have enoughвАЭ). It is positive in the first colon, but negative in the secondвАФwith an ironic twist to say one is вАЬsatisfiedвАЭ with poverty.
n2002857:
tn Heb вАЬa man of faithfulness,вАЭ although the context does not indicate this should be limited only to males.
sn The text does not qualify the nature of the faithfulness. While this would certainly have implications for the personвАЩs righteous acts, its primary meaning may be his diligence and reliability in his work. His faithful work will bring the returns.
n2002858: sn The proverb is not rebuking diligent labor. One who is eager to get rich quickly is the opposite of the faithful person. The first person is faithful to God and to the covenant community; the second is trying to get rich as quickly as possible, at the least without doing an honest dayвАЩs work and at the worst dishonestly. In a hurry to gain wealth, he falls into various schemes and will pay for it. Tg. Prov 28:20 interprets this to say he hastens through deceit and wrongdoing.
n2002859: tn The construction uses the Hiphil infinitive вАП„Ф÷Ј„Ы÷Љ÷µ„®вАО (hakken) as the subject of the sentence: вАЬto have respect for [or, recognize] persons is not goodвАЭ (e.g., 24:23; 18:5; Deut 1:17; Lev 19:15). Such favoritism is вАЬnot goodвАЭ; instead, it is a miscarriage of justice and is to be avoided.
n2002860: tn Heb вАЬnot good.вАЭ This is a figure of speech known as tapeinosisвАФa deliberate understatement to emphasize a worst-case scenario: вАЬit is terrible!вАЭ
n2002861: tn The meaning and connection of the line is not readily clear. It could be taken in one of two ways: (1) a person can steal even a small piece of bread if hungry, and so the court should show some compassion, or it should show no partiality even in such a pathetic case; (2) a person could be bribed for a very small price (a small piece of bread being the figure representing this). This second view harmonizes best with the law.
n2002862: tn Heb вАЬa man with an evil eyeвАЭ (as opposed to the generous man who has a вАЬgoodвАЭ eye). This individual is selfish, unkind, unsympathetic to others. He looks only to his own gain. Cf. NAB вАЬThe avaricious manвАЭ; NLT вАЬA greedy person.вАЭ
n2002863: sn The one who is hasty to gain wealth is involved in sin in some way, for which he will be punished by poverty. The idea of вАЬhasteningвАЭ after riches suggests a dishonest approach to acquiring wealth.
n2002865: tn Heb вАЬa man,вАЭ but the context does not indicate this should be limited only to males.
n2002866: tn There is a problem with вАП„Р÷Ј„Ч÷≤„®÷Ј„ЩвАО ( Њakharay), which in the MT reads вАЬafter me.вАЭ This could be taken to mean вАЬafter my instructions,вАЭ but that is forced. C. H. Toy suggests simply changing it to вАЬafterвАЭ or вАЬafterward,вАЭ i.e., вАЬin the endвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 504), a solution most English versions adopt. G. R. Driver suggested an Akkadian cognate aбЄЂurr√ї, вАЬcommon man,вАЭ reading вАЬas a rebuker an ordinary manвАЭ (вАЬHebrew Notes,вАЭ ZAW 52 [1934]: 147). The Akkadian term can refer to a coarse, uneducated person (CAD A1: 216), if so here, then вАЬone who rebukes a lout/oaf.вАЭ
n2002867: tn The construction uses the Hiphil participle вАП„Ю÷Ј„Ч÷≤„Ь÷і„Щ„ІвАО (makhaliq, вАЬmakes smoothвАЭ) followed by the adverbial accusative of means, the metonymy вАЬtongueвАЭвАФhe makes what he says smooth. This will be pleasing for the moment, but it will offer no constructive help like the rebuke would.
n2002868: sn While the expression is general enough to cover any kind of robbery, the point seems to be that because it can be rationalized it may refer to prematurely trying to gain control of the family property through some form of pressure and in the process reducing the parentsвАЩ possessions and standing in the community. The culprit could claim what he does is not wrong because the estate would be his anyway.
n2002869: sn The metaphor of вАЬcompanionвАЭ here means that a person who would do this is just like the criminally destructive person. It is as if they were working together, for the results are the same.
n2002870: tn Heb вАЬman who destroysвАЭ (so NASB); TEV вАЬno better than a common thief.вАЭ
n2002871: tn Heb вАЬwide of soul.вАЭ This is an idiom meaning вАЬa greedy person.вАЭ The term вАП„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (nefesh, traditionally, вАЬsoulвАЭ) has here its more basic meaning of appetites (a person is a soul, a bundle of appetites; BDB 660 s.v. 5.a). It would mean вАЬwide of appetiteвАЭ (вАП„®÷∞„Ф÷Ј„С÷Њ„†÷ґ„§÷ґ„©„БвАО, rehav nefesh) thus вАЬgreedy.вАЭ
n2002872: sn Greed вАЬstirs upвАЭ the strife. This individualвАЩs attitude and actions stir up dissension because people do not long tolerate him.
n2002873: tn The construction uses the participle вАП„С÷Љ„Х÷є„Ш÷µ„Ч÷ЈвАО (boteakh) followed by вАП„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„Щ÷∞„Ф„Х÷Є„ФвАО ( њal yehvah), which gives the sense of вАЬrelying confidently on the Lord.вАЭ This is the antithesis of the greedy person who pushes to get what he desires.
n2002874: tn The verb вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„©„Б÷µ„ЯвАО (dashen) means вАЬto be fat,вАЭ and in the Piel/Pual stems вАЬto make fat/to be made fatвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV). The idea of being вАЬfatвАЭ was symbolic of health and prosperityвАФthe one who trusts in the Lord will be abundantly prosperous and fully gratified (cf. NRSV вАЬwill be enrichedвАЭ).
n2002875: sn The idea of вАЬtrusting in oneвАЩs own heartвАЭ is a way of describing one who is self-reliant. C. H. Toy says it means to follow the untrained suggestions of the mind or to rely on oneвАЩs own mental resources (Proverbs [ICC], 505). It is arrogant to take no counsel but to rely only on oneвАЩs own intelligence.
n2002876: sn The idiom of вАЬwalking in wisdomвАЭ means to live life according to the acquired skill and knowledge passed on from the sages. It is the wisdom from above that the book of Proverbs presents, not the undisciplined and uninformed wit and wisdom from below.
n2002877: tn The verb form вАП„Щ÷і„Ю÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷µ„ШвАО (yimmalet) is the Niphal imperfect; the form means вАЬto escape.вАЭ In this context one would conclude that it means вАЬto escape from trouble,вАЭ because the one who lives in this life by wisdom will escape trouble, and the one who trusts in himself will not.
n2002878: sn The generous individual will be rewarded. He will not lack nor miss what he has given away to the poor.
n2002879: tn Heb вАЬhides his eyesвАЭ; вАЬto themвАЭ is supplied in the translation to indicate the link with the poor in the preceding line. Hiding or closing the eyes is a metonymy of cause or of adjunct, indicating a decision not to look on and thereby help the poor. It could also be taken as an implied comparison, i.e., not helping the poor is like closing the eyes to them.
n2002880: tn The term вАЬreceivesвАЭ is not in the Hebrew text but is implied, and is supplied in the translation.
n2002881: sn The text does not specify the nature or the source of the curses. It is natural to think that they would be given by the poor who are being mistreated and ignored. Far from being praised for their contributions to society, selfish, stingy people will be reviled for their heartless indifference.
n2002882:
tn Heb вАЬthe wicked rise,вАЭ referring to an accession to power, as in a government. Cf. TEV вАЬcome to powerвАЭ; NLT вАЬtake charge.вАЭ
sn The proverb is essentially the same as 28:12 (e.g., Prov 11:10; 29:2, 16). It refers to the wicked вАЬrising to powerвАЭ in government.
n2002883: tn Heb вАЬa manвАЭ or вАЬmankindвАЭ in a generic sense.
n2002884: tn The form is the Niphal imperfect of вАП„°÷Є„™÷Ј„®вАО (satar, вАЬto hideвАЭ); in this stem it can mean вАЬto hide themselvesвАЭ or вАЬto go into hiding.вАЭ In either case the expression would be a hyperbole; the populace would not go into hiding, but they would tread softly and move about cautiously. G. R. Driver suggests the Akkadian sataru instead, which means вАЬto demolish,вАЭ and is cognate to the Aramaic вАЬto destroy.вАЭ This would produce the idea that people are вАЬdestroyedвАЭ when the wicked come to power (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew Text of Proverbs,вАЭ Bib 32 [1951]: 192-93). That meaning certainly fits the idea, but there is no reason for the change because the MT is perfectly readable as it is and makes good sense.
n2002885: tn The two clauses have parallel constructions: They both begin with infinitives construct with prepositions functioning as temporal clauses, followed by subjective genitives (first the wicked, and then the pronoun referring to them). This heightens the antithesis: вАЬwhen the wicked riseвА¶when they perish.вАЭ
n200291: tn The idiom вАЬto harden the neckвАЭ (вАП„Ю÷Ј„І÷∞„©„Б÷ґ„Ф÷Њ„Ґ÷є„®÷ґ„£вАО, maqsheh  њoref) is the idea of resisting the rebukes and persisting in obstinacy (e.g., Exod 32:9). The opposite of a вАЬstiff neckвАЭ would be the bending back, i.e., submission.
n200292: tn The Hebrew construction is вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„Б „™÷Љ„Х÷є„Ы÷Є„Ч„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њish tokhakhot, вАЬa man of rebukesвАЭ), meaning вАЬa man who has (or receives) many rebukes.вАЭ This describes a person who is deserving of punishment and who has been given many warnings. The text says, then, вАЬa man of rebukes hardening himself.вАЭ
n200293: sn The stubborn person refuses to listen; he will suddenly be destroyed when the calamity strikes (e.g., Prov 6:15; 13:18; 15:10).
n200295: tn The Hebrew form вАП„С÷Љ÷і„®÷∞„С„Х÷є„™вАО (birvot) is the Qal infinitive construct of вАП„®÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО (ravah) with a вАП„СвАО (bet) preposition, forming a temporal clause with a subjective genitive following it.
