It is paralleled in the second colon by the same construction, showing the antithesis: вАП„Х÷Љ„С÷і„Ю÷∞„©„Б÷є„ЬвАО (uvimshol), вАЬand when the wicked rule.вАЭ Some commentators wish to change the first verb to make it parallel this more closely, e.g., вАП„®÷Є„У÷Є„ФвАО (radah, вАЬto ruleвАЭ), but that would be too neat and is completely unsupported. The contrast is between when the righteous increase and when the wicked rule. It is not hard to see how this contrast works out in society.
n200296: tn The Niphal verb вАП„Р÷Є„†÷Ј„ЧвАО ( Њanakh) means вАЬto sigh; to groan,вАЭ usually because of grief or physical and emotional distress. The word is a metonymy of effect; the cause is the oppression and distress due to evil rulers.
n200297: tn Heb вАЬa man.вАЭ Here вАЬmanвАЭ is retained in the translation because the second colon mentions prostitutes.
n200298: tn Or вАЬcauses his father to rejoiceвАЭ; NAB вАЬmakes his father glad.вАЭ
n200299: tn The active participle вАП„®÷є„Ґ÷ґ„ФвАО (ro њeh) is from the second root вАП„®÷Є„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (ra њah), meaning вАЬto associate with.вАЭ The verb occurs only a few times, and mostly in the book of Proverbs. It is related to вАП„®÷µ„Ґ÷ґ„ФвАО (re њeh, вАЬfriend; companion; fellowвАЭ). To describe someone as a вАЬcompanionвАЭ or вАЬfriendвАЭ of prostitutes is somewhat euphemistic; it surely means someone who is frequently engaging the services of prostitutes.
n2002910: tn The Hebrew verb вАП„Щ÷∞„Р÷Ј„С÷Љ÷ґ„УвАО (ye Њabbed) means вАЬdestroysвАЭ; it is the Piel imperfect of the verb that means вАЬto perish.вАЭ
n2002911: sn Wealth was seen as a sign of success and of GodвАЩs blessings, pretty much as it always has been. To be seen as honorable in the community meant one had acquired some substance and kept his reputation. It would be a disgrace to the family to have a son who squandered his money on prostitutes (e.g., Prov 5:10; 6:31).
n2002912: tn The form is the Hiphil imperfect of the verb вАП„Ґ÷Є„Ю÷Ј„УвАО ( њamad, вАЬto standвАЭ), hence, вАЬto cause to stand.вАЭ It means that the king makes the nation вАЬstand firm,вАЭ with вАЬstanding firmвАЭ being a figure for strength, security, and stability. Cf. NCV вАЬmakes his country (the nation CEV) strong.вАЭ
n2002913: tn Or вАЬcountry.вАЭ This term functions as a metonymy of subject for the people in the land.
n2002914: tn The Hebrew text reads вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„Б „™÷Љ÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„Ю„Х÷є„™вАО ( Њish terumot, вАЬa man of offeringsвАЭ), which could refer to a man who вАЬreceives giftsвАЭ or вАЬgives gifts.вАЭ Because of its destructive nature on the country, here the phrase must mean that he receives or вАЬexactsвАЭ the money (cf. NRSV вАЬmakes heavy exactionsвАЭ). This seems to go beyond the ordinary taxation for two reasons: (1) this ruler is a вАЬman of offerings,вАЭ indicating that it is in his nature to do this, and (2) it tears down the country. The word вАЬofferingsвАЭ has been taken to refer to gifts or bribes (cf. NASB, NIV, CEV, NLT), but the word itself suggests more the idea of tribute or taxes that are demanded; this Hebrew word was used in Leviticus for offerings given to the priests, and in Ezek 45:16 for taxes. The point seems to be that this ruler or administrator is breaking the backs of the people with heavy taxes or tribute (e.g., 1 Sam 8:11-18), and this causes division and strife.
n2002915: tn Heb вАЬa man,вАЭ but the context here does not suggest that the proverb refers to males only.
n2002916:
tn The form is the Hiphil participle, literally вАЬdeals smoothly,вАЭ i.e., smoothing over things that should be brought to oneвАЩs attention.
sn The flatterer is too smooth; his words are intended to gratify. In this proverb some malice is attached to the flattery, for the words prove to be destructive.
n2002917: sn The image of вАЬspreading a netвАЭ for someoneвАЩs steps is an implied comparison (a figure of speech known as hypocatastasis): As one would literally spread a net, this individualвАЩs flattery will come back to destroy him. A net would be spread to catch the prey, and so the idea is one of being caught and destroyed.
n2002918: tn There is some ambiguity concerning the referent of вАЬhis steps.вАЭ The net could be spread for the one flattered (cf. NRSV, вАЬa net for the neighborвАЩs feetвАЭ; NLT, вАЬtheir feet,вАЭ referring to others), or for the flatterer himself (cf. TEV вАЬyou set a trap for yourselfвАЭ). The latter idea would make the verse more powerful: In flattering someone the flatterer is getting himself into a trap (e.g., 2:16; 7:5; 26:28; 28:23).
n2002919: tn The Syriac and Tg. Prov 29:6 simplify the meaning by writing it with a passive verb: вАЬthe evil man is ensnared by his guilt.вАЭ The metaphor of the snare indicates that the evil person will be caught in his own transgression.
n2002920: tc The two verbs create some difficulty because the book of Proverbs does not usually duplicate verbs like this and because the first verb вАП„Щ÷Є„®„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО (yarun) is irregular. The BHS editors prefer to emend it to вАП„Щ÷Є„®„Х÷Љ„•вАО (yaruts, вАЬwill rushвАЭ; cf. NAB вАЬruns on joyfullyвАЭ). W. McKane emends it to вАЬexultвАЭ to form a hendiadys: вАЬis deliriously happyвАЭ (Proverbs [OTL], 638). G. R. Driver suggests changing the word to вАП„Щ÷Є„У„Х÷є„ЯвАО (yadon) based on two Hebrew mss and an Arabic cognate dana, вАЬcontinue.вАЭ He translates it вАЬbut the righteous remains and rejoicesвАЭ (вАЬProblems in the Hebrew Text of Proverbs,вАЭ Bib 32 [1951]: 193-94). None of these changes are particularly helpful. The verb is unusual for a geminate root, but Gesenius shows several places where the same pattern can be seen in other geminate verbs (GKC 180 ¬І67.q). In light of this it is preferable to retain the reading of the MT here.
n2002921: sn These two verbs express the confidence of the righteousвАФthey have no fears and so can sing. So the proverb is saying that only the righteous can enjoy a sense of security.
n2002922: tn The form is an active participle, вАП„Щ÷є„У÷µ„Ґ÷ЈвАО (yodea њ); it describes the righteous as вАЬknowing, caring for, having sympathetic knowledge for, or considering favorablyвАЭ the legal needs of the poor. Cf. NAB вАЬhas a care forвАЭ; NASB вАЬis concerned for.вАЭ
n2002923: tn The Hebrew word used here is вАП„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (din), which typically means вАЬjudgment,вАЭ but can also mean вАЬstrifeвАЭ and вАЬcause.вАЭ Here it refers to the вАЬcauseвАЭ of the poor (so KJV, ASV), their plea, their case, their legal rights. A righteous person is sympathetic to this.
n2002924: tn The term вАЬsuchвАЭ is supplied in the translation for clarification. It is not simply any knowledge that the wicked do not understand, but the knowledge mentioned in the first colon. They do not understand the вАЬsympathetic knowledgeвАЭ or вАЬconcernвАЭ for the cause of the poor.
n2002925: tn Heb вАЬmen of scornвАЭ; NAB вАЬArrogant menвАЭ; ASV, NRSV вАЬScoffersвАЭ; NIV, NLT вАЬMockers.вАЭ
n2002926: tn The verb means вАЬto blow; to breatheвАЭ (BDB 806 s.v. вАП„§÷Љ„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО). In the Hiphil imperfect its meaning here is вАЬto excite; to inflameвАЭ a city, as in blowing up a flame or kindling a fire. It is also used with вАЬwordsвАЭ in 6:19 and 12:17вАФthey вАЬpuff out words.вАЭ Such scornful people make dangerous situations worse, whereas the wise calm things down (e.g., 2 Sam 20).
n2002927: tn The term вАЬcityвАЭ is a metonymy of subject; it refers to the people in the city who can easily be set in an uproar by such scornful people.
n2002928: tn The word вАЬwhenвАЭ does not occur in the Hebrew. But the verse presents the situation as a typical example. Hebrew proverbs can begin by presenting a setting in the first line and then developing or commenting on it.
n2002929: tn The verb вАП„†÷і„©÷∞„Б„§÷Љ÷Є„ШвАО (nishpat) is a Niphal participle of вАП„©÷Є„Б„§÷Ј„ШвАО (shafat) вАЬto judge.вАЭ In the Niphal stem it could be passive, but is more frequently reciprocal: вАЬto enter into controversyвАЭ or вАЬto go to court.вАЭ The word is usually used in connection with a lawsuit (so many recent English versions), but can also refer to an argument (e.g., 1 Sam 12:7; Isa 43:26); cf. NAB вАЬdisputesвАЭ; NASB вАЬhas a controversy.вАЭ
n2002930:
tn The noun вАП„†÷Є„Ч÷Ј„™вАО (nakhat) is a derivative of вАП„†„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (nuakh, вАЬto restвАЭ) and so means вАЬquietnessвАЭ or вАЬrest,вАЭ i.e., вАЬpeace.вАЭ
sn The proverb is saying that there will be no possibility of settling the matter in a calm way, no matter what mood the fool is in (e.g., Prov 26:4). R. N. Whybray says one can only cut the losses and have no further dealings with the fool (Proverbs [CBC], 168).
n2002931: tn Heb вАЬthen he gets angry and laughs and there is no peace.вАЭ The verbs вАП„Х÷∞„®÷Є„Т÷Ј„ЦвАО (veragaz; to be angry or agitated) and вАП„Х÷∞„©÷Є„В„Ч÷Ј„ІвАО (vesakhaq; to laugh or mock) are each a vav plus perfect consecutive. They may refer to alternative actions or alternating actions. Grammatically the subject of these verbs is not clear, whether вАЬthe wise man (whose tactics are all unavailing) or, as seems more probable, the fool (who will adopt any approach but the quietly objective)вАЭ (D. Kidner, Proverbs [TOTC], 174).
n2002932: tn Heb вАЬmen of bloods.вАЭ The Hebrew word for вАЬbloodвАЭ is written in the plural to reflect the shedding of blood. So the expression вАЬmen of bloodsвАЭ means people who shed bloodвАФmurderers, bloodthirsty men, or those who would not hesitate to commit murder in order to get what they want.
n2002933: sn The Hebrew word describes the вАЬblamelessвАЭ or вАЬinnocentвАЭ who maintain integrity. The bloodthirsty despise people who insist on decency and integrity.
n2002934: tn Heb вАЬand the upright seek his life.вАЭ There are two ways this second line can be taken. (1) One can see it as a continuation of the first line, meaning that the bloodthirsty men also вАЬseek the life of the uprightвАЭ (cf. NIV, NRSV). The difficulty is that the suffix is singular but the apparent referent is plural. (2) One can take it is as a contrast: вАЬbut as for the upright, they seek his lifeвАЭвАФa fairly straightforward rendering (cf. ASV). The difficulty here is that вАЬseeking a lifeвАЭ is normally a hostile act, but it would here be positive: вАЬseekingвАЭ a life to preserve it. The verse would then say that the bloodthirsty hate the innocent, but the righteous protect them (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 637; cf. NAB, NASB, TEV).
n2002935: tn Heb вАЬhis spirit.вАЭ It has been commonly interpreted to mean вАЬhis angerвАЭ (ASV, NAB, NIV, NRSV), but it probably means more than that. The fool gives full expression to his вАЬsoul,вАЭ whether it is anger or bitterness or frustration or any other emotions. He has no self-control.
n2002936: tn The line is difficult. The MT has вАП„С÷Љ÷∞„Р÷Є„Ч„Х÷є„® „Щ÷∞„©„Б÷Ј„С÷Љ÷∞„Ч÷ґ„†÷Љ÷Є„ФвАО (be Њakhor yeshabbekhennah), which literally means вАЬsteals it back.вАЭ The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„С÷Ј„ЧвАО (shavakh) means вАЬto soothe; to still,вАЭ as with a storm, or here with the temper. But because вАП„Р÷Є„Ч„Х÷є„®вАО ( Њakhor) does not fit very well with this verb, most commentators offer some suggested change. C. H. Toy reads вАЬangerвАЭ instead of вАЬbackвАЭ and translates the verb вАЬrestrainвАЭ following the LXX, which has вАЬself-controlвАЭ (Proverbs [ICC], 510). The idea of self-control is what is intended, but the changes suggested are not entirely warranted. A number of English versions have вАЬholds it backвАЭ (e.g., NASB, NRSV, NLT), and this fits the Hebrew as well as any.
n2002937:
tn The Hiphil participle вАП„Ю÷Ј„І÷∞„©„Б÷і„Щ„СвАО (maqshiv) means вАЬto give attention to; to regard; to heed.вАЭ Cf. NASB, NCV, TEV вАЬpays attention to.вАЭ
sn Such a ruler would become known as one who could be lied to, because he paid attention to lies.
n2002938: tn Heb вАЬword of falsehoodвАЭ or вАЬlying word.вАЭ Cf. TEV вАЬfalse information.вАЭ
n2002939: tn The verb вАП„©„Б÷Є„®÷Ј„™вАО (sharat) means вАЬto minister; to serve.вАЭ The Piel plural participle here refers to servants of the king who attend to himвАФcourtiers and ministers (cf. NIV, NRSV, TEV, CEV вАЬofficialsвАЭ; NLT вАЬadvisersвАЭ). This, his entourage, will have to resort to evil practices to gain his favor if he is swayed by such lies.
n2002940: sn The servants of the monarch adjust to their ruler; when they see that court flattery and deception are effective, they will begin to practice it and in the end become wicked (e.g., Prov 16:10; 20:8; 25:2).
n2002941: tn Heb вАЬa man of oppressionsвАЭ; KJV вАЬthe deceitful man.вАЭ The noun вАП„™÷Љ÷є„Ъ÷∞вАО (tokh) means вАЬinjury; oppressionвАЭ (BDB 1067 s.v.). Such men were usually the rich and powerful. The Greek and the Latin versions have вАЬthe debtor and creditor.вАЭ
n2002942: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Т÷Ј„©„БвАО (pagash) means вАЬto meet; to encounter.вАЭ In the Niphal it means вАЬto meet each other; to meet togetherвАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV). The focus in this passage is on what they share in common.
n2002943: sn The expression gives light to the eyes means вАЬgives them sightвАЭ (cf. NIV). The expression means that by giving them sight the Lord gives them the light of life (e.g., Job 33:30; Ps 13:3). God creates and controls them all. So in spite of their circumstances in life, all people receive their life from God.
n2002944: tn The king must judge вАЬin truthвАЭ (вАП„С÷Љ÷ґ„Р÷±„Ю÷ґ„™вАО, be Њemet). Some have interpreted this to mean вАЬfaithfullyвАЭ (KJV, ASV) but that is somewhat unclear. The idea is that the poor must be treated fairly and justly (cf. NIV вАЬwith fairnessвАЭ; NRSV вАЬwith equityвАЭ); вАЬtruthвАЭ is that which corresponds to the standard of the law revealed by God. There must be no miscarriage of justice for these people simply because they are poor.
n2002945: sn The term вАЬthroneвАЭ is a metonymy of subject; it represents the dynasty, the reign of this particular king and his descendants. The qualification of the enduring administration is its moral character. The language of this proverb reflects the promise of the Davidic Covenant (e.g., Prov 16:12; 20:28; 25:5; 31:5).
n2002946: tn The word вАЬrodвАЭ is a metonymy of cause, in which the instrument being used to discipline is mentioned in place of the process of disciplining someone. So the expression refers to the process of discipline that is designed to correct someone. Some understand the words вАЬrod and reproofвАЭ to form a hendiadys, meaning вАЬa correcting [or, reproving] rodвАЭ (cf. NAB, NIV вАЬthe rod of correctionвАЭ).
n2002948: tn The form is a Pual participle; the form means вАЬto let looseвАЭ (from the meaning вАЬto sendвАЭ; cf. KJV, NIV вАЬleft to himselfвАЭ), and so in this context вАЬunrestrained.вАЭ
n2002949: sn The Hebrew participle translated вАЬbrings shameвАЭ is a metonymy of effect; the cause is the unruly and foolish things that an unrestrained child will do.
n2002950: sn The focus on the mother is probably a rhetorical variation for the вАЬparentвАЭ (e.g., 17:21; 23:24-25) and is not meant to assume that only the mother will do the training and endure the shame for a case like this (e.g., 13:24; 23:13).
n2002951: tn The verb вАП„®÷Є„С÷Є„ФвАО (ravah), which is repeated twice in this line, means вАЬto increase.вАЭ The first occurrence here is usually taken to mean that when the wicked increase they hold the power (cf. NRSV, NLT вАЬare in authorityвАЭ; TEV, CEV вАЬare in powerвАЭ). The text does not explain the details, only that when the wicked increase sin will increase in the land.
n2002952: sn The Hebrew verb translated вАЬseeвАЭ in this context indicates a triumph: The righteous will gaze with satisfaction, or they will look on the downfall of the wicked triumphantly (e.g., Pss 37:4 and 112:8). The verse is teaching that no matter how widespread evil may be, the righteous will someday see its destruction.
n2002953: tn The verb, a Hiphil imperfect with a suffix, could be subordinated to the preceding imperative to form a purpose clause (indirect volitive classification): вАЬthat he may give you rest.вАЭ The same then could apply to the second part of the verse.
n2002954: tn Heb вАЬyour soul.вАЭ The noun вАП„†÷Ј„§÷∞„©„Б÷ґ„Ъ÷ЄвАО (nafshekha, вАЬyour soulвАЭ) is a synecdoche of part (= inner soul) for the whole person (= you); see, e.g., Isa 43:4; 51:23; BDB 600 s.v. 4.a.2.
n2002955: sn The parallelism of this verse is synthetic; the second half adds the idea of вАЬdelight/pleasureвАЭ to that of вАЬrest.вАЭ So a disciplined child will both relieve anxiety (вАЬgiveвА¶restвАЭ) and bring happiness to the parents.
n2002956: tn Heb вАЬno vision.вАЭ The Hebrew word вАЬvisionвАЭ (from the verb вАП„Ч÷Є„Ц÷Є„ФвАО [khazah, вАЬto seeвАЭ]) refers to divine communication to prophets (as in 1 Sam 3:1) and not to individual goals or plans. C. H. Toy sees a problem here: The most calamitous period of IsraelвАЩs history was when prophetic vision was at its height, whereas people were often more obedient when God was silent. He also notes that in the book of Proverbs there is no mention of prophetic teaching with wisdom as a guide. So he emends the word to вАЬguidanceвАЭ following the LXX (Proverbs [ICC], 512). The TEV has вАЬguidanceвАЭ; the NIV retains вАЬrevelation.вАЭ It must be stated that the prophetic ministry was usually in response to the calamitous periods, calling the people back to God. Without them the downward rush to anarchy and destruction would have been faster than with these prophetic calls from God.
n2002957: tn The verb вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„ҐвАО (para њ) means вАЬto let go; to let alone.вАЭ It occurs here in the Niphal with the meaning of вАЬ[the people] are let loose,вАЭ meaning, they cast off restraint (e.g., Exod 32:25). Cf. NLT вАЬrun wild.вАЭ
n2002958: sn The law here refers to scripture, the concrete form of revelation. So the two halves of the verse provide the contrast: When there is no prophetic revelation there is chaos, but those who keep the revelation contained in scripture find blessing.
n2002959: tn There is a tendency among commentators and English versions to translate вАП„Р÷Ј„©„Б÷∞„®÷µ„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО ( Њashrehu) as вАЬhappy is he!вАЭ (cf. KJV, ASV, NAB, NASB, TEV, NLT). But вАЬhappyвАЭ can be a misleading translation. The Hebrew word refers to a heavenly bliss, an inner joy, that comes from knowing one is right with God and experiencing his blessing. вАЬHappiness,вАЭ on the other hand, depends on what happens.
n2002960: sn Servants could not be corrected by mere words; they had to be treated like children for they were frequently unresponsive. This, of course, would apply to certain kinds of servants. The Greek version translated this as вАЬa stubborn servant.вАЭ
n2002961: tn The Niphal imperfect here is best rendered as a potential imperfectвАФвАЬcannot be corrected.вАЭ The second line of the verse clarifies that even though the servant understands the words, he does not respond. It will take more.
n2002962: tn Heb вАЬfor he understands, but there is no answer.вАЭ The concessive idea (вАЬalthoughвАЭ) is taken from the juxtaposition of the two parts.
n2002963: sn To say вАЬthere is no answerвАЭ means that this servant does not obeyвАФhe has to be trained in a different way.
n2002964: tn Most translations render the verse as a present tense question (вАЬDo you see?вАЭ so KJV, NASB, NIV, ESV). But the Hebrew has a perfect verb form (вАП„Ч÷Є„Ц÷і„Щ„™÷ЄвАО; khazita) without an interrogative marker. Hebrew proverbs can use the past tense to set the topic or opening premise of a proverb, and then comment on it in the second half of the proverb. English translators of proverbial sayings tend to want to make the past time verbs in Hebrew into present tense in English. But this convention is difficult with second person verb forms, so the translations tend to take the tactic of changing the nature of the sentence to interrogative or conditional. The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„Ц÷Є„ФвАО (khazah) means вАЬto look at, watch,вАЭ but is rendered to match the English lead-in expression вАЬyouвАЩve seen XвА¶.вАЭ
n2002965: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО ( Њish) is commonly translated вАЬa man,вАЭ but can in fact refer to a man or a woman. There is no indication in the immediate context that this should be limited only to males.
n2002966: sn The focus of this proverb is on someone who is hasty in his words. This is the person who does not stop to think, but acts on the spur of the moment. To speak before thinking is foolishness.
n2002967: sn Rash speech cannot easily be remedied. The prospects for a fool are better (e.g., Prov 26:12).
n2002968: tn There is no conditional particle at the beginning of the verse; however, the relationship of the clauses, which lay down the condition first and then (with a vav) the consequences, indicates a conditional construction here. Cf. also NAB, NIV, NCV, TEV.
n2002969: tn The word вАП„Ю÷Є„†„Х÷є„ЯвАО (manon) is a hapax legomenon; accordingly, it has been given a variety of interpretations. The LXX has вАЬgrief,вАЭ and this has been adopted by some versions (e.g., NIV, NCV). The idea would be that treating the servant too easily for so long would not train him at all, so he will be of little use, and therefore a grief. J. Reider takes the word to mean вАЬweaklingвАЭ from the Arabic root na Њna (вАЬto be weakвАЭ), with a noun/adjective form muna Њana Њ (вАЬweak; feebleвАЭ); see his вАЬEtymological Studies in Biblical Hebrew,вАЭ VT 4 [1954]: 276-95. This would give a different emphasis to the sentence, but on the whole not very different than the first. In both cases the servant will not be trained well. Rashi, a Jewish scholar who lived a.d. 1040-1105, had the translation вАЬa master.вАЭ The servant trained this way will assume authority in the household even as the son. This may be behind the KJV translation вАЬsonвАЭ (likewise ASV, NASB). Tg. Prov 29:21 and the Syriac have вАЬto be uprooted,вАЭ which may reflect a different text entirely.
n2002970: tn Heb вАЬa man of anger.вАЭ Here вАЬangerвАЭ is an attributive (вАЬan angry manвАЭ). This expression describes one given to or characterized by anger, not merely temporarily angry. The same is true of the next description.
n2002971: tn Heb вАЬpossessor of wrath.вАЭ Here вАЬwrathвАЭ is an attributive (cf. ASV вАЬa wrathful manвАЭ; KJV вАЬa furious manвАЭ).
n2002972: tn Heb вАЬan abundance of transgression.вАЭ The phrase means вАЬabounding in transgressionвАЭ (BDB 913 s.v. вАП„®÷Ј„СвАО 1.d]). Not only does the angry person stir up dissension, but he also frequently causes sin in himself and in others (e.g., 14:17, 29; 15:18; 16:32; 22:24).
n2002973: tn Heb вАЬpride of a man,вАЭ with вАЬmanвАЭ functioning as a possessive. There is no indication in the immediate context that this is restricted only to males.
n2002974: tn There is a wordplay here due to the repetition of the root вАП„©„Б÷Є„§÷µ„ЬвАО (shafel). In the first line the verb вАП„™÷Љ÷і„©„Б÷∞„§÷Љ÷і„Щ„Ь÷ґ„†÷Љ„Х÷ЉвАО (tishpilennu) is the Hiphil imperfect of the root, rendered вАЬwill bring him low.вАЭ In the second line the word is used in the description of the вАЬlowly of spirit,вАЭ вАП„©„Б÷∞„§÷Ј„Ь÷Њ„®„Х÷Љ„Ч÷ЈвАО (shefal ruakh). The contrast works well: The proud will be brought вАЬlow,вАЭ but the one who is вАЬlowlyвАЭ will be honored. In this instance the wordplay can be preserved in the translation.
n2002975:
tn Heb вАЬlow in spiritвАЭ; KJV вАЬhumble in spirit.вАЭ This refers to an attitude of humility.
sn The Hebrew word translated вАЬlowlyвАЭ forms an implied comparison: To be humble is like being low, base, earthbound; whereas pride is often compared to being high, loftyвАФat least in oneвАЩs own eyes.
n2002976: sn The expression shares with a thief describes someone who is an вАЬaccompliceвАЭ (cf. NAB, NIV) because he is willing to share in the loot without taking part in the crime.
n2002977: tn Heb вАЬhates his soul.вАЭ The accomplice is working against himself, for he will be punished along with the thief if he is caught.
n2002978:
tn Heb вАЬoathвАЭ or вАЬimprecationвАЭ; ASV вАЬadjuration.вАЭ This amounted to an вАЬoathвАЭ or вАЬcurseвАЭ (cf. NAB вАЬhe hears himself put under a curseвАЭ; NRSV вАЬone hears the victimвАЩs curseвАЭ) either by or on behalf of the victim, that any witness to the crime must testify (cf. Lev 5:1). However, in this legal setting referring to вАЬa victimвАЩs curseвАЭ could be misleading (cf. also KJV вАЬhe heareth cursingвАЭ), since it could be understood to refer to profanity directed against those guilty of the crime rather than an imprecation called down on a witness who refused to testify (as in the present proverb). The present translation specifies this as an вАЬoath to testify.вАЭ
sn The oath to testify was not an oath to tell the truth before a court of law in the modern sense. Instead it was a вАЬcurseвАЭ or вАЬimprecationвАЭ expressed by the victim of the theft, or by the legal authorities, called down on any witness of the crime who kept silent or refused to testify (as here). According to Lev 5:1, if a witness does not speak up he is accountable for the crime. This person hears the adjuration, but if he speaks up he is condemned, and if he does not speak up he is guilty under the law. The proverb is an unusual one; it seems to be warning against getting mixed up in any way with the thief, for it will create a serious ethical dilemma.
n2002979: tn Heb вАЬthe fear of man.вАЭ This uses an objective genitive to describe a situation where fearing what people might do or think controls oneвАЩs life. There is no indication in the immediate context that this should be limited only to males, so the translation uses the more generic вАЬpeopleвАЭ here.
n2002980: tn Heb вАЬgives [or yields, or produces]вАЭ; NIV вАЬwill prove to be.вАЭ
n2002981: sn вАЬSnareвАЭ is an implied comparison; fearing people is like being in a trapвАФthere is no freedom of movement or sense of security.
n2002982: sn The image of being set on high comes from the military experience of finding a defensible position, a place of safety and security, such as a high wall or a mountain. Trusting in the Lord sets people free and gives them a sense of safety and security (e.g., Prov 10:27; 12:2).
n2002983: sn The idiom seek the face means to try to obtain favor from someone. According to the proverb, many people assume that true justice depends on the disposition of some earthly ruler.
n2002984: tn Heb вАЬbut from the Lord [is] justice of a man.вАЭ The last part uses the construct state followed by the genitive, which here shows the advantageвАФit is justice for the person. The implication of the matter is that people should seek the LordвАЩs favor (rather than a human rulerвАЩs) if they want true justice.
n2002985:
tn Heb вАЬwho is upright in the wayвАЭ (so NASB; KJV and ASV are similar). Here вАЬin the wayвАЭ refers to the course of a personвАЩs life, hence вАЬwho lives an upright life.вАЭ Cf. NAB вАЬhe who walks uprightly.вАЭ
sn The proverb makes a simple observation on life: The righteous detest the wicked, and the wicked detest the lifestyle of the righteous. Each is troublesome to the beliefs and the activities of the other.
n200301: sn This chapter has a title (30:1), AgurвАЩs confession and petition (30:2-9), and a series of AgurвАЩs admonitions (30:10-33).
n200302: tn The title вАП„Ф÷Ј„Ю÷Ј÷Љ„©÷Є÷Љ„В„РвАО (hammassa Њ) means вАЬthe burden,вАЭ a frequently used title in prophetic oracles. It may be that the word is a place name, although it is more likely that it describes what follows as an important revelation.
n200303: tn The definite article is used here as a demonstrative, clarifying the reference to Agur.
n200304: sn The word translated вАЬsaysвАЭ (вАП„†÷∞„Р÷ї„ЭвАО, ne Њum) is a verbal noun; it is also a term that describes an oracle. It is usually followed by the subjective genitive: вАЬthe oracle of this man to Ithiel.вАЭ
n200305: tn There have been numerous attempts to reinterpret the first two verses of the chapter. The Greek version translated the names вАЬIthielвАЭ and вАЬUkal,вАЭ resulting in вАЬI am weary, O God, I am weary and faintвАЭ (C. C. Torrey, вАЬProverbs Chapter 30,вАЭ JBL 73 [1954]: 93-96). The LXXвАЩs approach is followed by some English versions (e.g., NRSV, NLT). The Midrash tried through a clever etymologizing translation to attribute the works to Solomon (explained by W. G. Plaut, Proverbs, 299). It is most likely that someone other than Solomon wrote these sayings; they have a different, almost non-proverbial, tone to them. See P. Franklyn, вАЬThe Sayings of Agur in Proverbs 30: Piety or Skepticism,вАЭ ZAW 95 (1983): 239-52.
n200306: tn The particle вАП„Ы÷Љ÷і„ЩвАО (ki) functions in an asseverative sense, вАЬsurely; indeed; trulyвАЭ (R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 73, ¬І449).
n200307: tn The noun вАП„С÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„®вАО (ba њar) means вАЬbrutishnessвАЭ; here it functions as a predicate adjective. It is followed by вАП„Ю÷µ„Р÷і„Щ„©„БвАО (me Њish) expressing comparative degree: вАЬmore than a manвАЭ or вАЬmore than any man,вАЭ with вАЬmanвАЭ used in a generic sense. He is saying that he has fallen beneath the level of mankind. Cf. NRSV вАЬI am too stupid to be human.вАЭ
n200308: tn Heb вАЬthan man.вАЭ The verse is using hyperbole; this individual feels as if he has no intelligence at all, that he is more brutish than any other human. Of course this is not true, or he would not be able to speculate on the God of the universe at all. On the other hand he may simply be employing sarcasm. He says what others think, knowing that it is not true.
n200309: tn Heb вАЬthe understanding of a man,вАЭ with вАЬmanвАЭ used attributively here.
n2003010: tn The verb вАП„Р÷µ„У÷Є„ҐвАО ( Њeda њ) is the imperfect form of the stative verb вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Є„ҐвАО (yada њ) вАЬto know.вАЭ The imperfect form of a stative verb should be understood as future or modal and is translated here as an abilitive modal. By using a perfect verb for past time in the first half and in imperfect form in the second half, the verb is strongly negative, denying both learning in the past and the possibility of learning in the future.
n2003011: sn The construction uses repetition to make the point emphatically: вАЬI do not know the knowledge of the Holy One.вАЭ AgurвАЩs claim to being вАЬbrutishвАЭ is here clarifiedвАФhe is not one of those who has knowledge or understanding of God. C. H. Toy thinks the speaker is being sarcastic in reference to others who may have claimed such knowledge (Proverbs [ICC], 521).
n2003012: tn The epithet вАЬthe Holy OneвАЭ is the adjective вАЬholyвАЭ put in the masculine plural (as in 9:10). This will harmonize with the plural of majesty used to explain the plural with titles for God. However, NRSV takes the plural as a reference to the вАЬholy ones,вАЭ presumably referring to angelic beings.
n2003013: sn To make his point Agur includes five questions. These, like Job 38-41, or Proverbs 8:24-29, focus on the divine acts to show that it is absurd for a mere mortal to think that he can explain GodвАЩs work or compare himself to God. These questions display mankindвАЩs limitations and GodвАЩs incomparable nature. The first question could be open to include humans, but may refer to God alone (as the other questions do).
n2003014: sn The questions are filled with anthropomorphic language. The questioner is asking what humans have ever done this, but the meaning is that only God has done this. вАЬGathering the wind in his fistsвАЭ is a way of expressing absolute sovereign control over the forces of nature.
n2003015: sn The question is comparing the clouds of the heavens to garments (e.g., Job 26:8). T. T. Perowne writes, вАЬMen bind up water in skins or bottles; God binds up the rain-floods in the thin, gauzy texture of the changing clouds, which yet by his power does not rend under its burden of waters.вАЭ
n2003016: sn The ends of the earth is an expression often used in scripture as a metonymy of subject referring to the people who live in the ends of the earth, the far off and remote lands and islands. While that is possible here as well, this may simply be a synecdoche saying that God created the whole world, even the most remote and distant places.
n2003017: sn The reference to вАЬsonвАЭ in this passage has prompted many suggestions down through the years: It was identified as Israel in the Jewish Midrashim, the Logos or demiurge by some of the philosophers and allegorical writers, as simple poetic parallelism without a separate identity by some critical scholars, and as Jesus by Christian commentators. Parallels with Ugaritic are interesting because Baal is referred to as a son, but that is bound up within the pantheon where there was a father god. Some of the Jewish commentators exhibit a strange logic in expressing what Christians would say is only their blindness to the full revelation: There is little cogency in this being a reference to Jesus because if there had been such a person at any time in the past he would have left some tradition about it through his descendants (J. H. Greenstone, Proverbs, 317). But Judaism has taught from the earliest times that Messiah was preexistent (especially in view of Micah 5 and Daniel 7); and the claims of Jesus in the Gospels bear this out. It seems best to say that there is a hint here of the nature of the Messiah as Son, a hint that will later be revealed in full through the incarnation.
n2003018: tn The particle вАП„Ы÷і÷Љ„ЩвАО (ki) functions in an asseverative sense, вАЬsurely; indeed; trulyвАЭ (R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 73, ¬І449). The statement appears sarcastic, suggesting that verses 2-3 are also sarcastic.
n2003019: tn The verb вАП„™÷µ„У÷Є„ҐвАО (teda њ) is the imperfect form of the stative verb вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Є„ҐвАО (yada њ) вАЬto know.вАЭ The imperfect form of a stative verb should be understood as future or modal and is translated here as an abilitive modal.
n2003020: sn The text here uses an implied comparison (a figure of speech known as hypocatastasis): It compares the perfection of every word from God with some precious metal that has been refined and purified (e.g., Ps 12:6). The point is that GodвАЩs word is trustworthy; it has no defects and flaws, nothing false or misleading. The second half of the verse explains the significance of this pointвАФit is safe to trust the Lord.
n2003021: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2003022: sn The line uses two more figures of speech to declare that God can be trusted for security and salvation. вАЬShieldвАЭ is a simple metaphorвАФGod protects. вАЬTake refugeвАЭ is another implied comparison (hypocatastasis)вАФGod provides spiritual rest and security for those who put their trust in him.
n2003023: tn The form of the verb is a Niphal perfect tense with a vav consecutive from the root вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ц÷Ј„СвАО (kazav, вАЬto lieвАЭ). In this stem it has the ideas of вАЬbeen made deceptive,вАЭ or вАЬshown to be falseвАЭ or вАЬproved to be a liar.вАЭ One who adds to or changes the word of the Lord will be seen as a liar.
n2003024: sn Wisdom literature often groups things in twos and fours, or in other numerical arrangements (e.g., Amos 1:3-2:6; Job 5:19; Prov 6:16-19).
n2003025: tn Assuming that the contents of vv. 7-9 are a prayer, several English versions have supplied a vocative phrase: вАЬO LordвАЭ (NIV); вАЬO GodвАЭ (NLT); others have supplied a similar phrase without the vocative вАЬOвАЭ: NCV, CEV вАЬLordвАЭ; TEV вАЬGod.вАЭ
n2003026: tn The two words might form a hendiadys: вАЬfalsehood and liesвАЭ being equivalent to вАЬcomplete deception.вАЭ The word вАП„©„Б÷Є„Х÷∞„РвАО (shav Њ) means вАЬfalse; empty; vain; to a false purpose.вАЭ The second word means вАЬword of lying,вАЭ thus вАЬa lying word.вАЭ Taken separately they might refer to false intentions and false words.
n2003027: tn The word вАП„Ч÷є„ІвАО (khoq) means вАЬstatuteвАЭ; it is also used of a definite assignment in labor (Exod 5:14; Prov 31:15), or of a set portion of food (Gen 47:22). Here it refers to food that is the proper proportion for the speaker.
n2003028: sn Agur requested an honest life (not deceitful) and a balanced life (not self-sufficient). The second request about his provision is clarified in v. 9.
n2003029: tn The verb вАП„Ы÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷Ј„©„БвАО (kakhash) means вАЬto be disappointing; to deceive; to fail; to grow lean.вАЭ In the Piel stem it means вАЬto deceive; to act deceptively; to cringe; to disappoint.вАЭ The idea of acting deceptively is illustrated in Hos 9:2 where it has the connotation of вАЬdisowningвАЭ or вАЬrefusing to acknowledgeвАЭ (a meaning very close to its meaning here).
n2003030: tn The Hebrew verb literally means вАЬto take hold of; to seizeвАЭ; this produces the idea of doing violence to the reputation of God.
n2003031: tn The form вАП„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ь÷∞„©„Б÷µ„ЯвАО (talshen) is the Hiphil jussive (with the negative вАП„Р÷Ј„ЬвАО,  Њal); it is a denominative verb from the noun вАЬtongueвАЭ (Heb вАЬwag the tongueвАЭ). It means вАЬto defame; to slander,вАЭ if the accusation is untrue. Some have suggested that the word might have the force of вАЬdenouncingвАЭ a slave to his master, accusing him before authorities (e.g., Deut 23:15-16). This proverb would then be a warning against meddling in the affairs of someone else.
n2003032: tn If what was said were true, then there would be no culpability. But the implication here is that it was slander. And the effect of that will be a curseвАФthe person who is the target of the slander will вАЬcurseвАЭ the person who slandered him (вАП„І÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО [qalal] in the Piel means вАЬto treat lightly [or, with contempt]; to curseвАЭ), and culpability will result (the verb вАП„Р÷Є„©„Б÷Ј„ЭвАО [ Њasham] means вАЬto be guilty; to make a guilt offering [or, reparation offering]вАЭ). This word for guilt suggests a connection to the Levitical teaching that the guilty had to make reparation for damages done (Lev 5). Cf. NAB вАЬyou will have to pay the penaltyвАЭ; NIV, NLT вАЬyou will pay for it.вАЭ
n2003033: sn The next four verses all start with the Hebrew expression translated вАЬThere is a generation.вАЭ This is a series of denunciations of things that are dangerous in society without mentioning specific punishments or proscriptions. The word вАЬgenerationвАЭ as used in this passage refers to a class or group of people.
n2003034: sn The first observation is that there is a segment in society that lacks respect for parents. This uses the antonyms вАЬcurseвАЭ and [not] вАЬblessвАЭ to make the point. To вАЬcurseвАЭ a parent could include treating them lightly, defaming them, or showing disrespect in general. To вАЬblessвАЭ would mean to honor, respect, or enrich in some way, which is what should be done (e.g., Exod 21:17; Prov 20:20).
n2003035:
tn The verb вАП„®÷Є„Ч÷Ј„•вАО (rakhats) means вАЬto wash; to wash off; to wash away; to bathe.вАЭ It is used of physical washing, ceremonial washings, and hence figuratively of removing sin and guilt through confession (e.g., Isa 1:16). Here the form is the Pual perfect (unless it is a rare old Qal passive, since there is no Piel and no apparent change of meaning from the Qal). The perfective meaning вАЬhas not been washedвАЭ focuses on the continuing result вАЬare not washed.вАЭ
sn The point of the verse is that there are people who observe outer ritual and think they are pure (вАП„Ш÷Є„Ф„Х÷є„®вАО [tahor] is the Levitical standard for entrance into the sanctuary), but who pay no attention to inner cleansing (e.g., Matt 23:27).
n2003036: sn Filthiness often refers to physical uncleanness, but here it refers to moral defilement. Zech 3:3-4 uses it metaphorically as well for the sin of the nation (e.g., Isa 36:12).
n2003037: tn Heb вАЬhow high are its eyes!вАЭ This is a use of the interrogative pronoun in exclamatory sentences (R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 25, ¬І127).
n2003038:
tn Heb вАЬits eyelids are lifted up,вАЭ a gesture indicating arrogance and contempt or disdain for others. To make this clear, the present translation supplies the adverb вАЬdisdainfullyвАЭ at the end of the verse.
sn The verbs вАЬto be highвАЭ (translated вАЬareвА¶loftyвАЭ) and вАЬto be lifted upвАЭ depict arrogance and disdain for others. The emphasis on the eyes and eyelids (parasynonyms in poetry) is employed because the glance, the look, is the immediate evidence of contempt for others (e.g., also 6:17 and Ps 131:1).
n2003039: tn The comparative вАЬlikeвАЭ does not appear in the Hebrew text, but is implied by the metaphor; it is supplied in the translation for the sake of clarity.
n2003040: sn There are two figures used in each of these lines: teeth/great teeth and вАЬswords/knives.вАЭ The term вАЬteethвАЭ is a metonymy for the process of chewing and eating. This goes with the figure of the second half of the verse that speaks about вАЬdevouringвАЭ the poorвАФso the whole image of eating and chewing refers to destroying the poor (an implied comparison). The figures of вАЬswords/knivesвАЭ are metaphors within this image. Comparing teeth to swords means that they are sharp and powerful. The imagery captures the rapacity of their power.
n2003041: tn Heb вАЬteethвАЭ (so NRSV) or вАЬjaw teethвАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NASB) or perhaps вАЬjawbone.вАЭ This is a different Hebrew word for вАЬteethвАЭ than the one in the previous line; if it refers to вАЬjaw teethвАЭ then a translation like вАЬmolarsвАЭ would be appropriate, although this image might not fit with the metaphor (вАЬlike knivesвАЭ) unless the other teeth, the incisors or front teeth, are pictured as being even longer (вАЬlike swordsвАЭ).
n2003042: tn The Hebrew form вАП„Ь÷ґ„Р÷±„Ы÷є„ЬвАО (le Њekhol) is the Qal infinitive construct; it indicates the purpose of this generationвАЩs ruthless powerвАФit is destructive. The figure is an implied comparison (known as hypocatastasis) between вАЬdevouringвАЭ and вАЬdestroying.вАЭ
n2003043: sn The next two verses describe insatiable things, things that are problematic to normal life. The meaning of v. 15a and its relationship to 15b is debated. But the вАЬleechвАЭ seems to have been selected to begin the section because it was symbolic of greedвАФit sucks blood through its two suckers. This may be what the reference to two daughters calling вАЬGive! Give!вАЭ might signify (if so, this is an implied comparison, a figure known as hypocatastasis).
n2003044: sn As one might expect, there have been various attempts to identify the вАЬtwo daughters.вАЭ In the Rabbinic literature some identified Alukah (the вАЬleechвАЭ) with Sheol, and the two daughters with paradise and hell, one claiming the righteous and the other the unrighteous; others identified Alukah with Gehenna, and the two daughters with heresy and government, neither of which is ever satisfied (Midrash Tehillim quoted by Rashi, a Jewish scholar who lived a.d. 1040-1105, and in the Talmud, b. Avodah Zarah 17a). J. J. Glueck suggests that what is in view is erotic passion (and not a leech) with its two maidens of burning desire crying for more (вАЬProverbs 30:15a, вАЭ VT 14 [1964]: 367-70). F. S. North rightly criticizes this view as gratuitous; he argues for the view of a leech with two suckers (вАЬThe Four Insatiables,вАЭ VT 15 [1965]: 281-82).
n2003045: tn The two imperatives вАП„Ф÷Ј„С „Ф÷Ј„СвАО (hav hav, вАЬgive, give,вАЭ from вАП„Щ÷Є„Ф÷Ј„СвАО, yahav) correspond to the two daughters, and form their appeal. This would then be a personificationвАФit is as if the leech is crying out, вАЬGive! Give!вАЭ
n2003046: tn This verb is a Hebrew imperfect for the future tense, while the next verb is a Hebrew perfect for the perfective. Most translations render both as present tense вАЬare satisfiedвА¶sayвАЭ (KJV, NIV, ESV, Holman, while NASB gives both as future вАЬwill not be satisfiedвА¶will not sayвАЭ). Using both the future and the past is more emphatic, these never have been and never will be satisfied.
n2003047: sn There is a noticeable rhetorical sequence here: two daughters, three things, four (see W. M. Roth, вАЬThe Numerical Sequence x / x +1 in the Old Testament,вАЭ VT 12 [1962]: 300-311, and вАЬNumerical Sayings in the Old Testament,вАЭ VT 13 [1965]: 86). W. McKane thinks the series builds to a climax with the four, and in the four the barren woman is the focal point, the other three being metaphors for her sexual desire (Proverbs [OTL], 656). This interpretation is a minority view, however, and has not won widespread support.
n2003048: tn Throughout the book of Proverbs вАП„Ф„Х÷є„ЯвАО (hon) means вАЬwealth,вАЭ but here it has the nuance of вАЬsufficiencyвАЭ (cf. TEV, CEV, NLT вАЬsatisfiedвАЭ) or вАЬenoughвАЭ (BDB 223 s.v.).
n2003049: tn The term вАП„©÷∞„Б„Р„Х÷є„ЬвАО (she Њol, вАЬSheolвАЭ) refers here to the realm of the dead: вАЬthe graveвАЭ (so KJV, NIV, NLT); cf. TEV, CEV вАЬthe world of the deadвАЭ; NAB вАЬthe nether world.вАЭ
n2003050: tn Heb вАЬthe closing of the womb,вАЭ a situation especially troubling for one who is consumed with a desire for children (e.g., Gen 16:2; 30:1).
n2003051: tn The verb вАП„©÷Є„В„С÷∞„Р÷Є„ФвАО (save Њah) means вАЬto eat/drink oneвАЩs fill; to satisfy oneself.вАЭ Most translations make it present tense (KJV, ESV, Holman, NASB, NIV). As the perfect form of a dynamic root, it should be understood as past or perfective, a condition known to have been true, which is prototypical.
n2003052: tn Most translations make the verb present tense (KJV, ESV, Holman, NASB, NIV). As the perfect form of a dynamic root, вАП„Р÷Є„Ю÷∞„®÷Є„ФвАО ( Њamerah) should be understood as past or perfective, a fact known from past experience which is prototypical.
n2003053: sn There is no clear lesson made from these observations. But one point that could be made is that greed, symbolized by the leech, is as insatiable as all these other things. If that is the case, the proverb would constitute a warning against the insatiable nature of greed.
n2003054: sn The вАЬeyeвАЭ as the organ that exhibits the inner feelings most clearly, here represents a look of scorn or disdain that speaks volumes (a metonymy of cause or of adjunct). It is comparable to the вАЬevil eyeвАЭ which is stinginess (28:22).
n2003055: tn The Hebrew word вАП„Ь÷і„Щ„І÷Љ÷≤„Ф÷Ј„™вАО (liqqahat, вАЬobeyingвАЭ) occurs only here and in Gen 49:10; it seems to mean вАЬto receiveвАЭ in the sense of вАЬreceiving instructionвАЭ or вАЬobeying.вАЭ C. H. Toy suggests emending to вАЬto old ageвАЭ (вАП„Ь÷∞„Ц÷і„І÷∞„†÷Ј„™вАО, leziqnat) of the mother (Proverbs [ICC], 530). The LXX with ќ≥бњЖѕБќ±ѕВ (gƒУras, вАЬold ageвАЭ) suggests that a root lhq had something to do with вАЬwhite hair.вАЭ D. W. Thomas suggests emending from lhyqt to lyqht; it would have read, вАЬThe eye that mocks a father and despises an aged motherвАЭ (вАЬA Note on вАП„Ь÷і„Щ„І÷Љ÷≤„Ф÷Ј„™вАО in Proverbs 30:17, вАЭ JTS 42 [1941]: 154-55); this is followed by NAB вАЬor scorns an aged mother.вАЭ
n2003056: sn The sternest punishment is for the evil eye. The punishment is talionicвАФeye for eye. The reference to вАЬthe valleyвАЭ may indicate a place where people are not be given decent burials and the birds of prey pick the corpses clean. It is an image the prophets use in judgment passages.
n2003057: tn The form вАП„†÷і„§÷∞„Ь÷∞„Р„Х÷ЉвАО (nifle Њu) is the Niphal perfect from вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„Ь÷Є„РвАО (pala Њ); the verb means вАЬto be wonderful; to be extraordinary; to be surpassingвАЭ; cf. NIV вАЬtoo amazing.вАЭ The things mentioned are things that the sage finds incomprehensible (e.g., Gen 18:14; Judg 13:18; Ps 139:6; Isa 9:6 [5]). The sage can only admire these wondersвАФhe is at a loss to explain them.
n2003058: sn It is difficult to know for certain what these four things had in common for the sage. They are all linked by the word вАЬwayвАЭ (meaning вАЬa course of actionвАЭ) and by a sense of mystery in each area. Suggestions for the connections between the four include: (1) all four things are hidden from continued observation, for they are in majestic form and then gone; (2) they all have a mysterious means of propulsion or motivation; (3) they all describe the movement of one thing within the sphere or domain of another; or (4) the first three serve as illustrations of the fourth and greatest wonder, which concerns human relationships and is slightly different than the first three.
n2003059: tn This last item in the series is the most difficult to understand. The MT reads вАП„Х÷∞„У÷Љ÷ґ„®÷ґ„Ъ÷∞ „Т÷Љ÷ґ„С÷ґ„® „С÷Љ÷∞„Ґ÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (vederekh gever be Њalmah, вАЬand the way of a man with a maid,вАЭ so KJV, NASB). The last term does not in and of itself mean вАЬvirginвАЭ but rather describes a young woman who is sexually ready for marriage. What is probably in view here is the wonder of human sexuality, for the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet) in this sequence indicates that the вАЬway of the manвАЭ is вАЬwithвАЭ the woman. This mystery might begin with the manner of obtaining the love of the young woman, but focuses on the most intimate part of human relationships. And all of this was amazing to the sage. All of it is part of GodвАЩs creative plan and therefore can be enjoyed and appreciated without fully comprehending it.
n2003060: sn Equally amazing is the insensitivity of the adulterous woman to the sin. The use of the word вАЬwayвАЭ clearly connects this and the preceding material. Its presence here also supports the interpretation of the final clause in v. 19 as referring to sexual intimacy. While that is a wonder of GodвАЩs creation, so is the way that human nature has distorted it and ruined it.
n2003061: sn The word clearly indicates that the woman is married and unchaste, but the text describes her as amoral as much as immoralвАФshe sees nothing wrong with what she does.
n2003062: sn The acts of вАЬeatingвАЭ and вАЬwiping her mouthвАЭ are euphemistic; these acts of having a meal stand in as a metaphor for sexual activity (e.g., Prov 9:17).
n2003063: sn This is the amazing part of the observation. It is one thing to sin, for everyone sins, but to dismiss the act of adultery so easily, as if it were no more significant than a meal, is incredibly brazen.
n2003064: sn The Hebrew verb means вАЬto rage; to quake; to be in tumult.вАЭ The sage is using humorous and satirical hyperbole to say that the changes described in the following verses shake up the whole order of life. The sayings assume that the new, elevated status of the individuals was not accompanied by a change in nature. For example, it was not completely unknown in the ancient world for a servant to become king, and in the process begin to behave like a king.
n2003065: sn A servant coming to power could become a tyrant if he is unaccustomed to the use of such power, or he might retain the attitude of a servant and be useless as a leader.
n2003066:
tn Heb вАЬfilled with foodвАЭ (so ASV); NASB вАЬsatisfied with foodвАЭ; NAB, NRSV вАЬglutted with foodвАЭ; CEV вАЬwho eats too muchвАЭ; NLT вАЬwho prospers.вАЭ
sn The expression stuffed with food probably represents prosperity in general. So the line portrays someone who suddenly comes into wealth, but continues to be boorish and irreligious.
n2003067: tn The Hebrew word is a feminine passive participle from вАП„©„В÷Є„†÷µ„РвАО (sane Њ), вАЬto hate.вАЭ The verb can mean вАЬto rejectвАЭ or вАЬto dislike, disregardвАЭ on various levels. Based on cognate use some have speculated that she might be odious (cf. KJV, ASV, NAB) or unattractive. An ancient Jewish Aramaic cognate uses this root to refer to divorce (Hoftijzer, DISO 311). In contrast to вАЬloved/preferred,вАЭ вАП„©„В÷Є„†÷µ„РвАО (sane Њ) can be relative вАЬless beloved, neglected, scorned,вАЭ (Jenni, TLOT, 1278). The passive participle is used this way in situations where there are two wives (e.g., Gen 29:31, 33, of JacobвАЩs reaction to Leah, and Deut 21:15). Part of the difficulty in understanding this proverb is that the main verb is a Hebrew imperfect, and like all the verbs in this series refers to what she does, in this case вАЬbecomes married,вАЭ after having the status, in this case being вАП„©„В÷∞„†„Х÷Љ„Р÷Є„ФвАО (senu Њah) вАЬunloved, hateful.вАЭ The passive participle more often refers to having received or bearing the imprint of the action, so perhaps she could be a rejected or unpreferred woman heading into an arranged marriage. Yet the problem behavior in each of the other parts of the proverb belongs to person who is the subject of the verb, i.e., what the servant, fool, and female servant do or are implied to do. The parallels do not direct us to see the вАЬunlovedвАЭ woman as a victim in this marriage but as a cause of difficulty. The LXX inferred a contrast with the husband translating вАЬwhen a hateful woman obtains a good husband.вАЭ Here it is taken to mean she has some hateful quality (e.g. being contentious, controlling, selfish).
n2003068: tn The verb вАП„Щ÷Є„®÷Ј„©„БвАО (yarash) means either (1) вАЬto possess; to inheritвАЭ or (2) вАЬto dispossess.вАЭ Often the process of possessing meant the dispossessing of those already there (e.g., Hagar and Sarah in Gen 16:5; 21:10); another example is the IsraelitesвАЩ wars against the Canaanites.
n2003069: tn Heb вАЬFour are the small things of the earth.вАЭ TEV has вАЬfour animals,вАЭ though in the list of four that follows, two are insects and one is a reptile.
n2003070: tn The construction uses the Pual participle with the plural adjective as an intensive; these four creatures are the very embodiment of wisdom (BDB 314 s.v. вАП„Ч÷Є„Ы÷Ј„ЭвАО Pu).
n2003071: sn The wisdom of the ants is found in their diligent preparation (вАП„Ы÷Љ„Х÷Љ„ЯвАО, kun) of food supplies in the summer for times in the winter when food is scarce. See S. P. Toperoff, вАЬThe Ant in the Bible and Midrash,вАЭ Dor le Dor 13 (1985): 179-83. According to this, being prepared ahead of time is a mark of true wisdom.
n2003072:
tn Or вАЬhyraxes.вАЭ This is the Syrian Hyrax, also known as the rock badger. KJV, ASV has вАЬconiesвАЭ (alternately spelled вАЬconeysвАЭ by NIV), a term usually associated with the European rabbit (Oryctolagus cuniculus) but which can also refer to the pika or the hyrax. Scholars today generally agree that the Hebrew term used here refers to a type of hyrax, a small ungulate mammal of the family Procaviidae native to Africa and the Middle East which has a thick body, short legs and ears and a rudimentary tail. The simple вАЬbadgerвАЭ (so NASB, NRSV, CEV) could lead to confusion with the badger, an entirely unrelated species of burrowing mammal related to weasels.
sn Modern scholars identify this creature with the rock badger (the Syrian hyrax), a small mammal that lives in the crevices of the rock. Its wisdom consists in its ingenuity to find a place of security.
n2003073: sn The Hebrew term means вАЬdividedвАЭ; they go forward in orderly divisions, or ranks (C. H. Toy, Proverbs [ICC], 535). Joel 1:4 describes their order and uses it as a picture of a coming invasion (e.g., Joel 2:7, 8). Therefore the wisdom of the locust is in their order and cooperation.
n2003074:
tn The KJV appears to have followed Rashi in translating this term as вАЬspider,вАЭ so also JPS (and the note in the NRSV). But almost all modern English versions and commentators, following the Greek and the Latin versions, have вАЬlizard.вАЭ See HALOT 1338 s.v. вАП„©÷∞„В„Ю÷Є„Ю÷і„Щ„™вАО.
sn The point of this saying is that a weak creature like a lizard, that is so easily caught, cannot be prevented from getting into the most significant places.
n2003075: tn Although the Hebrew noun translated вАЬkingвАЭ is singular here, it is traditionally translated as plural: вАЬkingsвАЩ palacesвАЭ (so KJV, NASB, NIV, NRSV).
n2003076: tn The form вАП„Ю÷µ„Щ„Ш÷і„Щ„С÷µ„ЩвАО (metive) is the Hiphil participle, plural construct. It has the idea of вАЬdoing good [in] their step.вАЭ They move about well, i.e., magnificently. The genitive would be a genitive of specification.
n2003077: tn The construction uses the Hiphil participle again (as in the previous line) followed by the infinitive construct of вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„Ъ÷∞вАО (halakh). This forms a verbal hendiadys, the infinitive becoming the main verb and the participle before it the adverb.
n2003078: tn Heb вАЬmighty among the beasts,вАЭ but referring to a superlative degree (вАЬmightiestвАЭ).
n2003079: tn The Hebrew term вАП„Ц÷Ј„®÷∞„Ц÷і„Щ„®вАО (zarzir) means вАЬgirtвАЭ; it occurs only here with вАЬloinsвАЭ in the Bible: вАЬthat which is girt in the loinsвАЭ (BDB 267 s.v.). Some have interpreted this to be the вАЬgreyhoundвАЭ because it is narrow in the flanks (J. H. Greenstone, Proverbs, 327); so KJV, ASV. Others have suggested the warhorse, zebra, raven, or starling. Tg. Prov 30:31 has it as the large fighting cock that struts around among the hens. There is no clear referent that is convincing, although most modern English versions use вАЬstrutting roosterвАЭ or something similar (cf. CEV вАЬproud roostersвАЭ).
n2003080: tc This last line has inspired many suggestions. The MT has вАЬwith his army around himвАЭ (вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷∞„І„Х÷Љ„Э „Ґ÷і„Ю÷Љ„Х÷євАО,  Њalqum  њimmo); so NIV. This has been emended to read вАЬagainst whom there is no rising upвАЭ (so KJV, ASV) or вАЬstanding over his people.вАЭ The LXX has вАЬa king haranguing his people.вАЭ Tg. Prov 30:31 has, вАЬa king who stands up before his people and addresses them.вАЭ Some have attempted to identify this with Alcimus, the high priest who aspired to kingship (1 Macc 7:5-22), but such a suggestion is quite remote. Another interpretation sees the word for вАЬGodвАЭ in the line: вАЬa king with whom God is.вАЭ Furthermore, C. H. Toy thinks the text is defective and must have at one time referred to some majestic animal (Proverbs [ICC], 537). While all these suggestions are fascinating, they have not improved or corrected the Hebrew text. At least one can say the focus is on the stately appearance of the king at some auspicious moment. The word occurs only here, but if it is interpreted with its Arabic cognate in mind, then it refers to a band of soldiers (BDB 39 s.v. вАП„Р÷Ј„Ь÷∞„І„Х÷Љ„ЭвАО).
n2003081: tn The construction has the вАП„СвАО (bet) preposition with the Hitpael infinitive construct, forming a temporal clause. This clause explains the way in which the person has acted foolishly.
n2003082: tn Heb вАЬhand to mouth.вАЭ This expression means вАЬput your hand to your mouthвАЭ (e.g., Job 40:4, 5); cf. NIV вАЬclap your hand over.вАЭ
n2003083: tn This line provides the explanation for the instruction to keep silent in the previous verse. It uses two images to make the point, and in so doing repeats two words throughout. The first is the word вАП„Ю÷і„Щ„•вАО (mits), which is translated (in sequence) вАЬchurning,вАЭ вАЬpunching,вАЭ and вАЬstirring up.вАЭ The form is a noun, and BDB 568 s.v. suggests translating it as вАЬsqueezingвАЭ in all three places, even in the last where it describes the pressure or the insistence on strife. This noun occurs only here. The second repeated word, the verb вАП„Щ„Х÷є„¶÷і„Щ„РвАО (yotsi Њ), also occurs three times; it is the Hiphil imperfect, meaning вАЬproducesвАЭ (i.e., causes to go out).
n2003084: sn There is a subtle wordplay here with the word for anger: It is related to the word for nose in the preceding colon.
n2003085: sn The analogy indicates that continuously pressing certain things will yield results, some good, some bad. So pressing anger produces strife. The proverb advises people to strive for peace and harmony through humility and righteousness. To do that will require вАЬletting upвАЭ on anger.
n200311: sn Nothing else is known about King Lemuel aside from this mention in the book of Proverbs. Jewish legend identifies him as Solomon, making this advice from his mother Bathsheba, but there is no evidence for that. The passage is the only direct address to a king in the book of ProverbsвАФsomething that was the norm in wisdom literature of the ancient world (Leah L. Brunner, вАЬKing and Commoner in Proverbs and Near Eastern Sources,вАЭ Dor le Dor 10 [1982]: 210-19; Brunner argues that the advice is religious and not secular).
n200312: tn Some English versions take the Hebrew noun translated вАЬoracleвАЭ here as a place name specifying the kingdom of King Lemuel; cf. NAB вАЬking of MassaвАЭ; CEV вАЬKing Lemuel of Massa.вАЭ
n200313: tn The form вАП„Ю÷Ј„ФвАО (mah), normally the interrogative вАЬwhat?вАЭ (so KJV, ASV, NAB, NASB) is best interpreted here as an exclamation. Tg. Prov 31:2 has вАЬWoe!вАЭ
n200314: tn In all three occurrences in this verse the word вАЬsonвАЭ has the Aramaic spelling, вАП„С÷Ј„®вАО (bar), rather than the Hebrew вАП„С÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (ben). The repetition of the word вАЬsonвАЭ shows the seriousness of the warning; and the expression вАЬson of my wombвАЭ and вАЬson of my vowsвАЭ are endearing epithets to show the great investment she has made in his religious place in GodвАЩs program. For a view that вАЬson of my wombвАЭ should be вАЬmy own son,вАЭ see F. Deist, вАЬProverbs 31:1, A Case of Constant Mistranslation,вАЭ JNSL 6 (1978): 1-3; cf. TEV вАЬmy own dear son.вАЭ
n200315: sn The word translated вАЬstrengthвАЭ refers to physical powers here, i.e., вАЬvigorвАЭ (so NAB) or вАЬstamina.вАЭ It is therefore a metonymy of cause; the effect would be what spending this strength meantвАФsexual involvement with women. It would be easy for a king to spend his energy enjoying women, but that would be unwise.
n200316: sn The word вАЬwaysвАЭ may in general refer to the heartвАЩs affection for or attention to, or it may more specifically refer to sexual intercourse. While in the book of Proverbs the term is an idiom for the course of life, in this context it must refer to the energy spent in this activity.
n200317: tn The construction uses Qal infinitive construct вАП„Ь÷Ј„Ю÷∞„Ч„Х÷є„™вАО (lamekhot, вАЬto wipe out; to blot out; to destroyвАЭ). The construction is somewhat strange, and so some interpreters suggest changing it to вАП„Ю÷є„Ч„Х÷є„™вАО (mokhot, вАЬdestroyers of kingsвАЭ); cf. BDB 562 s.v. вАП„Ю÷Є„Ч÷Є„ФвАО Qal.3. Commentators note that the form is close to an Aramaic word that means вАЬconcubine,вАЭ and an Arabic word that is an indelicate description for women.
n200319: sn This second warning for kings concerns the use of alcohol. If this passage is meant to prohibit any use of alcohol by kings, it would be unheard of in any ancient royal court. What is probably meant is an excessive and unwarranted use of alcohol, or a troubling need for it, so that the meaning is вАЬto drink wine in excessвАЭ (cf. NLT вАЬto guzzle wineвАЭ; CEV вАЬshould not get drunkвАЭ). The danger, of course, would be that excessive use of alcohol would cloud the mind and deprive a king of true administrative ability and justice.
n2003110:
tn The MT has вАП„Р÷µ„ХвАО ( Њev), a Kethib/Qere reading. The Kethib is вАП„Р„Х÷євАО ( Њo) but the Qere is вАП„Р÷µ„ЩвАО ( Њe). Some follow the Qere and take the word as a shortened form of вАП„Х÷є÷Ј„Щ÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО, вАЬwhere?вАЭ This would mean the ruler would be always asking for drink (cf. ASV). Others reconstruct to вАП„Р÷Ј„Х÷Љ÷µ„ФвАО ( Њavveh, вАЬto desire; to craveвАЭ). In either case, the verse would be saying that a king is not to be wanting/seeking alcohol.
tn Here вАЬstrong drinkвАЭ probably refers to barley beer (cf. NIV, NCV вАЬbeerвАЭ).
n2003111: tn The verb means вАЬchange,вАЭ perhaps expressed in reversing decisions or removing rights.
n2003112: tn Heb вАЬall the children of poverty.вАЭ This expression refers to the poor by nature. Cf. KJV, NASB, NRSV вАЬthe afflictedвАЭ; NIV вАЬoppressed.вАЭ
n2003113: sn The word is вАП„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (din, вАЬjudgmentвАЭ; so KJV). In this passage it refers to the cause or the plea for justice, i.e., the вАЬlegal rights.вАЭ
n2003114: sn Wine and beer should be given to those distressed and dying in order to ease their suffering and help them forget.
n2003115: tn Heb вАЬto the bitter of soul.вАЭ The phrase вАП„Ь÷∞„Ю÷Є„®÷µ„Щ „†÷Є„§÷ґ„©„БвАО (lemare nafesh) has been translated вАЬof heavy heartsвАЭ (KJV); вАЬin anguishвАЭ (NIV); вАЬin miseryвАЭ (TEV); вАЬin bitter distressвАЭ (NRSV); вАЬsorely depressedвАЭ (NAB); вАЬin deep depression (NLT); вАЬhave lost all hopeвАЭ (CEV). The word вАЬbitterвАЭ (вАП„Ю÷Ј„®вАО, mar) describes the physical and mental/spiritual suffering as a result of affliction, grief, or sufferingвАФthese people are in emotional pain. So the idea of вАЬbitterly distressedвАЭ works as well as any other translation.
n2003116: tn The subjects and suffixes are singular (cf. KJV, ASV, NASB). Most other English versions render this as plural for stylistic reasons, in light of the preceding context.
n2003117: tn The king was not to вАЬdrink and forgetвАЭ; the suffering are to вАЬdrink and forget.вАЭ
n2003118: sn The instruction to вАЬopen your mouthвАЭ is a metonymy of cause; it means вАЬspeak up forвАЭ (so NIV, TEV, NLT) or in this context вАЬserve as an advocate in judgmentвАЭ (cf. CEV вАЬyou must defendвАЭ).
n2003119: sn The instruction compares people who cannot defend themselves in court with those who are physically unable to speak (this is a figure of speech known as hypocatastasis, an implied comparison). The former can physically speak, but because they are the poor, the uneducated, the oppressed, they are unable to conduct a legal defense. They may as well be speechless.
n2003120: tn Or вАЬof all the defenseless.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ч÷≤„Ь„Х÷є„£вАО (khalof) means вАЬpassing away; vanishingвАЭ (properly an infinitive); in this construction вАЬthe sons of the passing awayвАЭ means people who by nature are transitory, people who are dyingвАФmortals. But in this context it would indicate people who are вАЬdefenselessвАЭ as opposed to those who are healthy and powerful.
n2003121: tn The noun вАП„¶÷ґ„У÷ґ„ІвАО (tsedeq) serves here as an adverbial accusative of manner. The decisions reached (вАП„©„Б÷∞„§÷Є„ШвАО, shefat) in this advocacy must conform to the standard of the law. So it is a little stronger than вАЬjudging fairlyвАЭ (cf. NIV, NCV), although it will be fair if it is done righteously for all.
n2003122: sn Previously the noun вАП„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО (din, judgmentвАЭ) was used, signifying the legal rights or the pleas of the people. Now the imperative вАП„У÷Љ÷і„Щ„ЯвАО is used. It could be translated вАЬjudge,вАЭ but in this context вАЬjudge the poorвАЭ could be misunderstood to mean вАЬcondemn.вАЭ Here advocacy is in view, and so вАЬplead the causeвАЭ is a better translation (cf. NASB, NIV, NRSV вАЬdefend the rightsвАЭ). It wasвАФand isвАФthe responsibility of the king (ruler) to champion the rights of the poor and needy, who otherwise would be ignored and oppressed. They are the ones left destitute by the cruelties and inequalities of life (e.g., 2 Sam 14:4-11; 1 Kgs 3:16-28; Pss 45:3-5; 72:4; Isa 9:6-7).
n2003123: sn The book of Proverbs comes to a close with this poem about the noble wife. A careful reading of the poem will show that it is extolling godly wisdom that is beneficial to the family and the society. Traditionally it has been interpreted as a paradigm for godly women. And while that is valid in part, there is much more here. The poem captures all the themes of wisdom that have been presented in the book and arranges them in this portrait of the ideal woman (Claudia V. Camp, Wisdom and the Feminine in the Book of Proverbs, 92-93). Any careful reading of the passage would have to conclude that if it were merely a paradigm for women what it portrays may well be out of reachвАФshe is a wealthy aristocrat who runs an estate with servants and conducts business affairs of real estate, vineyards, and merchandising, and also takes care of domestic matters and is involved with charity. Moreover, it says nothing about the womanвАЩs personal relationship with her husband, her intellectual and emotional strengths, or her religious activities (E. Jacob, вАЬSagesse et Alphabet: Pr. 31:10-31,вАЭ Hommages √† A. Dont-Sommer, 287-95). In general, it appears that the вАЬwomanвАЭ of Proverbs 31 is a symbol of all that wisdom represents. The poem, then, plays an important part in the personification of wisdom so common in the ancient Near East. But rather than deify Wisdom as the other ANE cultures did, Proverbs simply describes wisdom as a woman. Several features will stand out in the study of this passage. First, it is an alphabetic arrangement of the virtues of wisdom (an acrostic poem). Such an acrostic was a way of organizing the thoughts and making them more memorable (M. H. Lichtenstein, вАЬChiasm and Symmetry in Proverbs 31,вАЭ CBQ 44 [1982]: 202-11). Second, the passage is similar to hymns, but this one extols wisdom. A comparison with Psalm 111 will illustrate the similarities. Third, the passage has similarities with heroic literature. The vocabulary and the expressions often sound more like an ode to a champion than to a domestic scene. Putting these features together, one would conclude that Proverbs 31:10-31 is a hymn to Lady Wisdom, written in the heroic mode. Using this arrangement allows the sage to make all the lessons of wisdom in the book concrete and practical, it provides a polemic against the culture that saw women as merely decorative, and it depicts the greater heroism as moral and domestic rather than only exploits on the battlefield. The poem certainly presents a pattern for women to follow. But it also presents a pattern for men to follow as well, for this is the message of the book of Proverbs in summary.
n2003124: sn The poem begins with a rhetorical question (a figure of speech known as erotesis). This is intended to establish the point that such a noble wife is rare. As with wisdom in the book of Proverbs, she has to be found.
n2003125: tn The first word in the Hebrew text (вАП„Р÷µ„©„Б÷ґ„™вАО,  Њeshet) begins with вАП„РвАО ( Њalef), the first letter in the Hebrew alphabet. The word вАП„Р÷µ„©„Б÷ґ„™вАО, ( Њeshet) can refer to a wife or to a woman. Ruth is called an вАП„Р÷µ„©„Б÷ґ„™ „Ч÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЬвАО ( Њeshet khayil) вАЬworthy womanвАЭ while still a widow. While the term need not refer to a wife, that was certainly the most common status of the adult woman in ancient Israel and the following description portrays a woman who is both wife and mother.
n2003126: tn Heb вАЬa woman of valor.вАЭ This is the same expression used to describe Ruth (e.g., Ruth 3:11). The term вАП„Ч÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЬвАО (khayil) here means вАЬmoral worthвАЭ (BDB 298 s.v.); cf. KJV вАЬa virtuous woman.вАЭ Elsewhere the term is used of physical valor in battle, e.g., вАЬmighty man of valor,вАЭ the land-owning aristocrat who could champion the needs of his people in times of peace or war (e.g., Judg 6:12). Here the title indicates that the woman possesses all the virtues, honor, and strength to do the things that the poem will set forth.
n2003127: sn This line expresses that her value (Heb вАЬher priceвАЭ), like wisdom, is worth more than rubies (e.g., 3:15; 8:11).
n2003128: tn Heb вАЬgems.вАЭ It is not known which particular gem the term refers to or whether it means gems in a generic sense.
n2003129:
tn The first word of the second line begins with вАП„СвАО (bet), the second letter in the Hebrew alphabet.
tn The verb вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ш÷Ј„ЧвАО (batakh) means вАЬto trust; to have confidence in.вАЭ With the subject of the verb being вАЬthe heart of her husband,вАЭ the idea is strengthenedвАФhe truly trusts her. Cf. NCV вАЬtrusts her completelyвАЭ; NIV вАЬhas full confidence in her.вАЭ The verb вАП„С÷Љ÷Є„Ш÷Ј„ЧвАО (batakh) may be stative or dynamic (the evidence is inconclusive). The perfect form of a stative verb could be past tense or present tense, while a dynamic verb would be past or perfective. Given the context of past time verbs throughout the description, it is best to understand this verb as perfective, вАЬhas trusted.вАЭ
n2003130: sn The Hebrew word used here for вАЬdividendsвАЭ (вАП„©„Б÷Є„Ь÷Є„ЬвАО, shalal) usually refers to вАЬplunder, spoil,вАЭ primarily from war (e.g., Isa 8:1-4 and the name Maher Shalal Hash Baz). Here it refers to gain in a more broad sense, but a gain that has come through the work of another. Having unleashed her capabilities through his trust, her work has enriched the husband and family.
n2003131:
tn The first word of the third line begins with вАП„ТвАО (gimel), the third letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
tn As the perfect form of a dynamic root, the verb (вАП„Т÷∞÷Љ„Ю÷Є„Ь÷Ј„™÷∞„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО, gemalatehu) should be understood as past or perfective. It represents prototypical behavior whose effects continue. The verb means to вАЬrepay; reward.вАЭ This is how she has rewarded her husbandвАЩs trust.
tn The passage begins a description of the woman given in the past tense, predominantly with perfect verbs (past tense or perfective for dynamic roots) and preterite verbs (past tense). The few participles and imperfect verbs (here past habitual) derive their time frame from context and are also past time. Most translations have rendered all the descriptions of the woman in the present tense, perhaps out of the habit of changing the Hebrew past tense verbs to present tense in English in the short proverbial sayings. (Most English proverbs are in the present tense, some in the future, the fewest in the past, e.g. вАЬcuriosity killed the cat.вАЭ) The Hebrew verb forms were considered to have a present tense in proverbial sayings, but proverbial sayings do not need to be in the present tense and the understanding of the Hebrew forms has been corrected (M. Rogland, Alleged Non-Past Uses of Qatal in Classical Hebrew [Assen, Netherlands: Van Gorcum, 2003]; J. Cook, вАЬGenericity, Tense, and Verbal Patterns in the Sentence Literature of ProverbsвАЭ in Seeking Out the Wisdom of the Ancients, ed. Ronald Troxel [Winona Lake, IN: Eisenbrauns, 2005]; B. Webster вАЬThe Perfect Verb and the Perfect Woman in ProverbsвАЭ in Windows to the Ancient World of the Hebrew Bible, ed. B. Arnold, N. Erickson, J. Walton [Winona Lake, IN: Eisenbrauns, 2014]).
n2003132: sn The Bible frequently joins these two words, вАЬgoodвАЭ and вАЬevil,вАЭ (or вАЬbenefitвАЭ and вАЬharmвАЭ). They contrast the prosperity and well-being of her contribution with what would be devastating and painful. The way of wisdom is always characterized by вАЬgoodвАЭ; the way of folly is associated with вАЬevil.вАЭ
n2003133:
tn The first word of the fourth line begins with вАП„УвАО (dalet), the fourth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
tn The verb вАП„У÷Љ÷Є„®÷Ј„©„БвАО (darash) means вАЬto seek; to inquire; to investigate.вАЭ The Hebrew perfect form of a dynamic verb should be understood as past or perfective; here it is part of listing her past accomplishments. She was proactive in obtaining these products and perhaps had inspected them for quality so that she could work with them with satisfaction rather than frustration.
n2003134: tn The verb вАП„Х÷Ј„™÷Љ÷Ј„Ґ÷Ј„©„ВвАО (vata њas) is a preterite, conveying the next thing she did in a past time sequence.
n2003135: tn Or вАЬwith the pleasure of her hands.вАЭ The noun вАП„Ч÷µ„§÷ґ„•вАО (khefets) means вАЬdelight; pleasureвАЭ and the form may be either construct вАЬdelight of,вАЭ or absolute вАЬdelight.вАЭ BDB suggests it means here вАЬthat in which one takes pleasure,вАЭ i.e., a business, and translates the line вАЬin the business of her handsвАЭ (BDB 343 s.v. 4). But that translation reduces the emphasis on pleasure and could have easily been expressed in other ways. The prepositional phrase вАЬwith delightвАЭ describes the manner in which she worked. If the noun is absolute, then the second noun вАЬhandsвАЭ is an adverbial accusative of means. If вАЬdelightвАЭ is part of the construct relationship, then вАЬdelightвАЭ is first applied to вАЬhandsвАЭ (genitive of specification) and then back to the verb. In either case, she worked with her hands and in an eager or happy manner. Tg. Prov 31:13 has, вАЬshe works with her hands in accordance with her pleasure.вАЭ
n2003136:
tn The first word of the fifth line begins with вАП„ФвАО (he), the fifth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
tn The verb вАП„Ф÷Є„Щ÷Є„ФвАО (hayah) is stative, so theoretically its perfect form could be present tense or past tense. But the context gives her past accomplishments, so it is past tense here.
n2003137: sn The point of the simile is that she goes wherever she needs to go, near and far, to gather in all the food for the needs and the likes of the family. The line captures the vision and the industry of this woman.
n2003138: tn The imperfect verb (вАП„™÷Љ÷Є„С÷і„Щ„РвАО, tavi Њ) is used in its past habitual sense. The verbs describing the woman from verses 12-29 include 19 perfects and 9 preterites which describe actions with past time references. Thus the four imperfect verbs that describe her (vv. 14, 18, 21, 27) should be understood as modal and operating in a past time frame.
n2003139:
tn The first word of the sixth line begins with вАП„ХвАО (vav), the sixth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
tn The verb вАП„Х÷Є„™÷Љ÷Є„І÷Є„ЭвАО (vattaqom) is a preterite and therefore is past tense.
n2003140: tn The verb вАП„Х÷Ј„™÷Љ÷і„™÷Љ÷µ„ЯвАО (vattitten) is a preterite and therefore is past tense.
n2003141: sn The word for вАЬfoodвАЭ is вАП„Ш÷ґ„®÷ґ„£вАО (teref, вАЬpreyвАЭ; KJV вАЬmeatвАЭ), another word that does not normally fit the domestic scene. This word also is used in a similar way in Ps 111:5, which says the Lord gives food. Here it is the noble woman who gives food to her family and servants.
n2003142: sn The word вАП„Ч÷є„ІвАО (khoq) probably means вАЬallotted portion of foodвАЭ as before, but some suggest it means the task that is allotted to the servants, meaning that the wise woman gets up early enough to give out the work assignments (Tg. Prov 31:15, RSV, NRSV, TEV, NLT). That is possible, but seems an unnecessary direction for the line to take. Others, however, simply wish to delete this last colon, leaving two cola and not three, but that is unwarranted.
n2003143:
tn The first word of the seventh line begins with вАП„ЦвАО (zayin), the seventh letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
tn As the perfect form of a dynamic verb, вАП„Ц÷Є„Ю÷∞„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (zamemah) should be understood as past tense or perfective. A simple past tense translation is particularly well suited here. Her past actions are collected in this portrait to typify her character whether she did those actions frequently or rarely. Although she bought a field, that does not mean that she regularly traded in real estate or even that she bought more than one field in her lifetime. It also does not mean that a woman has to make a real estate transaction to be a good wife.
tn The word вАЬconsideredвАЭ means вАЬto plan carefullyвАЭ in accordance with her purposes. The word is often used in the book of Proverbs for devising evil, but here it is used positively of the womanвАЩs wise investment.
n2003144: tn The verb вАП„Х÷Ј„™÷Љ÷і„І÷Љ÷Є„Ч÷µ„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (vattiqqakhehu) is a preterite and therefore is past tense.
n2003145: tn Heb вАЬfrom the fruit of her hands.вАЭ The expression employs two figures. вАЬHandsвАЭ is a metonymy of cause, indicating the work she does. вАЬFruitвАЭ is a hypocatastasis, an implied comparison meaning what she produces, the income she earns. She is able to plant a vineyard from her income.
n2003146: tn As the perfect form of a dynamic verb, вАП„†÷Є„Ш÷∞„Ґ÷Є„ФвАО (nate њah) should be understood as past tense or perfective.
n2003147:
tn The first word of the eighth line begins with вАП„ЧвАО (khet), the eighth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
tn Heb вАЬshe girded her loins with strength.вАЭ As the perfect form of a dynamic verb, it should be understood as past tense or perfective. The verb вАП„Ч÷Є„Т÷Є„®вАО (khagar) means to strap something on in the area of the waist. (The related noun [вАП„Ч÷≤„Т„Х÷є„®вАО; khagor] means вАЬbelt.вАЭ) When only вАЬloinsвАЭ (hips and waist) are mentioned, the idea is that of gathering up the long robes with a sash or belt so that they do not get in the way of the work. With another direct object or with the preposition вАП„СвАО (bet), it states what is strapped on (e.g. a belt, the ephod, sackcloth; cf. Lev. 8:7; 1 Kgs 20:32).
