The figure here is putting strength on the вАЬloinsвАЭ (вАП„Ю÷Є„™÷∞„†÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЭвАО; motnayim), the muscles that tie the abdomen to the hips. It is a metonymy for hard work. But it can be debated whether it refers to preparation for hard work, which would seem typical, or whether it works off of a literal understanding of putting strength on these muscles, which would be the result of hard work.
n2003148:
tn The verb вАП„Х÷Ј„™÷Љ÷∞„Р÷Ј„Ю÷µ÷Љ„•вАО (vatte Њammets) is a preterite and therefore past tense.
sn The expression вАЬshe made her arm strongвАЭ parallels the first half of the verse and indicates that she gets down to her work with vigor and strength. There may be some indication here of вАЬrolling up the sleevesвАЭ to ready the arms for the task, but that is not clear.
n2003149:
tn The first word of the ninth line begins with вАП„ШвАО (tet), the ninth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
tn As the perfect form of a dynamic verb, вАП„Ш÷Є„Ґ÷≤„Ю÷Є„ФвАО (ta њamah) should be understood as past tense or perfective. The basic meaning of the word is to вАЬtaste.вАЭ By extension it means to вАЬperceive; discern; evaluateвАЭ (cf. Job 12:11; 34:3). It either refers to evaluating the quality of her merchandise (that she sells) or to being sure that she is making a good and profitable trade.
n2003150: sn The line may be taken literally to mean that she is industrious throughout the night (вАЬburning the midnight oilвАЭ) when she must in order to follow through a business deal (W. McKane, Proverbs [OTL], 668); cf. TEV. But the line could also be taken figuratively, comparing вАЬher lightвАЭ to the prosperity of her householdвАФher whole lifeвАФwhich continues night and day.
n2003151: tn The imperfect verb вАП„Щ÷і„Ы÷∞„С÷ґ÷Љ„ФвАО (yikbeh) is used in its past habitual sense. The verbs describing the woman from verses 12-29 include 19 perfects and 9 preterites which describe actions with past time references. Thus the four imperfect verbs that describe her (vv. 14, 18, 21, 27) should be understood as modal and operating in a past time frame. Technically this verb does not describe her directly, though it refers to her lamp.
n2003152: tn The verb вАП„©÷і„Б„Ь÷∞÷Љ„Ч÷Є„ФвАО (shillekhah) is the Piel perfect of the root вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ь÷Ј„ЧвАО (shalakh) вАЬto extend; send.вАЭ As the perfect form of a dynamic verb, it should be understood as past tense or perfective. The Piel stem is commonly plurative for dynamic verbs. Applied here, the plurative notion refers to the repetition of reaching to the spool while working cloth.
n2003153:
tn The first word of the tenth line begins with вАП„ЩвАО (yod) the tenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
sn The words for вАЬhandsвАЭ are often paired in poetry; the first (вАП„Щ÷Є„УвАО, yad) means the hand and the forearm and usually indicates strength, and the second (вАП„Ы÷Ј÷Љ„£вАО, kaf) means the palm of the hand and usually indicates the more intricate activity.
n2003154: tn As the perfect form of a dynamic verb, вАП„™÷Є„Ю÷∞„Ы„Х÷ЉвАО (tamekhu) should be understood as past tense or perfective.
n2003155:
tn The verb (вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„®÷∞„©÷Є„Б„ФвАО, pareshah) is a perfect form of a dynamic verb. As such, it should be understood as past tense or perfective.
sn The parallel expressions here underscore her care for the needy. The first part uses вАЬshe spread her palmвАЭ and the second вАЬshe extended her hands,вАЭ repeating some of the vocabulary introduced in the last verse.
n2003156: tn The first word of the eleventh line begins with вАП„ЫвАО (kaf), the eleventh letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
n2003157: tn This is the same verb form that began verse 19, the Piel perfect of вАП„©÷Є„Б„Ь÷Ј„ЧвАО (shalakh). It may have the same plurative notion emphasizing that she often ministered to the poor. As the perfect form of a dynamic verb it should be understood as past tense or perfective.
n2003158: tn The first word of the twelfth line begins with вАП„ЬвАО (lamed), the twelfth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
n2003159: tn The imperfect verb (вАП„™÷і„Щ„®÷Є„РвАО, tira Њ) is used in its past habitual sense. The verbs describing the woman from verses 12-29 include 19 perfects and 9 preterites which describe actions with past time references. Thus the four imperfect verbs that describe her (vv. 14, 18, 21, 27) should be understood as modal and operating in a past time frame.
n2003160: sn вАЬSnowвАЭ is a metonymy of adjunct; it refers to the cold weather when snow comes. The verse is saying that this time is not a concern for the wise woman because the family is well prepared.
n2003161: tn For the MTвАЩs вАЬscarletвАЭ the LXX and the Latin have вАЬtwoвАЭ or вАЬdoubleвАЭвАФthe difference being essentially the vocalization of a plural as opposed to a dual. The word is taken in the versions with the word that follows (вАЬcoversвАЭ) to mean вАЬdouble garments.вАЭ The question to be asked is whether scarlet would keep one warm in winter or double garments. The latter is the easier reading and therefore suspect.
n2003162: tn The word вАЬbecauseвАЭ does not occur in the Hebrew in this verse, but continues to apply from the end of verse 21. It is added to both halves of this verse for clarity.
n2003163: tn The verb (вАП„Ґ÷Є„©÷∞„В„™÷Є„ФвАО;  њasetah) is the Hebrew perfect form of a dynamic verb. It contains background material in a causal clause and so is past perfect in English translation.
n2003164: tn The first word of the thirteenth line begins with вАП„ЮвАО (mem), the thirteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet. The word rendered вАЬcoverletsвАЭ appears in 7:16, where it has the idea of вАЬcovered.вАЭ K&D 17:335 suggests вАЬpillowsвАЭ or вАЬmattressesвАЭ here. The Greek version has вАЬlined overcoatsвАЭ or вАЬgarments,вАЭ but brings over the last word of the previous verse to form this line and parallel the second half, which has clothing in view.
n2003165: sn The вАЬfine linenвАЭ refers to expensive clothing (e.g., Gen 41:42), as does the вАЬpurpleвАЭ (e.g., Exod 26:7; 27:9, 18). Garments dyed with purple indicated wealth and high rank (e.g., Song 3:5). The rich man in Luke 16:19 was clothed in fine linen and purple as well. The difference is that the wise woman is charitable, but he is not.
n2003166:
tn The first word of the fourteenth line begins with вАП„†вАО (nun), the fourteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
tn The form is the Niphal participle of вАП„Щ÷Є„У÷Ј„ҐвАО (yada њ); it means that her husband is вАЬknown.вАЭ The point is that he is a prominent person, respected in the community. While the description of the wifeвАЩs work is given in the past tense (primarily through perfect and preterite verbs), the husband is described in the present tense with a participle. Her husbandвАЩs status has resulted to some degree from her faithful work and was not confined to the past but continues into the present time frame of the passage.
n2003167:
tn Heb вАЬgateвАЭ; the term вАЬcityвАЭ has been supplied in the translation for clarity.
sn The вАЬgateвАЭ was the area inside the entrance to the city, usually made with rooms at each side of the main street where there would be seats for the elders. This was the place of assembly for the elders who had judicial responsibilities.
n2003168: tn The construction uses the infinitive construct with the preposition and a pronominal suffix that serves as the subject (subjective genitive) to form a temporal clause. The fact that he вАЬsits with the eldersвАЭ means he is one of the elders; he sits as a judge among the people.
n2003169: tn The verb (вАП„Ґ÷Є„©÷∞„В„™÷Є„ФвАО,  њasetah) is the perfect form of a dynamic root and should be understood as past tense or perfective.
n2003170:
tn The first word of the fifteenth line begins with вАП„°вАО (samek), the fifteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
sn The poet did not think it strange or unworthy for a woman of this stature to be a businesswoman engaged in an honest trade. In fact, weaving of fine linens was a common trade for women in the ancient world.
n2003171: tn The verb вАП„Х÷Ј„™÷Љ÷і„Ю÷∞„Ы÷Љ÷є„®вАО (vattimkor) is a preterite and therefore is past tense. The preterite normally portrays a sequential action in the past.
n2003172: tn The verb (вАП„†÷Є„™÷∞„†÷Є„ФвАО, natenah) is the perfect form of a dynamic root and should be understood as past tense or perfective.
n2003173: tn Heb вАЬto the Canaanites.вАЭ These are the Phoenician traders that survived the wars and continued to do business down to the exile.
n2003174: sn The idea of clothing and being clothed is a favorite figure in Hebrew. It makes a comparison between wearing clothes and having strength and honor. Just as clothes immediately indicate something of the nature and circumstances of the person, so do these virtues.
n2003175: tn Or вАЬstrength and splendor have been her clothing.вАЭ This is a verbless clause so it takes its time frame from the context. It may be a comment on the goods she traded to the merchants. Or it may be a word picture about her character, in which case вАЬdignityвАЭ may be a better rendering than вАЬsplendor.вАЭ
n2003176: tn The first word of the sixteenth line begins with вАП„ҐвАО ( њayin), the sixteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
n2003177:
tn The verb вАП„Х÷Ј„™÷Љ÷і„©÷∞„В„Ч÷Ј„ІвАО (vattiskhaq) is a preterite and therefore is past tense.
sn Here вАЬlaughвАЭ is either a metonymy of adjunct or effect. The point is that she is confident for the future because of all her industry and planning.
n2003178: tn Heb вАЬday.вАЭ This word is a metonymy of subject meaning any events that take place on the day or in the time to come.
n2003179: tn The Hebrew verb (вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„™÷∞„Ч÷Є„ФвАО, patekhah) is the perfect form of a dynamic verb and should be understood as past tense or perfective. Most of the Hebrew perfect verbs in this description of the wife have been translated as simple past tense because in this portrait her actions are examples that typify her character whether she did then often or rarely. For example, although this woman bought a field (vs 16), that does not mean that she regularly traded in real estate or even that she bought more than one field in her lifetime. However it would be outside the character developed in this portrait to think that she only once said something wise. The Hebrew verbal construction is not specifically modal (вАЬwould open her mouth with wisdomвАЭ). However the word picture of opening the mouth is one that pictures the start of an activity that continues. For example in Ps 109:2, when the Psalmist complains that the wicked have opened (Hebrew perfect of вАП„§÷Љ÷Є„™÷Ј„ЧвАО, patakh) their mouth with deceit, he does not mean that they told only one lie. The opened mouth pictures talking, in contrast to the closed mouth which pictures silence (cf. Isa 53:7).
n2003180:
tn The first word of the seventeenth line begins with вАП„§вАО (pe), the seventeenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
sn The words вАЬmouthвАЭ (вАЬopened her mouthвАЭ) and вАЬtongueвАЭ (вАЬon her tongueвАЭ) here are also metonymies of cause, referring to her speaking.
n2003181: tn The Hebrew phrase вАП„™÷Љ„Х÷є„®÷Ј„™÷Њ„Ч÷ґ„°÷ґ„УвАО (torat khesed) is open to different interpretations. (1) The word вАЬlawвАЭ could here refer to вАЬteachingвАЭ as it does frequently in the book of Proverbs, and the word вАЬlove,вАЭ which means вАЬloyal, covenant love,вАЭ could have the emphasis on faithfulness, yielding the idea of вАЬfaithful teachingвАЭ to parallel вАЬwisdomвАЭ (cf. NIV). (2) The word вАЬloveвАЭ should probably have more of the emphasis on its basic meaning of вАЬloyal love, lovingkindness.вАЭ It also would be an attributive genitive, but its force would be that of вАЬloving instructionвАЭ or вАЬteaching with kindness.вАЭ
n2003182:
tn The first word of the eighteenth line begins with вАП„¶вАО (tsade), the eighteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
tn This is the only participle used in the description of the woman. Since participles receive their time frame from context, this should be understood to be part of the past time frame of the passage. Here it provides the contrast to the idleness mentioned in the latter half of the verse.
n2003183: tn The imperfect verb (вАП„™÷є„Р„Ы÷µ„ЬвАО, to Њkel) is used in its past habitual sense. The verbs describing the woman from verses 12-29 include 19 perfects and 9 preterites which describe actions with past time references. Thus the four imperfect verbs that describe her (vv. 14, 18, 21, 27) should be understood as modal and operating in a past time frame.
n2003184: sn The expression bread of idleness refers to food that is gained through idleness, perhaps given or provided for her. In the description of the passage one could conclude that this woman did not have to do everything she did; and this line affirms that even though she is well off, she will eat the bread of her industrious activity.
n2003185: sn This is certainly not an activity of infants and toddlers and probably refers to her grown children. In addition to the past tense verbs that describe her, this is another indication that this passage is giving us a retrospective view of her life and not a glimpse at her day-planner.
n2003186:
tn The first word of the nineteenth line begins with вАП„ІвАО (qof), the nineteenth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
tn The verb (вАП„І÷Є„Ю„Х÷ЉвАО; qamu) is the perfect form of a dynamic verb and should be understood as past tense or perfective. It is implied that her children have done this on more than one occasion.
sn The deliberate action of вАЬrising upвАЭ to call her blessed is the Hebrew way of indicating something important is about to be done that has to be prepared for.
n2003187: tn The verb вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷∞„Р÷Ј„©„Б÷Љ÷∞„®„Х÷Љ„Ф„Х÷ЉвАО (vaye Њasheruhu) is a preterite and therefore is past tense.
n2003188: tn The text uses an independent nominative absolute to draw attention to her husband: вАЬher husband, and he praises her.вАЭ Prominent as he is, her husband speaks in glowing terms of his noble wife.
n2003189: tn The verb вАП„Х÷Ј„Щ÷∞„Ф÷Ј„Ь÷∞„Ь÷Є„Ф÷ЉвАО (vayehalelah) is a preterite and therefore is past tense.
n2003190: tn The first word of the twentieth line begins with вАП„®вАО (resh), the twentieth letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
n2003192: tn The word is the same as in v. 10, вАЬnoble, valiant.вАЭ
n2003193: tn The word вАП„Ч÷µ„ЯвАО (khen) refers to вАЬcharm, gracefulness, graciousness, favor.вАЭ It frequently occurs in the phrase to вАЬfind favor in the eyesвАЭ of someone. So it appears to have a broad meaning that includes whatever may have the effect of eliciting a favorable response from someone else, something that they find charming (вАЬpleasant, agreeable qualitiesвАЭ HALOT, 322).
n2003194:
tn The first word of the twenty-first line begins with вАП„©„БвАО (shin), the twenty-first letter of the Hebrew alphabet. The graphic distinction between вАП„©„БвАО (shin) and вАП„©„ВвАО (sin) had not been made at the time the book of Proverbs was written; that graphic distinction was introduced by the Masoretes, ca. a.d. 1000.
tn The noun вАП„©÷ґ„Б„І÷ґ„®вАО (sheqer) means a вАЬlie; breach of faithвАЭ (HALOT, 1648). While it is not true that everything that incites favor is a lie (e.g. Boaz responded to RuthвАЩs character and NaomiвАЩs need when Ruth found вАЬfavorвАЭ in his eyes), this is a strong declaration against relying on the emotional impulse of attraction. Many messages in Western culture and media to вАЬfollow your heartвАЭ actually amount to little or no more than вАЬfollow whatever gives you a charmed feeling while ignoring moral constraints and potential consequences.вАЭ
n2003195: sn The verse shows that вАЬcharmвАЭ and вАЬbeautyвАЭ do not endure as do those qualities that the fear of the Lord produces. Charm is deceitful: One may be disappointed in the character of the one with beauty. Beauty is vain (fleeting as a vapor): Physical appearance will not last. The writer is not saying these are worthless; he is saying there is something infinitely more valuable.
n2003196: sn This chapter describes the wise woman as fearing the Lord. It is the fear of the Lord that is the beginning of wisdomвАФthat was the motto of the book (1:7). Psalm 111:10 also repeats that the fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom.
n2003197:
tn The verb вАП„™÷і„™÷∞„Ф÷Ј„Ь÷Љ÷Є„ЬвАО (tithallal) is a Hitpael imperfect. This is not the passive form (to вАЬbe praised,вАЭ so KJV, NIV, NASB, ESV, ASV, Holman) as occurs in Prov 12:8. The Hitpael of вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷Ј„ЬвАО (halal) is reflexive with meanings like вАЬto boast; take glory in.вАЭ It is sometimes parallel to terms for rejoicing (Pss 34:2; 63:11; 64:10; Isa 41:16), being exultant over accomplishment or victory. In the context of contrasting misleading and fleeting external qualities, the reflexive translation вАЬmakes herself praiseworthyвАЭ emphasizes her character. The imperfect form could also be understood as modal вАЬshe can boast.вАЭ This would not mean a selfish bragging since the term is used to boast in the Lord (1 Chr 16:10; Isa 41:6; Jer 4:2; Ps 34:2). Rather it would mean she has a sound basis for being proud of her accomplishments achieved under the umbrella of the fear of the Lord.
sn The last two verses of the chapter have shifted from the past tense description of the woman to commentary. This section began by asking who can find a wife/woman of noble character. It then described such a woman in past tense terms that are only fully evident in a retrospective of her life. The commentary at this point serves to remind that the fear of the Lord is the quality to look for rather than merely external beauty. While there is certainly an implication for women to develop good character, the direct teaching is to men. The issue before fathers is how to direct their sons to value the right things in a woman against the pressure to emphasize outward appearance or perhaps even aspects of personality.
n2003198: tn The first word of the twenty-second line begins with вАП„™вАО (tav), the last letter of the Hebrew alphabet.
n2003199: tn Heb вАЬGive her from the fruit of her hands.вАЭ The expression вАЬthe fruit of her handsвАЭ employs two figures. The word вАЬfruitвАЭ is a figure known as hypocatastasis, an implied comparison, meaning вАЬwhat she produces.вАЭ The word вАЬhandвАЭ is a metonymy of cause, meaning her efforts to produce things. So the line is saying essentially вАЬgive her her due.вАЭ This would either mean give her credit for what she has done (the option followed by the present translation; cf. TEV) or reward her for what she has done (cf. NAB, NIV, NLT).
n20031100: sn Psalm 111 began with the imperative вАП„Ф÷Є„Ь÷∞„Ь„Х÷Љ „Щ÷Є„ФвАО (halelu yah, вАЬpraise the LordвАЭ); this poem ends with the jussive вАП„Х÷і„Щ„Ф÷Є„Ь÷∞„Ь„Х÷Љ„Ф÷Є÷ЉвАО (vihaleluha, вАЬand let [her works] praise herвАЭ). Psalm 111:2 speaks of GodвАЩs works and most of the Psalm describes his works; this verse speaks of the womanвАЩs work that deserves praise and most of this section describes her works. Psalm 111 ends with reference to вАЬthe fear of the LordвАЭ and Prov 31 ends with reference to вАЬthe fear of the Lord.вАЭ
n20031101: tn вАЬGatesвАЭ is a metonymy of subject. It refers to the people and the activity that occurs in the gatesвАФbusiness dealings, legal transactions, and social meetings. The term вАЬcityвАЭ is supplied in the translation for clarity. One is reminded of the acclaim given to Ruth by Boaz: вАЬfor all the gate of my people knows that you are a noble woman [вАП„Р÷µ„©÷ґ„Б„™ „Ч÷Ј„Щ÷і„ЬвАО,  Њeshet khayil]вАЭ (Ruth 3:11).
